title: A pioneer and a superstar, Kevin Keegan’s achievements were extraordinary | Soccer | The Guardian
description: The former England manager may be remembered by many for his ‘I’d love it’ rant in 1996, but that should not obscure the remarkable nature of his football career
Author: Rob Draper
Headline: The former England manager may be remembered by many for his ‘I’d love it’ rant in 1996, but that should not obscure the remarkable nature of his football career
Content: Kevin Keegan in action for Liverpool in August 1976.Photograph: Colorsport/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenKevin Keegan in action for Liverpool in August 1976.Photograph: Colorsport/ShutterstockSoccerA pioneer and a superstar, Kevin Keegan’s achievements were extraordinaryThe former England manager may be remembered by many for his ‘I’d love it’ rant in 1996, but that should not obscure the remarkable nature of his football careerRob DraperMon 20 Jul 2026 11.02 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleKevin Keegan,who has died aged 75, is the only British player to win the Ballon d’Or twice, was the star of Liverpool’s first European Cup-winning team, a messianic figure at Newcastle United as a player and coach, forging a team so memorable that Fifa’s president wrote to commend him, and was the captain and then manager of the England men’s national team. And yet, as Keegan would ruefully reflect, he will principally be remembered by many for an emotional outburst that precipitated Newcastle’s historic failure in a Premier League title race, a TV interview which presaged the coming era of the modern game.Keegan’s “I’d love it” rantof April 1996, directed at Alex Ferguson, became a meme before the modern meaning of the word and social media even existed, repeatedly played on Sky Sports and becoming indelibly marked in the minds of multiple generations, many not even born when it occurred. It frequently tops polls as the most memorable moment in Premier League history and foreshadowed an era where digital clips and personality narratives would dominate football coverage.Kevin Keegan, Liverpool legend and former Newcastle manager, dies aged 75Read moreThere is a danger, however, of reducing Keegan’s life to a series of ever-more emotional explosions, of which there were many, such as his punch-up with Leeds United’s Billy Bremner in the 1974 Charity Shield. For that would obscure the extraordinary nature of his achievements and the fact he was arguably the most charismatic modern English footballer; more eloquent than David Beckham and Paul Gascoigne and more passionate than Bobby Moore. He had a rare gift for inspiring players and almost everyone who interacted with him at his best came away uplifted.Still, Keegan’s meltdown on TV, where he took issue with Ferguson’s supposed “mind games” in suggesting Leeds players would go easy on Newcastle during a tight title run-in with Manchester United, became a defining moment. “Strangers shout ‘love it’ to me from car windows,” wrote Keegan in his autobiography. “Autograph hunters tell me they would ‘love it’ if I could sign their pieces of paper. When I go to charity events I am often asked to sign photographs showing me in that classic pose: headphones on, finger jabbing, blazing eyes. Those pictures tend to be auctioned for a lot more money than the ones where I am playing forEnglandor any of my clubs.”Keegan claimed to be relaxed about the characterisation, skewed though it was, perhaps because more than any English footballer before he had maximised football’s commercial and media opportunities and accepted the respective bouquets and brickbats that ensued. Even before that meltdown, he would be best remembered by many in the 1970s for his excruciating bike crash on Superstars, a TV show which pitted athletes from different sports against each other in unfamiliar disciplines. Sprawled across the cycle track, Keegan’s bare flesh was ripped red raw by the gravel. Most remember the drama but few recall that, showing characteristic pig-headed determination, he got back on the bike, bloodied and bandaged, for a rematch, which he won before winning the entire contest.Keegan was born in Armthorpe, Doncaster, to Doris and Joe, a war veteran Durham miner who had migrated south in search of work at Markham colliery. His upbringing echoed Monty Python’s Yorkshiremen sketch, only his memories were authentic, a throwback to a different century. Their terrace house “had a detached outhouse where we washed. Our bath was an old zinc tub and the toilet was at the foot of the garden and we never had electricity until I was 10 and we moved into a council house in Balby.”View image in fullscreenKevin Keegan shares a joke with Alex Ferguson after the rollercoaster 1995-96 season.Photograph: Adam Butler/PAKeegan’s ascent to the peak of the game’s elite was unforeseen, few recognising his talent early on, most dismissing him as too small. He always credited Sister Mary Oliver, the nun who ran his Catholic primary school football team and who refereed with a crucifix around her neck, as being as important to him as a mentor as Bill Shankly was. “Kevin’s football must be encouraged,” she wrote in a school report, which gave him immense self-belief.Doncaster Rovers rejected him and he started work at Peglers Brass Works at 16, playing for their football team but only in the reserves and also for a pub team on Sundays, where he fortuitously played against a Scunthorpe scout, Bob Nellis, who got him a trial at the struggling Third Division club. Keegan referred to himself as a “mongrel” and “weasel” and the “least-talented” Ballon d’Or winner, and despite impressing at Scunthorpe it was still a major shock when Shankly, the man he regarded as a second father and progenitor of his success, took him toLiverpool. Shankly told him: “You’re not just a great player – you’re one of the great players!” It seemed an unlikely boast but Keegan scored on his Liverpool debut and was never dropped thereafter. He would inherit from Shankly comparable motivational skills.Quick GuideThe Keegan years: from player to managerShow1951Kevin Keegan is born on 14 February in Armthorpe, near Doncaster.1968Begins playing career at Scunthorpe United.1971Signed by Bill Shankly for Liverpool at age of 20, initially as a midfielder.1972Makes England debut against Wales in November.1973Liverpool win the league; Keegan scores twice in Uefa Cup final win over Borussia Mönchengladbach.1974Scores twice as Liverpool beat Newcastle 3-0 in the FA Cup final. His first England goal comes against Wales.1976Liverpool win the league title and Keegan scores in each leg of  Uefa Cup final win against Club Brugge. His celebrity is such he appears on the TV show Superstars, and famously crashes his bike during one of the races.1977Another league title for Liverpool, who also win their first European Cup, a 3-1 victory over Mönchengladbach. Keegan moves to Hamburg for £500,000, nearly double the German transfer record.1978Wins Ballon d’Or.1979Wins Ballon d’Or again as Hamburg secure their first German ‌championship title in 19 years, and first in the Bundesliga era.1980Captains England at Euros but they are knocked out in the group stage. Leaves Hamburg to join Southampton for two seasons.1982Transfers to Newcastle in the Second Division. Plays final match for England, after 63 caps (31 as captain) and 21 goals.1984Helps Newcastle secure promotion in his second season, then retires from playing. Across his club career, he scores 204 goals in 592 ‌appearances.1992Takes first coaching role as manager of struggling Newcastle in February. Danger of relegation to the Third Division is averted.1993Leads them to the First Division title and promotion to the Premier League in his first full season in charge.1994Newcastle finish third in Premier League.1996Newcastle finish second in the Premier League, ​despite leading for most ​of campaign – by 12 points at one stage. Season includes famous “I would love it if we beat them” comments when discussing rivals Manchester United on Sky Sports.1997Leaves Newcastle and takes over at Fulham for two seasons.1999Takes charge ​of England national ‌team in February.2000Coaches England at Euro 2000 where they beat Germany but still go out in the group stage. Resigns in ​October after a 1–0 loss to Germany in World Cup qualifying.2001Takes over at Manchester City.2008Returns to Newcastle but lasts eight months after tensions with the board and owner Mike Ashley.Was this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.Though reclusive, Keegan also had a streak of extrovertism, embracing every aspect of the game’s growing commercial opportunities, from his famousBrutadvert to appearances on Top of the Pops, where his single Head Over Heels in Lovereached the top 40. Though he enjoyed standing out, he admitted he would never have got away with his trademark bubble perm in the old-school Liverpool dressing room. His wife, Jean, whom he met on the waltzers at the Doncaster St Leger horse racing meeting when she was 16 and he was 19 and to whom he remained married for life, burst into fits of laughter when she first saw it. He had the last laugh, the hairstyle inspiring alarming copycat versions across the football world.Keegan remained true to what he considered to be his working-class, mining roots, declaring himself a Labour man, annoyed that a staged photo of him feigning to kiss then prime minister Margaret Thatcher on the cheek was interpreted as support for economic policies which had ravaged heavy industry. He was more at home playing head tennis with a later PM, Tony Blair, to whom he was close enough to host at his home for dinner.View image in fullscreenThe then Newcastle manager Keegan chats to the Labour leader Tony Blair in the summer of 1996.Photograph: Adam Butler/PAKeegan inherited two mantles from Britain’s greatest players of the 1960s. As George Best, the original footballer sex symbol of the modern era, lapsed into alcoholism and increasing unreliability, Keegan became the more wholesome and dependable commercial face of the superstar footballer in Britain, inundated with deals. For England he effectively became Moore’s successor as captain but at a time when the national team was in decline from his predecessor’s World Cup-winning heights. For Keegan, as the sole outfield world-class player, it was a heavy burden to bear as they missed out on successive World Cup finals.His first major tournament for his country was the ill-fated 1980 European Championship and his World Cup career amounted to 20 minutes in 1982, a campaign which was disrupted by a back injury and where “my only contribution was a dreadful miss against the host nation to send us home. That moment is probably the only moment of my England career that people remember now.” It would prove to be his last game for England at the age of just 31.More notable features of Keegan’s England career were his courage in defying IRA death threats to play against Northern Ireland in Belfast, then walking out on the team when Don Revie dropped him shortly afterwards and being arbitrarily beaten up by Yugoslav custom officials prior to a game in Belgrade, a horrific and wholly underserved ordeal that sparked an international incident. His frustration was best summed up in a story he told about the great Dutch player Johnny Rep. Surveying an England team at Wembley in 1977, Rep turned to Keegan and said: “Kevvy, you have problems here: this is the worst England side I have seen.” The Netherlands won 2-0 and, as Keegan recalled, could have scored at will.Keegan was a pioneering player, negotiating release clauses, unheard of at the time in England, at Liverpool andHamburg, allowing him to leave if a club met a £500,000 fee, thereby controlling his moves. Leaving Liverpool at what was assumed to be their peak – he had just helped them win their first European Cup in 1977 – to join a team sixth in the Bundesliga was demonstrative of both Keegan’s single-mindedness and capacity to challenge himself. He also quadrupled his salary, reflecting the economic state of English football at the time.View image in fullscreenKeegan with the ball at his feet for Hamburg in the 1977-78 Bundesliga season.Photograph: Colorsport/ShutterstockHamburg was not an initial success. Jealous teammates held meetings to get him kicked out of the club and, according to Keegan, refused to pass to him. His nadir came at Christmas 1977 when, in a festive-season friendly, unhappy at his plight and frustrated with over-physical treatment from the Lübeck defender Erhard Press, Keegan punched him on the jaw and subsequently knocked him out, receiving an eight-week ban as punishment. Yet, a year later, he won his first Ballon d’Or and went on to lead Hamburg to their first Bundesliga title since 1960.Keegan embraced Germany, learning the language and was nicknamed “Mighty Mouse”, a tribute to his astonishing strength given his diminutive size. His first daughter, Laura, was born there but his finale, a European Cup final that would have sealed Hamburg’s place in history, was a sad one, ending as it did in defeat to Nottingham Forest. Having won three league titles, two Uefa Cups, an FA Cup and European Cup at Liverpool, the Bundesliga titlewould be the last trophy Keegan ever won. He was just 29 years old.Keegan made a typically unconventional choice in joining Southampton, a promising yet small-market team under a talented, persuasive manager in Lawrie McMenemy. Keegan propelled them to a top-six finish and qualification for Europe, but they faded to seventh in the second season and Keegan, disillusioned, made a decision that would define the second half of his professional life.Newcastle were in one of their periodic financial and footballing crises and in the old Second Division when, with money from brewers Scottish & Newcastle, they persuaded him to join in 1982. His father’s links to the north-east and reverence for Newcastle players such as Jackie Milburn were an emotional pull on Keegan, but one that cost him his England career. The manager, Bobby Robson, didn’t feel it appropriate to pick a second-tier player for England and Keegan, still captain and by far the most iconic national team player, learned from the press he had been “dumped”. He felt “betrayed, hurt and humiliated”.Crowds doubled at St James’ Park with Keegan’s arrival and Newcastle were promoted as champions in Keegan’s second season, 1983-84, by which point he had already decided to quit playing at the age of 33. Typically impulsive, it was when Mark Lawrenson outsprinted Keegan to dispossess him during an FA Cup tie at Liverpool that he decided he would finish his career at the end of the season.View image in fullscreenKeegan in action for England in May 1979. His international playing career ended in acrimonious fashion.Photograph: Peter Robinson/Empics Sport/PAKeegan and family, which now included his second daughter, Sarah, born in 1982, decamped to his Marbella villa and, other than periodic business deals and discussions about a long-held dream to launch a Soccer Circus, he embraced retirement. Yet endless rounds of golf failed to satisfy. The family had moved back to his Hampshire home when Sir John Hall, a dynamic north-east businessman, called in 1992, begging Keegan to help Newcastle avoid relegation to the third tier and potential liquidation. Despite having watched no football for years, Keegan accepted. He threatened to quit after a few games but stayed to keep Newcastle up and promptly announced that he wanted to build a dynasty to rival Liverpool and challenge for the Premier League title.Most considered that quixotic and yet Newcastle won promotion to the Premier League as champions and with judicious youthful signings such as Andy Cole and Rob Lee, and veteran players such as Peter Beardsley, they came third and sixth in the top flight. So great was his authority, Keegan could even placate angry fans on the steps of St James’ Park when the club sold Cole to Manchester United.With that money the club signed David Ginola and Les Ferdinand and won nine of their first 10 games in 1995-96, often in thrilling fashion, establishing a seemingly unassailable 12-point lead by January. But a crucial defeat at home to Ferguson’s United in March 1996 and the Scot’s repeated use of psychological barbs seemed to weigh heavy on Keegan as his team’s form disintegrated. A dramatic yet debilitating 4-3 defeat at Liverpool led Sepp Blatter to write to Keegan but the sight of him slumped over advertising hoardings as Liverpool scored a last-minute winner suggested the toll was too much.Keegan eventually cracked, denouncing Ferguson in his famous TV interview. “You can tell him now, we’re still fighting for this title and I’ll tell you, honestly, I will love it if we beat them. Love it!” United won the title with four points to spare.View image in fullscreenKeegan (left) during a Fulham training session at Roehampton in 1999.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianKeegan initially seemed determined to rebuild as the club signed Alan Shearer for a world-record £15m and they beat United 5-0 at St James’ Park that autumn, but Keegan quit soon after, outraged that a city banker preparing the club for flotation had issued him an ultimatum to sign a contract. Football’s new era seemed untethered from the idealistic foundational values imbued in him by Shankly.Keegan seemed done with football until the now-disgraced Harrods owner Mohamed Al Fayed used speculative talks about his much-vaunted Soccer Circus to persuade him to become chief operating officer and eventually manager of Fulham in the third tier. He almost seemed at his happiest as the club accelerated to promotion, but his time was interrupted by a national crisis when Glenn Hoddle was sacked as England manager for making offensive comments about disabled people. The clamour for Keegan to take over was indicative of his popularity with ordinary fans. However, it proved a decision he came to greatly regret, taking in a humiliating Euro 2000 group-stage exit andending with him resigningin the toilets after an ignominious defeat against Germany in England’s last game at the old Wembley Stadium. It was, he conceded, a job beyond his abilities.That said, Keegan was happy to take on Manchester City in the old First Division, getting them promoted to the Premier League as champions with 99 points. He remained at the club for four years, an almost unprecedented period of stability at the club at that time. When he finally left City he seemed to be done with coaching and finally launched his Soccer Circus in Glasgow, though it closed after a few years. Yet there was to be one last comeback at Newcastle under the ill-starred ownership of Mike Ashley, which proved a disaster and lasted just eight months.The idea that a high-minded, old-fashioned football manager schooled by Shankly could coexist with a modern businessman as ruthless as Ashley and his cast of minions running the club always seemed improbable. When he walked away from the job, eventually winning a lengthy and bitter constructive dismissal claim, it was to be Keegan’s last act in football. So acrimonious was the departure that he was only able to return in disguise to attend a function at the club.The current regime made clear Keegan was always welcome back at St James’ Park. That was after his stage four cancer had been revealed in a series of speaking engagements in May 2026, one of which in Newcastle was, in retrospect, a heartfelt farewell to the fans to whom he had always felt closest. After that draining but exhilarating evening Keegan’s associates reported that he was in high spirits, energised by the reconnection with the fans who loved him most.It was to prove Keegan’s final goodbye. Football, as he observed, had given him everything except for a happy ending.Explore more on these topicsFootballNewcastle UnitedLiverpoolEnglandHamburgEuropean club footballfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: With PM Burnham, we’ll get warmth, audacity and knowhow. For everything else, cling to hope | Polly Toynbee | The Guardian
description: He looks the part, with a rise as impressive as his speeches, but is that enough? You cannot know if someone is up to the job until they have to do it, says Guardian columnist Polly Toynbee
Author: Polly Toynbee
Headline: He looks the part, with a rise as impressive as his speeches, but is that enough? You cannot know if someone is up to the job until they have to do it, says Guardian columnist Polly Toynbee
Content: Andy Burnham delivers a speech outside No 10, 20 July.Photograph: Andy Rain/EPAView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham delivers a speech outside No 10, 20 July.Photograph: Andy Rain/EPAOpinionAndy BurnhamWith PM Burnham, we’ll get warmth, audacity and knowhow. For everything else, cling to hopePolly ToynbeeHe looks the part, with a rise as impressive as his speeches, but is that enough? You cannot know if someone is up to the job until they have to do itMon 20 Jul 2026 13.28 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 16.28 EDTShareOnly vaulting ambition takes anyone to No 10, but Andy Burnham’s ambition reaches phenomenal heights. Can he really make the biggest changes in 40 years? Can he build a new economy? Thenew prime ministerlashes himself to the mast of those mighty aspirations, but adds in something even riskier – anend to rough sleeping, ordered immediately. That’s so highly visible everywhere that it will be a test of the extent of his power – will it reach into each dank alley and shop doorway?He understands the immensity of the tasks ahead. “I know what to do,” he said on taking up the leadership. Indeed, he is the most experienced prime minister to enter that door in recent years. He knows the Treasury as its chief secretary, he knows the inside ways of the cabinet,he has run the NHSand GreaterManchester. He knows Whitehall and Westminster, not to their advantage. Beyond that, he knows the disgruntled mood in the everyday world outside, whereincomes stagnate.“I have a plan,” he dares to claim, making cynics and malevolents smirk because everyone knows that wars, plagues, riots, Donald Trump and unknown unknowns upset the best-laid grids in Downing Street spreadsheets. He knows this is the best of times, the crest of the wave, the rising tide of hope. So he must not waste it: on the progressive side of life, they don’t come round often.As a reminder that the time is short and the path hard, Keir Starmer gracefully bore the gruesome farewell ritual. He rightly says he leaves the country “stronger and fairer”. He was ousted not by plotters but by a public still unrelenting in its hostility: only28% agree he did well, against 64% who don’t. It was his triumph to attract enough votes to sweep away the party that brought Brexit destruction, wrecked the NHS, denuded councils, blocked the courts, packed the prisons, made food banks the default social security system and degraded public services.Burnham arrives asthe most liked PM in recent times:his net popularity isfar higher than that enjoyed by any of Keir Starmer, Boris Johnson, Liz Truss or Rishi Sunak when they arrived in office. It wasn’t long before they all tanked, as Burnham well knows. Expect an avalanche of signature policies in these first 100 days. Read the runes of his cabinet choices, after he has instructed each on precisely what he wants of them: John Healey as chancellor will be key to his plan, whatever it is, as will Ed Miliband as foreign secretary, Yvette Cooper at health and Shabana Mahmood, who remains as home secretary.Expect more of the audacity that he showed in standing for election in Makerfield, where Reform had just swept up every council seat. Had he lost, it would all be over for him and probably for Labour. Look at how he dares to disrespect Margaret Thatcher, blaming her for 40 years of neoliberalism, the parlous state of public utilities and sold-off council housing, causing a “concentration of more wealth and powerin the hands of fewer people and fewer places”. Still in awe, Tony Blair and Gordon Brown both made a public exhibition of inviting her for tea and talk soon after entering Downing Street. That spell is broken.Burnham may be a luckier general than Starmer: the FT reports he has an unexpected extra £16bn for capital spending. Economists pile his desk with suggested billions to be gleaned without breaching manifesto promises or frightening bond markets: one of them, Will Hutton, collects them up, declaring “the cupboard is not bare”.He is lucky in his adversaries. Nigel Farage makes an ass of himself fighting Count Binface in Clacton, while angrily ducking questions about vast sums of money, such as never contaminated British politics before. Meanwhile, Kemi Badenoch leads her party ever-further from voters, misled by her rowdy media ideologues: the net zero climate target is backed bytwice as many as oppose it, yet she thinks abandoning it is a winning ticket,banning any pro-net zero parliamentary candidates, even in the middle of a heatwave. Burnham will hold steady on manifesto pledges on this. As the public regrets Brexit, wanting closer European ties, Badenoch perversely plans to eject any “wets” who back staying in the European convention on human rights. Until her generation of Tories has departed or begged forgiveness for the Brexit disaster, her party looks destined for the wilderness.She has reason to worry. What Badenoch dubs “airy fairy” about Burnham is a warmth and likeability she lacks. It can’t be faked. “Relatable” is a word often used about him – others are “connecting”, “clicking”, “listening” and “understanding”. He is lavish with language and mood music. I have met him over many years and he has always been a man of ideas, who has grown in stature and self-confidence with each promotion. He is good at acknowledging how far he has come without humble-bragging or inverted snobbery.Writing my memoir about class, I asked everyone I met to tell me about a moment when their class embarrassed them, whether they had felt too posh or not posh enough. I liked his answer: at Cambridge a girl invited this working-class boy for dinner, but he turned up for lunch instead of in the evening. That’s the kind of experience that at first gave him impostor syndrome, but he seems at ease now anywhere: proud of home, place and background, he climbed the ladder without tearing up his roots. That was Keir Starmer’s honest story too, yet however often he talked of his tool-maker father, his nurse mother, his care worker sister, their pebble-dash home, some lack in warm communication stopped people seeing him that way. The PM who went to Leeds University was seen as a “sir” in a suit, while the Cambridge-educated PM Andy is Mr Relatable in a T-shirt.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe qualities that make a successful prime minister are mercurial; no manual exists. You never know until too late how firm is their backbone, how steely their resolution. Will they buckle under adversity, or be blown off-course by gales of contradictory advice – or will they know when to bend if necessary? I thought Starmer and Brown would have them: both clever, decent, serious men, but not leaders, it turned out. The public warmed to neither. No doubt the peculiar people who dare aspire to this most monstrous job never know themselves if they really have it. Do they all feel like impostors?Listening to his words in Downing Street, we hear a good speech-maker, with a good political record and a good character who brings goodwill with him. The rest is hope.Polly Toynbee is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionKeir StarmercommentShareReuse this content

title: ‘Most people don’t know we’ve set up a doomsday system’: physicist Laura Grego on satellites, nuclear weapons and the battle for our skies | Space | The Guardian
description: Elon Musk is trying to own it, competing powers are trying to fight in it and nobody seems to be regulating it. So who does space really belong to?
Author: Aida Edemariam
Headline: Elon Musk is trying to own it, competing powers are trying to fight in it and nobody seems to be regulating it. So who does space really belong to?
Content: ‘If we think humanity is something worth saving, then we have a lot we need to do to make sure that happens’ … Laura Grego.Photograph: Lucy Lu/The GuardianView image in fullscreen‘If we think humanity is something worth saving, then we have a lot we need to do to make sure that happens’ … Laura Grego.Photograph: Lucy Lu/The GuardianThe ideas interviewSpace‘Most people don’t know we’ve set up a doomsday system’: physicist Laura Grego on satellites, nuclear weapons and the battle for our skiesElon Musk is trying to own it, competing powers are trying to fight in it and nobody seems to be regulating it. So who does space really belong to?Aida EdemariamMon 20 Jul 2026 09.09 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 09.10 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe last time I saw Laura Grego was in a tent in a baking field on the Welsh borders in May. The conversation on stage ranged from the dangers ofspace debristo how the law of the sea might pertain to outer space; from our sense of the sacred to our interplanetary futures. At one point Grego, a physicist, said intently, “Peoplehaveto be aware of what is happening in space. Public knowledgehasto catch up.” Three weeks later, SpaceX launched on the stock exchange, temporarily making Elon Musk the world’s first trillionaire.So, I ask her now, what is it that we should all know? Well, she says, the average person’s experience of space is about to change. If, so far, that has been “looking at the stars and being full of wonder, full of connection with the universe”, perhaps noticing the occasional streaking object that was not a shooting star, now “there are plans for tens of thousands more satellites, which would really change your relationship with what you see in the sky”.SpaceX, specifically, has just applied to a federal regulator for permission to launch up to 1m of them.Grego, who is now back in the US and speaking to me on screen from Cambridge, Massachusetts, has an unforced warmth and urgency, and a lack of self-aggrandisement in striking contrast to the gung-ho attitude that seems to accompany so much discussion of space. She became a physicist when she arrived at university and looked for the hardest thing she could find to study, so as to get her time’s worth (“a very midwestern perspective”). Then she became an observational cosmologist, which she found she loved. This merged with her other life as an activist (“anti-war, workers’ rights, racial justice – all the things important for a better society”) when she became research director of the Global Security programme at the Union of Concerned Scientists.View image in fullscreen‘I happen to think Earth is a really nice place’ … Laura Grego.Photograph: Lucy Lu/The GuardianThe union, founded at the height of the Vietnam war by American scientists horrified by their government’s misuse of science, aims to direct research energies toward solving environmental and social problems instead; Grego sees herself as working in the tradition of Leo Szilard, who tried to warn the US government against using an atomic bomb on Japan, and of “scientists taking seriously the responsibility to illuminate the dangers”. So she does research, she educates, sometimes she lobbies about specific issues: explaining to the George W Bush administration, for example, that no, their giddy visions of “space weapons, very much inspired by science fiction, maybe movies, things that would zip marines around in space and pew, pew, pew!” would not work. The current administration, which appears to treat war like a video game for which they can make up the rules, is unsurprisingly proving trickier.There are in fact rules in space. Almost all countries are signed up to the OuterSpaceTreaty of 1967, which requires that space be used for peaceful purposes only, that no one stations nuclear weapons in space, or any weapons on any celestial bodies, and that no one may claim ownership of any celestial body. It is silent on weapons transiting through space, or indeed being put into orbit.The truth is, Grego says, space has always been a military zone, insofar as the very first satellite, Sputnik 1, sent up in 1957 – “a civilian object in that all it really did was go beep, beep, beep” – was a geopolitical assertion: “Who’s better, who’s on top technologically.” Then there was how it got there. A rocket launcher is not so different from an intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM). “And once you have a working ICBM, you could put a nuclear weapon on top of it and send it from the Soviet Union to the United States in half an hour, right?”It is probably helpful, here, to remember that space is not that far away: the atmosphere begins to peter out about 100km up – roughly the distance from London to Portsmouth, say. “And ICBMs, if you’re sending them 10,000km from one country to another, they pop up above the atmosphere within a few minutes and then they spend most of their time where the lower satellites are. So they spend most of their time in space.”Because it is so close, and has none of the airspace restrictions we have within the atmosphere, there was quickly another military use to space, which was “overflights of each other’s countries to see what they’re doing. How many nuclear missiles do they have? What are they doing with their troops?” This is an important aspect of enforcing arms control agreements: “Taking pictures every day to see, are you keeping your word?”But with such range and sophistication comes vulnerability. There is standard military vulnerability: “For a military like the US, which is global, extremely modern, space is the backbone. Space provides long-distance communications, targeting, battle-assessment, weather prediction – even drone warfare runs off satellite signals in some cases.” But satellites themselves are very valuable, and largely undefended, which has been “an uncomfortable position for militaries to be in”.Then, given that we now use satellites to find our way from A to B, to order a cab or check the weather – not to mention the infinite commercial uses, from airlines to inventory management – there is the economic vulnerability. “You can jam them, you can spoof them, you could cyber-attack them. And that’s not totally uncommon.” In fact, earlier this month Russiawas reportedto have tried to jam Musk’s Starlink satellites, so as to impede Ukrainian drones.No country has yet destroyed another country’s satellite intentionally, Grego continues: “That’s been a line we haven’t crossed. But most of the big spacefaring countries, which also happen to be nuclear countries, are testing weapons that you could use against other satellites.” Avant-garde space defence now looks for ways to sidle up to opposition satellites. “If you get that close, you could set your adversary’s satellite spinning. You could spray-paint it. You could blast it with radiation” – effectively, make it “stop working without blasting it into little pieces”. This is an important consideration when all of this stuff is circling the world every 90 minutes, at a speed at which a rogue fragment the size of a marble could take out any satellite in its way.And finally, there is the nuclear vulnerability. When, last autumn, Kathryn Bigelow’s Netflix thriller A House of Dynamite had22m views within three daysof its release, Pete Hegseth’s Pentagon circulated an internal memo that wasobtained by Bloomberg. The movie’s depiction of a US system with under 60% accuracy was, they insisted to their staff, untrue; Trump’s $1.2tn Golden Dome system would be 100% effective.Grego was asked about the memo live on BBC TV. The movie is in fact correct, she said; just over half of tests have failed. Though actually, she says now, A House of Dynamite – which was well researched, just without asking the Pentagon – portrayed “a strange, very unrealistic case where an adversary might have launched a single weapon without any countermeasures, or any real attempt to disguise it. It’s hard to imagine why a country would do that unless it was an accidental launch.”View image in fullscreenThe Pentagon complained about the depiction of the US defence system in A House of Dynamite.Photograph: Eros Hoagland/PABut where it was realistic, she adds, was in the timelines. The standard way of protecting ICBMs is to put them in silos – “dig a big hole, pour the concrete, put a shield on top.” But everyone knows where they are because there have been satellites flying over for decades. “And the worry is somebody will pre-emptively attack those ICBMs and leave you without them to retaliate, which would upset the balance of mutually assured destruction. And so a key reason you have these satellites looking is that once you see anything headed in, you can quickly launch those missiles before they’re destroyed on the ground. It’s kind of: use them or lose them.”The thing is, the whole ICBM trajectory only lasts half an hour, and “you’re compressed by detection, communication. So a president would have less than 10 minutes to decide whether to launch ICBMs or to wait it out … the system is built so that question gets asked right away. And it creates this intense time-pressure” – and on imperfect people, with incomplete information.She adds: “If the president decided to launch a counterattack, even if the initial attack was launched by accident, certainly the adversary is going to retaliate. That’s a civilisation-ending day – or an afternoon.” I’m so taken aback I laugh out loud. “I know,” she says. “I’m sorry. I’ve had many years to acclimate to this. But most people haven’t. Most people don’t know we’ve set up a system that is essentially a doomsday system. And we can dismantle it. We just have not.”And not only that, Grego says, but we are upping the ante. “All of the nuclear-armed countries are investing in improving or modernising or expanding their nuclear arsenals.” China tested an ICBM in early July; the US is accusing Russia of building (though not yet deploying) a nuclear weapon that would be based in space and target satellites, “which would be totally counter to the Outer Space Treaty”. Not to mention the possibility of AI autonomous systems that are likely to speed up decision-making times, and have less accountability. “Most of the nuclear-armed countries are involved in some kind of conflict somewhere,” she says. All arms-control treaties have expired, and if countries have learned anything from the Iran war, where the US bombed them in the middle of diplomatic negotiations, it’s that “if you don’t have a weapon right now, you might want to get one so this doesn’t happen to you.” As for the Golden Dome, “it’s a fool’s errand. It’s expensive. It’s strategically unwise, and technically just unachievable.”Against this backdrop, the other great expansion is, of course, in the commercial use of space, both current and projected. Much of the excitement of the SpaceX IPO was predicated on Musk’s stated intention of launching AI datacentres into space – even though no one has yet made one work, not least because cooling things in a vacuum is not at all straightforward. The main value of the company is currently in Starlink, which already provides internet inmore than 160 countriesand territories (though not yet in South Africa, which Musk has claimed is“because I’m not black”; South Africa replied he was welcome, provided it could see “compliance with local laws”, which require 30% ownership by local historically disadvantaged communities.Musk’s company has 9,532 satellites in orbit (there are about 17,600 active satellites in total); if permission is granted for the new constellation he wants to build, this will vastly increase. Then there is his stated aim of a colony on Mars.View image in fullscreenA SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket lifts off in California on 13 July.Photograph: Erick Madrid/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockSo one person, with questionable views, is aiming not only to rewrite the night sky but in the process gain unfettered control of our heavens? Grego initially responds quite carefully. “You know, there’s a legal discussion about at what point does that become appropriation, if you’ve put so many satellites in a space? Have you essentially claimed it? I don’t know where that line is.”What she does know is, “I don’t think we’re talking enough about it in the right places. We’re racing ahead with technical capabilities beyond our legal and normative structures and where the conversation is on Earth” – which clearly has more than a few other things to contend with. A recent opinion piecein this papernoted that even the Artemis mission, with all its wonder and its joy, was in essence a civilisational move, in which the vast majority of people had been relegated to the status of audience, as opposed to active participants in deciding how we would relate to a universe we all share – or even whether we should be leaving Earth in the first place.Rather like the race for generative AI, or in fact mutually assured destruction, the companies themselves have a momentum that creates facts before anyone has properly thought about what those facts will entail. “Commercial companies are bumping up against what they see are the restrictions on them. Like, ‘If I can’t appropriate, it’s not clear what I can do. But do we all interpret this the same way?’ And that does engender a bit of a race to the moon. You don’t want to be second. You’re not clear that you’re ready to be first, but nobody wants to be second, because there are some places on the moon which are particularly useful, that have near-perpetual sunlight and access to water, like near the south pole. You’re not allowed to claim it for your country but if you’re there building infrastructure” – which is effectively claiming it – “how do we deal with that? And if you have many powerful nuclear-armed countries trying to jostle for the same things in a distant place, that’s another place where you might spark a crisis.”We only have to look at every environment on Earth, she adds, to “see that the extractive colonialist framework has led to an existential crisis for human beings. And we have a chance to do this better as we move into space. And so we shouldn’t cede that power to the first most powerful company that’s able to do it.” She points to a piece of small print in theStarlink user agreements, about jurisdictions, and governing law. For any disputes about services “provided to, on, or in orbit around the planet Earth or the moon”, Texas state law applies, it says, while “for services provided on Mars, or in transit to Mars … no Earth-based government has authority or sovereignty over Martian activities. Accordingly, Disputes will be settled through self-governing principles, established in good faith, at the time of Martian settlement.”It’s not as though Martian settlement is imminent, but the intent is effectively there in print, to refuse all other jurisdiction but of those who get there first. “I obviously don’t know him [Musk]. I can only see what he says and what he does. But I don’t share a view of humanity that he does. I probably shouldn’t go any further. But I don’t think it’s inclusive. I don’t think it values human life in the same way that I do. I don’t think it prioritises future generations in the way that I would, or human culture. What’s the best of us, not what’s the worst of us. I don’t know the mechanism to make a course correction right now but I do know that it won’t happen unless people demand it.”There are a couple of levers. Although the Outer Space Treaty did not imagine Musk – or any private actor being able to function so widely in space – it does clearly say that the launch country is responsible for any subsequent activity “in outer space, including the moon and other celestial bodies”. So the “United States government could say yes or no, or: ‘To do this, you need to show me a plan for why this is in our interest, not justyourinterest.’”There is a United Nations of Outer Space Affairs – but little in the way of mechanisms of enforcement. Any brakes have so far in effect been left to bureaucratic regulatory bodies, Grego says – though from a military point of view it is hopeful that just this week there has been a meeting in Geneva of the UN’s Open-Ended Working Group on Preventing an Arms Race in Outer Space.One reason Grego really enjoyed talking at the philosophy and music festival in Wales, How the Light Gets In, was the opportunity to think about things on a bigger horizon than her usual diet of research and policy papers. In an ideal world, she argues, we would completely reframe the narrative. Currently the dominant story is “really problematic, which is: space is the final frontier. The US military likes to call it the space domain, and that it needs to be dominated.” It is all about the singular (male) hero, long pointy rockets, speed, to boldly go.But there are other ways. Not all scientists, but most, she says, “whether they’re looking at very, very small scales or very, very large scales, pretty quickly come to the realisation that human beings are extraordinarily interesting and rare. How do we safeguard that? And how do we steward that? If we think that humanity is something worth saving, then we actually have a lot we need to do to make sure that happens.”That might include considering whether we should just step back altogether. “I happen to think Earth is a really nice place. It’s beautiful. I love the beaches, the mountains. Mars has none of that. Mars is harsh. The moon is harsh. They’re very difficult places to live. It will take a long time to make them habitable or pleasant. And Earth has so much going for it.“So that’s really the horizon that I spend my time on. My kids and my grandkids. And I hope that there’s a world, a beautiful world for them, you know?”Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsSpaceThe ideas interviewSpaceXSatellitesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Who are India’s Cockroach Janta party and why are they staging protests? | India | The Guardian
description: Explosion of anger over education system and youth unemployment has led to movement’s rapid rise
Author: Hannah Ellis-Petersen
Headline: Explosion of anger over education system and youth unemployment has led to movement’s rapid rise
Content: Supporters of the CJP attempted to march toward parliament in New Delhi, India, on Monday.Photograph: Vijay Pandey/EPAView image in fullscreenSupporters of the CJP attempted to march toward parliament in New Delhi, India, on Monday.Photograph: Vijay Pandey/EPAIndiaExplainerWho are India’s Cockroach Janta party and why are they staging protests?Explosion of anger over education system and youth unemployment has led to movement’s rapid riseTens of thousands of Cockroach party protesters defy police with Delhi marchHannah Ellis-Petersenin DelhiMon 20 Jul 2026 10.28 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHow did the Cockroach Janta party emerge?The Cockroach Janta party (CJP) might have started out as an online joke, but – driven by millions of discontented young people in India – it has swiftly grown into one of the most unexpected challenges to the powerful rightwing government ofNarendra Modi.The CJP was founded in May by Abhijeet Dipke, 30, an Indian national who recently graduated from university in the US. Enraged to hear comments made by the chief justice of India, who compared the country’s unemployed youth to “parasites” and “cockroaches” during a supreme court hearing, Dipke jokingly put out a call on social media:“What if all cockroaches came together?”View image in fullscreenSupporters of the Cockroach Janta party (CJP) clash with police during Monday’s protests.Photograph: Arun Sankar/AFP/Getty ImagesThe response was overwhelming. Dipke built a website and social media accounts for a satirical Cockroach Janta party – a poke at Modi’s ruling Bharatiya Janata party (BJP) – complete with a biting manifesto taking aim at the government and a tagline: “A political party for the people the system forgot to count.”Within two weeks, the CJP’s Instagram page had more than 22 million followers, overtaking the BJP. The Modi government, highly intolerant of criticism, attempted to block its social media accounts on national security grounds but was not successful.What are the causes championed by the CJP?As the CJP began to mobilise support, Dipke focused the movement on a burning issue for many of India’s millions of students: leaks of medical entrance exams, which have repeatedly been sold to the highest bidder.‘This is the most stressed city in India’: the dark side of coaching capital KotaRead moreThe pressure on students to succeed is cripplingly high: more than 2 million students compete for just 130,000 places. Many families spend their life savings and get into high levels of debt to pay for their children to be coached for these notoriously gruelling medical exams, known by the acronym NEET. More is now spent in India on private tuition than the government’s entire higher education budget.A string of exam leaks has forced millions of students into re-takes and been linked to dozens of suicides in recent years. The NEET exams are also not the only papers to have been leaked.View image in fullscreenStudents and other CJP members are demanding the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan.Photograph: Jagadeesh Nv/EPAThe Modi government has been accused of not taking responsibility for the exams fiasco. The CJP began to rally supporters around the issue; calling for the government to be held accountable for exam leaks and for the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, to resign.For many of India’s gen Zs and millennials, the CJP has also given voice to wider frustrations over an education system in crisis and a job market that has been systematically failing them. According to one recent study, almost 40% of India’s graduates below the age of 25 are unemployed.When did the CJP first take to the streets?The CJP’s first offline protest took place in Delhi in early June, calling for the resignation of Pradhan. Dipke returned from the US to lead the demonstration, first in Delhi and then at events across the country.‘I’m sorry but I’m unable to speak’: hero of India’s Cockroach party weakens on 19th day of hunger strikeRead moreLater that month, CJP supporters held their second major protest in Delhi, where this time they pledged not to move until Pradhan was gone. The protesters were joined by Sonam Wangchuk, an eminent engineer and education activist from the Indian state of Ladakh, who was last year arrested and detained under terror laws by the government for his activism.Wangchuk announced he would embark on a Gandhian hunger strike in solidarity with the students andwould not eat until the education minister stepped down.View image in fullscreenSonam Wangchuk, left, has been on hunger strike for several weeks.Photograph: Bhawika Chhabra/ReutersAs the protest and Wangchuk’s hunger strike went on into July, they were visited by multiple senior opposition figures. However, the Modi government did not engage with the CJP or Wangchuk and Pradhan dismissed them as the “B-team of terrorists”.How has Sonam Wangchuk mobilised support?As Wangchuk’s conditioned worsened more than two weeks into his hunger strike, the government faced mounting criticism. After 18 days, the Delhi court ordered for his health to be monitored on a daily basis.Early on Sunday morning, 20 days into his hunger strike, Delhi police sparked national outrage after they arrived and forcibly removed Wangchuk and took him to a government hospital against his will. The police said it was to protect his health – his wife condemned it as “illegal detention”.From his hospital bed, Wangchuk called on CJP supporters to march to parliament on Monday and demand Pradhan’s resignation.How large was Monday’s protest?The CJP protest on Monday swelled into one of the largest anti-government protests in years. By early morning, thousands of protesters began to flock to the streets of central Delhi, joining hundreds who had already been camped out overnight.View image in fullscreenThe CJP march is thought to have attracted more than 20,000 protesters.Photograph: Rajat Gupta/EPAThe crowd was largely made up of students and other young people, but parents whose children had been affected by the leaks were also present in high numbers.Delhi police refused permission for the protesters to march on parliament. Roads across the city were blockaded with barriers, the internet was shut down and protesters were kettled into a designated protest site about a mile away from parliament. However, protesters still continued to arrive in droves, with more than 20,000 people gathered by the early afternoon.As the crowds tried to push towards parliament, police responded with violence, including baton charges and teargas, prompting criticism from CJP leaders and opposition politicians. Protesters and police were injured in the clashes. Dipke condemned the police response as “absolutely brutal”.What happens next?After weeks of ignoring the CJP movement, the Modi government offered a meeting on Monday morning. At a brief 10-minute interaction, three CJP demands were put forward but so far “no commitments were made”, according to the CJP’s spokesperson.There was no public response to the protest by Pradhan. By late on Monday, even as the police tried to clear the Delhi protest camp, Dipke emphasised that as long as Pradhan remained in his position, they would not be moved. “This peaceful protest is not ending!” read the party post on X.Explore more on these topicsIndiaSouth and central AsiaNarendra ModiexplainersShareReuse this content

title: How Elephant and Castle risks becoming London’s gentrification ‘patient zero’ | Gentrification | The Guardian
description: New £500m town centre welcomes its first tenant this month, but displaced traders say promises that they could return have been broken
Author: Phillip Inman
Headline: New £500m town centre welcomes its first tenant this month, but displaced traders say promises that they could return have been broken
Content: New flats and retail units at the Elephant Park development.Photograph: Linda Nylind/The GuardianView image in fullscreenNew flats and retail units at the Elephant Park development.Photograph: Linda Nylind/The GuardianGentrificationHow Elephant and Castle risks becoming London’s gentrification ‘patient zero’New £500m town centre welcomes its first tenant this month, but displaced traders say promises that they could return have been brokenPhillip InmanandSarah ButlerMon 20 Jul 2026 05.58 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDiana Sach once employed more than 30 people and catered for hundreds more every day from her Latin American restaurant and delicatessen, famous for its Peruvian empanadas, in south London’s Elephant and Castle district.Now, she looks longingly at the shiny tall buildings that have replaced the brightly coloured but shabby shopping centre that was her home from home before beingdemolished in 2021.“I hoped to be part of it. But there has been delay after delay and now I can’t see how it will happen for me,” says Sach.The Elephant, which will this month welcome its first commercial tenant – the matcha-serving Blank Street Coffee – and where residents are due to move in later this summer, is part of a £500m redevelopment of the south London landmark once renowned for its urban deprivation.A modern town square surrounded by tower blocks has replaced the old shopping mall – a brutalist fortress surrounded by spooky underground walkways and tunnels, which was one of Europe’s first large indoor shopping centres when it opened in 1965.It is the latest step in the multi-billion pound reinvention of the area, which has seen the neighbouringHeygate housing estatedemolished and rebuilt as modern towers, with shops and eateries on the ground floor, surrounding a leafy park.View image in fullscreenDiana Sach, one of many Elephant and Castle small business owners who no longer sees a place for herself in the new development.Photograph: Linda Nylind/The GuardianThe opening of the Elephant, which includes about 40 shops, restaurants and bars as well as a cinema and gym, a new home for the London College of Communication, 55,000 sq ft of officesand hundreds of flats – all watched over by the former centre’s distinctive statue of a pachy­derm carrying a tower on its back – marks a new start for the area after years of construction.The regeneration plan has been the subject of wrangling between local people, the council and developers since the 1990s, with residents nervous that their needs would be steamrollered along with the mall in favour of gentrification.It is part of an ongoing battle across London, and other UK cities, between communities and redevelopers.Oli Mould, professor of social, cultural and historical geography at Royal Holloway, University of London, says the redevelopment of Elephant and Castle, which began with the razing of Heygate, is almost “‘patient zero’ of London’s gentrification.”“A lot of these processes happen quite fiercely and quickly in London then spread across the country,” he says.Mould suggests the new development has created an “identikit homogeneous gentrified place”, which risks “destroying the very thing that makes a community thriving and beautiful in the first place”.View image in fullscreenThe statue of a pachy­derm with a tower on its back still graces the development.Photograph: RichardBaker/AlamySach, who came to the UK from Colombia 30 years ago,is not alone in feeling there is no place for her in the Elephant development.She says the developers, Get Living, and Southwark council have failed to honour promises that her restaurant would find a new home.She and the 90 displaced Latin American businesses that brought colour to Elephant and Castle were told there would be adequate temporary accommodation for them to maintain their businesses during construction. But only a handful found a new place to trade on the temporary site. For the restaurant and cafe owners, the shoe boxes on offer within a stack of metal containers, now set to be dismantled, proved unviable.Many closed down as the difficulty in finding an appropriate alternative site, compounded by the impact of Covid, took its toll.Once the development was completed, the businesses were told a more permanent, low-cost sites would be available in the new Elephant development or new streets nearby.However, fewer than half of the displaced small businesses are being offered new premises in the neighbourhood, according toa report by local charity Latin Elephant.Alongside Blank Street Coffee, a Marks & Spencer food store, a Pret a Manger, an Everyman cinema and DistriAndina, a Latin American supermarket currently based in the neighbouring railway arches, are among the first signed tenants.Only five of the 40 commercial sites are being set aside for the return of local businesses at affordable rents, as well as 10 small spaces within the railway arches.A food market hall, expected to be run by the Blend Family operator, will have space for 14 small food stalls, but none have been specifically set aside for former tenants of the defunct mall.It is a similar story above the shops and restaurants, where there are 485 homes to rent across three towers, including just five at social rent tenure, 45 on London living rent (LLR) terms and 124 at discounted market rent. A further phase of the development, yet to be built, will add 116 social rent, 12 LLR and 37 discounted market rent homes.View image in fullscreenThe brutalist Heygate housing estate was razed to make way for the new development, which includes just five homes at social rent tenure, 45 on London living rent terms and 124 at discounted market rent.Photograph: Alan Gallery/AlamyGet Living says it is “working closely with Southwark council to finalise the affordable retail strategy, with a clear commitment to ensuring that eligible independent traders from the former Elephant and Castle shopping centre are given first priority when applying for discounted market rent units within the Elephant”.It says the “ambition is to create a vibrant and diverse town centre, and a core part of making that happen is ensuring it continues to support the local and independent businesses that make Elephant and Castle what it is today”.On a tour of the site, Rick de Blaby, the outgoing boss of Get Living, acknowledges local people have experienced a “decade of destruction”, demolition and rebuilding.“In the end, the developers have got to show them it was all worthwhile. We have got to make it a place people engage with and come back to time and time again, where they can connect and feel safe,” he says.John Batteson, Southwark council’s cabinet member for climate emergency, jobs and transport, said he was committed to “keeping the cultural diversity that makes Southwark such a special place”.He said it was a “cast-iron condition of planning permission” that the developer must provide affordable retail space for local businesses and traders.However, today it feels as if the independent shops, cafes and restaurants that inhabited the area, bar a few exceptions, have been priced out, making way for a more corporate, orderly vision, similar to many other developments across Britain.Even those independent businesses that have found appropriate space in the wider Elephant and Castle redevelopment say they are struggling to hang on.View image in fullscreenModern tower blocks with shops and eateries on the ground floor now surround a leafy park, but only five units have been set aside for the return of local businesses at affordable rents.Photograph: Linda Nylind/The GuardianDario Lopez, an accountant representing the few Latin American businesses that managed to secure premises nearby, says most now fear they will need to leave, despite having taken out large loans to fit out their shops, cafes and restaurants.Miko, a Venezuelan restaurant, started life as a kiosk in the old shopping centre before taking up residence at the base of a new tower block.Lopez says Miko’s owner, Lenin Erazo, was offered a lease with zero rent in the first year, rising to £3,000 a month at the end of a five-year period.Now, he is in dispute with a new landlord, who wants to double the rent to more than £6,000 a month.“The area is still a building site, and in the first five years the developers have failed to invest in anything that will bring people from outside the area to visit. There are homes, but nothing to increase the footfall for local shops,” he says.Mould says local authorities and developers looking to revitalise town centres should be wary of what they could lose as well as what can be gained through redevelopments.“You can address problems without having to displace people with multi-billion pound programmes. If you can make a developer out of the community, that’s better,” he suggests, pointing to efforts by traders in south London’s Brixton tobuy their colourful local market.“It might not make as much money short term, but longer term it keeps cultural vibrancy in the area, which makes it attractive and makes people want to come.”Explore more on these topicsGentrificationLondonHospitality industryRetail industryHousingRegenerationCommunitiesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘We didn’t want him to be punchable!’: Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck on Ferris Bueller’s Day Off | Ferris Bueller's Day Off | The Guardian
description: ‘Alan and I have neat hair because we’d both just been in a military play. If we’d had crazy 1980s mullets, it wouldn’t have become a timeless classic’
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: ‘Alan and I have neat hair because we’d both just been in a military play. If we’d had crazy 1980s mullets, it wouldn’t have become a timeless classic’
Content: ‘S-wing batter’ … Alan Ruck and Matthew Broderick in Ferris Bueller’s Day Off.Photograph: Cinetext Bildarchiv/Paramount/AllstarView image in fullscreen‘S-wing batter’ … Alan Ruck and Matthew Broderick in Ferris Bueller’s Day Off.Photograph: Cinetext Bildarchiv/Paramount/AllstarHow we madeFerris Bueller's Day Off‘We didn’t want him to be punchable!’: Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck on Ferris Bueller’s Day Off‘Alan and I have neat hair because we’d both just been in a military play. If we’d had crazy 1980s mullets, it wouldn’t have become a timeless classic’Interviews by Simon BlandMon 20 Jul 2026 08.58 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 22.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMatthew Broderick, played Ferris BuellerI’d done WarGames and Ladyhawke and was getting sent a lot of “young guy” parts. I was a little concerned at first because Ferris talks to the audience – I’d done that in two plays and didn’t want to it to seem like that’s all I could do. Meanwhile, my agent said: “You’ve been asked to do aJohn Hughesmovie. You’re doing it.” I didn’t know much about John at the time but I kept hearing him being referred to as the “Spielberg of teen films”.I thought Ferris shouldn’t seem to be trying too hard, that things came easy to him. I wanted to be a little cool and aloof. We didn’t want him to be somebody you wanted to punch in the face. That’s always the danger with guys who get away with everything.After a test screening, one young woman’s feedback was that she didn’t like Ferris because “he’d make me do things I don’t want to do” so they got rid of a scene where I try to get Sloane [Mia Sara] not to go back home and keep hanging out having fun.Alan [Ruck, played Cameron] and I have timeless hair-dos because we’d both just been in a military play, Biloxi Blues. Given the date the film was made, had we been able to, we might’ve both had crazy mullets, in which case, it would not have become a timeless classic.John could be a very exacting director. Often, I’d be doing his ideas as carefully as I could but the scenes where I’m alone in the house talking to the camera? We’d just make stuff up. I picked up the clarinet and started playing just because it was there and I pretended I was playing great. The plan was to replace the sound and make it really good but John saw the footage and said: “I’m not doing that.”Marilyn Vance [costume designer] and John came up with my outfit. There’s details you don’t even see. My socks had flags of all the nations on them. The rabbit’s foot in my pocket was just there. John thought Ferris’s leather coat should be simple, like James Dean’s red jacket but we went very much the other way. The beret was my idea.double quotation markThey had a radio contest inviting people to be in a John Hughes movie and 10,000 people showed up. The window washers dancing? The camera guys just noticed themThe parade scene was huge. We shot at an actual parade one weekend, then did closeups the next. They had a radio contest inviting people to be in a John Hughes movie and 10,000 people showed up. It felt real. The window washers dancing? The camera guys just noticed them. They were catching whatever they could.I spent weeks with choreographer Kenny Ortega but blew my knee out doing thoseback yard running scenes. It made the dancing very spontaneous. John would be far away with a megaphone shouting: “Make something up!” I was scared but once everybody’s cheering you on, I felt like I was the king of the world.When John was alive, I always thought, “Maybe we’ll do it again?” but too much time passed. What happened to Ferris? He’s pretty charming and creative but he probably turned into his dad. That’s what most of us do.Alan Ruck, played Cameron FryeJohn Hughes originally thought of Anthony Michael Hall or Emilio Estevez for Ferris but they wanted to move on to different things so he wisely cast Matthew. We were doing this play together, Biloxi Blues. In one scene, he’s writing in his journal and we’re supposed to be asleep. One night, he nudges me. I open my eyes and he shows me his journal. It said: “They offered me Ferris Bueller.”The casting directors didn’t want to see me because I was 28 and thought I was too old. My agent said: “He plays opposite Broderick every night. They look the same age.” I auditioned and they thought, “This’ll work,” but they didn’t offer it to me right away. Matthew’s manager kept laying the idea in John’s ear, saying: “Matthew would like to work with Alan.” That went a long way to getting me the part. Matthew and I were already friends. We didn’t have to make that up.I always felt on the outside of life. Besides having Ferris for his best friend, Cameron’s definitely on the outside too, I figured because of his repressive home life. He wasn’t free and easy. That was also easy to tap into.View image in fullscreen‘When we sent the car flying out the window, the whole crew erupted into an enormous cheer’ … Matthew Broderick, Mia Sara and Alan Ruck.Photograph: Everett Collection Inc/AlamyMatthew has a photographic memory for comedy routines and movie scenes. We were big Richard Pryor fans and he knew every routine. He’d do monologues ofTony Montana from Scarfaceand crack me up. Biloxi Blues director Gene Saks would get mad at us because we were always screwing around. Matthew used to imitate him backstage, going: “I don’t believe you’re soldiers in world war two!” When John told me to come up with an authoritarian voice for Sloan’s dad, that was me imitating Matthew, imitating Gene. I did it just to see if I could make Matthew laugh – and it did.I honestly can’t remember what was going on in my head during the gallery scene. I had a number of personal losses and tragedies in my life, as we all do and I tapped into some of that.We had three cars. One had heavy-duty suspension for stunts. The one we drove was plagued with electrical problems. When we collect it after the parade, we’d do the scene and it wouldn’t start. We did 16 takes. When we sent the third one flying out the window, the whole crew erupted into an enormous cheer.There was a period where I didn’t want anything to do with the film. I was having trouble getting work and everybody kept talking about Ferris Bueller. I thought that’s as good as it’s going to get for me. As soon as I started working again, I really came to appreciate it. My joke is that Cameron’s father came home and threw him out the window. The rest of that day wasn’t a happy one, but maybe things turned out OK. Maybe now he’s a lawyer in Chicago.Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck will reunite in the upcoming movieThe Best Is Yet to ComeExplore more on these topicsFerris Bueller's Day OffHow we madeMatthew BroderickJohn HughesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham vows to ‘bring back hope’ as he unveils his cabinet | Andy Burnham | The Guardian
description: New prime minister says government must ‘do better’ to deliver change but promises prudent approach to economy
Author: Pippa Crerar
Headline: New prime minister says government must ‘do better’ to deliver change but promises prudent approach to economy
Content: Andy Burnham is the UK’s sixth prime minister in a decade.Photograph: Xinhua/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham is the UK’s sixth prime minister in a decade.Photograph: Xinhua/ShutterstockAndy BurnhamAndy Burnham vows to ‘bring back hope’ as he unveils his cabinetNew prime minister says government must ‘do better’ to deliver change but promises prudent approach to economyPippa CrerarPolitical editorMon 20 Jul 2026 19.32 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 15.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham has said his arrival in power will “bring back hope” to Britain as he launched his premiership with the surprise appointment of John Healey as chancellor and a promise to repair the long-term damage caused to communities during the Thatcher era.The new UK prime minister acknowledged people were “fed up” with politics as he addressed the country after taking over fromKeir Starmerin Downing Street, saying the government needed to “do better” to deliver real change.Six highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speechRead moreIn his speech outside No 10, he said the government would announce “some breathing space” on Tuesday for people regarding the cost of living, with plans to cut energy bills and bus fares expected as part of a fully funded package.Burnham later told reporters that he would look at “any flexibility” within the government’s existing fiscal rules to help borrow billions more to invest in infrastructure – but would take a “very prudent” approach to the economy.UK 10-year gilt prices dropped after his comments, with the markets already on the edge over the risks of higher borrowing and increased debt. But they had closed by the time that Healey, 66, regarded as a safe pair of hands, was announced.The former defence secretary, whose resignation pushed Starmer to the brink, emerged as the compromise candidate after Ed Miliband andShabana Mahmoodwere tipped for the role. Miliband takes over as foreign secretary while Mahmood remains at the Home Office.View image in fullscreenJohn Healey was a surprise choice as chancellor, but is considered a safe pair of hands.Photograph: Victoria Jones/ShutterstockIn his first world leader call, Burnham spoke to Donald Trump – who had previously suggested he was just mayor of “some town” – inviting him to Manchester for next year’s G20 summit.Trump later wrote on social media that the two leaders “had a very good conversation”, discussing Iran, the US-UK military alliance and trade. Trump said he and Burnham plan to meet “in the not too distant future”.Burnham also called Ukraine’s Volodymyr Zelenskyy and European Union chief Ursula von der Leyen.Burnham’s arrival in Downing Street as the UK’s sixth prime minister in a decade, afterLabourMPs brutally toppled Starmer from office, gives his party an opportunity for a second hearing with the public, but could also unleash yet more instability.Speaking without notes, and watched by his wife, Marie-France van Heel, he said: “I am acutely conscious that I am the sixth person in the last 10 years to walk up this street, the seventh prime minister since 2016, making this a moment for reflection and new resolution.“It requires my generation of politicians to raise our game and rise to the new challenge. Britain needs to show the world that we can regain our stability once again, and that is our challenge to make politics work, to make it work better.“I know people at home are fed up with politics. I hear you, and I want to be honest with you. We have not been good enough, and we need to be better. We will be.”View image in fullscreenEd Miliband takes over as foreign secretary while Shabana Mahmood remains at the Home Office.Photograph: Temilade Adelaja/ReutersHe told the assembled crowd: “Let’s build a new national sense of unity, of common purpose, and positivity. Let us make this the moment when Britain starts to believe again, the moment we bring back hope.”After sacking a tranche of Starmer loyalists, including chancellor Rachel Reeves – who was understood to have turned down another job to return to the backbenches – and deputy prime minister David Lammy, Burnham spent Monday evening making his cabinet appointments.In another senior appointment, Louise Haigh, Burnham’s right-hand woman, was made first secretary of state and chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster, running a beefed-up Cabinet Office that will be the “political engine room” driving his agenda.Yvette Cooper will move from the Foreign Office to the Department of Health, where she will oversee Burnham’s plans to transform the broken social care system, while Pat McFadden stays at the Department for Work and Pensions.Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister whoresigned after a row over stamp duty, was reappointed to her previous role as housing secretary.View image in fullscreenLouise Haigh, Burnham’s right-hand woman, was made first secretary of state and chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster.Photograph: Ryan Jenkinson/Getty ImagesBurnham, who took over from Starmer as the leader of the Labour partyon Friday, has put transforming the UK’s economic system at the heart of his plans, despite limited room for manoeuvre before the next election given his commitment to the fiscal rules.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“We will make this momenta circuit breaker for Britain, bringing forward the biggest changes in the last 40 years. A new political model and a new economic model,” he told the crowd gathered in the Downing Street sunshine.“In the 1980s, Britain took some wrong turns. Political power was centralised, economic power privatised, large parts of the country deindustrialised and they still haven’t recovered.“Many feel as though they’re still in decline, and they don’t have the ability to turn things around. And that’s why we will change politics to make it more collaborative, more about problem-solving than point-scoring. And we will take power out of here and carry it into every postcode in the land so that they can do more.”In appointing Healey, a former Treasury minister under Gordon Brown, Burnham has put an experienced figure in charge of delivering on his vision for the UK economy,combining fiscal responsibility with a focus on reindustrialisation, devolution, the cost of living and growth.Briefing wars: how Labour ministers tried, and failed, to get into Burnham’s cabinetRead moreThe new chancellor will now also be responsible for finding the money to raise military spending to 3% by 2030, and is expected to look closely at defence bonds, which he had argued for when at the Ministry of Defence but were rejected by the Treasury as they meant more borrowing.After his speech, Burnham told reporters he was planning to announce a fully funded cost of living support package including support for energy bills and bus fares this year, which could eventually include more radical plans such as freezing private sector rents.“We just want to do some things that people will feel, and feel quite quickly. It won’t solve everything, it won’t take all of the pressure off, but it just shows the direction of travel and that we’re serious about helping them out,” he said.He also made comments about borrowing more, within the fiscal rules, which he had mooted on the Makerfield campaign trail, but which nevertheless unsettled the markets when made from No 10.“I’ve said we’ll stick to the fiscal rules and by that I mean the existing fiscal rules and use, obviously, any flexibility within them,” he said. “So none of this is about taking risks with the economy. I’ve never done that in any role that I’ve had.
