I’ve always taken a very prudent approach.”View image in fullscreenKeir Starmer told the assembled crowd that his work was ‘done’ as he left Downing Street.Photograph: Seiya Tanase/NurPhoto/ShutterstockIn his speech, Burnhamannounced his first instruction in power would be to end rough sleeping, after a visit to homeless people in central London on Monday. He made a similar pledge when he became Greater Manchester mayor but oversaw four years of rising street homelessness.He also suggested he could cut income tax for lower earners in a future budget, potentially raising the tax-free personal allowance, which has been frozen for the last five years. But he refused to rule out bringing back the 50p top rate of tax to pay for it.Speaking before leaving No 10 for the final time, Starmer told the assembled crowd that “my work is done” of turning round the Labour party, but gave Burnham a final warning about foreign affairs. “We face a world now where our enemies will stop at nothing to divide us,” he said.The outgoing prime minister, who left handwritten notes for all Downing Street staff, added: “I go with good grace, I go with a smile and I go proud of everything that we have achieved.”Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamLabourKeir StarmerJohn HealeyEconomic policyEd MilibandShabana MahmoodnewsShareReuse this content

title: Burnham pledges to end rough sleeping in England in first speech as prime minister  | Homelessness | The Guardian
description: Additional £340m over three years allocated to get people off the streets ‘at the earliest opportunity’
Author: Kiran Stacey
Headline: Additional £340m over three years allocated to get people off the streets ‘at the earliest opportunity’
Content: Andy Burnham’s announcement aims to replicate the schemes he rolled out during his time as mayor of Greater Manchester.Photograph: Fred Duval/Sopa Images/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham’s announcement aims to replicate the schemes he rolled out during his time as mayor of Greater Manchester.Photograph: Fred Duval/Sopa Images/ShutterstockHomelessnessBurnham pledges to end rough sleeping in England in first speech as prime ministerAdditional £340m over three years allocated to get people off the streets ‘at the earliest opportunity’Kiran StaceyPolicy editorMon 20 Jul 2026 11.55 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham has promised to end rough sleeping in England in his first pledge as prime minister, allocating an additional £340m over three years as he seeks to replicate the homelessness schemes he rolled out inGreater Manchester.Speaking in front of Downing Street immediately after becoming prime minister, Burnham said he would try to get people off the streets “at the earliest opportunity”.Six highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speechRead moreThe announcement was welcomed by homelessness campaigners, who say the pledge can be met with enough money and political will.The prime minister’s critics, however, greeted the promise with scepticism, pointing out that despite having set out a similar goal as Greater Manchester mayor, Burnham had overseenfour years of rising street homelessness.Burnham said on Monday: “On my first day as prime minister, I’m announcing this government’s commitment to finally end long-term rough sleeping across the UK at the earliest opportunity.“We’ll get to work immediately, bringing together national and local government with the investment needed to get it done.”Officials said the government would now put an extra £340m into its homelessness strategy – equivalent to £113m a year – which would come from unused parts of the housing department’s budget.Graph showing rough sleeping by regional mayoraltyThey said the money would pay for 1,200 new homes and help support 3,000 people over five years.The money comes on top of £3.5bn, the previous government’s allocation for itshomelessness strategy, which ministers said at the time was designed to halve the rates of long-term rough sleeping.Steve Reed, the housing secretary who oversaw that strategy,was sackedfrom his post on Monday as Burnham began the process of putting his cabinet together.Burnham’s promise, which the former Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn had planned to announce if elected prime minister, aims to emulate the success of the“Everyone In” policywhich the government briefly enacted during the Covid pandemic.It also echoes a pledge he made when he was running for the Greater Manchester mayoralty in 2017, when he promised to eliminate rough sleeping in Manchester within three years if elected.8:31Andy Burnham becomes Britain’s new prime minister - The LatestAfter he won that election, he had some early success with his homelessness policy, both with his rough sleeping programme, known as A Bed Every Night, and his broader scheme to tackle long-term homelessness, calledHousingFirst.In his first five years as mayor, rough sleeping dropped by two-thirds, which supporters say showed the merits of corralling different parts of government around the same aim.Those in Manchester at the time say Burnham was able to use his position as mayor to force greater collaboration between different councils and government bodies to tackle the problem.For example, he made the 10 councils under his jurisdiction share data so that someone who was rough sleeping in Bolton, for example, could also be tracked if they came into central Manchester during the day.Andy Burnham’s difficult first cabinet – a visual guide to the options and dilemmasRead moreHe also made sure landlords did not evict tenants because of relatively minor misdemeanours, which experts say is a major cause of street homelessness.Shabnum Mustapha, a former official at the Greater Manchester mayoralty, said: “In the early days it was hard, trying to bring together a bunch of statutory agencies and organisations which have been working on this problem for decades and decades.“But by taking a big tent approach, working with different councils, other parties and organisations such as faith groups, he had a lot of success.”Critics, however, point out that in each of the past four years, rough sleeping has risen, so the level is now three-quarters of what it was in 2017.Supporters say rates were difficult to keep down after the national government ended its pandemic support schemes. His opponents, however, accuse Burnham of having taken his focus off one of his foundational policies.James Cleverly, the shadow housing secretary, said: “His record in Manchester showed initial success, then failure once his focus shifted. It looks like he’s taking money from local projects to fund this announcement, so lots of the country will be disappointed.”Explore more on these topicsHomelessnessHousingSocial exclusionAndy BurnhamGreater ManchesternewsShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham’s first Labour cabinet: who’s in and who’s out? | Labour | The Guardian
description: From the Treasury to the Home Office, we look at who will head up key departments in new PM’s government
Author: Rowena Mason
Headline: From the Treasury to the Home Office, we look at who will head up key departments in new PM’s government
Content: Shabana Mahmood, John Healey, Louise Haigh and Ed Miliband.Composite: PA/Getty image/Rex features/ReutersView image in fullscreenShabana Mahmood, John Healey, Louise Haigh and Ed Miliband.Composite: PA/Getty image/Rex features/ReutersLabourExplainerAndy Burnham’s first Labour cabinet: who’s in and who’s out?From the Treasury to the Home Office, we look at who is heading up key departments in new PM’s governmentBurnham vows his arrival in power will be ‘circuit breaker’ moment for UKSix highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speechRowena MasonandPeter WalkerMon 20 Jul 2026 19.00 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 14.27 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham has appointed his first cabinet, with the new prime minister rewarding those who have been loyal to his takeover efforts. He had promised to pick his top team from all wings of the party in an effort to show he is trying to run a more inclusive administration.But at the same time, his decision to have a brutal clearout of Starmer allies resulted in many considered to be on the right of the party or close to the former prime minister losing their jobs. These are the key announcements so far:Who is in?John HealeyChancellorView image in fullscreenJohn Healey.Photograph: UK Parliament/PAThe former defence secretary who quit the government over a lack of funding for the defence investment plan is the surprise appointment to the Treasury.Healey, 66, is a steady and experienced force, having been an MP since 1997, and serving as a Treasury minister under Tony Blair.The job had been expected to go to Ed Miliband and then it was mooted for Shabana Mahmood. But one advantage of Healey is his longstanding history on Labour frontbenches for every faction of the party. He was a minister in the Blair-Brown years and a loyal member of the shadow cabinets of Miliband, Jeremy Corbyn and Starmer.Healey grew up in Wakefield, studied political science at Cambridge, and was a journalist, campaigns director at the Trades Union Congress and a disability rights campaigner before entering parliament.Unshowy and not part of the usual Westminster dramas or scandals, it was a shock when he resigned his role under Starmer, in what would be one of the fatal blows for his premiership.In the job, he will be expected to push for a fuller funding settlement for the Ministry of Defence, which was denied to him under the Treasury of Rachel Reeves. His other major shared interest with Burnham is reindustrialisation.His X bio describes him as “Husband and Dad. Proud Yorkshireman. Labour MP for Rawmarsh and Conisbrough and former secretary of state for defence”. He can now add chancellor to that list.Shabana MahmoodThe home secretaryShabana Mahmood.Photograph: ITV/ShutterstockMahmood will keep her role in Burnham’s cabinet after being appointed home secretary by Keir Starmer last year and charged with driving throughcontroversial immigration policies.She is considered to be on the Blue Labour right of the party, which is socially conservative and tough on crime. She grew up in Small Heath in inner-city Birmingham in the 1980s and 90s, where her father kept a cricket bat behind the till of the family shop to fend off would-be robbers. After going to the University of Oxford to study law, she worked as a barrister.The 45-year-old is an MP in Birmingham and ran Labour’s election campaign in 2024, before a stint as justice secretary overseeing an early release programme to ease overcrowding. She is viewed as steely and decisive.A cabinet colleague told the Guardian earlier this year that she “never wastes a word” in meetings, and last year warned that the government was still caught up in an opposition mindset and preoccupied with taking on the left instead of speaking to the concerns of the country more widely. She has also been a leading advocate for the UK to takea more pro-Palestinian stance, including on statehood.Ed MilibandForeign secretaryEd Miliband.Photograph: Getty ImagesMiliband is a great survivor in the Labour party – almost as much so as Andy Burnham. A former cabinet minister under Gordon Brown, he led Labour to anelection defeat in 2015before serving in the shadow cabinet of Keir Starmer.He was a leading contender to be chancellor but there were fears his positioning on the soft left and as a firm defender of Britain’s net zero commitments would spook the financial markets. Having once made a speech pitching some companies as producers and others as predators, he is not a favourite politician of big business.Born in north London in a political family, his father was a Marxist academic. He studied PPE at Oxford and worked mostly in politics and research before joining his older brother, David, in the House of Commons as a Labour MP.They became the first brothers to both serve around the cabinet table since before the second world war, and their sibling relationship was put to the test when Ed beat David in the leadership contest to succeed Brown with the support of the trade union vote.As Labour leader, he led in the polls for many months against David Cameron and identified ahead of his time the public mood on the cost of living but was pilloried by some of the rightwing media as gaffe-prone and somewhat awkward.In Starmer’s cabinet he was viewed as one of the most successful cabinet ministers at driving through his priorities, includingGreat British Energy, and blocking more drilling in the North Sea – a policy expected to be partially overturned by Burnham.As foreign secretary, he will be expected to take international matters off Burnham’s hands as much as possible, take a more pro-Gaza stance and potentially set a path to restoring Britain’s aid commitments to 0.7% of national income. He replaces Yvette Cooper.Yvette CooperHealth secretaryView image in fullscreenYvette Cooper.Photograph: David Woolfall/UK Parliament/PAAmong the Starmer-era big beasts who had been tipped to survive, Cooper is shifted from the Foreign Office to the vital domestic post of secretary of state for health and social care, the suffix especially important given Burnham’s stated mission to unlock the clogged adult care system.Cooper does, of course, have recent experience of a tricky UK-focused post, starting in Starmer’s government as home secretary, and she held several posts under Tony Blair and Gordon Brown.While still in her 50s, Cooper is approaching Labour “grandee” status, having been an MP since 1997 and getting her first taste of ministerial life at the tail end of the 20th century. When Labour’s 2015 leadership campaign began she was initially viewed as a favourite, but finished third behind Corbyn and a certain Andy Burnham.The daughter of a trade union leader, Cooper is a near-political lifer, having begun her career with then-shadow chancellor John Smith, although she did spend a period as a journalist before entering parliament.For a decade of her time in parliament, Cooper was part of a political double act with her husband, Ed Balls, who lost his seat in 2015 and later became a broadcaster.Pat McFaddenWork and pensions secretaryView image in fullscreenPat McFadden.Photograph: Carl Court/Getty ImagesSome continuity had been expected with the new ministerial slate and it makes sense for this to happen with a department that could be central to Burnham’s political fortunes.McFadden, one of the more experienced operators in government, will be tasked with continuing to pilot through some sort of reform to social security, with the intention of limiting the costs without cuts or conditions of the sort that prompted a major backbench rebellion for Starmer.An MP since 2005, McFadden was soon propelled into the Blair and Brown frontbenches, and spent the buildup to the 2024 election as Starmer’s national campaigns coordinator, going on to take the Cabinet Office post at the centre of policymaking.A trusted voice on even the trickiest morning broadcast round, the child of two Irish speakers from County Donegal transplanted to Paisley, McFadden trod the traditional political path of researcher and policy adviser before winning his Wolverhampton South East seat.Louise HaighChancellor of the duchy of Lancaster, first secretary of state and Cabinet Office ministerLouise Haigh.Photograph: Getty ImagesHaigh became a linchpin of Burnham’s byelection operation, so it is no surprise she is now his chief organiser in government and effectively his deputy as first secretary of state. As the most senior Cabinet Office minister, she will coordinate work across the government and oversee any changes to Whitehall.It marks a return to cabinet for the 38-year-old Haigh, who was sacked byKeir Starmer as transport secretaryfor not having told him fully about a fraud conviction over a missing mobile phone when she was in her 20s.She grew up in Sheffield and studied politics and government at LSE, then Nottingham University, before working in parliament, for Unite and later at the insurer Aviva.It was during her time at that company where she got in hot water over the phone, having reported it stolen but failed to update the police when it was found. She pleaded guilty to fraud, but it did not stop her political career.Elected in 2015, she was one of 36 Labour MPs to nominate Jeremy Corbyn for the Labour leadership that year and was given a frontbench role whenhe made her a shadow Cabinet Office ministerat age 28.The MP for Sheffield Heeley rose through the junior ministerial ranks and was one of a handful of Corbyn’s frontbench to be appointed to Keir Starmer’s first cabinet when he took over. She became the shadow Northern Ireland secretary.Known for an engaging communication style, she retained her frontbench role even as Starmer moved his party to the right. As transport secretary, she oversaw the nationalisation of railways legislation until she was returned to the backbenches. She replaces Darren Jones.Wes StreetingDefence secretaryView image in fullscreenWes StreetingPhotograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockStreeting is one of the only appointments to cabinet who is considered to be towards the right of the party. He is also one of the reasons Burnham is prime minister, after attempting unsuccessfully to challenge Starmer, kicking off the process of removing him.Having lost out on the top job, the role of defence secretary is still a substantial one. However, Streeting’s power is likely to be limited by the fact his predecessor, John Healey, is in the Treasury and will decide what kind of settlement the department gets for its investment plan.A former health secretary, the ambitious Streeting is likely to be pleased at getting an even bigger job, especially at such a crucial moment in the Ukraine war and during a time of warnings that Britain needs to rearm against global threats.Miatta FahnbullehEnergy secretaryFahnbulleh is a rising star in the party who contributed heavily to Burnham’s policy ideas as he was preparing for government.She previously worked closely with Miliband so was a natural choice for the energy role and will have a crucial task in Burnham’s mission of cutting bills while sticking to commitments in net zero.Alex NorrisJustice secretaryThe relatively low profile Alex Norris was a Home Office minister and an unexpected choice for justice secretary. A Nottingham MP, he was a trade union organiser for Unison and councillor before he entered parliament in 2017.Jonathan ReynoldsBusiness secretaryView image in fullscreenJonathan Reynolds.Photograph: Thomas Krych/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockReynolds returns to his former role after being shuffled sideways to the job of chief whip against his wishes.A fellow northwest MP, Reynolds has long been a friend of Burnham and has been given a crucial role in a beefed-up department that will cover science as well as his old portfolio.Burnham has stated he wants to be pro-business, as well as fighting for reindustrialisation and potentially overseeing the return of some utilities to more public control.Angela RaynerHousing secretaryView image in fullscreenAngela Rayner.Photograph: UK Parliament/PARayner will be pleased to get back the job of housing secretary less than a year after she resigned over underpaid stamp duty on the purchase of a second property.She had been the subject of speculation that she would be health secretary but sources had suggested she would take some persuading when her main interest is devolution and housing.In this brief, she will be in charge of two of Burnham’s key priorities: spreading power to local government and embarking on a huge council house building programme.Lucy PowellEducation secretaryView image in fullscreenLucy Powell.Photograph: Christopher Thomond/The GuardianPowell is likely to be pleased with this substantive brief but Burnham has so far stopped short of making her deputy prime minister – despite her job as deputy leader of the party.Another Manchester area MP, Powell has long been an ally of Burnham and advocated for him to be allowed to run for parliament.She replaces Bridget Phillipson, her former rival in the race to be deputy leader, who is now minister for women and equalities in a stripped-back cabinet role.Anneliese MidgleyChief whipView image in fullscreenAnneliese Midgley.Photograph: AlamyMidgley, a former political director for Unite, had been instrumental in helping Burnham with his Makerfield campaign. She was thought to be a contender for party chair but instead Burnham is seeking to harness her talent for organising as chief whip.Who’s out?Rachel ReevesFormer chancellorReeves had always wanted the job of chancellor and was proud to be the UK’s first female holder of the office. She had worked hard to instil ironclad fiscal discipline on Labour, sticking broadly to her self-imposed fiscal rules and enforcing spending curbs across departments.However, some in Labour blamed early missteps from her Treasury for Starmer’s downfall, starting with the cut to the winter fuel payment, but also the rise in national insurance for employers that her critics in business claim stymied the jobs market and growth.Reeves can, however, claim a record of having started to turn the economy around. She made a valiant effort to stay in her job but ultimately was judged too close to the Starmer project to stay in the Treasury. She is believed to have turned down another senior position.A former child chess champion and Oxford economics graduate, Reeves had also faced criticisms from some newspapers for having exaggerated her credentials as an economist on her LinkedIn page, having allowed some plagiarism to creep into a book she wrote on female economists and having inadvertently failed to pay for a landlord’s licence.In her departing statement, she said: “It has been the privilege of my life to serve as the chancellor of the exchequer. The economy today is stronger, fairer and more resilient because of the choices we have taken as a Labour government over the past two years.“Stability restored, investment delivered and reform to our economy under way … I’m proud to say that they have. And to every young woman and girl let my time in office show there should be no ceilings on your ambitions, your hopes or your dreams.”David LammyFormer foreign secretaryLammy has been a longstanding frontbencher after serving as a minister in the last New Labour governments.A former lawyer, he served as foreign secretary for Keir Starmer and then won a promotion to deputy prime minister and justice secretary when Angela Rayner stood down. He has been at the former prime minister’s side for many of his toughest moments.Such is his experience and standing that there was some speculation he might stay on in a different ministerial role. In a post on X announcing his departure, Lammy made it plain he had been happy to stay on but was not wanted.Hewrote: “I would have been proud to continue serving in cabinet. But a new prime minister is entitled to build his own team, and I wish Andy Burnham and the new cabinet every success.”Darren JonesFormer chief secretary to the prime ministerFrom the centre of No 10 operations to nothing. As Jonesput itin a bluntly worded social media post: “Andy Burnham has called to tell me I won’t be a part of his new cabinet. I wish him and his team every success.”An MP since 2017, Jones’s fairly rapid rise to the top table began with well-reviewed performances as chair of the business and trade committee. It began with the tricky if powerful role of Reeves’s number two as chief secretary to the Treasury.There he was so central to spending negotiations, that when Starmer undertook one of his several organisational revamps, as part of the September 2025 reshuffle, Jones took on the new and somewhat artificial role of chief secretary to the prime minister, tasked with leading change from No 10.Vague hints that Jones might contest for the Labour leadership came to nothing, and while – as his tweet indicated – he would have been happy to do another role, this was also not on the cards.Steve ReedFormer housing secretaryReed was one of most loyal cabinet ministers to the Starmer administration, although he had made a play to keep his job in recent weeks by highlighting his achievements.An old ally of Morgan McSweeney, Starmer’s former chief of staff, Reed was a veteran of south London local government before entering parliament in 2012. He served as environment secretary before his current job and will now return to the backbenches.His letter to Burnham after his departure from government was somewhat spiky, warning him not to disadvantage London and also sending his hopes that those who were not loyal to Starmer will be loyal to the new prime minister.Peter KyleFormer business secretaryKyle took on the job of business secretary last year in the emergency reshuffle prompted by the departure of Angela Rayner over her§ tax affairs.It meant he found himself on flights to the US and China at a moment’s notice at a critical time for negotiations over trade and tech companies. He previously served as science secretary – a role that is now likely to be folded into the business department.Kyle entered parliament in 2015 and has been open about his struggles to get there. He had difficulties at school owing to dyslexia and left, in his own words, “without any usable” qualifications. He made it to university in his 20s after several failed attempts.An ally of Wes Streeting and Starmer, Kyle appears to be departing from cabinet largely to make way for closer supporters of Burnham although he has also been criticised for a perception that he has been too open towards tech companies.Richard HermerFormer attorney generalHermer was probably Starmer’s closest friend in politics. A fellow human rights lawyer, he was made a peer so that he could join the prime minister’s first cabinet.However, he quickly became a bogeyman for the rightwing press, which disliked his role in signing over the Chagos Islands to Mauritius and generally espousing views on the liberal left. His past legal work, including his representation ofGerry Adamsand advice toCaribbean countries seeking reparations, has been raked over by Tories who have demanded that he publicly recuse himself from some areas.The criticism was not confined to the right. Maurice Glasman, the Blue Labour founder and associate of the US vice-president, JD Vance,called Hermer“an arrogant, progressive fool who thinks that law is a replacement for politics”.Starmer steadfastly stuck by him though, and Hermer has been an invaluable source of counsel and friendship through his final months as premier. He was also a bastion of progressive thought in a cabinet that many in the Labour party felt had veered too far away from core party values.Liz KendallFormer science and technology secretaryAlso to be filed under: no surprise here. Kendall has always been on the right of Labour and ideologically much closer to Starmer than to the new PM.An ever-present figure on Starmer’s frontbench, the Leicester West MP started as work and pensions secretary, the job she had shadowed in opposition, before moving to the science brief in the wholesale cabinet reshuffle of September last year.An MP from 2010, Kendall supported David Miliband in the Labour leadership election of that year. Five years later, she stood herself, coming last of the four candidates when Jeremy Corbyn became leader.In a tweeted letter, Kendall thanked Starmer and other cabinet colleagues, saying she was ready to support Burnham “in any way I can”.Jo StevensFormer Wales secretaryAlso not expected to stay on, the Cardiff East MP has been a fixture in the Wales office, filling the brief in opposition and then government since 2021, having previously been Starmer’s shadow culture secretary.A backer of Starmer when he stood to be Labour leader, Stevens had served as shadow Wales secretary under Corbyn,but resignedin opposition to Corbyn’s decision to back formal moves for the UK to leave the EU.Hilary BennFormer Northern Ireland secretarySome had speculated that Benn could be among ministers to stay in their job and provide continuity, given that he is not overtly partisan and was largely seen as doing a quietly good job in a sensitive brief.But Benn tweeted that he has gone. It is a return to the backbenches for a Labour grandee, in parliament for more than 25 years, who held a series of ministerial jobs under Blair and Brown.Explore more on these topicsLabourAndy BurnhamEd MilibandLouise HaighShabana MahmoodfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Can Andy Burnham’s cabinet fix Britain? – podcast | Labour | The Guardian
description: Pippa Crerar and Kiran Stacey look at how the UK’s new prime minister will try to do things differently and who will be helping him in the cabinet

Content: Politics Weekly UKLabourCan Andy Burnham’s cabinet fix Britain? – podcastMore ways to listenApple podcastsSpotifyRSS FeedDownloadPresented byPippa CrerarandKiran Stacey, produced bySam GruetandNada Smiljanic, music byAxel Kacoutié, the executive producer isFrankie TobiMon 20 Jul 2026 16.56 EDTShare00:00:0000:00:00The new prime minister has promised a ‘circuit breaker’ moment for the country, as he becomes the UK’s seventh leader since 2016. title: Andy Burnham becomes Britain’s new prime minister - The Latest  | Andy Burnham | The Guardian

Content: 8:31Today in Focus: The LatestAndy BurnhamAndy Burnham becomes Britain’s new prime minister - The LatestAndy Burnham has become the sixth UK prime minister in a decade after a transfer of power from his predecessor Keir Starmer. title: Kevin Keegan, Liverpool legend and former Newcastle manager, dies aged 75  | Soccer | The Guardian
description: Kevin Keegan, the former England and Newcastle manager who was a member of Liverpool’s serial champions in the 1970s, has died aged 75
Author: Louise Taylor
Headline: Kevin Keegan, the former England and Newcastle manager who was a member of Liverpool’s serial champions in the 1970s, has died aged 75
Content: Kevin Keegan, seen here celebrating after scoring for England against Scotland in 1979, has died at the age of 75.Photograph: Eamonn McCabe/GuardianView image in fullscreenKevin Keegan, seen here celebrating after scoring for England against Scotland in 1979, has died at the age of 75.Photograph: Eamonn McCabe/GuardianSoccerKevin Keegan, Liverpool legend and former Newcastle manager, dies aged 75Keegan revealed in June 2026 he had stage four cancerStarred for Liverpool, Hamburg, Newcastle and EnglandLouise TaylorandAndy MartinMon 20 Jul 2026 10.28 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 16.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleKevin Keegan, the former England and Newcastle manager who was twice named European Footballer of the Year, has died aged 75. As a player, Keegan illuminated countless matches and in the 1970s proved a stalwart member of Liverpool’s serial domestic and European trophy winning team.Keegan’s family announced in January that he had cancer and, seven weeks ago, he himself revealed that it was stage four, the most advanced form of the disease. “A double Ballon d’Or winner, Kevin was a much loved husband, father and grandfather,” read a statement. “The family would like to thank Kevin’s incredible medical team for all their support. This is a hugely difficult time and they are requesting space and privacy.”A pioneer and a superstar, Kevin Keegan’s achievements were extraordinaryRead moreAlan Shearer, who played under Keegan with Newcastle andEngland, paid his own tribute on social media. “My hero. My manager. My friend,” wrote the former striker. “Sleep easy Boss.”The Colombian former Newcastle striker Faustino Asprilla wrote: “I am absolutely heartbroken. Kevin wasn’t just a football manager to me. Through his passion, belief and love for the game he inspired so many people, myself included. He taught me that football is about much more than winning.“It’s about passion, loyalty, hope and never giving up. Those lessons have stayed with me throughout my life and, for that, I will be forever grateful.”View image in fullscreenA Newcastle fan leaves flowers outside St James’ Park in tribute to Kevin Keegan.Photograph: Scott Heppell/ReutersNewcastle released a heartfelt statement describing Keegan as “one of the most iconic, influential and deeply loved figures in our club’s history”. As it explained: “Kevin was more than a legendary footballer and manager. He was the beating heart ofNewcastle Unitedfor generations of supporters.” Prince William said he was “incredibly saddened to hear of the passing of Kevin Keegan. A truly remarkable footballer and manager who inspired generations with his talent, passion and love of the game. Above all he will be remembered as a kind and generous man.”By late on Monday afternoon, floral tributes were piling up outside St James’ Park as Newcastle fans paid their respects to an enduring hero. Liverpool, meanwhile, said Keegan’s “indomitable spirit and remarkable contribution will be forever etched in our history and his legacy will live on”.Keegan started his career at Scunthorpe before being signed for Liverpool by Bill Shankly for £33,000 in 1971. It did not take long for the son of a South Yorkshire miner to become “King Kev”, with his invariably imperious attacking performances helping Shankly’s side win three First Division titles, the FA Cup, the Uefa Cup (twice) and the 1977 European Cup.View image in fullscreenLiverpool’s Ian Callaghan, Kevin Keegan and Ray Clemence parade the winning trophy after the 1974 FA Cup final against Newcastle at Wembley.Photograph: Liverpool FC/Getty ImagesBy his own admission not a natural talent, Keegan turned himself into one of the world’s best players. “Mighty Mouse” was relentlessly combative, never ducked a challenge, and remained refreshingly humble even as he became a firm Kop favourite. “I learned so much from Shanks,” Keegan said. “He used to say: ‘If I was a road sweeper my street would be the cleanest in the borough.’ I took that to heart.”Keegan left Liverpool in 1977 for Hamburg, where he became the only British player to twice win the Ballon d’Or. Upon returning from Germany in 1980 he enjoyed further playing stints with Southampton and Newcastle before retiring in 1984. By then he had also won 63 England caps, scoring 21 goals in the course of an international career that involved playing at the 1982 World Cup.Quick GuideThe Keegan years: from player to managerShow1951Kevin Keegan is born on 14 February in Armthorpe, near Doncaster.1968Begins playing career at Scunthorpe United.1971Signed by Bill Shankly for Liverpool at age of 20, initially as a midfielder.1972Makes England debut against Wales in November.1973Liverpool win the league; Keegan scores twice in Uefa Cup final win over Borussia Mönchengladbach.1974Scores twice as Liverpool beat Newcastle 3-0 in the FA Cup final. His first England goal comes against Wales.1976Liverpool win the league title and Keegan scores in each leg of  Uefa Cup final win against Club Brugge. His celebrity is such he appears on the TV show Superstars, and famously crashes his bike during one of the races.1977Another league title for Liverpool, who also win their first European Cup, a 3-1 victory over Mönchengladbach. Keegan moves to Hamburg for £500,000, nearly double the German transfer record.1978Wins Ballon d’Or.1979Wins Ballon d’Or again as Hamburg secure their first German ‌championship title in 19 years, and first in the Bundesliga era.1980Captains England at Euros but they are knocked out in the group stage. Leaves Hamburg to join Southampton for two seasons.1982Transfers to Newcastle in the Second Division. Plays final match for England, after 63 caps (31 as captain) and 21 goals.1984Helps Newcastle secure promotion in his second season, then retires from playing. Across his club career, he scores 204 goals in 592 ‌appearances.1992Takes first coaching role as manager of struggling Newcastle in February. Danger of relegation to the Third Division is averted.1993Leads them to the First Division title and promotion to the Premier League in his first full season in charge.1994Newcastle finish third in Premier League.1996Newcastle finish second in the Premier League, ​despite leading for most ​of campaign – by 12 points at one stage. Season includes famous “I would love it if we beat them” comments when discussing rivals Manchester United on Sky Sports.1997Leaves Newcastle and takes over at Fulham for two seasons.1999Takes charge ​of England national ‌team in February.2000Coaches England at Euro 2000 where they beat Germany but still go out in the group stage. Resigns in ​October after a 1–0 loss to Germany in World Cup qualifying.2001Takes over at Manchester City.2008Returns to Newcastle but lasts eight months after tensions with the board and owner Mike Ashley.Was this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.Keegan was never one to hold back. He was memorably sent off for scrapping with Leeds’ Billy Bremner in the 1974 Charity Shield and harboured a certain volatility allied to a pugnacious intolerance of football’s machinations, real or perceived. “I don’t like people to deceive me and tell me lies,” hetold the Guardian in 2018.Although a private man, Keegan also embraced fame. He was one of the English game’s first players to wear personalised branded football boots, recorded a hit single Head Over Heels in Love, and became an enduring star of the BBC’s Superstars. He was also the public face of 70s aftershave Brut 33 and became close friends with George Best. “I wanted so much to have his ability,” Keegan said. “His skill was unbelievable. I played against [Johan] Cruyff, [Franz] Beckenbauer, [Diego] Maradona but I still say Besty was the best.”Having hung up his boots at the age of 33, Keegan moved to Spain where his two daughters grew up, initially unaware he had been a footballer. “It was the best decision I made in my life,” hetold the Guardian in 2011. “I was at the highest level and the only way was down. I call it the glass mountain – you can’t climb a glass mountain.”View image in fullscreenKevin Keegan in 2008.Photograph: Michael Mayhew/SportsphotoIn 1992 he decided to venture into management at Newcastle, guiding them into the Premier League and to within touching distance of the 1995-96 title, only to miss out to Alex Ferguson’s Manchester United. His finger-jabbing“I will love it”rant from that season has become part of the English game’s lore.After leaving St James’ Park in January 1997 Keegan took charge of Fulham before becoming England manager in 1999. That 20-month, 18-game tenure would end in despair when, following a disappointing Euro 2000 campaign, he resigned after a defeat by Germany at Wembley. The Football Association has announced it will pay tribute to Keegan before England’s Nations League game with Spain at Wembley in September.A happier spell at Manchester City – who on Monday credited Keegan with establishing them as a Premier League club – followed before an emotional return to Newcastle in 2008. That swansongended within eight months, however, amid acute tensions with the club’s then owner Mike Ashley and his board. In 2009 a tribunal awarded Keegan £2m damages for constructive dismissal.Keegan, who was made an OBE in 1982 for services to football, made a fleeting return to public life in June. During an event on Tyneside he talked about his desire to return to St James’ Park to say “goodbye” and joked the club would need to “wait until I die” to erect a statue of him. He was optimistic at the time that gruelling treatment was keeping his cancer at bay.“Kevin’s impact will not be measured by appearances or results,” read Newcastle’s statement. “It will live on in the memories he created with club and country, the dreams he inspired wherever he went and the generations of supporters around the world who fell in love with football because of him.”Explore more on these topicsFootballLiverpoolNewcastle UnitedEnglandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Man, 28, charged with murder of former MP Ann Widdecombe | UK news | The Guardian
description: Joshua Kerry from Rotherham charged over killing of politician whose body was found at her Devon home
Author: Jamie Grierson
Headline: Joshua Kerry from Rotherham charged over killing of politician whose body was found at her Devon home
Content: Joshua Kerry has been charged with the murder of the former MP.Photograph: The SunView image in fullscreenJoshua Kerry has been charged with the murder of the former MP.Photograph: The SunUK newsMan, 28, charged with murder of former MP Ann WiddecombeJoshua Kerry from Rotherham charged over killing of politician whose body was found at her Devon homeJamie GriersonMon 20 Jul 2026 14.10 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA 28-year-old man from Rotherham has been charged with the murder of the former MP and Reform UK party spokesperson Ann Widdecombe.Joshua Kerry was charged with Widdecombe’s murder and will appear at Westminster magistrates court on Tuesday, the Crown Prosecution Service said.Widdecombe’s body was found with “serious injuries” by the ambulance service at her home in Haytor Vale, Devon, at 11.40am on Thursday 9 July.Devon and Cornwall police subsequently said they believed the 78-year-old was attacked the day before at 12.30pm.Frank Ferguson, the chief crown prosecutor of the CPS special crime and counter-terrorism division, said: “We have decided to prosecute Joshua Kerry for murder following a police investigation into the death of Ann Widdecombe in Haytor, Dartmoor.“Police inquiries into the defendant’s motives are ongoing, including a potential political or terrorist connection.“Our prosecutors have worked closely with the police to establish that there is sufficient evidence to bring this case to court, and we will continue working with them as their investigation progresses.“We remind all concerned that proceedings against the defendant are active and he has the right to a fair trial.“It is vital that there should be no reporting, commentary or sharing of information which could in any way prejudice these proceedings.”Last Monday, counter-terrorism policing south-east took the case from Devon and Cornwall police after new information and evidence came to light.The suspect, who was described by police officers as a white British national, was in police custody after being arrested in Rotherham.Devon and Cornwall police initially arrested a 26-year-old man from Newton Abbot in Devon, but later released him and said he was no longer part of the investigation.Explore more on these topicsUK newsEnglandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Tate brothers fight extradition to UK over rape and sex-trafficking charges | US news | The Guardian
description: Polarizing social media influencers are being held in federal detention in Miami as district judge considers extradition
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Polarizing social media influencers are being held in federal detention in Miami as district judge considers extradition
Content: Andrew Tate, right, and his brother, Tristan, arrive at an event on 11 April 2026 in Miami, Florida.Photograph: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/APView image in fullscreenAndrew Tate, right, and his brother, Tristan, arrive at an event on 11 April 2026 in Miami, Florida.Photograph: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/APUS newsTate brothers fight extradition to UK over rape and sex-trafficking chargesPolarizing social media influencers are being held in federal detention in Miami as district judge considers extraditionAssociated PressMon 20 Jul 2026 15.01 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 15.05 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSocial media influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate will fight extradition to the United Kingdom, their lawyer told a US magistrate in Miami on Monday after the brothers were arrested to face rape and sex-trafficking charges.The Tates appeared in federal court for the first time since their arrest on Saturday and are being held in federal detention. In the coming weeks, a US district judge will consider whether they meet the conditions for extradition.The brothers, normally photographed in tailored suits and stylish fitted shirts, wore tan jail uniforms and handcuffs during the brief hearing.Andrew and Tristan Tate arrested in US on UK rape and sex trafficking chargesRead moreJoseph McBride, an attorney for the brothers, has decried the charges as “filth and slander”. On Sunday, McBride said he was confident the extradition request would be denied.US magistrate Lauren Louis set another hearing for next Monday.Prosecutors in Britain said the new charges relate to four new victims and were brought after authorities received evidence from Bedfordshire police in south-eastern England. The allegations, spanning from 2010 to 2017, include rape, assault, trafficking and offenses relating to “indecent images of a child and extreme pornography”.The brothers are already charged with rape, bodily harm, human trafficking and controlling prostitution for gain relating to three other alleged victims in the UK between 2012 and 2015.Their lawyer said in a statement on Saturday that he believed the arrests were “greenlighted by a low-level functionary” at the justice department without input from leadership.The justice department told the Associated Press that the arrests were approved by leadership of its criminal division.The new allegations are the latest in a long-running international legal saga involving the Tates that has spanned the US, Britain and Romania. The brothers have repeatedly denied wrongdoing.Their social media empire promoting male dominance, misogyny and wealth has made them among the world’s most polarizing internet personalities.The dual US and British citizens moved to Romania in 2016. They were arrested there in 2022, accused of participating in schemes to lure women for sexual exploitation. They denied those allegations and the Romanian case has not gone forward because of legal and procedural problems.Last year, they were allowed to leave Romania and flew to Florida.Explore more on these topicsUS newsLaw (US)Social mediaMiaminewsShareReuse this content

title: Nearly two million people join Spain’s World Cup celebrations in Madrid | Spain | The Guardian
description: More than a million Spaniards descended on central Madrid on Monday night to welcome the triumphant national team home
Author: Sam Jones
Headline: More than a million Spaniards descended on central Madrid on Monday night to welcome the triumphant national team home
Content: Spain’s players parade the World Cup trophy through Madrid on Monday.Photograph: BALLESTEROS/EPAView image in fullscreenSpain’s players parade the World Cup trophy through Madrid on Monday.Photograph: BALLESTEROS/EPASpainNearly two million people join Spain’s World Cup celebrations in MadridSquad arrived home at 2.30pm local time on MondayVictory festivities to centre around the Plaza de CibelesSam Jonesin MadridMon 20 Jul 2026 16.01 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 10.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAlmost two million Spaniards, many still bleary-eyed and feeling a little delicate after thecountry’s historic World Cup victory against Argentina, descended on central Madrid on Monday night to welcome the triumphant national team home.La Roja, who beat Argentina 1-0 in the final in New Jersey on Sunday night, were afforded the first of many heroes’ welcomes when they touched down at Madrid’s Barajas airport at 2.30pm local time on Monday.World Cup 2026: Spain’s champions land in Madrid for heroes’ welcome after beating Argentina – liveRead moreSpain’s captain, Rodri, waved the trophy aloft as he emerged from the plane, his medal from the previous night around his neck. The team posed for a photo before heading into terminal four where hundreds of delighted fans, many inSpainstrips and carrying the country’s red-and-yellowrojigualdaflag, waited and hoped for a glimpse of the freshly minted champions.Their first stop was an audience with King Felipe, Queen Letizia and their daughters, Princess Leonor and Princess Sofía, at the Zarzuela palace, northwest of Madrid, where the king paid tribute to their talent and determination.“It’s been an epic triumph,” he said. “It’s been a privilege and a joy for the four of us to see you play in the final and to be able to celebrate with you that moment of lifting the cup, with immense thanks from all of Spain.”Felipe also praised their fortitude, saying: “You’ve suffered, you’ve come home bruised and tired, you’ve endured some criticism, but what is all that compared with the joy of bringing the cup home?”View image in fullscreenThousands of Spain supporters gather at Plaza de Cibeles in Madrid on Monday.Photograph: Richard Zubelzu/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockThe team’s next meeting was a visit to the Moncloa palace – the office and residence of the prime minister – to see Pedro Sánchez. The socialist prime minister offered the players and technical staff “massive thanks … for the game, for the effort and for the victory”.Speaking at the Moncloa palace, Rodri said that “winning for you country is the most beautiful thing there is”. He added: “This generation grew up watching [Iker] Casillas and [Andrés] Iniesta lift the cup [in 2010] and having been able to do that today is the biggest thing you can do in football.”The highlight for the fans – who braved the 34C evening heat – was the victory parade that wound its way from Moncloa to the Plaza de Cibeles just outside Madrid city hall. They crowded into the square, some sheltering from the sun under umbrellas, as the team approached in an open-topped bus. The central government’s delegate in Madrid put attendance along the route at around 1.8 million.Madrid’s mayor, José Luis Martínez Almeida, told Radio Nacional de España that the capital had been working to give the team the homecoming they deserved. He also hailed the peaceful and joyous scenes that had greeted Spain’s victory on Sunday night.View image in fullscreenRodri carries the World Cup at Barajas airport on Monday.Photograph: Sergio R Moreno/GTRES/Shutterstock“Even though there were thousands and thousands of people on the streets last night, there were practically no incidents in the city,” he said.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“We’re getting everything ready for tonight … The thing we’re focusing on is how to celebrate and greet a team that will live for ever in our memory.”The victory – Spain’s second men’s World Cup win after their triumph against the Netherlands in South Africa in 2010 – wasseen by hundreds of thousand of Spaniardswho followed the match in bars, parks, fairgrounds, bullrings, and on giant screens in the centre of Madrid. The Spanish press have hailed it as“a unique collective experience”made possible by a young team that reflects all that is best about a modern, diverse and determined country.The win has also been enthusiastically hailed by the country’s political class.“WE ARE WORLD CHAMPIONS!!” Sánchez wrote in a social media post. “What an amazing national squad. Thank you, team.”Alberto Núñez Feijóo, the leader of Spain’s conservative People’s party, was equally free with the capital letters. “Well-deserved WORLD CHAMPIONS! Values, hard work, humility, and faith. You have made history. There are no limits for this team … Long live Spain!!!” he wrote on X.Santiago Abascal, the leader of the far-right Vox party, also offered his congratulations to the team for carrying “the flag of Spain so far and so high”.The Spanish postal service, Correos, has announced the creation of a special stamp to commemorate what it called “one of the greatest achievements in the history of Spanish sport”. The red-coloured stamp will bear the word CAMPEONES and feature two stars – one for each of Spain’sWorld Cupvictories.Explore more on these topicsSpainWorld Cup 2026World CupnewsShareReuse this content

title: Tens of thousands of Cockroach movement protesters defy police with Delhi march | India | The Guardian
description: Youth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in years
Author: Hannah Ellis-Petersen
Headline: Youth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in years
Content: 0:51'Cockroach' movement: thousands march in anti-government protest in New DelhiIndiaTens of thousands of Cockroach movement protesters defy police with Delhi marchYouth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in yearsHannah Ellis-Petersenin DelhiMon 20 Jul 2026 10.01 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 20.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTens of thousands of protesters have defied police orders and descended on Delhi to demand the resignation of India’s education minister in one of the largest anti-government demonstrations in years.A huge crowd, largely made up of students and gen Z-ers, filled the streets in the heart of India’s capital from early Monday morning shouting anti-government slogans. Hundreds had already camped out overnight.Delhi police, which are under the control of the central government, had denied permission for the march to take place. A ban was imposed on all public gatherings, roads across the city were barricaded and the internet was shut down in the centre. Attempts by protesters to move towards the parliament building were met with police brutality including the use of batons and teargas.However, this did little to prevent protesters turning out in their thousands, many of whom were vocal in their anger at Narendra Modi’s government and his ruling Bharatiya Janata party (BJP), accusing them of failing the country’s young people.“They are calling this protest illegal, they are calling us anti-national,” said Rhythm Katoch. “But I am 22 years old and I am standing here to fight for our education system, for the rights of the young people and for our democracy. Is it too much to ask for the government to listen to us?”View image in fullscreenThe protesters were vocal in their anger at Narendra Modi’s government and his ruling Bharatiya Janata party.Photograph: Shekhar Yadav/APA call had been made to march on parliament by the Cockroach Janta party (CJP), a new youth-led movement whose name was inspired by remarks made in May by Surya Kant, the chief justice of India, when he called some activists and unemployed youth “cockroaches”. While the CJP began as satire, founded by the graduate Abhijeet Dipke in May,it has swiftly mobilised intoa serious political challenge to the Modi government.Its core demand has been the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, over exam chaos, after this year’s medical entrance exam – where more than 2 million students compete for just 130,000 places – was again leaked to the highest bidder, forcing it to be scrapped and for students to face retaking the gruelling tests. The fiasco has been linked to more than 20 suicides.Wider frustrations over alleged failures in the education system and a lack of job opportunities for India’s youth have also mobilised support.View image in fullscreenCJP supporters clash with police during the march.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty ImagesRage was palpable on Delhi’s streets on Monday. Harion Sharma, 18, was among those forced to retake the medical exam last month after it was leaked, which he said had put “unbearable” pressure on him and his fellow students.“This is a government that does not care about education, it does not care about the pressures and pain of young people. Because of those leaks, students have died. So we are here to call for accountability and for the education minister to resign. We are young but we are powerful.”Sharma described the high turnout of the protest as historic. “They might be trying to crush our futures and our democracy, but look around, how young people have come to the streets in such massive numbers,” he added. “Now, finally, the government is scared of us.”The use of police force against the protesters was met with condemnation by opposition politicians. Speaking outside parliament, Priyanka Gandhi, a leader of the opposition Congress party, said: “There are massive issues … but you are firing teargas at children and beating them up? For what?”For weeks, the government had ignored the CJP supporters camped out less than a mile from parliament. Pradhan had sought to dismiss their calls for his resignation by calling them “terrorists”. However, after the huge turnout on Monday, the Modi government invited several CJP spokespeople for talks.In a social media post, the CJP spokesperson Saurav Das said they had waited two hours and were given a brief meeting with a cabinet minister, JP Nadda. Das said the government had made no commitments so far.View image in fullscreenThe renowned engineer and education activist Sonam Wangchuk has been on hunger strike in solidarity with the students.Photograph: ReutersWhile neighbouring countries such as Nepal, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka have had governments toppled by youth-led movements, such large-scale expressions of dissent have become unusual in India in recent years.The BJP, which has ruled for 12 years under Modi, has amassed unprecedented power at national and state level while also routinely jailing and persecuting its critics and opponents, leaving little room for dissent.The BJP MP Kangana Ranaut was among the ruling government politicians to criticise the protest, telling reporters that “no one can pressure the government into who to sack and who to retain”.The CJP protest had been galvanised by ahunger strike carried out by the renowned engineer and education activist Sonam Wangchuk, who in solidarity with the students has refused all food and water for the past 21 days until Pradhan agrees to resign.View image in fullscreenA police officer fires teargas shells towards the protesters.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty ImagesNationwide outrage grew after the Delhi police arrived early on Sunday morning and forcibly removed Wangchuk against his will, with footage of the incident going viral.Wangchuk was taken to a government hospital where he claimed he was being “held hostage”. In a statement, his wife, Gitanjali Angmo, condemned the heavy police presence around Wangchuk in the hospital ward. “It is not medical care,” she said. “It is illegal detention.”In response, organisers of the CJP also began a hunger strike and called on supporters to join Monday’s march, which Wangchuk said would be “India’s second freedom movement”.Amrita, 27, said she had taken a day off work to join the protest, calling the government’s treatment of Wangchuk “shameless”.“We have a coward government who is afraid of the young people and afraid of Sonam Wangchuk,” she said. “He is a brilliant brain and he has put his life on the line for young people. How he is being treated is a shame on India. That’s why the gen Zs have come out today to speak out.”By Monday evening there had been no formal response by Pradhan to the calls for his resignation and CJP organisers said they would remain at the protest site. In a handwritten note from hospital, Wangchuk said that “seeing the brutality with which peacefully protesting youth are being dealt with, I have decided to continue my fast”.Explore more on these topicsIndiaNarendra ModiSouth and central AsianewsShareReuse this content

title: Nicaragua’s president says country will not hold any more elections | Nicaragua | The Guardian
description: Daniel Ortega, the 80-year-old authoritarian leader, says move will ‘build a wall’ against the opposition
Author: Tiago Rogero
Headline: Daniel Ortega, the 80-year-old authoritarian leader, says move will ‘build a wall’ against the opposition
Content: Daniel Ortega made the announcement on Sunday during the official ceremony marking the 47th anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenDaniel Ortega made the announcement on Sunday during the official ceremony marking the 47th anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesNicaraguaNicaragua’s president says country will not hold any more electionsDaniel Ortega, the 80-year-old authoritarian leader, says move will ‘build a wall’ against the oppositionTiago RogeroSouth America correspondentMon 20 Jul 2026 17.36 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.50 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleNicaragua’s long-serving president,Daniel Ortega, has said the country will hold no further elections to prevent the opposition from coming to power.The 80-year-old authoritarian leader, who has been in office since 2007, also said he would work with the congress he controls to pass new laws that would “build a wall” against the opposition.Ortega made the announcement on Sunday during the official ceremony marking the 47th anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution, in which he helped overthrow the brutal dictatorship of Anastasio Somoza.Although his administration and that of his wife and “co-president”, Rosario Murillo, had already been marked by the steady erosion of democracy in the Central American country, many are describing his announcement as a significant rhetorical escalation.“With this decision, Ortega and Murillo have sealed the country’s transition to a full-fledged family dictatorship,” said Tiziano Breda, Latin America senior analyst at the NGO Armed Conflict Location and Event Data.During a speech in front of thousands of state employees who,according to local media, had been coerced into attending, Ortega said: “There will be no more elections here so that they [the opposition parties] can try to seize the government, seize power.”The president, who had not appeared in public for 61 days, added: “We will work with the National Assembly and the relevant institutions on laws, because we need laws that build a wall, a barrier, against the coup plotters and the traitors who sell out their country.”The elections were due to take place next year, but Ortega gave no details on whether they would be cancelled or whether the opposition would be barred from taking part – something that has already happened in practice. During the 2021 campaign, his regime outlawed parties and jailed all opposition presidential hopefuls.View image in fullscreenDaniel Ortega last year promoted his wife, then vice-president, to the position of ‘co-president’.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesBreda said: “Ortega and Murillo are evidently afraid of the idea that the slightest political opening could create the conditions for dissent to manifest and threaten their grip on power, possibly suggesting that their already feeble domestic support is further falling.“Therefore, instead of staging a rigged election, they have opted to eliminate electoral competition altogether.”Last year, Ortega pushed through a series of constitutional reforms that included extending the presidential term from five to six years and promoting his wife, then vice-president, to the position of “co-president”. The USrefersto their administration as the “Murillo-Ortega dictatorship”.During his speech, the Nicaraguan president criticised the US over the January operation that abducted the Venezuelan dictator Nicolás Maduro: “With all their power, they stormed the presidential palace, where Nicolás and his wife, Cilia [Flores], were. They killed the entire security detail there – most of them were Cubans – and took them away to imprison them in the US. It is monstrous.”The president said preventing the opposition from returning to power was necessary to protect the country from the “coup plotters” who, he claimed, had been behind the nationwideprotests of 2018– when, according to organisations including the UN, his regime carried out a violent crackdown that killed more than 350 people, wounded hundreds, jailed thousands and forced nearly 1 million Nicaraguans into exile.Recently, the UN, the US, human rights organisations and 30 former Ibero-American presidents have directly blamed Ortega and Murillo for the death of the Indigenous leader and four-term congressmanBrooklyn Rivera, 73, who spent nearly three years “unjustly imprisoned” by the regime, the US state department said.According to organisations including Human Rights Watch, there are about 50 political prisoners in the country. The regime has stripped 546 people of their citizenship and shut down more than 5,600 non-governmental organisations, 29 universities and 58 media outlets.Explore more on these topicsNicaraguaAmericasDaniel OrteganewsShareReuse this content

title: Small dog missing for weeks rescued while swimming in San Francisco Bay | San Francisco | The Guardian
description: Team of firefighters saved the pup from the water near East Palo Alto in a mission that utilized boats, trucks and a drone
Author: Roque Planas
Headline: Team of firefighters saved the pup from the water near East Palo Alto in a mission that utilized boats, trucks and a drone
Content: Firefighters rescue dog from San Francisco Bay - loopSan FranciscoSmall dog missing for weeks rescued while swimming in San Francisco BayTeam of firefighters saved the pup from the water near East Palo Alto in a mission that utilized boats, trucks and a droneRoque PlanasMon 20 Jul 2026 16.31 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.53 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFirefighters rescued a small dog that had been missing for weeks after finding it swimming into theSan FranciscoBay.The dog had gone missing over the Fourth of July weekend, according to the Menlo Park fire protection district. But someone called 911 on 16 July to say they had seen a dog enter the bay near the Dumbarton Bridge in East Palo Alto.The dog “just kept swimming”, according to aMenlo Park fire district Instagram post.View image in fullscreenMenlo Park Fire Protection District rescued a dog in the San Francisco Bay.Photograph: Menlo Park fire protection districtThe Menlo Park fire protection district mobilized at least two trucks, a boat and an airboat in pursuit of the dog. Rescuers first put eyes on it from the ground with binoculars. Later, they used a drone while pursuing it from the water.The department later released footage of the dog rescue on social media.Both fire and police officers assisted with saving the missing dog, which was photographed getting toweled off by ateam of tattooed firefighters.Additional footage released by the East Palo Alto police department showed the dog beingfollowed by seagullsin the water. According toInMenlo, the dog was rescued a half mile off the shore.Authorities later turned over to the local Society for the Prevention of Cruelty toAnimals(SPCA), which located the pup’s family and returned it.“Dog and family have been reunited,” the Menlo Park fire protection district’s Instagram post reads.Explore more on these topicsSan FranciscoAnimalsDogsPetsCaliforniaWest CoastnewsShareReuse this content

title: Inside the horrifying online network where children extort others to commit violence and self-harm | Internet safety | The Guardian
description: The 764 network is just one part of a growing online ecosystem that pushes young members into brutal and degrading acts. It has perpetrators and victims in dozens of countries
Author: Ariel Bogle
Headline: The 764 network is just one part of a growing online ecosystem that pushes young members into brutal and degrading acts. It has perpetrators and victims in dozens of countries
Content: The 764 network is one of several shadowy online global groupsInternet safetyInside the horrifying online network where children extort others to commit violence and self-harmThe 764 network is just one part of a growing online ecosystem that pushes young members into brutal and degrading acts. It has perpetrators and victims in dozens of countriesAriel BogleMon 20 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 20.18 EDTShareIn late 2024, in a quiet suburb of Stockholm, a 14-year-old boy approached a man in his 80s who was using a walker. He stabbed the man three times in the back.The video of the attack in Hässelby was streamed and spread quickly. It fed a global online ecosystem that actively encourages and glorifies acts of violence and degradation – the “gamification of harm”, as the researcher Marc-André Argentino puts it.The boy allegedlyhad linksto anetwork known as 764. Such groups are known to target young people in games including Roblox and Minecraft as well as communities struggling with mental health or sexual identity for online extortion, pressuring them into acts of violence and self-harm.The 764 network, founded by a Texas teenager, Bradley Cadenhead, is just one part of an evolving online ecosystem, with perpetrators and victims in dozens of countries. Cadenhead is now serving an 80-year sentence in the US for possessing child sexual abuse material.View image in fullscreenBradley Cadenhead – now serving an 80-year prison term in the US – was 15 when he started the 764 network.Photograph: Texas Department of Criminal JusticeThere are several cases currently before courts around the world with alleged links to such groups. Legal records from numerous countries suggest they often celebrate violence, manipulation and harm for its own sake, collecting records of such acts to gain notoriety and control.“These are young people recruiting other young people,” says Bjørn Ihler, the founder of the security firm Revontulet. “It is a youth phenomenon.”The Australian federal police commissioner, Krissy Barrett,has said: “A worrying trend is how victims are becoming perpetrators to try to escape their online bullies who have befriended and groomed them, and then forced them to hurt themselves.”A Swedish court decided in April 2025 that the teenager had carried out the stabbing although he was too young to be held criminally responsible. But another name emerged during the investigation, local authorities told the Guardian – an online username: “Chai.”In early 2025, as the country was still reeling from the attack, an Australian girl rang Swedish police. She wanted to tell authorities about Chai.‘It’s bigger than these few cases’Linus Svensson, an investigative reporter at the Swedish broadcaster SVT, had never heard of 764 before the 2024 stabbing.Svensson says he now knows of at least nine local court cases related to “the Com” – the term fora broad online network that engages in cybercrime, extortion and violence, and which includes 764. He says he has found dozens of Swedish members in chat groups.“It’s bigger than these few cases,” he says. “I think it took a while for Swedish authorities and also the general public to actually understand this.”As he began to wade through videos and imagery shared by the groupfor SVT’s investigation, Svensson came across a teenager in the north of Sweden who had been convicted in 2024 of trying to coerce a 13-year-old boy to take his own life.This, according to Jenny Östling, a senior public prosecutor with Sweden’s National Unit against International and Organised Crime, was Chai.Quick GuideContact us about this storyShowThe best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know.If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods.Secure Messaging in the Guardian appThe Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said.If you don't already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’.SecureDrop, instant messengers, email, telephone and postIf you can safely use the Tor network without being observed or monitored, you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via ourSecureDrop platform.Finally, our guide attheguardian.com/tipslists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each.Illustration: Guardian Design / Rich CousinsWas this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.The teenager, whose real name cannot be published, is on trial for more than 70 alleged offences, according to the indictment,including allegedly encouraging someone to self-harm and kill themselves, and the creation of child sexual abuse material. Most were allegedly committed against the Australian girl.He is also charged with inducing a young man in Germany to harm himself so badly he almost died.His lawyer, Emily Töyrä, told the Guardian he denies all the allegations.The trial was paused in late June after the Swedish court sent him for a forensicpsychiatric investigation, according to court records, stating there was “compelling evidence that [he] has committed several of the acts with which he has been charged”. The matter will return in August.Östling says the case will test whether people can be held responsible for remotely coercing others into acts of self-harm or violence – a hallmark of groups such as 764.“They have a goal for the group itself: to destroy the world … But [members] also have a goal for themselves: they want to gain status within the group,” Östling says.Blackmailing girls and encouraging suicide: the young British men in online gangsRead moreCreating new material can be a prerequisite of entry to closed chatrooms and other markers of membership. “They primarily coerce young victims into cutting themselves or sexual acts,” she says. “They use it to be able to coerce them into doing something worse.”Östling alleges Chai was “high status” within 764, and was invited into a chatroom known as “764 Inferno”.His lawyer disputes that characterisation, and says her client’s involvement was limited to editing images and videos. “The Com environment is highly fluid,” Töyrä says. “My client does not accept the characterisation of ‘764 Inferno’ as the type of organised group alleged by the prosecution. In his view, it was simply one group chat among many.”A global phenomenonAn indictmentunveiled by the US Department of Justice in April 2025 alleges that two American men in their early 20s, Leonidas Varagiannis and Prasan Nepal, were part of a chatroom with the same name which “was reserved for the inner core of 764 members”.The pair are accused of using “764 Inferno” to conspire with other members to create and distribute child exploitation images, among other charges. Researchershave found atleast 295 arrests linked to the Com network in 33 countries, including Australia, where there are matters before the courts.View image in fullscreenCameron Finnigan, then 19, was jailed last year in the UK for possessing a ‘kill guide’ and indecent images of children.Photograph: Counter Terrorism PolicingInNew Zealand, after a tipoff from US authorities, Ronndog Keefe pleaded guilty in 2025 to 13 charges, including the distribution of “horrific and graphic” child exploitation material. The 22-year-old was sentenced to more than five years’ jail in December.Keefe claimed he was also a victim of 764, which he said had threatened to dox him if he did not distribute such material, according to legal records, but the court found this “simply was not credible”.Last year in the UK, Cameron Finnigan, then 19, was given a nine-year extended sentence for possessing a terrorist document – a “kill guide” with advice about how to commit “truck attacks” – and indecent images of children. He was also convicted of encouraging someone online to take their own life.The court found Finnigan was part of 764. “The intended victims are … vulnerable individuals: in particular, LGBTQI+ youth, racial minorities and those suffering from mental illnesses or disorders,” the judge said at his sentencing. “They are targeted precisely because they are so obviously vulnerable.”The Com: the growing cybercrime network behind recent Pornhub hackRead moreIn June another British teenager, Elijah Palmer,was sentencedfor encouraging serious self-harm, among other charges. He was also linked to 764, police said, and had targeted his victim on Roblox and the chat platform Discord.The FBIuses the term“nihilistic violent extremism” to describe these activities but there is debate about the moniker. Some argueit is vagueand tries to unite too many different groups under one umbrella.Researchers such as Argentinohave notedthat ideology in these spaces can be “fluid, performative, and primarily symbolic” – occult or neo-Nazi imagery can function as markers of group membership rather than “as coherent political programs”.Membership is also vague, according to Bjørn Ihler, who prefers to add the term “misanthropic”. “These are basically Discord servers,” he says. “There’s channels upon channels upon channels.”Ultimately, the pursuit of sextortion material is about “power and … control of the victim”, Ihler says, and such content can be shared in so-called “lore books” for further victimisation by others.“Sharing those materials … leads to increased ‘clout’, which is like the social currency,” he says. “Grappling with that is incredibly important from a victim-protection perspective.”‘It could happen to anyone’Long before these arrests took place, an independent researcher, Becca Spinks, had been tracking these groups from her home in Texas.View image in fullscreenA knife found in Finnigan’s home.Photograph: Counter Terrorism Policing“As a mom, I’m just horrified,” she says. “I couldn’t close my eyes and just be like, ‘Oh, well, someone else will deal with it.’ Who’s working on this? What’s going on? How can we help?”As part of her work, she started to speak to the mother of Chai’s Australian alleged victim several years ago, who was looking for help to get the attention of authorities and assistance for her child.FBI opens inquiry into 764, online group that sexually exploits and encourages minors to self-harmRead moreThe trials of men linked to 764 taking place all over the world, Spinks says, have come as some relief. “She’s very brave,” she says of the alleged victim in Chai’s case. “And her mum also is really just a powerhouse.”Chai’s trial has largely taken place behind closed doors but the toll the alleged crimes have taken on victims is clear.Spinks says her biggest priority is raising awareness for parents and law enforcement officials.“A lot of times kids come forward and maybe [the police] don’t recognise the signs that it’s the 764 network, and so they don’t pursue it as aggressively as they should,” she says.Spinks says there needs to be greater awareness for parents too. The Australian federal policecite warning signsincluding indications of self-harm, sudden withdrawal from family and friends and secretive behaviour about online interactions.“One of the biggest, most dangerous misunderstandings is that this won’t happen to us, or this only happens to a certain type of kid, when in reality, it could honestly happen to anyone.”Do you know more? Contact ariel.bogle@theguardian.comIn Australia, support is available atBeyond Blueon 1300 22 4636,Lifelineon 13 11 14, and atMensLineon 1300 789 978. In the UK, the charityMindis available on 0300 123 3393 andChildlineon 0800 1111. In the US, call or textMental Health Americaat 988 or chat 988lifeline.org.
