title: Secret Lives of Gypsy Wives review – Trewley Precious and her glorious family are a gift to reality TV  | Television | The Guardian
description: The TikTok star describes them as a broke version of the Kardashians, but they are far too funny and charismatic for that. Channel 4 has hit the TV jackpot!
Author: Lucy Mangan
Headline: The TikTok star describes them as a broke version of the Kardashians, but they are far too funny and charismatic for that. Channel 4 has hit the TV jackpot!
Content: (From left) Trewley, Ruby-Anne, Pearlygirl, Honey and Tina.Photograph: Jude Edgington/Channel 4/OptomenView image in fullscreen(From left) Trewley, Ruby-Anne, Pearlygirl, Honey and Tina.Photograph: Jude Edgington/Channel 4/OptomenTV reviewTelevisionReviewSecret Lives of Gypsy Wives review – Trewley Precious and her glorious family are a gift to reality TVThe TikTok star describes them as a broke version of the Kardashians, but they are far too funny and charismatic for that. Channel 4 has hit the TV jackpot!Lucy ManganMon 20 Jul 2026 18.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleChannel 4 must have fallen on Trewley Precious and her family with sobbing gratitude. An articulate, charismatic gift to the camera who is also a member of the segment of the population with which the broadcaster has long been most pruriently fascinated. Jackpot!! I like to think of her standing there, in all her contoured, coiffed, lashed and fillered glory, patting the grovelling film-makers on the head with the satisfaction of knowing that her time has truly come.Trewley Precious, if you have not had what I suspect is soon to be the inescapable pleasure, is an online influencer (nearly a million people follow her on TikTok) and member of the Traveller community who now stars – alongside her four siblings and their magisterial mother Tina – in the new reality series Secret Lives of Gypsy Wives. Trewley describes the clan as “like the Kardashians if they were broke! If the Kardashians were fat and on benefits!” Which is fair comment, but they are also like the Kardashians if Kris’s pod people had a sense of humour, irrepressible energy, charm and an abundance of affection for each other. I cannot imagine Khloé replying to an inquiry from Kim about what she’s done to prepare for their mother’s birthday with a cheery “More than what you’ve done, fatty!” as Trewley does to brother Jamie.Trewley, Jamie, Honey, Ruby-Anne and Pearlygirl grew up as part of a traditional Traveller family – ruled by the patriarch, wives expected to cook, clean and have babies, daughters expected to grow up and do likewise – on a deprived council estate, where they have painted their house pink. Tina, not a Traveller, grew up in poverty and without a consistent home so insisted to her husband that, despite his natural wanderlust, they stay in one place and raise their family. She has now lived there 40 years. Ruby-Anne lives with her, Trewley lives in a pink chalet in the garden and the other girls all married young and live nearby with their own families. Jamie – much to the perturbation of his father, Sidney – eschewed the trad life and became an estate agent and remains just as close to his family in every sense as the rest.Sidney died less than three years ago and they are all still clearly devastated by the loss. “I can’t talk,” says Trewley, as she begins to describe it. “I feel like I’m in a different world.” They still buy presents from him for Tina on her birthday. “To my Teenie,” runs the accompanying message on Trewley’s. “Happy birthday, old girl. Thank you for looking after our babies. I love you in this life and the next.” The whole family falls silent for a moment in their shared grief. “That’s lovely, Trew,” says her brother approvingly. Then, nodding towards mum, who is having a good cry, “You’ve ruined the whole day.” The family erupts once more. Life goes on.View image in fullscreen‘I want an empire’ … Trewley Precious.Photograph: Jude Edginton/Jude Edgington/Channel 4/OptomenEven in the middle of sorrow, however, Trewley is beginning to see that her father’s absence may create more freedom for her to realise her non-traditional ambitions. For Trewley – then just 25 – does not want, yet at least, to be a wife or mother. “I want an empire.” Brand collaborations are beginning to come in and money is being made, though she, quite rightly, won’t say how much.She also wants a belly button piercing, but this – along with tattoos on women – is a Traveller no-no. “Your mum will fucking rip it out of you,” says Trewley’s friend Robyn, whom Tina absorbed into her own clan as she did many others who came and went as they needed it over the years. It’s only a slight exaggeration – Tina actually just throws her out of the house until the piercing is removed. Trewley is staying the course on the empire thing though.Beneath the glitz and glamour glimpses of darker, more painful truths can be seen. That they do live on the breadline is clear when Trewley reminisces fondly about how, “When Dad died, everyone [on the estate] was waiting for their benefits to hit so they could buy us milk.” That there are limits to the benefits of comradeship itself is also clear. “You either stay on that estate and slowly die or you go to prison,” Trewley states, matter-of-factly, during one of the solo interviews to camera with which the more raucous family scenes are intercut: “And I don’t want that for my life.”Trewley’s star is on the rise, you hope. You hope she gets everything she wants, and that she learns to want more too. If the bleak vision of Britain that underlies her family’s story is anything to go by, however, we will have to hope very hard indeed.Secret Lives of Gypsy Wives is on Channel 4 now.Explore more on these topicsTelevisionTV reviewreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Richard Dadd review – can his mental illness help explain these arresting, bizarre images?  | Art and design | The Guardian
description: Dadd was already a remarkable artist – with fairies as his hit theme – before he was locked up for murder. Yet it was within the confines of Bedlam that he saw nature and art afresh
Author: Jonathan Jones
Headline: Dadd was already a remarkable artist – with fairies as his hit theme – before he was locked up for murder. Yet it was within the confines of Bedlam that he saw nature and art afresh
Content: An uneasy adult fairyland … Richard Dadd, Titania Sleeping, 1841.Photograph: © GrandPalaisRmn (musée du Louvre) / Jean-Gilles BerizziView image in fullscreenAn uneasy adult fairyland … Richard Dadd, Titania Sleeping, 1841.Photograph: © GrandPalaisRmn (musée du Louvre) / Jean-Gilles BerizziArt and designReviewRichard Dadd review – can his mental illness help explain these arresting, bizarre images?Royal Academy, LondonDadd was already a remarkable artist – with fairies as his hit theme – before he was locked up for murder. Yet it was within the confines of Bedlam that he saw nature and art afreshJonathan JonesMon 20 Jul 2026 19.01 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 19.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe young man in the garden must be one of Richard Dadd’s fellow patients in Bedlam, you think. His unkempt hair and ruffled clothes are sure indications of a distracted mind as he sits, half his face in shadow, hands nervously clasped, on a fabulously twisted wooden bench surrounded by greenery that’s almost too lush for comfort. Yet the subject of Dadd’s 1853 Portrait of a Young Man is probably the superintendent of Bethlem Hospital, Dr Charles Hood. The painter sees reflections of his own mental state in this frail-seeming young clinician. Patient and doctor are not so different, he suggests. There is the finest of lines between “sanity” and “madness”.Dadd crossed that line when he murdered his father whom he thought was the devil, then tried to kill a stranger while fleeing to Paris. He was quickly caught and would spend the rest of his life in Bedlam and Broadmoor. But how much does his mental illness explain his arresting, bizarre, sometimes unforgettable images? Is he a kind of Victorian acid casualty whose visions of fairies are the peculiar fruit of his psychosis?View image in fullscreenRichard Dadd, Portrait of a Young Man, 1853.Photograph: Sonal Bakrania/© Tate / Tate ImagesTheRoyal Academy’s smallish Dadd surveyis called Beyond Bedlam and urges us to see him as more than just a “mad” artist. Yes, he spent more than 40 years in psychiatric hospitals where he was judged incurable and where he painted his masterpiece The Fairy Feller’s Master-Stroke, that monstrously magnified corner of an English country garden. But he was already a remarkable artist, with fairies as his hit theme.These are not cute Disneyfied fairies but the original mysterious beings from British folklore, sinister and destructive, as portrayed by Shakespeare. Dadd’s early painting of Puck has the mischievous sprite personified as a giant baby irradiated by lunar light. InTitania Sleeping, we glimpse, through a parted curtain of bats, nude female fairies. It’s an uneasy adult fairyland. So too isThe Haunt of the Fairiesin which a female fairy – or is it just a naked woman? – gazes at us against trees silhouetted in the red twilight of a very British landscape.Then Dadd went on an epic trip to the Middle East, filling his sketchbook with mosques, markets, Egyptian ruins, but what should have been a stimulating escape tipped him into severe paranoia and murder. This was when a promising life cracked open.When the fairies return to him in Bedlam they are brutally different. His early work is imaginative but visibly influenced by Romantic artists likeFuseliandRunge. But there’s no precedent that helps to understand The Fairy Feller’s Master-Stroke. Painted over a nine-year period, it disobeys the pictorial rules in which Dadd had been educated. When you try to fathom it, your eyes have to pick their way between tall dark strands of grass, daisies, dock leaves that block your view and clog and cramp the picture surface. Only through this tangle can you make out a detailed portrayal of fairy society. The tiny folk are gathered in awe and trepidation to watch the strongest fairy raise his axe to “fell” a horse chestnut to repair Queen Mab’s coach. These fairies are not beautiful or seductive. Two have outsized legs bulging from their pleated skirts. One has a vast bald blueish head.View image in fullscreenRichard Dadd, The Fairy Feller’s Master-Stroke, 1855-64.Photograph: © Tate, 2023It’s a picture full of conviction and not in any way an entertainment or conscious fantasy. This is what the fairies really look like, says Dadd, showing them to us like natural history specimens observed through a microscope.Perhaps he offered to show Dr Hood such fairies in a neglected recess of the garden of Bedlam.You can’t get away from it. Dadd was an interesting artist before Bedlam but became a great one in it – and a modern artist at that. In his own lonely way he overthrows the rules of art, reaching for unsettling new truths akin to those Manet and Whistler were probing, out in the “sane” world. What’s stranger is that within the confines of the asylum he sees nature afresh. Freed from artistic convention, he paints flowers, fruits, weeds with the savageness of their reality. InBacchanalian Scene, ecstatic, flushed faces are seen through an eerie morass of vegetation.Richard Dadd: the painter whose fantastical vision was unconfined by his 43 years in an asylumRead moreThe RA pointlessly insists on showing some duller works, as if to prove that not everything he did in Bedlam was charged with “mad” genius. But when he was moved to the new Broadmoor hospital his passion for nature was unleashed again. From fantastic fruits to an imaginary mountainous harbour that he designed for the hospital theatre’s drop curtain, these late watercolours have an ethereal, profoundly sad beauty.You can’t escape the tragedy of a gifted artist who spent so much of his life locked up, albeit in more liberal conditions than our stereotype of Victorian asylums might lead you to expect. But Dadd can’t be turned as art historians long to into a mainstream artist who just happened to be in Bedlam and Broadmoor. Thank Titania. For pitiable and painful as his life was, he cut through the weeds to see what others couldn’t: monstrous tiny creatures in the grass, ecstatic revels in the evening fields. Shine on, you crazy Victorian.Richard Dadd: Beyond Bedlam is at Royal Academy, London, from 25 July to 25 OctoberExplore more on these topicsArt and designRoyal Academy of ArtsExhibitionsPaintingMental healthreviewsShareReuse this content

title: The disturbing rise of ‘gooner’ assaults at Dollar Tree stores: ‘They find it funny to degrade women’ | US crime | The Guardian
description: In dark corners of the internet, men are goading each other to masturbate in public – and on female shoppers – and post the footage
Author: Andrew Gumbel
Headline: In dark corners of the internet, men are goading each other to masturbate in public – and on female shoppers – and post the footage
Content: ‘My life is not the same since that incident,” said MR of her alleged assault at a Dollar Tree location in Texas. ‘I totally was caught off guard. I didn’t have a chance to defend myself.’Illustration: Guardian Design / Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘My life is not the same since that incident,” said MR of her alleged assault at a Dollar Tree location in Texas. ‘I totally was caught off guard. I didn’t have a chance to defend myself.’Illustration: Guardian Design / Getty ImagesUS crimeThe disturbing rise of ‘gooner’ assaults at Dollar Tree stores: ‘They find it funny to degrade women’In dark corners of the internet, men are goading each other to masturbate in public – and on female shoppers – and post the footageAndrew GumbelMon 20 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.55 EDTShareThis article contains graphic descriptions of sexual assault.The woman known in court papers as MR never much liked going to dollar stores. She found them cluttered and chaotic, with too few staff on duty and too many strange people roaming the aisles for comfort.But in early December 2023 she went anyway, because she was planning a promotional photoshoot for her doggy daycare business in Houston and wanted some cheap Christmas props – reindeer ears and Santa hats – to put on her dogs. She and a friend picked one of the few Dollar Trees in an affluent neighborhood and arrived early, thinking they would be in and out quickly enough.As she walked the aisles, MR said in an interview, she sensed a man standing close behind her. She turned to confront him and noticed that he was a good six inches taller than her. He was talking in graphic detail about sexual things he wanted to do to her. “You’re so disgusting, get away from me,” she told him, and hurried off to find her friend.Still, he stayed close, and for a few tense minutes the two of them locked eyes while she waited in line to pay. When at last he left, she thought that was the end of it – until, back outside in the chill of the winter morning, she felt something cold against the back of her leggings.The man had ejaculated on her.“I didn’t know how to react. I wanted to kill this man,” said MR, who was 47 at the time, recalling the horror and helplessness she felt as her friend wiped her off with towels. (The Guardian has verified MR’s identity but is not publishing her full name to protect her privacy.)Hours later, on the advice of a sister who worked in law enforcement, she fished her soiled leggings out of the garbage and took them back to the store to report what had happened. A clerk listened to her account and, not seeming to realize that MR understood Spanish, shouted to a colleague in a backroom: “Tenemos otra.”“We’ve got another one.”View image in fullscreenA customer shops in a Dollar Tree store in Brooklyn, New York.Photograph: Spencer Platt/Getty ImagesOver the next several weeks and months, MR would learn not only that there had been reports of similar incidents at the same store in the same timeframe – one that had been shared on social mediaand at least one other she heard about anecdotally from shopping center employees – but that masturbating in front of female customers at branches of Dollar Tree was fast becoming a national phenomenon, a disturbing new form of sexual assault that men were documenting and bragging about in dark corners of the internet.It even came with its own specialized vocabulary. Masturbating in public and surreptitiously ejaculating on random women was known as “tagging” or “sharking” – or even “cum-sharking”. Online forums praised perpetrators for being “low inhibition” or “low inhib”, and users contributing to the discussion said they wished they could summon the same fearlessness in themselves.double quotation markThis is about trying to get a leg up on the lowest rung you can fall to – how to make a dollar on people who only have a dollarJamieson WebsterDollar Tree cropped up again and again as a favored location for such attacks, according to research conducted by MR’s lawyers and corroborated by police and media reports. While perpetrators have not spelled out the reasons, their choice appears connected to the same problems that made MR hesitant to shop at Dollar Tree in the first place and have led, over the years, to intense federal government scrutiny andmillions of dollars in fines:inadequate safety and security,understaffingon the premises, and disorganized aisles filled with unopened boxes that offer shoplifters and other criminals ample opportunity to skulk.In a statement to the Guardian, Dollar Tree did not address the specifics of what happened to MR but said it was committed to maintaining a safe and secure environment for its staff, customers and visitors. “We take reports of inappropriate, unlawful, or harassing behavior in our stores extremely seriously,” a spokesperson said. “Any incident of this nature is unacceptable, and when we become aware of criminal activity, we cooperate fully with law enforcement and support their investigations.”As soon as MR spoke to a local TV station in Houston about her story, she and her lawyers heard many similar ones – either via social media or directly from women who recognized what she was talking about and wanted legal representation of their own. “This happened to me in November of last year,” one woman wrote on X. “Same store, same location.” That woman had previously posted images, also on X, of a man she said followed her out of the same Dollar Tree, in Houston’s River Oaks shopping center, and ejaculated in front of her in the parking lot. (Attempts by the Guardian to reach the woman were unsuccessful.)View image in fullscreenA woman’s Instagram post about a man who allegedly sexually assaulted her in a Dollar Tree location in Houston, as shared on X.Photograph: @soakkedrice/XMR’s lawyers, Anna Greenberg and Ed Blizzard, were soon handling cases in Colorado Springs, San Diego and Miami, and collecting police and mediareports on more than a dozen similar instances at Dollar Tree or its former sister chain, Family Dollar, going back to 2017. In some cases, the victims were out shopping with their young children. One perpetrator was a freshly hiredsheriff’s deputy. Another was a Dollar Tree employee.“We couldn’t believe how big the scale was,” Greenberg said.Almost immediately, the lawyers stumbled on another weird dimension to the cases they were developing: an online subculture of lonely, disaffected men, known as “gooners”, who bond with one other over their shared fascination withobsessive masturbationand compete with each other to see who can keep going for hours or even days at a time.While most appear to pursue their self-pleasure in private “goon caves” and are not criminals, some gooners, the lawyers found, took pride in public acts of masturbation. In fact, some were committing sharking assaults at Dollar Tree stores and posting the footage online.Greenberg and Blizzard could not establish how public MR’s assailant had been with his exploits, since he has never been identified. But they did find an individual going by the name “Goon-God”, who had a page on an amateur porn site offering more than 200 sharking videos for $25 – along with a promotional clip that appeared to show an attack on an oblivious Dollar Tree shopper as she pushed a cart along a crowded soap aisle.Another gooner, who was caught filming himself while masturbating at a Dollar Tree in Fern Park, Florida, told police that he did this regularly “in different locations” and uploaded the videos to an OnlyFans page, according to his arrest report.double quotation markThis form of provocative behavior, no matter how distasteful, can be seen as a ticket to … attentionAdam LankfordMR’s lawyers believe this constitutes a new frontier in sexual assault – what Cecelia Prewett, a consultant to the legal team, describes as a “gamification” of sexual abuse perpetrated and encouraged by isolated men who pool their anger towards women in online forums.It’s a view broadly shared by criminologists and sociologists who study the intersection between human sexuality and crime – although they note a similar wave of sharking attacks in Japan about 15 years ago that attracted an online audience before subsiding again.“Masturbating over someone in public is done – and filmed – for the purposes of humiliating women and also receiving attention and accolades from other men,” said Michael Halpin, a sociologist from Dalhousie University in Canada who hasstudiedthe relationship between male “incel” culture and gender-based crime. “The [online] community applauds it, supports it, uplifts it and calls for more acts like it.”Money, muscles and anxiety: why the manosphere clicked with young men – a visual deep diveRead moreHalpin and other researchers say the misogyny is not restricted to people who are involuntarily celibate but extends to a broader community of men who are fueled by a combination of unfulfilled sexual desire, easy access to pornography and the lure of an attention-seeking economy.“Fame and celebrity are held up by many people as the ultimate form of success – more than wealth,” said Adam Lankford, a University of Alabama criminologist. “To some fringe individuals, this form of provocative behavior, no matter how distasteful, can be seen as a ticket to their own form of attention.”The degradation may even be the point, said Jamieson Webster, a psychoanalyst who teaches at the New School in New York. “A dollar store is the most abject place, where people who are already at the bottom go because they can’t afford anything else. In some way this is about trying to get a leg up on the lowest rung you can fall to – how to make a dollar on people who only have a dollar,” she said. “There’s something about that that feels so sad and tragic.”The acts themselves – public masturbation and ejaculating on strangers – long predate the internet, as does their place in the underbelly of popular culture. Jeffrey Dahmer had a history of indecent exposure before he became a serial killer, including anarrest in 1986for masturbating in front of two 12-year-old boys in Wisconsin. In both the book and film The Silence of the Lambs, a prisoner housed next to Thomas Harris’s fictional serial killer, Hannibal Lecter,flings his semenat FBI agent Clarice Starling in an effort to sexualize and humiliate her.What has changed in the digital age, Lankford argues, is the accessibility of material apt to arouse people in the most anodyne of settings, such as a traffic intersection or a dollar store.Long before it developed an association with criminal acts, “gooning” was understood as a pornography-fueled compulsion, sometimes involving multiple screens at once, that can become a substitute for intimate relationships with others.It also tends to come with a heavy dose of self-loathing. Experts say that online forums and video content aimed at gooners often send the message that obsessive masturbation – whether alone or in public – is as much as men like them can aspire to in life. Webster described it as a “disavowed manic state of triumph over something hideous”.One common trope of the pornography that these gooners consume has men ejaculating over women’s bodies, faces and hair – acts that, Lankford said, have no basis in evolutionary biology and are difficult to interpret as anything other than demeaning to the women.The Dollar Tree assaults essentially bring these elements together, turning a criminal act into some combination of a dare and a misogynistic joke. “Damn that’s pretty cool,” reads one comment on an incel forum in response to a 2018 sharking video. (The video has since been taken down.) “How did he not get caught there lol,” reads another.And the phenomenon appears to be growing.Six months after the attack on MR, a woman at a Dollar Tree in Philadelphiacaptured her own sharking episodeon video and the clip subsequently went viral.“He fucking nutted on my fucking leg!” the woman can be heard yelling as she chases her assailant out of the store. The man is clearly visible insurveillance photosreleased by the police.The Philadelphia rapper Meek Mill reposted the video with the offer of a $2,000 reward for anyone willing to “play manhunt”. Within hours, 35-year-old Gary Miles turned himself in and subsequently pleaded guilty to charges of indecent exposure, open lewdness and indecent assault without the consent of the other party.View image in fullscreenA censored screengrab from a video shared on X of a woman who captured her ‘sharking’ episode at a Dollar Tree in Philadelphia.Photograph: @CollinRugg/XOver the following months, the term “dollar store gooner” became a meme on the internet that spread far beyond the closed world of the gooners themselves. A “Dollar Tree gooner”crypto coinlaunched in January 2025, followed by an unofficialPokémon cardand asongreleased on SoundCloud by an underground noise band from Tennessee named Mukus.Around the same time, another episode went viral – not at a dollar store, but at a coffee shop called Bikini Beans in Tempe, Arizona, where a man wasfilmed masturbatingas he ordered from a female barista at the drive-through window. Again, the online response pointed to some constituency of men who find such things entertaining. “They find it funny that the women are getting degraded,” said Halpin, the sociologist. “That’s what they enjoy.”Within hours of the Arizona video appearing online, the perpetrator, who appeared to have no connection to the online subculture fascinated by him, wrote a note to say he couldn’t face his family and killed himself. The episode raised the complicated question of whether the man should be remembered primarily as a sex criminal or as a victim of public shaming. A group of supporters firmly believing the latter organized a protest at the coffee shop to pay homage to a man they called “goonlord” and dubbed the occasion a “gooneral”.MR, for her part, was so horrified by her assault that as soon as she got home she walked into the shower fully clothed, only later understanding that she was washing away crucial evidence. She subsequently made extensive efforts to find her assailant and was heartened when a store employee told her there were video surveillance cameras.But Dollar Tree never produced any footage, never apologized for what happened to her and, when challenged in a lawsuit alleging negligence and failure to provide a secure environment, argued that MR was not entitled to damages under Texas law because she had suffered no physical harm. Without video footage, police and private security officers at the River Oaks shopping center were unable to pursue an investigation.double quotation mark[Dollar Tree has] to take accountability of what is happening in [its] storesMRMR’s lawyers counter that the relevant legal standard is not, in fact, physical harm but “offensive contact”. And, in a testy exchange with a lawyer for Dollar Tree during a sworn deposition, MR vigorously challenged the notion that she had suffered no damage. She described how the incident had rocked her confidence, affected her relationships and made her afraid to go out after dark. “My life is not the same since that incident,” she said. “I totally was caught off guard. I didn’t have a chance to defend myself.”When the Dollar Tree lawyer asked her what she wanted out of the lawsuit, she replied: “You [have] to take accountability of what is happening in your stores. You’re causing pain to people. I don’t know if it’s because of the bottom line, or what, but it’s happening and you guys need to stop it.”In its statement to the Guardian, Dollar Tree said it was committed to effective safety and security. “Our efforts include ongoing safety education, regular policy reviews, risk assessment and mitigation activities, investments in physical security measures, and collaboration with local law enforcement,” the spokesperson said.With MR’s lawsuit, though, the company has attempted to limit the introduction of evidence from assaults in other states and other Dollar Tree outlets as a matter of legal strategy. In an August 2025 email to MR’s lawyers, company attorney Wesley Welmaker described the assault on MR as a “one-off event at Dollar Tree in the River Oaks shopping center”.In response, Greenberg told the court that MR’s assault was “anything but a ‘one-off event’” and pointed to 10 cases of sexual assault at Dollar Trees she had documented over the previous year and a half, six of them involving ejaculation.A year later, with the lawsuit still mired in preliminary motions and no trial date in sight, Greenberg’s research team has dug up63 officially documented sex crimes in all – fewer than five a year before 2020 and as many as 18 a year since the December 2023 attack on MR. Sixteen involved masturbation or ejaculation. The company’s attempts to disavow this, Greenberg said in an interview, amounted to “a plethora of stupid arguments” and only underlined what she saw as a pattern of poor corporate governance.Dollar Tree, its former sister company Family Dollar (which it sold last summer), and its rival Dollar General have all beenunder a microscopeaftermultiple reportsin recent years of safety violations, improper storage of drugs, rat infestations, poor security, shoplifting and other crimes including sexual assault that have occurred in its stores. Predominantly, the stores servelow-income neighborhoodswhere there are few alternatives, and critics often accuse them of preferring to endure some degree of criminal behavior occurring in stores and weather fines from the federal government’s workplace watchdog, the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (Osha), than pay for fully functioning security systems and boost staffing.View image in fullscreenPeople exit a Dollar Tree store in Richmond, California.Photograph: Bloomberg/Getty ImagesIn 2022, an Osha administratoraccusedDollar General of valuing “profits more than the safety of the people who work in their stores”. The next year, the Biden administration reached asettlementwith Dollar Tree and Family Dollar, under which the companies agreed without admitting liability to pay $1.35m in outstanding fines and overhaul workplace safety arrangements.At the time, Dollar Tree said it wastaking stepsto address safety concerns and that the foundations of the company were “continuous associate feedback, engagement and root cause analysis to understand issues and ensure compliance”. When asked by the Guardian about continuing criticisms that Dollar Tree was more interested in the bottom line than in adequate security or staffing at its 9,000 US outlets, the company had no comment.double quotation markThey’re not just doing this for personal gratification … They want other men potentially to do it, tooMichael HalpinGreenberg and Blizzard believe the Dollar Tree gooner phenomenon can shed further light on these issues and offer an opportunity to address some of them. In MR’s lawsuit and a similar case in San Diego, their legal filings argue that Dollar Tree should have had more staff and security, should have alerted the police to the sharking episodes on its premises and should have issued a warning to shoppers.In the San Diego case, Greenberg and Blizzard found eyewitness and photographic evidence that the same perpetrator had masturbated and ejaculated on another woman in the same store one day before – and returned six days later to assault a third victim. “Dollar Tree did nothing,” they and a team of California lawyers allege in court papers. Dollar Tree did not respond to questions about this accusation.The lawyers saw what they allege to be a similar corporate disregard for shopper safetyin a case they read about in Knoxville, Tennessee, where a Dollar Tree employee named Collin Miller was caught on camera as he masturbated in front of customers in November 2024. “The [security] video shows the defendant with his penis exposed as he walks past the register where the victim is checking out,” a police reportquoted by a local TV stationsaid. “The video also later shows the defendant rubbing his groin area for several minutes at the register while customers are present in the store.”The Pentagon says it’s ‘lethalitymaxxing’. Why has ‘incel’ slang crossed into the mainstream?Read moreAlthough Miller pleaded guilty to a misdemeanor charge of indecent exposure, he was not fired. And then, eight months later, he was accused of following a family around the store with a camera and takingsurreptitious photographsup the 16-year-old daughter’s skirt. Once again, the police investigated and, according to media reports, found 25,000 compromising images of women on his phone. He subsequently pleaded guilty to two counts of unlawful photographing in violation of privacy. Dollar Tree did not respond to questions about the case and Miller’s employment status.Halpin, the sociologist, said he sees an overarching pattern linking the Dollar Tree masturbation incidents, photographs like Miller’s – known as “creepshots” – and the compulsion of some perpetrators to post evidence of their exploits online.“They’re not just doing this for personal gratification,” he said. “They want an audience to engage with it, and they want other men potentially to do it too. When these things are posted, they look at view counts, comments and accolades from other men. And there are other people out there who look and think, ‘I want that attention as well.’”The attention-seeking has a cost. MR said that, two and a half years after her sharking episode, she had found no peace. “I feel like crying,” she said, “and then I get so fucking angry.”Information and support for anyone affected by rape or sexual abuse issues is available from the following organizations. In the US,Rainnoffers support on 800-656-4673. In the UK,Rape Crisisoffers support on 0808 500 2222. In Australia, support is available at1800Respect(1800 737 732). Other international helplines can be found atibiblio.org/rcip/internl.html

title: Sex, beds and grisly executions: what Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey changed, invented – and left out | Movies | The Guardian
description: From missing scenes and characters to a brand new ending, the director has taken some artistic liberties with Homer’s epic poem
Author: Chris Tryhorn
Headline: From missing scenes and characters to a brand new ending, the director has taken some artistic liberties with Homer’s epic poem
Content: A kingdom for a horse … The OdysseyPhotograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreenA kingdom for a horse … The OdysseyPhotograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesMoviesSex, beds and grisly executions: what Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey changed, invented – and left outFrom missing scenes and characters to a brand new ending, the director has taken some artistic liberties with Homer’s epic poemPeter Bradshaw’s five-star reviewanda less enthused classicist’s takeWill women also enjoy Nolan’s Odyssey?Christopher Nolan interviewedWhy shooting on occupied land feels like a betrayalChris TryhornMon 20 Jul 2026 08.17 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.16 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTurning a 12,000-line poem that takes a day to read aloud into a three-hour film was always going to require some pruning. So it has proved withChristopher Nolan’s The Odyssey, which dispenses with a number of scenes and characters from Homer’s ancient Greek epic. The director has also changed the emphasis in some of the story’s most famous episodes and made a few of his own additions. Here we run down the most significant alterations.The Odyssey review – Nolan goes god-tier with breathtaking epic of men, monsters and moral metamorphosisRead moreWhat Nolan has left outThe godsHomer’s Odyssey opens with a discussion between Zeus and Athena on Olympus; with Zeus’s blessing, Athena plays an active role in guiding Odysseus home, supporting him in vanquishing the suitors and offering encouragement to his son, Telemachus, while adopting multiple disguises. Meanwhile, Poseidon frustrates Odysseus’s progress and Hermes is sent on a couple of key missions. The only god Nolan depicts is Athena (Zendaya), who appears as a vision seen only by Odysseus (Matt Damon) and seems to represent his conscience rather than being a fully realised divine entity.Nausicaaand thePhaeaciansOne of the Odyssey’s most celebrated scenes is the meeting between Odysseus and the young princess Nausicaa; he has washed up naked on a strange island where she and her maids are playing with a ball. After they handle this awkward situation with delicacy, Odysseus follows Nausicaa to the city of the Phaeacians, where he recounts his tales of the Cyclops, Circe, Scylla and Charybdis before being given safe passage back to Ithaca. Nolan leaves out the Phaeacians entirely and has Odysseus tell those stories to Calypso (Charlize Theron) as recovered memories.‘At times I felt I’d bitten off more than I could chew’: Christopher Nolan on sweeping the Oscars, making The Odyssey – and getting a puppyRead moreSome major ghostsThe film shows Odysseus meeting his former commander Agamemnon (Benny Safdie) in the realm of the dead (Nolan is correct not to depict it as an underworld). But we don’t see Achilles, the greatest of the Greek warriors, nor Ajax, who in the poem silently rebuffs Odysseus’s attempt at reconciliation. Neither do we meet Odysseus’s mother, whom he tries and fails to embrace three times. Another character omitted is the luckless Elpenor, who plunges to his death after drinking too much and then pleads to be properly buried. It’s easy to see why Nolan wanted to economise on these spiritual encounters, despite their power and resonance in the poem.View image in fullscreenNo pushover … Anne Hathaway as Penelope with Tom Holland as Telemachus.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesA classicist’s verdict on Nolan’s Odyssey: a soulful hero flatters our times as women and nuance pushed overboardRead moreThe execution of the disloyal womenIt comes as no surprise that Nolan shies away from including the Odyssey’s nastiest scene, in which 12 enslaved women who have been disloyal to the household and slept with the suitors are hanged by Telemachus. But Nolan invents a detail along the same lines when he shows one of those treacherous women, Melantho (Mia Goth), effectively condemned to death by Odysseus’s wife, Penelope (Anne Hathaway), who shoves her into the carnage while the suitors are being slaughtered. He spares us the grisly spectacle of what happens in the poem to the odious goatherd Melanthius, who has his nose, ears, hands, feet and genitals chopped off.Penelope’s bed testHomer’s Penelope is no pushover when Odysseus returns, and to satisfy herself that the beggar who has just slaughtered the suitors is, in fact, her husband, she draws him into describing their marital bed, as only he and Penelope know that Odysseus had fashioned it from an olive tree. Nolan omits this detail, which demonstrates how Penelope matches her husband for guile; instead, Odysseus shows her a figurine of Athena to prove his identity.View image in fullscreenAgamemnon, commander of the Greek forces, seen from behind in The OdysseyPhotograph: Collection Christophel/AlamyWhat Nolan has addedThe wooden horseHomer does not tell the full story of the wooden horse, but it is mentioned on three occasions in the the Odyssey (and not at all in the Iliad). Given that using the horse to break into Troy was Odysseus’s idea and crucial to the Trojan saga, it seems appropriate to include it in the film.SinonThe character of Sinon (Elliot Page), who is shown in the film unwittingly tricking the Trojans into accepting the wooden horse, does not appear anywhere in Homer. Nolan is drawing on Virgil’s account of the fall of Troy in the second book of the Aeneid. He also invents the backstory of Sinon being made to take the place ofAntinous (Robert Pattinson)in the Greek army at Troy.The people from the seaThere is much talk in the film about the world of the Greek heroes being threatened by “people from the sea”. This is not a preoccupation of Homer’s Odyssey. Nor does any Homeric character say, as Nolan’s Odysseus does, that the Trojan war was really all about “trading routes”. But these nods to the concerns of ancient historians show Nolan has done his research.What Nolan has changedCalypsoNolan’s Calypso sedates Odysseus with lotus flowers to make him lose his memories, but somehow develops pity for him and lets him go on his way. The Calypso of the poem doesn’t do drugs but is very much into sex (not something we see in the film), forcing Odysseus to share her bed night after night even though he is desperate to get home. She releases him only after Hermes shows up to tell her that Zeus has decided she must do so; as a mere nymph, she knows she must defer to the authority of Olympus. Once it is clear he can leave, Odysseus is able to enjoy a last bit of lovemaking before heading off on his raft.Christopher Nolan’s Odyssey used occupied land as a film set. That feels like a betrayal | Mohamed Sleiman LabatRead moreThe CyclopsNolan’s Cyclops doesn’t speak (much), whereas in the poem he engages in lethally comic banter with Odysseus. The lack of dialogue in the film makes the one-eyed giant scarier, but it means we lose a couple of important details from the poem: the ruse whereby Odysseus identifies himself as Nobody so the other Cyclopes won’t come to the blinded monster’s aid, and the moment when Odysseus recklessly reveals his real name, which the Cyclops can then use to ask his father, Poseidon, to curse Odysseus’s return journey. These changes fit with Nolan’s decision to downplay Odysseus’s abilities as a trickster and foreground his battle-hardened resilience and trauma.View image in fullscreenDirector Christopher Nolan (left) with Matt Damon and Zendaya during the making of the film.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesCirceIt is never explained why Homer’s Circe turns some of Odysseus’s comrades into pigs. She turns them back into men because Odysseus has proved immune to her potion (thanks to a herb Hermes has given him) and then agrees to have sex with her. She proves to be a very helpful ally and he stays with her for a whole year. Nolan’s Circe (Samantha Morton) seems to be motivated by anger towards animalistic male behaviour, but then meekly agrees to restore Odysseus’s men to their human bodies after he intuits that he really shouldn’t touch that stew and makes her feel sorry for a bunch of guys who just want to get home.Chastity, nodding and enormous pores: will women also love Nolan’s Odyssey?Read moreThe ambush and father-son reunionIn the film, a band of villains hired by Antinous attempt to kill Telemachus (Tom Holland) at a temple, only to be thwarted by Odysseus, who has arrived on the scene in disguise. Soon afterwards, Telemachus realises his saviour is his father. In the poem, the ambush is led by Antinous out at sea and is easily (and undramatically) evaded by Telemachus, who then meets his father back in Ithaca in a reunion carefully stage-managed by Athena.The endingHomer’s Odyssey comes to a notoriously unsatisfactory conclusion. Nolan sensibly discards all the poem’s final scenes: the suitors’ arrival in the realm of the dead; Odysseus’s encounter with his father, Laertes, whom he needlessly upsets by adopting yet another fake persona; and the anticlimactic battle between Odysseus and the relatives of the suitors. In the film, Telemachus becomes king of Ithaca, allowing Odysseus and Penelope to sail off into the sunset on a mission to honour his fallen comrades.Chris Tryhorn is the author of Homer’s Odyssey, a new version of the original story now available as an ebookExplore more on these topicsFilmThe OdysseyChristopher NolanHomerFilm adaptationsMatt DamonAnne HathawayfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘We need to talk about how difficult it is’: why one couple are pinning surrogacy hopes on Mexico | Society | The Guardian
description: Jules Von Hep and Mark Beverlo, who have been trying to become parents since 2020, are now seeking help abroad
Author: Rachel Hall
Headline: Jules Von Hep and Mark Beverlo, who have been trying to become parents since 2020, are now seeking help abroad
Content: Jules Von Hep (seated) and his partner, Mark Beverlo.Photograph: Martin Godwin/The GuardianView image in fullscreenJules Von Hep (seated) and his partner, Mark Beverlo.Photograph: Martin Godwin/The GuardianSociety‘We need to talk about how difficult it is’: why one couple are pinning surrogacy hopes on MexicoJules Von Hep and Mark Beverlo, who have been trying to become parents since 2020, are now seeking help abroadRise in overseas surrogates ‘increases risk of stateless babies’Why Mexico is at the heart of ethical debate over global surrogacyRachel HallMon 20 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.25 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleJules Von Hep has wanted a baby ever since he can remember. For many people that dream is straightforward to realise – but, as a gay man, he has always known it would take years of saving and searching.The only way he can have a biological child is to use a surrogate, which costs tens of thousands of pounds, and it often takes years of searching to find the right match. For Von Hep, it has been an “incredibly painful, incredibly expensive” process.“I’ve always known that surrogacy is the option I wanted to go down. That, really, has led to the career choices that I’ve made, how I’ve worked, making sure that I was always saving money in the bank for fertility,” he said.Von Hep and his partner, Mark Beverlo, started trying to become parents in 2020. They signed up with an IVF clinic, bought some donor eggs and submitted themselves to rigorous sperm-testing requirements. They soon found themselves facing rapidly mounting charges, starting from £1,000 for single advisory meetings to £13,000 for a round of embryo creation, including a failed attempt along the way.They then signed up to an agency, where the waiting list for UK-based women volunteering to be surrogates was two to three years. Von Hep had a look at some unregulated Facebook groups, in which prospective parents pitch themselves to potential surrogates. “I just couldn’t bear the idea of pitching our lives, standing there in front of the house with a dog and saying: ‘This is what we’ve got’.”Eventually, the surrogacy agency matched the couple with a woman and the embryo transfers began, at a cost of £5,000 a go. When these didn’t work, they opted to attempt insemination at home, sometimes referred to as “turkey basting”.That approach also failed and Von Hep and his partner decided to give up on surrogacy in the UK. They turned their sights abroad: the most popular destination, Ukraine, was closed off due to the war, leaving the US – too expensive at about £250,000 – Canada andMexico, in which surrogacy was legalised for same-sex parents in 2021.At a cost of around £60-90,000, they were lucky to be able to afford to go to Mexico. Although the costs are similar to the UK, which has a non-commercial altruistic model of surrogacy in which a surrogate’s expenses are paid, some Mexican states take a commercial approach.“The difference doing it commercially is unbelievable. The knowledge that the egg donors are being paid, that everyone is – there’s nothing woolly about the situation,” he said.The cost includes all medical bills and expenses, plus a fee to carry the pregnancy, which the agency they are working with, MySurrogacyJourney Mexico City (MSJMX), said is usually around MXN$388,000 (£16,914).“[Our surrogate] has been a surrogate before, and she is also trying to build a business. I think that’s where commercial surrogacy helped. She was very open about it. She said: ‘Yeah, I’m trying to build my business, and this is a great way of earning extra money.’“For me, that answer was really refreshing. I’m like, OK, perfect. We’re all in this together. Everyone is getting what they need. There’s no murkiness.”The agency the couple is working with is UK-based and helped them find their surrogate, who they met via video call. It’s also queer-founded, which Von Hep felt made it more supportive and sensitive to their circumstances.There are concerns in Mexico that the patchwork of regulation – two states have banned it, while three have laws governing the practice – has left women open to abuse. Researchers have raised concerns that poor women could “rent their wombs as a survival strategy” and there have been reports of women coerced into surrogacy by abusive husbands.MSJMX says that “ensuring the ethical and legal protection of both intended parents and surrogates is one of our highest priorities”, and it only operates in “surrogacy-friendly states” like Mexico City. The agency said it works with “highly experienced, independent human rights specialists and leading surrogacy lawyers in Mexico City who have an exceptional understanding of reproductive legislation and family law”.The couple have shipped their sperm to Mexico, picked a new egg donor and had embryos fertilised. They are now waiting for the transfer to begin.If everything goes to plan, once their baby is born, Von Hep and Beverlo will move to Mexico for around three to six months while they arrange their baby’s passport – another potentially fraught process. On their return to the UK, they will have to apply for a parental order with the family courts to become legal parents.Von Hep and his partner have decided this will be their last attempt to become biological parents. Yet he is feeling tentatively excited that this time it might finally work – so much so he has recommended surrogacy in Mexico to other gay and straight couples he knows who are struggling with fertility.“It feels like the wild west in the UK. The professionalism from the Mexican clinics – I can’t fault it. It’s unbelievable and it costs the same price,” he said.Despite his frustration with the system, he is incredibly grateful to the women who carry other people’s babies. “Anyone who is willing to be a surrogate is like an angel on earth. Whether it’s to help a friend, relative or total stranger, it’s one of the most selfless acts of kindness and love I can think of. We wouldn’t be able to even dream of having children of our own if it wasn’t for the benevolence of surrogates,” he said.But mostly he wants to talk about his surrogacy journey to challenge the taboo, and add his voice to the growing conversation on social media around the difficulties gay men face in becoming parents, from adoption to surrogacy.“I feel like the conversation around gay men having babies, and the money that it costs, and how difficult it is, needs to be talked about so that it’s not as difficult for the generations under me,” he said.“The more same-sex relationships are accepted and embraced within this country, the more gay men are going to want to have a family, because this social norm is shifting to embrace same-sex couples.”Explore more on these topicsSocietyFertility problemsLGBTQ+ rightsChildrenMexicoHealthSurrogacynewsShareReuse this content

title: The one change that worked: I tried the ‘no poo’ method – and it transformed my hair | Life and style | The Guardian
description: When I gave up washing my hair, the first two weeks were the worst. But I was determined to follow my barber’s advice ...
Author: George Francis Lee
Headline: When I gave up washing my hair, the first two weeks were the worst. But I was determined to follow my barber’s advice ...
Content: ‘For better or worse, my hair is usually the first thing someone will notice about me’ … George Francis Lee.Photograph: Sadie MearsView image in fullscreen‘For better or worse, my hair is usually the first thing someone will notice about me’ … George Francis Lee.Photograph: Sadie MearsThe one change that workedLife and styleThe one change that worked: I tried the ‘no poo’ method – and it transformed my hairWhen I gave up washing my hair, the first two weeks were the worst. But I was determined to follow my barber’s advice ...George Francis LeeMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn 2025 I’d not had a paid-for haircut in about five years, preferring to ask my ex to cut it with kitchen scissors in the bathroom. But now I was in my late 20s and single and I decided that it was time to look more grown up. Not knowing the first thing about looking after my hair, I felt that a trip to an establishment that offered something beyond a short back and sides – a trendy, gentrified place where a trim puts you back a cool £38 –was sorely overdue.“You need to stop washing your hair,” my barber told me bluntly. As they ruffled through my shapeless, overgrown mop I was warned about using too much shampoo, which can leave your hair dry, lifeless and coarse. I sat in the swivel chair feeling a bit embarrassed. My barber’s words were less a caution and more a diagnosis. I was instructed to drop shampoo immediately for at least two weeks. The dry variety was fine if I got desperate for a clean and I could maybe use a bit of conditioner as a treat. But for the most part, water was my entire regime.To my mum’s delight and my own teenage dismay, I have thick, ginger hair. Years of heckling and having a name that starts with the letter G have made me and my gingerness inseparable. For better or worse, my hair is usually the first thing someone will notice about me. I wanted to take some pride in my hair, and the knowledge that I’d unwittingly been sapping the life out of it was jarring.Like most people, I found a daily squirt of shampoo to be as much of a ritual as brushing my teeth. It felt counterintuitive, then, to forgo it completely.The first two weeks were the worst. Advocates of the “no poo” method – ditching shampoo for water or homemade concoctions due to claims that commercial products cause repeated, unnecessary damage to your locks – often tell you that it gets worse before it gets better. There is a sort of dirty threshold you have to pass before reaping the benefits of poo-less hair, they say.View image in fullscreen‘I felt as if my hair was finding a natural balance’ … Lee.Photograph: Niamh MarlowThis was harder than I first imagined. I would step into the shower, dunk my head under the cascading water and immediately reach for the bottle without a second thought. A glob of apple-scented shampoo would ooze on to my scalp and then, suddenly realising what I’d done, I’d have to scrape it off. A few days in and my head was crying out for a lather. But I was determined to follow my barber’s advice.I carried on, counting the days until I could wash my hair. But slowly I felt no need. Two weeks became three. Three became four. Eventually, I lost track. “I can’t remember the last time I washed my hair,” I said to my best friend, who thought it sounded a bit gross. But my hair had never felt better. It was wavier, bouncier, glossier. The dirty, itchy feeling that had lingered during the first fortnight disappeared, and suddenly I felt as if my hair was finding a natural balance after years of endless shampooing. Until then, I hadn’t realised just how dry my hair had been.My scalp used to feel irritated, but it was one of those minor issues we all tend to leave unspoken unless they snowball into something more troubling. Shampoo seemed to have been stripping away the natural oils that were supposed to moisturise and protect my hair.Obviously, everyone’s hair is different. I’m told people with fine or greasy hair don’t last long without needing to shampoo. But for people with my hair type, it’s really not necessary.More than a year later, my hair is shinier and curlier than ever. A wash at my quarterly haircut does me just fine, and I could probably go even longer. The apple-scented shampoo I bought a year ago remains untouched on my bathroom shelf.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsLife and styleThe one change that workedMen's hairBeautyHealth & wellbeingHygienefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The rave secret: why middle-aged women are larging it – and seeing big health benefits | Women | The Guardian
description: For those who are burned out, perimenopausal, or both, dancing all night can be a panacea. It’s great exercise, lowers stress, boosts energy and provides connection. No wonder so many are doing it
Author: Amy Fleming
Headline: For those who are burned out, perimenopausal, or both, dancing all night can be a panacea. It’s great exercise, lowers stress, boosts energy and provides connection. No wonder so many are doing it
Content: ‘There’s something about it being completely separate from parenting and work that feels really special.’Photograph: Posed by models; Dan Dalton/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘There’s something about it being completely separate from parenting and work that feels really special.’Photograph: Posed by models; Dan Dalton/Getty ImagesWomenThe rave secret: why middle-aged women are larging it – and seeing big health benefitsFor those who are burned out, perimenopausal, or both, dancing all night can be a panacea. It’s great exercise, lowers stress, boosts energy and provides connection. No wonder so many are doing itAmy FlemingMon 20 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.27 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIt’s 4am and I’m in a club – the first time I’ve done this in years. I’m 51 and in my sandwich era, meaning I’ve got teenage and elderly loved ones on my mind – a situation that generally calls for early nights. But when you’re responsibilities-maxxing, sometimes you need the opposite. A blowout. A huge, transcendent, resetting release. A rave.I don’t know what took me so long to get around to it. Misplaced parental guilt? Internalised ageism? Exhaustion? It’s not as though I didn’t know other gen-Xers who have been regularly getting blissfully – ceremonially, even – lost in house, techno or drum’n’bass, and they’re not alone.A 2025 studyby the University of Leeds went viral: “Women aged 40-65 are turning to raves for real mental health,” said one of many Instagram posts and press headlines. “Why many women over 40 still go to raves,” read another. But surely the question should be: why the hell wouldn’t they?Many of those who do never actually stopped. Of the 136 women surveyed for the study, “82% had been attending for more than 20 years”, says one of its authors, Alinka Greasley, professor of music psychology at the University of Leeds. “I’m a DJ myself,” she says. “I still go out raving and I’ve been doing so for 26 years.”View image in fullscreen‘Going out on the dancefloor is healing.’Photograph: Makl.N/Getty ImagesBut others like me had lapsed, only to return when the need became borderline desperate. There is a small amount of psychological literature looking at why clubbers stop as they get older, with women citing family responsibilities, says Greasley, “or saying: ‘I feel that women of my age shouldn’t be out doing those activities.’” Joanna, 50, started clubbing again six years ago and now goes every few months with old friends. “There’s something about it being completely separate from parenting and work that feels really special,” she says. She realised she had been missing nightlife when her children were very little. “And I love club culture,” she says.When going raving was first mooted by her friend, Joanna’s internalised ageism reared its head. “I was so resistant, I was almost repulsed by the idea of a woman in her 40s going clubbing – and possibly taking drugs.” This unease at entering spaces that are traditionally associated with youth culture is not unusual. Greasley says that before she and her colleague Alice O’Grady did the study, women O’Grady had previously interviewed “had experienced comments on the dancefloor such as: ‘Whose mum are you?’” And while some said they dressed up for self expression, many dressed down to escape attention in a sea of 18- to 25-year-olds. Other comments included people saying such things as: “Oh, can I take a photo of you?” or: “Wow, I hope I’m still clubbing when I’m your age.” But, says Greasley, “negative attitudes were not a defining part of their participation”. Quite the contrary.The music itself and dancing were the top draws for the women, followed by socialising with friends. But, “overwhelmingly”, says Greasley, “they were reporting that they did feel a really strong sense of belonging. There’s a strong theme of age being irrelevant, they talk about making friends with people in different generations – predominantly they’re going to spend time with their own friends but also get something from being there, around other people.” A couple of women even said they go with their children “and feel at home”.View image in fullscreen‘When you go into an inclusive dance space, you can enter a liminal space where time dissolves and everybody is treated as an equal.’Photograph: Posed by model; FujiCraft/Getty ImagesRachel Giddings, a psychotherapist whoworks with (mostly EDM) artistson their wellbeing and uses psychology to hone their performances, says that while “women often feel lost in society at a certain age, when you go into an inclusive dance space, you can enter a liminal space where time dissolves and everybody is treated as an equal. Doesn’t matter if you’re a CEO, a road sweeper, a woman or a man, if you’re 25 or 55, you can access that space, and that gives you a sense of belonging.”Joanna says she did feel conspicuously “‘a mum’ at first, and like: ‘Oh, I won’t know how to dance any more.’ But after I had been once, that melted away. There are a lot of young girls out, but you see other people in their 30s, 40s, 50s and actually begin to stop noticing.” On one early outing, she saw a couple in the queue who looked as if they could be in their 60s. “She was wearing a tweed suit – it was almost as if they were in fancy dress, like: ‘Hi, we’re the old couple,’ and I saw them dancing later on, so that made me feel a lot better.” In any case, she says, clubbing now isn’t about trying to get back to her younger self. “I’m seeing where it’s at these days, and I quite like it. It’s something that you can dip in and dip out of.”My big night out was in a vast club filled with mostly young people, but all the eye contact from strangers was warm, inclusive and full of shared wonder. To be fair, pretty much everyone seemed to be high or drunk or both, but as Giddings points out – with no judgment either way – you can reach that awed, transcendental state without drugs. “These psychological states are absolutely accessible without substances,” she says. When she posts on this topic on social media, she says, “there are thousands of comments where people say: ‘Never had a drug in my life but I feel these feelings,’ or: ‘I’ve done drugs and don’t any more, and feel these feelings.’”View image in fullscreen‘People moving together increases social connection and bonding.’Photograph: Posed by models; FG Trade Latin/Getty ImagesGiddings says part of her mission is to redefine society’s perception of rave culture as naughty or illicit. “When you are going out on the dancefloor, you are doing it because it’s healing, because it’s connective.” People often say clubbing is escapist, “but actually, the reframe is: it’s self-regulation together. We say: ‘I’m escaping from my normal life,’ but we’re actually seeking togetherness, identity, belonging.”This raving-for-wellbeing narrative, as Giddings points out, also appeals to gen Z – an unprecedentedly health-conscious youth demographic. “At age 14, they’re in my gym on the treadmill next to me,” she says. Even on some fundamental levels, clubbing now is more clean-cut than it was in the 90s. Joanna says when she first started going again, “I was just stunned by needing a photo ID, andthe air-con – no sweat dripping from the ceilings. And no one’s smoking [inside], obviously, so your hair and clothes don’t stink.”Dancing all night undeniably counts as exercise and comes with added mental-health benefits that can be especially helpful when you’re middle-aged, burned out or perimenopausal. Greasley says these include “feeling alive, feeling energised, both at the time and afterwards. And then also a lot of women report that the ability to go out and enjoy nights and to dance for hours has a positive impact on lowering anxiety and depression and low-mood states.” I have often likened the mood swings that come with perimenopause to the “hormonal” moods of adolescence – perhaps for women it’s both of these times that we seek a release the most.View image in fullscreen‘Women report that dancing for hours has a positive impact on lowering anxiety.’Photograph: Maskot/Getty ImagesDancing with others is particularly medicinal. As Greasley says: “People moving together increases social connection and bonding, and that entrainment with other people I think is particularly the case for beat-based music. There is research showing that this physical movement entrainment [when the body synchronises its movements to an external rhythm]leads to feelings of pleasure and social connectedness.” EDM is structured to elicit sweet anticipation building to huge group highs. “You get buildups, you get breaks – the moment where you know that the beat might come back in after a prolonged period – and everybody starts moving together, and that’s what creates these highly pleasurable embodied experiences, and that can create these really powerful emotional experiences and chills within the body.”“The group becomes one organism,” says Giddings, “so we start to sync into each other’s nervous systems and when you’re waiting for the beat, your heart rate starts to rise, and then boom, it’s that somatic release of energy and we can release that stress we brought with us that we don’t want any more.”It is also an opportunity for women, she says, “to reclaim their identity outside ‘I’m a carer’, ‘I’m a parent’, ‘I’m a worker’ – I’m not just one dimension but multifaceted and this amazing part of me can be spontaneous and free and connected”.For those who are put off by late nights, says Greasley, “all sorts of nights are cropping up, targeted at older people. Here in Leeds, I’ve noticed that there are an increasing number of raves starting at 6pm and finishing at midnight. I love going to them myself. You can dance for hours and still be in bed by midnight or 1am.”This suits the OG acts and DJs who have also got older. EDM clubbing started in the 80s, so, says Greasley, “while it still remains a youth culture, the culture as a whole has aged”. In November, Leftfield performed at an event with the promoters Bugged Out! at Drumsheds – a 15,000-capacity former Ikea store in Edmonton, north London – which ran from 1pm until 10.30pm.But Joanna says she loves her occasional late nights. “Going out for me, and staying up until 3am or 4am – when I tell some people that, they’re horrified but I find it really energising. Obviously, not all the time, or if you’ve got full-on childcare the next day.”It took me days to recover from my late night. It was gone 5am by the time I got into bed, ears ringing, body clock thinking it was nearly time to wake up. I slept terribly, but I felt revived in other ways. Being at the rave didn’t make me feel old. I felt as if I’d come home. After all, my generation invented this.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsWomenMiddle agePsychologyClubbingElectronic musicMental healthfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: A big red speaker, Louis Vuitton trophy trunks and a tattoo bet – how Spain celebrated World Cup win | Spain | The Guardian
description: Luis de la Fuente’s team was the best at the tournament by some margin but are not satisfied yet with focus now on ‘the next game in September’
Author: Sid Lowe
Headline: Luis de la Fuente’s team was the best at the tournament by some margin but are not satisfied yet with focus now on ‘the next game in September’
Content: Gavi, Lamine Yamal and Pedri of Spain with the World Cup trophy after victory against Argentina.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenGavi, Lamine Yamal and Pedri of Spain with the World Cup trophy after victory against Argentina.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesSpainA big red speaker, Louis Vuitton trophy trunks and a tattoo bet – how Spain celebrated World Cup winLuis de la Fuente’s team was the best at the tournament by some margin but are not satisfied yet with focus now on ‘the next game in September’Sid Lowein New YorkMon 20 Jul 2026 15.05 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOn the night that Spain won their first World Cup in 2010, Rodrigo Hernández was on camp somewhere lost in the woods of Connecticut. He had been canoeing when theselecciónbeat Germany in the semi-final, upset to have missed it, and for the final he convinced one of the supervisors to let him watch, alone, on a mini-laptop in the hut where the staff slept. When Andrés Iniesta scored thewinning goalin the 116th minute, he ran off screaming, doing laps of the lake. He was 14.On the night that Spain won their second World Cup 16 years later,Rodrigo Hernández was back in the US, not so far from there. WhenFerran Torres scoredthe winning goal in the 106th minute, he ran off screaming in a straighter line and accompanied this time: towards the corner where his otherfamilywere. And then, when it ended, when the fighting finished too, he collected the trophy, pausing just long enough for Donald Trump to leave centre stage before lifting it, his timing perfect even here.It wasn’t the only trophy he had, but those could be handed over and this could not.By the time Rodri departed at 9.38pm eastern time, the last man out of the dressing room at the New Jersey New York Stadium, he couldn’t carry them all: walking with him, just ahead, Ladis had to lend a hand. A football team is never just the players, nor even just the coaches. When he departed the dressing room nine minutes earlier, the same happened for Pau Cubarsí. Nuria helped him, their boy: just 19, looking like a kid down here in the stadium in real life but not on the pitch where he’s a leader. Luis de la Fuente was accompanied by Paloma.Rodri came with the trophy for the tournament’s best player too. Cubarsí had the young player award,Unai Simón the Golden Glove. They also had two World Cups, every Spain player did: a replica of their own in a black Louis Vuitton trophy trunk, not easy to fit under an arm, and another in Lego. Rodri had three: both of those andthecup. They departed laden down, great big plastic bags full, pieces of the history they made. Shirts, in red and yellow and blue and white. Balls, boots. They had cut the net and taken pieces of that too. They had been given rings, NBA style.View image in fullscreenMikel Merino holds a replica of the trophy in the dressing room after Spain’s triumph.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesBefore the final began, Marc Cucurella had sneaked off towards the touchline and buried a coin in the pitch, like that other left-back Joan Capdevila had done in 2010. Iniesta had been out there, his name chanted, as he showed the cup to the stands. After it, Sergio Ramos brought it back again. Dashing out, excitedly kissing it, he placed it on the plinth for this new generation, achieving the impossible in emulating them, to go and collect. One by one they passed, Borja Iglesias and Lamine Yamal among the most evasive in shaking Trump’s hand, until Rodri took the trophy and lifted it to the sky.Nico Williams went to find his mum in the front row and handed over his medal. Lamine Yamal had made his way across to Lionel Messi, who was sitting but who stood for him, embracingthe boy in the bath, the successor: a rite of passage, an image that will be for ever. The actor Javier Bardem was hugging Iglesias, his new friend and idol. Ferran Torres was talking about a “great liberation”: he had suffered, felt how cruel this game could be, how people could be too, but he said: “Destiny is written: God gives to those who deserve.” AndKeyne was, well, Keyne. In the dressing room – the same one, Cucurella noted, where he had been at last year’s Club World Cup – the champagne was cracked open.It was brief – the flight toSpainwas scheduled for 11pm and they wanted to get back – and soon they were on their way again. Behind the screen, along the corridor they came, way down in the depths and the half-light of the stadium. The president, the staff, all the players; the many, many people who made this so. Iglesias carried a bag so big it was like he was a student taking his washing home, and he was struggling to stop that box slipping. In the other hand he wheeled a big red speaker. Now playing: Dirty Cash (Money Talks) by PAWSA & Adventures of Stevie V. Lamine Yamal came, via the anti-doping room, in orange sunglasses, four chains, black cap on backwards. He didn’t wear his medal, but most did, thick discs round their necks. And commemorative shirts, ordered up in case.View image in fullscreenSpain’s Lamine Yamal and Argentina’s Lionel Messi embrace after the match.Photograph: Jeenah Moon/ReutersBack in Dallas, at a celebrationafter the semi-final victory, someone had joked to the federation president, Rafael Louzán, that the shirts should say world champions on one side and Finalissima winners on the other, two trophies in one. Tempting, but these bore just the one trophy, “Spain 26” on the back. The war in the Middle East had prevented the Finalissima from going ahead in Doha as scheduled in March and every time another option was offered, Argentina said no, the impression that of a team, a federation, running scared. “Good job we didn’t play,” Emi Martínez had said then, after they scraped past Mauritania in a hastily arranged friendly that filled the hole. “If we had played [Spain] like this …”Something like this might have happened. Not just because Argentina played like this but because Spain made them play like this. “We gave them a battering,” came the verdict from one of those heading out of the dressing room. This wasn’t just a win; this was more. Has a 1-0 ever been as dominant? There was a moment late in the game, still at 0-0, when the Spain fans chanted: “Look at them! Look at them! How they shit themselves!” and they weren’t wrong. Argentina could not get out all game; as Spain turned the screw, still less, the goal seemingly inevitable; the surprise was that it hadn’t come yet, that there would be just one. When it did it came as a liberation but also an act of irresistible logic.View image in fullscreenFerran Torres scores the only goal of the World Cup final.Photograph: Jewel Samad/AFP/Getty ImagesTorres’s shot was Spain’s 20th. Argentina didn’t manage one until the 115th minute. They didn’t manage any on target, all game. Across 900World Cupgames for which Opta has the stats, it is only the third time that a team has failed to shoot. And this in the final. They had done to Argentina what they did to France and to everyone, choking them. For the whole tournament Spain had an xG against of 0.3 per game. “If my gloves could talk, they would say they didn’t have much work to do,” Simón said. “Many teams depend too much on the back line,” Aymeric Laporte said, but not Spain.This was thetriumph of a collective, of a group; it ended in New York, but it was built in Chattanooga and beyond, over 51 days. It is the triumph of an idea, a conviction and of a coach too: a victory for De la Fuente and the system he leads and the system that made him. He began his coaching career at Deportivo Alavés in the third tier, where he lasted just 11 games. It was November 2011 when he left and he has not had a club job since. For months, no one returned his calls. He was on the dole for a year and a half until he saw an advert for a job: the Spanish federation was looking for a coach for the youth system. He started in the under-17s. He won the European Championship with the under-19s and the under-21s. Then he won it with the senior side; now he has won the World Cup.“They asked me what there is left to win; we said the next game in September because they’re insatiable,” he insisted afterwards. Simón said: “The manager is outstanding; he has been our leader.” Cucurella, meanwhile, was in trouble. He had promised that if Spain won the World Cup, he would get a tattoo of De la Fuente’s face and now he had to decide where to put it to minimise the damage, the flight home an opportunity to think. Not least to think what a fool he had been.View image in fullscreenMarc Cucurella is to have a tattoo of Luis de la Fuente’s face after Spain’s win.Photograph: Lisi Niesner/Reuters“I said: ‘You made a bad mistake’,” the coach joked. “But it’s not like I am so horrendously ugly that you have to put it somewhere it can’t be seen. And they are men of their word.” For all the laughs, that final line really mattered, and he knows: 10 of them played with him at junior level. The first title he ever won was the Under-19 Euros in 2015. In the middle of his midfield that day was Rodrigo Hernández.Eleven years on, 16 since he watched Spain’s first World Cup win, motivation sought and identification found in what they achieved, Rodri was back in the US. The embodiment of that idea, a player who approached perfection here, no one played more passes nor made more tackles than him. No one had his weight either, the captain leading Spain out and leading them back too. Like so many of his teammates at a tournament that was an expression of justice and vindication too, he had suffered for this,a torn cruciate ligament overcome.“It doesn’t matter if you fall, there’s always a reason to get up again,” he said and now he had it right here in his hands. It was 9.40pm when he boarded the bus bound for the airport, carrying the actual World Cup.Explore more on these topicsSpainWorld Cup 2026World CupfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Argentina players face bans as Fifa opens investigation into World Cup final brawls | Argentina | The Guardian
description: Leandro Paredes was shown a red card after the final whistle and Fifa said it was looking into the Falklands banner on the pitch after England v Argentina
Author: Matt Hughes
