Headline: Leandro Paredes was shown a red card after the final whistle and Fifa said it was looking into the Falklands banner on the pitch after England v Argentina
Content: Gavi is attacked by Thiago Almada and Leandro Paredes of Argentina after the World Cup final.Photograph: Michael Zemanek/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenGavi is attacked by Thiago Almada and Leandro Paredes of Argentina after the World Cup final.Photograph: Michael Zemanek/ShutterstockArgentinaArgentina players face bans as Fifa opens investigation into World Cup final brawlsLeandro Paredes was shown red card after final whistleFifa looking into Falklands banner at England gameMatt Hughesin New YorkMon 20 Jul 2026 16.42 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 17.44 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFifa has opened an investigation into the violent scenes that followed Spain’sWorld Cupfinal win on Sunday, with several Argentina players facing bans.Nahuel Molina appeared to strike Spain’s captain Rodri in the stomach as he ran on to the pitch following the final whistle, while there was also a large melee on the halfway line, where Leandro Paredes appeared to grab Eric García by the throat before throwing Gavi to the ground.Gangs of New Jersey: how the World Cup final descended into ugly scenes between Argentina and SpainRead moreParades was subsequently shown a red card by the referee, Slavko Vincic, who had also sent off Enzo Fernández towards the end of normal time, as well as booking three otherArgentinaplayers and the head coach, Lionel Scaloni.“Following an assessment of the relevant match reports for the Fifa World Cup final between Spain and Argentina, and in line with article 36 of the Fifa Disciplinary Code (FDC), the Fifa Disciplinary Committee has appointed a Disciplinary and Ethics Prosecutor to investigate potential breaches of the FDC with regard to the post-match incidents,” Fifa said in a statement.Fifa is also investigating Argentina’s conduct after their semi-final win over England, when their players celebrated with a banner proclaiming “Las Malvinas son Argentinas”, which was smuggled into the Atlanta Stadium by supporters.Fifa prohibits players or teams making political statements, so those involved could be banned.Explore more on these topicsArgentinaWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainnewsShareReuse this content

title: The 2026 World Cup was full of spectacle, scandal and compromise | Soccer | The Guardian
description: This was a good rather than great World Cup on the field. In the background, things were a lot less edifying
Author: Jonathan Wilson
Headline: This was a good rather than great World Cup on the field. In the background, things were a lot less edifying
Content: Folarin Balogun, Leandro Paredes, Gavi, Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino all played roles in the tournament.Composite: Getty Images, Guardian designView image in fullscreenFolarin Balogun, Leandro Paredes, Gavi, Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino all played roles in the tournament.Composite: Getty Images, Guardian designSoccer with Jonathan WilsonSoccerThe 2026 World Cup was full of spectacle, scandal and compromiseThis was a good rather than great World Cup on the field. In the background, things were a lot less edifyingJonathan WilsonMon 20 Jul 2026 12.45 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 12.47 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePerhaps it’s too early, with the shreds of gold tape still fluttering through the New Jersey sky, to make a proper assessment. That will only come when everything has sunk in properly, when memories have had the chance to settle and shape themselves, when the consequences of certain events have become clear. But let’s try anyway to make some sort of assessment of where the World Cup that ended withLeandro Paredes grappling with Gaviranks historically.The Balogun Affaircould yet have major consequences. At the moment, it feels that the world has moved on, but that’s the nature of tournaments. They have a momentum of their own, a drive towards the finish that tends to sweep everything else aside. But there can be little doubt that the bending before political pressure, sweeping aside 64 years of precedent to ensure a particular player could take the stage, just as Gianni Infantinohad done with Cristiano Ronaldo, just as he had done in gerrymandering the qualification criteria for the Club World Cup to ensure Lionel Messi played, represents a milestone.‘Beyond reproach’: Argentinian media praises Scaloni’s team despite World Cup final defeatRead morePerhaps in the future we will simply accept the principle that famous players get dispensation because making money is more important than justice or the good of the game – the prioritisation of revenue generation has been a feature of the 2026 tournament – or perhaps this will stiffen the sinews of opposition to Infantino; it’s been notable, for instance, how many British politicians – and some American ones – have taken to criticising Fifa and its president. An immediate consequence could be the expansion of theWorld Cupto 64 teams as Infantino looks to shore up his base, particularly given the damage US (and Canadian) immigration policy has done to his claims to be a champion of the global south.Other issues that have sunk into the mulch of games will surely resurface.The treatment of Iran, for instance, was scandalous, and sets a worrying precedent. Ticket prices may be lower at the 2030 World Cup, but the idea that a reasonable proportion should be available to the regular fans who go to league football every week is now gone. The World Cup is no longer a mass movement but a luxury event.The price paid by the host cities, similarly, will only become truly apparent in retrospect. That is financial, but it’s also social. In the US and Canada, there have beenstreet sweeps of the homeless, people with very little deprived of what few possessions they have, ID and medication. In Mexico, there has been an outcry about water extraction and environmental damage linked to World Cup projects.The Italian newspaper la Gazzetta dello Sport ran a double page on Saturday calling the World Cup a “disaster”, outlining certain major issues: ticket prices, Balogun,the weather, the increase in games, the decrease of rest. That may be an overstatement, but what followed really was a disaster as the newspaper found its ticket allocation for the final slashed from five to two. It’s not entirely clear whether the two were linked, but the idea that a media outlet could be denied access for rejecting the official narrative that this World Cup has been the greatest ever is dystopian.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice weeklySign up toSoccer Desk: World Cup EditionJonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDonald Trump has to let it linger as he hangs on for Spain’s World Cup celebrationsRead moreThat fitted the generally contemptuous attitude of Fifa towards the media at this tournament. While some venues – notably Houston and Atlanta – went out of their way to be welcoming, basic organisation and facilities were the worst for at least six World Cups. Quite understandably, nobody in the real world cares about journalists and whether the wifi works, but it’s indicative of a more general approach: fans are revenue units to be milked mercilessly; the media an irritating imposition.The football, meanwhile, was good but not great. There were a number of fine and dramatic games – Brazil v Japan, Morocco v the Netherlands, England v Mexico,Argentinav Cape Verde, Norway v Côte d’Ivoire, Belgium v Senegal, Norway v Brazil, Argentina v Egypt – but unfortunately almost all of them were in the last 32 or last 16. The quarter-finals were not especially inspiring. Argentina’s win over England in the semi was thrilling and involved a collapse that will be discussed for years, but Spain’s win over France was almost too easy; Spain quickly established a degree of superiority over the French that was never really challenged.And the final was dismal, an awful affair. The right team won, in their familiar bloodless way, but the grim spectacle was a direct consequence of the indulgence shown Argentina earlier in the tournament. The ending, inevitably, colours what went before, and drags this tournament behind 2022 and 2018, let alone the glorious World Cups of the 1980s or 1998. Posterity can judge where exactly in the middle rank it sits, whether above or below 2014 and 2006. It was not great but it was good and, beyond the Fifa bluster, that’s fine.This is an extract from Soccer Desk, a newsletter from the Guardian.Subscribe for free here.Explore more on these topicsFootballSoccer with Jonathan WilsonWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainArgentinanewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Tuchel is clinging to an outdated English style – he must let players take risks | England | The Guardian
description: Head coach should stop relying on a physical approach that has rarely worked and embrace England’s gifted ball players
Author: Jacob Steinberg
Headline: Head coach should stop relying on a physical approach that has rarely worked and embrace England’s gifted ball players
Content: Thomas Tuchel was hired by the FA to make the difference in the big games.Photograph: Martin Rickett/PAView image in fullscreenThomas Tuchel was hired by the FA to make the difference in the big games.Photograph: Martin Rickett/PAEnglandAnalysisTuchel is clinging to an outdated English style – he must let players take risksJacob Steinbergin MiamiHead coach should ditch a physical approach that has rarely worked and embrace England’s gifted ball playersMon 20 Jul 2026 09.55 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 15.14 EDTShareOnly England can claim the bronze medal at the World Cup by beating France ina 10-goal thrillerand end up even more annoyed with the manager. Yet emotions are still running high after the collapse against Argentina last week and it is going to take more than winning a game low on jeopardy for the criticism of Thomas Tuchel to die down.Hiring Tuchel was centred around the promise that he would make the difference in the big games. When that fell down, all that was left was anger. It exposed the core emptiness of the appointment, of a rich federation covering up the flaws in its coaching development by trying to game the system by throwing money at an expensive outsider.Top 30: from half a million World Cup photos, why I love these the mostRead moreWhat is the Football Association’s vision? The initial idea was Tuchel providing a short, sharp shock and his tactical acumen helping England to get over the line. Then came a change after a successful qualifying campaign against modest opposition. The FA moved the goalposts by handing Tuchel a contract extension, and suddenly winning theWorld Cupdid not seem quite as pressing.Yet the idea of Tuchel as a project builder seemed far-fetched during the bitter recriminations after thedefeat by Argentina. Criticised for his negative substitutions when England led, the German talked dismissively of English DNA, lamenting the lack of care in possession. Fair enough, you thought, because this has long been a weakness – only, hadn’t Tuchel caused some of the damage by leaving out most of the ball players from his squad? Would it have been different if Adam Wharton, Trent Alexander-Arnold and Cole Palmer had been around in Atlanta? Maybe not, but a lot of money has been spent on developing a different type of English player in recent decades.Perhaps Tuchel was thinking of an old English DNA. But maybe this is part of the issue with employing a foreign coach whose focus is on winning by any means possible. This is the third timeEnglandhave been down this road. There is an idea of the manager from abroad as a sophisticate. But Sven-Göran Eriksson was not as tactically adroit as Glenn Hoddle. Eriksson played a traditional 4-4-2 whereas Hoddle was flexible with his formations.Tuchel, too, has struggled to build an identity. He seems taken with an outdated English style. Tuchel wanted to play like a Premier League team, with pace and physicality, but that has rarely worked for England. They tend to be outpassed in big knockout games, which is why there has been such focus in youth football in developing players more at ease with the dominant Spanish passing style.View image in fullscreenEngland players look dejected after the semi-final defeat by Argentina.Photograph: Paul Childs/ReutersSpain have justwon another World Cup, their method too much for Argentina in a dour final, and for those watching from afar a big takeaway will surely be that the last two teams standing were led by homegrown coaches.Luis de la Fuente and Lionel Scaloni have risen through the ranks and are part of a system. They have a feeling for their countries, a sense of the identity. Tuchel has been inserted in the hope he can cover up the FA’s failure to produce its own coaches. No male team led by a foreign manager has secured the World Cup and it should worry the FA when bringing in an outsider with a win-now mentality ends with the senior side using a very different style of play to younger age groups.There is a view within youth coaching circles that Tuchel has taken England backwards at this tournament. The under-21s have won the past two European Championships by being brave on the ball. They have beaten Spain in consecutive tournaments and the demand from Lee Carsley is that his players take risks in possession. Tuchel finished the semi-final by lobbing balls towards Dan Burn. He played a pragmatic style and his England ended up unable to throw caution to the wind. This World Cup underlines that turning to celebrated coaches from the club game is not a recipe for success.Brazil appointed Carlo Ancelotti but went out in the last 16. Roberto Martínez failed with Portugal after sticking with Cristiano Ronaldo. Mauricio Pochettino oversaw the USA sidebeing humiliated by Belgium.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionScaloni came through the national setup before winning the World Cup with Argentina in 2022. Similarly De la Fuente won the European Under-19 Championship with Spain in 2015 and the European Under-21 Championship in 2019. He has brought players through and, having earned a promotion, won the senior European title in 2024.View image in fullscreenLuis de la Fuente was a successful age-group manager before taking over Spain’s senior side and leading them to glory.Photograph: Franck Fife/AFP/Getty ImagesSpain show smooth progression. Players who beat Argentina in the final played youth football for De la Fuente. This is not to say that England’s next step should be to appoint Carsley, who did not seem as if he had the temperament for the senior job when he filled in as caretaker between Gareth Southgate’s departure and Tuchel’s arrival. Even so, Carsley is closer to the Spanish style and has shown it is possible for England teams to be risk-takers.It is food for thought for the FA as it reviews England’s World Cup. As it stands, Tuchel will get another tournament. There are bridges to build but finishing third is not a sackable offence. In any case there is no other idea at the FA. Tuchel was not produced, he was bought. Hopefully he adapts during the Nations League. There should be a shift away from this physical style. But do not be surprised if the hired gun’s views on English DNA end up making him sound out of touch. It would not happen to Spain. De la Fuente knows their identity. He is part of it.Explore more on these topicsEnglandThomas TuchelThe FAWorld Cup 2026World CupanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Top 30: from half a million World Cup photos, why I love these the most | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Our picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral, that delicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in Gaza
Author: Jonny Weeks
Headline: Our picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral, that delicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in Gaza
Content: Harry Kane is embraced by Jude Bellingham after his first goal against DR Congo.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenHarry Kane is embraced by Jude Bellingham after his first goal against DR Congo.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Top 30: from half a million World Cup photos, why I love these the mostOur picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral,thatdelicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in GazaJonny WeeksMon 20 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.34 EDTShareWhat makes a greatWorld Cupphotograph? Now there’s a question with myriad answers. Composition, newsworthiness, emotional resonance, expert timing and luck can all contribute to varying degrees. But one thing they all share is the power to capture attention.On the Guardian sport picture desk, we have received more than 500,000 World Cup photographs over the past six weeks, with so many gems among them. Here, with much applause to the photographers who created them and a slight bias towardsEngland, are my favourite images of the tournament.1Messi’s cathedralView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi prepares to take a corner kick against Switzerland at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Julian Finney/Fifa/Getty ImagesAn absolute cracker to start with. In this picture, the cavernous Kansas City Stadium sweeps around a small corner of grass on which the world’s most famous footballer stands alone. You can’t really see it’s Lionel Messi, but somehow you can tell it’s him. It feels as though everybody’s eyes are pointed in his direction, like they’re worshipping a deity in an epic footballing cathedral.2Goalline scrambleView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Endrick flings his boot at a loose ball as Japan’s players scramble to clear their lines.Photograph: Annegret Hilse/ReutersTechnological improvements have led to the creation of high-quality miniature cameras which can be placed inside goalmouths and operated remotely. Seeing the game at such close quarters feels wonderfully dynamic. This frame in particular captures a mad scramble between Brazil and Japan; the wonky angle adds to the sense of chaos.3Tuchel’s close upView image in fullscreenEngland’s manager, Thomas Tuchel, during the national anthem before the match with Croatia at Dallas Stadium.Photograph: Bradley Collyer/PAA self-referential choice, but this was a big news story at the time: England played Croatia in their first game and the England manager, Thomas Tuchel, was so furious he couldn’t see the players during the national anthem that he asked Fifa to move the photographers for subsequent games. In fairness to him, you can see why. Four weeks later, Tuchel would garner headlines again after his substitutions in the semi-final againstArgentinatriggered England’s collapse.4Balogun sees redView image in fullscreenThe USA’s Folarin Balogun fouls Tarik Muharemovic of Bosnia and Herzegovina, leading to a red card after a VAR check.Photograph: Michael Steele/Getty ImagesAt first glance it looks innocuous enough, then you notice Tarik Muharemovic’s right ankle buckling under the pressure applied by Folarin Balogun’s mistimed challenge. Replays of the incident suggested the foul was unintentional, though the Bosnian was lucky to escape injury. Balogun received a red card following a VAR review, but his one-game ban was suspended by Fifa after lobbying from Donald Trump. With Balogun back in the team, the USA were dumped out of their own tournament by Belgium in the next round.5Tartan traumaView image in fullscreenScotland’s Findlay Curtis (left) and Jack Hendry in the tunnel after their defeat by Brazil in Miami.Photograph: Carmen Mandato/Fifa/Getty ImagesI’ve relished seeing so many photographsfrom inside the tunnels at this World Cupbecause the players seem to reveal a little more of themselves away from the public eye. This candid shot of Scotland players Findlay Curtis and Jack Hendry shows them in the wake of their 3-0 loss to Brazil – a defeat that was filled with calamity, and which ultimately led to Scotland’s early exit. They look like they’re outside a head teacher’s office waiting to be reprimanded.6Diving headerView image in fullscreenEngland’s midfielder Jude Bellingham scores his team’s first goal against Mexico at the Azteca.Photograph: Ulises Ruiz/AFP/Getty ImagesI love a good diving header and this one by Jude Bellingham against Mexico was peachy. From a photographic point of view, you seldom get shots from tribune positions like this during a Premier League season, so they have a real novelty value during major tournaments. But it’s more than just novelty: the elevated vantage point helps to remove the surrounding clutter and give a proper fan’s eye view of the goal. I also love the verdant grass, the shocking pink goalkeeper’s jersey and the sense of anticipation because the ball has yet to cross the line. It reminds me of the signed photograph of Coventry’s Keith Houchen, from the 1987 FA Cup final, that hangs proudly on my dad’s office wall.7Bruno’s early bathView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Bruno Guimarães cools down with a splash of water in the first half against Morocco in New Jersey.Photograph: Adam Hunger/APSo many hydration breaks, so much Powerade, but only one player managed to look like a Portuguese water dog fresh from a dip in a lake. The Brazil midfielder Bruno Guimarães is pictured here dousing himself in H2O when his side faced Morocco in their opening game at New York New Jersey Stadium. Brazil ultimately went home for an early bath after losing to Norway in the last 16.8Viking row boatView image in fullscreenNorway supporters gather to perform their rowing celebration on the iconic red steps in Times Square.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesThe mass rowing displays performed by Norway’s fans this summer have been a viral sensation. Seen everywhere from Times Square to Miami Beach, they also became a fixture of the team’s post-match partying, with Erling Haaland and Martin Ødegaard both banging a fan’s drum as the side progressed to the quarter-finals. The Vikings were stopped by England, but their journey was one of the feelgood stories of the tournament.9Light and shadeView image in fullscreenKylian Mbappé of France dribbles the ball against Sweden at New York New Jersey Stadium.Photograph: Justin Setterfield/Getty ImagesA gorgeous pool of afternoon sunlight spilling on to the pitch, surrounded by deep shadows. You couldn’t really create better low-key lighting conditions if you tried. The photographer just had to nail the exposure and wait for a subject to pass through. The fact that it’s Kylian Mbappé who’s skating across the turf in his rich blue France top adds a nice splash of colour and gravity to the scene.10Tunnel visionView image in fullscreenLuka Modric of Croatia prepares in the tunnel before their match with Panama in Toronto.Photograph: Joosep Martinson/Fifa/Getty ImagesLuka Modric has spoken in the past aboutnot wanting to let his people down, and of carrying memories of his grandpa, Luka, with him. He appears to be deep in thought here before playing Panama. Perhaps it’s just a fleeting moment – a man simply massaging his temple or visualising the first tackle – or maybe he’s remembering his war-torn roots and the family member he was named after.11Loving VozinhaView image in fullscreenCape Verde’s Vozinha after the match against Spain in Atlanta.Photograph: Brett Davis/Imagn Images/ReutersAt the ripe age of 40 and with a club career that has taken him to Cyprus, Moldova, Portugal and Slovakia, the Cape Verde goalkeeper Vozinha became the breakout star at this year’s World Cup. His numerous saves againstSpainin the group stage earned him global praise as well as 17 million followers on Instagram. Another stellar display against Argentina, in which he memorably denied a quick free-kick by Messi, further enhanced his reputation. I like his expression in this photograph as he soaks up the adulation from the crowd following the Blue Sharks’ remarkable goalless draw with Spain.12Gaza screeningView image in fullscreenPalestinian football fans gather among war-damaged buildings in Gaza City to watch the match between Argentina and Egypt on a giant outdoor screen.Photograph: Mohammed Skaik/TheNews2/Cover ImagesThis photograph shows the power of football while also putting the World Cup into perspective. Palestinian residents in Gaza gathered amid the ruins to watch a screening of Egypt’s game against Argentina. A moment of normality amid the horror and heartache.13Power headerView image in fullscreenErling Haaland of Norway scores against Brazil at New York New Jersey Stadium.Photograph: Image Photo Agency/Getty ImagesErling Haaland’s first goal against Brazil left his old Premier League rival, Gabriel Magalhães, in a heap. In this shot you can see water (or perhaps sweat) flying off the striker’s forehead, his neck fully strained after connecting with the ball. Haaland scored seven goals in five World Cup games: he netted from inside the box, outside the box, with his left foot, right foot and of course his head. Norway have still never been beaten by Brazil after five matches. No other nation can say that.14Lamine Yamal’s bracesView image in fullscreenLamine Yamal bites the Spain badge during an official Fifa photocall prior to the tournament.Photograph: Karl Bridgeman/Fifa/Getty ImagesThis image was shot before the tournament,as part of Fifa’s official player portraits, but I’ve only recently seen it – and it’s well worth a mention. You don’t see many players with braces, but Lamine Yamal, who only turned 19 this month, wears his with pride. This frame was shot using a 24-70mm lens on a Canon R5 Mark II which has a whopping 45 megapixels, so I suspect it’s been cropped quite hard in post-production to achieve this composition.15Into orbitView image in fullscreenSwitzerland’s Manuel Akanji misses a penalty during the shootout against Colombia.Photograph: Agustín Marcarian/ReutersSome penalty misses are worse than others. Germany’s Jonathan Tah and Manuel Akanji of Switzerland both had Chris Waddle moments as they blew their chances from 12 yards over the bar. Ordinarily I’d say you need to see the crossbar to illustrate that the shot went over, but the way the ball is rocketing skywards – almost as if it could escape the stadium roof – tells the story perfectly.16Neymar’s tearsView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Neymar is consoled by Raphinha after their side were eliminated from the World Cup by Norway.Photograph: James Lang/Imagn Images/ReutersEven when your ego is as big as the sun, sometimes you just need a cuddle. This photograph captures Neymar Jr’s visible distress as he realises his international career is over and his childhood ambitions of lifting the World Cup will never be fulfilled. His teammate Raphina is there to support him. It lacks the humility ofFreddie Flintoff consoling his opponent Brett Leein the Ashes, or thestoicism of Derek Redmond being helped across the finish lineby his father at the 1992 Olympics, but it still packs an emotional gut punch.17Upside downView image in fullscreenJulián Alvarez with teammates after scoring Argentina’s second goal against Switzerland at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Ryan Pierse/Fifa/Getty ImagesThis photograph was flipped 180 degrees by Getty Images before being transmitted to us. Manipulation of this ilk feels almost heretical in the world of photojournalism, but the result is very compelling. It shows Julián Alvarez celebrating his late winner against Switzerland, and it makes me question my fundamental photographic principles. Would I publish it like this in print to illustrate a news story? Absolutely not. Would I include it in an online gallery of images that caught my eye this summer? Evidently so.18Big screenView image in fullscreenA giant video screen signals a goal for Spain against Saudi Arabia in Atlanta.Photograph: Colin Hubbard/APThe stadums in the US have been mind-blowing in scale. Atlanta Stadium, home of the NFL’s Atlanta Falcons and the city’s MLS and NWSL teams, has a maximum capacity of 75,000 people, a climate-controlled environment and a 335-metre display known as the “halo”. This picture from up in the gods looks suitably epic. Initially I assumed the camera was remote-controlled, but it transpires some photographers were actually working up there on the gantry. What a view they had!19Motion blurView image in fullscreenSweden winger Anthony Elanga is challenged by two Japan defenders during their game at Dallas Stadium.Photograph: Lars Baron/Getty ImagesThis is a beautiful example of a photographer using a slow shutter speed while panning gently with the action. I especially like the details within: Anthony Elanga’s facial expression is crisp, and the pink boots leave a painterly trail.20Heroic HarryView image in fullscreenHarry Kane and Jude Bellingham of England celebrate against DR Congo at Atlanta Stadium.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesHarry Kane’s late strikes against the Democratic Republic of Congo in the last 32 led to an outpouring of emotion from the England captain. I was torn over which frame to select – there are some lovely ones of him sprinting across the turf, flanked by Jude Bellingham and Anthony Gordon – but this one of him being embraced by Bellingham serves as a perfect reminder of their twin heroics at the tournament. It’s clean, nicely balanced and full of bromance.21Gómez goes berserkView image in fullscreenGustavo Gómez of Paraguay looks delighted after José Canale scored the winning penalty in a  shootout against Germany in Boston.Photograph: Robert Cianflone/Getty ImagesThe guaranteed agony and ecstasy ofpenalty shootouts always makes for brilliant images. This year, Gustavo Gómez of Paraguay takes the crown for the wildest reaction. After beating Germany during sudden death, Gómez, who scored his own spot-kick, surged across the pitch like a heron taking flight, almost vaulting his teammates José Canale and Orlando Gill as he went to embrace them. He then careered through the photographers behind the goal in pursuit of a mobile phone so that he could document the moment himself.22Living statueView image in fullscreenMichel Nkuka Mboladinga, also known as Lumumba Vea, holds firm in the stands during their match against Colombia.Photograph: Daniel Becerril/ReutersMichel Nkuka Mboladinga, also known as Lumumba Vea, is dubbed a “living statue” because he doesn’t move during games. Instead, the DRC fan poses on a plinth for the entire match, raising his right arm to mimic the statue ofPatrice Lumumba, the first prime minister of the First Congolese Republic, which stands in Kinshasa. Why anyone would go to a football match and not respond to the on-pitch action is beyond me, but Mboladinga insists it’s his patriotic duty.23Senegal swan diveView image in fullscreenYouri Tielemans heads in Belgium’s second goal against Senegal at Seattle Stadium.Photograph: Jared C Tilton/Fifa/Getty ImagesYouri Tielemans scored twice inBelgium’s stunning comeback against Senegalin the last 32. This 89th-minute header might have been a little trickier to control had the defender Moussa Niakhaté competed for the ball instead of diving theatrically to the ground at the first sign of contact. The virtue of modern cameras, many of which shoot 30 frames per second, is that the full sequence of this goal was captured, and we could choose the exact frame when Tielemans’s head connects with the ball.24Comeback kingView image in fullscreenSasa Kalajdzic after scoring Austria’s third goal during a thrilling game against Algeria at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesSasa Kalajdzic overcame three anterior cruciate ligament injuries in the years and months prior to the 2026 World Cup, but the Austrian enjoyed a moment to savour when he netted a 96th-minute equaliser against Algeria to keep his side in the tournament. The game ignited accusations of fixing, because a 3-3 draw ensured both sides went through, but a finale like that – and celebrations like this – cannot be scripted.25Football, bloody hellView image in fullscreenConnor Metcalfe of Australia shows the referee his bloodied forehead against Paraguay at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium.Photograph: Stu Forster/Getty ImagesAustralia’s Connor Metcalfe needed three stitches in his eyebrow after being clipped by a stray boot from Andrés Cubas during the Socceroos’ group match with Paraguay. Maybe it’s the slumped shoulders or the shirt pulled up around his neckline, but he looks like a forlorn child who needs a parent to clean him up.26Big Dan BurnView image in fullscreenEngland’s Dan Burn blocks a bicycle kick from Raúl Jiménez during the last-16 match in Mexico City.Photograph: Jam Media/Getty ImagesYou could say this is just another “two men and a ball” photograph (an expression the Guardian’s photographer, Tom Jenkins, uses to describe generic images of sporting action). But I particularly like this one because of the contrast of sporting prowess – the acrobat versus the brick wall – and for the misshapen ball which appears ready to pop. Dan Burn has become a cult figure for England, in the mould of Peter Crouch. Many supporters call the 6ft 7in defender Big Dan Burn, but Bellingham apparently calls him Burninho. There’s no way Raúl Jiménez, the Mexico striker, was ever getting the ball past him.27Emotional MessiView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi after their 3-2 victory over Egypt at Atlanta Stadium.Photograph: Patrick Smith/Fifa/Getty ImagesLet’s be honest, Lionel Messi is not very good at penalties. He’s missed four of eight in World Cup matches and 34 of 151 in his career, including shootouts. A botched effort against Egypt, in a match which the African side went on to lead 2-0, could have been a pitiful ending to his gilded international career. Instead, Messi scored the equaliser and drove his team to a remarkable 3-2 win in normal time. This picture, taken after the match, speaks to the weight he carries on his shoulders. Is it relief or guilt? Either way, he looks tortured by expectation.28Crowd sceneView image in fullscreenCanada fans take in their game against Bosnia and Herzegovina at Toronto Stadium.Photograph: Joosep Martinson/Fifa/Getty ImagesThe composition of this photograph is simple but beautiful. The intersecting diagonals, the contrast between light and shade, and the depth within the scene … it makes for rich viewing.29Koné’s injury painView image in fullscreenCanada’s Ismaël Koné on the ground after sustaining a broken leg against Qatar.Photograph: Lee Smith/ReutersIsmaël Koné’s eyes say it all. The Canada midfielder had just broken the tibia and fibula bones in his lower left leg after a challenge by Qatar’s midfielder Assim Madibo, who would receive a red card and a five-match ban. As a photo editor, when incidents like this occur you have to weigh up decency and news value before publishing (I still feel traumatised by the uncensored images I saw of Eduardo’s broken leg in 2008). I think this image conveys the severity of the incident without being unduly graphic. Koné left the pitch on a stretcher but waved to fans as he exited.30Cubarsi’s cartwheelView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Enzo Fernández fouls Spain’s Pau Cubarsi and is later shown a second yellow card leading to a red.Photograph: Maddie Meyer/Fifa/Getty ImagesI’d typically include a celebration photograph from the World Cup final among my favourites from a tournament, but this ‘tackle’ by Enzo Fernández was so bad – and the image of Pau Cubarsi flying through the air so striking – that I couldn’t look beyond it when reviewing the match. Cubarsi looks like he’s falling from space. An absolutely dreadful final sparked into life at this defining moment. Cubarsi rose from the turf to claim the title of ‘best young player’ of the tournament and, more importantly, a World Cup winner’s medal.

title: No tears from Keir as Burnham strolls into No 10 with the clock already ticking | John Crace | The Guardian
description: Rapid turnover of prime ministers continues as UK gets another promise that, this time, things will be different
Author: John Crace
Headline: Rapid turnover of prime ministers continues as UK gets another promise that, this time, things will be different
Content: No notes, from the heart – and without a lectern: Andy’s first speech as leader of the country was to be short and sweet.Photograph: Temilade Adelaja/ReutersView image in fullscreenNo notes, from the heart – and without a lectern: Andy’s first speech as leader of the country was to be short and sweet.Photograph: Temilade Adelaja/ReutersThe politics sketchAndy BurnhamNo tears from Keir as Burnham strolls into No 10 with the clock already tickingJohn CraceRapid turnover of prime ministers continues as UK gets another promise that, this time, things will be differentMon 20 Jul 2026 11.34 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShareNot another one. It would be no surprise to find out that overseas tour companies now include package holiday visits to watch the UK’s rapid turnover of prime ministers. “Come to London to watch the motorcade nearly run over a group of fancy dress soldiers playing a Bon Jovi medley just outside Buckingham Palace.” Two years is a long time in the lifetime of a UK leader. Liz Truss was in and out in seconds. Our buy one, get one free prime minister. In Britain, we sure know how to Dance Me to the End of Love.Still, practice makes perfect and we now have the handover of power nailed to a T. Everyone knows exactly what they are doing. Even King Charles, who is now on to his fourth prime minister in as many years. Though presumably he’s moderately pissed off about having to go to Buckingham Palace for 90 minutes to do the business. I guess he has to take the rough with the smooth. After all, he’s managed to get his subjects to pay for its upkeep even though he can’t be arsed to live there.At about 10.30am “Hot Podium Guy” – actually, it’s a lectern – appeared in Downing Street to set things up for Keir Starmer’s farewell speech, and a while later a large crowd of former cabinet colleagues, No 10 staff and friends and supporters began to assemble in a semi-circle outside the front door. Then out came Keir with his wife, Vic. Both smiled and looked almost relieved that it was all over. An end to months of speculation followed by a weeks-long protracted exit.Sometimes these events are emotional for the outgoing prime minister. But there was no sign of any tears. Keir can now enjoy the next six weeks of the summer with his family, safe in the knowledge that no one is going to wake him at three in the morning with the news that Donald Trump has just started a third world war.Instead, he chose to keep his speech short and sweet. To say that his work was done. That he was leaving the country in a stronger and fairer place. Which is probably true. Just not strong and fair enough for us to have noticed and for him to have been able to persuade us.1:34Keir Starmer wishes Andy Burnham success as he leaves No 10 - videoBut that’s part of Keir’s personal tragedy. He governed and led in the dullest of prose, unable to construct a narrative that could take you with him. Yet no matter how many of these prime ministerial send-offs I see, they are never not affecting. It’s like seeing someone being airbrushed out of history in real time. You can almost see them disappearing in front of your eyes. First they live in colour before rapidly turning to sepia and by the end they are barely even a pencil outline. Keir who?And now even his legacy won’t be his to control. Anything bad that happens in the next three years will be blamed on him. And anything good,Andy Burnhamwill claim as his own. It was always thus. Keir ended by thanking everyone for joining him on his journey. The British people, colleagues and family. Though presumably not Peter Mandelson. He probably wishes he had never met Mandy. “I leave with good grace and a smile,” he said. And he did. There was no Boris Johnson temper tantrum. Rachel Reeves looked a bit glum, mind. Inside No 10, Larry the Cat sharpened his claws and scratched another notch into the back of the door. His sixth.With that, Keir got into the car and headed off to Buck House to take his leave of the king. And it was time for Fiona Bruce and “senior” – is there really a junior? – royal reporter Daniela Relph to talk pure bollocks for the best part of an hour and a half. The role of royal correspondent must be the most thankless job in journalism. Years wasted having to infer meaning from a new hairstyle or who gets to sit where. So we were breathlessly told that Keir would be arriving at the “luggage door” – who doesn’t have one of those? – before being taken to the Chinese dining room. Presumably soon to become sponsored and renamed the KFC dining room.A while later, Keir left, soon to be replaced by Andy and his wife, Marie-France. “He will be kissing the king’s hand,” said an overexcited Daniela, “though of course he won’t actually be kissing the king’s hand.” I mean, who really gives a flying fuck? Next we were told that Andy might have been dreaming about this moment for a while. Where’s Sigmund Freud when you need him? “Andy got 35 minutes with the king. A little longer than Keir.” I’m sure Keir will be devastated.Back in Downing Street, Andy’s old muckers were beginning to assemble, Keir’s having been hurried off the premises. There was the former Everton footballer Peter Reid, the eco entrepreneur Dale Vince and the mayor of Liverpool city region, Steve Rotheram, who was holding his grandson. Monday is his day on the babysitting rota. There was no getting out of it. Not even to see a mate become prime minister.View image in fullscreenThe former Everton footballer Peter Reid, left, and Liverpool city region mayor, Steve Rotheram, centre, wait outside No 10 for Burnham’s arrival.Photograph: Wiktor Szymanowicz/Future Publishing/Getty ImagesThen the arrival of Andy at his new address. Hugs and kisses for various people in the crowd – a special one for his mum – and then he walked towards the microphone. The lectern had long since been put back inside. Andy was going to do this speechlet from the heart, with no notes. That should give Nigel Farage and Kemi Badenoch something to think about. Life is about to get a lot more difficult for the opposition parties.Six highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speechRead moreSteve Bray had been banned from using his PA machine – he’s had a habit of wrecking every Downing Street speech – but you could still just about hear him singing Beethoven’s Ode to Joy from outside the gates. But Andy wasn’t bothered.This was to be short and sweet. Mainly a precis of what he had said when hebecame leader of the Labour partyon Friday. A mention that people were fed up with a politics that produced seven prime ministers since 2016. He would do things differently. Be better. They all say that. But he sounded more persuasive. A poet with imagination in his heart, who could connect.He would give the country some breathing space from the cost of living. Starting from tomorrow. It wasn’t clear what he meant, but it sounded good. He would end rough sleeping. Something he had failed to do in Manchester. Good for him for not having given up on it. Though still a hostage to fortune.“I will give it my all,” he said. It was the time for common purpose and positivity. Good luck with that. You could hear the country reply: “We’ll be the judge of that.” You’ve got a couple of years, Andy. The clock is ticking.Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamThe politics sketchKeir StarmerLabour party leadershipLabourKing Charles IIIcommentShareReuse this content

title: What’s left after this World Cup? A kind of messy hope | Jonathan Liew | The Guardian
description: Kazakhs cheering Uzbeks, Bengalis wild for Argentina … This tournament was once a playground for nationalism. Now it shows how flimsy that allegiance is, says Guardian columnist Jonathan Liew
Author: Jonathan Liew
Headline: Kazakhs cheering Uzbeks, Bengalis wild for Argentina … This tournament was once a playground for nationalism. Now it shows how flimsy that allegiance is, says Guardian columnist Jonathan Liew
Content: A boy in an Argentina jersey looks at a mural in Dhaka, Bangladesh, 27 June 2026.Photograph: Md Rafayat Haque Khan/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenA boy in an Argentina jersey looks at a mural in Dhaka, Bangladesh, 27 June 2026.Photograph: Md Rafayat Haque Khan/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockOpinionWorld Cup 2026What’s left after this World Cup? A kind of messy hopeJonathan LiewKazakhs cheering Uzbeks, Bengalis wild for Argentina … This tournament was once a playground for nationalism. Now it shows how flimsy that allegiance isMon 20 Jul 2026 11.20 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 14.44 EDTShareIt’s theWorld Cup final, so you pick a side. And don’t worry if you happen to be part of the 98.9% of the human race that is neither Spanish nor Argentinian – just make something up. Maybe you like the Spain kit. Maybe you’ve always been an admirer of Lionel Messi. Maybe you’re still raw about the Falklands or Diego Maradona, maybe you disagree with the Pedro Sánchez government’s stance on Palestine/Israel, maybe you once ate a dodgy empanada on your gap year. Nobody needs to see your working. Only two rules really apply: be partisan and be quick about it.The MetLife Stadium on Sunday night was a melting pot of colliding incongruities and temporary identities. The former heavyweight boxing champion Mike Tyson waswearing an Argentina shirt. The comedian Trevor Noahwore a Spain shirt. Indeed,ticket sales datasuggests that an overwhelming proportion of the crowd was American. The most consequential fixture in international sport was, as it turned out, little more than a grand cosplay for the New Jersey leisure market.And beyond the final, perhaps this has been one of the hidden stories of the World Cup: the gradual reimagining of national allegiance as a kind of single-use consumer brand. For six weeks, the entire nation ofBangladesh went crazy for Argentina. A group of central Asian friends in north London put aside their internecine rivalries to cheer for Uzbekistan. The Arab diaspora swung firmly behind Morocco and Egypt as the tournament progressed. Leftwing progressives who revile the cross of St George when painted on a mini-roundabout were suddenly daubing it on their cheeks.All of which upends what we have been told for decades about theWorld Cup, and international sport in general. A tournament that should in theory be the ultimate playground for nationalism has in fact demonstrated the very opposite: the increasing fragility of nationhood as an idea. What the social psychologist Michael Billig termed banal nationalism – the subliminal everyday symbols of nationhood – has now become so banal as to be in essence disposable. If national identity is now something that can be slipped on and off as easily as a football shirt, then what use is it, really?We live in an age where the boundaries of nationhood are both ever-more piercingly evoked and ever-more hazily defined. Again, football illustrates this confusion better than almost any other human endeavour. Across western Europe, far-right voices preach the doctrine of nationalism while reviling their own national teams for having too many black players. In Colombia and Brazil, many leftwing fans have chosen to spurn the national team shirt as a result of itsappropriation by rightwing populists.This is before we even address the teams themselves: in many cases not so much cogent expressions of national identity or even recognisable products of a system as they are snapshots of a sprawling diaspora at a particular moment in time, splintered on multiple planes by globalisation and migration, assembled out of convenience and expediency. Forty of the 48 competing nationscontained a player born overseas. Only one member of the Curaçao squad was actually born on the Caribbean island. The Cape Verde squad that held the eventual champions, Spain, to a heroic 0-0 draw in the group phase featuredmore players born in Rotterdamthan in the country’s capital.And so the modern World Cup serves two abrupt functions. First, it forces us to confront the idea that most of us have multiple identities cutting across or even against national boundaries, that everywhere is the product of everywhere. Second, it interrogates the criteria by which we pledge allegiance to a country, a process that most of the time lies latent and unspoken, only bursting to the surface in the form of an urgent binary question: “Who do I support for this game?” The historian Eric Hobsbawm’s famous quote about football gestating an “imagined community of millions” takes on a subtly different resonance when you realise that very often this is a community with no defined borders or polity, a community that for 90 minutes draws its number from every corner of the planet.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionHere’s the lesson to learn from England’s World Cup joy: shared purpose is key, not shared ancestry | Maya TudorRead moreWeirdly, this is a phenomenon that has become neither exclusively left- nor right-coded. If nationalism was long regarded as the preserve of the populist right and internationalism as the preserve of the intellectual left, these days the picture is far more nuanced. Today’s rightwing agitators are as likely to pledge allegiance to Donald Trump or wave an Israeli flag as they are to champion the country they’re actually from. The Trump administration has consistently demonstrated greater solidarity withwhite South Africansthan with Black Americans. Across the English-speaking world, conservative outlets freely make alliances across national borders and appeal to abstract concepts such as “Judeo-Christian values” or “western civilisation” while denouncing and dehumanising many of their fellow citizens as traitors and scum.Football’s role as a nation builder is often overstated. France’s World Cup triumph in 1998 was hailed as a vindication of the country’s multiethnic identity, but four years later it sent Jean-Marie Le Pen into the final round of the presidential election. Argentina’s 1978 victory on home soil generated a surge of popularity for the country’s military dictatorship, but by 1983 it had collapsed. England’s run to the European Championship final in 2021 did not, for all the good vibes and open letters, arrest our wider descent into gutter politics and culture war.But occasionally it can hold up a mirror. And perhaps what this World Cup reflects back at us is a kind of messy hope: that a world of hardening physical borders is doomed to disintegrate on contact with reality. That for all the flags and pageantry, nationality is ultimately little more than a brand, a consumer choice, a piece of content – a mask anyone can happily wear for six weeks, and shed just as easily.Jonathan Liew is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026OpinionWorld CupcommentShareReuse this content

title: Vamos España? Why this World Cup victory for Spain feels so different from the others  | María Ramírez | The Guardian
description: The success belongs to many more people around the world from Gaza to Latin America – even if the collegiate spirit of the team has yet to be mirrored in our public life, says journalist María Ramírez
Author: María Ramírez
Headline: The success belongs to many more people around the world from Gaza to Latin America – even if the collegiate spirit of the team has yet to be mirrored in our public life, says journalist María Ramírez
Content: Fans gather in the streets of Madrid to celebrate Spain’s World Cup triumph.Photograph: Luis Soto/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenFans gather in the streets of Madrid to celebrate Spain’s World Cup triumph.Photograph: Luis Soto/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockOpinionSpainVamos España? Why this World Cup victory for Spain feels so different from the othersMaría RamírezThe success belongs to many more people around the world from Gaza to Latin America – even if the collegiate spirit of the team has yet to be mirrored in our public lifeMon 20 Jul 2026 08.52 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 10.24 EDTShareJust past midnight, the cheers erupted from blocks of lit flats in my neighbourhood in central Madrid. Hours later, people of all generations were still out in the streets in the middle of another tropical night, and not only because it was warm, with temperatures hovering close to 30C. In the big pedestrianised square Plaza de Olavide, fans had gathered around screens, some sitting on the ground while children and dogs ran around. A girl played on the swings, her Spanish-flag cape fluttering as she swung back and forth.Argentinian Lionel Messi jerseys were visible throughout the crowd. Many fans gathered also at a nearby Argentinian bar. Not too surprising when you consider there aremore than 400,000 Argentiniansliving in Spain. Messi, whose life and career are embedded with Barcelona, is a football hero in Spain too.It was the same across the city and throughout the country. Barcelona had one of the largest public gatherings, with more than 30,000 people watching on a giant screen installedby the city council. Catalonia is the home region of stars including the 19-year-old Lamine Yamal, the national side’s most promising player. There is asmuch regional varietyin Spain’s team as there is in the country. Several come from the Basque Country, once only a reluctant supporter of the national team. One player, Aymeric Laporte, was born in France. Backgrounds are as diverse as the population.It was hard not to agree with Ferran Torres, who scored the extra-time goal that brought us victory, when he said his achievement belonged to “47 million people,not just me or my teammates”. But it felt as though the success belonged to many more people around the world, from Gaza, where Spain has come to represent solidarity with the Palestinian cause,to Brazil.The celebratory mood was just as palpable three years ago when we gathered to watch the women’s national team win the World Cup. But the joy was overshadowed bysexual harassmentand misogyny after the Jenni Hermoso case.View image in fullscreenDisplaced Palestinians watch the World Cup final in Gaza City, 19 July 2026.Photograph: Belal Osama/APAImages/ShutterstockSixteen years ago I watched Spain’s firstWorld Cupvictory among late-night crowds in Brussels, where I then lived. That night felt joyful, but those were painful times. I was covering the eurozone crisis that was already hitting Spain harder than almost anywhere else, as a burst property bubble and soaring unemployment left many people with a sense of hopelessness.This win feels somehow more rounded. The country itself is in a better place despite the deep political polarisation and widespread mood of dissatisfaction that often dominates public debate. Spain’s economy has become one of the strongest performers in the developed world and its cities feel more prosperous, confident and dynamic than many inEurope.The national team is young, diverse and relentlessly professional. The cheers in Lavapiés, one of Madrid’s most diverse neighbourhoods, felt momentous in a country wheremore than 10 million people, about 20% of the population, were born abroad.Spain’s players also offered an example of maturity under pressure. The clarity with whichYamal respondedto former prime minister Mariano Rajoy’s racist comments about the French national team stood out in contrast to the crudeness of a supposedly experienced politician.The contrast between football’s power to promote integration, which this team seems to fully understand, with the constant partisan battles dominating public life inSpaincouldn’t be sharper.The message repeated most often by the coach, Luis de la Fuente, and his players is simple and powerful: “We are a team. We embrace diversity while working together towards a common goal.” Spain’s greatest strength on the pitch lies not in individual brilliance but in a cohesiveness that becomes greater than the sum of its parts. Ironically, that sense of shared purpose and collective responsibility is perhaps what Spanish public life is missing the most.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionYet even if the impact of football seems fleeting and insignificant, especially in these challenging times, its extraordinary appeal can inspire a shared spirit. At its best, it fosters solidarity and camaraderie. At its worst, it can fuel violence and disrespect, as Argentina’s team sadly showed before, during and after the match.Everyone sees what they want to see in the Spanish national team, be it nationalism, diversity or simply great football. Of course, not every message is inclusive, but the variety of fans on the street, regardless of age, origin or gender, is a reminder of the immense power of football to bring together very different people.I keep thinking these days about the motto of the country that hosted the World Cup final:E pluribus unum– out of many, one. Those words reflected the US founding fathers’ thinking. They believed the Declaration of Independence’s ideals would unite people from very different backgrounds without needing centuries of shared religions, cultures and wars, as in Europe.Unity in diversity remains a painfully unfulfilled ideal in the US and in much of the world – also in Spain. But at least for a night, amid the chants, klaxons and fireworks, one could hope that somehow it is still possible.María Ramírez is a journalist and deputy managing editor of elDiario.es, a news outlet in SpainExplore more on these topicsSpainOpinionWorld Cup 2026World CupEuropecommentShareReuse this content

title: Why has Britain’s oldest political magazine become such a rabid cheerleader for Islamophobia? | Nesrine Malik | The Guardian
description: A report on the Spectator’s coverage of Muslims makes for grim reading. Conservatism is now focused on this minority to the exclusion of everything else, says Guardian columnist Nesrine Malik
Author: Nesrine Malik
Headline: A report on the Spectator’s coverage of Muslims makes for grim reading. Conservatism is now focused on this minority to the exclusion of everything else, says Guardian columnist Nesrine Malik
Content: Illustration: Nate Kitch/The GuardianView image in fullscreenIllustration: Nate Kitch/The GuardianOpinionIslamophobiaWhy has Britain’s oldest political magazine become such a rabid cheerleader for Islamophobia?Nesrine MalikA report on the Spectator’s coverage of Muslims makes for grim reading. Conservatism is now focused on this minority to the exclusion of everything elseMon 20 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 03.16 EDTShareIstart this column already resigned to the tune out, to the glaze over, the click away. This is not to say that I do not have faith in you, dear reader, to keep reading and caring. But I am aware, and this is central to the problem, that it is now normal, when it comes to a certain minority in this country, to speak of them as if they were some sort of plague.Islamophobiahas become so common as to be mundane in large parts of the British media. Foremost among the offenders is Britain’s oldest political magazine: the Spectator.Alarge and comprehensive reportfrom the Centre for Media Monitoring reviewed 3,733 articles published in the Spectator relating to Islam or Muslims. Released last week, it criticised the publication for “a sustained and systemic pattern of biased coverage of Muslims and Islam”, according to the CMM, “with more than half of all relevant articles rated as biased over an eight-year period”. It is grim, grim reading.Among the conclusions reached by the report are that articles in the magazine regularly portrayed Muslims as a “problem population”, delegitimised the very notion of Islamophobia, and consistently presented Muslims as exceptional or threatening. The overall effect is of a minority that is not only sectarian and separate, but also, ironically, from within its ghetto, dangerous and aggressively colonising British society.Articles cited refer to Muslim concerns about racist abuse as “dainty sensibilities” and “handwringing”. Presumably, this precious response includes objections to statements in the magazine panicking that “organised Islam is strong and vociferous” and, in reference to the growing population of Muslims in the country, that “there are now more than double the number of semi-literate, virgin-obsessed potential murderers among [sic] our midst”.Even the mere existence of Muslims in the UK is presented as something akin to a poisonous foreign presence. “Prominent historians … have noted entirely correctly that Islam seems to have things about it which make it uniquely indigestible to the modern secularised state,”wrote one columnist.“We should have second thoughts about approving mosques,” wrote another contributor.The coverage depicts a country that is going to the dogs. Politicians quake in the face of Muslims, the left aids and abets them, the courts indulge them. The scale of the onslaught is staggering and relentless. It would be unacceptable to speak of another minority group in such terms, but Muslims occupy a sweet spot that makes them convenient subjects for the forces of reaction. They are racialised, they are immigrants or the descendants of immigrants, some of them dare to practise their own religion. Their unique profile makes it easy to channel racism, anti-immigration sentiment and just plain old petty parochialism. Islamophobia itself has metastasised, becoming a vehicle for the sort of things you can’t say out loud about people of colour in general, but which you can say about Muslims.In the foreword of the report, the writer and broadcaster Peter Oborne – the magazine’s former political editor – traces the history of the Spectator, and how it went from condemning the anti-immigration Enoch Powell to “steadily rebuilding his reputation”. Oborne cites a piece from earlier this year that says Powell “had a view of the British polity, values and way of life with which many Britons to this day feel they identify, and that is not least what establishes him as one of the country’s greatest conservative influences”. Now, the publication platforms broader far-right politics, in one articlepraising Tommy Robinsonas likable, “intelligent, quick, articulate, well-informed, good-mannered”.And yet despite such extremism, the Spectator snugly occupies a position of such respectability that its summer party is attended by Labour and Tory politicians and journalists from across the British media, who rub shoulders there with Nigel Farage and the broader Spectator-verse. “The jamboree remains the most sought-after social ticket of the Westminster summer season,”boasted the Spectatorin July after this year’s edition. A refutation of the charge that there is a wholesale coddling of Muslims if there ever was one. There is something crudely and unmistakably symbolic in the magazine’s influence. As the country is riven by far-right politics and protests, as mosques are attacked and hate crimesreach new levels, our elected politicians and opinion-makers are comfortable to drink and make merry among those who mainstream and disseminate inflammatory rhetoric.But something else struck me while reading example after example of quoted texts. It was all tawdry, poorly written, poorly argued stuff. You come away not with the sense that this is an august magazine dedicating its formidable intellectual and journalistic resources towards a problematic minority it is concerned about, but with the sense that it isn’t a serious publication.Its articles are the sort of content you get from GB News, or a bellicose radio phone-in, or a tabloid on a tear. A convergence has occurred among the rightwing media, a radicalisation that has alighted onanger and scaremongering, which feeds readers’ and viewers’ fears and paranoia. It is a successful commercial strategy, but there is no community here, no solace or even loving depiction of the precious country vandalised. There is little to bind other than hate and outrage, no hold on what it is we are ostensibly saving from Muslims or immigrants or the left. Tuning in, what one hears in concert is: “You have once again woken up in Britain. Welcome to hell.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionVandalism, taunts and hijabs torn off: Muslim leaders in UK say hate crime hitting new levelsRead moreThe result is a climate that is hostile and dangerous not just to Muslims but all who it has been decided are stealthily annexing the country. But there is also a wider degradation that comes with the relentless focus on Muslims.The project of developing a vision of conservatism that goes beyond purification has been abandoned. Interested in, say, how the contemporary movement can reconcile austerity and free-market policies with stagnant wages and productivity and declining public services? In the ideological fissures in modern conservatism and its splintering? Perhaps a rant about Muslim public prayer as an act of “public domination” can shed some light on these crucial questions.The Spectator and its broader universe constitute what is now mostly a low-brow media attempting to dress up racist audience-baiting as high-end journalism, all of which is met not with alarm and objection by establishment politics, but a “cheers” in the sunshine. It is not that Muslims have turned into a bigger problem for the country, but that the country itself, and its so-called intellectual elites, have become small. In resisting the feeling that this is all just how it is, in not glazing over, we resist becoming small along with them.Nesrine Malik is a Guardian columnistDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsIslamophobiaOpinionThe SpectatorIslamReligionMagazinesNewspapers & magazinescommentShareReuse this content

title: Welcome to the age of extreme job anxiety | Zoe Williams | The Guardian
description: Young, old, midlife – whatever your age, gender or sector, the unemployment crisis is hitting us all. The only way through it is together
Author: Zoe Williams
Headline: Young, old, midlife – whatever your age, gender or sector, the unemployment crisis is hitting us all. The only way through it is together
Content: Nearly one-third of women in the 50-64 age bracket are economically inactive in the UK.Photograph: Posed by models; 10,000 Hours/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenNearly one-third of women in the 50-64 age bracket are economically inactive in the UK.Photograph: Posed by models; 10,000 Hours/Getty ImagesOpinionWork & careersWelcome to the age of extreme job anxietyZoe WilliamsYoung, old, midlife – whatever your age, gender or sector, the unemployment crisis is hitting us all. The only way through it is togetherMon 20 Jul 2026 12.27 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShareThe personal experience of long-term unemployment is one people rarely share, for completely understandable reasons. There’s a bit of superstition that saying it out loud will bed it in, and there’s always one person in 100 who will choose to explain away the misfortune as your own personal failing.Even if they’re clearly in the grip of superstitions of their own – trying to ward off your fate for themselves – it’s hard not to take criticism personally in such a personal situation. The conventions of human interaction are that you shouldn’t really mention a cloud unless you have a silver lining handy. For instance: after the financial crash in 2008, I remember hearing a couple interviewed on the radio who had both been made redundant when the private school they worked for closed down. After describing months of fruitless job applications, the wife ended saying they’d been able to spend much more time in their allotment, and she reckoned their rocket yield alone had saved them a small fortune. That, I thought, is a remarkable and yet probably universal commitment to looking on the bright side.So when someone breaks the omertà – asStacey Duguiddid on Instagram three weeks ago – the conversation explodes like any that’s been waiting a long time to happen.Duguid, 52, after a varied and high-achieving career in fashion and journalism, has spent more than a year sending out 150 job applications, many of which don’t even get replies. When she said that out loud, thousands of women messaged her with similar experiences. Accordingly, it has been taken as an “older woman” or, more bluntly, a perimenopause story, which is borne out bythe data– 30% of women aged 50 to 64 are economically inactive in the UK, against 22% of men – but I’d want to see more field work before I’d settle for hormones as the main reason.Age-wise, this is also peak time for the “sandwich years”. They’re called that to describe a person trapped between childcare and elderly parents, and the term is not especially accurate, since childcare and end-of-life care are as different from one another as two bits of bread are similar. But as euphemisms go, it works, because what could be more practical and manageable than a sandwich?By contrast, keeping regular working hours while remaining available for the sudden and irregular crises that attend the end of a life is completely impossible. My sister quit her job while our mum was dying, and it was only because she did that I didn’t have to. In other words: yes, the gender split makes sense, but no, more HRT probably isn’t the silver bullet, and don’t even start looking for a lining. This is a silver-free scenario.‘You can’t make billions without hurting people’: Cory Doctorow on Elon Musk, the AI bubble and bosses’ cruel fantasiesRead moreFor many at the start of their careers, things are just as bad.Government analysislast month insisted that entry-level positions were declining in line with the rest of the job market, but the general feeling that AI is wiping out starter jobs is backed up by research. The sectors worst hit are the most AI-friendly – software engineers, accountants, legal assistants, data analysts. A year ago,figures showednew graduate vacancies had dropped by a third since the arrival of ChatGPT. Applicants themselves describe the harrowing experience of being rejected in less than the time it would have taken for any human to read their application – in other words, by AI.This creates a pipeline problem for employers, who will eventually land in a world where there’s no one to fill the middle tier of their staff hierarchy with the experience of having done the grunt work at the bottom. But being able to say “serves you right”, not even to a person but to asector, lacks some quality of emotional satisfaction.Can ageism be the issue, in a system that punishes the workforce at both ends, and traps everyone who has a job in a terrorised, scarcity mindset? Is AI really on the brink of making all human endeavour obsolete, or have AI companies just made some vast claims for it that shortsighted employers are choosing to believe, ultimately at their own expense? We’ll never find out in generational or gender silos, and the code of silence isn’t helping, either. It’s important to keep your skills updated, the employment coaches always say – the trait this modern world demands is openness. Only in talking about worklessness does it become clear: it’s not a you problem, it’s an everyone problem.Zoe Williams is a Guardian columnistDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? title: Feel guilty after gossiping? You’re clearly not doing it right | Polly Hudson | The Guardian
description: Some social media users claim they get a horrible sinking feeling called ‘post-gossip clarity’ after dishing the dirt, writes Polly Hudson. May I suggest they try harder?
Author: Polly Hudson
Headline: Some social media users claim they get a horrible sinking feeling called ‘post-gossip clarity’ after dishing the dirt, writes Polly Hudson. May I suggest they try harder?
Content: Wishing you had said less? That’s post-gossip clarity.Photograph: Posed by models; Xavier Lorenzo/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenWishing you had said less? That’s post-gossip clarity.Photograph: Posed by models; Xavier Lorenzo/Getty ImagesOpinionLife and styleFeel guilty after gossiping? You’re clearly not doing it rightPolly HudsonSome social media users claim they get a horrible sinking feeling called ‘post-gossip clarity’ after dishing the dirt. May I suggest they try harder?Mon 20 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.38 EDTShareMany phrases sound more elegant in French and post-coital tristesse is one of them. The English version is direct to the point of rudeness: post-nut clarity. This phenomenon, much discussed on social media, isn’t exactly the sadnessàla Française, it’s more the sudden clear-headedness and, often, sense of regret men experience after climax, once the dopamine and arousal chemicals have crashed and their prefrontal cortex – responsible for logic and reasoning – is back online. It’s a calm, rational, remorseful view of the situation.Now, there is a new offshoot of this sensation for 2026: post-gossip clarity. Ugh! Also originating on socials, it is that horrible, sinking feeling you get when the buzz has worn off after a particularly juicy goss sesh, especially if you got carried away and spilled a little too much tea. This is easily done, especially if you have fully committed to the conversation, which you always should when blessed with killer info.Let’s not get into whether gossip is better than sex, but there are certainly parallels. During arousal, desire clouds the brain and leaves you unable to concentrate on anything else. If it’s good, it can make you gasp. There is also a certain level of gratitude involved. You don’t want to be selfish; you have to give as well as receive. But when you’re overexcited, it’s hard to hold back, which can lead to premature revelation.‘It’s not whether you do it – it’s how you do it’: the expert guide to healthy gossipingRead moreDishing the dirt well is an art form and perhaps post-gossip clarity is simply an occupational hazard, like an unfortunate side-effect of medication that makes you feel better in every other way. Besides, surely the unpleasantness of the deep wish that you hadn’t said something is more than cancelled out by what you heard in return? If not, maybe you’re doing it wrong.Polly Hudson is a freelance writerDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? title: Two new tortoises have hatched. Now we have seven – and it’s chaos | Emma Beddington | The Guardian
description: The males have to be separated or they attack each other. The tiny new arrivals are a constant source of anxiety. Yet, despite all this, my husband is considering adopting more, writes Emma Beddington
Author: Emma Beddington
Headline: The males have to be separated or they attack each other. The tiny new arrivals are a constant source of anxiety. Yet, despite all this, my husband is considering adopting more, writes Emma Beddington
Content: New kids on the block … Emma Beddington’s baby tortoises.Photograph: Courtesy of Emma BeddingtonView image in fullscreenNew kids on the block … Emma Beddington’s baby tortoises.Photograph: Courtesy of Emma BeddingtonOpinionPetsTwo new tortoises have hatched. Now we have seven – and it’s chaosEmma BeddingtonThe males have to be separated or they attack each other. The tiny new arrivals are a constant source of anxiety. Yet, despite all this, my husband is considering adopting moreMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.18 EDTShareTo hatchone unexpected baby tortoiseis universally viewed as a blessing; hatch two more and people start asking pointed questions, such as, “Are you running a tortoise sanctuary now?” and “Do you have the horniest tortoises in Yorkshire?”The answer to both is “apparently”, and it’s chaos. We now have seven tortoises of which at least three are male and therefore – sorry for the gender essentialism, butit’s true– trouble. The two male adults have to be separated or they attack each other and we got confirmation that the previous baby, who hatched in 2024, is also male when we found him trying to have sex with his mother (it’s OK – she is 10 times his size).We thought it would be cute to take a picture of all seven together last week but within seconds, it descended into violent anarchy with everyone biting and scattering in opposite directions at high speed (tortoise slowness is a myth – they move like Usain Bolt in this heatwave).Our current strategy is divide and contain: the most violent recidivist has been taken to my stepfather’s yard where the only things he can hurt are hardy perennials. The other adult male is temporarily in the greenhouse. This has confirmed our suspicion that he is really quite dim: he keeps capsizing despite there being absolutely no obstacles in his way and is unable to right himself – I’m constantly having to flip him over when I’m watering the tomatoes. Meanwhile, I am brokering the wayward two-year-old toddler’s adoption and re-education by a lesbian couple who have vowed to try to “raise a good man” (rather them than me).That leaves the females (serene, unbothered, bankrupting us in chicory) and the two, as yet unsexed, new arrivals. Barely bigger than a 50-pence piece, they are a source of constant anxiety. Back Dot (we took a marker to their shells) keeps trying to escape; Side Dot is dozy and reluctant to eat. The collective noun for tortoises should be a torment, not a creep. Despite all this, I’ve just found my husband reading aboutTheo, a 40kg teenage African spurred tortoise currently up for adoption, with a furtive, calculating expression. Grounds for divorce!Emma Beddington is a Guardian columnistDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? title: The Guardian view on Andy Burnham’s first day: the right vision, but time is of the essence | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: Outside Downing Street, Britain’s new prime minister struck the right notes. Now he and his cabinet need to be brave
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: Outside Downing Street, Britain’s new prime minister struck the right notes. Now he and his cabinet need to be brave
