Content: Andy Burnham and his wife, Marie-France van Heel, greet supporters in Downing Street.Photograph: Carl Court/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham and his wife, Marie-France van Heel, greet supporters in Downing Street.Photograph: Carl Court/Getty ImagesOpinionAndy BurnhamThe Guardian view on Andy Burnham’s first day: the right vision, but time is of the essenceEditorialOutside Downing Street, Britain’s new prime minister struck the right notes. Now he and his cabinet need to be braveMon 20 Jul 2026 14.35 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShareA1970s adageattributedto the former Tory chancellor Reginald Maudling maintained that Britain was essentially a Conservative country that sometimes voted Labour. That was always a questionable assertion, given the defining postwar role of Labour in shaping a fairer social settlement. But the trauma of successive electionvictoriesby Margaret Thatcher from 1979 onwards meant that senior figures on the centre left began to think along similar lines; a sometimes debilitatingcautioncrept into the leadership of a party founded to serve those outside the gilded circles of money, power and influence.Outside 10 Downing Streeton his first dayas prime minister, Andy Burnham in effect argued that only through being braver and truer to its original vocation can Labour restore faith in politics and renew a disillusioned nation. Mr Burnham’s strikingpledgeto end rough sleeping may be something of a hostage to fortune. As mayor of Greater Manchester he could only partially deliver on the same commitment. But on the day he entered No 10, it vividly conveyed what was meant when he talked about “the right values and the right standards”.In a speech delivered without lectern or notes, Mr Burnham also said that corrosive imbalances of wealth, power and status must be challenged through a “circuit-breaker” administration – one that truly empowers places like his own Makerfield constituency, and eases the cost of living by introducing greater public control over economic essentials. This is a vision that, as he stated, challenges the political and economic assumptions of the last 40 years. It can give hope to millions of votersalienatedby missteps and misplaced priorities over the last two years under Sir Keir Starmer.To enact Mr Burnham’s plans, though, will mean governing differently and almost certainly confronting stubborn resistance in parts of Whitehall – and in particular in the Treasury. The decision to choose John Healey rather than Ed Miliband to replaceRachel Reevesas chancellor – easily the most crucial early decision that Mr Burnham will make – is disappointing. This newspaper has argued that Mr Miliband’s authority, experience and reforming zeal meant that he was the best placed to drive the new prime minister’s economic agenda.Mr Healey is an astute politician and a popular figure with the trade unions, and was a Treasury minister under Gordon Brown. But thepolitical windowfor Mr Burnham to make a tangible impact is short and he will need to hit the ground running. Much will depend on relations between the Treasury and No 10 North in Manchester, envisaged by Mr Burnham as the centre of operations for decentralising power away from London. Mr Miliband’s appointment as foreign secretary, after his sterling work on the climate agenda, promises some interesting encounters in Washington DC.The appointment of Mr Burnham’s close ally,Louise Haigh, as chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster and first secretary of state confirms her status as the administration’s chief powerbroker. In a cabinet shorn of Starmer loyalists, the new prime minister must hope that promises to support him from the backbenches hold good.For all the talk of the 1980s, Mr Burnham’s mood music, ethical ambition and determination to forge a new political consensus are reminiscent of 1945, when a new Labour government delivered lasting progress through a revitalised state and a massive council-ledexpansionof public housing. From now on, things will only get tougher for Britain’s seventh prime minister in a decade. But after Mr Burnham’s first day there are also grounds for hoping they will get better.Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionLabourLabour party leadershipEconomic policyKeir StarmerRachel ReevesJohn HealeyeditorialsShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on humanities cuts in universities: academic departments are more than cost centres | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: Literature, the arts and social sciences must not be sacrificed as higher education funding is squeezed
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: Literature, the arts and social sciences must not be sacrificed as higher education funding is squeezed
Content: ‘The choices that students make are partly driven by changes further down the education system.’Photograph: Andrew Fox/AlamyView image in fullscreen‘The choices that students make are partly driven by changes further down the education system.’Photograph: Andrew Fox/AlamyOpinionHigher educationThe Guardian view on humanities cuts in universities: academic departments are more than cost centresEditorialLiterature, the arts and social sciences must not be sacrificed as higher education funding is squeezedMon 20 Jul 2026 14.33 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShare“Iask nothing more from you than to do what you have been doing. Go out into the fray and keep reading,” wasPercival Everett’s advicewhen he addressed graduating students at Wesleyan university in Connecticut last summer. Fitting words from a prize-winning novelist, at a time when gambling has become a more common leisure activity in the US thanreading a book. In the UK, too, concerns are growing about the decline of reading, partly due to ubiquitous screen-based entertainment. Last week, parliament’s education committee called for theNational Year of Reading to be extendedto a decade, to inculcate the habit in children and young people.All academic disciplines use books to convey information, but for literature and the humanities more widely, they are an indispensable currency. As the 2026 graduation season draws to a close, and robes and mortarboards are put away for another year, there is growing anger that these subjects are under attack from university administrations looking for ways to save money – while ministers look away.Analysis of 2024 data for the Guardian uncoverednearly 4,000 job cutsin Shape (the acronym for social sciences, humanities and arts). All but 110 were in non-Russell Group universities, which have lower entry requirements and recruit more socioeconomically disadvantaged students, leading to concerns that some courses might become the preserve of an elite. But humanities cuts are not limited to one type of institution. Exeter plansto cut 150 full-time posts. Nottingham expects to become the first Russell Group university towithdraw all its modern foreign language degrees.Prof Susan J Smith, president of the British Academy, recently described the situation as “an emergency, threatening all subjects”, and singled out the problem of “cold spots” where some disciplines are at risk of disappearing. It is not clear that the review of higher education which the academy wants would provide a solution. But she is right to highlight the negative effects of fragmented decision-making. To accountants, some courses or departments may be viewed as cost centres. But to lecturers and students, and the places in which they are based, they are cultural resources – repositories of knowledge built up over decades, some of which will have a specific, local character and relevance.No one argues that no course should ever close. But the choices that students make are partly driven by changes further down the education system. The rising popularity of Stem (science, technology, engineering and maths) is partly due to the Conservative policy of promoting them; the fall in teenagers opting for literature is linked to an unimaginative GCSE (surely something that a new prime minister with an English degree could fix).Universities make their own decisions about what and who to teach. But ministers must be upfront about the constraints in which they operate. An expected £100m cut to teaching grants, combined with the introduction of an international student levy, mean that a years-long financial squeeze is set to carry on. In such a climate, standing up for the value of literature and the humanities ought not to be left to famous novelists.Explore more on these topicsHigher educationOpinionUniversity fundingUniversitiesHumanitiesArts and humanitiesUniversity of NottinghamUniversity of ExetereditorialsShareReuse this content

title: British workplaces and schools have to adapt to keep employees and pupils safe | Extreme heat | The Guardian
description: Letters: Ruth Wilkinson on employers’ need to safeguard staff during heatwaves, and Christine Özden on keeping children safe in overheated schools
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Ruth Wilkinson on employers’ need to safeguard staff during heatwaves, and Christine Özden on keeping children safe in overheated schools
Content: ‘Extreme temperatures can have a serious impact on worker health and safety.’Photograph: Tolga Akmen/EPAView image in fullscreen‘Extreme temperatures can have a serious impact on worker health and safety.’Photograph: Tolga Akmen/EPAExtreme heatLettersBritish workplaces and schools have to adapt to keep employees and pupils safeRuth Wilkinsononemployers’ need to safeguard staff during heatwaves, andChristine Özdenon keeping children safe in overheated schoolsMon 20 Jul 2026 12.29 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShareHannah Spencer’s proposal for maximum workplace temperatures (Green MP Hannah Spencer to introduce bill on maximum workplace temperatures, 12 July)highlights an increasingly urgent issue for employers and policymakers alike. While legislation may help provide clarity in the future, businesses should not wait for a maximum legal limit before acting to protect workers.Extreme temperatures can have a serious impact on worker health and safety as they can impair concentration and increase fatigue, both of which raise the likelihood of accidents.The law already requires employers to provide a reasonable indoor workplace temperature. Bosses are also required to assess workplace risks and take reasonably practicable steps to protect people. For hot weather, this can include amending work schedules, moving physically demanding tasks to cooler parts of the day, providing shaded rest areas and free access to drinking water, and relaxing dress codes where appropriate.The debate over a UK maximum workplace temperature is an important one. But workers need protection now, not after new legislation has worked its way through parliament.Good employers will recognise that safeguarding staff during heatwaves is not simply about compliance; it is also about preventing harm and demonstrating a genuine commitment to a safe, healthy and productive workforce.Ruth WilkinsonHead of policy and public affairs, Institution of Occupational Safety and HealthHow can children learn in overheated schools? (‘Children were calling for their mummies’: UK pupils struggle in 40C-plus classrooms, 11 July)(Classrooms: Schools ‘need a plan’ to cope with heat, 11 July). Wet paper towels and ice pops are inadequate. Our education system needs to build climate resilience – and fast.Climate action plans, required in schools by the Department for Education, are helpful. But, while there are pockets of good practice, the country needs a more systematic approach. School leaders and teachers require clear, practical and detailed guidance to take the best action against extreme weather.We can learn resilience from other countries experiencing record-breaking temperatures. In India and Pakistan, where we offer climate literacy courses,Cambridge researchershave studied how school buildings are using techniques like natural vegetation to cool school spaces and protect academic attainment. Since Covid, Cambridge OCR has been calling for a more resilient and flexible approach to assessment.In June in Bonn, I spoke on a UN climate conference panel with Unicef on loss and damage caused by climate change.My messagewas that if we invest in resilient education systems, we will not only help children recover from crisis but enable them to shape their own futures. The UK is in crisis too, and education must be factored into practical adaptation plans.Summer terms have many “milestone moments”. Hotter summers threaten these critical moments in young people’s lives. We must adapt with safe climate resilient learning infrastructure. A generation’s right to education is at stake.Christine ÖzdenGlobal director of climate education, Cambridge University Press & AssessmentHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsExtreme heatWorkers' rightsSchoolsChildrenUK weatherSummerlettersShareReuse this content

title: Palantir isn’t the only option for the NHS | NHS | The Guardian
description: Letter: Manchester’s integrated care board offers the health service a domestic alternative to the US firm, says Dr Devan Moodley
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letter: Manchester’s integrated care board offers the health service a domestic alternative to the US firm, says Dr Devan Moodley
Content: ‘If one English region can build sovereign health data infrastructure that outperforms the imported version, why can’t the country?’Photograph: Tommy London/AlamyView image in fullscreen‘If one English region can build sovereign health data infrastructure that outperforms the imported version, why can’t the country?’Photograph: Tommy London/AlamyNHSLettersPalantir isn’t the only option for the NHSManchester’s integrated care board offers the health service a domestic alternative to the US firm, saysDr Devan MoodleyMon 20 Jul 2026 12.28 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShareRegarding Peter Geoghegan’s article (I investigated Palantir’s foothold in the British state – and what I found should worry us all, 15 July), the nine years that Greater Manchester’s health system has spent without Palantir is a result not of caution but bold conviction.Greater Manchester’s integrated care board remains the only one in England to decline the Palantir-built federated data platform, having concluded that its own homegrown data capability was stronger. It built that capability with British universities, NHS clinicians and domestic technology firms.IfNHSstaff don’t like this tech, and if Andy Burnham believes this use of tech is turning off voters, he should carry that same approach into Downing Street and put power back in the hands of UK companies.As healthcare becomes increasingly dependent on digital platforms, cloud infrastructure, AI systems and data management technology, there is a growing risk of an excessive reliance on a small number of dominant overseas – particularly American – technology companies.This is not anti-American sentiment; it is common sense. Downing Street should ask a simple question: if one English region can build sovereign health data infrastructure that outperforms the imported version, why can’t the country? The domestic alternative is not hypothetical. It is already running.Dr Devan MoodleyCEO,HealthConnect GlobalHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsNHSHealthPalantirManchesterEnglandData protectionlettersShareReuse this content

title: Will a cohabitation law be bad for couples? | Cohabitation | The Guardian
description: Letters: Readers on the pros and cons of the government’s plan to give cohabiting partners marriage-like rights
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Readers on the pros and cons of the government’s plan to give cohabiting partners marriage-like rights
Content: ‘Countries across the world have introduced sensible protections for cohabiting couples without undermining marriage or having the catastrophic consequences opponents predict.’Photograph: Catherine Falls Commercial/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Countries across the world have introduced sensible protections for cohabiting couples without undermining marriage or having the catastrophic consequences opponents predict.’Photograph: Catherine Falls Commercial/Getty ImagesCohabitationLettersWill a cohabitation law be bad for couples?Readers on the pros and cons of the government’s plan to give cohabitingpartners marriage-like rightsMon 20 Jul 2026 12.31 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.25 EDTShareRuth Deech’s article opposing cohabitation law reform overlooks the reality faced by 3.5 million cohabiting couples in the UK who are not married (David Lammy’s proposed cohabitation law would be bad for couples – and worse for some women, 14 July). While she argues that couples who choose not to marry should not acquire legal rights automatically, the current system of no rights leaves too many vulnerable people, particularly women and children, without meaningful protection when a relationship ends or a partner dies. Many still mistakenly believe in the myth of “common law marriage”. The proposals aim to strike a balance between respecting autonomy and protecting vulnerable people.The law must reflect modern life. Many couples raise children, share finances and build lives together without marrying, sometimes for cultural, personal or financial reasons, and often because one person doesn’t want to marry. That should not mean that the financially weaker party is left facing hardship.Reform is also essential for victims of domestic abuse. Many remain trapped because they simply cannot afford to leave an abusive relationship without any legal or financial safety net. A clear legal framework should provide greater certainty and fairness while remaining straightforward enough for people to navigate. An important aspect of the proposals is the ability to opt out of the suggested legal framework.Other countries, such as Australia and New Zealand, have introduced sensible protections for cohabiting couples without undermining marriage or having the catastrophic consequences opponents predict. The consultation offers a once-in-a-generation opportunity to modernise family law and create a fairer system of rights and responsibilities. This is a pivotal moment for family law reform and one that professional organisations such as Resolution have campaigned for for decades. It is an opportunity that the government is right to seize.Jo EdwardsChair,family law reform committee, ResolutionRuth Deech is right – we must resist cohabitation law reform. It is premised on a patronising and outmoded model, according to which a dependent partner (assumed to be a woman), finds that, when discarded by a much wealthier partner (assumed to be a man), she does not enjoy the same “protection” of the law as married people. But cohabitants choose not to marry for many different reasons; they are not always ignorant of the law and may be rightly suspicious of just how protective court-sanctioned divorce-like settlements will be.Those with property will have ensured that they co-own their home (mortgagees insist on it) and those with access to pensions will have built up their own. This means that at the end of a relationship, they will not be left penniless, but will enjoy rights independent of their partner, which the law cannot touch. If the law steps in to regulate cohabitation, those careful financial arrangements will be up for grabs, often by less-than-deserving men claiming a share of their former partner’s assets.Some years ago when I interviewed people who had dissolved their civil partnerships, many were furious to discover that on dissolution of a civil partnership, as on divorce, their previously agreed financial arrangements could be overturned by a court impressed by a former partner’s apparent “dependency”. This is what cohabitation law reform will do.It is always better to have one’s own assets than to rely on a partner who, statistics show, may not always be there, or always solvent, and better to have one’s independent rights than to look to the courts for a remedy that history shows is rarely generous to women.Rosemary AuchmutyProfessor of law emerita, University of ReadingHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsCohabitationMarriageMenWomenFamilyEquality Act 2010Equal marriagelettersShareReuse this content

title: Ensure children can play outside independently | Children's health | The Guardian
description: Letters: Urban transport policies and residential design need to respect outdoor play as a vital necessity for children, says Ellen Weaver
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Urban transport policies and residential design need to respect outdoor play as a vital necessity for children, says Ellen Weaver
Content: ‘The consequences are disastrous … when we prevent children from access to free play and independence.’Photograph: monkeybusinessimages/Getty Images/iStockphotoView image in fullscreen‘The consequences are disastrous … when we prevent children from access to free play and independence.’Photograph: monkeybusinessimages/Getty Images/iStockphotoChildren's healthLettersEnsure children can play outside independentlyUrban transport policies and residential design need to respect outdoor play as a vital necessity for children, saysEllen WeaverMon 20 Jul 2026 12.26 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 12.28 EDTShareIn the brilliant Swiss research cited by Lenore Skenazy(If you want your kids to thrive this summer, don’t just turn off their phones – open your front door, too,15 July), young children playing unsupervised was culturally accepted and normal. The children in the Swiss study who did not play outside independently were prevented from doing so by traffic.Yes, we need to relieve parents of the horrendous pressure of cultural expectation that children will always be supervised, but parents can’t open their doors for children to go out and play in traffic. Urban transport policies and residential design need to respect outdoor play as a vital necessity for children.The Swiss research proved that playgrounds don’t meet children’s needs for play. In the study, the parents of children who couldn’t play out where they lived frequently took them to playgrounds. Nevertheless, those children were significantly limited in their personal confidence, cognitive development, social abilities, and fine and gross motor skills, compared with children who played out by their homes, where the amount and quality of play was far greater.You recentlyreportedthat UK children will be one of unhealthiest generations in decades.In much of the rest of Europe, it is still normal for children to go places without an adult taking them. Children meet up with friends to play in public spaces without anyone calling the police – no wonder they’re much more active, confident and healthy. The consequences are disastrous and enormously costly when we prevent children from access to sufficient free play and independence.Tech companies must be made to end harmful, addictive content, but we shouldn’t be surprised that children play virtually when so often they cannot actually play where they live. Andy Burnham should join the dots, take play seriously and appoint a minister for children.Ellen WeaverHartland, DevonExplore more on these topicsChildren's healthChildrenSmartphonesParents and parentingAccess to green spacelettersShareReuse this content

title: DeChambeau moves the dial for golf but is his posturing and pantomime worth the hassle? | Bryson DeChambeau | The Guardian
description: Petulant reaction to a justified two-stroke penalty at the Open illustrates why the American is happier on the LIV tour
Author: Ewan Murray
Headline: Petulant reaction to a justified two-stroke penalty at the Open illustrates why the American is happier on the LIV tour
Content: Bryson DeChambeau during his third round of the Open at Royal Birkdale.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianView image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau during his third round of the Open at Royal Birkdale.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianBryson DeChambeauDeChambeau moves the dial for golf but is his posturing and pantomime worth the hassle?Ewan MurrayPetulant reaction to a justified two-stroke penalty at the Open illustrates why the American is happier on the LIV tourMon 20 Jul 2026 11.05 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.38 EDTShareThe potential for stigma was laid bare close to the conclusion of Saturday at the Open Championship. “Bryson, ya cheat,” a lone voice bellowed from the stand adjacent to Royal Birkdale’s 18th green. This is an environment in which being unfaithful to one’s spouse would be seen as preferable to breaching the sacred rules of a sacred game.By Sunday the world No 1, Scottie Scheffler, was riding to the defence ofBryson DeChambeau. “I’ve known him for a long time and he’s a lot of things, but he’s definitely not a cheater,” Scheffler said. Except in this case, nuance is required. No one in authority has actually accused DeChambeau of cheating for the incident which incurred a two-stroke penalty. The R&A’s executive director of governance stipulated that DeChambeau’s action when breaching the rules during round two was “accidental”.Fleetwood, Fox and fury: Tom Jenkins’ best images from the OpenRead moreIt was an odd thing for Grant Moir to say, which smacked not only as if trying to placate an irate DeChambeau but working a little too hard to protect the reputation of the Open. It does not matter whether DeChambeau’s action in stamping down long grass behind his ball was deliberate or not.Intention is irrelevant, just as it was whenShane Lowry incurred a two-stroke penaltyin the Open of 2025. Then, as on Friday, a rule was breached. The R&A’s misstep here was nonetheless a trivial one. DeChambeau was in the wrong, far more in respect of his petulant response to the situation than the fact that it arose in the first place.If sport were pure, this Open would be known forRyan Fox’s nerveless finish. The popular New Zealander earned his maiden major win in emphatic fashion. The pace and simplicity with which Fox plies his trade offers lessons for us all.It was, instead, a tournament dominated by matters involving DeChambeau. By Monday morning, the R&A was fielding requests over whetherDeChambeau threatened to bring Donald Trumpinto heated discussions over his sanction on Friday evening. While this scenario was easy to believe, such action would also be futile; given the R&A has left Trump’s Turnberry resort in the Open wilderness for years, it is hardly ever going to let the US president influence a leaderboard. Trump has always been deferential towards the R&A. DeChambeau’s protests felt telling. This is a player subject to very little external scrutiny on the LIV tour. He never appeared to have any intention of fielding media questions on the Open affair.Golf has endured or enjoyed, depending on one’s outlook, a heated news cycle for years. It has been a notable and intriguing shift. Those who insist that the sport at core level is boring are ignorant to the fact that it is no more or less tedious than cricket, Formula One or cycling.View image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau talks to an R&A rules official on the 18th hole of his third round.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianLIV’s arrival and subsequent fallout had just about settled down by the time Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund determined golf was an expense it could do without. Cue the latest, ongoing theme; LIV’s probable demise and how a fractured sport pieces itself together again. Birdies and bogeys, brilliance such as that delivered by Fox, are all still covered but the noise outside the ropes matters more than ever before.In this sense, DeChambeau and associated chaos was an Open gift. Rory McIlroy, once a defender of DeChambeau, said what a lot of players were understood to be thinkingby berating the Americanfor the posturing and pantomime of Friday. The R&A’s hierarchy, who were furious at DeChambeau, surely approved. McIlroy’s remarks lit up the internet far more than Sam Burns’s run to the top of the leaderboard.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |FortnightlySign up toThe HotspotA new fortnightly newsletter covering the intersection of sport and the climate crisisPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe question is whether golf needs the hassle. We may be about to find out. DeChambeau’s clear preference is to remain on the LIV circuit, even in modified form, if fresh funding can be sourced for 2027. If that does not come to pass, or if DeChambeau reckons a lack of competition there is harming his chances on bigger stages, he will have to beat a path back towards thePGA Tour. That circuit will have no intention of making life easy for DeChambeau but what price the eyeballs, the drama, the noise that some insist this sport needs? DeChambeau up against McIlroy on a regular basis would be box office.If DeChambeau has his way, no one will look too closely at a major record for 2026 which involves three missed cuts and a tie for 14th. His Open Sunday was dismal, from an earlier position of great promise. Nonetheless, an expectant golf public awaits DeChambeau’s bulletin on all things Birkdale; most likely coming to a media outlet influenced by the man himself very soon. In DeChambeau, LIV has an attention-grabbing asset.DeChambeau is treading a fine line between force for good and comedic distraction. The sport’s relationship with this player is a complex one. Birkdale did Bryson no favours.Explore more on these topicsBryson DeChambeauThe OpenPGA TourGolfUS sportscommentShareReuse this content

title: England players question ‘brutal’ travel demands in Nations Championship format  | England rugby union team | The Guardian
description: England’s players have questioned the Nations Championship format after being required to play Test matches on three different continents on successive weekends
Author: Robert Kitson
Headline: England’s players have questioned the Nations Championship format after being required to play Test matches on three different continents on successive weekends
Content: Ben Earl described England’s global schedule this summer as a ‘tough way of ending’ the season.Photograph: Joaquin Camiletti/RFU/The RFU Collection/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenBen Earl described England’s global schedule this summer as a ‘tough way of ending’ the season.Photograph: Joaquin Camiletti/RFU/The RFU Collection/Getty ImagesEngland rugby union teamEngland players question ‘brutal’ travel demands in Nations Championship formatPlayed in South Africa, England and Argentina across three weekendsSquad covered 26,000 miles this month in hectic travel scheduleRobert Kitsonin Buenos AiresMon 20 Jul 2026 13.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEngland’s players have questioned the currentNations Championshipformat after being required to play Test matches on three different continents on successive weekends.Steve Borthwick’s squad have covered 26,000 miles this month and at least one senior player believes the tournament should consider reducing the travel demands for all involved.Having spent the past three weeks flitting between Johannesburg, Liverpool and Santiago del Estero for games against South Africa, Fiji and Argentina respectively, the England No 8 Ben Earl is among those with strong views about the logistical demands of the schedule. “It’s been brutal,” said Earl, who was in the starting XV for all three fixtures.Feyi-Waboso gives England more bite but there is no ignoring elephant in the roomRead more“There’s no two ways about it, it’s tough, but we’ve got to do what we can and try to make the best of it. At the end of what’s been quite a long season for most of us, it’s been a tough way of ending: back and forth to England, seeing your families and then going away again. It has seemed a bit weird but it’s something we’re going to have to get used to. I think it’s just the beginning.”England have been the team most obviously affected this month, with Ireland having insisted on facing Japan on neutral ground in Australia rather than trekking to Tokyo as well as Sydney and Auckland. England’s players, however, believe all involved would benefit from more teams being based in the same location.View image in fullscreenBen Earl scored twice for England in their victory against Argentina.Photograph: Gero Uranga/Shutterstock“We’ve been speaking about it,” confirmed Earl. “You just go to one nation and have a festival feel. Why would we not all be playing in one country? I don’t know the financials but how good would that be for the fans? You come to Australia, South Africa or wherever and there’s just so much rugby going on. How cool would that be? [But] I’m not paid to make those decisions. We just get told where to go and where to play, and we just try and do that.”The Nations Championship was launched this year as a broadcaster-friendly alternative to traditional tours, with all gamesavailable to watch free-to-air on ITV. From a playing perspective, however, the extra travel involved has affected the amount of preparation time available to visiting teams. “You just don’t do as much training,” confirmed Earl. “You train a little bit later in the week and you train harder, closer to the game.“You’ve got to do certain things in the week to be able to perform. There are myriad different things that make it tricky but we’ve been adaptable. For those of us who were on the British & Irish Lions tour last summer, it was pretty similar.”With a busy World Cup year in prospect in 2027, however, the likes of Earl currently have no choice other than to grit their teeth if they are to retain their Test positions. “I love playing for my country,” stressed Earl, who rounded off his personal campaignwith two tries against Argentinaon Saturday. “You forget about all that other stuff the moment you pull on the shirt. Once you get to the stadiums and into a warm-up (everything else) pales into insignificance.”Explore more on these topicsEngland rugby union teamNations ChampionshipRugby unionnewsShareReuse this content

title: England’s white-ball success risks baking in a divide with future Test setup | England cricket team | The Guardian
description: Finding a candidate to lead the red-ball side is no easy task, with Ben Stokes gone and Brendon McCullum still around
Author: Ali Martin
Headline: Finding a candidate to lead the red-ball side is no easy task, with Ben Stokes gone and Brendon McCullum still around
Content: Harry Brook and Brendon McCullum appear to have a good rapport as a leadership pairing for England.Photograph: Rafiq Maqbool/APView image in fullscreenHarry Brook and Brendon McCullum appear to have a good rapport as a leadership pairing for England.Photograph: Rafiq Maqbool/APEngland cricket teamAnalysisEngland’s white-ball success risks baking in a divide with future Test setupAli MartinFinding a candidate to lead the red-ball side is no easy task, with Ben Stokes gone and Brendon McCullum still aroundMon 20 Jul 2026 07.50 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 14.58 EDTShareAs England’s men scatter for the Hundred, ready to pull on new colours, ingratiate themselves with their new paymasters and use the word “exciting” repeatedly, the management above them could be forgiven for letting out a sigh of relief.Not that everything is shipshape going into their month-long recess. Far from it. Test cricket is king in this country, No 1 across all age groups and demographics – as per Richard Gould, chief executive of the England and WalesCricketBoard, in his Ashes review in March – and in that regard the first half of the season still goes down as a bit of a shambles.Langer leads shortlist to become next England Test coach after Flower rules himself outRead moreThe 2-1 defeat by New Zealand forced the removal ofBrendon McCullumfrom one half of his role as head coach, while a strung out Ben Stokes threw himself overboard into retirement and was last seen playing for Durham against Northumberland in front of 300 people at Allendale Cricket Club. Three Tests on from Gould and Rob Key insisting the status quo with just a bit more rigour was the way forward, the team have no coach and no captain.Butthe T20andODI seriesvictories, against an India side at No 1 in the rankings for both, should not to be sniffed at. McCullum’s retention for the limited-over teams was not universally popular but over the past year he and Harry Brook really do seem to have struck upon something as a leadership pairing.India admittedly chose to reset their world champion T20 side and a one-sided 4-0 defeat showed they swung the pendulum too far. But England’s 2-1 victory in the one-dayers displayed some serious gumption from 1-0 down, the wins in Cardiff and Lord’s equally hard fought but achieved in wildly contrasting conditions.Cardiff was a low-scoring dog-fight that owed plenty to the enduring class ofJoe Root, whereas Lord’s was a high-scoring gunfight on an unusually flat pitch. That England found a way in both – even if Jasprit Bumrah’s absence from the series decider was a bonus – said a lot about the variety they have cultivated.For all that England are playing catch-up before the 2027 World Cup after a few years of 50-over neglect, Brook leads a side with bases covered. His batting lineup has touch players, power players, and left-right combinations. Brook also had eight varied bowling options at Lord’s: three quicks, wrist-spin, a left-arm angle, two better than part-time off-spinners and Jacob Bethell tweaking it the other way.Brook’s use of Bethell in particular goes back to the chemistry struck with McCullum, having run off the field mid-innings for advice and been told that a spinner driving the ball into the surface à la Ravindra Jadeja was the play. The wicket this produced of Rohit Sharma on 138 was gamechanging.Although, this is where things get tricky and we circle back to the strange place that England find themselves. Because for all that white-ball success has mildly offset the anger caused by the Test team’s further nosedive, it risks baking in a divide that may be hard to square when the vacancies for Test coach and captain are filled.Right now, England seemingly have only two options for captain. One is Root, who would be doing it out of a sense of duty rather than any desire, and the other is Brook himself. Neither is ideal, while both would need strong guidance on tactics and leadership from the new coach: Root because he did previously – and never quite received it – and Brook because everything would be new to him.In an ideal world, Stokes would have held the role until the 2027 Ashes, by which time Brook’s education as vice-captain would have been greater and he could slide across post-World Cup. This is far from an ideal world, however, and for all that his early appointment would invite questions about his maturity on and off the field, a personal view is that he is the correct, forward-looking choice.But as well as the burden that a three-format captaincy would place on Brook, can doing this under two separate coaches work in practice? Whoever is tasked with nursing Brook through the early stages of the Test role, they will need a strong alignment with McCullum so as not to overwhelm him with conflicting methods.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe SpinSubscribe to our cricket newsletter for our writers' thoughts on the biggest stories and a review of the week’s actionPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionAndy Flower, the leading coach in the world,turned down the vacancy last weekciting an incompatibility with the two franchise teams to which he is contracted. But for all that they probably are incompatible – both the logistics and the message it would send – one wonders whether he also spotted these limitations.Attractive though the job is, candidates will look at the setup and surely clock that no one bar Stokes – the one player any coach would want –has left the building. Essentially, the person being asked to put out the fire still raging fromthat failed Ashes campaignalso needs to fit in with the people who caused it.All of which goes back to that tour review in March, the upshot of which was to keep everyone in situ,hire Marcus North as a selector(not head selector, tellingly), flood training with extra coaching bodies, and offer a nod to county cricket by arranging quarterly meetings with domestic coaches andpicking the in-form Emilio Gay. Oh, andOllie Robinson came back for one Test.Had more fundamental change come about back then – including an approach for Flower with the promise of a far blanker canvas – there is a chance Stokes might have been reinvigorated at the start of the summer, rather than triggered by walking back into an environment in which he had stopped believing.Who knows, perhaps Gould and Key will find the ideal candidate to make this split-screen approach work. As good as the two white-ball series wins were, and as much as the next month will be about some fun, disposable, content-generation cricket, they can ill afford another mis-step in the format that is still No 1.Explore more on these topicsEngland cricket teamHarry BrookJoe RootBen StokesBrendon McCullumAndy FlowerCricketanalysisShareReuse this content

title: F1’s algorithms spark driver fury as numbers neuter thrills of Spa-Francorchamps | Formula One 2026 | The Guardian
description: Computer-controlled energy deployment now dictates races – it turns out drivers don’t like Skynet controlling their cars
Author: Giles Richards
Headline: Computer-controlled energy deployment now dictates races – it turns out drivers don’t like Skynet controlling their cars
Content: A furious George Russell clambers from his Mercedes after crashing out of the Belgian Grand Prix.Photograph: Jens Mommens/Momenol Images/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenA furious George Russell clambers from his Mercedes after crashing out of the Belgian Grand Prix.Photograph: Jens Mommens/Momenol Images/ShutterstockFormula One 2026AnalysisF1’s algorithms spark driver fury as numbers neuter thrills of Spa-FrancorchampsGiles RichardsComputer-controlled energy deployment now dictates races – it turns out drivers don’t like Skynet controlling their carsMon 20 Jul 2026 14.12 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.37 EDTShareAnother race down another classic circuit, in this case Spa-Francorchamps, left neutered for drivers and so their slow, dissonant discontent rumbles ever onward. In the wake ofthe Belgian Grand Prix, there was an overwhelming sense they wished to simply escape. Not so much from Spa but away from the numbers.George Russell was at the centre of attention in Belgium, the Mercedes driver’s championship hopes taking a severe blow as, inexplicably to him and his team, his engine did not recover energy through turn one and he was left with no electrical power to deploy. He was swallowed up by the Ferraris and vying with Lewis Hamilton, who clipped him through Les Combes, was pitched out of the race.Kimi Antonelli tightens F1 title grip with Belgian GP win after Russell crashes outRead moreHe was understandably furious. Not with Hamilton but that the energy issue meant he was in a fight with him at all. “I had no fucking battery down the straight. Guys, unacceptable,” he told the team over the radio.Mercedes are investigating but his fury is the tip of the iceberg of the knock-on effects of this year’s regulations that were once more laid bare in Spa.Nor was it a surprise, pre-race predictions from drivers proving all too prescient. With two sectors of long straights and little chance to harvest, the cars were energy starved, reducing the middle sector to internal combustion engine power only, effectively half of their potential output. It meant the cars were so down on power that corners such as Pouhon, an exquisite test of risk and reward, of seeing quite how far a car could be pushed, were now barely worth a consideration.Cornering with the speed of an F3 car but with F1 downforce had emasculated one of Formula One’s great tests, as Lando Norris noted: “It’s not a corner any more, it was easy flat.”Race-winner Kimi Antonelli’s pole time was almost four seconds slower than last year’s and while F1 is taking comfort in the fact that the racing is entertaining, those who drive the cars continue to bemoan the actual experience behind the wheel.Part of Russell’s experience at Spa highlighted another issue that cuts to their very core – that they too have been neutered. The computer-controlled deployment of electrical energy now so central to the sport has become F1’s new darkest art, more arcane and far less interesting even than the tyre management which used to be of such import. Remember when tyres were a thing rather than an algorithm?View image in fullscreenFans watch the race up Eau Rouge on Sunday.Photograph: Florent Gooden/DPPI/ShutterstockThe algorithms are now vital, determining when and how much energy to deploy during a lap, and drivers are, to an extent, at their mercy. It turns out they don’t like Skynet driving their cars.When asked about the importance of these algorithms and energy deployment in Spa, Max Verstappen gave an entertainingly blunt answer. “Welcome to F1 2026, sometimes it can be a jungle,” he said.Verstappen has been clear in his dislike of the role of energy management but in Spa it was damningly clear that his views were widespread. McLaren’s Oscar Piastri, ordinarily an even-handed observer – who notably was also suffering from an unfathomable deployment issue from his engine that was robbing him of time – had lost all patience with the AI overlords nestling in the F1 matrix.“It sucks. I can’t really say it any other way than that,” he said. “George has had a lot of issues with that this weekend and maybe the last couple of weekends, and speaking to some others, it’s a similar story.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |FortnightlySign up toThe HotspotA new fortnightly newsletter covering the intersection of sport and the climate crisisPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“When you’ve got qualifying grids decided by computers behaving or misbehaving, it’s a pretty crap way of going racing.”View image in fullscreen‘A pretty crap way of going racing’ – McLaren’s Oscar Piastri is not enjoying the 2026 season.Photograph: Eric Alonso/DPPI/ShutterstockNor did it end there. Just as damning was Antonelli. “Sometimes, especially on tracks like this that are very energy-demanding if you start driving differently, then for the system it’s a bit crazy,” he said.No one wants a crazy system, not least when unexpected changes in closing speed can be extraordinarily dangerous, as was demonstrated by the great sudden concentration of cars hurtling past one another on the Kemmel straight and into Les Combes on that opening lap.Second-placed Charles Leclerc was part of it and came up like a rocket behind Verstappen. “These power units are so complex that there are some days when you scratch your head trying to understand why you are losing so much on the straights,” he said. “It’s just very difficult but also very frustrating sometimes for a driver because even when you are doing your best there are things in the background that change constantly and that’s not so nice.”So it goes on. It is not a good look for the sport but it is not going to change this season at least and will come up again and again. On the basis of the reactions to Spa, the sport had better brace itself for an orgiastic wailing and gnashing of teeth when it arrives in Monza.Explore more on these topicsFormula One 2026Formula OneMotor sportanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Vingegaard’s and Pogacar’s night-time doping tests authorised by Paris judge | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: The International Testing Agency had petitioned the Paris prosecutor’s office to test the duo outside of the normal 6am to 11pm window before stage 15
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: The International Testing Agency had petitioned the Paris prosecutor’s office to test the duo outside of the normal 6am to 11pm window before stage 15
Content: Tadej Pogacar and Jonas Vingegaard were subjected to unexpected nocturnal doping controls before stage 15 of the Tour de France.Photograph: Stéphanie Lecocq/ReutersView image in fullscreenTadej Pogacar and Jonas Vingegaard were subjected to unexpected nocturnal doping controls before stage 15 of the Tour de France.Photograph: Stéphanie Lecocq/ReutersTour de France 2026Vingegaard’s and Pogacar’s night-time doping tests authorised by Paris judgeInternational Testing Agency petitioned for testsDuo tested outside of normal 6am to 11pm windowJeremy Whittlein La Roche sur ForonMon 20 Jul 2026 16.24 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 17.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA Paris judge authorised the night-time doping tests of theTour de France leader, Tadej Pogacar, and his nearest rival, Jonas Vingegaard, in the early hours of Sunday morning.The Paris prosecutor’s office confirmed on Monday that the International Testing Agency (ITA) petitioned the “Paris judge specialising in liberty and detention matters … to authorise the anti-doping tests that took place overnight between July 18 and 19”.Vingegaard crashes out of Tour de France after ‘inhuman’ 2am doping controlRead moreVingegaard and Pogacar were subjected to unexpected nocturnal doping controls, outside the normal window of 6am to 11pm, before stage 15, as the peloton headed to Plateau de Solaison in the Savoyard Alps.Vingegaard was tested at 2am on Sunday by the ITA, while Pogacar’s unexpected wake-up call came at 5am. Pogacar described Vingegaard’s 2am test as “inhuman” and suggested that it “ruined” his longstanding rival’s sleep and could have been afactor in the crashon Sunday, in which he broke his collarbone, and forced him to quit the Tour.Before the stage start on Sunday, Vingegaard said: “I was sleeping very well. It’s good that they test, but when it’s affecting performance and your sleep, I don’t think it’s so good.”Under the anti-doping regulations of the InternationalCyclingUnion (UCI) which delegated its anti-doping programme to the ITA in 2021, the UCI must have “serious and specific suspicions that the rider may be involved in doping activities” to conduct tests between 11pm and 6am. The judges responsible for liberty and detention (JLD) “have concurrent jurisdiction to rule on requests for night-time anti-doping tests”, the Paris public prosecutor’s office told AFP.The timing of the tests was widely condemned by leading riders and team staff, including Remco Evenepoel, now second overall in the Tour, and Vingegaard’s teammate Matteo Jorgenson, who said it showed “a complete lack of respect” towards the riders.The ITA said it was “conscious that night-time testing may be disruptive to riders’ rest and recovery. Due consideration has therefore been given to minimise as much as possible the impact testing activities have on riders. However, the ITA, on behalf of the UCI, has the responsibility to ensure the effectiveness of the cycling anti‑doping programme and the integrity of cycling races.”It is now also believed that up to five teams, including Pogacar’s UAE Team Emirates XRG and Vingegaard’s Visma Lease-a-bike, have been subject to nocturnal testing during this year’s Tour. While not specifying numbers, the ITA confirmed to the Guardian that “other teams also have been subjected to ‘night-time’ or early morning testing”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |FortnightlySign up toThe HotspotA new fortnightly newsletter covering the intersection of sport and the climate crisisPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionNight-time testing was introduced primarily to combat the use of micro-dosing of performance-enhancing products, a practice which allows only a short window for detection.Jonathan Vaughters, now manager of the EF-Education EasyPost team currently riding in the Tour, and a former teammate to Lance Armstrong who was stripped of seven Tour wins for doping, supported the nocturnal tests. Vaughters, posting on X, said: “Two am testing is one of the very effective ways to prevent peptide (EPO, HGH) and testosterone micro dosing. If [the] UCI had tested us in 2001 at 2am? We’d all have been suspended and saved the sport 25 years of drama.”The Tour ends in Paris on Sunday evening but there is no known timescale for the publication of results. The ITA added that the “outcome of these controls, as any other conducted by the ITA, would be published according to our public disclosure policy, only in the case of an apparent anti-doping rule violation”.Explore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Jonas VingegaardTadej PogacarTour de FranceCyclingnewsShareReuse this content

title: Melvine Malard hungry for trophies after joining Chelsea from Manchester United | Chelsea Women | The Guardian
description: Chelsea have announced the signing of the France forward Melvine Malard on a four-year deal from Manchester United for about £850,000
Author: Suzanne Wrack
Headline: Chelsea have announced the signing of the France forward Melvine Malard on a four-year deal from Manchester United for about £850,000
Content: Melvine Malard scored six times in the Women’s Super League and four times in the Champions League for Manchester United last season.Photograph: Ian Walton/Ian Walton/Chelsea FCView image in fullscreenMelvine Malard scored six times in the Women’s Super League and four times in the Champions League for Manchester United last season.Photograph: Ian Walton/Ian Walton/Chelsea FCChelsea WomenMelvine Malard hungry for trophies after joining Chelsea from Manchester UnitedUnited get club-record fee of about £850,000 for forwardMalard follows McCabe and Matsukubo into ChelseaSuzanne WrackMon 20 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.17 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleChelsea have announced the signing of the France forward Melvine Malard from Manchester United on a four-year contract. The Guardianrevealed last weekthat a deal was close for the 26-year-old, who has left United after three seasons for about £850,000.Malard had one year on her contract and United were keen to keep the forward. However, once it became clear Malard wanted a move United decided to sell rather than lose a high-value player on a free next summer.Women’s transfer window summer 2026: all deals from world’s top six leaguesRead moreIt is believed United will reinvest the fee, which is a record sale for the club, into the squad, with the transfer window open until 3 September.United have made one summer signing, the full-back Andrea Medina from Atlético Madrid, but more incomings are expected after the departures of Leah Galton, Hannah Blundell, Sienna Wareing, Millie Turner, Lisa Naalsund and Malard.“I’m so happy because I have a plan for my life and my career, and Chelsea were a part of that plan,” Malard said. ”My objective is to become better every single day and to win trophies. I know Chelsea always have that as an objective.”The France international played under Chelsea’s head coach, Sonia Bompastor, at Lyonnes (formerly Lyon) and was recruited to their academy by Bompastor. “I have known Sonia since I was 14 years old, so it is great to work with her again,” Malard said.It is understood Chelsea have been tracking Malard for some time. They have lost a number of players across the forward line, with the club record-goalscorer Sam Kerr and Guro Reitendeparting for Gotham FC, Catarina Macario joining San Diego Wave and Johanna Rytting Kaneryd signing for Lyonnes.Malard is Chelsea’s third signing of the summer, after the former Arsenal full-back Katie McCabe and the forward Manaka Matsukubo, who was with North Carolina Courage.Malard spent seven seasons at Lyonnes, winning four Champions Leagues, three league titles and two Coupes de France. Her first season with United was on loan.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMoving the GoalpostsNo topic is too small or too big for us to cover as we deliver a twice-weekly roundup of the wonderful world of women’s footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLast season she scored four goals in United’s run to a first Champions League quarter-final and six times in 18 Women’s Super League games.Liverpoolhave signed the Japanese attacking midfielder Mao Itamura from Feyenoord. The 19-year-old, who won the Under-20 Women’s Asian Cup in April, was the Eredivisie player of the year last season in the Netherlands. She scored eight times and provided six assists as Feyenoord qualified for European competition for the first time, their third-place finish earning a place in the Europa Cup.Itamura is Liverpool’s fifth summer signing, after Vivien Endemann, Sara Agrez, Mari Ward and Khiara Keating.Explore more on these topicsChelsea WomenManchester United WomenWomen's Super LeagueTransfer windowWomen's footballnewsShareReuse this content

title: New York sculptor spent $100,000 to make a Charlie Kirk statue no one wants to buy | Charlie Kirk shooting | The Guardian
description: Sergio Furnari said he thought the lifesize sculpture – which no one commissioned – would raise ‘millions’ of dollars
Author: Adam Gabbatt
Headline: Sergio Furnari said he thought the lifesize sculpture – which no one commissioned – would raise ‘millions’ of dollars
Content: Sergio Furnari's Charlie Kirk statue.Photograph: Sergio FurnariView image in fullscreenSergio Furnari's Charlie Kirk statue.Photograph: Sergio FurnariCharlie Kirk shootingNew York sculptor spent $100,000 to make a Charlie Kirk statue no one wants to buySergio Furnari said he thought the lifesize sculpture – which no one commissioned – would raise ‘millions’ of dollarsAdam GabbattMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleANew Yorksculptor has been left furious after spending $100,000 of his own money to build a lifesize statue of Charlie Kirk, only to find that no one wants to buy it.Sergio Furnari, originally from Italy, had hoped the statue of the deceased far-right activist, which he intends to display in Times Square, would raise “millions” of dollars. Instead, he said he has been inundated by messages from people saying they intend to urinate on the sculpture.To add injury to insult, Furnari recently broke his foot working on the statue, after tripping over a roll of tape in his studio.“Is it cursed? Maybe. I don’t know,” he told the Guardian.Kirk, a far-right political figure who served as a sort of Pied Piper for young Republicans through his Turning Point USA foundation, was shot dead in September last year. He was known for his racist and bigoted views, using his podcastto claimthat “prowling Blacks go around for fun to go target white people” andspeculating: “If I see a Black pilot, I’m going to be like: ‘Boy, I hope he’s qualified.’”Despite, or perhaps because of, such beliefs, Kirk became a hugely celebrated and influential figure in Maga circles. In New York City, one of the most diverse, and Democratic, cities in the US, Kirk is far less popular.Furnari said he did not agree with Kirk on “certain issues”.“I wasn’t a fan of Charlie,” he said. “Like one thing that I couldn’t stand about him while he was alive [was] when he was talking about Palestine. [Kirk would say]: ‘Oh no, Palestine doesn’t exist.’ I thought: ‘Well, this guy must be out of his mind.’”Instead, Furnari said he made the statue in an effort to “glorify and to exercise the first amendment, this freedom of speech” – although at points during the conversation, Furnari also said he made it to support Charlie Kirk’s wife; to “glorify democracy”; and to “commemorate a person”.View image in fullscreenSergio Furnari with his statue of Charlie Kirk.Photograph: Sergio FurnariHe later added: “Why am I making the statue? The same reason why Michelangelo did the Pietà, or Michelangelo did the David, or Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa. Why? Just to commemorate a moment, you know, a person.”It’s also clear, however, that Furnari, despite no one having commissioned him to make the statue, was hoping to make money. He said he had expected unnamed wealthy figures to donate to him – he spent $100,000 on steel, resin and labor – but that has not come to pass.In desperation, Furnari set up an online fundraising campaign, hoping to raise an initial $150,000, but potentially millions of dollars, which he said would allow him to take the statue on tour. As of Thursday, he had raised $1,400.“I was thinking that it was going to resonate with the world, with whoever, but man, people are strange out there,” he said. “Meaning like, nobody is really coming up with support, especially financial support. So far I’ve spent all my money on this.”Regardless of his broken foot and light wallet, Furnari said he did not really believe the Kirk statue was cursed. “The statue is not cursed. The statue is blessed,” he said, in spite of all available evidence – but he has certainly been left angry.“Things financially are a total, absolute nightmare. It’s like wow. People are cheap, man. You know, people talk, talk – I know lots of people out there, and everybody’s so cheap. You know, like, it’s disgusting how cheap they are.”Furnari’s latest plan is to place the statue in Times Square on 10 September – the anniversary of Kirk’s death. He hopes that will drum up interest, and maybe allow him to recoup some of his costs.Until then, Furnari is left with a 6ft 4in statue of a renowned racist, which he has spent $100,000 making, and which no one wants to buy. Maybe it is cursed after all.Explore more on these topicsCharlie Kirk shootingNew YorkSculpturenewsShareReuse this content

title: Trump says Benjamin Netanyahu ‘will not be arrested’ if he visits the US | Donald Trump | The Guardian
description: Israeli prime minister is subject of an arrest warrant issued by international criminal court over alleged war crimes in Gaza
Author: Maya Yang
Headline: Israeli prime minister is subject of an arrest warrant issued by international criminal court over alleged war crimes in Gaza
Content: Donald Trump shakes hands with Benjamin Netanyahu at his Mar-a-Lago club on 29 December 2025 in Palm Beach, Florida.Photograph: Joe Raedle/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenDonald Trump shakes hands with Benjamin Netanyahu at his Mar-a-Lago club on 29 December 2025 in Palm Beach, Florida.Photograph: Joe Raedle/Getty ImagesDonald TrumpTrump says Benjamin Netanyahu ‘will not be arrested’ if he visits the USIsraeli prime minister is subject of an arrest warrant issued by international criminal court over alleged war crimes in GazaMaya Yangin New YorkMon 20 Jul 2026 17.08 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trumphas saidBenjamin Netanyahu, the Israeli prime minister, “will not be arrested” if he visits the US.His comments came days after Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, said he wasexploringwhether his administration could arrest Netanyahu if he visits the city during the UN general assembly in September.In a Truth Socialposton Monday, Trump said Netanyahu, who has been the subject of an international criminal courtarrest warrantsince November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, “will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form while in the United States of America”.Trump added that Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and said “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”.Why does the US want to ‘dismantle’ the international criminal court? | Kenneth RothRead moreSpeaking tothe New York Times on Saturday, Mamdani had said: “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” where the international criminal court is based, adding: “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years.”Mamdani, whohasdescribedIsrael’s war on Gaza as a genocide, also said he is in “an active conversation” with New York City’s law department about his legal authority to direct the New York police to detain Netanyahu. “Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” he said, adding: “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.”Although the US does not recognize the ICC’s jurisdiction and is not a party to the court, Mamdanipledgedduring his mayoral campaign last September that he would arrest Netanyahu if he traveled to New York. “It is my desire to ensure that this be a city that stands up for international law,” he said.Other Israeli leaders, including Yoav Gallant, the country’s former defense minister, as well as the Hamas military leaderMohammed Deif, have also been the subject of ICC arrest warrants. The courtaccusedthe Israeli leaders of war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”.Explore more on these topicsDonald TrumpBenjamin NetanyahuZohran MamdaniInternational criminal courtNew YorkGazaPalestinenewsShareReuse this content

title: Ryanair says its planes are safe despite passenger being nearly sucked out of window | Ryanair | The Guardian
description: Airline says crew did a “great job” after incident and that it has been in touch with family of man saved from death
Author: Mark Sweney
Headline: Airline says crew did a “great job” after incident and that it has been in touch with family of man saved from death
Content: The broken window of the Ryanair aircraft in a picture obtained from social media. An engine failure sent parts loose, which smashed the window.Photograph: Despoina Papapavlou/ReutersView image in fullscreenThe broken window of the Ryanair aircraft in a picture obtained from social media. An engine failure sent parts loose, which smashed the window.Photograph: Despoina Papapavlou/ReutersRyanairRyanair says its planes are safe despite passenger being nearly sucked out of windowAirline says crew did a “great job” after incident and that it has been in touch with family of man saved from deathBusiness live – latest updatesMark SweneyMon 20 Jul 2026 09.42 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 05.52 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRyanair has moved to reassure travellers that its planes are safe after a passenger was saved from beingsucked out of a window mid-flightand said it had been in touch with his family.This month, Ljubisa Karović was sucked out headfirst after an engine failure resulted in parts smashing an acrylic window during a flight from Thessaloniki in Greece to Memmingen near Munich in Germany.His wife, Svetlana Grković, saved the 61-year old from falling out of the aircraftby holding on to his legsand managed to pull him back in with the help of two other passengers.Woman speaks out on husband being pulled from Ryanair plane – video“Our customer care team have actively been in touch with the family since the incident,” said Neil Sorahan, Ryanair’s group chief financial officer. “Our crew did a phenomenal job and got the aircraft back to Thessaloniki. Everyone bar none walked off the aircraft. It was a great job done by the cabin crew and the pilots.”Sorahan said it was “way too early” to discuss whether Ryanair would have to compensate the couple while the airline awaited the outcome of an investigation into the incident.Why did Ryanair-Air Malta plane window blow out mid-air and could it happen again?Read moreLast week, the US National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) said it would lead the investigation into the incident, which occurred on a Boeing 737 operated by the Ryanair subsidiary Malta Air.“We welcome the appointment of the NTSB,” Sorahan said. “They are going to do a full, independent investigation. We are participating fully. We have people on the ground actively involved in assisting.”His comments are the first by Ryanair on the incident, bar a short statement released in the immediate aftermath.Sorahan said bookings remained “very strong” heading into the peak summer season, despite a re-escalation of the conflict in the Middle East. He added that travellers did not need to worry about the safety of Ryanair’s fleet.He said neither the Federal Aviation Administration nor the European Union Aviation Safety Agency, which regulate civil aviation in the US and Europe respectively, have required Ryanair to make any operational changes.“We are very happy with safety across all of our five airlines, they operate at the highest standards of European aviation regulations,” Sorahan said. “We have a relatively young fleet and are very pleased with safety and maintenance within Ryanair group.“There [have been] literally hundreds of millions of flights on these [Boeing 737] aircraft globally since they first came into being. They are probably the safest aircraft ever built, they are highly maintained and we have very well-trained crew.”Sorahan also criticised the EU’s new digital entry-exit system (EES), which requires non-EU citizens to register fingerprints and a photo when they arrive. EES hasnearly tripled the timeit takes travellers to get through passport control at some of Europe’s most popular airports.“People are starting to travel in greater numbers [heading into summer] and they will need to get to airports early and expect delays,” Sorahan said. “[EES] has been badly implemented and is another challenge British families have to face this summer.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLast week, Ryanair said UK passengers faced hours of extra queueing time and called for airports to be allowed the flexibility to not use the system at peak times during the holiday period.The airlinepublished a list of “hotspots”where passengers were “experiencing significant delays due to slow processing times and excessive passport control queues on both arrivals and departures”.The list includes Lisbon, Tenerife South, Madrid, Lanzarote, Alicante, Málaga, Milan Bergamo, Milan Malpensa, Verona, Paris Beauvais, Berlin, Cologne, Frankfurt Hahn, Kraków and Budapest.Ryanair reported on Monday that profits after tax slumped by more than a third (34%) to €538m (£457m) in the three months to the end of June. It said this was mainly due to the price of jet fuel doubling amid the Iran war.The increase affected the cost of the 20% of fuel the airline needed for its fleet that was not hedged against price fluctuations.Ryanair’s passenger numbers rose 6% year on year, from 57.9 million to 61.3 million, although the increase came at the same time as a 6% drop in the price of an average fare.“Fares required stimulation as the Middle East conflict led to consumer hesitancy, concerns about EU jet fuel shortages, economic uncertainty and later bookings,” the airline said.Ryanair’s operating costs rose 11% year on year in the quarter to €3.81bn, as total revenues increased 1% to €4.38bn.The possible sale of Ryanair’s rival easyJet, which is thesubject of a bidding war, could also lead to a reduction in capacity and could trigger a “domino effect” of consolidation in Europe, Sorahan said.Explore more on these topicsRyanairAirline industrynewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Israeli settlers are in partnership with the army,’ say Palestinians after two shot dead in West Bank | West Bank | The Guardian
description: Residents of Deir Jarir say raids late on Sunday, also involving security forces, are part of pattern of violence
Author: Andrew Roth
Headline: Residents of Deir Jarir say raids late on Sunday, also involving security forces, are part of pattern of violence
Content: The bodies of Awda Abulrahim Farahneh and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu, killed in Israeli raids at the village of Deir Jarir, are taken to a burial ground on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyView image in fullscreenThe bodies of Awda Abulrahim Farahneh and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu, killed in Israeli raids at the village of Deir Jarir, are taken to a burial ground on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyWest Bank‘Israeli settlers are in partnership with the army,’ say Palestinians after two shot dead in West BankResidents of Deir Jarir say raids late on Sunday, also involving security forces, are part of pattern of violenceAndrew RothandSufian Tahain Deir Jarir, West BankMon 20 Jul 2026 13.53 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEven by the brutal standards of militant settler violence in theWest Bank, the raid on Deir Jarir late on Sunday night that killed two Palestinians and left another in hospital with a gunshot wound was extreme.Palestinians in the town said settlers from a nearby outpost arrived after 9pm on Sunday claiming that they were searching for stolen livestock. Israeli soldiers and police soon joined them, protecting them as they sought to take several villagers’ sheep by force.But when villagers raised the alarm and a crowd gathered, soldiers shot two men, beginning a night of three successive raids by settlers from nearby outposts and Israeli soldiers that lasted until after 2am, according to Palestinian residents of Deir Jarir.Saber Ajaj, a relative of one of the victims, 53-year-old Odeh Farajna, said he found him moments after he had been shot through the chest. Farajna, a member of the village council, owned a flower shop and helped with the stonework at the local graveyard.“The moment I put him in the car I knew that he was dead,” Ajaj said in an interview during a reception held after the funeral. “This shooting was meant to kill.”In interviews as they buried the two victims on Monday, family and friends of the dead Palestinian men described several attacks each month in which they said settlers, backed by security forces, arrived in houses on the outskirts of the village and then stole livestock.“It’s a partnership between the army and the settlers,” said Adel Rashid, the father of the younger victim, Abdul Adel Rashid Abu Mukh, 26.Rashid said local settlers on Sunday had stolen 21 of his sheep, while 50 more had been taken from another member of the village. Video of Sunday’s incident showed a settler protected by four IDF soldiers in green camouflage leading a sheep out of a local house.The raids are part of a picture of worsening settler violence in the region as Benjamin Netanyahu’s far-right allies have accelerated a land grab in the occupied West Bank.View image in fullscreenThe funeral of Awda Abulrahim Farahneh and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyArbitrary checkpoints and other restrictions to cut off towns like Deir Jarir after the7 October 2023 attacksby Hamas have been bolstered by plans for the government to endorsenew settlementsin the West Bank before nationalelectionsthat could challenge the Netanyahu government’s mandate.On the hill above the village is a settlement built last year that appears to share water infrastructure with an Israeli military base, according to Dror Etkes, an Israeli who founded Kerem Navot, an organisation that monitors Israeli land policy in occupiedPalestine. Residents said the settlers and military appeared to have coordinated the raid.Seven people have been killed in the village since 7 October 2023. Their portraits have been spray-painted on the walls of the town, along with the date they died.“The killing in Deir Jarir is one example of how Israel promotes ethnic cleansing in the West Bank: settlers invade and steal, while the military and police accompany them and shoot Palestinians who try to protect their homes and property,” said Yuli Novak, the executive director of B’Tselem, a human rights organisation based in Israel that catalogues violations by the security forces.A Guardian analysis has shown that Israel has not prosecuted its citizens for killing Palestinian civilians in the occupied West Bank since the start of this decade, creating impunity for a campaign of violence.The West Bank attacks are intensifying. Fadi Hamdallah al-Naasan, a 17-year-old from the town of al-Mughayyir, north-east of Ramallah, died on Saturday after succumbing to a gunshot wound to his leg during a raid on his house by the Israeli military and settlers on 11 July.Nearly a thousand mourners, mostly men, gathered in Deir Jarir on Monday shortly after noon for the burial. They marched from the mosque in the centre of the town, carrying the two bodies down to the old graveyard.On the road entering the area stands a new set of gates at a checkpoint established after 7 October that security forces are said to open or close arbitrarily, effectively cutting off the villages from the outside world.“Every time they come to attack us they are trying to make life here unlivable,” said Iyad Odah, 56, a member of the village council who knew Farajna. Since 7 October, he said, the town’s farmland, which once occupied thousands of hectares, was inaccessible as local settlers beat back farmers, attempting to access their orchards and grazing land.The Israeli military on Monday saidthey had gone to Deir Jarir to escort Israeli settlers, who claimed their livestock had been stolen and wanted to take it back. The military said they opened fire after Palestinians threw rocks and concrete blocks at the security forces. They did not respond to questions clarifying why they did not use non-lethal crowd control instead of live ammunition.Responding to the IDF’s claim that they had been attacked by “terrorists” in the village, Odah, of the town’s village council, said: “They are the real terrorists.”Explore more on these topicsWest BankPalestineMiddle East and north AfricaIsraelnewsShareReuse this content

title: Antibiotic-resistant bacteria increasingly leaving children vulnerable to common infections, global study finds | Health | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Research spanning 82 countries finds antibiotic resistance increased in every region between 2004 and 2022
Author: Melissa Davey
Headline: Exclusive: Research spanning 82 countries finds antibiotic resistance increased in every region between 2004 and 2022
