Content: Frequent antibiotic use is often a medically necessary, life-saving treatment for patients such as Harry Booth but using the same antibiotic too much can also make bacteria resistant to it.Photograph: suppliedView image in fullscreenFrequent antibiotic use is often a medically necessary, life-saving treatment for patients such as Harry Booth but using the same antibiotic too much can also make bacteria resistant to it.Photograph: suppliedHealthAntibiotic-resistant bacteria increasingly leaving children vulnerable to common infections, global study findsExclusive:Research spanning 82 countries finds antibiotic resistance increased in every region between 2004 and 2022Melissa DaveyMedical editorMon 20 Jul 2026 12.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 19.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHarry Booth has had a rough run through Australia’s coldest months. The 13-year-old has been in and out of hospital with a range of common chest and urinary tract infections that his doctors found challenging to treat.Harry was born with kidney failure and began dialysis at six months old, before receiving a kidney transplant from his father at age four. The surgery saved his life but comes with a trade-off: he now needs to take medications that suppress his immune system to prevent his body from rejecting the kidney.It makes him morevulnerable to infections – and the bacteria which cause these are resistant to several commonly used antibiotics.Instead, doctors reach for different, less commonly used drugs and administer them intravenously for several days, before sometimes giving him oral antibiotics. Once, he required a procedure to clean the bacteria from his bladder.The treatments are often demanding and take him away from home and school.Frequent antibiotic use is often a medically necessary, life-saving treatment for patients like Harrybut using the same antibiotic too much can also make bacteria resistantto it.‘Alarming’ rates of babies with antibiotic-resistant bugs in Asia-Pacific, Australian study findsRead moreHis mother, Eileen, says while back-up treatments are working, she worries about what will happen if her son’s infections become resistant to those drugs, too. As it stands, they are carefully treated only if they meet certain symptom and bacteria thresholds.Harry is not an outlier.Life-saving drugs used to treat common childhood infections are increasingly failing and the resistance of bacteria to these medicines will only worsen over the next decade,a global analysis has found.Led by Murdoch Children’s Research Institute (MCRI) in collaboration with the University of Sydney in Australia, researchers analysed 106,581 infection samples from children aged up to 18 years old across 82 countries.They found antibiotic resistance – when bacteria are no longer killed by antimicrobial medicines – increased in every region between 2004 and 2022.Babies and children in intensive care, and countries with fewer healthcare resources, are most affected, according to the findings published in theJournal of the American Medical Associationon Tuesday.Paper co-author and MCRI associate professor Penelope Bryant, who is also one of Harry’s doctors, said severe infections such as sepsis and pneumonia are driving the increase.These are often caused byAcinetobacter baumannii, a bacteria with a tough double-membrane and the highest level of drug resistance, according to the findings.The study foundanother bacteria,Klebsiella, a common cause of urinary tract infections and liver abscesses, recorded the fastest increase in resistance, particularly in south-east Asia, eastern Europe and the western Pacific.The researchers also used statistical modelling and found resistance to last-line antibiotics is projected to rise substantially by 2035, reaching 82% and 35% forAcinetobacter baumanniiandKlebsiellarespectively.“We are finding antibiotic resistance not just in severe diseases but in more simple, uncomplicated infections such as urinary tract infections in children,” Bryant said.Despite antimicrobial resistance being one of the biggest threats to children’s health globally, Bryant said children had been largely overlooked in global antibiotic resistance surveillance and research, resulting in a lack of child-friendly antibiotic formulations or clear child-dosing guidelines.This is because there is sometimes a reluctance to run trials on children and developing treatments for them can be less lucrative for pharmaceutical companies, she said.Children are also especially vulnerable as they experience high rates of bacterial infections and have fewer antibiotic options than adults.To better understand antibiotic resistance by country, bacteria and type of drug, Bryant and her colleagues launched a website calledAMR in Kids, providing region and pathogen-specific forecasts to 2035. They hope it will help identify emerging threats, inform treatment decisions and help guide planning and research.A paediatrician and incoming president of the Australasian Society for Infectious Diseases, Prof Chris Blyth, described the study and website as “really important work”.Australia’s overuse of antibiotics found to be driving rate of drug-resistant infectionsRead more“It highlights the size of the problem and that the problem is not equally spread globally,” he said.In low-income countries, antibiotic resistance is being driven by unregulated antibiotic use and poor sanitation, making better access to diagnostic techniques and first-line antibiotics a priority. In high-income countries, antibiotic overuse across farming, veterinarian care and healthcare is fuelling drug resistance.Treatments that do not work for children with resistant infections are rare in high-income countries but in low-income countries drug-resistant infections are one of the highest causes of death in children under five.The WorldHealthOrganization classifies antibiotics into three groups to help prevent antimicrobial resistance: “access” antibiotics are more targeted, first-line treatments; “watch” drugs are used more broadly but carry higher resistance risks; and “reserve” are last-resort drugs for treating the most resistant infections.The study found while first-line access resistance is declining or stabilising in high-income regions, lower-resource settings are seeing a sharp rise in resistance to critical watch and reserve drugs.“It highlights the real need to have global surveillance systems in place, both in Australia and internationally, to track and identify resistance,” Blyth said.“I’m frequently seeing children with difficult-to-treat infections that are resistant to first and second-line agents [drugs], where I’m having to reach for agents that are more difficult to get, more difficult to give, more expensive, and potentially more toxic.”The Booth family hopes that resources such as the AMR in Kids website will mean children like Harry are better protected.“People are already starting to understand the long-term effects of antibiotics,” Eileen Booth said, “but there is a long way to go with education around resistance.”Explore more on these topicsHealthAntibioticsDrugsMedical researchnewsShareReuse this content

title: Slowdown in Arctic ice melt ends after largest winter decline on record | Environment | The Guardian
description: Scientists recorded slower reduction over past 20 years, but study finds this was ‘temporary rather than sustained’
Author: Pippa Neill
Headline: Scientists recorded slower reduction over past 20 years, but study finds this was ‘temporary rather than sustained’
Content: Researchers found between 2024 and 2025 Arctic sea ice declined by 5.8%, the largest year-to-year wintertime drop ever recorded.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenResearchers found between 2024 and 2025 Arctic sea ice declined by 5.8%, the largest year-to-year wintertime drop ever recorded.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesEnvironmentSlowdown in Arctic ice melt ends after largest winter decline on recordScientists recorded slower reduction over past 20 years, but study finds this was ‘temporary rather than sustained’Pippa NeillEnvironment reporterMon 20 Jul 2026 15.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA previously recorded slowdown inArcticsea ice decline has ended, according to a new study, with the largest year-to-year wintertime reduction ever recorded.In astudypublished last summer, researchers found that melting sea ice in the Arctic had slowed down over the past 20 years, with no statistically significant decline in its extent recorded between 2005 and 2024.Given the increase in global temperatures during this period, the finding was surprising.However, the researchers said natural variations in ocean currents that limit ice melting probably balanced the rise in global temperatures. They said this was only a temporary reprieve and melting was highly likely to start again at about double the long-term rate at some point in the next five to 10 years.Just two years later, thenew studyhas found the slowdown in sea ice decline is now over.Using the most recent satellite observations of sea ice coverage, the researchers found that the 2005 to 2024 slowdown was “temporary rather than sustained”.Dr Duo Chan, lead author of the paper from the University of Southampton’s School of Ocean and Earth Science, said that what “looked like a pause in the early 2020s now appears to have just been a temporary slowdown within a longer-term decline”.Sea ice area in September, when it reaches its annual minimum, has halved since 1979, when satellite measurements began.The researchers found that between 2024 and 2025 Arctic sea ice declined by 5.8%. This is the largest year-to-year wintertime drop ever recorded.Dr Alessandro Silvano, co-author of the paper at the University of Southampton, said this sharp drop in 2025 was unusual, but physically plausible under current Arctic warming.Because the winter sea ice acts as an insulator between the relatively warm Arctic ocean and the much colder atmosphere, a reduction in ice will mean more ocean heat and moisture will enter the atmosphere.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toDown to EarthThe planet's most important stories. Get all the week's environment news - the good, the bad and the essentialPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe authors say this can affect pressure patterns, storm development and large-scale atmospheric circulation, affecting the weather farther south.Chan said the study acted as a warning of the ongoing climate crisis.“Short-term recoveries can temporarily obscure the longer-term direction of Arctic sea ice change. The low winter sea ice extents in 2025 and 2026 clearly show that Arctic sea ice loss has not stopped – it is continuing in a warming climate,” he said.Explore more on these topicsEnvironmentArcticClimate crisisnewsShareReuse this content

title: Canada issues air quality warnings over US wildfire smoke after Trump tariff threat | Wildfires | The Guardian
description: Officials say smoke from US fires has crossed border days after US president accused Canada of improper fire management
Author: Olivia Bowden
Headline: Officials say smoke from US fires has crossed border days after US president accused Canada of improper fire management
Content: Wildfires have already had an impact on air quality in Toronto.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenWildfires have already had an impact on air quality in Toronto.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesWildfiresCanada issues air quality warnings over US wildfire smoke after Trump tariff threatOfficials say smoke from US fires has crossed border days after US president accused Canada of improper fire managementOlivia Bowdenin TorontoMon 20 Jul 2026 11.52 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfter days of acrid smoke from northern wildfires choking major US cities and inspiring Donald Trump to threatenCanadawith tariffs, officials now say the reverse is also happening as parts of Canada issue air quality warnings caused by American fires to the south.Officials in the Canadian province of British Columbia warned in an alert on Saturday evening that smoke from US fires, spurred on by thousands of lightning strikes in the north-east, was creating smoky conditions as polluted air drifted north.“Much of this smoke is originating from fires south of the Canada-US border in Washington and Oregon,” the provincial wildfire service said.Twenty-two large fires are burning across Washington and Oregon, according to the Northwest Interagency Coordination Center, the fire coordination agency for both states. British Columbia has air quality warnings in place for two regions, Kootenay Lake and Cranbrook, located along the south-east border with the US.Despite the US facing its own wildfire issues, Donald Trump and his fellow Republicans have chosen to accuse Canada of improper wildfire management and have threatened to hit Canadian officials with sanctions.Republicans threaten Canada with sanctions over drifting wildfire smokeRead moreIn a social media post on Friday, Trump wrote the US had been “invaded” by dirty air. “[The] ‌cost of this pollution must of necessity be added ⁠to the TARIFFS Canada is currently paying,” he said. TheTrump administrationhas taken a sledgehammer to several climate protections.Wildfires continue to blaze across the US and Canada. According to the Canadian government, as of Monday there were 843 active wildfires burning across Canada. Of those 181 are considered “out of control”, meaning the fires are spreading and not responding to suppressive actions. Another 532 of those fires are also considered “out of control” but suppressive actions have not yet been taken.The Canadian prime minister, Mark Carney, who sat alongside Trump at the World Cup final on Sunday, where the US president again suggested slapping Canada with tariffs, has sidestepped the criticisms. “Fighting climate change is the responsibility of all countries, including the United States,” he told reporters last week.But Doug Ford, the premier of Ontario, where many fires are burning, said on Friday: “If there’s politicians out there chirping away, well maybe what you should do rather than complain is send support, send help, because we have done the exact same thing for our American friends.”Eleanor Olszewski, Canada’s minister of emergency preparedness, said in a statement that Canada had invested C$12bn (£6.36bn) on fire prevention and forest sustainability over the past five years. “This is a challenge that knows no borders,” she said.Explore more on these topicsWildfiresCanadaTrump administrationAmericasnewsShareReuse this content

title: AI-altered images on birdwatching forums putting research at risk | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Experts warn increase in enhanced photos on birding platforms creating fake sightings, threatening credibility of tool used by scientists
Author: Patrick Greenfield
Headline: Experts warn increase in enhanced photos on birding platforms creating fake sightings, threatening credibility of tool used by scientists
Content: ‘Nobody is falling for a toucan sighting in Siberia’ … This image is AI-generated, using ChatGPT.Illustration: ChatGPTView image in fullscreen‘Nobody is falling for a toucan sighting in Siberia’ … This image is AI-generated, using ChatGPT.Illustration: ChatGPTThe age of extinctionAI (artificial intelligence)AI-altered images on birdwatching forums putting research at riskExperts warn increase in enhanced photos on birding platforms creating fake sightings, threatening credibility of tool used by scientistsThe age of extinction is supported byAbout this contentPatrick GreenfieldMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFor many birdwatchers, recording a species outside its normal range is the holy grail. In the UK, the discoveries often make national headlines. The western reef heron, for example, usually found in Africa and southern Europe,was spotted in a seaside town in north Walesin June, and widely celebrated on birding forums.But a new scourge is threatening to disrupt the fun: AI slop.Scientists are appealing to birders to limit their use of AI when editing images over fears that it could undermine the credibility of popular citizen science platforms such as iNaturalist and Macaulay Library, which are routinely used in scientific research to monitor species’ habitat range.The rise of generative AI platforms such asChatGPTand Google Gemini has led to a sharp increase in fake and enhanced images of rare and celebrated birds across wildlife photography forums. Users can create high-quality fake images in seconds – or enhance a photograph by asking the AI to remove a branch or leaf that might be obscuring part of the subject, which can inadvertently introduce significant changes.In arecent commentaryin the science journal Nature, researchers warned that hundreds of fake images have already been discovered on popular databases for recording species. They said that the true scale of the issue is unknown – as many could go undetected – and could contaminate records collected by the public.“My experience of looking at Facebook these days is that a huge volume of wildlife photos now are simply AI-generated imagery,” said Dr Alexander Lees, an ecologist at Manchester Metropolitan University who authored the journal article. “The idea that we could maybe use those photos to help us understand where species are in space and time is very difficult.”Outright hoaxes remain rare and are often easy to spot, said Lees – nobody is falling for a toucan sighting in Siberia – but he says birders often use AI to edit and improve an image, and the algorithm can then introduce parts from different bird species to create the new image.Lees points to the example of a false sighting of a red-winged blackbird in central Brazil, which is usually found in North America and had never been seen in this area of Brazil until the reported sighting. The bird was, in fact, an epaulet oriole – a commonnew world bird species– but the photographer had asked an AI platform to make the picture “look better”, which is when parts of the red-winged blackbird were added, resulting in the false sighting.Bird migration is changing. What does this reveal about our planet? – visualisedRead more“Wildlife photographers can be quite obsessed with getting a beautiful photo, but there’s a risk that the image might actually cause problems down the line when AI has been used to edit it,” said Lees.Citizen science organisations are still working to understand the scale of the issue. On iNaturalist, a social network where nature enthusiasts can record sightings of plants and animals, just 1,400 of the more than 610m images on the platform have been flagged for AI use. From monitoring how plants and animals move in response to climate breakdown to recording new behaviour in species,dozens of discoverieshave been made usingcitizen science.Tony Iwane, iNaturalist’s director of community support, who was a co-author on the Nature paper, said most of these cases were unlikely to be malicious, but appealed to users to be vigilant.“On platforms like ours, regular people are posting information that a scientist could probably never get at scale. It is also almost like a sensor of what is happening on Earth in real time: are plants flowering early? Are species moving north as the climate warms? The more we know about where species are, the better informed we can be as conservationists. But the information needs to be accurate,” he said.Find moreage of extinction coverage here, and follow the biodiversity reportersRebecca Ratcliffe,Phoebe WestonandPatrick Greenfieldin the Guardian app for more nature coverage.Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)The age of extinctionBirdsAnimalsWildlifeClimate crisisComputingChatGPTnewsShareReuse this content

title: Investigation of Nigel Farage over undeclared gifts is ‘kangaroo court’, says Reform MP | Nigel Farage | The Guardian
description: Runcorn MP Sarah Pochin says parliament mounting ‘vicious and desperate campaign to stop Reform UK’
Author: Rowena Mason
Headline: Runcorn MP Sarah Pochin says parliament mounting ‘vicious and desperate campaign to stop Reform UK’
Content: Nigel Farage and Sarah Pochin in parliament.Photograph: House of Commons/UK Parliament/PAView image in fullscreenNigel Farage and Sarah Pochin in parliament.Photograph: House of Commons/UK Parliament/PANigel FarageInvestigation of Nigel Farage over undeclared gifts is ‘kangaroo court’, says Reform MPRuncorn MP Sarah Pochin says parliament mounting ‘vicious and desperate campaign to stop Reform UK’Rowena MasonWhitehall editorMon 20 Jul 2026 07.37 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA Reform UK MP has complained that the standards system investigatingNigel Farageover undeclared gifts is a “kangaroo court”.Sarah Pochin, the Runcorn MP, said Farage had called abyelection in his Clacton seatso voters could decide whether they backed him or not, and argued that “the media, the British state and the old parties have mounted a vicious and desperate campaign to stop Reform UK”.She posted on X: “We can’t call a general election, so rather than submit to a kangaroo court in parliament, Nigel Farage has handed the power back to the people of Clacton. P.S. Clacton was once one of the safest Tory seats … now they won’t even field a candidate against him. Wonder why?”Farage had previously said the investigation into him by the parliamentary standards watchdog was “being used as a political tool” and Reform sources had suggested the commissioner and committee were illegitimate.However, Pochin’s remarks go further in suggesting that the process is unfair, with a predetermined verdict, and mark an escalation of Farage’s supporters attempting to undermine the standards system.Her remarks echo those of Boris Johnson, who claimed that the privileges committee was a “kangaroo court” when it found he deliberately misled parliament over Covid rule-breaking in No 10.The standards commissioner is currently looking into whether Farage should have declared a £5m giftfrom the Thailand-based businessman Christopher Harbornegiven to him before the last election. Farage has always said he was not in politics at the time the gift was made.The inquiry was later widened to look at whether he should have declared supportfrom his friend and fraudster George Cottrell, who provided accommodation at one of the London homes he rented to Farage, and also paid for some social media filming in 2024.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionNigel Farage says questions over his finances are part of ‘coordinated pile-on’Read moreFarage told the Triggernometry podcast over the weekend that it was “totally undeclarable in every single way”.He acknowledged that the parliamentary standards system could find against him but claimed he had sought advice from a “top international lawyer” who told him the £5m did not need to be declared because it was unconditional and had a document drawn up saying it was given freely.The rules state that gifts and benefits in kind have to be declared for the 12 months prior to an MP entering parliament, but only if they are political.Farage is fighting a byelection against more than 30 candidates in Clacton but none of them are from the main parties, as they decided to boycott the contest.He could potentially face a second byelection if the commissioner finds against him with a substantial sanction recommending suspension from the Commons, triggering a possible recall petition. In that possible second contest, the major parties have vowed to stand against him.Explore more on these topicsNigel FarageReform UKParty fundingHouse of CommonsnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘My shirt was wringing’: National Express bus drivers speak out on 40C conditions  | Extreme heat | The Guardian
description: Drivers in West Midlands say buses ‘unbearable’ during heatwaves, raising employee and passenger safety fears
Author: Neha Gohil
Headline: Drivers in West Midlands say buses ‘unbearable’ during heatwaves, raising employee and passenger safety fears
Content: National Express buses at Birmingham Central bus garage in Digbeth.Photograph: Anthony Devlin/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenNational Express buses at Birmingham Central bus garage in Digbeth.Photograph: Anthony Devlin/AFP/Getty ImagesExtreme heat‘My shirt was wringing’: National Express bus drivers speak out on 40C conditionsDrivers in West Midlands say buses ‘unbearable’ during heatwaves, raising employee and passenger safety fearsNeha GohilMidlands correspondentMon 20 Jul 2026 09.51 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBus drivers in theWest Midlandshave spoken out about the “horrendous” working conditions they have experienced during heatwaves, which led to 40C temperatures in their cabs.The UK was hit by high temperatures and dry weather in three successive heatwaves, with temperatures topping 37C last month when the country recorded itshottest ever June day. Last week, the Met Office warned climate extremes were becoming the “new ‘normal’ for the UK”.Three bus drivers working in the West Midlands said the conditions in their vehicles had been “unbearable” and criticised the absence of adequate air conditioning, which threatened the safety of employees and passengers. The drivers, who did not want to be named, have each worked forNational Expressfor more than 20 years.“You’re sweating down your back, your face … it’s just been terrible, it’s just been too hot,” one driver said. “Some drivers will carry on and you can see that they’re exhausted. They’re not sleeping at night because of the heat and they’re coming to work the next day, expected to maintain massive concentration driving a 12-tonne vehicle.”Another driver said of the conditions: “It’s like you’ve wet yourself. Your shirt is wringing wet. We’ve even had passengers pass out on the bus.”Persistent high temperatures have led to a renewed focus on working conditions fortransport workers in the UK and across Europe. Last week, the Green MP Hannah Spencer said she would introduce a bill in parliament to pave the way fora maximum workplace temperature. In June, the London mayor, Sadiq Khan, pledged further support for bus drivers during the heatwaves.Transformingthe UK’s public transport infrastructureis likely to be a key focus for the new prime minister, Andy Burnham, who brought buses in Manchester under local public control as franchises after nearly four decades of a deregulation.Drivers in the West Midlands said they had been granted additional 30-minute breaks to cope with the heat, but many employees were not aware of this and feared the impact on their pay and delays to the service for passengers.One driver criticised National Express, which they believed was not taking the concerns of drivers seriously. “I think they’ll only take it seriously if someone passed out at the wheel,” he said.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionMost UK media reports on June heatwave failed to mention climate crisisRead moreUnite, the union representing the drivers working for National Express, said the high temperatures in the driver cabs were compounded by glass windows that trapped heat and created a “greenhouse effect”.The general secretary of the union, Sharon Graham, said bus drivers had been left “sweltering during heatwaves, risking their own health and that of their passengers”.“We know that heatwaves are becoming more frequent, but this fact seems to take the bus operators by complete surprise every year. Bus firms must stop putting profits before people and act now. This is a problem that is worsening every year and needs urgent attention to keep people safe,” Graham added.Unite provided the bus drivers it supports with a letter to show their employers when conditions become too hot, highlighting their legal right to stop work for safety reasons.The letter states: “To confirm, the reason for my decision is the condition/lack of an adequate, working cooling system on the bus I am being expected to drive in the current extreme heat. I am not refusing to work today.”A spokesperson for National Express bus service said the recent weather conditions were “unprecedented” and that they would like to “thank all our drivers for keeping the region moving”.“We recognise that bus driving in the kind of extreme heat experienced recently can be very unpleasant,” the spokesperson said, adding that all of its new electric vehicles have air conditioning.“Because historically the UK had a mild climate, retrofitting air conditioning in older buses was not cost-effective for the limited number of days it would have been useful. However, if recent weather conditions become the norm, this may need to be reviewed.”They added: “In the meantime, in addition to providing extra breaks and free water, we are very clear that our drivers must take any precautions necessary to stay hydrated and safe. They are doing a fantastic job in difficult conditions.”Explore more on these topicsExtreme heatNational ExpressWest MidlandsPublic transportClimate crisisUniteWorkers' rightsnewsShareReuse this content

title: GB News will not be investigated over ‘no riot in Belfast’ claim | GB News | The Guardian
description: Media regulator will not take action despite receiving 446 complaints about comments by Bev Turner
Author: Michael Savage
Headline: Media regulator will not take action despite receiving 446 complaints about comments by Bev Turner
Content: 1:08Belfast residents run from burning homes as violence erupts after protests – videoGB NewsGB News will not be investigated over ‘no riot in Belfast’ claimMedia regulator will not take action despite receiving 446 complaints about comments by Bev TurnerMichael SavageMedia editorMon 20 Jul 2026 11.45 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.05 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGB News will not be investigated by the media regulator after a presenter insisted there was noriot in Belfastin June, despite cars and houses being set alight, attacks on police and families fleeing their homes.Ofcom received more than 400 complaints after the comments by Bev Turner, presenter of the broadcaster’s Late Show Live, who said there “weren’t riots” and that no houses had been burned in the violent disorder.“There was no riot,” she said in a show broadcast hours after the unrest. “There is not a house that has been burnt down. There are no riots.”When a guest on her show, political commentator Matthew Stadlen, pointed to footage of burning cars and houses, Turner replied she had seen it and it was “not that dramatic”.View image in fullscreenBurned cars and blackened walls of houses on 12 June after rioting in Belfast, Northern Ireland.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesHowever, the regulator, which has come underheavy criticism over its approach to maintaining broadcasting accuracy and impartiality, said it would not investigate the comments because Turner was challenged and had been expressing her own view.The violence in Belfast broke out after a Sudanese man was charged with attempted murder in relation to a knife attack. A video of the attack was posted by Tommy Robinson and other far-right figures,prompting demands for protests in response.Turner later said on social media that she had been speaking in the early hours of the morning, when she had been “relying on sparse footage and interpreting media descriptions”.She said she feared the disorder would be used “by the government to introduce more powers of control” and that she believed Stadlen was “priming viewers at home to accept incoming restrictions”.Critics of Ofcom’s decision not to investigate, despite a total of 446 complaints, say it demonstrates the low bar the regulator has effectively set to comply with broadcasting rules.Richard Wilson, the director of campaign group Reliable Media, was among those who made a complaint. He said: “Whether or not a house burned down is not a matter of opinion … It now looks like Ofcom has toldGB Newsthat as long as someone in the studio disagrees with you, you can say what you like about settled facts. That’s not regulation. It’s a green light.”Chris Banatvala, an independent media consultant who drafted Ofcom’s code and investigation procedures when he was its founding director of standards, said Turner’s comments were “clearly misleading” and risked “fuelling conspiracy theories”.“A broadcaster has an editorial voice which must respect the facts,” he said. “At a time when Ofcom is cracking down on misinformation and adopting emergency measures for social media during periods of civil unrest, it appears to be allowing GB News to go unchecked, despite the higher legal standards that apply to broadcasters.”View image in fullscreenA vehicle burns as riot squad officers battle protesters throwing projectiles on 10 June in Ballymena, Northern Ireland.Photograph: Charles McQuillan/Getty ImagesA spokesperson for Ofcom said it had “carefully considered complaints about comments made by the presenter Bev Turner regarding civil unrest in Belfast”.They added: “Taking into account that the comments reflected her personal view and received robust challenge by a guest – who referenced specific aspects of the disorder which countered the presenter’s own analysis – the programme did not raise potentially substantive issues warranting further investigation.”GB News was approached for comment.Explore more on these topicsGB NewsOfcomBelfastNorthern IrelandTelevision industrynewsShareReuse this content

title: Double murder accused strangled HMP Exeter cellmate while awaiting trial | UK news | The Guardian
description: Jury at Winchester crown court told James Desborough was convicted in March of murdering Steven Kempster
Author: Steven Morris
Headline: Jury at Winchester crown court told James Desborough was convicted in March of murdering Steven Kempster
Content: James Desborough, who is on trial in Winchester accused of murdering two men and hiding their remains in Cornish woodland.Photograph: FacebookView image in fullscreenJames Desborough, who is on trial in Winchester accused of murdering two men and hiding their remains in Cornish woodland.Photograph: FacebookUK newsDouble murder accused strangled HMP Exeter cellmate while awaiting trialJury at Winchester crown court told James Desborough was convicted in March of murdering Steven KempsterSteven MorrisMon 20 Jul 2026 10.19 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA fan of a TV show about a serial killer strangled a cellmate in prison while awaiting trial for allegedly murdering two men and hiding their remains in Cornish woodland, a jury has heard.James Desborough, 40, is accused of killing Claudio Aquilino and Daniel Coleman and concealing parts of their bodies in the dense undergrowth close to his cabin near St Austell.On Monday the jury at Winchester crown court was told that earlier this year Desborough was found guilty of murdering a man called Steven Kempster in a Devon prison.In agreed facts read to the jury, Ahmed Hossain KC, prosecuting, said: “On 27 March 2026 at Exeter crown court, James Desborough was convicted by a jury of the murder of Steven Kempster.“The facts of this conviction are that on 15 September 2025 James Desborough murdered Steven Kempster (with whom he shared a cell at HMP Exeter, whilst on remand for the current allegations) by compression of the neck by strangulation.”View image in fullscreenClaudio Aquilino. He was last seen alive in the company of Desborough, the court has heard.Photograph: Devon and Cornwall police/PAThe jury in the current case has heard that after killing Aquilino and Coleman, Desborough allegedly accessed their bank accounts and tried to cover his tracks by making it appear that one had gone to London and the other was walking a long-distance trail.Police spent five months searching the woods around Desborough’s cabin, discovering burnt bone fragments, believed to have belonged to Aquilino, in a stream, the court heard.View image in fullscreenDaniel Coleman.Photograph: Devon and Cornwall police/PAParts of Coleman’s body, including his torso, legs and feet, were found in a shallow grave nearby. An axe hung from a tree close to the grave.Hossain told the jury that Desborough was present at times when police searched the woods and that, while chatting to a forensic biologist involved in the operation, had said he “particularly enjoyed the dismemberment scenes” in the television programme Dexter.In the agreed facts, the jury was told that both men who died had suffered from mental health problems.Aquilino, 57, had been a target of intimidation and harassment that he believed to be racially motivated and he felt it was safer to be homeless and live in a tent, the jury heard. In 2022 he told police he was being bullied by “scouse” dealers in the Cornish town of Callington, who “make him do jobs”, had stolen his phone and entered his home when he did not want them to.Coleman, 43, had suffered mental health problems after being kidnapped at the age of 13 when he was stabbed, the jury heard.The two alleged victims and Desborough lived together for a while at accommodation in Newquay for homeless people and people with addiction problems, the court has heard.The prosecution alleged that Aquilino and Coleman were last seen alive in the company of Desborough and police began searching the woods in July last year.Jurors were told that the month before, Desborough had repeatedly searched for details about cosmetic surgery in Thailand and airports there. Following a review of handwritten notes they found in his cabin, police also investigated an email address associated with a private cosmetic surgery clinic.The jury was shown images of knives, saws and axes found in and around Desborough’s cabin, and one picture showing a ghillie suit – a specialised camouflage suit – that was found hanging outside his home.Desborough denies murdering Aquilino and Coleman between April and July last year. The jury heard he had admitted preventing their lawful burial.The trial continues.Explore more on these topicsUK newsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Afghanistan floods kill at least 20 people, with more than 100 missing | Afghanistan | The Guardian
description: National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in remote province of Nuristan
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in remote province of Nuristan
Content: Precarious mountainside homes in the Parun district of Afghanistan’s Nuristan province last month.Photograph: Wakil Kohsar/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenPrecarious mountainside homes in the Parun district of Afghanistan’s Nuristan province last month.Photograph: Wakil Kohsar/AFP/Getty ImagesAfghanistanAfghanistan floods kill at least 20 people, with more than 100 missingNational disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in remote province of NuristanAgence France-Press in KabulMon 20 Jul 2026 13.31 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.40 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAt least 20 people have been killed and more than 80 injured in flooding that hit Afghanistan’s Nuristan province, the country’s disaster management agency said on Monday.In Parun, the provincial capital, a further 105 people including the mayor were missing, said Feraidon Samim, a spokesperson for the Nuristan governor.Journalists familiar with the remote north-eastern area said a market, shops and other buildings were located near Parun’s riverside.Ashiqullah Safi, the head of an NGO based in the town, said the flood had “completely destroyed the municipality”, including hotels and dozens of shops.He added that flood waters had left big boulders in the town and residents were searching for the missing without equipment.Afghanistan’s national disaster management agency said floods had caused “severe financial and human losses” in Parun, where efforts were under way to rescue the injured and recover the bodies of those killed.Zabihullah Mujahid, the chief spokesperson for the Afghan government, posted on X to confirm reports of deaths and property damage in Nuristan.Earlier this month, the head of Afghanistan’s environmental protection agency, Matiul Haq Khalis, said flooding in the country had increased.He said environmental problems “have severely affected the Afghan people and have contributed to a humanitarian crisis in the country”.Extreme rainfall has become more common and more intense across most of the world because of the human-caused climate crisis. This is because warmer air can hold more water vapour, leading to more severe downpours.Explore more on these topicsAfghanistanClimate crisisSouth and central AsiaFloodingExtreme weathernewsShareReuse this content

title: Hungary’s chess queen declines Péter Magyar’s offer of role as president | Péter Magyar | The Guardian
description: Judit Polgár says she does not ‘feel enough strength within me’ to take on the responsibility of uniting a divided nation
Author: Ashifa Kassam
Headline: Judit Polgár says she does not ‘feel enough strength within me’ to take on the responsibility of uniting a divided nation
Content: Judit Polgár is interviewed in 2017. She became the youngest grandmaster in the world at the age of 15.Photograph: Peter Kohalmi/AFP/GettyView image in fullscreenJudit Polgár is interviewed in 2017. She became the youngest grandmaster in the world at the age of 15.Photograph: Peter Kohalmi/AFP/GettyPéter MagyarHungary’s chess queen declines Péter Magyar’s offer of role as presidentJudit Polgár says she does not ‘feel enough strength within me’ to take on the responsibility of uniting a divided nationAshifa Kassamand agencyMon 20 Jul 2026 14.44 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Hungarian chess ⁠legend Judit Polgár has said she will not accept the nomination for president, only a day after the ⁠prime minister,Péter Magyar, ⁠said he ​would ask her to take up the largely ceremonial role.Magyar had said he would meet Polgár on Monday to discuss the matter. “Our country needs unity, peace and a president that all Hungarians can be proud of,” he wrote on social media. “Judit Polgár’s name has been synonymous with talent and perseverance for decades.”Polgár, 49, said later in a Facebook post that she was grateful to ‌be asked but added: “I do not feel enough strength within me to take on the historic responsibility of uniting a divided nation, ​so I am unable to accept the request.”Polgár – the subject of the documentaryQueen of Chess, released in February – is the only female player to have been ranked in the world’s top 10 of the male-dominated sport.She is ⁠the only woman to have crossed the rating of 2700, the ​threshold that many ​consider the definition of a super grandmaster.View image in fullscreenJudit Polgár aged 12. She was the top-ranked female player for 25 years.Photograph: Yves Forestier/Sygma/GettyThe latest developments come after amonths-long standoffbetween the newly elected Magyar and Tamás Sulyok, the president appointed during the former leader Viktor Orbán’s time in power.Since hislandslide victoryin April, Magyar has repeatedly called on Sulyok to step down, accusing him of being a puppet who had failed to stand up for the country as Orbán’s rightwing, nationalist Fidesz party was criticised for weakening democratic institutions and undermining the rule of law.Hungary’s parliament – where Magyar’s centre-right Tisza party holds a two-thirds majority – voted last week to back a constitutional amendment that included animmediate end to Sulyok’s term. From there, Sulyok had five days to sign off on the amendment.The manoeuvre was criticised by several rights groups in Hungary, including Human Rights Watch,which saidthe rushed constitutional changes to oust the president were “reminiscent of [the] Fidesz era”.Magyar, however, has insisted that the changes are needed as part of a wider push to loosen Orbán’s lingering grip on the country.As news emerged that Sulyok had signed the legislation,bringing an endto his time in office, Magyar proposed nominating Polgár as his replacement. The idea was first floated in an open letter signed by 16 prominent Hungarians, including Ernő Rubik, theinventor of the Rubik’s Cube.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBorn in Budapest, Polgár, who will turn 50 in a few days, had been the top-ranked female player for 25 years when she retired in 2014. She became the youngest grandmaster in the world at the age of 15,breaking the recordset in 1958 by Bobby Fischer.She won victories against several male world champions including Garry Kasparov in 2002.Since being swept into power on the promise of “regime change”, Magyar and his party have moved swiftly to dismantle the bastions of Orbán’s 16 years in power, fromsuspending news broadcastsby public media outlets with ties to the former prime minister toabolishingthe sovereignty protection office, which had allowed intelligence services to access information on individuals and organisations without judicial oversight.Additional reporting from ReutersExplore more on these topicsPéter MagyarHungaryChessEuropeWomennewsShareReuse this content

title: Prickly pears, avocados and lemons: how rainwater harvesting is feeding Mexico City’s gardens and homes | Global development | The Guardian
description: Thousands of low-income households are benefiting from a simple system that captures and filters rainfall from the city’s roofs
Author: Suzanne Bearne
Headline: Thousands of low-income households are benefiting from a simple system that captures and filters rainfall from the city’s roofs
Content: Ana Aparicio Bahena using harvested rainwater to maintain her vegetable garden at her home in Cristo Rey, Xochimilco, Mexico City. As in many informal settlements in Mexico, Aparicio Bahena’s metal-roofed home is not connected to the water network.Photograph: Suzanne BearneView image in fullscreenAna Aparicio Bahena using harvested rainwater to maintain her vegetable garden at her home in Cristo Rey, Xochimilco, Mexico City. As in many informal settlements in Mexico, Aparicio Bahena’s metal-roofed home is not connected to the water network.Photograph: Suzanne BearneSouthern frontlines: Latin America and the CaribbeanGlobal developmentPrickly pears, avocados and lemons: how rainwater harvesting is feeding Mexico City’s gardens and homesThousands of low-income households are benefiting from a simple system that captures and filters rainfall from the city’s roofsSupported byAbout this contentSuzanne Bearnein Mexico CityMon 20 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWalking up a steep hill to a small informal settlement of about 45 houses in Cristo Rey, above San Gregorio Atlapulco inMexicoCity, Ana Aparicio Bahena arrives at her simple home. As she walks through her lush, verdant garden, she points out plants such as the prickly pear, cactus leaves with tiny, hair-like spines, and picks blackberries hanging from a bush, handing them to her two daughters.“I can grow vegetables,” says Aparicio Bahena proudly, holding up a small onion. “I have avocados, lemons, potatoes … I never used to be able to grow my own food. It’s wonderful.”Like many informal settlements acrossMexico, Aparicio Bahena’s metal-roofed home is not connected to the water network, and for years she had to collect water from a well about 30 minutes away. With such water scarcity, gardening wasn’t a priority.That was until a rainwater harvesting system, which captures water from the roof and filters it, was installed in her garden four years ago. It stores 2,500 litres of water, while a smaller tank, used only for watering plants, holds 1,100 litres.View image in fullscreenAparicio Bahena’s rainwater harvesting system was installed four years ago.Photograph: Suzanne BearneThe system is provided byIsla Urbana, an organisation dedicated to tackling water scarcity. It creates rainwater harvesting systems that provide mainly low-income households with sustainable water access for five to eight months of the year (during the dry season rainfall is insufficient). Founded in 2009, it aims to reach1 million peoplewho would previously have had to collect water themselves, by next year.While most users are based in Mexico City, Jalisco and the State of Mexico, it has installed systems nationwide, mainly for Indigenous communities, who lack access to water due to geographic, economic or sociopolitical reasons.“Much of our work involves delivering water to areas that are hard to reach with conventional systems,” says co-founder Enrique Lomnitz, who studied industrial design at university before deciding to develop sustainable solutions for low-income households.The company operates as a non-profit when installing its systems for low-income families, while selling its systems privately to fund its work, alongside grants and donations from Mexico, the US and elsewhere. Many of its private contracts are with the government to supply water-scarce, low-income regions.View image in fullscreenAn Isla Urbana team installing a rainwater harvesting system near Mexico CityFor governments, the system either replaces or complements fleets of water trucks, says Lomnitz. In the past three years, Isla Urbana has increased its focus on schools, installing rainwater harvesting systems at 2,000 public schools across the country, as well as educating children on water sustainability.“The intention is to build the foundations for a much more water-conscious and water-capable population, with useful knowledge about how they could harvest and store water safely at home,” says Lomnitz.While Mexico approved anational water access policylast year that outlines the human right to water and prioritises personal and domestic consumption over industrial use, water scarcity remains widespread. Droughts are a regular occurrence and have becomemore frequent and severedue to the climate crisis.View image in fullscreenPeople queue to collect water from a tanker truck in Nuevo Leon in 2022.Photograph: Gary Coronado/Los Angeles Times/Getty ImagesAlmost17% of households struggleto meet basic water needs such as drinking, cooking and handwashing, according to theNational Health and Nutrition Survey, while74 million people(57% of the population) lack access to safe water.In the neighbourhood of Benito Juárez in Mexico City, Dora Napolitano relies on a 5,000-litre rainwater harvesting system in her garden to cope with scarcity. While Napolitano has access to tap water, unlike Aparicio Bahena, the textile artist says she bought the system in 2018 for environmental reasons.“I was increasingly aware that the way we were living in Mexico City was completely unsustainable,” says Napolitano, who lives with her husband and four children, aged between 17 and 25. “Parents would complain about the traffic, the poor air quality, the flooding and the lack of water, but nobody took any responsibility. I’m not exonerating the government, but I felt the need to discover how much I could do.”Napolitano says the system, which cost about 40,000 Mexican pesos (£1,700), including installation, has been “a life-saver” during intense periods of drought, such as in 2018 and two years ago, when there was no mains water for several weeks.View image in fullscreenIn Benito Juárez, Mexico City, Dora Napolitano has installed a 5,000-litre rainwater harvesting system in her garden.Photograph: Suzanne Bearne“It has got us out of trouble several times,” she says. “We live in a privileged area of Mexico City, but if we lived somewhere without [tap] water, we would be using it a lot more. I really wanted to show that it was viable.”Judith Domínguez, a research professor specialising in water management, the climate crisis and urban environment at El Colegio de México, says rainwater harvesting is a good alternative in areas lacking formal public services.“These systems can mitigate water scarcity, especially during rainy seasons, as well as address social and water vulnerability,” she says. “They require minimal maintenance, are installed at the household level, and can purify the water on-site for various uses, including drinking.”Yet the systems must be constantly monitored to ensure water quality is maintained to prevent health problems, says Domínguez.‘I fear people will go to war over water’: as wells run dry, farmers struggle to survive in BangladeshRead more“This also involves technical issues such as filter inspection and replacement, which the household must do itself once government or non-governmental organisation assistance is withdrawn,” she says. “The household bears the cost. Mechanisms must be found to make it more affordable than constantly buying bottled water or water delivered by tanker trucks from private vendors.”View image in fullscreenA rainwater harvesting system in Guadalajara, part of a programme run by the Jalisco state governmentBack in Cristo Rey, for Aparicio Bahena, the rainwater has not only enabled her to grow food but is also used for washing clothes, flushing toilets and cleaning, allowing the six-person household to bathe regularly. “We used to only be able to wash once a week,” she says.While the harvested water is not drinkable, Aparicio Bahena uses a small plastic filter to convert this greywater into safe drinking water. For her, water is no longer a source of stress, adding to the two-hour journey she faces every day to get to work as a maid.“The water harvesting system really changed our lives. Now we are able to dream and have more time,” she says. “It changed everything.”Explore more on these topicsGlobal developmentSouthern frontlines: Latin America and the CaribbeanAccess to waterWaterMexicoAmericasDroughtClimate crisisfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Experts warn of rise in sadistic online exploitation of vulnerable children | Technology | The Guardian
description: Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material
Author: Katie McQue
Headline: Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material
Content: The cases received by the NCMEC represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say.Photograph: Chainarong Prasertthai/Getty Images/iStockphotoView image in fullscreenThe cases received by the NCMEC represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say.Photograph: Chainarong Prasertthai/Getty Images/iStockphotoTechnologyExperts warn of rise in sadistic online exploitation of vulnerable childrenGlobal increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse materialKatie McQueMon 20 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCases of predators coercing children into producing sexual photos and videos of themselves have surged globally in the past year, a cruel phenomenon known as “sadistic online exploitation”, child safety experts and law enforcement officials warn.In cases of sadistic online exploitation, perpetrators target vulnerable teens on social media platforms and online games. These predators then compel their victims into producing child sexual abuse material (CSAM), harming themselves and others, or, in extreme cases, attempting suicide.“We often see that the offenders will communicate with that child and, through that initial communication, ask them for nude or explicit imagery of themselves,” said Kathryn Rifenbark, a director at the National Center of Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC). “Then they’re using that as a leverage to cause the child to harm themselves or harm other children or other people in their home or create more explicit imagery of them or other children in their home.”Recent cases include theJune sentencingof a 21-year-old Maryland man to 30 years in prison for sexually exploiting 10 female minors and cyberstalking.The perpetrator “used the internet to create and share extreme content – such as gore, violence, and child sexual abuse material – to extort and blackmail vulnerable teenagers”, and the man sexually exploited his victims and encouraged them to engage in self-harm, according to a Department of Justice press release.The NCMEC received more than 3,000 reports of sadistic online exploitation worldwide in 2025, a 125% increase from the previous year. But those cases represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say, because the NCMEC is only notified if child sexual abuse material has been detected and reported.Predators targeting children often belong to online networks, and are often teens or young adults themselves. To recruit their victims, the perpetrators engage with video games popular with children, such as Roblox and Minecraft. A spokesperson for Microsoft, which owns Minecraft, said: “Our goal is to make Minecraft one of the hardest places for bad actors to operate, and we have a variety of tools we use to help foster a safe online experience, such as parental controls, chat filtering, in-game reporting, and dedicated moderation teams.”They may also search social media groups that focus on mental health issues such as eating disorders or suicide ideation because they are specifically looking for vulnerable children to target and groom, child safety experts interviewed said.Perpetrators of sadistic online exploitation who coerce children into inflicting other forms of harm on themselves fall outside those reports.The coerced abuses can include forcing children to cut themselves, eat their own vomit, drink urine, die by suicide, or harm other children and pets in their homes. Some groups also engage in mass casualty planning, doxing and cyber-harassment.A common form of abuse is forcing victims to carve perpetrators’ social media usernames on their bodies, said Amanda Goharian, director of research at Thorn, a child safety non-profit.“One of the key things in these groups is that the level of severity of the harms that are being committed are extreme, and they can rapidly develop into outcomes such as completed suicides,” said Goharian.In February, theFBI issued a warningto parents and caregivers about the “sharp increase” in the activity of violent online networks, including the “764” group, targeting and exploiting children and other vulnerable individuals.FBI opens inquiry into 764, online group that sexually exploits and encourages minors to self-harmRead more“The term nihilistic violent extremism (NVE) refers to what is motivating most of these subjects to commit criminal acts. They want to sow chaos and facilitate the destruction of society,” said the letter. “These subjects may be engaging in criminal activity for sexual gratification, social status, a sense of belonging, or for a mix of other reasons.”The predators target vulnerable children who have disclosed personal information online about the struggles they face, such as mental health challenges, eating disorders, self-harm or suicidal ideation, via gaming or social media platforms. These children are looking for connections and support online, which can render them more susceptible to manipulation, said the experts interviewed.Predators typically invite victims to move the conversation to a messaging app such as Telegram, which, according to Rifenbark, “doesn’t report very much” to the NCMEC, meaning the organization has limited visibility into potential child sexual exploitation.It’s on these messaging platforms where the sadistic online exploitation takes place. Telegram did not respond to a request for comment.Many of the perpetrators have troubled lives or are socially isolated in real life, and are looking for notoriety online as a form of empowerment and attention from others, experts interviewed said.“These groups are focused on harming children and targeting children for the purposes of clout and like gaining hierarchy and power both within their groups and over the victims,” said Maria Thomas, investigator at Rise Information Security Foundation, a non-profit focused on cybersecurity. “Teens join them because it’s seen as a subversive and edgy thing to do.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe perpetrators gain status within their groups from posting evidence of their abuse, images such as dossiers of photos and screenshots of messages with their victims, which are known as “lore books”. Typically, these collections are uploaded on to a Telegram channel labeled as “Victim Name Lore Book”, experts said.“Harm is perpetrated as a way to essentially gain clout within the network, and to be elevated,” said Goharian.Organized nihilistic violent extremism and sadistic online exploitation were first documented in the 2010s, when a youth cybergang called the Com, which focused on hacking and identity theft before some subsets shifted into child exploitation. Splinter networks, such as CVLT, an online group that espoused neo-Nazism, nihilism and pedophilia as its core principles, were formed. The group 764 was formed in 2021 by a teenager in Texas who had learned grooming and exploitation methods as a member of CVLT.Online exploitation can escalate into dramatic real-world violence, experts warn. In February, an 18-year-old man from Pennsylvania wasarrested and chargedwith grooming a 13-year-old girl he met on social gaming platform Discord, allegedly convincing her to send him sexually explicit material of herself and images of self-harm. The man travelled across the country to engage in sexual activity with the girl in a motel, according to the US justice department. “Discord has a longstanding policy onTeen and Child Safetythat prohibits sexual exploitation and predatory behavior, including content or communities that facilitate grooming,” said a Discord spokesperson.“When law enforcement arrived at the motel room, they encountered [the man] and the victim and found condoms, a knife, lubricant, razor blades, bloody tissues,” stated a Department of Justice press release about the case. “The victim told law enforcement that [the man] and she had engaged in sexual conduct and that he had used a knife to repeatedly cut her.”In May, a 16-year-old boy from South Carolina was arrested for engaging inexploitation tied to 764after receiving a tip from Discord regarding possible school violence.“Offenders have a very, very high level of control over the children. They are using that connection that they’ve built with that child to really try to control that child to do some pretty harmful things to themselves,” said Rifenbark.Rifenbark said the NCMEC had received reports from parents who discovered what was happening to their child and took away the child’s phone, only for the child to find another device in the home, such as an old device the family was no longer using, and use it to re-engage with the offenders online, probably due to the high levels of control and grooming they have been subjected to.“A lot of times when victims are coming and reporting to us, they’re very scared, they’re scared of retaliation from offenders, they’re worried that their personal information might get leaked, because that’s something that the offenders are doing as part of their victimization,” Rifenbark said.According to Goharian, the networks are highly persistent, with individual perpetrators often operating hundreds of accounts. If one account is reported for violating a platform’s terms of service and removed, they can quickly switch to “shadow” accounts that are already established and immediately resume their activity.According to a state prosecutor, speaking on the condition of anonymity out of concern for professional reprisals, some juveniles who commit sexual abuse do not outgrow the behavior but often go on to offend as adults. Better interventions are needed for young offenders before their behavior escalates, they said.“We just do a really bad job sometimes of recognizing dangerous kids,” said the prosecutor. “I don’t think these kids are going to grow out of it. I think they’re growing into it.”In the US, call or text theChildhelpabuse hotline on 800-422-4453 or visittheir websitefor more resources and to report child abuse or DM for help. For adult survivors of child abuse, help is available atascasupport.org. In the UK, theNSPCCoffers support to children on 0800 1111, and adults concerned about a child on 0808 800 5000. The National Association for People Abused in Childhood (Napac) offers support for adult survivors on 0808 801 0331. In Australia, children, young adults, parents and teachers can contact the Kids Helpline on 1800 55 1800, orBraveheartson 1800 272 831, and adult survivors can contactBlue Knot Foundationon 1300 657 380. Other sources of help can be found atChild Helplines InternationalExplore more on these topicsTechnologyChildrenChild protectionInternetnewsShareReuse this content

title: Jeff Bezos and UK government invest in £2bn British startup CuspAI | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Company aims to develop AI software that cuts research times and use of rare metals in chipmakers’ supply chains
Author: Mark Sweney
Headline: Company aims to develop AI software that cuts research times and use of rare metals in chipmakers’ supply chains
Content: Nvidia's Groq 3 chip. The firm is part of coalition launched by CuspAI that aims to build software to discover and developed new materials for chipmakers.Photograph: Max A Cherney/ReutersView image in fullscreenNvidia's Groq 3 chip. The firm is part of coalition launched by CuspAI that aims to build software to discover and developed new materials for chipmakers.Photograph: Max A Cherney/ReutersAI (artificial intelligence)Jeff Bezos and UK government invest in £2bn British startup CuspAICompany aims to develop AI software that cuts research times and use of rare metals in chipmakers’ supply chainsBusiness live – latest updatesMark SweneyMon 20 Jul 2026 04.37 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAmazon’s founder,Jeff Bezos, and the UK government have invested in a £2bn British artificial intelligence startup that is aiming to become the “search engine for rare materials” that accelerates the next wave of technological breakthroughs.The Cambridge-based CuspAI hasraised $450m (£330m) in fundingfrom investors including Bezos and the government’s sovereign AI fund, valuing the two-year-old business at $2.6bn.The company has launched the AI Materials Foundry, a coalition of more than 48 tech companies, industrial firms and research facilities, with the aim of building software underpinned by artificial intelligence to discover and develop new materials for chipmakers and other industries.CuspAI hopes to significantly cut research times and reduce or eliminate the use of rare metals – including iridium and ruthenium – in chipmakers’ supply chains.The coalition, which includes the chipmaker Nvidia, the Facebook and Instagram owner, Meta Platforms, and the carmaker Hyundai, aims to use frontier AI, the most advanced, general-purpose artificial intelligence models, to discover new materials that could make energy networks and batteries more efficient and advance the power of semiconductors that underpin the AI revolution.“If we don’t make progress fast, the next 50 years of industrial progress will be constrained by a single challenge: the world needs materials that don’t yet exist,” said the CuspAI co-founders Dr Chad Edwards and Prof Max Welling. “That’s what we’re on a mission to solve – combining frontier agentic AI with deep domain expertise, exclusive data access and close customer partnerships. The world simply can’t make the progress it needs to with known materials, that much is clear.”The company said the round ofseries Bfunding would enable it to expand internationally, opening a new office in Singapore and increasing staff numbers in Cambridge, Amsterdam, Berlin, Tokyo and the US.Josh Coyne, a partner at Kleiner Perkins, the venture capital firm that co-led the latest round of funding, said: “Most big leaps in technology come down to a material, and the next set, from cheaper carbon capture to semiconductors and cleaner water, is stuck waiting on materials nobody has discovered yet. CuspAI built a search engine that changes that.”The latest fundraise followed a $100m series A investment last year.CuspAI is the fourth company to receive funding from the government’s fund targeting investment in early-stage British AI firms to help them scale up to compete on the world stage. The government did not disclose the size of the investment.Criteria for receiving funding from the sovereign AI fund, which makes a typical equity investment in a startup of between £1m and £10m, include having a main office in the UK and the company’s founders being British.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLiz Kendall, the science and technology secretary, said: “CuspAI is putting the power of AI to work to help us tackle some of the biggest challenges facing the UK and the world today, such as energy and climate change. By backing British companies such as this we are paving the way for breakthroughs that are set to grow our economy, protect the natural world and improve everyone’s lives.”CuspAI has brought on board John Giannandrea, a former Apple and Google executive, who is helping set up its US operations.The company is also working with Abhi Talwalkar, a board member at chip maker AMD and chair of Lam Research.Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)Technology startupsJeff BezosTechnology policyTechnology sectorEconomic policyComputingnewsShareReuse this content

title: AliExpress fined record €550m by EU for failing to stop sale of illegal and fake goods | Retail industry | The Guardian
description: Chinese retail platform handed penalty over listings for items such as counterfeit products and unsafe toys
Author: Lisa O’Carroll
Headline: Chinese retail platform handed penalty over listings for items such as counterfeit products and unsafe toys
Content: AliExpress is the largest online retail operator in the EU with 193 million users.Photograph: Sheldon Cooper/SOPA Images/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenAliExpress is the largest online retail operator in the EU with 193 million users.Photograph: Sheldon Cooper/SOPA Images/ShutterstockRetail industryAliExpress fined record €550m by EU for failing to stop sale of illegal and fake goodsChinese retail platform handed penalty over listings for items such as counterfeit products and unsafe toysBusiness live – latest updatesLisa O’CarrollMon 20 Jul 2026 10.57 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.56 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Chinese online retail platform AliExpress has been fined a record €550m (£470m) by the EU over its failure to stop illegal goods including harmful clothing, cosmetics and kitchen gadgets being sold through its site.The European Commission fine is the biggest imposed by the bloc under Digital Services Act (DSA) legislation to protect consumers from illegal goods and deceptive or addictive marketing techniques.Henna Virkkunen, the commission’s executive vice-president for tech sovereignty, security and democracy, said: “The spread of counterfeit clothing, unsafe toys, dangerous cosmetics and other illegal and harmful products is not an unavoidable cost of shopping online – it is a failure by AliExpress to comply with its obligations under the Digital Services Act.EU fines Temu for failing to stop sale of illegal and dangerous productsRead more“Scale is not an excuse; risks must be identified and addressed systematically to ensure consumers can safely shop online. Today, we are holding AliExpress to this standard and request it to take action.”While the fine was much larger than those previously issued under the DSA to Temu (€200m) and X (€120m), it represented less than 1% of the €122bn that AliExpress’s parent company,Alibaba, generated in revenue last year. It could have been fined a maximum of 6% of global annual revenue.Temu wasfined in Mayfor failing to stop the sale of illegal and dangerous products, whileX was finedfor breaches including what the EU said was a “deceptive” blue tick verification badge given to users and the lack of transparency of the platform’s advertising. Temu is still under EU investigation on other issues and may yet face another fine.The European Commission found AliExpress did not have enough staff to assess the legality of products, sometimes giving them just “tens of seconds” to judge whether a product met EU standards.It also found many illegal products were being promoted under AliExpress’s recommendation systems and that the company’s internal risk assessments failed.“Many illegal products, from counterfeit products to unsafe toys and dangerous cosmetics, circulated on the platform and, even if detected, remained online for multiple weeks,” the commission said.The size of the fine reflected “the nature and gravity” of the platform’s failure to implement mitigating measures to stop consumers being offered harmful or dangerous goods, a senior commission official added.AliExpress immediately condemned the fine as “disproportionate”. It said: “We disagree with today’s decision and the disproportionate fine, which ‌does not adequately reflect our established framework and the significant, proactive enhancements we ⁠have made.The company said it would “appeal the decision” by the commission, claiming the fine “ignores our sound risk management framework and the significant, proactive enhancements we have made”.Previous investigations by the EU of a sample of products sold on large retail platforms including Shein found 65% of cosmetics, 63% of food supplements and 60% of personal protection equipment, such as hard hats and steel toe cap boots for building sites, were non-compliant.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionOfficials said the fine was not a result of the discovery of illegal products on AliExpress but its failure to put in place barriers or mitigations that would have protected consumers from “illegal, non-compliant and counterfeit goods”, which is illegal under EU law.After an investigation process lasting more than two years, the company was given the opportunity to rectify its compliance and risk procedures but failed to do so, the European Commission said.AliExpress operated terms and conditions that had the appearance of being compliant with EU law – that retailers should not place any illegal products on its platform – but the reality was that sellers could easily place non-compliant goods on the platform, the commission found.Through its internal testing, the commission found large-scale breaches despite the company’s claims to be operating within the scope of the EU’s “safety gate”, a rapid alert system for non-food products that allows unsafe products to be quickly identified, recalled or banned.The commission said it “found millions of products that reappeared online which sometimes stayed for longer than a month” after they had been flagged as being illegal under EU law.The commission was particularly concerned about the high number of counterfeit goods on the site and the ease with which a seller could get around barriers, sometimes “miscategorising” a counterfeit fashion label as non-branded.AliExpress is the largest Chinese online retail operator in the EU with 193 million users, making it significantly bigger than Shein with 156 million and Temu with 130 million.Explore more on these topicsRetail industryAlibabaEuropean UnionEuropeChinanewsShareReuse this content

title: Shania Twain: Little Miss Twain review – rootsy revival strays from the dirt track to the middle of the road | Shania Twain | The Guardian
description: The country legend’s seventh album revisits her troubled early life but plays it safe with zany humour and fluffy songs about young love
Author: Katie Hawthorne
Headline: The country legend’s seventh album revisits her troubled early life but plays it safe with zany humour and fluffy songs about young love
Content: Little Eilleen … Shania Twain.Photograph: Timothy WhiteView image in fullscreenLittle Eilleen … Shania Twain.Photograph: Timothy WhiteShania TwainReviewShania Twain: Little Miss Twain review – rootsy revival strays from the dirt track to the middle of the road(Republic Nashville)The country legend’s seventh album revisits her troubled early life but plays it safe with zany humour and fluffy songs about young loveKatie HawthorneMon 20 Jul 2026 03.16 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘When I was a kid, I started on stages just like this,” Shania Twain told the 200 fanssardined into a London pub back in June, trailing her seventh album Little Miss Twain. A reintroduction to the Canadian country legend, the record mines memories from her Ontario childhood for hints of the arena-filling star she would soon become.View image in fullscreenThe artwork for Little Miss Twain.Photograph: Publicity imageOver wistful Mellotron and pedal steel, the title track marvels at little Eilleen, as she was called back then, escaping the rural north and making it big in Nashville. Yet, somehow, it all feels inevitable – as if Twain’s career was conjured into being by the conviction of her mother, who drove her to those tiny bars in a beaten-up Chevy. And yes, from the vantage point of 2026, it’s difficult to imagine it going any other way. Twain’s icon status is certified by five Grammys and the way her free-spirited hits have become karaoke classics – not to mention the fact that her 1997 album Come on Over remains the highest-selling studio album by any female artist in history.But it was far from inevitable. The first decades of Twain’s life were shaped by poverty, violence and the shocking death of her parents, which left her responsible for raising three younger siblings. Northern Town, a noirish track with upright bass and flourishes of classical guitar, gives a veiled insight into those early years, its lyrics thick with cigarette smoke and the irony of being hard-up in gold rush territory.
