On a double-time bridge, she fantasises about cutting loose and driving south to sing: “Just so you know, I’m ready to go,” she murmurs.Shania Twain: Dirty Rosie – videoUnfortunately, the rest of Little Miss Twain is vague on the details. Songs such as Quit My Life (an upbeat number about taking the day off) could be imagined from the perspective of young Twain weighed down by responsibility – but truthfully, there’s little to suggest it. Instead, memory lane is left in the rear-view, and Twain pivots from tender autobiography to her tried-and-tested comfort zone: zany humour and unadventurous but catchy melodies, all within the pop-rock-country Venn diagram she made her own in the 90s.Much of it feels far too safe, from the soul-lite Take It to the River, featuring Ronnie Wood and The War and Treaty, to the clutch of fluffy, overproduced tracks about young love. The best of them, New Love, has a cantering beat, blushing strings and a lovely lightness of touch, so it’s a shame its themes are rehashed on the hokey saloon song Down the Middle and the forgettable Scary Little Thing.Grab your Stetsons! How country music is taking over the UKRead moreThankfully, recent single Dirty Rosie is hoedown pop with real personality. Doing doughnuts around truck-obsessed country bro stereotypes, it puts Twain’s increasingly textured voice to great use. By contrast, I’d Be Loving Me sounds vocally strained, but in a way that brings much-needed gravity and ache to the record. Ditching the denim and dirt roads of country cliche, this deceptively simple ballad wonders how Twain would apologise if she were in the shoes of her cheating partner, and whether, roles reversed, her partner would accept that apology – all with the sinking feeling that little will change.On the title track, Twain sings of two dreams: she wanted to ride horses, while her mother wanted her to become “the next Tanya Tucker”. The two dreams intertwined: Tucker duets on those very lines, and Twain sighs over a lovely horse on the insouciant That’s the Money. Little Miss Twain may not be a return to peak powers, but amid country’s fresh crest in popularity it’s a sharply timed reminder of Shania’s rich legacy and influence. The genre would be far blander without her.Explore more on these topicsShania TwainCountryPop and rockreviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Being American feels helpless and scary’: actor Ben Platt on nerves, queer songs and why he couldn’t do it without his husband | Theatre | The Guardian
description: The star of Dear Evan Hansen plays a 1960s Greenwich Village crooner in hit queer musical Midnight at the Never Get. It’s the perfect outlet for his anxiety, he says
Author: David Jays
Headline: The star of Dear Evan Hansen plays a 1960s Greenwich Village crooner in hit queer musical Midnight at the Never Get. It’s the perfect outlet for his anxiety, he says
Content: ‘It unfolds into something deeper and stranger’ … Ben Platt in Midnight at the Never Get.Photograph: Matt CrockettView image in fullscreen‘It unfolds into something deeper and stranger’ … Ben Platt in Midnight at the Never Get.Photograph: Matt CrockettTheatre‘Being American feels helpless and scary’: actor Ben Platt on nerves, queer songs and why he couldn’t do it without his husbandThe star of Dear Evan Hansen plays a 1960s Greenwich Village crooner in hit queer musical Midnight at the Never Get. It’s the perfect outlet for his anxiety, he saysDavid JaysMon 20 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn performance, Ben Platt is a mellifluous singer and loose-limbed dancer, with a fizzy back-note of neurotic New York. The stage – most famously, as star of Dear Evan Hansen – is his safe space. In person, in a south London rehearsal room, he is sweet, earnest, nervy. He crosses his legs, knots his arms about him. The chat roams widely, the body language is wound tight.Platt talks frankly about anxiety, like it’s his constant companion. So, how’s that going? “My relationship with my husband is a huge factor in being able to manage anxiety. There’s still highs and lows, but everything’s more in my sights, because I thankfully have him. And having something creative to focus on is a big help.”Luckily, Platt comes to London with both husband (actor Noah Galvin, starring inHit Machineat Soho theatre) and a creative project – the musicalMidnight at the Never Get. It takes us to a 1960s Greenwich Village gay bar (though the piece, Platt hints, “unfolds into something deeper and stranger”). Platt plays cabaret crooner Trevor. “I don’t really like the word flamboyant, because it’s overused, but he’s effervescent. He very much wears his heart on his sleeve.” New YorkerDavid Cromerdirects, and Platt praises “his very subtle hand. He watches what people are bringing, and then he’ll go in and sculpt.”View image in fullscreen‘He wears his heart on his sleeve’ … Ben Platt rehearsing for Midnight at the Never Get at the Menier Chocolate Factory, London.Photograph: Johan PerssonThe “extraordinary” songs byMark Sonnenblick(KPop Demon Hunters) let Platt sing love lyrics to another man. Does that make a difference to him? “One hundred per cent. I grew up doing musicals – years and years, so many shows – but never did I get to play a queer character on stage. To play a three-dimensional character who is singing expressly queer songs with details of queer romance, it does feel so liberating.”Midnight at the Never Get isn’t explicitly political, but lands at a moment when the progress of gay rights seems to have halted, even reversed, in Platt’s home nation. “Being American feels quite helpless and scary,” he admits. “But being in New York I find optimistic and hopeful.” New mayor Zohran Mamdani represents “a different class of politics, as opposed to abusive painful noise for the sake of abusive painful noise”.double quotation markTo play a three-dimensional character who sings expressly queer songs feels so liberatingAt 32, Platt has a quarter century of stage experience. His first school show, at six, saw him don a sparkly jacket as the prince in Cinderella. “I mostly remember the energy simmer of getting ready, excited to be in that fizziness.” Professional gigs followed quickly – anxious young Ben was also focused and self-directed. “I wanted to grow up quickly,” he reflects. “I was so hungry to be like the adults I was working with, and be counted among them.”The show that made Platt – as both performer and person – wasDear Evan Hansen, playing the problematic high-school hero from early read-throughs and on to Broadway. “I had never been asked to go to those emotional lengths before. The material was so strong, it forced me to rise to the occasion.” Committing to the show, he reflects, meant “putting my entire life on mute. I put my own development on the back burner, and just lived in the space of this character for many years.”Platt emerged with a Tony and in 2021 was cast tolead the subsequent movie. This was less joyful: Platt was derided as too old at 27, too much the producer’s son. “It was tough, but it’s a lesson you have to learn in this business, which is sometimes it doesn’t go well and you have to let the rollercoaster dip so that it can go back up. The fact that it was that character made it a little more painful – but we move forward.” So the brouhaha didn’t tarnish his bond to Evan Hansen? “No, no. It was completely life-changing in every way. I’m happy that the movie exists, and hope history will be kind to it.”View image in fullscreen‘It was tough’ … Ben Platt as Evan Hansen with Kaitlyn Dever as Zoe Murphy in the Dear Evan Hansen movie.Photograph: UniversalEarlier this year, Platt worked the art theme of the Met Gala,his jacketreferencing the Seurat painting which inspired Stephen Sondheim’sSunday in the Park With George. That’s the role Platt covets above all others. “George, George, George,” he sighs. “It is everything that is meaningful to me in a musical. It’s about the sacrifice of being an artist – how you have to live somewhat in your mind, and that’s a beautiful thing, but also a hard truth.”Platt may consider the composer “my rabbi, my imam, I live my life by his words” – but he has not yet done a professional stage Sondheim. On film, however, he is deep into making Merrily We Roll Along. Sondheim’s 1981 musical follows its central trio in reverse, from jaded middle age back to youthful optimism – and director Richard Linklater has been filming it forwards, in real time. “The musical takes place over 19 or 20 years,” Platt explains. If there are three years between scenes, “we wait three years exactly, so our characters grow the same amount”. Platt and co-stars Paul Mescal and Beanie Feldstein are now about a third of the way through. “We’re meant to finish in 2038. I’ll be 45. It’s completely insane, and also like a family reunion that happens every couple of years.”Any other ambitions? “Like every gay person in the world, I hope to do Angels [in America, by Tony Kushner] at some point. But the ideal scenario is always for something new to come crashing through.”Midnight at the Never Get is atMenier Chocolate Factory, London, until 12 SeptemberExplore more on these topicsTheatreMusicalsMenier Chocolate FactoryLGBTQ+ rightsStephen SondheimfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Mockbuster review – how to survive the nightmare of directing a terrible low-budget horror film | Movies | The Guardian
description: Struggling to make it in Australia, aspiring director Anthony Frith cold-calls the team behind Sharknado and bites off more than he can chew in this making-of documentary
Author: Phil Hoad
Headline: Struggling to make it in Australia, aspiring director Anthony Frith cold-calls the team behind Sharknado and bites off more than he can chew in this making-of documentary
Content: Career shock therapy … director Anthony Frith.View image in fullscreenCareer shock therapy … director Anthony Frith.MoviesReviewMockbuster review – how to survive the nightmare of directing a terrible low-budget horror filmStruggling to make it in Australia, aspiring director Anthony Frith cold-calls the team behind Sharknado and bites off more than he can chew in this making-of documentaryPhil HoadMon 20 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Abad movie is better than no movie” is the motto of this behind-the-scenes documentary, which charts director Anthony Frith’s quest to make his debut feature for ultra-schlock studio The Asylum; it doubles up as an affable salute to film-making savoir-faire.Stuck making corporate videos to support his young family, Adelaide-based Frith is in danger of letting his artistic ambitions atrophy. So he opts for career shock therapy by cold-calling The Asylum (ofSharknado notoriety)in Burbank, California and proposing he oversee one of their straight-to-the-digital-cesspit features.It turns out that if working for The Asylum were a film genre, it would be the against-all-odds suicide mission type of story. Frith is given six days to complete filming on a new version of The Land That Time Forgot, which will be the studio’s first movie shot in Australia. The budget is subatomic, there is no finished script when he signs the contract and he is told, only days before the shoot, to secure his own production team. Relying on this extreme frugality to ensure their profit margins, The Asylum executives laughingly dote on their directors with quasi-sadistic fondness. Frith might just make it through this, they say – if he doesn’t try to get “artistic”.Any such notions quickly go out the window. Facing the schedule from hell, Frith canvasses survival advice from The Asylum veterans, such as actor Eric Roberts, and low-budget grafters includinghorror wonderkid Danny Philippou, who scoffs that Frith looks broken before he’s begun. On the executive side, there’s something almost refreshing in The Asylum’s cynicism – the studio’s credo is that the majority of viewers have no patience for arthouse complexity, so why should film-makers? As the LA movie ecosystem dries up, these survivalist cockroaches may well be the last outfit standing; movie-biz expediency in its most atavistic form, where “the genre is the star”.Frith can be candid too: chivvied through by Brendan Petrizzo, a faintly unsettling in-house producer with a frosted fringe, the director reflects that he may have found his natural level. Maybe … or maybe not. Presumably too harassed by shooting his disasterpiece, he sometimes struggles to break down his experience into dramatically parsable portions for this making-of documentary, leaning on voiceover to tell the story. But, ironically, he succeeds in making quite a good film; a companionable reflection on artistic aspirations, compromise and on-set camaraderie, all – true to The Asylum’s spirit – in a solid 90 minutes.Mockbuster is on digital platforms in the US now and in the UK from 27 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmDocumentary filmsHorror filmsFilm industryreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Out of Sight review – mountain idyll in the Austrian uplands is a gilded cage for distressed asylum seekers | Movies | The Guardian
description: Young men share their heartbreaking experiences of an Alpine ‘deportation camp’ in Lisa Polster’s impactful, gentle documentary
Author: Phuong Le
Headline: Young men share their heartbreaking experiences of an Alpine ‘deportation camp’ in Lisa Polster’s impactful, gentle documentary
Content: No freedom … Out of Sight.Photograph: True StoryView image in fullscreenNo freedom … Out of Sight.Photograph: True StoryMoviesReviewOut of Sight review – mountain idyll in the Austrian uplands is a gilded cage for distressed asylum seekersYoung men share their heartbreaking experiences of an Alpine ‘deportation camp’ in Lisa Polster’s impactful, gentle documentaryPhuong LeMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.13 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLocated in the Austrian Tyrol, the Bürglkopf mountain is a picture-perfect Alpine landscape, with its verdant fields and scenic trails. The tranquil idyll is a popular holiday spot, with thousands of tourists each year. Some visitors, however, are less welcome than others. Nestled among the lush forest on the mountaintop is a so-called deportation camp, where asylum seekers are held for months, even years. In Lisa Polster’s impactful documentary, one of our first glimpses of the facility and its surroundings is through the window of a moving car. The tightly composed shot suggests the secrecy that enshrouds this place, described by its inhabitants as a prison rather than a shelter.Through interviews with current residents, Polster’s film weaves a sobering tapestry of experiences – in spite of the centre’s resistance to the documentary project. Many asylum seekers speak of poor living conditions, lack of access to legal support, and the impossibility of social integration due to the camp’s remote location. Their legal limbo, caused by excessive delays in application processing, leads to further psychological distress. In one heartbreaking scene, a detainee looks wistfully at the cattle herds that graze nearby, envious of their freedom of movement. With strict curfew hours and few recreational activities on offer, time at the Bürglkopf camp stretches on seemingly infinitely.While much of the film unfolds in a straightforward talking-head style, there’s a gentleness to how the detainees are photographed. These young men are constantly demonised by the far-right media, yet in Polster’s film they are framed as slight figures against the vastness of the landscape. To rob these men of their time, the only valuable currency they can call their own, is to deny them any kind of future at all.Out of Sight is on True Story from 24 July.This article was amended on 20 July 2026 to correct the location of the camp, which is in Austria, but not on the country’s border withSwitzerlandas an earlier version said.Explore more on these topicsFilmDocumentary filmsAustriaMigrationEuropeSwitzerlandreviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Justice, morality and triumph’: why Matilda is my feelgood movie | Movies | The Guardian
description: The latest in our series of writers picking their most meaningful comfort watches is a tribute to the inspiring power of the 1996 Roald Dahl adaptation
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The latest in our series of writers picking their most meaningful comfort watches is a tribute to the inspiring power of the 1996 Roald Dahl adaptation
Content: Mara Wilson in Matilda.Photograph: Tristar/AllstarView image in fullscreenMara Wilson in Matilda.Photograph: Tristar/AllstarMy feelgood movieMovies‘Justice, morality and triumph’: why Matilda is my feelgood movieThe latest in our series of writers picking their most meaningful comfort watches is a tribute to the inspiring power of the 1996 Roald Dahl adaptationLydia Spencer-ElliottMon 20 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.27 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhen I think about what makes me feel good, it isn’t fantasy or escapism; it’s justice, morality and triumph – sizing up to a bully bigger than you and prevailing against the odds. I never fail to be charmed by the story of an underdog, someone who shouldn’t win but finds a way through resilience, determination or sometimes a genius loophole and a little bit of magic, and no film hits those attributes harder for me than Danny DeVito’s Matilda.‘You want to reach out and touch everything’: why Labyrinth is my feelgood movieRead moreThe zany adaptation of Roald Dahl’s 1988 children’s novel came out in cinemas in December 1996, four months after I came into the world. The film, set (unlike the supremely British source text) in a leopard-print-clad edition of suburban California, focuses on the titular character (played by Mara Wilson), who grows up unseen and unheard by her grifter, TV-obsessed parents, Harry and Zinnia Wormwood, played by DeVito and his then-wife Rhea Perlman.As an infant, Matilda can write and spell her own name. Aged just four, she makes herself pancakes, locates a library in the Yellow Pages and walks 10 blocks in search of something to read while her mother goes to bingo. Her brain is ravenous, and by six and a half years old (the age her parents finally agree to send her to school), Matilda is reading Herman Melville’s 1851 epic novel Moby-Dick. As the velvet-voiced narrator (also DeVito) tells us: “These books gave Matilda a hopeful and comforting message: you are not alone.”As a bookish, independent child who spent large chunks of my young life alone, I saw myself in Matilda. I grew up an only child, with half-siblings I didn’t know or care for, and a father who, although he loved me, didn’t entirely understand who I was and could, as a consequence, become quite hostile. Books, when we were alone, were a safe space to run to. To this day, they’re still how I get out of my own head; often, they’re how I fall asleep at night.Cornelia Funke’s Inkheart, Nina Bawden’s The Peppermint Pig, and Michael Morpurgo’s The Butterfly Lion were all examples that provided comfort. But, truly, I would read anything I could get my hands on. In one unfortunate incident, I acquired a Mills & Boon novel at a book exchange aged seven and emerged the next morning, asking my father what a virgin was.Matilda, a character with a reading age far superior to mine, taught me the reassuring steadfastness of escapism through books, the importance of kindness and the power of intellect, which manifests itself on screen as Matilda discovering that, thanks to her brimming brain, she’s developed the gift of telekinesis. But she was small and mighty even before that: teaching her dad a lesson by putting peroxide in his hair oil, before gluing his porkpie hat to his head with the “super superglue” he was using to stick stolen car parts on to dodgy motors.I’ve never felt like a particularly powerful force, as a child or an adult. But I cannot tell you how many times, after watching Matilda in those formative years, I spent staring at a glass of water, willing it to tip over with the sheer force of my mind. Outside of my imagination, telekinesis was never going to happen for me, but building up my brain to become my most powerful asset could. As a journalist, if I make a life change, even in a small way, that glass has moved.At a time when society is markedly corrupt and individualistic, the hope and bravery evident in Matilda make my heart glow. When I need to feel comforted after a particularly long day or anxiety-inducing hangover, I turn to Jean Speegle Howard’s softly spoken, cardigan-clad librarian, David Newman’s soaring score and the happy ending provided by Embeth Davitz’s Miss Honey, who has always reminded me of my kind and clever mum.Matilda serves as a reminder that, even if the Miss Trunchbull-esque powers that be don’t have the everyman’s interest at heart, change can be achieved by the smallest in society with integrity and compassion. Mara Wilson, sending her headteacher running scared for the hills out of town, never fails to shake off my pessimism and reignite my belief that things will get better – that that little girl, in a floral dress with a bow in her hair, can – and will – make a difference.Matilda is available on Netflix in the US, Amazon Prime and Now TV in the UK and on Netflix and Amazon Prime in AustraliaExplore more on these topicsFilmMy feelgood movieRoald DahlDanny DeVitofeaturesShareReuse this content

title: A Useful Ghost review – exhilarating black comedy summons ghosts of Thai past via a haunted hoover | Movies | The Guardian
description: Ratchapoom Boonbunchachoke’s stylish and unique movie features an unquiet spirit that inhabits a vacuum cleaner, before political interests intervene
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: Ratchapoom Boonbunchachoke’s stylish and unique movie features an unquiet spirit that inhabits a vacuum cleaner, before political interests intervene
Content: Disquieting, erotic and terribly sad … A Useful Ghost.Photograph: MubiView image in fullscreenDisquieting, erotic and terribly sad … A Useful Ghost.Photograph: MubiMoviesReviewA Useful Ghost review – exhilarating black comedy summons ghosts of Thai past via a haunted hooverRatchapoom Boonbunchachoke’s stylish and unique movie features an unquiet spirit that inhabits a vacuum cleaner, before political interests intervenePeter BradshawMon 20 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHere is a stylish and exhilaratingly confident debut from 39-year-old Thai film-maker Ratchapoom Boonbunchachoke, which won thecritics’ week grand prize at Cannes last year. It is a supernatural black-comic fantasy of national grief executed with passion and panache, about the ghosts who have refused their afterlife confinementafter the country’s succeeding catastrophes: the bloody results of a military crackdown in 2010, coup d’etats in 2006 and 2014, and before them, the 1976 Thammasat University demonstrations, along with loved ones that went to an unquiet grave of their own.A Useful Ghost is heartfelt and elegant in its compositional flair; dreamlike tableaux are crisply juxtaposed in the edit and the whole effect is disquieting, erotic and terribly sad. It’s in some ways a humorous twist on the serious spiritual realities laid out in the movies of Thai master Joe Weerasethakul, likeUncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past LivesandCemetery of Splendour. And you might also sense the spectral shimmer of western films like Ghost and Truly, Madly, Deeply.Boonbunchachokeenvisions a Thailand which is vigorously effacing and pulverising its inconvenient past; roughly removing, for example, a frieze fromBangkok’s 1940 Democracy Monument, creating clouds of metaphorical toxic dust. A queer academic (Wisarut Homhuan), writing a paper on the iconography of this frieze, is astonished when his new vacuum cleaner starts coughing up the dust it has only just inhaled. Repairman Krong (Wanlop Rungkumjad) who arrives with eerie speed after his phone call to customer services, tells him the problem is that the factory which made the hoover is haunted … and we flashback to the strange story he has to tell.A worker called Tok (Krittin Thongmai) haunted this factory as a poltergeist after he collapsed on the shop floor due to ingesting poisonous dust. And the bad karma extends to the factory owner’s own family: Nat (Davika Hoorne), the young daughter-in-law of the owner’s widow Suman (Apasiri Nitibhon) died in childbirth due to dust inhalation and she returns to invade one of the factory’s vacuum cleaners; it comes to life and tries to have sex with her grieving husband March (Wisarut Himmarat) who is poignantly ready to have this consolatory contact. But Nat’s ghost is prevailed upon by Suman and then by government minister Dr Paul (Gandhi Wasuvitchayagit) to make herself useful to the state by invading the dreams of workers who are keeping all sorts of commercially and politically inconvenient ghosts alive by remembering them.Suman has to get these undesirables forgotten again – she is the ghost in the machine of repression, a ghost who the machine itself tries to turn against all the other ghosts. And there is something deeply troubling in the way Nat’s ghost can be “turned” by exploiting her yearning to have a baby, even in her own ectoplasmic state. Without the written permission that would otherwise be necessary, her widower is told he can use the eggs she had frozen for this purpose, and Nat’s ghost is presented with a macabre “death day” gift at the minister’s luxurious modernist home. It is a movie with flesh-and-blood intensity.A Useful Ghost is on Mubi from 24 July and on SBS On Demand in Australia now.Explore more on these topicsFilmDrama filmsScience fiction and fantasy filmsThailandAsia PacificreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Chris Packham takes on a terrifying crime ring: best podcasts of the week | Television & radio | The Guardian
description: The popular broadcaster investigates what happened after a large pile of murdered animals were dumped outside a primary school in Hampshire. Plus, Lena Headey tackles an idea from an ‘ethical pornographer’
Author: Alexi Duggins
Headline: The popular broadcaster investigates what happened after a large pile of murdered animals were dumped outside a primary school in Hampshire. Plus, Lena Headey tackles an idea from an ‘ethical pornographer’
Content: Chris Packham, one of the stars of Buried: Dead Rabbit.Photograph: Dan Kitwood/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenChris Packham, one of the stars of Buried: Dead Rabbit.Photograph: Dan Kitwood/Getty ImagesBest podcasts of the weekTelevision & radioChris Packham takes on a terrifying crime ring: best podcasts of the weekThe popular broadcaster investigates what happened after a large pile of murdered animals were dumped outside a primary school in Hampshire. Plus, Lena Headey tackles an idea from an ‘ethical pornographer’Alexi Duggins,Hollie RichardsonandHannah J DaviesMon 20 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 02.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePick of the weekBuried: Dead RabbitMangled pheasants, mutilated foxes, weeping kids: the third series of the multi-award-winning show about environmental crime opens with a huge pile of murdered animals outside a primary school. The investigation leads to revelations about the world of illegal bloodsports and rural organised crime. It even recruits Chris Packham. “It’s low-level terrorism, isn’t it?” he exclaims at one point – and it’s hard not to agree. An essentiallisten.AlexiDugginsBBC Sounds, all episodes availableMystic MatesSecretly curious about tarot cards, crystals, astrology, reading tea leaves and the spiritual world? For actor mates Helen George and Yvette Robinson, this hobby has been a “dirty little secret” for 20 years. Now they’re ready to ask the big questions andshareexperiences – while having chummy fun.Hollie RichardsonWidely available, episodes weeklyIntimacyView image in fullscreenChanging everything … Lena Headey.Photograph: Maya Dehlin Spach/WireImage“This job is going to change everything!” So says Liza (Lena Headey), an intimacy coordinator with a major film gig that quickly becomes disturbingly personal. Written and directed by Headey, this funny, shockingaudio dramais based on an idea by “ethical pornographer” Erika Lust and is executive produced by Sharon Horgan. Hannah Waddingham, Alison Steadman and Maisie Williams also star.Hannah J DaviesBBC Sounds, episodes weeklyPretty Scary With Clair Buckley“Some people relax with a bubble bath or a meditation app; I relax by reading about things lurking in the woods …” Clair Buckley, best known forpodcastingwith her actor husband James, branches out with a series about unexplained mysteries, UFOs, true crime and more. While the premise isn’t that original, she is an excellent, eerie compere.HJDWidely available, episodes weeklyskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toWhat's OnGet the best TV reviews, news and features in your inbox every MondayPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDoing It AnywaySelf-belief is a passion project for the host of The Rest Is Politics US, Katty Kay, who has penned books on the subject. Each weekshe tacklesissues around confidence through an expert interview, starting with Mika Brzezinski, founder of the Know Your Value movement – a community helping women succeed at work.ADWidely available, episodes weeklyExplore more on these topicsTelevision & radioBest podcasts of the weekPodcastsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: A Sudden Flicker of Light by David Thomson review – a brilliantly eccentric history of the movies | Film books | The Guardian
description: From the golden age of Hollywood to harder times today, this is a rapturous account of more than a century of cinema
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: From the golden age of Hollywood to harder times today, this is a rapturous account of more than a century of cinema
Content: Tom Ewell and Marilyn Monroe in The Seven Year Itch (1955).Photograph: Cinematic/AlamyView image in fullscreenTom Ewell and Marilyn Monroe in The Seven Year Itch (1955).Photograph: Cinematic/AlamyBook of the dayFilm booksReviewA Sudden Flicker of Light by David Thomson review – a brilliantly eccentric history of the moviesFrom the golden age of Hollywood to harder times today, this is a rapturous account of more than a century of cinemaPeter BradshawMon 20 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFilm critic, historian and author of more than 40 books, David Thomson is back with another three-hour free jazz solo on the subject of the movies, though it’s a performance delivered with the magnificent elan of a 1940s big band leader. He’s as brilliant, eccentric, exasperating and absorbing as ever.By “the movies”, incidentally, he means Hollywood, with a side order of established European and east Asian arthouse: he takes us from the cine-prehistory of photographerEadweard Muybridgeto Fritz Lang’s Metropolis, DW Griffith’s Birth of a Nation with its sinister Ku Klux Klan hero-worship, Chaplin, Stroheim, Hitchcock, Capra, Renoir, Bogart, Marilyn Monroe in The Seven-Year Itch, Monroe Stahr in The Last Tycoon, Scorsese’s GoodFellas, David Lynch’s Mulholland Drive (“Is it drive or dream?”), Kirk Douglas and his alleged rape of Natalie Wood, Barbara Loden’s Wanda, Sean Baker’s Anora, Tracey Emin’s Why I Never Became a Dancer and much more. He subtitles his book a “revisionist” history.Revisionist? Well, it certainly takes us to wintry and sombre ponderings on the movies’ (perennial) tendency to coarsen and to cheapen, to enforce cultural mob-rule. He cites King Vidor’s 1928 film The Crowd, starring James Murray, a former extra plucked from the ranks and groomed for glory but whose awful destiny was to descend into alcoholism and homelessness – back into the crowd. He emphasises movies’ amazing populist-democratic power and reach, their power embodied by the fact that, unlike in the theatre or concert hall, a film’s action could not be stopped or affected by the crowd in front of it. Like a theologian dragged by doubts to the foot of the cross, Thomson wonders if the movies will always lack literature’s way of allowing us to enlarge our imaginations, as opposed to forcing a submission to the screen.He descants on the triumph of Stephen Graham’s Netflix drama Adolescence and uses it to suggest that the big-screen theatrical experience may indeed be on the way out, because “the image on our home screens is brighter and more transporting. If you are inclined to dispute that, look very closely.” Well, I have looked closely and I do dispute it. All the same, Thomson’s contrarian, non-handwringing opinion on the subject is refreshing. Only he could have got away with that gleeful heresy, designed to wake us up to the commercial forces that brought big screens into being in the first place.So it is revisionist. In a way. But the book’s body language is non-revisionist: it’s a straightforwardly rapturous, rhapsodic celebration of the extraordinary adventure of cinema in the 20th century and beyond with an old-fashioned feel. The author is a great believer in the importance of profits and grosses, in telling us how much money the key films made, as if that proves anything, though he’s also a great respecter of the importance of Sight and Sound magazine’s decennialGreatest Film of All Timepoll.The point is that Thomson, with that marvellous ambition that is all his own, always wants to recount the whole story, to start again with that task, to encompass the giant shape in its awe-inspiring entirety, the “whole equation”, to borrow F Scott Fitzgerald’s phrase which he brilliantly used as the title for a previous book. That is his revisionism: always to take on the whole thing. He rockets through his story while firing off into the slipstream provocative generalisations and opinions, gags and barbs.He contrasts the darker moods of the American New Wave with the contemporary sunlit positivity of Steven Spielberg, who, he says, is “the most decisive figure in the history of film, a guy who came upon an art experimenting with difficulty or doubt and talked it in off the ledge”. He says that the film industry’s pious and glib dedication to the shopworn concept of “love” has brought it to an evolutionary crisis: “The species is at a point where LOVE can start to feel as hollow as HOLLYWOOD above Los Angeles.”But weirdly, Thomson has a priestly tendency to intone the names of the accepted greats and to expect a kind of cinephile genuflexion in each case. Clive James did something like it in his TV documentary series Fame in the 20th Century: he would give an introductory upward-lift of acclaim to the first mention of each new famous name, as if bringing them on to a chatshow, and there is something of that in Thomson’s prose which can stimulate, amuse and bewilder all at once.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toBookmarksDiscover new books and learn more about your favourite authors with our expert reviews, interviews and news stories. Literary delights delivered direct to youPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotiondouble quotation markAre the movies beginning to flicker in another sense, flicker out into darkness?He takes his title from a moment in Henry James’s 1881 novel The Portrait of a Lady, which he regards a classic proto-cinematic revelation: heroine Isabel Archer has married Gilbert Osmond and one day comes home to find their mutual friend Madame Merle and Osmond in an attitude of disconcerting intimacy and ease, he seated and she standing: “… the thing made an image, lasting only a moment, like a sudden flicker of light”. Thomson enthuses about that moment, whose vividness hints that James has mastered the arts of cinematographer, director and editor, and capriciously adds: “I want to bring this scene to life, with maybe Meryl Streep as Madame Merle, and who would you like as Osmond?” (It is a characteristically wilful and faintly baffling aside: Thomson appears here to disregard the excellent film version with John Malkovich as Osmond and Barbara Hershey as Madame Merle, directed by Jane Campion and starring Nicole Kidman, whose superfan biography Thomson has written. To be fair, Campion’s film doesn’t actually include that flickering moment.)So how about now? Are the movies beginning to flicker in another sense, flicker out into darkness? Well, the gloomy-elegaic insistence on the end of cinema is a tiresome kind of cinephile affectation that’s been around for decades, usually from writers who are in fact mourning the passing of their own movie-filled youth and middle age. Thomson thankfully does not indulge it. But there are flickers of anxiety whose implications the author doesn’t explore at any length.In the midst of discussing Sergei Eisenstein, whose longing to make films was shared by so many, Thomson remarks: “That longing lasted until … well, the end of the century? But I think it’s in retreat now. Do we feel we’ve seen it all?” That throwaway lands in our lap. In retreat? Seen it all? If he is serious, shouldn’t the author stop what he’s doing and pursue the point more rigorously?Well, it could be that this book is not a conventional critical work at all, nothing so workaday; it is rather quasi-fictional, like an action painting made from melted celluloid in which Thomson is the creator-hero, splashing around on the canvas in his multicoloured liquid element. Or simply an epic novel: he alludes repeatedly to EL Doctorow’s 1975 book Ragtime, about immigration and the American dream in the first years of the 20th century, that mixes real figures with imaginary ones. Here, Thomson is both the narrator and underdog, meeting with legends in the crowded streets of Los Angeles or New York, feeling their pain, sensing their euphoria, foretelling their downfall or triumph. I very much hope that this is not his last book. I can’t imagine film criticism or films without him.A Sudden Flicker of Light: A Revisionist History of the Movies by David Thomson is published by Allen Lane (£25). To support the Guardian order your copy atguardianbookshop.com. Delivery charges may apply.Explore more on these topicsFilm booksBook of the dayreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Yellow Pine by Claire Vaye Watkins review – messy portrait of protest subculture | Fiction | The Guardian
description: Watkins should have been the ideal writer for this sympathetic take on new age spirituality and desert eco-activism – but the results are confusing
Author: Sandra Newman
Headline: Watkins should have been the ideal writer for this sympathetic take on new age spirituality and desert eco-activism – but the results are confusing
Content:
Keenly attentive … Claire Vaye Watkins.Photograph: alyson aliano
917 771 4858
www.alysonaliano.com/Alyson AlianoView image in fullscreenKeenly attentive … Claire Vaye Watkins.Photograph: alyson aliano
917 771 4858
www.alysonaliano.com/Alyson AlianoFictionReviewYellow Pine by Claire Vaye Watkins review – messy portrait of protest subcultureWatkins should have been the ideal writer for this sympathetic take on new age spirituality and desert eco-activism – but the results are confusingSandra NewmanMon 20 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRose, the protagonist of Claire Vaye Watkins’s latest novel, has retreated from the world to live in Nothingness Flats, a cinder-block cabin in the Nevada desert. On indefinite leave from her job at an environmental organisation, Rose works on her Al-Anon recovery, goes hiking and joins protests against the Yellow Pine of the title: a solar array being built over a fragile desert ecosystem. She writes in her journal: “I am on this side of the portal. I have compassion in my heart for anyone back on the other side, sending emails, bickering, buying useless shit, getting run by the algorithm, living in the past.” As the story begins, she is reunited with her first love, Miles, who has “fallen apart beautifully: bopped in and out of law school, then spent six years in what’s left of the redwoods reading horny 400-year-old poems before bailing on his PhD”. Soon, Rose realises what she truly wants, this side of the portal, is to have his child.You don’t often see a sympathetic take on the subculture of 21st-century hippies, with their eclectic leftwing philosophies, new age spirituality and earnest striving to do good and be kind. It’s an important strain in modern life, and one typically dismissed with facile mockery and condescension. As a serious treatment of it, Yellow Pine has notable strengths. The love story between Rose and Miles is very real and specific to this world, and gives a backbone of plot to material that otherwise tends to sprawl. Watkins’s prose can be musical and keenly attentive, and she excels at conveying starry-eyed love. “[Miles] was brilliant, peaceful, grounded as a mountain, kind even when he was blue, an unabashed loner agog at the whole burning world.”However, the plot meanders and has odd discrepancies. Crucially, in the beginning, Miles agrees to have unprotected sex with Rose, knowing she will keep the baby if she gets pregnant. Later, confusingly, the main conflict between them is whether he’s ready for a child, with no mention of their previous conversation.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toBookmarksDiscover new books and learn more about your favourite authors with our expert reviews, interviews and news stories. Literary delights delivered direct to youPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotiondouble quotation markIt becomes maddeningly unclear how Watkins wants us to view her charactersIt also becomes maddeningly unclear how Watkins wants us to view her characters. The narration treats them affectionately – we’re clearly meant to be on their side – but their actions are often jarringly self-centred. Rose, for instance, engineers a fully paid leave from her job by threatening to support staff efforts to unionise, but she never considers really doing it; it’s just a means for her to be paid for doing nothing. She also narcs out all the local weed farms because she worries their fertiliser is leaching into her water. Many pages later, we learn that when these farms are raided by police, all their undocumented workers are deported. Rose never pauses to contemplate her role in their eventual fate. She has chosen to leave her young daughter, Juniper, in the care of her father in Pennsylvania, and Rose often laments how painful the separation is to them both. But she never considers moving closer to Juniper, and this history forms no part of her thinking about having another baby.It’s also a problem that there are so many scenes where the characters lecture each other. This is reasonable when we’re being educated about why industrial solar arrays are a legitimate target for protest, information we need to understand the plot. It’s not so excusable when characters are making speeches about prehistoric goddess worship, the gnostic gospels or the decline in the quality of commercially available apples. In one particularly baffling scene, Rose brings a stack of climate books with her to a bar and recites long passages from them to a nonplussed bartender. Open Yellow Pine at random and you’re liable to find someone saying: “since we’re all made of star stuff and the big wheel has been turning for eternity, it makes sense that some people feel like part of you … Like maybe your potassium and my potassium were once together in the bones of some fish in Lake Lahontan”, or, “Some scholars do surmise that marriage was patriarchal religion’s incentive to power via state-sanctioned violence against women in an alliance intended to suppress the resistant Goddess worshippers”.This often feels like hostile satire, and there are occasional judgmental speech tags such as “Rose brayed” and “Joskie squealed”. But this is apparently not the intention; in her prefatory letter to the reader, Watkins describes Yellow Pine not as satire, but as “a spiritually seeking eco-socialist novel about disillusionment and re-enchantment, and the daily practice of defending the sacred”.Watkins’s history as a political writer couldn’t be stronger: the viral essay On Pandering, the climate classicGold Fame Citrus. She should have been the ideal person to write a sympathetic novel about this world. But in Yellow Pine, what could have been an important addition to the genre of climate fiction is instead an often likable but ultimately confusing mess.Yellow Pine by Claire Vaye Watkins is published by Riverrun (£22). To support the Guardian, order your copy atguardianbookshop.comExplore more on these topicsFictionreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Apple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own family | Games | The Guardian
description: The developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edge
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edge
Content: Someone wants to kill grandma … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesView image in fullscreenSomeone wants to kill grandma … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesSummer games previewsGamesApple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own familyThe developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edgeNicole CarpenterMon 20 Jul 2026 12.35 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 12.36 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSomeone wants to kill grandma. In thisKnives Out-inspired mystery game, each of your family members is suspicious in their own way: your stressed mum, your “midlife crisis uncle” and especially that stranger in your bedroom. Yikes!While Apple Crumble is still a mystery, it is mechanically different from Happy Broccoli Games’ previous game,Duck Detective, in which the player solves crimes in a small office. Here you are largely snooping through your family home. “I love the idea of having a walking sim where you can really open up every drawer, every door, and see what’s behind there,” says founder and creative director Annika Maar. As you learn more about the different grievances and troubles of your family members, the mystery slowly unwinds.View image in fullscreen‘You can open up every drawer, every door, and see what’s behind there’ … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesAfter Duck Detective, the team wanted to make a “sad grandma” game – something that may come as a surprise after the humour of the studio’s previous games. Apple Crumble begins comedically, says Maar, but the tone shifts throughout the game; it gets more serious and a bit sad: “I felt very emotionally attached to the death of my grandma. There’s some baggage there and I thought it would be fun to explore that.”You’ll pick up the plot by digging through stuff. Lots of stuff. Apple Crumble’s storytelling extends into the different made-up fiction placed throughout the house. “There’s a film script that the midlife crisis uncle wrote which he’s convinced is going to get money from investors and become huge,” says Maar. “Just so many small details that I’m hoping will make the world feel very believable.”That depth of hidden detail also applies to the art style, which was inspired, in part, by concept art for Puss in Boots. “In the movie and the concept art, they use a lot of these paint splatters,” says Maar. “From a distance it’s not immediately apparent, but when you look, you’re like, ‘Wow, there are so many different colours in this.’ Your eyes kind of blend it together and it just makes it feel more natural. Less clinical. More exciting to look at. It ended up being a lot more work than I thought it would be, but I think it was worth it.”Maar is currently finishing up the script and will soon begin the process of recording voice actors. Happy Broccoli is booking the actors together so that they can really riff off each other. After all, with a grandma-killer in their midst, there are a lot of arguments to record.Apple Crumble is in development forPC. Release is planned for later in 2026Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsPCfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Trent Park House of Secrets review – the bugged stately home where German prisoners blabbed their secrets  | Museums | The Guardian
description: A new museum in this redeveloped country house relives its fascinating period as a palatial prison of war camp – with Jewish refugees listening in from the basement
Author: Eddy Frankel
Headline: A new museum in this redeveloped country house relives its fascinating period as a palatial prison of war camp – with Jewish refugees listening in from the basement
Content: Listening in … a mannequin in the basement of Trent Park House of Secrets.Photograph: James O DaviesView image in fullscreenListening in … a mannequin in the basement of Trent Park House of Secrets.Photograph: James O DaviesMuseumsReviewTrent Park House of Secrets review – the bugged stately home where German prisoners blabbed their secretsTrent Park, LondonA new museum in this redeveloped country house relives its fascinating period as a palatial prison of war camp – with Jewish refugees listening in from the basementEddy FrankelMon 20 Jul 2026 11.45 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHistory happened here. I mean, history happens everywhere. But serious, important, world-shaping history happened at Trent Park.Built on the grounds of Enfield Chase, an expanse of Hertfordshire countryside used as hunting grounds by royalty, this country house has lived many lives. It was a Georgian retreat, a Victorian mansion, a post-Edwardian sanctuary, and then, during the second world war, it became a place of secrets. Afterwards, it became a university campus, before falling into dereliction. Now, it has been revived, and its hidden stories brought into the light.The house was built after George III leased some land to his favourite doctor, Sir Richard Jebb, in the 1770s. It ended up in the hands of the Bevan banking family, who expanded and remodelled it, before the Sassoon family acquired it and did the same. Mega-socialite Philip Sassoon spent the equivalent of millions turning it into his dream country house in the 1920s – a suburban party palace frequented by the likes of Churchill and Wallis Simpson.View image in fullscreenRefurbished … Trent Park House.Photograph: James O Davies. Courtesy of Trent Park Museum TrustThe house has been refurbished now, and is largely as Sassoon would’ve left it: pastel walls, floral curtains, countless deep, plush sofas for guests to lounge in. Flanking a huge bookcase in the library are portraits of Sassoon and his sister Sibyl by John Singer Sargent. They’re gorgeous paintings. Philip is intense, quiet, aloof. Sibyl is dark, elegant, sombre.But it’s what is between the portraits that matters. Give that bookcase a shove and – hey presto! – a secret entrance down into the basement.Of all of Trent Park’s transformations, this is its greatest: from aristo bolthole to audacious experiment in spycraft. After Sassoon died in 1939, the house was nabbed by the government and used as a prisoner of war camp for high-ranking German generals. Here, in these palatial surroundings, the prisoners were given time and space to pass the war in comfort, to kick back and let loose. But every inch of the house was bugged, even the rose bushes in the garden, every conversation recorded down in the basement, and listened to largely by German-speaking Jewish refugees. The prisoners felt at ease, so they spilled their secrets, and every word was recorded and transcribed.View image in fullscreenRestored … looking from the saloon to the library and John Singer Sargent’s portrait of Sir Philip Sassoon.Photograph: James O DaviesThe listeners got some pretty big intel, too. Information was fed to the Enigma code-breaking team over in Bletchley. General Ludwig Crüwell let slip to a fellow high-ranking officer about German ballistics, and the allies got the jump on the V2 rocket. Someone else blabbed about how German planes communicated with a system of transmitted beams, and the British kiboshed that too.The basement has been faithfully recreated here: a mannequin sits at his station, headphones on, pen poised. The captain’s bed is neatly made, the housekeeper’s cellar is stocked with tea, herbs, wine and German books. It smells of damp, the wool blankets are rough and itchy, the sheets starched stiff. This was a place of resistance, of subterfuge, a place where the Germans could be lulled into a false sense of security and taken for all they had.It wasn’t all good. Listeners heard conversations about German atrocities, but authorities did nothing with the information. They didn’t even submit the intelligence to the Nuremberg trials in the 1950s, worried their secret tech would be exposed.View image in fullscreenLulled into a false sense of security … prisoners of war at Trent Park.Photograph: Houghton HallGood or bad, it’s all lovingly recreated here, filled with neatly presented information and era-appropriate trinkets and decoration, though it’s a shame there aren’t more original artworks dotted throughout the house instead of copies.The museum largely concentrates on the house’s role in the war, mainly because it’s interesting, and sets it apart from every other gaudy National Trust-style mansion in this country (though it is still definitely one of those). But it’s the clashing layers of history built into the house’s very foundations – caked into its paint and wallpaper – that make it feel special. In its transformation from Georgian to Victorian to mock-Georgian to war-time austerity and postwar dereliction, the house becomes a portrait of England’s changing fortunes and tastes, the way wealth has ebbed and flowed, the impact of immigration in shaping the country, and the way war can tear everything apart. That’s what is amazing about places like this, they’re not just grand houses, or follies of the rich brought back to life, they’re containers of history, and Trent Park sure saw its fair share of that.Trent Park House of Secrets opens in London on 21 JulyExplore more on these topicsMuseumsLondonSecond world warreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Ariadne auf Naxos review – Laurent Pelly’s staging has plenty of wit but lacks weight | Opera | The Guardian
description: The director hits perfectly all the comic marks of Strauss’s opera about an opera, but there’s few insights in a work that should be moving as well as frothy and fun.
Author: Erica Jeal
Headline: The director hits perfectly all the comic marks of Strauss’s opera about an opera, but there’s few insights in a work that should be moving as well as frothy and fun.
Content: Dazzling brightness … Alina Wunderlin as Zerbinetta (centre) in Ariadne auf Naxos at Glyndebourne festival.Photograph: Tristram Kenton/The GuardianView image in fullscreenDazzling brightness … Alina Wunderlin as Zerbinetta (centre) in Ariadne auf Naxos at Glyndebourne festival.Photograph: Tristram Kenton/The GuardianOperaReviewAriadne auf Naxos review – Laurent Pelly’s staging has plenty of wit but lacks weightGlyndebourne, East SussexThe director hits perfectly all the comic marks of Strauss’s opera about an opera, but there’s few insights in a work that should be moving as well as frothy and fun.Erica JealMon 20 Jul 2026 10.47 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGlyndebourne’s new productions this summer have followed a theme: firstTosca, about a fictional opera singer; thenL’Orfeo, about the mythical musician. Now comes Richard Strauss’s Ariadne auf Naxos – an opera about an opera. It starts with the shenanigans backstage as two troupes – one high art, one low – prepare after-dinner entertainment for their rich patron, then the performance that ensues when they are forced to combine their acts. The real subject is, of course, love – and that comes across only partially inLaurent Pelly’s production.The first part finds Pelly at his witty best. We’re in the house of the richest man in Vienna, whose taste is immediately in question; Massimo Troncanetti’s set has a huge doorway of gilded, inlaid wood clashing with a very 20th-century statement chandelier. William Relton’s fearsome Major-Domo pops up everywhere, cowing even Michael Kraus’s classily sung Music Master; Ru Charlesworth’s chainsmoking Dancing Master slinks around dressed as a sequined, velvety advert for Silk Cut. Samantha Hankey, her mezzo-soprano mellow but gleaming, plays the Composer as an irascible would-be genius; Zerbinetta, the comic leading lady, teeters in stockings and suspenders. Buoyantly conducted by Robin Ticciati, the players of the London Philharmonic, reduced in number as per Strauss’s stipulations, revel in the characters’ grand hopes and small tragedies, and Pelly plays to the stereotypes brilliantly, hitting the comic marks without sending things up.View image in fullscreenHopes and tragedies … David Butt Philip as the Tenor, Rachel Willis-Sørensen as the Prima Donna and Alina Wunderlin as Zerbinetta in Ariadne auf Naxos.Photograph: Tristram Kenton/The GuardianBut this is an opera of two parts. Having been told, several times, that these dinner guests are going to find the combination of rich food and serious opera conducive to sleep, it’s disappointing to find, post-picnic, that Pelly’s own handling of the second half is less sparky and less insightful than the first. Ariadne and the rest have to clamber over a huge mound of earth that feels like an operatic in-joke, and in the twilight it’s hard to see what effect, if any, each character’s words are having on anyone else.Alina Wunderlinis her own dazzling spotlight of brightness in Zerbinetta’s monologue, throwing out high notes and little waves to the audience with equal ease, but her potential subplot with Mikhail Timoshenko’s Harlequin doesn’t amount to much. He and his scene-stealing comedian companions remain in puppet-like character throughout, and we don’t see behind the facepaint. As the Prima Donna who sings Ariadne, Rachel Willis-Sørensen has the soaring voice but barely hints at the vulnerable woman beneath, either in her long-phrased aria or in duet with David Butt Philip’s powerfully sung Bacchus.We at least get to see the Composer vindicated, mirroring Ariadne’s movements in the gloaming behind her – and well may he revel in “his” music, as lushly played as it is here. But have any of the characters been changed by their experience? It seems not, and that leaves this Ariadne enjoyable but ultimately unmoving.Until 29 August.Explore more on these topicsOperaClassical musicLondon Philharmonic OrchestraGlyndebourneRichard StraussreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Spanish National Orchestra/Afkham review – Proms debut sees orchestra’s celebrations kick off early | Proms 2026 | The Guardian
description: Outgoing chief conductor David Afkham brought Spanish repertoire, castanets and a certain team’s football scarf in a programme heavy with national treasures
Author: Alexandra Coghlan
Headline: Outgoing chief conductor David Afkham brought Spanish repertoire, castanets and a certain team’s football scarf in a programme heavy with national treasures
Content: Viva España! … David Afkham conducts the Spanish National Orchestra at the BBC Proms.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouView image in fullscreenViva España! … David Afkham conducts the Spanish National Orchestra at the BBC Proms.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouProms 2026ReviewSpanish National Orchestra/Afkham review – Proms debut sees orchestra’s celebrations kick off earlyRoyal Albert Hall, LondonOutgoing chief conductor David Afkham brought Spanish repertoire, castanets and a certain team’s football scarf in a programme heavy with national treasuresAlexandra CoghlanMon 20 Jul 2026 07.14 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.34 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhat a night for Spain. No, not because ofsporting happenings in New Jersey, but because – nearly 90 years since their founding – their National Orchestra finally made their Proms debut under chief conductorDavid Afkhamto a packed Royal Albert Hall. It was a beginning, but also a celebratory end: Afkham hands over the baton to Kent Nagano next season. They came prepared, suitcases stuffed with Spanish repertoire and castanets – oh, and a certain team’s football scarf, as modelled by Afkham during the encore.Afkham’s more than a decade with the orchestra hit its heights with Spanish repertoire, building on a legacy that earned the ensemble a Latin Grammy for best flamenco album under Josep Pons in 2010. Afkham didn’t stray far from the formula here in a programme heavy with national treasures, though noticeably lacking in Spanish contemporary music.View image in fullscreenGuitarist Rafael Aguirre, with Afkham conducting.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouWe were promised the “rhythms of Spain” and they got straight to work in the wriggling seguidilla of Ravel’s Alborada del gracioso, solo bassoon playing the mournful jester. Like De Falla’s Fantasia baetica which followed (heard for the first time at the Proms in Franciso Coll’s colourful 2022 orchestration), it is originally a solo piano piece; flashiness is built in. But in these comfortable accounts, mid-paced and spacious, virtuosity became something softer and more relaxed.GuitaristRafael Aguirre(winner of an astonishing 13 first prizes at international competitions) rode this wave of sun-warmed reverie in a lean-back account of Rodrigo’s Concierto de Aranjuez. Good-natured and folk-direct in the framing outer movements, he found another colour for the wistful slow movement, his cadenza beckoning us in rather than reaching out. Sensitive to his soloist, Afkham kept the orchestra on a tight rein. The effect was oddly muted, subdued even in key moments.Two suites whipped us efficiently through the highlights of De Falla’s ballet The Three-Cornered Hat, leaving it to Ravel’s Boléro to bring us home with the most famous rhythm in the business. The work’s slow-build arc from flickering pianissimo pulse to thumping heartbeat is a tough ask in this difficult acoustic, and tension was lost as we waited for gestures to become sounds. But perhaps the real tension was off-stage last night, orchestral hearts in the US even as their heads and fingers took us into extra time with an exuberant encore from Gerónimo Giménez’s zarzuela La boda de Luis Alonso.Available on BBC Soundsuntil 12 October. The Promscontinue until 12 September.Explore more on these topicsProms 2026Classical musicBBCPromsRoyal Albert HallreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Avengers: Doomsday trailer reveals star-packed Marvel adventure | Avengers: Doomsday | The Guardian
description: The first full look at the big-budget December tentpole features returning heroes such as Chris Hemsworth, Patrick Stewart, Florence Pugh and Chris Evans
Author: Benjamin Lee
Headline: The first full look at the big-budget December tentpole features returning heroes such as Chris Hemsworth, Patrick Stewart, Florence Pugh and Chris Evans
Content: Chris Hemsworth in Avengers: Doomsday.Photograph: YouTubeView image in fullscreenChris Hemsworth in Avengers: Doomsday.Photograph: YouTubeAvengers: DoomsdayAvengers: Doomsday trailer reveals star-packed Marvel adventureThe first full look at the big-budget December tentpole features returning heroes such as Chris Hemsworth, Patrick Stewart, Florence Pugh and Chris EvansBenjamin LeeMon 20 Jul 2026 10.17 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 10.24 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe first full trailer forAvengers: Doomsdayhas been released online offering a look at Marvel’s big-budget big hope.“Something’s coming, something we may not be able to deter,” is the first line heard in the new preview which proceeds to bring back multiple characters from variousMarvelmovies and universes.How Marvel deals with Doctor Doom is make or break for the MCU. No one wants a watered-down Tony StarkRead moreReturning Avengers featured in the trailer include Chris Hemsworth as Thor, Paul Rudd as Ant-Man and Chris Evans as Captain America while Fantastic Four’s Pedro Pascal, X-Men’s Patrick Stewart, Black Panther’s Letitia Wright and Thunderbolts’ Florence Pugh also appear.There’s also a glimpse of Ian McKellen, returning as Magneto, whorecentlyspoke about the film at the Piazza festival in Rome. The actor told the audience that “they got me at one point to destroy New Jersey”. He added that directing duo the Russo brothers “told me to look more furious: make it look as if you hate what you’re destroying. So I stood there and I shouted: ‘Mar-a-Lago!’”The trailer teases an “unthinkable decision” as well as the arrival of Robert Downey Jr’s Doctor Doom. The actor, who previously played Iron Man, is returning to play the famed villain in a move that isreportedlyearning the actor an estimated $100m for both Doomsday and its follow-up Secret Wars.Returning co-director Joe Russo hassaidthey are “back to phase zero” of the MCU with the new film. “This is starting over from scratch,” he said. “We want to make sure everybody feels like this isn’t leaning on anything from the past.”The film arrives at a difficult time for Marvel, once box office champions but now struggling to return to former glories. The last few years have seen adventures such as Thunderbolts, Fantastic Four: First Steps and Captain America: Brave New World achieving mediocre results.Toronto film festival: Helen Mirren, Chris Rock and Seth Rogen lead lineupRead moreBut in 2019, Avengers: Endgame became one of the highest-grossing films of all time with a box office total of almost $2.8bn.The Guardian’s Ben Childrecentlywrote: “the success or failure of this next superhero extravaganza might well decide the future of the entire franchise.”Avengers: Doomsday is releasing on 18 December, the same date as Dune: Part Three leading box office pundits to predict a Barbenheimer-style showdown.Explore more on these topicsAvengers: DoomsdayMarvelSuperhero moviesRobert Downey JrnewsShareReuse this content

title: Acting the fool: why have Hollywood press tours become so gimmicky? | Movies | The Guardian
description: From playing would you rather to ranking snacks, A-listers navigating the promo trail are now forced on a set schedule of silliness
Author: Jesse Hassenger
Headline: From playing would you rather to ranking snacks, A-listers navigating the promo trail are now forced on a set schedule of silliness
Content: The Odyssey cast on The Tonight Show.Photograph: YouTubeView image in fullscreenThe Odyssey cast on The Tonight Show.Photograph: YouTubeMoviesActing the fool: why have Hollywood press tours become so gimmicky?From playing would you rather to ranking snacks, A-listers navigating the promo trail are now forced on a set schedule of sillinessJesse HassengerMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThis summer, we’ve been blessed with new movies from the legendary director Steven Spielberg and one of his biggest and most beloved successors, Christopher Nolan. In both cases, the movies have been heavily sold on the directors’ names, and, befitting two of the most famous film-makers to ever do it, Spielberg and Nolan have been making the press rounds. This has meant a wealth of interviews and insights – a bonus feature unfolding alongside the selling of the actual movies that will delight movie nerds the world over.Celebrity on celebrity: are we losing the art of the big star interview?Read moreSo at first, when I saw a60-second videoof Christopher Nolan answering a barrage of yes-or-no questions come across my social-media feed, of course I stopped and watched as a famously buttoned-up (if obviously still passionate) film-maker attempted some spontaneity with no danger of follow-up questions (or obligation to follow-up answers). From this, there is admittedly plenty of information to be gleaned: he has no designs on retiring, Tarantino-style. He will not make another superhero movie or a TV show. He’s fine with people watching movies on their phones or laptops. He will neither confirm nor deny that Interstellar is his favorite of his films. He will also neither confirm nor deny that he would like to make a Star Wars movie. (Perhaps the most intriguing, given that you’d rather expect that to be a firm “no”.)But after the rush of information subsided came a feeling of profound emptiness. One of the world’s most beloved film-makers is talking to some kid on some channel called Back2FilmSchool? And come to think of it, hasn’t he answered all of these questions before, in greater detail? He seems like a perfectly good sport in the video, but then, plenty of brave hostages probably come across with similar composure, to say nothing of those who can keep cool in the face of a nuisance. A great interview might have some friction, but it will probably not have you wondering if the subject was genuinely annoyed for the entire thing, or simply half-smiling politely. Why do so many press-tour stops these days suddenly feel like the halfway point between TikTok and a cult’s humiliation ritual?Now, not every Nolan or Spielberg interview has such punishing time limits or gimmicky hooks. In fact, if you don’t want to watch Nolan interviewed in 60 seconds, he had asegmenton 60 Minutes (technically only 13 minutes, and lamentably the version of the show run by the eminently unqualified Bari Weiss, but, you know, still a marquee attraction). Throughout the history of film promotion, stars and film-makers have participated in a range of interviews, for a range of outlets, with a range of detail or seriousness.But this year in particular, it’s not even the TikTok-iness of many interviews that feels so uniform – as mentioned, you can find the extra-long counterparts to balance out the minute-long nothing-bites. What’s become even more prevalent is what an older person might call the Fallon-ification of journalism, where a high-profile cast sits not for an interview but a series of games. If you look at the website LADBible, for example, you might assume that it’s probably not scoring interviews with Matt Damon or Anne Hathaway. (As someone who has occasionally arranged interviews for less famous people than Damon or Hathaway, for websites not even called LADBible, I can assure you this assumption would be entirely reasonable.) And it’s not – not really. It is, however, hosting a YouTube-distributed round ofWould You Ratherfeaturing some of the biggest stars in the world, under the guise of promoting their excellent film. Pursue their channel for stars doing impromptu ratings and talking about snacks. Anne Hathaway in particular will play a countless number of these games in a year when she’s appearing in five films; she’s already logged time with the LADs forThe Devil Wears Prada 2earlier this summer.It makes sense that people enjoy this stuff in its native formats: TikTok,YouTube, The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon, and so on. Fallon may be frequently insufferable, but his refresh of Jay Leno’s Tonight Show did dispense with a lot of canned interview anecdotes and accompanying shtick, all as tired as Celebrity Twister Trivia or whatever might seem now. What, is Fallon suddenly going to become an incisive and unpredictable interviewer at his age? That would be great, but it’s not happening.But as Fallon-ification and broader social-video trends feed into each other, they threaten to create less enjoyable, more inexplicable hybrid franchises, free-floating from the comforting bounds of an actual TV show. Somehow, The Tonight Show is able to do this, too, making content not actually involving Fallon himself (although maybe that makes sense; why create even a moment of suggestion that Jimmy Fallon might turn up in your adaptation of classic literature?). One recurring bit is a rhythmic, quasi-hooky, all-starplot recap, where celebs do a Fallon-approved cutesy semi-rap based on short phrases and fast cuts, assembled to form some rudimentary yet forced rhymes about the project at hand. This erases even the novelty of seeing Matt Damon, Anne Hathaway, Zendaya, Charlize Theron, Robert Pattinson, Tom Holland and Lupita Nyong’o interact, however superficially, or even watching them at any length on their own. It’s a minute-long segment by design, so you get microdoses of famous faces, reciting fragments. Has it really convinced a single person to see The Odyssey? Publicists will doubtless claim that it’s the awareness of the movie, not the specific desire to see it, that these videos aid. Attentive viewers, meanwhile, might study the screen to figure out who looks genuinely fine with it and who looks like they’re considering poisoning themselves or their agents.And look, forcible irritation of celebrities and diminishment of artists will probably always have a place in the promotional cycle. But the sheer volume of it, at a time when name publications staffed with people who genuinely enjoy and are good at conducting interviews are bought, sold and gutted on the regular, can feel particularly smug – a bonus victory pivot following yet another pivot to video putting writers out of work in favor of off-screen prompts.Of course, viewers can’t be expected to think about journalists when they’re enjoying celebrities goofing around. I am a journalist, and I don’t think about journalists when I watch literallyany videofeaturingany combinationof Saturday Night Live cast members doing anything together. What viewers might think about, however, is why, exactly, they’re watching Jon Bernthal and Charlize Theron grimace through a Fallon-sponsored assemblage of stupid rhymes. We’ve all had moments of why-did-I-click-that remorse involving online videos; I would humbly suggest that genuine interviews, while not always as revealing as you would hope, rarely produce those sinking feelings. Studios, in the meantime, are moving toward making some of the most famous faces and revered creative minds in the entertainment world the unlikely mascots for endless-scroll regret.Explore more on these topicsFilmThe OdysseyFilm industryYouTubeInternetfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: A Ruhr treat: can a giant pigeon and a pink basketball revive Germany’s depressed industrial heartland?  | Architecture | The Guardian
description: The Manifesta biennale is taking over a dozen tiny churches in German towns ravaged by division and decline – and filling them with ball games, bouncy castles and a radical community spirit
Author: Owen Hatherley
Headline: The Manifesta biennale is taking over a dozen tiny churches in German towns ravaged by division and decline – and filling them with ball games, bouncy castles and a radical community spirit
Content: Printed pigeon … Friend by artist Gülbin Ünlü at Thomaskirche.Photograph: Ivan ErofeevView image in fullscreenPrinted pigeon … Friend by artist Gülbin Ünlü at Thomaskirche.Photograph: Ivan ErofeevArchitectureA Ruhr treat: can a giant pigeon and a pink basketball revive Germany’s depressed industrial heartland?The Manifesta biennale is taking over a dozen tiny churches in German towns ravaged by division and decline – and filling them with ball games, bouncy castles and a radical community spiritOwen HatherleyMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.25 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfter Germany’s Ruhr conurbation – the third-largest urban area in western Europe – was pummelled during the second world war, planners came up with an ingenious idea. About 80% of the sprawl – which incorporates big cities such as Essen and Duisburg, and dozens of smaller towns like Bochum and Gelsenkirchen – had been destroyed. And so, for the reconstruction, a newly formed Diocese of the Ruhr built literally hundreds of closely packedPantoffelkirchenor “slipper churches”, so named because they were close enough to housing developments for churchgoers to walk to mass in their slippers.Today,Pantoffelkirchenare declining sharply. Estimates of the amount of disused or derelict churches are as high as 300, and 100 have been sold off in the last year alone. Until October, 12 of them are home to Manifesta, the travelling art biennale. They stand as centrepieces of communities that have been ravaged this time not by war, but by deindustrialisation, mass unemployment and environmental decline.Manifesta 16 Ruhr is not the Venice Biennale. Think more a Capital of Culture event held in the West Midlands – which the Ruhr closely resembles, as the stricken driver of national growth. Instead of exhibits in, say, Birmingham and Coventry, you’d be expected to travel to Bilston, Smethwick, Stourbridge. This is much easier to do in the Ruhr, where trams are ubiquitous and even tiny Bochum has a U-Bahn – though you’ll also find these networks have been neglected since they were built in the 1970s. At first, the cities and towns appear greener, sleeker and better connected than their British cousins, but this is a little deceptive. The A40 autobahn slices much of it into poorer and more affluent enclaves, seldom walkable one to the other.View image in fullscreenSurreal ...a basketball court where a miss triggers a blast of the church organ.Photograph: Rainer SchlautmannMuch of the Ruhr is today poorer than east Germany, after decades of deindustrialisation and the dismantlement of the welfare state. Gelsenkirchen has the highest vote for the far-right AfD in western Germany, and the highest rate of unemployment. So Manifesta goes into some very charged territory by taking over and trying to re-imagine the social infrastructure of what can seem a dying place, with divided communities and a dysfunctional economy. It avoids big, sexy industrial spaces, focusing instead on tiny derelict churches in suburbs and housing estates. “I’ll be honest: I don’t care about churches, I care about neighbourhoods,” says Catalan architect and Manifesta curator Josep Bohigas. “[The churches] were built for people who are no longer there.” The festival tries to use these apparently useless, if often beautiful, structures to bridge this gap between the past and the future.double quotation markThe churches were built for people who are no longer thereIt doesn’t all work. Some of the bigger empty churches contain some big empty art – an inflatable blue bubble containing a beach, comes to mind, mounted in the 19th century neo-gothic St Josef, Gelsenkirchen. Essen’s gothic St Gertrud, deconsecrated and now used by the local School of Fine Arts, is a distant cousin of Coventry Cathedral; the surviving ruins were patched up and joined on to a modern church in the 50s. Now, their polychrome expressionist-Byzantine murals face upturned pews, turned into abstract sculptures by Nasan Tur, an unsubtle gesture.The flagship is the Kulturkirche Liebfrauen, Duisburg, of 1958, which was turned into an arts centre in 2011: a flamboyant modernist building with the pulsating drama of Liverpool’s Catholic cathedral. Some installations here struggle to compete with the gorgeous murals and reliefs, made originally for the Vatican Pavilion at Expo 58, between luminescent walls of Plexiglas.
