But a fascinating two-screen film by Elizabeth Price works as an introduction to the biennale’s ideas, juxtaposing memories of second world war bombing with a history of those modernist Catholic churches built in postwar Britain for Irish immigrants.View image in fullscreenWeimar vision … Manifesta 16 artwork inside Bochum’s Christ-König.Photograph: Ivan ErofeevMostly, these churches were architecturally radical. One, Christ-König in Bochum, is a brick roar of Fritz Lang melodrama built in the last year of the Weimar Republic – but 10 of the churches are postwar. Hedwig Fijen, director of Manifesta Ruhr, describes them as places where architects and congregations tried “to imagine a different kind of society”, after “years of war, genocide and destruction”. But they did so within the confines of the old world. The new churches were keenly supported by the local industrial families, Thyssen and Krupp, beneficiaries of nazism who preferred their workers devout. The immigration of “guest workers” was encouraged; many of the miners and factory workers who rebuilt the conurbation and powered the “economic miracle” were from Catholic southernEurope, alongside Greece and, especially, Turkey.Their story is told here in the slipper churches, sparkling architectural miniatures that have clearly elicited the most thought on the part of the curators and artists. The first I saw, St Marien, Essen, was a harsh contrast with the tidy city-centre churches. Designed in 1963, with explosively exciting stained glass by Manfred Ott, it’s tough, red brick and semi-ruined, located in a working-class multicultural area opposite a retail park where the factories once stood. The church has been disused for many years and the artworks play with a bomb-site theme, all fragments and detritus. You realise the sheer range of architectural expression in the slipper churches when you go from there to Essen’s Protestant Markuskirche, of 1961, with its gorgeous wall of gauzy abstract stained glass. It is also tightly integrated with its community, next to a kindergarten and a care home. The major “artwork” here is a set of wooden seats and play areas by the Portuguese Pele Collective; when I visit, it feels more like a school fete than a ponderous art exhibition.View image in fullscreenPostwar planning … Kulturkirche Liebfrauen in Duisburg was turned into an arts centre in 2011.Photograph: Ivan ErofeevThe most powerful, convincing projects are less cute. Both are curated by Gürsoy Doğtaş, an art historian whose parents were Turkish “guest workers”. His team renamed two churches in Gelsenkirchen: St Bonifatius, as the Ferdane Satır Tea Garden, after one of seven Turkish guest workers murdered by fascists in an arson attack in Duisburg in 1984; and Thomaskirche, after Hava Güleç, a local factory worker considered a pillar of the community. St Bonifatius (1963) is in the hard, red brick expressionist tradition, and stands in a densely urban area along a main road. It has become effectively an art museum of the guest worker experience.At Thomaskirche, the crystalline space, overlooked by Friend, a massive printed pigeon by artist Gülbin Ünlü, includes Judith Raum’s film about the German-engineered Baghdad Railway, which works as prehistory: German industrialists make approving comments about docile Ottoman workers who don’t strike and don’t ask for pay rises; below, photographs document Turkish guest workers’ struggles, and strikes, in Germany 70 years later. Thomaskirche is mostly devoted to textiles, feminised factory labour in an area of old mills – there are public weaving workshops in the church hall. Impressively, the Gelsenkirchen churches are full of artbyworkers, rather than work about them. Many began as guest workers – Abuzer Güler, whose expressionist Anatolian scenes are hung on industrial frames in Thomaskirche, or Hanefi Yeter, whose When the Earth Burns, a Diego Rivera-like apocalyptic Ruhr allegory of 1984, hangs in St Bonifatius.View image in fullscreen‘Gorgeous gauzy abstract stained glass’ … more Manifesta artwork inside Markuskirche in Essen.Photograph: Silviu GuimanBut the Ruhr’s first boom, in the late 19th and early 20th century was significantly powered by Polish labour. In Bochum’s disused Gethsemane church – one of several “emergency churches” designed by the Weimar modernist Otto Bartning, built literally from rubble by the local community in 1947 – the Polish curator Krzysztof Kosciuczuk brings together more ironic, game-playing work on migration. Standing outside the church, in a sleepy area of modest houses and allotments, a bouncy castle in the shape of a church bell: designed by Belarusian artist Marina Naprushkina, it initially looks like a silly, friendly gesture, until you read the legend, in big letters: “We’ll Never Go Back!”All of this is, obviously, temporary. One work in a 1960s slipper church, Bochum’s tiny, angular St Ludgerus, has been turned into a basketball court. This isn’t new – in Gelsenkirchen, St Josef had one for a time – but what this one, designed by Catalan artists Cabosanroque does, is encourage ineptitude. If you miss the net, a big blast from the church organ is triggered. You can sit in “stands” of church pews and watch a bizarre scene unfold, as local youth and biennale-hoppers, lit by stained glass, try to miss their goals. It’s interesting precisely because of its unrepeatable surrealism and incongruity.These micro-modernist slipper churches were based on a consensus: their strident modernism was founded on beliefs their working-class congregants all held. In the absence of a similar unity, this montage of protest, whimsy and spatial experiment suggests diversity might just be able to create spaces that are equally extraordinary – if given the money and time, both of which are usually so lacking in places like the Ruhr.Manifesta 16 Ruhris on until 4 OctoberExplore more on these topicsArchitectureGermanyEuropefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: From Gary Lineker’s revenge to the bizarre tale of a missing cat: the best viral TV moments of the World Cup | Television | The Guardian
description: A pranked England manager, a bored Beckham, a Viking takeover and an unwise Wayne Rooney wager: here are the TV treasures of the beautiful game’s biggest ever tournament
Author: Michael Hogan
Headline: A pranked England manager, a bored Beckham, a Viking takeover and an unwise Wayne Rooney wager: here are the TV treasures of the beautiful game’s biggest ever tournament
Content: Showboating … fans do the Viking row that has swept the globe.Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersView image in fullscreenShowboating … fans do the Viking row that has swept the globe.Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersTelevisionFrom Gary Lineker’s revenge to the bizarre tale of a missing cat: the best viral TV moments of the World CupA pranked England manager, a bored Beckham, a Viking takeover and an unwise Wayne Rooney wager: here are the TV treasures of the beautiful game’s biggest ever tournamentMichael HoganMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 00.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAstodgy final saw silky Spain deservedly beat cynical Argentina. Football wasn’t coming home after all as England suffered semi-final heartbreak again, before salvaging some pride to finish third – their best World Cup finish since 1966. The Tartan Army drank America’s bars dry as their team went out with a whimper. Cape Verde lasted longer than Germany. It was the end of the road for Ronaldo and Modric, while the phrase “hydration break” entered everyday parlance. But beyond the pitch, it has been an eventful five weeks, too.Here are 26 viral World Cup moments you might have missed …LOLing StonesAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueWon’t somebody consider Tommy T’s blood pressure? After England defeated Mexico, defender John Stones suddenly clutched his shoulder in the Azteca Stadium dressing room. Teammate Declan Rice, who was in on the gag, called over manager Thomas Tuchel, whose face fell with worry. At which point, perfectly timed with the music pumping out, Stones broke into an air-punching dance. Tuchel saw the funny side and hugged him. Stones reprised the prank after the Norway victory. You can’t kid a kidder.Is Vicky bovvered, though?View image in fullscreenVictoria: the one Beckham non-plussed at England v Norway.Photograph: Javier García/ShutterstockWho says WAGs are all pout and no passion?Victoria Beckham became an instant memeafter failing to react to England’s equaliser during the tense quarter-final against Norway. When Jude Bellingham scored, her husband, David, and son Romeo leapt to their feet in joy. Playing to type, unsmiling Posh Spice was too busy chatting to her hairdresser Ken Pavés to even notice. “She was celebrating inside, I promise,” David said later.Shaggy cat storyCommentary came over all stream-of-consciousness during Côte d’Ivoire v Norway. When winger Oscar Bobb came on,the BBC’s Danny Murphy said wistfully:“I used to have a cat called Bob. He jumped in the back of a Royal Mail van and we lost him. Sad, really.” “The game isn’t that bad,” chuckled Murphy’s colleague Steve Bower. When the throwaway remark captured viewers’ hearts,Murphy revisited it during Spain v Austria. “Bob didn’t actually die. We got him back six or seven weeks later. Unfortunately he’d gone feral and we couldn’t reintegrate him into the home. He ended up living with my friend’s parents in Preston.” A furry-tale for the ages.Vikings make wavesNorway fans did it in stadiums, on subway escalators and in Times Square. The synchronised “Viking row” celebration soon spread around the globe. Norwegian politicians put their differences aside to do it, with speaker Masud Gharahkhani turning coxswain. Golf fans did it to celebrate a Viktor Hovland birdie on the PGA Tour. Care home residents did it. Fighter pilots did it in their F-35 cockpits. Trust England to poop the party. After the Three Lions knocked Norway out, fans chanted: “There’ll be no f*cking rowing in New York.”Rowing refusenikNot everyone had a liking for Vikings. One Norway supporter ostentatiously refused to join in and went on news programmes worldwide to explain why. “It drives me crazy,” said Emil Anners Lappen. “The Vikings did not row across the Atlantic. They sailed. They can win whatever they want. I will not row.” All very principled until viewers pointed out thatLappen had a set of oarsmounted on the wall behind him.Belgians troll TrumpIt sparked accusations of cheating when President Trump personally lobbied the Fifa boss, Gianni Infantino, to defer the red card ban given to USA striker Folarin Balogun, allowing him to play in the last-16 tie against Belgium. Justice was done when the co-hosts were thrashed 4-1 by a Belgian side fired up by the scandal. “Overturn this!” they shouted, before gleefully mimicking Trump’s signature YMCA dad-dance. Bigly gratifying.Harry the teenager’s voice breaksAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueSomeone pass that man a lozenge. Having lost his voice during the celebratory rendition of Wonderwall after the nail-biting win over Mexico, England captain Harry Kane conducted a hilariously croaky on-pitch BBC interview. Oasis frontman Liam Gallagher sympathised, tweeting: “It’s hard work that singing, Harry Kane. C’mon England, c’mon Wonderwall.”Jude and Erling’s cute bromanceView image in fullscreenEmbracing … Erling Braut Haaland and Jude Bellingham.Photograph: Roberto Schmidt/AFP/Getty ImagesThey might have been quarter-final opponents but England’s Jude Bellingham and Norway’s Erling Haaland have an endearing friendship, dating back to their Borussia Dortmund days. Social media was awash with pics of the pair embracing and Bellingham kissing Haaland on the cheek mid-interview. Some jokingly compared it to gay ice hockey romanceHeated Rivalryand wrote erotic fan-fic. Even after defeat, Haaland sportingly insisted: “Bellingham has too many critics … He’s one of the best in the world … Anybody would want a Jude in their team.”Allow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueRooney sticks his oar inAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueA decade since Gary Lineker was forced to host Match of the Day in his underpants after vowing to do so if Leicester City won the Premier League, BBC pundit Wayne Rooney made his own unwise on-air wager. The former England captain said that if Norway reached the quarter-finals, he would row down the River Mersey. Norwegian striker Erling Haaland duly called out Rooney. Rooney turned out to be a man of his word and dulyrowed down the Hudsonwith colleagues Joe Hart and Micah Richards.Javier’s X-rated heckleDuring the Mexico v England epic, cameras captured the home team’s coach, Javier Aguirre, calling out to Three Lions winger Anthony Gordon, who was hugging the touchline in front of Aguirre’s technical area. Why? Just to tell him: “Hey Gordon, fuck you!” It was merely potty-mouthed banter and both cracked up laughing. Gordon later said being sworn at was “just a bit of fun” and “a compliment”.Haaland hits the catwalkAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueRussian model Anastasia Kostromitina went viral for being an Erling Haaland lookalike, embracing the resemblance by posting Instagram videos recreating the striker’s facial expressions. As she gained modelling offers and millions of followers, the Moscow-based 24-year-old said it was “like a dream”.A different kind of rowingAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueWhen cameras caught them squabbling in the stands, one couple sparked speculation worldwide about what they were arguing about. The South Africa supporters initially seemed to be having a good time. The mood turned sour when the male fan checked his mobile and she glanced at the screen. He appeared to say “It’s nothing” before swiftly pocketing the phone. She disagreed. He unwisely told her to “calm down”. She sat down looking furious while he repeatedly apologised. As social media users urged her to “file for divorce”, it was like a footballingColdplay kiss cam.Roon or Poch: who wore it best?USA manager Mauricio Pochettino became an unlikely style icon. Pundit Wayne Rooney liked Poch’s touchline look so much, he bought the same outfit andwore it on the BBC coverage. Rooney drew the line at rolling up his sleeves, though. He’s not a complete copycat.Your coach is now departingEngland’s assistant coach Anthony Barry became a cult hero for his pithily revealing interviews in the tunnel at half-time, offering insights into Tuchel’s tactics and the dressing room mood. During the third-place playoff, Barry became tearful talking about his pride in “11 lads with broken hearts”. Just as enjoyable was his habit ofabruptly exitingat the end of the conversation. Well, he had a dugout to get back to.Leads unitedAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueA dog that had been missing for a month was reunited with her owner after appearing in a viral video of celebrating Mexico fans. Gorda was spotted by her owner’s brother in footage from Ciudad Victoria after the co-hosts beat Czechia. “Life is good,” posted Ale Garcia after they were reunited. “Mexico won and I found my dog after a loooong time thanks to a livestream lol.”WonderwalletAllow content provided by a third party?This article includes content hosted onembed.bsky.app. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as the provider may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueArgentina fans took a break from Messi mania and Falklands chants to provide a heartwarming moment. During their victory over Jordan, a fellow fan dropped his wallet. Fans checked the name on the cards inside, then sang it until the owner appeared and was reunited with his lost property.Gary Lingerer, more likeOne gets the distinct impression that Gary Lineker enjoyed making the World Cup uncomfortable for his former BBC bosses. Having criticised his ex-colleagues for broadcasting from “a green box in Salford”, grinning Gaz not only made a cameo as an ITV pundit but unexpectedly returned to the BBC bygatecrashing Micah Richards’ Match of the Day interviewafter England beat Norway. “Look who’s turned up,” said an awkward Richards.Have you missed me?Thanks to mobile phones, people are rarely out of contact nowadays. Hence it felt like a throwback when 65-year-old England fan Michael Hewitt went awol for 10 days. En route to Boston to support the Three Lions, “Little Mick” lost his phone and plane tickets, so decided to turn a brief layover in Spain intoa longer break. Interpol, Spanish police and the British consulate launched a manhunt. Football fans spread the word online. The appeal appeared on TV bulletins. Hewitt turned out to be happily watching games in the bars of Barcelona, according to his relatives, “blissfully unaware that the world was searching for him”.Get outta my cup!Allow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueIt wouldn’t be a World Cup without *checks notes* Ross Kemp filming himself in front of the TV. The EastEnders hard man and documentary-maker has become a cult hero for posting vein-popping videos. “Come on, England, shut that crowd up! Jude, Jude, Jude! Yes, Harry Kane! That’s what I’m talking about!” Bonus points for excessive drooling and having his reading glasses perched atop his head.Nosubtitles requiredIt’s the international language of football. Japanese fan Hirochika Nakakuki went viral after a vox pop interview with Fox Sport, when he didn’t let the language barrier stand in the way. “Excuse me,” he said charmingly. “I cannot speak English but I am EXCITED!”‘Not my finest hour’Veteran midfielder Jordan Henderson suffered a freak injury while celebrating England’s win over Mexico. Having received a yellow card, Hendo climbed over the advertising hoardings at full-time, only to fall badly, break his wrist and get taken to hospital by paramedics. He became the first footballer to be booked and stretchered off without actually playing. “Not my finest hour,” he admitted sheepishly.Danny the champion of the World CupBBC commentator Danny Murphy was every England fan when they missed a chance to score against France andhe drummed the desk in frustration. “Danny, people can hear you if you bang your microphone on the table,” noted colleague Steve Wilson. “Sorry,” muttered Murphy. “It was that or shout really loud.”Kenny Dog-leashAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueIt’s been a classic World Cup for canines. During Argentina v Cape Verde, cameras cut to a dog wearing ear defenders, casually sitting in the VIP section of Miami Stadium. Brodie the golden doodle has become a crowd favourite, amassing 2.3 million Instagram followers. He was even made an honorary member of the Tartan Army after partying with Scotland fans earlier in the tournament.Big brother’s little brotherView image in fullscreenLamine Yamal celebrates with his brother Keyne after winning the 2026 World Cup football tournament final.Photograph: Franck Fife/AFP/Getty ImagesSpain’s teenage sensation Lamine Yamal might have won the final buthis three-year-old brother Keyneenjoyed an equally good World Cup, following his gifted elder sibling around. When the pair spotted each other on the big screen after the win over Belgium, broad grins and toddler face-pulling ensued. Wholesome content.Tequila shots on usSouth Korean fans enjoyed sampling the local culture ahead of their group game against Mexico but were foiled when they tried to take a bottle of tequila into Estadio Guadalajara. Refused entry at the gate, they couldn’t let it go to waste, so cracked it open and invited passing home fans to help finish it. Impromptu party! Mexico supporters had already earned credit by dancing to Gangnam Style when South Korea beat Czechia.Everyone’s a criticView image in fullscreen‘My favourite bit was when it finished’ … BTS perform at the Super Bowl-style half-time World Cup final show.Photograph: Carl Recine/Getty ImagesThe final’s Super Bowl-style half-time show kicked off with Madonna arriving in a dune buggy driven by Ronaldo and Ronaldinho. A tag-team production proceeded to take in the Muppets, BTS, Justin Bieber, Shakira, Burna Boy, Coldplay and a children’s choir wanging on about love. When BBC presenter Gabby Logan turned to the pundits for their verdict, Wayne Rooney spoke for the nation: “My favourite bit was when it finished. I like a lot of them artists butI thought it was crap.”Explore more on these topicsTelevisionWorld Cup 2026featuresShareReuse this content

title: ‘Everything I know about evil I learned in that small town’: John Carpenter on terrifying the world with Halloween, The Thing and The Fog | John Carpenter | The Guardian
description: At 78, the king of horror and sci-fi movies is as much of a rebel as ever. He talks about creativity, compromise (he’s not a fan) and how he became addicted to moviemaking
Author: Steve Rose
Headline: At 78, the king of horror and sci-fi movies is as much of a rebel as ever. He talks about creativity, compromise (he’s not a fan) and how he became addicted to moviemaking
Content: ‘Nightmares for me are personal’ … John Carpenter.Photograph: Philip Cheung/The Guardian; photographer's assistant: Jhovany QuirozView image in fullscreen‘Nightmares for me are personal’ … John Carpenter.Photograph: Philip Cheung/The Guardian; photographer's assistant: Jhovany QuirozThe big interviewJohn CarpenterInterview‘Everything I know about evil I learned in that small town’: John Carpenter on terrifying the world with Halloween, The Thing and The FogSteve RoseAt 78, the king of horror and sci-fi movies is as much of a rebel as ever. He talks about creativity, compromise (he’s not a fan) and how he became addicted to moviemakingMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn 2024,John Carpenterhad a dream that sounds like one of his movies: it involved a portal to the underworld beneath a disused church, monstrous demons and people being turned inside out. “I woke up and thought: wow! What an elaborate story,” he says. “I rarely have cinematic dreams, but this was one of them.” He liked it so much that he has turned it into a graphic novel, Cathedral, with an accompanying soundtrack. “It seemed to fit this heavy metal groove that we were starting on, and it worked out pretty well.”Most people would consider this a nightmare rather than a dream, I suggest. “No. Nightmares, for me, are personal – something that happened to me,” he replies. “Usually my nightmares are, I’m directing a movie and no one will listen to me. No one will follow my directions. Seriously, that’s what I wake up from.”It doesn’t take a Freudian analyst to unpack that one. Carpenter’s film career effectively wound down at the end of the 20th century, after 25 years of defiantly making movies his own way – often tense, terrifying, violent, gory movies. He is the epitome of the stubbornly independent film-maker, who demands complete creative control, who goes further than Hollywood studios would usually dare and whose name above the title is a seal of cult quality. “‘Don’t tell me what to do’ is John Carpenter’s credo!” he tells me. Few of his movies were commercial hits at the time, though, and a great many were the opposite, including his final two movies, 2001’s Ghosts of Mars and 2010’s The Ward.View image in fullscreenCarpenter and Kurt Russell on the set of Big Trouble in Little China (1986).Photograph: Photos 12/AlamyDoes he miss making movies? “Yes and no,” he says. He is talking on a video call from his home in the Hollywood Hills and, despite his reputation as an uncompromising grouch, the 78-year-old is in good spirits and good health. “It’s really weird. Yes, because I’ve always loved cinema and the process. But no, working in the studio system in Hollywood is very difficult.”He seems to have managed pretty well, I suggest. “You have to fight to do that. That’s the unfortunate thing. And that takes a little bit out of you, as a person, every time it has to happen,” he says. “There’s a reason why directors come out of the other end looking as if they’ve been in a war. Because they have been: it’s a creative war. And so there are a lot of drunks who are directors, that’s one way of coping, or taking other drugs. Anyway, we won’t get into all that.”Was that ever you? I ask.“No, no, no, let’s not,” he waves off the question. “I’m not going to tell you all my secrets.”Carpenter may have lost battles, but he won the war. Many of his films are considered classics: Halloween, The Thing, Assault on Precinct 13, The Fog, Escape from New York. And Carpenter’s film-making style continues to cast a long shadow – not only over horror and sci-fi movies, but also mainstream television, especially anything set in the 80s or with the look of that era (Stranger Things, say, or the current Widow’s Bay), and even philosophical culture-mongers such as Adam Curtis and Slavoj Žižek. Musicians also bow to him: his spare, synth-driven scores have been namechecked by many, from Portishead to Daft Punk, Trent Reznor to Hans Zimmer.Carpenter seems unaware of all this. When I tell him there is almost a paragraph of his Wikipedia entry listing film-makers who have cited him as an influence – Guillermo del Toro, Quentin Tarantino, Edgar Wright, James Cameron (who worked for Carpenter once) – he seems genuinely oblivious, pained even. “What does that mean to me? I can’t do anything about that. It’s wonderful, I think it’s fantastic, I don’t knowwhy. If you were gonna emulate a director, you’d emulate somebody whose moviesaren’tpanned.” It sounds as if he is not online much then? “No! I rebelled against mobile phones. I rebelled against computers. I can’t do that, I don’t want to do that.”View image in fullscreenCarpenter’s 1974 debut, Dark Star.Photograph: United Archives/FilmPublicityArchive/Getty ImagesCarpenter’s rebellious streak goes way back, and is inseparable from his art. His father was a music teacher and an orchestra violinist. In 1953, when John was five, the family relocated from northern New York when his dad got a teaching job at Western Kentucky University. “It was the Jim Crow south in the 1950s. Boy, were we fish out of water,” Carpenter says. “Everything I know about evil I learned in that small town of Bowling Green, Kentucky.”His father had encouraged John to learn violin too. “You’re walking to school in the morning and you have a violin under your arm; these country boys in Kentucky decided I was great to beat up. I was a mark for violence with my violin, which made me hate it all the more.”Classical music’s loss would ultimately be synthwave’s gain, but Carpenter was also just the right age to hook into the 60s counterculture. He rebelled against everything, he says: the town he grew up in, the Vietnam war, the system, “all forms of authority”. But he came to love and appreciate his father. “He told me early on: just create something, whether it’s music, writing, painting; whatever it is, create. And here I am, still doing it.”View image in fullscreen‘Working in the studio system in Hollywood is very difficult’ … Carpenter in his office.Photograph: Philip Cheung/The Guardian; photographer’s assistant: Jhovany QuirozHis mother regularly took him to the movies. “She gave me the gift of fantasy, and my father gave me a rigorous approach to creating.” Seeing the sci-fi movie Forbidden Planet when he was eight was a transformative experience: “I remember walking out and thinking: I’m gonna be a movie director.”While near-contemporaries such as Francis Ford Coppola and Martin Scorsese were taking over Hollywood through the front door, Carpenter operated on the genre sidelines. His 1974 debut, Dark Star, was a lo-fi hippy parody of Kubrick’s 2001: A Space Odyssey, which saw space travel less as a symphonic leap into the future and more as a grotty flatshare: boring, unhygienic and fractious. It was made on a shoestring, with egg cartons for spaceship interiors and an inflatable beach ball for an alien, but the movie did reasonably well, alerting Hollywood to the fact he could produce results on a small budget.It all came together with Halloween, in 1978. Led by a then unknown Jamie Lee Curtis and made for $325,000, it grossed over $70m. It opened the floodgates for highly profitable low-budget horror films and is often cited as the foundational slasher movie. Carpenter makes no such claims. “I put in what I thought was the summation of all the horror that I had seen. I wanted to use the audience, manipulate them like a ride at a carnival and have them scream, jump, not knowing where the thing is. Is it a man? Is it not a man?”View image in fullscreenA then relatively unknown Jamie Lee Curtis, in Halloween (1978).Photograph: Falcon International/Kim Gottlieb/AllstarHalloween was an assault on cosy smalltown domesticity: the horror wasn’t confined to a haunted house or a gothic castle; it was in your neighbourhood, in your home, preying on you and your children. “That’s the whole opening,” Carpenter says, “when it pulls back with this little kid with a knife in his hand [young Michael Myers, the future slasher] and it reveals we’re in the suburbs. That’s the whole idea.”Inevitably, the studio wanted Carpenter to repeat the trick with a sequel, especially since Halloween left the fate of its anti-hero open-ended. “I didn’t think there was any story left,” he says. “But they – meaning Universal Pictures and the producers – were gonna do it whether I wanted to or not. So what are you gonna do?” Carpenter wrote, but did not direct, Halloween 2, and after that: “They took it out of my hands, but they had to pay me every time they made a movie. I sit on the couch and a cheque arrives in my hand. What’s wrong with that? I have to admit, with all the rebellion in the world, I am a happy capitalist.”Nevertheless, Carpenter continued to stick it to the man, puncturing his era’s cheery certainties and self-congratulation, ridiculing authority figures, standing up for the underclass. It’s there in Assault on Precinct 13, where racial, gender and law-enforcement hierarchies are upended during a relentless night-time siege. It’s there in Escape from New York, where Kurt Russell’s snarling anti-hero is forced to rescue Donald Pleasence’s feeble American president, but has zero respect for him. It’s even there in his goofy action comedy Big Trouble In Little China – a movie where the ostensible leading man (Russell again) is actually a klutzy sidekick and the Asian cast are the real action heroes.View image in fullscreenHorror masterpiece … Kurt Russell in The Thing.Photograph: Universal Pictures/AllstarCarpenter was possibly at his most out of step with American sensibilities in the summer of 1982, when he released The Thing – a paranoid sci-fi thriller centred on a shape-shifting alien picking off members of an Antarctic research station, aided by some fantastically disgusting special effects. It is now recognised as a horror masterpiece. Back then, it arrived in cinemas two weeks after Steven Spielberg’s ET the Extra-Terrestrial. The contrast could not have been more jarring.Carpenter recalls going to see ET that summer: “I remember seeing the boys on their bicycles and the ET wrapped up, and then they go up into the sky magically. And that’s when I threw up.” He’s joking, but only half. “I was like: ‘Come on, don’t do this.’” He’s actually full of admiration for Spielberg, not least for reading the 80s zeitgeist. But he prefers his version: “If you really want to know the truth about alien encounters, I think that my movies are more realistic about what will happen. Think of the settlers who came to the United States and their encounters with the Indians. It didn’t really go so well for the Indians, OK? Our encounters with aliens – I imagine it won’t go so well for us.”That said, Carpenter did make a relatively feelgood alien story two years later: 1984’s Starman, in which a visiting alien inhabits the body of Karen Allen’s deceased husband, Jeff Bridges (who earned an Oscar nomination for his performance). The result was almost a grownup ET; a tender, comical, road-trip romance. It showed Carpenter’s surprising capacity for sensitive, emotional drama. “I wanted to show I could do it,” he says. “Bicycles taking off and flying – I wasn’t going to go that far, but …”View image in fullscreenJohn Carpenter with his son,Cody (left), and godson, Daniel Davies.Photograph: Sophie GransardThen there’s They Live, from 1988, which seems to have taken on a life of its own. The story concerns a homeless worker (played by wrestler “Rowdy” Roddy Piper) who discovers some sunglasses that reveal subliminal propaganda messages beneath every advert, magazine and piece of packaging he sees: “Obey”, “Marry and Reproduce”, “Stay Asleep”. Even the dollar bills secretly say: “This is your God.” The glasses also reveal the aliens who walk among us undetected, controlling humanity.Not exactly subtle, and They Live is by no means his best film (Piper is no Kurt Russell), but it has become part of the culture, endlessly mimicked, turned intomemesand even co-opted intoa successful clothing brandby the graphic artist Shepard Fairey – the ultimate irony, perhaps. Just last week, prankstersdoctored billboards for Meta’s AI glassesThey-Live-style, revealing Kylie Jenner to be an alien and the ad’s subliminal slogan to be “We’re always watching”.Carpenter was specifically targeting the Reagan era in They Live, he says, the hyperconsumerism, but also the inequality – contrasting Piper’s homeless community with the affluent heights of 80s Los Angeles. “It’s a documentary, and it didn’t end. It’s still going on now, even worse.” He stops himself. “You’ll get me ranting here, and that’s not a good thing.” But, almost needless to say, he’s depressed by the state of his country. “Things are bad here. I guess you guys realise that over there?”Žižek, the Slovenian philosopher, called They Live “one of the forgotten masterpieces of the Hollywood left” in his 2012 documentary The Pervert’s Guide to Ideology, which saw the film as a metaphor for not just consumerism but ideology as a whole: “When you put the glasses on, you see dictatorship in democracy. It’s the invisible order which sustains your apparent freedom.”“I don’t know if I’d go that far,” says Carpenter (he has never heard of Žižek, and asks me to send him the clip). “It’s actually democracy under pressure from the dictator. I’m a real sucker for American values. There’s a lot of people like me and we’re constantly fighting, but we’re doing it within the system. I vote. That’s the only power I have. I’m not a rich guy like these guys. And I keep believing that we’ll fight them off one day. We’re all going to be like Roddy Piper, we’re going to give ’em the finger at the end there.”View image in fullscreen‘Targeting the Reagan era’ … an alien policeman  in Carpenter’s 1988 film They Live.Photograph: MCA/Universal/AllstarOf course, voting is not the only power Carpenter has; his cinema is a textbook lesson in how beliefs and values can be transmitted through popular culture, even when they are in opposition to the beliefs of those who control that culture. But he is happy for others to take up the fight these days. He didn’t realise how much film-making was taking out of him until he stopped doing it, he says. “I was exhausted, and then I took this extended vacation and I got happier the more I was away from the thing I love. I thought: what’s going on here? I was addicted to moviemaking and I needed rehab. So I had to get away from it.”‘We used a beachball as an alien!’ John Carpenter on his gloriously shonky sci-fi comedy Dark StarRead moreSince then, he has toured the world playing his film music, with his son Cody and his godson, Daniel Davies, as well as recording new material. He has no regrets. “I’ve had great experiences with actors. I loved working with Sam Neill [who led Carpenter’s 1994 mind-bender In The Mouth of Madness], Jeff Bridges, Kurt, and my leading ladies have been great. I love them all. I’ve had a fantastic time. Now I’m playing music. I mean, it doesn’t get better than this. It really doesn’t.”Most of his days are spent at a leisurely pace, he says. “Basketball, the news and video games. That’s John’s entertainment. Then I go to have long, beautiful sleeps – and dream.”John Carpenter’s new album, Cathedral, is released on 7 August on Sacred Bones Records. His first graphic novel is released on 4 August4 via Storm King ComicsDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsJohn CarpenterThe big interviewHorror (TV)US politicsinterviewsShareReuse this content

title: Pesto Trapanese, pasta pomodoro and torta caprese: Dara Klein’s favourite Italian recipes | Food | The Guardian
description: Rustic, authentic and utterly delicious – three dishes that bring the taste of the trattoria straight to your table
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Rustic, authentic and utterly delicious – three dishes that bring the taste of the trattoria straight to your table
Content: Dara Klein's pesto trapanese.Photograph: Ola O Smit/The Guardian. Food styling: Aya Nishimura. Prop styling: Polly Webb-Wilson. Food styling assistant: Laura Lawrence.View image in fullscreenDara Klein's pesto trapanese.Photograph: Ola O Smit/The Guardian. Food styling: Aya Nishimura. Prop styling: Polly Webb-Wilson. Food styling assistant: Laura Lawrence.FoodPesto Trapanese, pasta pomodoro and torta caprese: Dara Klein’s favourite Italian recipesRustic, authentic and utterly delicious – three dishes that bring the taste of the trattoria straight to your tableDara KleinMon 20 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePesto trapanese combines almonds, tomatoes, pecorino and a hint of garlic and basil to create a fresher version thanthe typical Genovese variety, and I especially love the way the richness of the almonds is lifted by the fresh tomatoes – when they’re pureed together, it creates a wonderful mouthfeel. For me, though, pasta pomodoro symbolises home. My mum’s was a cornerstone of our trattoria, as it is at Italian restaurants around the world, and every family has their own fiercely guarded recipe. I developed this particular method when I startedPasta Princess, which delivered hand-rolled pasta and pomodoro sauce all over London during lockdown. It kept me grounded when I was in between kitchens, and resurfaced at Tiella, where it features regularly on the menu. Finally, torta caprese is a wonderfully rich flourless chocolate cake made with ground almonds. Mum often made a similar cake called torta gianduia, which uses hazelnuts instead of almonds, a recipe that hails from Piedmont, the heart of Italy’s hazelnut production (the Tonda Gentile delle Langhe variety is particularly prized for its incredible quality and flavour, carrying the IGP label to denote regionality and strict production standards). I remember her once buying these special hazelnuts on a trip to Italy, and taking them back home to New Zealand so she could makegianduia. When the cake came out of the oven, the aroma was so incredible that she decided to bring it home rather than putting it on the menu, and shared it with us instead.Pesto trapanese (pictured top)I prefer to make this in a mortar to achieve a rustic paste. You can use a blender, but operate it gently so you don’t end up with a smooth paste. Once the pesto is tossed with the pasta, finish with a touch of lemon juice and black pepper just before plating to add some brightness. You can omit the cheese for a dairy-free pesto: it still blends beautifully, and has a greater concentration of almonds, tomatoes and basil.Prep10 minCook30 minServes485g blanched almondsSea salt and freshly ground black pepper250g tomatoes,finely diced75ml extra-virgin olive oil, plus extra to serve1 garlic clove,peeled, halved and germ removed10g basil,leaves picked, plus extra to serve45g pecorino, grated, plus extra to serve320-400g pasta– busiate, penne, spaghetti, bucatini or fusilliLemon juice,to serveHeat the oven to 180C (160C fan)/350F/gas 4. Spread the almonds on a lined baking tray, sprinkle lightly with sea salt, and roast for 13 minutes. Remove and leave to cool completely.Using a pestle and mortar, stand blender or hand-held blender, blitz the almonds, tomatoes, oil, garlic and a big pinch of salt until almost smooth, but with a few chunks for texture. Add the basil and pecorino, pulse until the leaves have broken down, then taste and add more salt, if need be. If you’re not using the pesto straight away, transfer to a jar, pour a little olive oil on top to stop it oxidising, seal and keep in the fridge for up to three days.Cook your chosen pasta as per the packet instructions, lifting it out of the water one minute before it’s ready. While the pasta is cooking, loosen the pesto with a small ladleful of the starchy pasta water and warm over a low heat; do not blast it with heat, because this will ruin the flavour. Turn up the heat to medium, add the pasta and toss back and forth vigorously, adding a little more pasta water as needed.Continue tossing the pasta until the sauce thickens and turns glossy. Season to taste. I like to add a few drops of lemon juice – just enough to balance the dish, but not so much that it’s detectable. Plate immediately, garnish with the extra basil, a drop of good olive oil and an extra grating of pecorino, and serve.Sugo di pomodoroView image in fullscreenYou can cook pomodoro a hundred different ways. With this method, you get the deep umami effect of a long, slow cook in just one hour; though you can cook it for an hour longer if you want to slowly coax out the sweetness of the tomato even further. Use this recipe as a launchpad for your own variations, perhaps trying it with cherry tomatoes and fresh basil. I like to make this a day ahead and let it sit overnight. Tomato sauces are always better the next day.Prep5 minCook1 hr+Serves4120ml extra-virgin olive oil, plus extra for drizzling3 fresh bay leaves2 onions, peeled and thinly slicedSea salt80ml white wine700g passata400g pasta– orecchiette, penne, spaghetti, rigatoniParmigiano, or pecorino or ricotta, to serve (optional)Pangrattato, to serve (optional)Put a medium-sized, high-sided saucepan on a medium-low heat – you want a pan large enough to avoid excessive spitting while the sauce cooks. Once hot, add the oil, bay leaves, onions and a couple of pinches of salt, and cook, stirring frequently so they don’t colour, until the onions are translucent and soft. Turn up the heat to high, add the wine and cook for a few minutes, until most of the liquid has evaporated.Turn down the heat to medium, add the passata and a couple of pinches of salt, and stir to combine. Leave to simmer for 10 minutes without stirring, so the bottom of the pan almost catches, then take off the heat. There should be deep red-brown crust at the bottom of the pan. If it hasn’t formed, return to the heat for a few minutes more. Scrape the caramelised tomato off the bottom of the pan and stir it into the sauce; this will greatly deepen the flavour. During this process, there will be a fair bit of splashing from the pan.Return the pan to the stove and simmer slowly for 30-45 minutes, stirring every five minutes, during which time the sauce will thicken and deepen in colour. If you’re not using the sauce immediately, leave it to cool, transfer to a sealed container and put in the fridge for up to four days.Cook your chosen pasta as per the packet instructions, lifting it out of the water one minute before it’s ready and reserving a ladleful of the starchy water. Bring the sauce back up to heat, add the pasta and toss so it’s evenly distributed. Add the pasta water little by little to emulsify and thicken the sauce, then check for seasoning. To serve, drizzle over a small amount of good olive oil and sprinkle with your favourite cheese or pangrattato.Torta capreseView image in fullscreenThis recipe was developed at Tiella with my first pastry chef, Maximiliane Krauss-Waller. I love this cake because it’s so easy to make and hits that sweet spot between decadence and lightness. Use good-quality chocolate – it makes all the difference – and get creative with the garnish: I like whipped cream and Amarena cherries preserved in syrup, but it also works nicely with mascarpone laced with espresso, or even fresh berries.Prep10 minCook1 hr 10 min, plus chillingServes8170g hazelnuts(preferably skinless)170g unsalted butter, plus extra for greasing170g 70% cocoa dark chocolate170g light brown sugarSea salt4 eggs, separated1½ tbsp marsala, or amaretto or rum (optional)To serveIcing sugar, or cocoa powderAmarena cherries in syrup, or fresh raspberries or strawberriesWhipped cream, or mascarpone or creme fraicheHeat the oven to 180C (160C fan)/350F/gas 4 and grease and line a 20cm round springform cake tin with baking paper.Toast the hazelnuts in the oven for about 11 minutes, until golden, then remove and leave to cool. If the hazelnuts still have skins on, wrap them in a clean tea towel and rub vigorously to knock off the skins. Blitz the hazelnuts to a fine, sandy texture; stop if they start to clump, or you’ll end up with nut butter. Conversely, make sure the hazelnuts aren’t too coarse or this will result in a very crumbly cake.Melt the butter and chocolate in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of simmering water, stirring occasionally to incorporate – make sure the heat isn’t too high and the water isn’t touching the bowl, or the chocolate might burn.Off the heat, stir in the ground hazelnuts, sugar and two pinches of salt. Leave the batter to cool slightly, then whisk in the egg yolks and booze, if using.In a second bowl, whip the egg whites to soft peaks, then fold them through the chocolate batter in three instalments. Pour into the lined cake tin and smooth the top with a spatula or palette knife. Bake for 40 minutes, then remove and leave to cool at room temperature. This cake benefits from an overnight rest; I like to chill it overnight before turning it out and slicing.To serve, flash bake individual slices of the cake in a 180C (160C fan)/350F/gas 4 oven for a few minutes, so they warm through and become gooey. Traditionally, icing sugar is sifted on top, but I prefer to use good-quality cocoa powder. Serve with Amarena cherries and whipped cream or creme fraiche on the side.These recipes are edited extracts from La Trattoria, by Dara Klein, published by Ebury Press at £30. To order a copy for £27, go toguardianbookshop.comExplore more on these topicsFoodPastaItalian food and drinkMain courseStarterDessertCakerecipesShareReuse this content

title: From fidget bags to sticker books: readers’ hard-won hacks for travelling with kids | Family holidays | The Guardian
description: You told us your tried-and-tested tricks for keeping children entertained. Plus, the best eye masks and natural deodorants, tested
Author: Lucy Pavia
Headline: You told us your tried-and-tested tricks for keeping children entertained. Plus, the best eye masks and natural deodorants, tested
Content: On the right track … readers share the tips that make travelling with kids that bit easier.Photograph: ballyscanlon/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenOn the right track … readers share the tips that make travelling with kids that bit easier.Photograph: ballyscanlon/Getty ImagesThe Filter UK newsletterFamily holidaysFrom fidget bags to sticker books: readers’ hard-won hacks for travelling with kidsYou told us your tried-and-tested tricks for keeping children entertained. Plus, the best eye masks and natural deodorants, testedDon’t get the Filter delivered to your inbox? Sign up hereLucy PaviaMon 20 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.If there’s one thing worse than a long journey, it’s a long journey with bored, fractious children. So what helps smooth things along? We asked for your best, tried-and-testedhacks for travelling with kids, from “fidget bags” to lollipops.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.On a long car trip from the UK to France with his kids, readerTim Leadbeaterattached small plastic baskets with string around the pillars of the front headrest: “They held everything from felt tips to half-eaten food, not only preventing stains on covers but also keeping everything reachable for kids in car seats.”Likewise,Deborah Berzinsused to make what she calls a “fidget bag full of little characters, atoy snake, astretchy man toy, andfinger puppets”, which only came out oncar journeysso the novelty didn’t wear off.Another trick suggested by readerKateis to wrap small, individual toys in old wrapping paper: “Even the oldest crayon, book ortiny Lego figurebecame an exciting discovery; the unwrapping was a game in itself, and the activity helped pass the time,” she says.Boredom bustersView image in fullscreenDrawing it out … colouring books were among readers’ favourite journey distractions.Photograph: SanyaSM/GettyNettie Hall, who has a no-screen policy, survived a solo trip from London to Cornwall with her three-year-old by arming herself with multiple activities: “I got severalsticker books, asimple crown-making kit(sticking gems on a cardboard crown that ties around the head with string), small pots of play-dough, colouring pencils andOrchard Toys packable games,” she says. “Don’t overestimate the attention span of very young children!”Sophierecommends packing “thousands of snacks”, but this isn’t just to keep her daughter preoccupied. “My daughter is intolerant to loads of things, so this is extra important as we can never guarantee a station shop will have anything she can eat,” she says.Meanwhile, one reader says she has never boarded a plane or train without the following: a smallcolouring bookfor inevitable delays, sticking plasters and antiseptic wipes for inevitable cut knees, and small lollipops or similar for instantly cheering up children with cut knees – and probably sometimes adults too. Happy travels!This week’s picks‘This is my bucket-list spot for wild camping’: Outdoors expert Sian Lewis answered your questions‘It’s the first thing we set up’: parents on everything you need for camping with kids – and what you don’tThe best camping tents: 10 expert picks for every outdoor adventureDo natural deodorants actually work? I put 18 to the test – here are my favouritesThe best walking sandals for women: 10 comfy and supportive styles for summerCurrentBody Multi Light Therapy LED mask review: hands down the best I’ve testedThe best air coolers to chill your home during heatwaves – tested‘Proof that delicious tomatoes can be grown in the UK’: the best supermarket vine tomatoes, tasted and ratedHow I Shop with Angela Hartnett: ‘The purchase I regret the most? Any fitness machine!’The best fake tan for a sunkissed, streak-free glow – testedEditor’s pickView image in fullscreenEye’ll be back … after a solid eight hours.Photograph: Jane Hoskyn/The GuardianWhen Jane Hoskyn testedsleep aidslast year, she was surprised that a sub-£10eye maskended up giving her the best night’s sleep. Now she’s tested a range of 12, including masks that play music, massage your temples and give you psychedelic light shows to get you to sleep. But has anything toppled her favourite? Read her guide to thebest eye masksto find out.Monica HorridgeDeputy editor, the FilterIn case you missed it …View image in fullscreenPower to the people: portable chargers can be a saving grace when devices look as if they’re fading.Heading off on holiday, or to a festival? If digitally unplugging for the entire time feels like a step too far, don’t forget to pack a portable charger (they’ve saved many a camping trip here on the Filter). Our test of thebest power banksfeatures everything froma sub-£20 devicethat fits in a pocket toone for multiple usersthat might just stop family squabbles.And don’t forget: if you’re choosing to fly,keep it in your hand luggage.Get involvedView image in fullscreenDetour ahead … what helped when your holiday didn’t go to plan?Photograph: nicoletaionescu/GettySummer holidays aren’t all fabulous beach days, gorgeous villas and good vibes (as anyone who has watchedTwo Weeks in Augustcan attest). Sometimes we get sunburnt, bitten by midges, lose our luggage, eat something that didn’t agree with us – or the other family’s kids simply drive you nuts.How did you cope when things went wrong? What saved the day (or week)? What one thing do you wish you’d taken with you, or left behind? Let us know by emailing us atthefilter@theguardian.com.Explore more on these topicsFamily holidaysThe Filter UK newsletterParents and parentingActivities for childrenFamilynewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Rukmini Iyer’s quick and easy recipe for courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishes | Food | The Guardian
description: Perfect for an al fresco lunch spread, these bruschetta come together as quickly as they disappear …
Author: Rukmini Iyer
Headline: Perfect for an al fresco lunch spread, these bruschetta come together as quickly as they disappear …
Content: Rukmini Iyer’s courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishes.Photograph: Beca B Jones/The Guardian: Food stylist: El Kemp. Prop stylist: Florence Blair. Food stylist assistant: Caitlin Macdonald.View image in fullscreenRukmini Iyer’s courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishes.Photograph: Beca B Jones/The Guardian: Food stylist: El Kemp. Prop stylist: Florence Blair. Food stylist assistant: Caitlin Macdonald.Quick and easyFoodRukmini Iyer’s quick and easy recipe for courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishesPerfect for an al fresco lunch spread, these bruschetta come together as quickly as they disappear …Rukmini IyerMon 20 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFresh, light but filling – these bruschetta are beautiful to bring outside as part of a lunch spread. They take minutes to whip together (sorry) and, for extra Marie Antoinette at Le Petit Trianon vibes, pick mint (or/and baby courgettes) from your own garden. You can get ahead by making the whipped feta and lemon dressing in advance, and keeping the courgette ribbons and radishes in cold water until needed. Drain and dress the courgettes just before serving – they’re lovely with the contrast of the toasted baguette and cool cheese.Courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishesPrep10 minCook15 minServes4 as a starter/side1 small yellow courgette1 small green courgette1bunch radishes, halved8 slices good baguette200g feta, roughly broken100g natural yoghurtZest of ½ lemon,plus 15ml lemon juice15ml extra-virgin olive oilBig pinch sea salt flakesUse a speed peeler to peel the courgette into thin ribbons, then transfer them to a bowl of cold water along with the halved radishes (this will help crisp them up).Toast the baguette slices in a single layer under a hot grill (I set mine at 200C) for two minutes, until well toasted on one side.Put the feta, yoghurt and lemon zest in a food processor, and blitz smooth. Alternatively, mash the feta in a large bowl with a fork, then whisk in the yoghurt and lemon zest as furiously as possible.Whisk the lemon juice, olive oil and salt until they’re a pale, buttercup yellow. Drain the courgettes and radishes well. Gently pat the courgettes dry with kitchen paper or a clean tea towel, then toss them with the dressing.Spread the untoasted side of the baguette slices with some of the whipped feta and arrange the courgette ribbons on top. Put the remaining whipped feta in a bowl and serve with the radishes alongside for dipping.Explore more on these topicsFoodQuick and easyCheeseVegetablesVegetarian food and drinkrecipesShareReuse this content

title: The pet I’ll never forget: Bird the hawk – the predator who once hugged me for 20 minutes | Life and style | The Guardian
description: A Harris’s hawk, she is capable of flying at more than 35mph, scouring the ground for prey, while also judging my footwear. The moments of connection with this wild creature are incredible
Author: Gabriel Stewart
Headline: A Harris’s hawk, she is capable of flying at more than 35mph, scouring the ground for prey, while also judging my footwear. The moments of connection with this wild creature are incredible
Content: ‘Now I only do the things that bring me joy’ … Candida Meyrick and Bird on Anglesey.Photograph: Jesse WilkinsonView image in fullscreen‘Now I only do the things that bring me joy’ … Candida Meyrick and Bird on Anglesey.Photograph: Jesse WilkinsonThe pet I'll never forgetLife and styleThe pet I’ll never forget: Bird the hawk – the predator who once hugged me for 20 minutesA Harris’s hawk, she is capable of flying at more than 35mph, scouring the ground for prey, while also judging my footwear. The moments of connection with this wild creature are incredibleCandida Meyrick, as told toGabriel StewartMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMy children brought hawks into my life. My youngest son, especially, became fascinated by birds of prey at the age of six, drawing beautiful pictures and reciting endless facts to bird owners at various country shows.Eventually I decided, why not? We live in the countryside, after all, on Anglesey (Ynys Môn). So, during Covid, Bird, the Harris’s hawk, came into our lives. We got her from a breeder after she hatched. It was perfect timing: I was home-schooling my three kids, and at 2pm every day, rain or shine, with a brilliant local falconer, we would set out on to the headland to watch her fly. It was beautiful to see. For years, my youngest never missed a day, no matter how cold.Bird’s full name is Sophia Houdini White Wing. My children wanted to name her after her mum, Sophie. White Wing relates to the bloodline she descends from and Houdini is because of her brilliant skill as a shapeshifter. She can imitate the branch tip of a tree or stand bolt upright in line with a trunk, leaving you unable to spot her as she hides from predators or prey. This is impressive considering she has a 1.5 metre wingspan.View image in fullscreenBeautiful to see … Meyrick with Bird.Photograph: Jesse WilkinsonShe lives in a mews with an outdoor area near our house and can fly at speeds in excess of 35mph. A Harris’s sight is said to be eight times greater than a human’s as she scours the terrain below for prey. But her personality is far more than her intelligence as a raptor. She has a great sense of humour and is very judgy about my footwear – literally turning her back if I’m not wearing the right boots for going out.Recently, approaching the moult (a period over the summer in which birds no longer fly in the wild as they shed their old feathers), thinking how much I was going to miss flying her, she suddenly got her right wing and slipped it around me, holding it there for about 20 minutes as we walked home – like an actual hug. I don’t want to humanise a wild predator but the moments of connection are incredible.Ten years ago, I had breast cancer and it was a big wake-up call. I vowed to stop making excuses, to do the things that bring me joy. In your short life, you’ve got to make the most of it, write the things you need to write, paint the things you need to paint, and say yes to a Harris’s hawk.Be More Bird: Life Lessons from a Hawk by Candida Meyrick is published by William Collins (16.99). To support the Guardian, order your copy atguardianbookshop.com. Delivery charges may applyExplore more on these topicsLife and styleThe pet I'll never forgetPetsBirdsAnimalsWildlifefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Why can’t Lifestyle & Mobility store show compassion after my loss? | Consumer affairs | The Guardian
description: I bought a wheelchair for my daughter, but she later died in hospital and the company refuses a refund
Author: Anna Tims
Headline: I bought a wheelchair for my daughter, but she later died in hospital and the company refuses a refund
Content: The store refused a return even though the chair had not been used.Photograph: Fahroni/AlamyView image in fullscreenThe store refused a return even though the chair had not been used.Photograph: Fahroni/AlamyConsumer championsConsumer affairsWhy can’t Lifestyle & Mobility store show compassion after my loss?I bought a wheelchair for my daughter, but she later died in hospital and the company refuses a refundAnna TimsMon 20 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.26 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleI bought a £435 wheelchair for my disabled daughter from my local Lifestyle & Mobility store.She had been admitted to hospital for tests and I planned to use the chair to take her on outings when she was discharged.The next day, her condition deteriorated and she died two days later. She was 46.The wheelchair had never left the boot of my car and, since the store and the car park were in an indoor shopping centre, had never been taken outside.I begged Lifestyle & Mobility to allow me to return it, even for a partial refund, but it has refused.My daughter was a single parent and the money would make a difference to her teenage daughter; moreover, the wheelchair is another reminder of our loss and it hurts to look at it.JS,BournemouthI’m very sorry to hear of your loss. You have tried for the past three months to sell the wheelchair privately online but to no avail.You bought it in person because you try to support local stores.If you had bought it online, you could have saved up to £100 and benefited from a statutory 14-day cooling-off period.Lifestyle & Mobility has no legal obligation to refund you, but I hoped, given the grievous circumstances, that compassion would prevail. It did not.It told me that customers are only refunded if a product is faulty or mis-sold. “Given the nature of our business, we are regrettably familiar with circumstances of the sort this customer is experiencing,” it says. “Be assured that we take no pleasure in having to apply the above criteria in such cases.”Its terms and conditions suggest differently. These state that any goodwill variations to the refund policy are at the discretion of the company.I asked it twice why it will not use its discretion in your case, which, given your daughter died so young, and so suddenly, before the chair was unpacked, strikes me as exceptional. It did not reply.We welcome letters but cannot answer individually. Email us atconsumer.champions@theguardian.comor write to Consumer Champions, Money, the Guardian, 90 York Way, London N1 9GU. Please include a daytime phone number. Submission and publication of all letters is subject toour terms and conditions.Explore more on these topicsConsumer affairsConsumer championsConsumer rightsDisabilityRetail industryfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Did you solve it? Do you think like a physicist? | Mathematics | The Guardian
description: The answer to today’s puzzle
Author: Alex Bellos
Headline: The answer to today’s puzzle
Content: Professor Stephen HawkingPhotograph: Murdo Macleod/The GuardianView image in fullscreenProfessor Stephen HawkingPhotograph: Murdo Macleod/The GuardianAlex Bellos's Monday puzzleMathematicsDid you solve it? Do you think like a physicist?The answer to today’s puzzleAlex BellosMon 20 Jul 2026 11.47 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 12.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEarlier todayI set you a puzzle about keeping homes cool in a heatwave. Here it is again with the solution.Imagine a room with a set of curtains. When the Sun is shining, its energy comes through the windows where it is absorbed by the curtains. The curtains get hotter and hotter until they reach a temperature at which they are radiating as much heat as they absorb. The curtains radiate 50 per cent of this heat back towards the window, and 50 per cent of the heat into the room.In other words, curtains stop 50 per cent of the energy from the Sun.Imagine a room with roller blinds and curtains. The blinds line the windows, and the curtains cover the blinds. There are thus two layers of protection from the Sun.Now what percentage of the Sun’s energy gets into the room?(Assume the blinds and curtains are made out of homogeneous material, and that the system has reached equilibrium. Also, this is a simplified model, so ignore the glass windows, the colour of the materials and the shape of the room.)Solution33 per cent.WorkingsBelow is a diagram that shows the heat flow when the system reaches equilibrium, i.e. when both curtains and blinds reach their individual stable temperatures.View image in fullscreenThe curtain is radiating the same amount inwards and outwards. Let the energy radiated in either direction beh.If the curtain has absorbed a total of 2h, it must have got this energy from the blind. We deduce that the blind is radiating 2hforwards and backwards.The blind, however, is also receivinghform the curtain. So, of the 4hthat it is radiating, only 3hcomes from the Sun.If 3his coming in, andhenters the room, the percentage of the Sun’s energy is 33 per cent.I hope you enjoyed this puzzle. I’ll be back in two weeks.Thanks to Alan Williams-Key for today’s puzzle.I’ve been setting a puzzle here on alternate Mondays since 2015. I’m always on the look-out for great puzzles. If you would like to suggest one,email me.Explore more on these topicsMathematicsAlex Bellos's Monday puzzlePhysicsShareReuse this content

title: Starwatch: Identifying the Scorpion, mythical adversary of Orion | Astronomy | The Guardian
description: The full constellation can be frustrating to track down in the UK owing to its low presence in the southern sky
Author: Stuart Clark
Headline: The full constellation can be frustrating to track down in the UK owing to its low presence in the southern sky
Content: The red-orange star Antares marks the heart of Scorpius.Illustration: Guardian DesignView image in fullscreenThe red-orange star Antares marks the heart of Scorpius.Illustration: Guardian DesignStarwatchAstronomyStarwatch: Identifying the Scorpion, mythical adversary of OrionThe full constellation can be frustrating to track down in the UK owing to its low presence in the southern skyStuart ClarkMon 20 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThis week we will take the opportunity to identify Scorpius, the Scorpion, one of the famous zodiacal constellations.In mythology, Scorpius is the creature sent to kill Orion, the hunter. The two constellations are therefore on opposite sides of the sky from one another, so that when Scorpius rises, Orion has already departed.‘Flight originated from the imagination’: how artists have captured space travelRead moreThe full constellation is visible from more southerly latitudes, but for stargazers in the UK it can be one of the more frustrating to track down because it is so low that it never fully rises above the horizon. The chart shows the view looking low in the southern sky from London at 11pm BST on 24 July. The constellation remains in roughly the same part of the sky all week, but on this night the moon also graces the scene.The bright waxing gibbous moon lies close to Antares, the red-orange star that marks the heart of the Scorpion. Its colour can be striking, appearing ruby jewel-like, although from Britain it often appears to twinkle because it is seen through the thicker, more turbulent air close to the horizon.Antares means “rival of Mars”, a reference to its reddish colour. In reality it is a red supergiant star, vastly larger than the sun and nearing the end of its life.Explore more on these topicsAstronomyStarwatchSpaceThe moonfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Country diary: Beaver-style flood defences take root amid a hosepipe ban | Drought | The Guardian
description: Caistor St Edmund, Norfolk: Underfoot the ground is as hard as metal, while the council’s ‘leaky dams’ are set for the next deluge
Author: Kate Blincoe
Headline: Caistor St Edmund, Norfolk: Underfoot the ground is as hard as metal, while the council’s ‘leaky dams’ are set for the next deluge
Content: One of the four ‘leaky dams’ on Wash Lane.Photograph: Kate BlincoeView image in fullscreenOne of the four ‘leaky dams’ on Wash Lane.Photograph: Kate BlincoeCountry diaryDroughtCountry diary: Beaver-style flood defences take root amid a hosepipe banCaistor St Edmund, Norfolk:Underfoot the ground is as hard as metal, while the council’s ‘leaky dams’ are set for the next delugeKate BlincoeMon 20 Jul 2026 00.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCatkins fall from the sweet chestnut trees like long yellow streamers from a party. More than 20 wasps fret over the bonnet of my car, feeding off something – maybe sticky tree sap or dead insects.The hay crop is in. No high yields, but better than last year. Two red kites hunt over the coumarin-sweet cut meadows and the swallows glide low. The empty fields usually beckon a gallop, but the ground rings out under hoof like a metalled road. If there is no rain, a second crop seems unlikely.A hosepipe ban has just been announced, with East Anglia the driest region in the country. Imagining a deluge seems almost impossible, yet my village, a few miles away, has been planning for just that.In December 2020, the rain didn’t stop. Drains couldn’t cope with the rush of water off the fields. The street became a fast, shallow, brown river and sandbags weren’t enough to stop homes from flooding. The road is often submerged, enough to stop smaller cars. The flood signs go up, and volunteers venture out to keep the drains clear. That Christmas was the worst.View image in fullscreenSweet chestnut flowers fall.Photograph: Kate BlincoeBut something has changed. When I walk down the aptly named Wash Lane, where a deep crevice holds the now completely dry watercourse, there are four large constructions. Made from wood, they are “leaky dams” or logjams, designed to collect debris and naturally slow the flow of water.This has cost Norfolk county council more than £35,000. The work is inspired by the original and bestecosystem engineers: beavers. They fell trees for constructing their dams and lodges, and in doing so reduce water flow and create pools for dragonflies and amphibians to breed.We are beaver-less round here. There are a handful of released beavers in Norfolk, some of which have bred successfully, but not close by.Yet while these timber barriers have been built to slow water naturally, the surrounding fields are proposed for a large-scale solar development, which could alter rainwater runoff. Climate change brings us extremes of weather, but surely the solutions must be beaver-clever, working for the health of the whole landscape and leaving more room for nature, not less.Under the Changing Skies: The Best of the Guardian’s Country Diary, 2018-2024, is available now atguardianbookshop.comExplore more on these topicsDroughtCountry diaryRural affairsFloodingAnimalsWildlifefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Barking mad: why would someone spend £28,000 on a home spa for their dog? | Dogs | The Guardian
description: The UK is a nation of animal lovers. But the rise of ‘barkitecture’ has taken it too far, with people investing in outdoor showers, whirlpool baths and ergonomic feeding stations for their faithful friends
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The UK is a nation of animal lovers. But the rise of ‘barkitecture’ has taken it too far, with people investing in outdoor showers, whirlpool baths and ergonomic feeding stations for their faithful friends
Content: Dog days …  the ‘Balmoral’ Chesterfield-style dog bed.Photograph: Etsy/ScottsLondonView image in fullscreenDog days …  the ‘Balmoral’ Chesterfield-style dog bed.Photograph: Etsy/ScottsLondonPass notesDogsBarking mad: why would someone spend £28,000 on a home spa for their dog?The UK is a nation of animal lovers. But the rise of ‘barkitecture’ has taken it too far, with people investing in outdoor showers, whirlpool baths and ergonomic feeding stations for their faithful friendsMon 20 Jul 2026 10.49 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleName:Barkitecture.Age:27 years old.Appearance:Plush provisions for posh pooches.It’s 27 years old? That’s hardly newsworthy.Well, there was Fred Albert’s 1999 book Barkitecture, about upmarket kennels. And then there was the 2020 Quibi platform show Barkitecture, where a Bachelorette runner-up made kennels for celebrities. Also, the Guardian hascovered the term before.Somy point stands.But wait, the term has mutated.Into what?It no longer covers just kennels. It is now about redesigning your house beyond all recognition in order to pamper your pet.Ineedspecifics.How about outdoor hot water showers for your dog? Or an ergonomic feeding station, so your pet doesn’t have to bend its neck to reach its dinner? Or a calming pheromone dispenser to keep your dog happy?That’s just what I’ve always wanted– a lazy, stoned dog.I suppose I had better not mention the rise of the £45 doggy dryrobe, then.Oh, forGod’s sake.Dogsare working animals.And you’re a working person. Do you want to be hosed down with freezing water and eat your food off the ground every day? Do you want to wander around naked?That’s different. I’m ahuman.Well, suck it up, because barkitecture is the new norm. The interior designer Laura Stephens told the Telegraph: “In high-end homes, dog showers are as essential as somewhere to put muddy boots … I’m seeing a lot more demand for dog beds too, and I’ve just designed an under-stairs cupboard for a cat with a built-in feeding station.”So much for the cost of living crisis.That’s it. Treating your pet as lavishly as possible is a sign of a large disposable income.This isn’t actually about the animals, is it?Of course it is. Everyone loves their pets. But if you can show your neighbours that you have enough money to love your pets more than they do, then that’s an added bonus.Well, I do love being obnoxious.In that case, may I introduce you to the£3,000 Wet Pets hydro spadog whirlpool bath, featuring antimicrobial walls and adjustable mood lighting? Or the £670 custom-built “Balmoral”Chesterfield-style leather dog bed?Good lord. How about a shepherd’s hut that has been converted into a dedicatedhigh-end dog spa? Yours for just £28,000.Won’t all thisspoil your pets?Yes, you’re completely right, your cockapoo is far less likely to help you track prey across the wild expanses if it has a warm shower once a week, isn’t it? I unreservedly apologise.Do say:“Rover, walkies!”Don’t say:“Once you’ve finished your massage, obviously.”Explore more on these topicsDogsPass notesHomesPetsAnimalsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘Front groups and fellow travelers’: state department attacks US left over Cuba | Cuba | The Guardian
description: New report denounces Havana and claims ‘campaign of subversion’ has infiltrated highest levels of US government
Author: Maya Yang
Headline: New report denounces Havana and claims ‘campaign of subversion’ has infiltrated highest levels of US government
Content: New York mayor Zohran Mamdani, US congresswoman Ilhan Omar and streamer and pundit Hasan Piker. Mamdani and Piker were mentioned in the report, as was Omar’s daughter Isra Hirsi.Photograph: Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenNew York mayor Zohran Mamdani, US congresswoman Ilhan Omar and streamer and pundit Hasan Piker. Mamdani and Piker were mentioned in the report, as was Omar’s daughter Isra Hirsi.Photograph: Getty ImagesCuba‘Front groups and fellow travelers’: state department attacks US left over CubaNew report denounces Havana and claims ‘campaign of subversion’ has infiltrated highest levels of US governmentMaya YangMon 20 Jul 2026 18.50 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 19.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAs part of the Trump administration’s continuing attack on left-leaning groups, the US state department has listed several activists and organizations as “front groups and fellow travelers” forCuba, including Hasan Piker and Amazon Labor Union founder Chris Smalls.In a 100-pagereportreleased on Monday, the state department denounces Cuba as the “capital of 21st-century communism”, accusing the Cuban government of carrying out a “campaign of subversion” that has “infiltrated the highest reaches of the US government, recruited and cultivated generations of American activists [and] backed an unprecedented wave of leftwing terrorism on American soil”.The state department report alleges that the so-called campaign is “designed to turn America against itself” and singles out several left-leaning activists and organizations, including the Democratic Socialists of America, Isra Hirsi – the daughter of Minnesota Democratic representative Ilhan Omar – New York City mayor Zohran Mamdani, and journalist Amy Goodman, citing their criticism of US policy toward Cuba or participation in Cuba solidarity movements.Describing the DSA, of which Mamdani is a member, the report says the organization “serves as a particularly potent illustration of the ideological victory of Cuba’s effort to position itself as the spiritual capital of Third Worldist radicalism”.
