It accuses the DSA of maintaining a “fierce, almost religious commitment to the cause of the Cuban regime – not because the group is controlled by Cuban agents, per se, but because that commitment is now simply the de facto policy orthodoxy on the American far left”.Trump hasdoubled downon similar messaging in recent days, using the term communism at political rallies and presidential engagements alike.For more than 60 years, the US has maintained a sweeping economic embargo on Cuba, in addition to crippling sanctions imposed by numerous administrations. Despite US claims that its policies are designed to pressure the Cuban government, Havana and many international critics includinghuman rights organizationssay they have severely hurt the island’s economy.The report comes just days after the state department, led by Marco Rubio – the son of Cuban immigrants and one of the US’s most outspoken critics of Cuba’s communist government –imposed further sanctionson the country, targeting its tourism ministry and several state-owned companies.The sanctions come as part of the Trump administration’s “comprehensive push to end the Cuban regime’s malign activities, both in Cuba and across our hemisphere”.Rubio has long advocated for“maximum pressure”toward Havana, arguing that easing US sanctions only strengthens Cuba’s communist government. Throughout his political career including his time as a senator, Rubio hasbacked tighter economic restrictionsand repeatedly accused the Cuban authorities ofrepressing political dissentandfueling violenceacross the region.Earlier this month, Cuba suffered itsthird nationwide blackoutin six months amid an oil blockade imposed by Trump on theisland of 9.6 million people, prompting many residents to say that “living like this is agony”.Explore more on these topicsCubaUS foreign policyCaribbeanAmericasnewsShareReuse this content

title: Nicaragua’s president says country will not hold any more elections | Nicaragua | The Guardian
description: Daniel Ortega, the 80-year-old authoritarian leader, says move will ‘build a wall’ against the opposition
Author: Tiago Rogero
Headline: Daniel Ortega, the 80-year-old authoritarian leader, says move will ‘build a wall’ against the opposition
Content: Daniel Ortega made the announcement on Sunday during the official ceremony marking the 47th anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenDaniel Ortega made the announcement on Sunday during the official ceremony marking the 47th anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesNicaraguaNicaragua’s president says country will not hold any more electionsDaniel Ortega, the 80-year-old authoritarian leader, says move will ‘build a wall’ against the oppositionTiago RogeroSouth America correspondentMon 20 Jul 2026 17.36 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.50 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleNicaragua’s long-serving president,Daniel Ortega, has said the country will hold no further elections to prevent the opposition from coming to power.The 80-year-old authoritarian leader, who has been in office since 2007, also said he would work with the congress he controls to pass new laws that would “build a wall” against the opposition.Ortega made the announcement on Sunday during the official ceremony marking the 47th anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution, in which he helped overthrow the brutal dictatorship of Anastasio Somoza.Although his administration and that of his wife and “co-president”, Rosario Murillo, had already been marked by the steady erosion of democracy in the Central American country, many are describing his announcement as a significant rhetorical escalation.“With this decision, Ortega and Murillo have sealed the country’s transition to a full-fledged family dictatorship,” said Tiziano Breda, Latin America senior analyst at the NGO Armed Conflict Location and Event Data.During a speech in front of thousands of state employees who,according to local media, had been coerced into attending, Ortega said: “There will be no more elections here so that they [the opposition parties] can try to seize the government, seize power.”The president, who had not appeared in public for 61 days, added: “We will work with the National Assembly and the relevant institutions on laws, because we need laws that build a wall, a barrier, against the coup plotters and the traitors who sell out their country.”The elections were due to take place next year, but Ortega gave no details on whether they would be cancelled or whether the opposition would be barred from taking part – something that has already happened in practice. During the 2021 campaign, his regime outlawed parties and jailed all opposition presidential hopefuls.View image in fullscreenDaniel Ortega last year promoted his wife, then vice-president, to the position of ‘co-president’.Photograph: Cesar Perez/El 19 DIGITAL/AFP/Getty ImagesBreda said: “Ortega and Murillo are evidently afraid of the idea that the slightest political opening could create the conditions for dissent to manifest and threaten their grip on power, possibly suggesting that their already feeble domestic support is further falling.“Therefore, instead of staging a rigged election, they have opted to eliminate electoral competition altogether.”Last year, Ortega pushed through a series of constitutional reforms that included extending the presidential term from five to six years and promoting his wife, then vice-president, to the position of “co-president”. The USrefersto their administration as the “Murillo-Ortega dictatorship”.During his speech, the Nicaraguan president criticised the US over the January operation that abducted the Venezuelan dictator Nicolás Maduro: “With all their power, they stormed the presidential palace, where Nicolás and his wife, Cilia [Flores], were. They killed the entire security detail there – most of them were Cubans – and took them away to imprison them in the US. It is monstrous.”The president said preventing the opposition from returning to power was necessary to protect the country from the “coup plotters” who, he claimed, had been behind the nationwideprotests of 2018– when, according to organisations including the UN, his regime carried out a violent crackdown that killed more than 350 people, wounded hundreds, jailed thousands and forced nearly 1 million Nicaraguans into exile.Recently, the UN, the US, human rights organisations and 30 former Ibero-American presidents have directly blamed Ortega and Murillo for the death of the Indigenous leader and four-term congressmanBrooklyn Rivera, 73, who spent nearly three years “unjustly imprisoned” by the regime, the US state department said.According to organisations including Human Rights Watch, there are about 50 political prisoners in the country. The regime has stripped 546 people of their citizenship and shut down more than 5,600 non-governmental organisations, 29 universities and 58 media outlets.Explore more on these topicsNicaraguaAmericasDaniel OrteganewsShareReuse this content

title: Mexican drug lord ‘El Mayo’ sentenced to life in prison in US federal court | US crime | The Guardian
description: Case against Ismael Zambada García, who helped co-found and lead the violent Sinaloa cartel, unfolded in New York
Author: José Olivares
Headline: Case against Ismael Zambada García, who helped co-found and lead the violent Sinaloa cartel, unfolded in New York
Content: A reward sign for former Sinaloa cartel leader Ismael ‘El Mayo’ Zambada García is posted next to an aircraft on 9 July 2026.Photograph: Paul Ratje/ReutersView image in fullscreenA reward sign for former Sinaloa cartel leader Ismael ‘El Mayo’ Zambada García is posted next to an aircraft on 9 July 2026.Photograph: Paul Ratje/ReutersUS crimeMexican drug lord ‘El Mayo’ sentenced to life in prison in US federal courtCase against Ismael Zambada García, who helped co-found and lead the violent Sinaloa cartel, unfolded in New YorkJosé Olivaresin New YorkMon 20 Jul 2026 11.58 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.39 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheMexicandrug kingpin, Ismael Zambada García, known as “El Mayo”, was sentenced in a US federal court on Monday to life in prison, after a plea deal last year in which he admitted ⁠to shipping vast amounts of cocaine and other substances as leader of the violent Sinaloa cartel.Zambada is 76 and one of the most high-profile Mexican ⁠drug traffickers to be sentenced in a US court since his fellow Sinaloa leader Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán ​wassentenced tolife in prison in ‌2019. The case unfolded in federal court in Brooklyn,New York.Zambada helped co-found and lead the cartel, becoming one of the most wanted targets by US intelligence and law enforcement agencies for decades.He signed a plea deal last August, admitting charges including racketeering conspiracy and continuing criminal enterprise, after the ‌US justice department said it would not seek the death penalty. The court also ordered him to forfeit $15bn from drug trade profits as part of his punishment.As one of the co-founders of the Sinaloa cartel, El Mayo is one of the biggest drug traffickers in the history of the US-Mexico drug war. He was accused of trafficking tons of cocaine, marijuana, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl to the US and other parts of the world.Prosecutors also accused him of being one of the leaders of a violent organization that led to corruption among Mexican politicians, military officials and law enforcement agents.Zambada, who has spent nearly two years inside a Brooklyn federal prison, limped inside the courtroom. He was wearing a beige prison outfit and had white hair and a beard.Before his sentencing, Zambada read a public letter, saying that he took responsibility for his crimes and calling for an end to the violence that has plagued Mexico for decades during the drug-fueled conflict.“I did not go to trial because I did not want to deny the truth,” Zambada read aloud in court, adding that “violence must end” and urging the next generation of drug traffickers to “choose a different path”.While El Chapo is better known as the face of the Sinaloa cartel, the elderly El Mayo was regarded as the elusive, behind the scenes leader running the operations, with a low profile and few public sightings.El Mayo’s arrest in the US in 2024 came as a surprise. During a mysterious operation, he was known to have been beaten and kidnapped by one of El Chapo’s sons, Joaquín Guzmán López, and was flown across the US border to New Mexico, where he was arrested by US federal agents.It remains unknown whether US agencies helped facilitate the cross-border kidnapping operation that led to his arrest. Guzmán López pled guilty in an abduction case late last year and has become a cooperating witness for the US justice department.El Mayo’s arrest led to a splintering of the Sinaloa cartel, with El Mayo’s son and El Chapo’s sons leading different factions, in a raging intra-cartel battle for territory that has ravaged parts of Mexico.El Mayo’s sentencing comes at a tense time in US-Mexico relations, as theTrump administrationhas escalated its pressure on the Mexican government to crack down harder on criminal groups. Last year, the Trump administration designated the Sinaloa cartel as a terrorist group and has threatened US military intervention to attack the cartel and other drug-trafficking organizations.US district judge Brian Cogan, who also presided over El Chapo’s trial, has been hearing the case inNew York.Cogan acknowledged that the sentencing hearing was “anticlimactic” because of the plea agreement. All parties involved understood that Zambada would spend the rest of his life in prison, the judge said.Frank Perez, a lawyer for El Mayo, ​had said in a 6 July court filing that his client was fully aware he would receive a life sentence when he decided to accept responsibility for his crimes. Perez asked Cogan to recommend to the US Bureau of Prisons that his client, as a septuagenarian, be placed in a ⁠facility where he would receive adequate medical care.Prosecutors with the Brooklyn US attorney’s office said the prisons ​suggested by Perez may not ​be equipped to handle the security concerns posed by Zambada, including the ​possibility that he could ‌continue to direct ​cartel activities from ​behind bars.“Those security concerns will continue for the foreseeable future because individuals loyal to the defendant continue to control the cartel,” the prosecutors wrote in a 13 July filing.Zambada’s defense attorneys on Monday requested that the judge recommend a federal medical facility, rather than a traditional prison, due to his medical conditions. Health details are redacted in court records, but the judge mentioned during the sentencing hearing that Zambada is “drifting” towards being “cognitively impaired”.Mexican outlets have reported Zambada is dealing with the early stages of dementia. The Bureau of Prisons still has to evaluate where Zambada will be sent to serve the rest of his days.El Chapo is serving his life sentence at a maximum-security prison in Colorado.Frank Perez, one of Zambada’s attorneys, said during the sentencing hearing that his client did not cooperate with the US government, neither providing information to US officials about other cartel members or leaders, nor Mexican officials who have protected the cartel’s drug-trafficking business.Reuters contributed reportingExplore more on these topicsUS crimeNew YorkMexicoDrugsDrugs tradeDrugs policyTrump administrationnewsShareReuse this content

title: Canada issues air quality warnings over US wildfire smoke after Trump tariff threat | Wildfires | The Guardian
description: Officials say smoke from US fires has crossed border days after US president accused Canada of improper fire management
Author: Olivia Bowden
Headline: Officials say smoke from US fires has crossed border days after US president accused Canada of improper fire management
Content: Wildfires have already had an impact on air quality in Toronto.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenWildfires have already had an impact on air quality in Toronto.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesWildfiresCanada issues air quality warnings over US wildfire smoke after Trump tariff threatOfficials say smoke from US fires has crossed border days after US president accused Canada of improper fire managementOlivia Bowdenin TorontoMon 20 Jul 2026 11.52 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfter days of acrid smoke from northern wildfires choking major US cities and inspiring Donald Trump to threatenCanadawith tariffs, officials now say the reverse is also happening as parts of Canada issue air quality warnings caused by American fires to the south.Officials in the Canadian province of British Columbia warned in an alert on Saturday evening that smoke from US fires, spurred on by thousands of lightning strikes in the north-east, was creating smoky conditions as polluted air drifted north.“Much of this smoke is originating from fires south of the Canada-US border in Washington and Oregon,” the provincial wildfire service said.Twenty-two large fires are burning across Washington and Oregon, according to the Northwest Interagency Coordination Center, the fire coordination agency for both states. British Columbia has air quality warnings in place for two regions, Kootenay Lake and Cranbrook, located along the south-east border with the US.Despite the US facing its own wildfire issues, Donald Trump and his fellow Republicans have chosen to accuse Canada of improper wildfire management and have threatened to hit Canadian officials with sanctions.Republicans threaten Canada with sanctions over drifting wildfire smokeRead moreIn a social media post on Friday, Trump wrote the US had been “invaded” by dirty air. “[The] ‌cost of this pollution must of necessity be added ⁠to the TARIFFS Canada is currently paying,” he said. TheTrump administrationhas taken a sledgehammer to several climate protections.Wildfires continue to blaze across the US and Canada. According to the Canadian government, as of Monday there were 843 active wildfires burning across Canada. Of those 181 are considered “out of control”, meaning the fires are spreading and not responding to suppressive actions. Another 532 of those fires are also considered “out of control” but suppressive actions have not yet been taken.The Canadian prime minister, Mark Carney, who sat alongside Trump at the World Cup final on Sunday, where the US president again suggested slapping Canada with tariffs, has sidestepped the criticisms. “Fighting climate change is the responsibility of all countries, including the United States,” he told reporters last week.But Doug Ford, the premier of Ontario, where many fires are burning, said on Friday: “If there’s politicians out there chirping away, well maybe what you should do rather than complain is send support, send help, because we have done the exact same thing for our American friends.”Eleanor Olszewski, Canada’s minister of emergency preparedness, said in a statement that Canada had invested C$12bn (£6.36bn) on fire prevention and forest sustainability over the past five years. “This is a challenge that knows no borders,” she said.Explore more on these topicsWildfiresCanadaTrump administrationAmericasnewsShareReuse this content

title: Australia spent more than $9m for each asylum seeker held on Nauru last year – so why can’t they afford to buy food? | Nauru | The Guardian
description: Many of the more than 100 people awaiting processing on the tiny Pacific island are skipping meals to make ends meet, a new survey has found
Author: Sarah Basford Canales
Headline: Many of the more than 100 people awaiting processing on the tiny Pacific island are skipping meals to make ends meet, a new survey has found
Content: The Budapest hotel houses some of the asylum seekers and refugees sent to Nauru while they await processing. The ASRC survey includes reports of substandard accommodation facilities.Photograph: SuppliedView image in fullscreenThe Budapest hotel houses some of the asylum seekers and refugees sent to Nauru while they await processing. The ASRC survey includes reports of substandard accommodation facilities.Photograph: SuppliedNauruAustralia spent more than $9m for each asylum seeker held on Nauru last year – so why can’t they afford to buy food?Many of the more than 100 people awaiting processing on the tiny Pacific island are skipping meals to make ends meet, a new survey has foundFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastSarah Basford CanalesMon 20 Jul 2026 00.43 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 01.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMost refugees and asylum seekers held in Nauru’s detention centres say they cannot afford to buy enough food or access clean drinking water, despite the Australian government spending the equivalent of more than $9m per person last financial year.The group of more than 100 people have been sent indefinitely to the tiny Pacific island while they wait for their asylum claims to be processed. Many of the cohort say they are skipping meals to make ends meet, according toa new surveyby the Asylum Seeker Resource Centre (ASRC) advocacy group.‘What is our future?’: the Nauru detention centre was empty. Now 100 asylum seekers are held thereRead moreRakib*, who has been on Nauru for more than two years without the ability to work, feels he is at breaking point.“I think it would be better if they kill us, or if we die,” says the man in his 20s, whom Guardian Australia has chosen not to identify for his safety. “Because, you know, like when you die, you get peace.”Prohibitive costsOf the 78 people ASRC surveyed between February and April, all but one said the Australian government’s $260 fortnightly payment, intended to cover food, drinking water, supplies, phone credit and internet service, was not enough to keep them from going hungry. (The minimum 28GBmonthly data planwith the country’s main internet and mobile provider, Digicel, costs $112, or about 20% of the monthly allowance provided.)Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailNauru’s population of about 13,000 is heavily reliant on imported goods. More than 90% of food consumed is imported and prices on the island can fluctuate depending on availability and trade disruptions. Vegetables such as tomatoes, capsicums and onions cost about $18 a kilo – up to three times as much as similar goods in Australia.Rakib has been on Nauru for 2.5 years after attempting to get to Australia by boat. He says the situation has worsened in recent months: the cost of a single banana has risen from $1.50 to about $4.He says there has also recently been shortage of rice, which is an affordable staple for many Nauruans and asylum seekers on the island.“We have been told that it might take two to three months for the next ship with rice to arrive, so we don’t know how will we survive in the meantime,” Rakib says through a translator.View image in fullscreenJana Favero, the ASRC’s deputy chief executive, saidthe new report’s findings showed the offshore detention system was ‘completely broken’.Photograph: SuppliedThe ASRC survey also includes reports of substandard accommodation facilities.The island’s group of refugees and asylum seekers mostly come from China, Bangladesh and Pakistan, and attempted to arrive in Australia by boat after 2023. Under a longstanding bipartisan government policy, they will never be permanently resettled in Australia, even if they are found to be fleeing persecution.If they are found to be refugees, they must wait for Australia to strike a deal with another country to take them in. While the offshore processing centrewas empty in mid-2023, a stream of arrivals since has meant about 100 or so people remain on the island in limbo.The Australian governmentspent $971.6m on offshore managementfor unauthorised maritime arrivals in the 2025-26 financial year, according to a home affairs portfolio budget statement publicly released in May. The figure equates to approximately $9.6m per person.The US private prisons operator, MTC Australia, willreceive $791.2m over five yearsto operate the offshore processing centre on Nauru, while a further $106.5m over three years will be provided to International Health and Medical Services to offer medical services to people awaiting processing.Those awaiting a refugee determination are not permitted to work, so can only rely on the government’s stipend. In December 2025, the government raised the fortnightly stipend from $230 to $260.View image in fullscreenAlmost all the people surveyed between February and April said the Australian government’s $260 fortnightly payment was not enough to keep them from going hungry on Nauru.Photograph: SuppliedOne asylum seeker quoted in the ASRC’s report claimed some people in the group had resorted to fishing to supplement their meals but their efforts were squandered by theft.“We bought a fishing net to try and supplement the food but our nets were stolen and broken three times. We had put the net outside due to the smell, and local Nauruans came and stole it,” the person claimed.“We planted fruit but unfortunately, all the fruit was stolen. Even my shoes got stolen.”‘We just want a peaceful life’While access to any food remains an issue for the group due to supply and affordability, safe drinking water is also a challenge on the island.The ASRC’s report notes that the island has no rivers or freshwater lakes and relies primarily on rainwater harvesting and reverse osmosis desalination. It found 82% of the survey’s respondents said they did not have access to clean drinking water.Rakib says his accommodation has a rainwater tank, which is used for showering, cooking and drinking. To safely consume the water, it must be boiled and stored in containers.“The water tank was not clean for the last two and a half years,” Rakib says.Australia refuses to say how many Chinese nationals are arriving by boat, saying it may damage bilateral relationsRead more“As we don’t have money, we cannot buy good-quality water.”The ASRC’s deputy chief executive, Jana Favero, saysthe new report’s findings show the offshore detention system is “completely broken”.“Even the most basic standards of safety and human dignity are not being met, and there is no excuse,” she says.“We know we are responsible and that this system destroys people’s physical and mental health, and now we have evidence that people are also being deprived of enough food to eat.”A home affairs spokesperson said the management of individuals, including their refugee status determination, is Nauru’s responsibility.The spokesperson said allowance payments are “reviewed regularly to ensure it remains fit for purpose” and are “based on the local cost of living and is designed to promote self-sufficiency while the transferees are living in the community”.“People transferred and accommodated under regional processing arrangements are treated with respect and dignity and in accordance with human rights standards,” the spokesperson said.*Name/s have been changedExplore more on these topicsNauruAustralian politicsAustralian immigration and asylumAsia PacificnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘One in 15 million’: Brisbane woman gives birth to identical quadruplets | Brisbane | The Guardian
description: Jenitar Na’amoana’s already had four children when she learned of surprise pregnancy ‘unheard of’ in Australia
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Jenitar Na’amoana’s already had four children when she learned of surprise pregnancy ‘unheard of’ in Australia
Content: The quadruplets were born at the Royal Brisbane and Women's hospital on 14 July.Photograph: Dave Hunt/AAPView image in fullscreenThe quadruplets were born at the Royal Brisbane and Women's hospital on 14 July.Photograph: Dave Hunt/AAPBrisbane‘One in 15 million’: Brisbane woman gives birth to identical quadrupletsJenitar Na’amoana’s already had four children when she learned of surprise pregnancy ‘unheard of’ in AustraliaFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastAustralian Associated PressMon 20 Jul 2026 15.09 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 17.43 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA 34-year-old woman fromBrisbanehas defied the odds and delivered four naturally conceived, identical quadruplets in what is believed to be an Australian first.Jenitar Na’amoana, who already had four children, was not planning having another but the quadruplets emerged from a “one in 15 million” pregnancy, splitting into four from a single fertilised egg.Na’amoana duly became a mother of eight after delivering her girls by C-section at just over 28 weeks at the Royal Brisbane and Women’s hospital surrounded by a huge support team.“This is unheard of ... we don’t think it has ever happened in Australia,” Alexa Bendall, a maternal foetal medicine specialist and obstetrician at the hospital, said.“There may have been a couple of cases elsewhere in the world and when I say a couple, literally two.”Na’amoana was still breastfeeding the youngest of her four children when she was told she had again fallen pregnant.They got the surprise of their lives when told their “little miracle” would be a medical marvel.“When they went along and found out they had four babies instead of one they were quite shocked,” Bendall said.There are now four more childre in the Na’amoana household after the 14 July arrival of Emily, Harriet, Catherine and Alexa – the latter named after Dr Bendall.The obstetrician has been caring for Na’amoana since she first discovered the surprise news of her bundles of joy.“They are very special. We are very excited to be able to care for this amazing little family, well big family now,” Bendall said.“One is named after me which is such a beautiful tribute.”The pregnancy was considered very high risk but Ms Na’amoana had “taken everything in her stride from day one”, Bendall said.“We have never seen a spontaneously conceived pregnancy of this type with quads, and she has somehow dodged every complication and risk for both herself and her babies to be here today, with four gorgeous newborn girls.”The girls are expected to spend an extended period in the hospital before being allowed home but their long-term outlook looks really good, Bendall said.The family have set up a GoFundMe fundraiser to help care for the girls with money going towards medical, travel and everyday living expenses as well as a van “with at least 10 or 12 seats”.Explore more on these topicsBrisbanenewsShareReuse this content

title: Antibiotic-resistant bacteria increasingly leaving children vulnerable to common infections, global study finds | Health | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Research spanning 82 countries finds antibiotic resistance increased in every region between 2004 and 2022
Author: Melissa Davey
Headline: Exclusive: Research spanning 82 countries finds antibiotic resistance increased in every region between 2004 and 2022
Content: Frequent antibiotic use is often a medically necessary, life-saving treatment for patients such as Harry Booth but using the same antibiotic too much can also make bacteria resistant to it.Photograph: suppliedView image in fullscreenFrequent antibiotic use is often a medically necessary, life-saving treatment for patients such as Harry Booth but using the same antibiotic too much can also make bacteria resistant to it.Photograph: suppliedHealthAntibiotic-resistant bacteria increasingly leaving children vulnerable to common infections, global study findsExclusive:Research spanning 82 countries finds antibiotic resistance increased in every region between 2004 and 2022Melissa DaveyMedical editorMon 20 Jul 2026 12.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 19.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHarry Booth has had a rough run through Australia’s coldest months. The 13-year-old has been in and out of hospital with a range of common chest and urinary tract infections that his doctors found challenging to treat.Harry was born with kidney failure and began dialysis at six months old, before receiving a kidney transplant from his father at age four. The surgery saved his life but comes with a trade-off: he now needs to take medications that suppress his immune system to prevent his body from rejecting the kidney.It makes him morevulnerable to infections – and the bacteria which cause these are resistant to several commonly used antibiotics.Instead, doctors reach for different, less commonly used drugs and administer them intravenously for several days, before sometimes giving him oral antibiotics. Once, he required a procedure to clean the bacteria from his bladder.The treatments are often demanding and take him away from home and school.Frequent antibiotic use is often a medically necessary, life-saving treatment for patients like Harrybut using the same antibiotic too much can also make bacteria resistantto it.‘Alarming’ rates of babies with antibiotic-resistant bugs in Asia-Pacific, Australian study findsRead moreHis mother, Eileen, says while back-up treatments are working, she worries about what will happen if her son’s infections become resistant to those drugs, too. As it stands, they are carefully treated only if they meet certain symptom and bacteria thresholds.Harry is not an outlier.Life-saving drugs used to treat common childhood infections are increasingly failing and the resistance of bacteria to these medicines will only worsen over the next decade,a global analysis has found.Led by Murdoch Children’s Research Institute (MCRI) in collaboration with the University of Sydney in Australia, researchers analysed 106,581 infection samples from children aged up to 18 years old across 82 countries.They found antibiotic resistance – when bacteria are no longer killed by antimicrobial medicines – increased in every region between 2004 and 2022.Babies and children in intensive care, and countries with fewer healthcare resources, are most affected, according to the findings published in theJournal of the American Medical Associationon Tuesday.Paper co-author and MCRI associate professor Penelope Bryant, who is also one of Harry’s doctors, said severe infections such as sepsis and pneumonia are driving the increase.These are often caused byAcinetobacter baumannii, a bacteria with a tough double-membrane and the highest level of drug resistance, according to the findings.The study foundanother bacteria,Klebsiella, a common cause of urinary tract infections and liver abscesses, recorded the fastest increase in resistance, particularly in south-east Asia, eastern Europe and the western Pacific.The researchers also used statistical modelling and found resistance to last-line antibiotics is projected to rise substantially by 2035, reaching 82% and 35% forAcinetobacter baumanniiandKlebsiellarespectively.“We are finding antibiotic resistance not just in severe diseases but in more simple, uncomplicated infections such as urinary tract infections in children,” Bryant said.Despite antimicrobial resistance being one of the biggest threats to children’s health globally, Bryant said children had been largely overlooked in global antibiotic resistance surveillance and research, resulting in a lack of child-friendly antibiotic formulations or clear child-dosing guidelines.This is because there is sometimes a reluctance to run trials on children and developing treatments for them can be less lucrative for pharmaceutical companies, she said.Children are also especially vulnerable as they experience high rates of bacterial infections and have fewer antibiotic options than adults.To better understand antibiotic resistance by country, bacteria and type of drug, Bryant and her colleagues launched a website calledAMR in Kids, providing region and pathogen-specific forecasts to 2035. They hope it will help identify emerging threats, inform treatment decisions and help guide planning and research.A paediatrician and incoming president of the Australasian Society for Infectious Diseases, Prof Chris Blyth, described the study and website as “really important work”.Australia’s overuse of antibiotics found to be driving rate of drug-resistant infectionsRead more“It highlights the size of the problem and that the problem is not equally spread globally,” he said.In low-income countries, antibiotic resistance is being driven by unregulated antibiotic use and poor sanitation, making better access to diagnostic techniques and first-line antibiotics a priority. In high-income countries, antibiotic overuse across farming, veterinarian care and healthcare is fuelling drug resistance.Treatments that do not work for children with resistant infections are rare in high-income countries but in low-income countries drug-resistant infections are one of the highest causes of death in children under five.The WorldHealthOrganization classifies antibiotics into three groups to help prevent antimicrobial resistance: “access” antibiotics are more targeted, first-line treatments; “watch” drugs are used more broadly but carry higher resistance risks; and “reserve” are last-resort drugs for treating the most resistant infections.The study found while first-line access resistance is declining or stabilising in high-income regions, lower-resource settings are seeing a sharp rise in resistance to critical watch and reserve drugs.“It highlights the real need to have global surveillance systems in place, both in Australia and internationally, to track and identify resistance,” Blyth said.“I’m frequently seeing children with difficult-to-treat infections that are resistant to first and second-line agents [drugs], where I’m having to reach for agents that are more difficult to get, more difficult to give, more expensive, and potentially more toxic.”The Booth family hopes that resources such as the AMR in Kids website will mean children like Harry are better protected.“People are already starting to understand the long-term effects of antibiotics,” Eileen Booth said, “but there is a long way to go with education around resistance.”Explore more on these topicsHealthAntibioticsDrugsMedical researchnewsShareReuse this content

title: Australian ski resorts smash July climate records with temperatures 12C above average  | Australia weather | The Guardian
description: Resorts in New South Wales and Victoria face a warm and dry start to the season with only sporadic falls of natural snow in many alpine areas
Author: Graham Readfearn
Headline: Resorts in New South Wales and Victoria face a warm and dry start to the season with only sporadic falls of natural snow in many alpine areas
Content: A view from the Falls Creek snowcam on Monday shows a lack of snow after a record was set for the hottest daytime and night-time July temperatures on the weekend.Photograph: Falls CreekView image in fullscreenA view from the Falls Creek snowcam on Monday shows a lack of snow after a record was set for the hottest daytime and night-time July temperatures on the weekend.Photograph: Falls CreekAustralia weatherAustralian ski resorts smash July climate records with temperatures 12C above averageResorts in New South Wales and Victoria face a warm and dry start to the season with only sporadic falls of natural snow in many alpine areasFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastGraham ReadfearnEnvironment and climate correspondentMon 20 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 17.43 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAlpine ski resorts inNew South Walesand Victoria saw climate records shattered at the weekend with maximum temperatures up to 12C above average.Ski areas of Mount Hotham and Falls Creek set July records for their hottest daytime and night-time temperatures and Thredbo also set a new hottest July day, according to the Bureau of Meteorology.The records come aftera challenging start for Australia’s ski seasonwith dry and warm temperatures seeing only sporadic falls of natural snow in many alpine areas, leaving resorts to rely mostly on snow-making technology.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailThe El Niño now in placein the tropical Pacific and the bureau’s long-range forecast both pointed to generally drier and warmer conditions for alpine regions this winter – a “bad combination” for snow.Climate scientists have long warned that global heating will create difficult conditions for Australia’s ski resorts.‘We are waiting with bated breath’: experts alarmed as BoM says gathering El Niño could be strongest on recordRead moreAngus Hines, a senior forecaster at the bureau, said a slow-moving high pressure system – characterised by clear skies and descending dry air – had been a key to the spiking temperatures.He said this had also caused a “temperature inversion” where it was cooler closer to sea level than it was at higher altitudes.“These temperatures were a substantial departure from the norm and even from previous bursts of heat for July,” he said.“This highlights the likely issues we will have with ski seasons into the future with a warming climate.”He said warmer than usual temperatures would remain in ski areas until about Wednesday and there was a chance of a snow flurry at the weekend, before temperatures were expected to climb again early next week.View image in fullscreenSnow melts at Mount Buffalo on the weekend.Photograph: Marni Cordell/The GuardianAt Mount Hotham in Victoria’s alpine country, where the official temperature record goes back 36 years, the previous hottest July day was 8.7C in 1994.But that record has been broken three times this month. The mercury peaked at 12C on Sunday in a month when theaverage daily maximum temperature is usually zero.At Falls Creek, the highest July temperature had been 9.8C recorded in 1992. But this was eclipsed with 11.5C recorded on Saturday and then 13C on Sunday. The average daily maximum for Falls Creek in July is 1.2C.New daily temperature records in alpine regions this weekend were also set in Thredbo (14C), Cabramurra (14.3C) and Mount William (15.2C).Record warm night-time temperatures were set in Mount Buller (5.7C), Mount Baw Baw (12C), Mount William (9.4C), Mount Hotham (4.6C) and Falls Creek (also 4.6C).skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotiongraph showing declining snow depth in the Snowy MountainsDr Andrew Watkins, a climate scientist at the Climate Council, skied at Mount Hotham on Friday when temperatures were already threatening the previous 1994 record.“It was incredibly warm up there,” he said.“You do not expect to see temperature records that are more than 30 years old being broken by two or three degrees. It is quite phenomenal.“I don’t think any climatologist is surprised by events like this any more. It is shocking in its own way.”Laura Gray, from Bright in Victoria, skied Mount Hotham and Falls Creek on the weekend and said she saw people skiing in T-shirts and without gloves.“It did feel pretty bizarre and I had to keep reminding myself that it was still mid-July and not the spring,” she said.“In the morning it was lovely to ski but once the sun got high enough on the slopes it turned slushy very quickly.”She said the ski season, which officially started in early June around the King’s birthday long weekend, had been “pretty grim” so far.“It is not unusual not to ski on opening weekend but it’s been pretty dire – just pretty dry and warm. Without snow-making, we absolutely would not have been skiing at all.“When we heard there was going to be an El Niño, that put us on edge but I don’t think anyone could have expected these July temperatures.”The Guardian has approached Snow Resorts Australia for comment.Explore more on these topicsAustralia weatherNew South WalesVictoriaClimate crisisnewsShareReuse this content

title: Hungary’s chess queen declines Péter Magyar’s offer of role as president | Péter Magyar | The Guardian
description: Judit Polgár says she does not ‘feel enough strength within me’ to take on the responsibility of uniting a divided nation
Author: Ashifa Kassam
Headline: Judit Polgár says she does not ‘feel enough strength within me’ to take on the responsibility of uniting a divided nation
Content: Judit Polgár is interviewed in 2017. She became the youngest grandmaster in the world at the age of 15.Photograph: Peter Kohalmi/AFP/GettyView image in fullscreenJudit Polgár is interviewed in 2017. She became the youngest grandmaster in the world at the age of 15.Photograph: Peter Kohalmi/AFP/GettyPéter MagyarHungary’s chess queen declines Péter Magyar’s offer of role as presidentJudit Polgár says she does not ‘feel enough strength within me’ to take on the responsibility of uniting a divided nationAshifa Kassamand agencyMon 20 Jul 2026 14.44 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Hungarian chess ⁠legend Judit Polgár has said she will not accept the nomination for president, only a day after the ⁠prime minister,Péter Magyar, ⁠said he ​would ask her to take up the largely ceremonial role.Magyar had said he would meet Polgár on Monday to discuss the matter. “Our country needs unity, peace and a president that all Hungarians can be proud of,” he wrote on social media. “Judit Polgár’s name has been synonymous with talent and perseverance for decades.”Polgár, 49, said later in a Facebook post that she was grateful to ‌be asked but added: “I do not feel enough strength within me to take on the historic responsibility of uniting a divided nation, ​so I am unable to accept the request.”Polgár – the subject of the documentaryQueen of Chess, released in February – is the only female player to have been ranked in the world’s top 10 of the male-dominated sport.She is ⁠the only woman to have crossed the rating of 2700, the ​threshold that many ​consider the definition of a super grandmaster.View image in fullscreenJudit Polgár aged 12. She was the top-ranked female player for 25 years.Photograph: Yves Forestier/Sygma/GettyThe latest developments come after amonths-long standoffbetween the newly elected Magyar and Tamás Sulyok, the president appointed during the former leader Viktor Orbán’s time in power.Since hislandslide victoryin April, Magyar has repeatedly called on Sulyok to step down, accusing him of being a puppet who had failed to stand up for the country as Orbán’s rightwing, nationalist Fidesz party was criticised for weakening democratic institutions and undermining the rule of law.Hungary’s parliament – where Magyar’s centre-right Tisza party holds a two-thirds majority – voted last week to back a constitutional amendment that included animmediate end to Sulyok’s term. From there, Sulyok had five days to sign off on the amendment.The manoeuvre was criticised by several rights groups in Hungary, including Human Rights Watch,which saidthe rushed constitutional changes to oust the president were “reminiscent of [the] Fidesz era”.Magyar, however, has insisted that the changes are needed as part of a wider push to loosen Orbán’s lingering grip on the country.As news emerged that Sulyok had signed the legislation,bringing an endto his time in office, Magyar proposed nominating Polgár as his replacement. The idea was first floated in an open letter signed by 16 prominent Hungarians, including Ernő Rubik, theinventor of the Rubik’s Cube.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBorn in Budapest, Polgár, who will turn 50 in a few days, had been the top-ranked female player for 25 years when she retired in 2014. She became the youngest grandmaster in the world at the age of 15,breaking the recordset in 1958 by Bobby Fischer.She won victories against several male world champions including Garry Kasparov in 2002.Since being swept into power on the promise of “regime change”, Magyar and his party have moved swiftly to dismantle the bastions of Orbán’s 16 years in power, fromsuspending news broadcastsby public media outlets with ties to the former prime minister toabolishingthe sovereignty protection office, which had allowed intelligence services to access information on individuals and organisations without judicial oversight.Additional reporting from ReutersExplore more on these topicsPéter MagyarHungaryChessEuropeWomennewsShareReuse this content

title: Ukraine’s army chief expected to lose job after week of protests | Europe | The Guardian
description: Volodymyr Zelenskyy’s chief of staff hints government may be about to sack Oleksandr Syrskyi
Author: Luke Harding
Headline: Volodymyr Zelenskyy’s chief of staff hints government may be about to sack Oleksandr Syrskyi
Content: Thousands of demonstrators have called for commander in chief Oleksandr Syrskyi to be removed from his post.Photograph: Valentyn Ogirenko/ReutersView image in fullscreenThousands of demonstrators have called for commander in chief Oleksandr Syrskyi to be removed from his post.Photograph: Valentyn Ogirenko/ReutersEuropeUkraine’s army chief expected to lose job after week of protestsVolodymyr Zelenskyy’s chief of staff hints government may be about to sack Oleksandr SyrskyiLuke Hardingin KyivMon 20 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleUkraine’s commander in chief Oleksandr Syrskyi is widely expected to lose his job amid signs thatVolodymyr Zelenskyyhas bowed to pressure from protesters and will reshuffle the army’s top leadership.Over the past five days, thousands of demonstrators have gathered outside the presidential office in Kyiv, chanting: “Syrskyi out.” They have demanded Zelenskyy reinstate Mykhailo Fedorov, who was fired as defence minister last week after a clash with Syrskyi and the general staff.Aged 35 and a moderniser, Fedorov is widely credited with improving the country’s position on the battlefield during the six months he was in the job.In a newspaper column published on Monday, Syrskyi said he was “surprised to learn the minister and I had a conflict”.“I perceived our relationship as a working one: with complex issues, with different positions. This is how it should be between two institutions during a major war,” he wrote.He offered a personal apology, saying that if he had offended Fedorov, he was “sorry”. The general added: “I can be harsh. But I ask you, and everyone who is following this story with enthusiasm: let’s focus not on the personal, but on the global. On victory.”Syrskyi also insisted it was wrong to accuse him of a “lack of strategy” and said he was at war with Moscow, not the defence ministry in Kyiv. “My job is war. Strategy, the frontline, the people. That is what I do and will continue to do,” he said.Ukraine’s general staff denied media reports on Monday that Syrskyi was about to be sacked. It said Syrskyi and his deputy, Andriy Hnatov, continued to carry out their duties. “Dismissal from these posts takes place on the basis of a decree by the president ofUkraine,” it added.View image in fullscreenProtests began in Kyiv last week after Volodymyr Zelenskyy sacked Ukraine’s young and popular defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov.Photograph: Roman Pilipey/AFP/Getty ImagesDespite these denials, Zelenskyy’s influential chief of staff, Kyrylo Budanov, hinted that the government had listened to the popular mood. “The public’s position has been heard,” he said. The situation was being resolved “professionally and constructively”, he added, urging “everyone to keep calm”.“The enemy is closely watching every one of our internal disputes and waiting for us to start fighting among ourselves … work is under way. Results will follow,” he said.The noisy protests began last Thursday. Several demonstrators waved cheese graters – a pun on the commander’s name, which sounds like cheese in Ukrainian.Zelenskyy confirmed last week that the relationship between Fedorov and Syrskyi had broken down. After his firing, Fedorov said Ukraine’s top brass had blocked reforms with “bureaucratic impediments” and ignored data, instead rewarding brigades that were loyal to Syrskyi personally.View image in fullscreenMykhailo Fedorov was dismissed from his post as defence minister last week.Photograph: Global Images Ukraine/Getty ImagesOne source in the armed forces confirmed Syrskyi’s imminent departure. “His destiny is decided. The question is who will replace him. There are a few important requirements. The person should be known and popular so everyone applauds,” they said.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“But they should also not have open political ambitions so as not to create a new rival. It should be an experienced military guy who listens to the president and does what he wants to do,” the source added.What is Ukraine’s 40-day campaign against Russia and has it worked?Read moreOn Monday, Zelenskyy held meetings with several possible replacements. The senior commanders included Mykhailo Drapatiy, the head of Ukraine’s joint armed forces. Drapatiy cemented his reputation by driving a tank through a pro-Russian blockade in 2014, whenRussiafirst tried to seize the eastern city of Mariupol.The meetings with top military figures were attended byYevheniy Khmara, whom Zelenskyy appointed as defence minister. It is unclear if Fedorov will be reinstated – a key demand from demonstrators – or brought back into government in another role.Protest organisers say that Zelenskyy has a deadline of Friday to give Fedorov his old job back. If this doesn’t happen they promise a “general indefinite protest” in towns and cities across the country.Zelenskyy praised recent long-range Ukrainian strikes on targets in Russia. “They have produced results against logistics facilities and an oil depot. The targets were more than 400km from our border. Two shadow fleet tankers and four cargo ships were also struck in the Black Sea,” he said.“We are responding, justly, to every Russian strike against our cities and communities. This war must be brought to an end, and that is only possible by increasing the pressure on the sole cause of this war – Russia,” Zelenskyy added.Explore more on these topicsEuropeVolodymyr ZelenskyyUkraineRussianewsShareReuse this content

title: Russian attack on Odesa kills three after Ukraine targets Moscow with drones overnight – as it happened | World news | The Guardian
description: Moscow targets southern city after the Russian capital was hit by 400 drones in response to one of the war’s biggest attacks on Kyiv at the weekend
Author: Jakub Krupa
Headline: Moscow targets southern city after the Russian capital was hit by 400 drones in response to one of the war’s biggest attacks on Kyiv at the weekend
Content: 09.33 EDTRussian attack on Odesa facility kills three, injures three moreMeanwhile,a Russian ⁠attack ⁠on ​an infrastructure facility ⁠in Ukraine’s ⁠southern ​city ‌of ‌Odesa on Monday ‌killed at least three ‌people and injured ​three others,⁠a city official ​said, as reported by Reuters.Share

title: AliExpress fined record €550m by EU for failing to stop sale of illegal and fake goods | Retail industry | The Guardian
description: Chinese retail platform handed penalty over listings for items such as counterfeit products and unsafe toys
Author: Lisa O’Carroll
Headline: Chinese retail platform handed penalty over listings for items such as counterfeit products and unsafe toys
Content: AliExpress is the largest online retail operator in the EU with 193 million users.Photograph: Sheldon Cooper/SOPA Images/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenAliExpress is the largest online retail operator in the EU with 193 million users.Photograph: Sheldon Cooper/SOPA Images/ShutterstockRetail industryAliExpress fined record €550m by EU for failing to stop sale of illegal and fake goodsChinese retail platform handed penalty over listings for items such as counterfeit products and unsafe toysBusiness live – latest updatesLisa O’CarrollMon 20 Jul 2026 10.57 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.56 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Chinese online retail platform AliExpress has been fined a record €550m (£470m) by the EU over its failure to stop illegal goods including harmful clothing, cosmetics and kitchen gadgets being sold through its site.The European Commission fine is the biggest imposed by the bloc under Digital Services Act (DSA) legislation to protect consumers from illegal goods and deceptive or addictive marketing techniques.Henna Virkkunen, the commission’s executive vice-president for tech sovereignty, security and democracy, said: “The spread of counterfeit clothing, unsafe toys, dangerous cosmetics and other illegal and harmful products is not an unavoidable cost of shopping online – it is a failure by AliExpress to comply with its obligations under the Digital Services Act.EU fines Temu for failing to stop sale of illegal and dangerous productsRead more“Scale is not an excuse; risks must be identified and addressed systematically to ensure consumers can safely shop online. Today, we are holding AliExpress to this standard and request it to take action.”While the fine was much larger than those previously issued under the DSA to Temu (€200m) and X (€120m), it represented less than 1% of the €122bn that AliExpress’s parent company,Alibaba, generated in revenue last year. It could have been fined a maximum of 6% of global annual revenue.Temu wasfined in Mayfor failing to stop the sale of illegal and dangerous products, whileX was finedfor breaches including what the EU said was a “deceptive” blue tick verification badge given to users and the lack of transparency of the platform’s advertising. Temu is still under EU investigation on other issues and may yet face another fine.The European Commission found AliExpress did not have enough staff to assess the legality of products, sometimes giving them just “tens of seconds” to judge whether a product met EU standards.It also found many illegal products were being promoted under AliExpress’s recommendation systems and that the company’s internal risk assessments failed.“Many illegal products, from counterfeit products to unsafe toys and dangerous cosmetics, circulated on the platform and, even if detected, remained online for multiple weeks,” the commission said.The size of the fine reflected “the nature and gravity” of the platform’s failure to implement mitigating measures to stop consumers being offered harmful or dangerous goods, a senior commission official added.AliExpress immediately condemned the fine as “disproportionate”. It said: “We disagree with today’s decision and the disproportionate fine, which ‌does not adequately reflect our established framework and the significant, proactive enhancements we ⁠have made.The company said it would “appeal the decision” by the commission, claiming the fine “ignores our sound risk management framework and the significant, proactive enhancements we have made”.Previous investigations by the EU of a sample of products sold on large retail platforms including Shein found 65% of cosmetics, 63% of food supplements and 60% of personal protection equipment, such as hard hats and steel toe cap boots for building sites, were non-compliant.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionOfficials said the fine was not a result of the discovery of illegal products on AliExpress but its failure to put in place barriers or mitigations that would have protected consumers from “illegal, non-compliant and counterfeit goods”, which is illegal under EU law.After an investigation process lasting more than two years, the company was given the opportunity to rectify its compliance and risk procedures but failed to do so, the European Commission said.AliExpress operated terms and conditions that had the appearance of being compliant with EU law – that retailers should not place any illegal products on its platform – but the reality was that sellers could easily place non-compliant goods on the platform, the commission found.Through its internal testing, the commission found large-scale breaches despite the company’s claims to be operating within the scope of the EU’s “safety gate”, a rapid alert system for non-food products that allows unsafe products to be quickly identified, recalled or banned.The commission said it “found millions of products that reappeared online which sometimes stayed for longer than a month” after they had been flagged as being illegal under EU law.The commission was particularly concerned about the high number of counterfeit goods on the site and the ease with which a seller could get around barriers, sometimes “miscategorising” a counterfeit fashion label as non-branded.AliExpress is the largest Chinese online retail operator in the EU with 193 million users, making it significantly bigger than Shein with 156 million and Temu with 130 million.Explore more on these topicsRetail industryAlibabaEuropean UnionEuropeChinanewsShareReuse this content

title: US launches 10th round of strikes as Iran says it is in ‘full-scale war’ | US-Israel war on Iran | The Guardian
description: Diplomats say de-escalation talks continue as Trump vows retaliation over US soldier deaths
Author: William Christou
Headline: Diplomats say de-escalation talks continue as Trump vows retaliation over US soldier deaths
Content: Still from video footage released by US Central Command, as the US launched a ninth night of strikes on Iran.Photograph: US Central Command/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenStill from video footage released by US Central Command, as the US launched a ninth night of strikes on Iran.Photograph: US Central Command/AFP/Getty ImagesUS-Israel war on IranUS launches 10th round of strikes as Iran says it is in ‘full-scale war’Diplomats say de-escalation talks continue as Trump vows retaliation over US soldier deathsMiddle East crisis live – latest updatesWilliam Christouin BeirutMon 20 Jul 2026 16.19 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 22.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheUS militarysaid it has launched its 10th consecutive round of strikes on Iran to force the reopening of the strait of Hormuz.Earlier on Monday, Iran’s president said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the US expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies, even as diplomats said talks to de-escalate were continuing.Iranian media reported strikes around Tabriz, believed to hold underground missile bases, as well as Bushehr, home to the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power plant.The US military said it had struck “military command centres, air defence and coastal surveillance sites, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites and communications networks”.Iran launched attacks againstBahrain, which hosts the US navy’s fifth fleet, and Kuwait, in addition to striking tankers in the strait of Hormuz, which carried a fifth of global oil and gas supplies before the war. Bahrain said its air traffic systems were damaged by the strikes, but that traffic was not affected.The Iran-allied Houthis in Yemen also announced a blockade of Saudi ports.“The reality is that today the Islamic Republic of Iran is engaged in a full-scale war,” the Iranian president, Masoud Pezeshkian, said in a statement on Monday, warning it might have to endure more strikes.US launches more airstrikes against IranMeanwhile, US presidentDonald Trumpsignalled an escalation in tactics, saying on ​Monday ⁠that Iran ⁠would “pay” ​for killing US soldiers “many times over” after multiple service members ‌were killed ​in action over the ​weekend.The US military on Mondayidentifiedtwo soldierskilledin Jordan while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks. An examination of remains is under way to determine whether they belong to a third service member who was reported missing in that attack. And in Iraq, another US service member died after “a controlled detonation” of an Iranian drone.Since the war began, 17 US service members have been killed. Iran’s health ministry said on Friday that 50 people had been killed and more than 500 injured in the current round of fighting.The latest tit-for-tat attacks came as the region lurched back towards a total war despite aninterim peace dealagreed last month. The memorandum of understanding (MoU) to halt attacks and open the strait is more than halfway through a 60-day implementation period that was intended to provide breathing room for diplomacy.Shipping in the strait has slowed to a trickle as a result of the fighting and the price of oil reached its highest in a month, passing $90 (£67) a barrel, exacerbating the global energy crisis.Hamas names new leader as Kuwait says it is under fire from Iran – Middle East crisis liveRead moreA Houthi blockade of Saudi ports announced on Monday could further strain global energy markets as Saudi Arabia was using the Red Sea route to export millions of barrels of oil and circumvent Iran’s closure of the strait of Hormuz.The group said it was responding to what it called Riyadh’s targeting of Sana’a airport last week after it was bombed by the Saudi-backed Yemen government, and that it would answer a “blockade with a blockade”.But in a sign that talks could still stop the escalating round of conflict, an Iranian official told Reuters that mediators gave Iran a proposal for a 10-day-ceasefire to find ways to revive the MoU.The proposal follows a statement by Iran that diplomacy with the US was continuing despite the fighting, without giving details on the content of the discussions.Iran’s interior minister was expected to travel to Islamabad on Monday, Agence France-Presse reported.Hours earlier, the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, said the US was still open to negotiations with Iran, but that “it has to be real”.“We’re going to continue to respond. If the door opens to diplomacy – if the guys that want to do something productive for Iran win and take control of that system, or take control of the negotiations – that’ll be a very positive development,” Rubio said.View image in fullscreenA bridge hit by a US strike in Iran’s Hormozgan province, pictured on Sunday.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesMediators including Qatar and Pakistan have continued to speak to both sides during the current round of escalation in an attempt to bring the two sides back to the negotiating table.As the strikes began early on Monday, a ship caught fire in the strait of Hormuz near the coastline of Oman in the southern corridor the US military has encouraged ships to use to avoid Iran’s control.The crew safely abandoned the vessel, while the ship was recovered by a tug boat, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency said.Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed twooil tankers were blown up after attempting to transit through the southern route, but there was no independent confirmation.Why is Trump risking midterm disaster by resuming the unpopular Iran war?Read moreThe IRGC also claimed to have launched a “surprise attack” on an enemy command centre in the al-Tanf region of Syria, in a statement reported by the state news agency IRNA.After Iran targeted sites in Jordan on Sunday,Israel warned of the danger ofthe strikes spilling across its borders, reigniting a direct regional conflict that had been restrained even as tensions between the Trump administration and Iran have soared again.“If Iran fires missiles atIsrael, we will attack them with full force,” said the Israeli defence minister, Israel Katz, on Sunday. The US is expected to send dozens of additional refuelling planes to Israel as part of a reinforcement of its force posture in the region, a US official told Reuters.Trump has threatened to target Iran’s power stations and bridges to try to compel Tehran to loosen its hold on the strait of Hormuz.View image in fullscreenA vessel in the strait of Hormuz, as seen from Musandam, Oman, on 17 July.Photograph: ReutersThe US energy secretary, Chris Wright, defended the Trump administration’s strategy for the war, but argued that the mission had since shifted towardensuring oil shipments could move through the strait.“We did a pause to try to get an agreement with the Iranian people toallow oil to flowwithout them attacking it,” Wright said on Sunday. “They’ve proven unwilling to do that, and we’ve pivoted. Now what we’re doing is assuring that that oil and gas and other products can flow through the strait of Hormuz with or without Iranian cooperation.”The US also announced that the “pilot zone” scheme in Lebanon had begun in three villages in the south, after peace talks in Rome between the governments of Lebanon and Israel.Under the programme, Israel will withdraw soldiers from areas it occupies in south Lebanon, to be replaced by the Lebanese army, which will be tasked with dismantling Hezbollah infrastructure in the areas.The US state department spokesperson Thomas Pigott said on Monday that the scheme started in the villages of Froun, Srifa and Zawtar al-Gharbiya.The Associated Press contributed reportingExplore more on these topicsUS-Israel war on IranIranIsraelStrait of HormuzUS foreign policyTrump administrationDonald TrumpnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Israeli settlers are in partnership with the army,’ say Palestinians after two shot dead in West Bank | West Bank | The Guardian
description: Residents of Deir Jarir say raids late on Sunday, also involving security forces, are part of pattern of violence
Author: Andrew Roth
Headline: Residents of Deir Jarir say raids late on Sunday, also involving security forces, are part of pattern of violence
Content: The bodies of Awda Abulrahim Farahneh and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu, killed in Israeli raids at the village of Deir Jarir, are taken to a burial ground on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyView image in fullscreenThe bodies of Awda Abulrahim Farahneh and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu, killed in Israeli raids at the village of Deir Jarir, are taken to a burial ground on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyWest Bank‘Israeli settlers are in partnership with the army,’ say Palestinians after two shot dead in West BankResidents of Deir Jarir say raids late on Sunday, also involving security forces, are part of pattern of violenceAndrew RothandSufian Tahain Deir Jarir, West BankMon 20 Jul 2026 13.53 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEven by the brutal standards of militant settler violence in theWest Bank, the raid on Deir Jarir late on Sunday night that killed two Palestinians and left another in hospital with a gunshot wound was extreme.Palestinians in the town said settlers from a nearby outpost arrived after 9pm on Sunday claiming that they were searching for stolen livestock. Israeli soldiers and police soon joined them, protecting them as they sought to take several villagers’ sheep by force.But when villagers raised the alarm and a crowd gathered, soldiers shot two men, beginning a night of three successive raids by settlers from nearby outposts and Israeli soldiers that lasted until after 2am, according to Palestinian residents of Deir Jarir.Saber Ajaj, a relative of one of the victims, 53-year-old Odeh Farajna, said he found him moments after he had been shot through the chest. Farajna, a member of the village council, owned a flower shop and helped with the stonework at the local graveyard.“The moment I put him in the car I knew that he was dead,” Ajaj said in an interview during a reception held after the funeral. “This shooting was meant to kill.”In interviews as they buried the two victims on Monday, family and friends of the dead Palestinian men described several attacks each month in which they said settlers, backed by security forces, arrived in houses on the outskirts of the village and then stole livestock.“It’s a partnership between the army and the settlers,” said Adel Rashid, the father of the younger victim, Abdul Adel Rashid Abu Mukh, 26.Rashid said local settlers on Sunday had stolen 21 of his sheep, while 50 more had been taken from another member of the village. Video of Sunday’s incident showed a settler protected by four IDF soldiers in green camouflage leading a sheep out of a local house.The raids are part of a picture of worsening settler violence in the region as Benjamin Netanyahu’s far-right allies have accelerated a land grab in the occupied West Bank.View image in fullscreenThe funeral of Awda Abulrahim Farahneh and Ahmed Adil Abu Muhu on Monday.Photograph: Anadolu/GettyArbitrary checkpoints and other restrictions to cut off towns like Deir Jarir after the7 October 2023 attacksby Hamas have been bolstered by plans for the government to endorsenew settlementsin the West Bank before nationalelectionsthat could challenge the Netanyahu government’s mandate.On the hill above the village is a settlement built last year that appears to share water infrastructure with an Israeli military base, according to Dror Etkes, an Israeli who founded Kerem Navot, an organisation that monitors Israeli land policy in occupiedPalestine. Residents said the settlers and military appeared to have coordinated the raid.Seven people have been killed in the village since 7 October 2023. Their portraits have been spray-painted on the walls of the town, along with the date they died.“The killing in Deir Jarir is one example of how Israel promotes ethnic cleansing in the West Bank: settlers invade and steal, while the military and police accompany them and shoot Palestinians who try to protect their homes and property,” said Yuli Novak, the executive director of B’Tselem, a human rights organisation based in Israel that catalogues violations by the security forces.A Guardian analysis has shown that Israel has not prosecuted its citizens for killing Palestinian civilians in the occupied West Bank since the start of this decade, creating impunity for a campaign of violence.The West Bank attacks are intensifying. Fadi Hamdallah al-Naasan, a 17-year-old from the town of al-Mughayyir, north-east of Ramallah, died on Saturday after succumbing to a gunshot wound to his leg during a raid on his house by the Israeli military and settlers on 11 July.Nearly a thousand mourners, mostly men, gathered in Deir Jarir on Monday shortly after noon for the burial. They marched from the mosque in the centre of the town, carrying the two bodies down to the old graveyard.On the road entering the area stands a new set of gates at a checkpoint established after 7 October that security forces are said to open or close arbitrarily, effectively cutting off the villages from the outside world.“Every time they come to attack us they are trying to make life here unlivable,” said Iyad Odah, 56, a member of the village council who knew Farajna. Since 7 October, he said, the town’s farmland, which once occupied thousands of hectares, was inaccessible as local settlers beat back farmers, attempting to access their orchards and grazing land.The Israeli military on Monday saidthey had gone to Deir Jarir to escort Israeli settlers, who claimed their livestock had been stolen and wanted to take it back. The military said they opened fire after Palestinians threw rocks and concrete blocks at the security forces. They did not respond to questions clarifying why they did not use non-lethal crowd control instead of live ammunition.Responding to the IDF’s claim that they had been attacked by “terrorists” in the village, Odah, of the town’s village council, said: “They are the real terrorists.”Explore more on these topicsWest BankPalestineMiddle East and north AfricaIsraelnewsShareReuse this content

title: Afghanistan floods kill at least 20 people, with more than 100 missing | Afghanistan | The Guardian
description: National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in remote province of Nuristan
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in remote province of Nuristan
Content: Precarious mountainside homes in the Parun district of Afghanistan’s Nuristan province last month.Photograph: Wakil Kohsar/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenPrecarious mountainside homes in the Parun district of Afghanistan’s Nuristan province last month.Photograph: Wakil Kohsar/AFP/Getty ImagesAfghanistanAfghanistan floods kill at least 20 people, with more than 100 missingNational disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in remote province of NuristanAgence France-Press in KabulMon 20 Jul 2026 13.31 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.40 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAt least 20 people have been killed and more than 80 injured in flooding that hit Afghanistan’s Nuristan province, the country’s disaster management agency said on Monday.In Parun, the provincial capital, a further 105 people including the mayor were missing, said Feraidon Samim, a spokesperson for the Nuristan governor.Journalists familiar with the remote north-eastern area said a market, shops and other buildings were located near Parun’s riverside.Ashiqullah Safi, the head of an NGO based in the town, said the flood had “completely destroyed the municipality”, including hotels and dozens of shops.He added that flood waters had left big boulders in the town and residents were searching for the missing without equipment.Afghanistan’s national disaster management agency said floods had caused “severe financial and human losses” in Parun, where efforts were under way to rescue the injured and recover the bodies of those killed.Zabihullah Mujahid, the chief spokesperson for the Afghan government, posted on X to confirm reports of deaths and property damage in Nuristan.Earlier this month, the head of Afghanistan’s environmental protection agency, Matiul Haq Khalis, said flooding in the country had increased.He said environmental problems “have severely affected the Afghan people and have contributed to a humanitarian crisis in the country”.Extreme rainfall has become more common and more intense across most of the world because of the human-caused climate crisis. This is because warmer air can hold more water vapour, leading to more severe downpours.Explore more on these topicsAfghanistanClimate crisisSouth and central AsiaFloodingExtreme weathernewsShareReuse this content

title: Tens of thousands of Cockroach movement protesters defy police with Delhi march | India | The Guardian
description: Youth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in years
Author: Hannah Ellis-Petersen
Headline: Youth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in years
Content: 0:51'Cockroach' movement: thousands march in anti-government protest in New DelhiIndiaTens of thousands of Cockroach movement protesters defy police with Delhi marchYouth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in yearsHannah Ellis-Petersenin DelhiMon 20 Jul 2026 10.01 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 20.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTens of thousands of protesters have defied police orders and descended on Delhi to demand the resignation of India’s education minister in one of the largest anti-government demonstrations in years.A huge crowd, largely made up of students and gen Z-ers, filled the streets in the heart of India’s capital from early Monday morning shouting anti-government slogans. Hundreds had already camped out overnight.Delhi police, which are under the control of the central government, had denied permission for the march to take place. A ban was imposed on all public gatherings, roads across the city were barricaded and the internet was shut down in the centre. Attempts by protesters to move towards the parliament building were met with police brutality including the use of batons and teargas.However, this did little to prevent protesters turning out in their thousands, many of whom were vocal in their anger at Narendra Modi’s government and his ruling Bharatiya Janata party (BJP), accusing them of failing the country’s young people.“They are calling this protest illegal, they are calling us anti-national,” said Rhythm Katoch. “But I am 22 years old and I am standing here to fight for our education system, for the rights of the young people and for our democracy. Is it too much to ask for the government to listen to us?”View image in fullscreenThe protesters were vocal in their anger at Narendra Modi’s government and his ruling Bharatiya Janata party.Photograph: Shekhar Yadav/APA call had been made to march on parliament by the Cockroach Janta party (CJP), a new youth-led movement whose name was inspired by remarks made in May by Surya Kant, the chief justice of India, when he called some activists and unemployed youth “cockroaches”.
