While the CJP began as satire, founded by the graduate Abhijeet Dipke in May,it has swiftly mobilised intoa serious political challenge to the Modi government.Its core demand has been the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, over exam chaos, after this year’s medical entrance exam – where more than 2 million students compete for just 130,000 places – was again leaked to the highest bidder, forcing it to be scrapped and for students to face retaking the gruelling tests. The fiasco has been linked to more than 20 suicides.Wider frustrations over alleged failures in the education system and a lack of job opportunities for India’s youth have also mobilised support.View image in fullscreenCJP supporters clash with police during the march.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty ImagesRage was palpable on Delhi’s streets on Monday. Harion Sharma, 18, was among those forced to retake the medical exam last month after it was leaked, which he said had put “unbearable” pressure on him and his fellow students.“This is a government that does not care about education, it does not care about the pressures and pain of young people. Because of those leaks, students have died. So we are here to call for accountability and for the education minister to resign. We are young but we are powerful.”Sharma described the high turnout of the protest as historic. “They might be trying to crush our futures and our democracy, but look around, how young people have come to the streets in such massive numbers,” he added. “Now, finally, the government is scared of us.”The use of police force against the protesters was met with condemnation by opposition politicians. Speaking outside parliament, Priyanka Gandhi, a leader of the opposition Congress party, said: “There are massive issues … but you are firing teargas at children and beating them up? For what?”For weeks, the government had ignored the CJP supporters camped out less than a mile from parliament. Pradhan had sought to dismiss their calls for his resignation by calling them “terrorists”. However, after the huge turnout on Monday, the Modi government invited several CJP spokespeople for talks.In a social media post, the CJP spokesperson Saurav Das said they had waited two hours and were given a brief meeting with a cabinet minister, JP Nadda. Das said the government had made no commitments so far.View image in fullscreenThe renowned engineer and education activist Sonam Wangchuk has been on hunger strike in solidarity with the students.Photograph: ReutersWhile neighbouring countries such as Nepal, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka have had governments toppled by youth-led movements, such large-scale expressions of dissent have become unusual in India in recent years.The BJP, which has ruled for 12 years under Modi, has amassed unprecedented power at national and state level while also routinely jailing and persecuting its critics and opponents, leaving little room for dissent.The BJP MP Kangana Ranaut was among the ruling government politicians to criticise the protest, telling reporters that “no one can pressure the government into who to sack and who to retain”.The CJP protest had been galvanised by ahunger strike carried out by the renowned engineer and education activist Sonam Wangchuk, who in solidarity with the students has refused all food and water for the past 21 days until Pradhan agrees to resign.View image in fullscreenA police officer fires teargas shells towards the protesters.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty ImagesNationwide outrage grew after the Delhi police arrived early on Sunday morning and forcibly removed Wangchuk against his will, with footage of the incident going viral.Wangchuk was taken to a government hospital where he claimed he was being “held hostage”. In a statement, his wife, Gitanjali Angmo, condemned the heavy police presence around Wangchuk in the hospital ward. “It is not medical care,” she said. “It is illegal detention.”In response, organisers of the CJP also began a hunger strike and called on supporters to join Monday’s march, which Wangchuk said would be “India’s second freedom movement”.Amrita, 27, said she had taken a day off work to join the protest, calling the government’s treatment of Wangchuk “shameless”.“We have a coward government who is afraid of the young people and afraid of Sonam Wangchuk,” she said. “He is a brilliant brain and he has put his life on the line for young people. How he is being treated is a shame on India. That’s why the gen Zs have come out today to speak out.”By Monday evening there had been no formal response by Pradhan to the calls for his resignation and CJP organisers said they would remain at the protest site. In a handwritten note from hospital, Wangchuk said that “seeing the brutality with which peacefully protesting youth are being dealt with, I have decided to continue my fast”.Explore more on these topicsIndiaNarendra ModiSouth and central AsianewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘When I get home, my whole body aches’: extreme heat saps health and wages of India’s poorest workers | Extreme heat | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Outdoor workers losing up to 9% of income as weather becomes ‘poverty multiplier’, research says
Author: Carmen Aguilar García
Headline: Exclusive: Outdoor workers losing up to 9% of income as weather becomes ‘poverty multiplier’, research says
Content: Workers in highly physical jobs in some sectors lost on average 6.6% of their salaries in 2024 due to extreme heat, analysis found.Photograph: Saqib Majeed/Sopa Images/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenWorkers in highly physical jobs in some sectors lost on average 6.6% of their salaries in 2024 due to extreme heat, analysis found.Photograph: Saqib Majeed/Sopa Images/ShutterstockExtreme heat‘When I get home, my whole body aches’: extreme heat saps health and wages of India’s poorest workersExclusive: Outdoor workers losing up to 9% of income as weather becomes ‘poverty multiplier’, research saysCarmen Aguilar GarcíaandAntonio Vocein London;Hannah Ellis-PetersenandAakash Hassanin DelhiSun 19 Jul 2026 23.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 05.15 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleExtreme heat is pushing some of India’s poorest workers further into poverty, with income losses of up to 9% for outdoor workers, new research has found.Analysis done by the International Institute for Environment and Development (IIED), and shared exclusively with the Guardian, found that extreme heat is acting as a “poverty multiplier” for hundreds of millions of workers in India’s informal sector.Researchers found that those working in highly physical jobs in sectors such as agriculture, construction and transport lost on average 6.6% of their salaries in 2024 due to extreme heat. Outdoor workers suffered the most, losing close to 9% of their salary in 2024, amounting to £112 ($151).Map of maximum temperatures in India in 2024The economic loss was the highest in the arid desert region of Rajasthan, an area with prolonged heatwaves and a rising death toll due to extreme heat. Informal workers in the region lost equivalent to 8.3% of their income, due to the gruelling effects of extreme heat on the body making them unable to work.TheIIED researchestimated that each year, 21.1 working days were lost in India due to extreme heat, an unaffordable loss for many of the 550 million workers in the informal sector.Ritu Bharadwaj, climate finance director at the IIED, said the research showed how extreme heat was deepening poverty and worsening existing structural inequalities in India, which is aiming to be a developed nation in the next two decades.She said: “Heat is quietly pushing these families deeper into poverty.“As the heat rises, their efficiency goes down, so they earn less. With lower income their food intake falls. And because they cannot afford to stop, they keep working through it, doing lasting damage to their health. They are paying for a crisis they did nothing to cause, with their health and with their savings.”View image in fullscreenInder Kumar, 25, hails from Chattarpur, Madhya Pradesh, and works as a contract labourer. He recently endured over a week of fever brought on by heat stress.Photograph: Smita Sharma/The GuardianHuman-caused climate breakdown is making every heatwave in the world more intense and more likely to happen. India, a country where nine out of 10 work in the informal economy, could become one of the first places where heatwaves break the human survivability limit, aWorld Bank report said.The research estimates that heat could cost the Indian economy at least £58bn – equivalent to 2% of its GDP – in a combination of wages lost due to reduced productivity, worksite closures and long-term losses from heat-related illnesses.The analysis accounts for three ways heat exposure reduces hours worked: heat-related illnesses, a reduction in the capacity of work or maintaining normal productivity, and time lost when workplaces suspend their operations.Informal workers include some of the most socially and economically marginalised people, who lack employment security, obliging them to work through illness and tolerate unsafe conditions.View image in fullscreenInder and Laxmi Kumar. ‘We kept working because if we stopped, we wouldn’t be paid,’ said Laxmi.Photograph: Smita Sharma/The GuardianAccess to drinking water, shade, rest areas, sanitation and medical support at the worksite was poor, said the report. Many workers then returned to homes that provided “no relief from heat”.Eight construction workers who spoke to the Guardian all said they had repeatedly fallen ill over the past four months because of the heat, forcing them to miss work and lose income. Some had collapsed and sustained serious injuries but were given no compensation by employers. Some were even refused pay.Inder and Laxmi Kumar, a husband and wife toiling on a dusty construction site in Gurugram, a satellite city outside Delhi, said they had both been confined to bed with fever, body aches and exhaustion. But after losing eight days’ wages, they could no longer afford to stay at home.“I felt like I would collapse, like my body was on fire,” Laxmi said. “We kept working because if we stopped, we wouldn’t be paid.”View image in fullscreenSatyam Kumar, 5, the son of Inder Kumar and Laxmi, drinks water at a construction site in Sector 92, Gurgaon. He doesn’t go to school and stays at the site while his parents work as labourers.Photograph: Smita Sharma/The GuardianDoctors told the couple they were suffering from dehydration and heat exhaustion and advised them to rest.The couple earn 800 rupees (£6) each a day, supporting their two young sons and four other family members back in their home village in Madhya Pradesh. They start around 9am and spend eight to 10 hours a day mixing cement, carrying construction materials and laying bricks, with hardly any breaks and little shade beyond the unfinished structure itself. Their five-year-old son plays in the rubble.In the past three months alone, they have fallen ill three times, losing nearly four weeks of work and spending much of their income on medicines.“We had no choice but to come back,” Inder said. “Whatever we manage to save is spent the next time we fall sick. We hardly have anything left to send home. I just pray this heat ends. It’s torture.”Inder said he did not know a single labourer at the site who had escaped illness this summer. The extreme heat often spoiled the food they made for lunch, making them sicker.The family’s home is a 2.5 metre (8ft) square of tarpaulin shelter inside the unfinished building. In the evening, a fan stops when the diesel generators are switched off just an hour after they return from work, leaving them to sleep in the stifling heat.“When I get home, my whole body aches,” said Laxmi. “But my son becomes restless in the heat and keeps crying. Then I still have to cook, and the room gets even hotter.“It feels like we work and sleep inside an oven.”View image in fullscreenKumkumari, 35, and her two-year-old daughter stand outside their makeshift tent at a construction site in Sector 92, Gurgaon.Photograph: Smita Sharma/The GuardianFor female labourers, the burden is often heavier. Many spend their working day also caring for young children.Beside a water tanker on a construction site, 35-year-old Kumkumari poured water over her two-year-old daughter. “This is the only way to cool her down,” Kumkumari said. “She falls sick often, and when that happens I have to look after her instead of working.”View image in fullscreenThe makeshift tent where Kumkumari, 35, lives with her family is built of plastic with no ventilation, it relies on a small temporary fan for cooling.Photograph: Smita Sharma/The GuardianProlonged exposure to heat and dehydration over timeincreases the risk of developing chronic kidney disease. The IIED research estimated that over years of repeated exposure to heat, one in 20 informal workers in India could develop chronic kidney disease – equivalent to more than 26 million workers.Global heating ‘may lead to epidemic of kidney disease’Read moreAmong them was 38-year-old Sattavir, who stood on the roadside in Gurugram, hoping for a contractor to offer him a day of labour work.In the past two months, he could only work for eight days after he developed a kidney infection, brought on by working in extreme heat and repeated dehydration.View image in fullscreenDaily-wage labourers wait at Badshahpur market, Gurgaon, to be picked by contractors for construction work.Photograph: Smita Sharma/The Guardian“The heat is killer,” he said. “In the last two months, I earned barely 7,000 rupees but spent around 9,000 rupees on medicines and treatment. I’m already in debt, but I’m still looking for work even though doctors say I need treatment for my kidneys.”After waiting for several hours in the sun, Sattavir was not picked for work – the third consecutive day he returned home empty-handed. “When people see you’re weak, they don’t hire you,” he said.Explore more on these topicsExtreme heatIndiaSouth and central AsiafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham vows to ‘bring back hope’ as he unveils his cabinet | Andy Burnham | The Guardian
description: New prime minister says government must ‘do better’ to deliver change but promises prudent approach to economy
Author: Pippa Crerar
Headline: New prime minister says government must ‘do better’ to deliver change but promises prudent approach to economy
Content: Andy Burnham is the UK’s sixth prime minister in a decade.Photograph: Xinhua/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham is the UK’s sixth prime minister in a decade.Photograph: Xinhua/ShutterstockAndy BurnhamAndy Burnham vows to ‘bring back hope’ as he unveils his cabinetNew prime minister says government must ‘do better’ to deliver change but promises prudent approach to economyPippa CrerarPolitical editorMon 20 Jul 2026 19.32 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 15.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham has said his arrival in power will “bring back hope” to Britain as he launched his premiership with the surprise appointment of John Healey as chancellor and a promise to repair the long-term damage caused to communities during the Thatcher era.The new UK prime minister acknowledged people were “fed up” with politics as he addressed the country after taking over fromKeir Starmerin Downing Street, saying the government needed to “do better” to deliver real change.Six highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speechRead moreIn his speech outside No 10, he said the government would announce “some breathing space” on Tuesday for people regarding the cost of living, with plans to cut energy bills and bus fares expected as part of a fully funded package.Burnham later told reporters that he would look at “any flexibility” within the government’s existing fiscal rules to help borrow billions more to invest in infrastructure – but would take a “very prudent” approach to the economy.UK 10-year gilt prices dropped after his comments, with the markets already on the edge over the risks of higher borrowing and increased debt. But they had closed by the time that Healey, 66, regarded as a safe pair of hands, was announced.The former defence secretary, whose resignation pushed Starmer to the brink, emerged as the compromise candidate after Ed Miliband andShabana Mahmoodwere tipped for the role. Miliband takes over as foreign secretary while Mahmood remains at the Home Office.View image in fullscreenJohn Healey was a surprise choice as chancellor, but is considered a safe pair of hands.Photograph: Victoria Jones/ShutterstockIn his first world leader call, Burnham spoke to Donald Trump – who had previously suggested he was just mayor of “some town” – inviting him to Manchester for next year’s G20 summit.Trump later wrote on social media that the two leaders “had a very good conversation”, discussing Iran, the US-UK military alliance and trade. Trump said he and Burnham plan to meet “in the not too distant future”.Burnham also called Ukraine’s Volodymyr Zelenskyy and European Union chief Ursula von der Leyen.Burnham’s arrival in Downing Street as the UK’s sixth prime minister in a decade, afterLabourMPs brutally toppled Starmer from office, gives his party an opportunity for a second hearing with the public, but could also unleash yet more instability.Speaking without notes, and watched by his wife, Marie-France van Heel, he said: “I am acutely conscious that I am the sixth person in the last 10 years to walk up this street, the seventh prime minister since 2016, making this a moment for reflection and new resolution.“It requires my generation of politicians to raise our game and rise to the new challenge. Britain needs to show the world that we can regain our stability once again, and that is our challenge to make politics work, to make it work better.“I know people at home are fed up with politics. I hear you, and I want to be honest with you. We have not been good enough, and we need to be better. We will be.”View image in fullscreenEd Miliband takes over as foreign secretary while Shabana Mahmood remains at the Home Office.Photograph: Temilade Adelaja/ReutersHe told the assembled crowd: “Let’s build a new national sense of unity, of common purpose, and positivity. Let us make this the moment when Britain starts to believe again, the moment we bring back hope.”After sacking a tranche of Starmer loyalists, including chancellor Rachel Reeves – who was understood to have turned down another job to return to the backbenches – and deputy prime minister David Lammy, Burnham spent Monday evening making his cabinet appointments.In another senior appointment, Louise Haigh, Burnham’s right-hand woman, was made first secretary of state and chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster, running a beefed-up Cabinet Office that will be the “political engine room” driving his agenda.Yvette Cooper will move from the Foreign Office to the Department of Health, where she will oversee Burnham’s plans to transform the broken social care system, while Pat McFadden stays at the Department for Work and Pensions.Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister whoresigned after a row over stamp duty, was reappointed to her previous role as housing secretary.View image in fullscreenLouise Haigh, Burnham’s right-hand woman, was made first secretary of state and chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster.Photograph: Ryan Jenkinson/Getty ImagesBurnham, who took over from Starmer as the leader of the Labour partyon Friday, has put transforming the UK’s economic system at the heart of his plans, despite limited room for manoeuvre before the next election given his commitment to the fiscal rules.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“We will make this momenta circuit breaker for Britain, bringing forward the biggest changes in the last 40 years. A new political model and a new economic model,” he told the crowd gathered in the Downing Street sunshine.“In the 1980s, Britain took some wrong turns. Political power was centralised, economic power privatised, large parts of the country deindustrialised and they still haven’t recovered.“Many feel as though they’re still in decline, and they don’t have the ability to turn things around. And that’s why we will change politics to make it more collaborative, more about problem-solving than point-scoring. And we will take power out of here and carry it into every postcode in the land so that they can do more.”In appointing Healey, a former Treasury minister under Gordon Brown, Burnham has put an experienced figure in charge of delivering on his vision for the UK economy,combining fiscal responsibility with a focus on reindustrialisation, devolution, the cost of living and growth.Briefing wars: how Labour ministers tried, and failed, to get into Burnham’s cabinetRead moreThe new chancellor will now also be responsible for finding the money to raise military spending to 3% by 2030, and is expected to look closely at defence bonds, which he had argued for when at the Ministry of Defence but were rejected by the Treasury as they meant more borrowing.After his speech, Burnham told reporters he was planning to announce a fully funded cost of living support package including support for energy bills and bus fares this year, which could eventually include more radical plans such as freezing private sector rents.“We just want to do some things that people will feel, and feel quite quickly. It won’t solve everything, it won’t take all of the pressure off, but it just shows the direction of travel and that we’re serious about helping them out,” he said.He also made comments about borrowing more, within the fiscal rules, which he had mooted on the Makerfield campaign trail, but which nevertheless unsettled the markets when made from No 10.“I’ve said we’ll stick to the fiscal rules and by that I mean the existing fiscal rules and use, obviously, any flexibility within them,” he said. “So none of this is about taking risks with the economy. I’ve never done that in any role that I’ve had. I’ve always taken a very prudent approach.”View image in fullscreenKeir Starmer told the assembled crowd that his work was ‘done’ as he left Downing Street.Photograph: Seiya Tanase/NurPhoto/ShutterstockIn his speech, Burnhamannounced his first instruction in power would be to end rough sleeping, after a visit to homeless people in central London on Monday. He made a similar pledge when he became Greater Manchester mayor but oversaw four years of rising street homelessness.He also suggested he could cut income tax for lower earners in a future budget, potentially raising the tax-free personal allowance, which has been frozen for the last five years. But he refused to rule out bringing back the 50p top rate of tax to pay for it.Speaking before leaving No 10 for the final time, Starmer told the assembled crowd that “my work is done” of turning round the Labour party, but gave Burnham a final warning about foreign affairs. “We face a world now where our enemies will stop at nothing to divide us,” he said.The outgoing prime minister, who left handwritten notes for all Downing Street staff, added: “I go with good grace, I go with a smile and I go proud of everything that we have achieved.”Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamLabourKeir StarmerJohn HealeyEconomic policyEd MilibandShabana MahmoodnewsShareReuse this content

title: Andy Burnham’s first Labour cabinet: who’s in and who’s out? | Labour | The Guardian
description: From the Treasury to the Home Office, we look at who will head up key departments in new PM’s government
Author: Rowena Mason
Headline: From the Treasury to the Home Office, we look at who will head up key departments in new PM’s government
Content: Shabana Mahmood, John Healey, Louise Haigh and Ed Miliband.Composite: PA/Getty image/Rex features/ReutersView image in fullscreenShabana Mahmood, John Healey, Louise Haigh and Ed Miliband.Composite: PA/Getty image/Rex features/ReutersLabourExplainerAndy Burnham’s first Labour cabinet: who’s in and who’s out?From the Treasury to the Home Office, we look at who is heading up key departments in new PM’s governmentBurnham vows his arrival in power will be ‘circuit breaker’ moment for UKSix highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speechRowena MasonandPeter WalkerMon 20 Jul 2026 19.00 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 14.27 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAndy Burnham has appointed his first cabinet, with the new prime minister rewarding those who have been loyal to his takeover efforts. He had promised to pick his top team from all wings of the party in an effort to show he is trying to run a more inclusive administration.But at the same time, his decision to have a brutal clearout of Starmer allies resulted in many considered to be on the right of the party or close to the former prime minister losing their jobs. These are the key announcements so far:Who is in?John HealeyChancellorView image in fullscreenJohn Healey.Photograph: UK Parliament/PAThe former defence secretary who quit the government over a lack of funding for the defence investment plan is the surprise appointment to the Treasury.Healey, 66, is a steady and experienced force, having been an MP since 1997, and serving as a Treasury minister under Tony Blair.The job had been expected to go to Ed Miliband and then it was mooted for Shabana Mahmood. But one advantage of Healey is his longstanding history on Labour frontbenches for every faction of the party. He was a minister in the Blair-Brown years and a loyal member of the shadow cabinets of Miliband, Jeremy Corbyn and Starmer.Healey grew up in Wakefield, studied political science at Cambridge, and was a journalist, campaigns director at the Trades Union Congress and a disability rights campaigner before entering parliament.Unshowy and not part of the usual Westminster dramas or scandals, it was a shock when he resigned his role under Starmer, in what would be one of the fatal blows for his premiership.In the job, he will be expected to push for a fuller funding settlement for the Ministry of Defence, which was denied to him under the Treasury of Rachel Reeves. His other major shared interest with Burnham is reindustrialisation.His X bio describes him as “Husband and Dad. Proud Yorkshireman. Labour MP for Rawmarsh and Conisbrough and former secretary of state for defence”. He can now add chancellor to that list.Shabana MahmoodThe home secretaryShabana Mahmood.Photograph: ITV/ShutterstockMahmood will keep her role in Burnham’s cabinet after being appointed home secretary by Keir Starmer last year and charged with driving throughcontroversial immigration policies.She is considered to be on the Blue Labour right of the party, which is socially conservative and tough on crime. She grew up in Small Heath in inner-city Birmingham in the 1980s and 90s, where her father kept a cricket bat behind the till of the family shop to fend off would-be robbers. After going to the University of Oxford to study law, she worked as a barrister.The 45-year-old is an MP in Birmingham and ran Labour’s election campaign in 2024, before a stint as justice secretary overseeing an early release programme to ease overcrowding. She is viewed as steely and decisive.A cabinet colleague told the Guardian earlier this year that she “never wastes a word” in meetings, and last year warned that the government was still caught up in an opposition mindset and preoccupied with taking on the left instead of speaking to the concerns of the country more widely. She has also been a leading advocate for the UK to takea more pro-Palestinian stance, including on statehood.Ed MilibandForeign secretaryEd Miliband.Photograph: Getty ImagesMiliband is a great survivor in the Labour party – almost as much so as Andy Burnham. A former cabinet minister under Gordon Brown, he led Labour to anelection defeat in 2015before serving in the shadow cabinet of Keir Starmer.He was a leading contender to be chancellor but there were fears his positioning on the soft left and as a firm defender of Britain’s net zero commitments would spook the financial markets. Having once made a speech pitching some companies as producers and others as predators, he is not a favourite politician of big business.Born in north London in a political family, his father was a Marxist academic. He studied PPE at Oxford and worked mostly in politics and research before joining his older brother, David, in the House of Commons as a Labour MP.They became the first brothers to both serve around the cabinet table since before the second world war, and their sibling relationship was put to the test when Ed beat David in the leadership contest to succeed Brown with the support of the trade union vote.As Labour leader, he led in the polls for many months against David Cameron and identified ahead of his time the public mood on the cost of living but was pilloried by some of the rightwing media as gaffe-prone and somewhat awkward.In Starmer’s cabinet he was viewed as one of the most successful cabinet ministers at driving through his priorities, includingGreat British Energy, and blocking more drilling in the North Sea – a policy expected to be partially overturned by Burnham.As foreign secretary, he will be expected to take international matters off Burnham’s hands as much as possible, take a more pro-Gaza stance and potentially set a path to restoring Britain’s aid commitments to 0.7% of national income. He replaces Yvette Cooper.Yvette CooperHealth secretaryView image in fullscreenYvette Cooper.Photograph: David Woolfall/UK Parliament/PAAmong the Starmer-era big beasts who had been tipped to survive, Cooper is shifted from the Foreign Office to the vital domestic post of secretary of state for health and social care, the suffix especially important given Burnham’s stated mission to unlock the clogged adult care system.Cooper does, of course, have recent experience of a tricky UK-focused post, starting in Starmer’s government as home secretary, and she held several posts under Tony Blair and Gordon Brown.While still in her 50s, Cooper is approaching Labour “grandee” status, having been an MP since 1997 and getting her first taste of ministerial life at the tail end of the 20th century. When Labour’s 2015 leadership campaign began she was initially viewed as a favourite, but finished third behind Corbyn and a certain Andy Burnham.The daughter of a trade union leader, Cooper is a near-political lifer, having begun her career with then-shadow chancellor John Smith, although she did spend a period as a journalist before entering parliament.For a decade of her time in parliament, Cooper was part of a political double act with her husband, Ed Balls, who lost his seat in 2015 and later became a broadcaster.Pat McFaddenWork and pensions secretaryView image in fullscreenPat McFadden.Photograph: Carl Court/Getty ImagesSome continuity had been expected with the new ministerial slate and it makes sense for this to happen with a department that could be central to Burnham’s political fortunes.McFadden, one of the more experienced operators in government, will be tasked with continuing to pilot through some sort of reform to social security, with the intention of limiting the costs without cuts or conditions of the sort that prompted a major backbench rebellion for Starmer.An MP since 2005, McFadden was soon propelled into the Blair and Brown frontbenches, and spent the buildup to the 2024 election as Starmer’s national campaigns coordinator, going on to take the Cabinet Office post at the centre of policymaking.A trusted voice on even the trickiest morning broadcast round, the child of two Irish speakers from County Donegal transplanted to Paisley, McFadden trod the traditional political path of researcher and policy adviser before winning his Wolverhampton South East seat.Louise HaighChancellor of the duchy of Lancaster, first secretary of state and Cabinet Office ministerLouise Haigh.Photograph: Getty ImagesHaigh became a linchpin of Burnham’s byelection operation, so it is no surprise she is now his chief organiser in government and effectively his deputy as first secretary of state. As the most senior Cabinet Office minister, she will coordinate work across the government and oversee any changes to Whitehall.It marks a return to cabinet for the 38-year-old Haigh, who was sacked byKeir Starmer as transport secretaryfor not having told him fully about a fraud conviction over a missing mobile phone when she was in her 20s.She grew up in Sheffield and studied politics and government at LSE, then Nottingham University, before working in parliament, for Unite and later at the insurer Aviva.It was during her time at that company where she got in hot water over the phone, having reported it stolen but failed to update the police when it was found. She pleaded guilty to fraud, but it did not stop her political career.Elected in 2015, she was one of 36 Labour MPs to nominate Jeremy Corbyn for the Labour leadership that year and was given a frontbench role whenhe made her a shadow Cabinet Office ministerat age 28.The MP for Sheffield Heeley rose through the junior ministerial ranks and was one of a handful of Corbyn’s frontbench to be appointed to Keir Starmer’s first cabinet when he took over. She became the shadow Northern Ireland secretary.Known for an engaging communication style, she retained her frontbench role even as Starmer moved his party to the right. As transport secretary, she oversaw the nationalisation of railways legislation until she was returned to the backbenches. She replaces Darren Jones.Wes StreetingDefence secretaryView image in fullscreenWes StreetingPhotograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockStreeting is one of the only appointments to cabinet who is considered to be towards the right of the party. He is also one of the reasons Burnham is prime minister, after attempting unsuccessfully to challenge Starmer, kicking off the process of removing him.Having lost out on the top job, the role of defence secretary is still a substantial one. However, Streeting’s power is likely to be limited by the fact his predecessor, John Healey, is in the Treasury and will decide what kind of settlement the department gets for its investment plan.A former health secretary, the ambitious Streeting is likely to be pleased at getting an even bigger job, especially at such a crucial moment in the Ukraine war and during a time of warnings that Britain needs to rearm against global threats.Miatta FahnbullehEnergy secretaryFahnbulleh is a rising star in the party who contributed heavily to Burnham’s policy ideas as he was preparing for government.She previously worked closely with Miliband so was a natural choice for the energy role and will have a crucial task in Burnham’s mission of cutting bills while sticking to commitments in net zero.Alex NorrisJustice secretaryThe relatively low profile Alex Norris was a Home Office minister and an unexpected choice for justice secretary. A Nottingham MP, he was a trade union organiser for Unison and councillor before he entered parliament in 2017.Jonathan ReynoldsBusiness secretaryView image in fullscreenJonathan Reynolds.Photograph: Thomas Krych/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockReynolds returns to his former role after being shuffled sideways to the job of chief whip against his wishes.A fellow northwest MP, Reynolds has long been a friend of Burnham and has been given a crucial role in a beefed-up department that will cover science as well as his old portfolio.Burnham has stated he wants to be pro-business, as well as fighting for reindustrialisation and potentially overseeing the return of some utilities to more public control.Angela RaynerHousing secretaryView image in fullscreenAngela Rayner.Photograph: UK Parliament/PARayner will be pleased to get back the job of housing secretary less than a year after she resigned over underpaid stamp duty on the purchase of a second property.She had been the subject of speculation that she would be health secretary but sources had suggested she would take some persuading when her main interest is devolution and housing.In this brief, she will be in charge of two of Burnham’s key priorities: spreading power to local government and embarking on a huge council house building programme.Lucy PowellEducation secretaryView image in fullscreenLucy Powell.Photograph: Christopher Thomond/The GuardianPowell is likely to be pleased with this substantive brief but Burnham has so far stopped short of making her deputy prime minister – despite her job as deputy leader of the party.Another Manchester area MP, Powell has long been an ally of Burnham and advocated for him to be allowed to run for parliament.She replaces Bridget Phillipson, her former rival in the race to be deputy leader, who is now minister for women and equalities in a stripped-back cabinet role.Anneliese MidgleyChief whipView image in fullscreenAnneliese Midgley.Photograph: AlamyMidgley, a former political director for Unite, had been instrumental in helping Burnham with his Makerfield campaign. She was thought to be a contender for party chair but instead Burnham is seeking to harness her talent for organising as chief whip.Who’s out?Rachel ReevesFormer chancellorReeves had always wanted the job of chancellor and was proud to be the UK’s first female holder of the office. She had worked hard to instil ironclad fiscal discipline on Labour, sticking broadly to her self-imposed fiscal rules and enforcing spending curbs across departments.However, some in Labour blamed early missteps from her Treasury for Starmer’s downfall, starting with the cut to the winter fuel payment, but also the rise in national insurance for employers that her critics in business claim stymied the jobs market and growth.Reeves can, however, claim a record of having started to turn the economy around. She made a valiant effort to stay in her job but ultimately was judged too close to the Starmer project to stay in the Treasury. She is believed to have turned down another senior position.A former child chess champion and Oxford economics graduate, Reeves had also faced criticisms from some newspapers for having exaggerated her credentials as an economist on her LinkedIn page, having allowed some plagiarism to creep into a book she wrote on female economists and having inadvertently failed to pay for a landlord’s licence.In her departing statement, she said: “It has been the privilege of my life to serve as the chancellor of the exchequer. The economy today is stronger, fairer and more resilient because of the choices we have taken as a Labour government over the past two years.“Stability restored, investment delivered and reform to our economy under way … I’m proud to say that they have. And to every young woman and girl let my time in office show there should be no ceilings on your ambitions, your hopes or your dreams.”David LammyFormer foreign secretaryLammy has been a longstanding frontbencher after serving as a minister in the last New Labour governments.A former lawyer, he served as foreign secretary for Keir Starmer and then won a promotion to deputy prime minister and justice secretary when Angela Rayner stood down. He has been at the former prime minister’s side for many of his toughest moments.Such is his experience and standing that there was some speculation he might stay on in a different ministerial role. In a post on X announcing his departure, Lammy made it plain he had been happy to stay on but was not wanted.Hewrote: “I would have been proud to continue serving in cabinet. But a new prime minister is entitled to build his own team, and I wish Andy Burnham and the new cabinet every success.”Darren JonesFormer chief secretary to the prime ministerFrom the centre of No 10 operations to nothing. As Jonesput itin a bluntly worded social media post: “Andy Burnham has called to tell me I won’t be a part of his new cabinet. I wish him and his team every success.”An MP since 2017, Jones’s fairly rapid rise to the top table began with well-reviewed performances as chair of the business and trade committee. It began with the tricky if powerful role of Reeves’s number two as chief secretary to the Treasury.There he was so central to spending negotiations, that when Starmer undertook one of his several organisational revamps, as part of the September 2025 reshuffle, Jones took on the new and somewhat artificial role of chief secretary to the prime minister, tasked with leading change from No 10.Vague hints that Jones might contest for the Labour leadership came to nothing, and while – as his tweet indicated – he would have been happy to do another role, this was also not on the cards.Steve ReedFormer housing secretaryReed was one of most loyal cabinet ministers to the Starmer administration, although he had made a play to keep his job in recent weeks by highlighting his achievements.An old ally of Morgan McSweeney, Starmer’s former chief of staff, Reed was a veteran of south London local government before entering parliament in 2012. He served as environment secretary before his current job and will now return to the backbenches.His letter to Burnham after his departure from government was somewhat spiky, warning him not to disadvantage London and also sending his hopes that those who were not loyal to Starmer will be loyal to the new prime minister.Peter KyleFormer business secretaryKyle took on the job of business secretary last year in the emergency reshuffle prompted by the departure of Angela Rayner over her§ tax affairs.It meant he found himself on flights to the US and China at a moment’s notice at a critical time for negotiations over trade and tech companies. He previously served as science secretary – a role that is now likely to be folded into the business department.Kyle entered parliament in 2015 and has been open about his struggles to get there. He had difficulties at school owing to dyslexia and left, in his own words, “without any usable” qualifications. He made it to university in his 20s after several failed attempts.An ally of Wes Streeting and Starmer, Kyle appears to be departing from cabinet largely to make way for closer supporters of Burnham although he has also been criticised for a perception that he has been too open towards tech companies.Richard HermerFormer attorney generalHermer was probably Starmer’s closest friend in politics. A fellow human rights lawyer, he was made a peer so that he could join the prime minister’s first cabinet.However, he quickly became a bogeyman for the rightwing press, which disliked his role in signing over the Chagos Islands to Mauritius and generally espousing views on the liberal left. His past legal work, including his representation ofGerry Adamsand advice toCaribbean countries seeking reparations, has been raked over by Tories who have demanded that he publicly recuse himself from some areas.The criticism was not confined to the right. Maurice Glasman, the Blue Labour founder and associate of the US vice-president, JD Vance,called Hermer“an arrogant, progressive fool who thinks that law is a replacement for politics”.Starmer steadfastly stuck by him though, and Hermer has been an invaluable source of counsel and friendship through his final months as premier. He was also a bastion of progressive thought in a cabinet that many in the Labour party felt had veered too far away from core party values.Liz KendallFormer science and technology secretaryAlso to be filed under: no surprise here. Kendall has always been on the right of Labour and ideologically much closer to Starmer than to the new PM.An ever-present figure on Starmer’s frontbench, the Leicester West MP started as work and pensions secretary, the job she had shadowed in opposition, before moving to the science brief in the wholesale cabinet reshuffle of September last year.An MP from 2010, Kendall supported David Miliband in the Labour leadership election of that year. Five years later, she stood herself, coming last of the four candidates when Jeremy Corbyn became leader.In a tweeted letter, Kendall thanked Starmer and other cabinet colleagues, saying she was ready to support Burnham “in any way I can”.Jo StevensFormer Wales secretaryAlso not expected to stay on, the Cardiff East MP has been a fixture in the Wales office, filling the brief in opposition and then government since 2021, having previously been Starmer’s shadow culture secretary.A backer of Starmer when he stood to be Labour leader, Stevens had served as shadow Wales secretary under Corbyn,but resignedin opposition to Corbyn’s decision to back formal moves for the UK to leave the EU.Hilary BennFormer Northern Ireland secretarySome had speculated that Benn could be among ministers to stay in their job and provide continuity, given that he is not overtly partisan and was largely seen as doing a quietly good job in a sensitive brief.But Benn tweeted that he has gone. It is a return to the backbenches for a Labour grandee, in parliament for more than 25 years, who held a series of ministerial jobs under Blair and Brown.Explore more on these topicsLabourAndy BurnhamEd MilibandLouise HaighShabana MahmoodfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Healey made chancellor, Miliband named foreign secretary and Yvette Cooper becomes health secretary as Burnham picks cabinet – UK politics as it happened | Politics | The Guardian
description: Rayner is back as housing secretary, while Mahmood and McFadden remain in their existing posts
Author: Andrew Sparrow
Headline: Rayner is back as housing secretary, while Mahmood and McFadden remain in their existing posts
Content: 13.18 EDTJohn Healey named chancellorJohn Healey has been named chancellor in Andy Burnham’s first Cabinet appointment.Healey resigned as defence secretary last month, in protest at Keir Starmer’s defence investment plan.Our political editorPippa Crerarwrites:double quotation markBurnham’s team were clear they want an experienced figure to deliver his vision for UK economy - combining fiscal responsibility with focus on re-industrialisation, devolution, cost of living crisis and driving growth.Healey quit cabinet last month accusing Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves of putting the UK’s security at risk, saying defence investment plan fell well short.He will now be responsible for finding the money to raise military spending to 3% by 2030. Could he opt for defence bonds to do so - which he argued for from MoD - even though it was dismissed as extra borrowing.ShareUpdated at13.36 EDT

title: ‘We need to be careful’: Andy Burnham’s team keenly aware of fiscal tightrope | Politics | The Guardian
description: Chancellor pick and early policies avoid market meltdown but next week remains ‘moment of real peril’
Author: Rowena Mason
Headline: Chancellor pick and early policies avoid market meltdown but next week remains ‘moment of real peril’
Content: Andy Burnham faces a balancing act between appearing bold but not reckless when it comes to the economy.Photograph: Andy Rain/EPAView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham faces a balancing act between appearing bold but not reckless when it comes to the economy.Photograph: Andy Rain/EPAPoliticsAnalysis‘We need to be careful’: Andy Burnham’s team keenly aware of fiscal tightropeRowena MasonandPippa CrerarChancellor pick and early policies avoid market meltdown but next week remains ‘moment of real peril’Mon 20 Jul 2026 15.24 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 14.51 EDTShareAndy Burnham has not quite gone for Keir Starmer’s extreme caution in the run-up to entering No 10 – but he is certainly aware that any early wrong moves would cost him.Back in 2024, Starmer was criticised for his “Ming vase” strategy of treading very carefully so as not to drop a valuable metaphorical piece of china being carried across the room.He was meticulous before entering office, giving little away on policy and insisting on ironclad fiscal discipline, but things began to crack in the first few months of government over winter fuel payments,the freebies scandaland blunders in appointing his top team.Now Burnham has been passed the priceless vase and those around him are acutely aware of the risks of a sudden drop. The rightwing press are desperate to portray him as a 1970s throwback, willing to jeopardise the nation’s finances in the pursuit of greater nationalisation of utilities and council housebuilding.But the new prime minister has so far shown he is aware of the balancing act he needs to pull off between appearing bold but not reckless when it comes to the economy.View image in fullscreenAndy Burnham speaks to staff and the media outside No 10 after officially becoming prime minister on Monday.Photograph: Thomas Krych/AP“We need to be very careful,” says one member of Burnham’s team. “We need to show that he’s serious about change as the public will form their opinion of him very quickly. But we have to avoid a Liz Truss-style moment: it’s almost like the markets are waiting to be spooked.”Another senior member of his team said: “We know that there’s a very delicate balance between capturing people’s attention and not spooking the markets. Next week is a moment of real peril for Andy and, while change is priced in, it feels like they’re teetering.”The act of unseating a sittingLabourprime minister carried huge danger in itself, although Burnham still maintains he was not directly involved and merely picked up the baton when passed to him. He has been mindful since then of not triggering an adverse financial reaction, given the markets have for a while been calmed by Rachel Reeves’s insistence on sticking to her fiscal rules.After previously causing alarm among investors by saying governments should not be in hock to the bond markets, Burnham appeared to slightly walk back from that position during the campaign, saying he had never said gilt investors could be ignored. “Access talks focused the mind even more on that point,” says one Whitehall source. “Burnham and his people knew they had to appoint a chancellor who has credibility.”View image in fullscreenBurnham’s choice of John Healey for chancellor, a former Treasury minister, suggests he is prioritising experience.Photograph: Kirsty Wigglesworth/APHis choice ofJohn Healeyas chancellor – an unshowy veteran MP who has been Treasury minister – and advisers such as Jim O’Neill and Andy Haldane is a sign he is prioritising steadiness and experience.With this in mind, there will be announcements in the coming weeks to make voters sit up and pay attention, but Burnham’s team has downplayed the prospect of a “big bang” policy comparable to Gordon Brown granting independence to the Bank of England. His major announcement on the steps of Downing Street was about ending rough sleeping – a tall order for him personally, but not a pledge to make the bond investors take fright.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIn the garden of Downing Street after he walked into No 10 for the first time, Burnham was clear his priority was stability. He said he would look at “any flexibility” within the government’s existing fiscal rules to help borrow billions more to invest in infrastructure – but insisted he would take a measured approach to the economy.Briefing wars: how Labour ministers tried, and failed, to get into Burnham’s cabinetRead more“I’ve said we’ll stick to the fiscal rules and by that I mean the existing fiscal rules and use, obviously, any flexibility within them. But we will stick to the existing rules,” he said.“So none of this is about taking risks with the economy. I’ve never done that in any role that I’ve had. I’ve always taken a very prudent approach.”He will have to plug a multibillion-pound hole in the defence budget, is expected look at raising the personal allowance to lower taxes, must fund a council housebuilding programme and will probably have to pay for the costs of taking Thames Water and potentially other utilities into public ownership.The markets were slightly jittery on Monday but there was no Truss-style meltdown – and businesses appeared reassured by the choice of Healey, while even rival politicians found it difficult to criticise the appointment. It’s only day one but there have been no smashed vases yet.Explore more on these topicsPoliticsAndy BurnhamLabourJohn HealeyanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Tate brothers fight extradition to UK over rape and sex-trafficking charges | US news | The Guardian
description: Polarizing social media influencers are being held in federal detention in Miami as district judge considers extradition
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Polarizing social media influencers are being held in federal detention in Miami as district judge considers extradition
Content: Andrew Tate, right, and his brother, Tristan, arrive at an event on 11 April 2026 in Miami, Florida.Photograph: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/APView image in fullscreenAndrew Tate, right, and his brother, Tristan, arrive at an event on 11 April 2026 in Miami, Florida.Photograph: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/APUS newsTate brothers fight extradition to UK over rape and sex-trafficking chargesPolarizing social media influencers are being held in federal detention in Miami as district judge considers extraditionAssociated PressMon 20 Jul 2026 15.01 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 15.05 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSocial media influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate will fight extradition to the United Kingdom, their lawyer told a US magistrate in Miami on Monday after the brothers were arrested to face rape and sex-trafficking charges.The Tates appeared in federal court for the first time since their arrest on Saturday and are being held in federal detention. In the coming weeks, a US district judge will consider whether they meet the conditions for extradition.The brothers, normally photographed in tailored suits and stylish fitted shirts, wore tan jail uniforms and handcuffs during the brief hearing.Andrew and Tristan Tate arrested in US on UK rape and sex trafficking chargesRead moreJoseph McBride, an attorney for the brothers, has decried the charges as “filth and slander”. On Sunday, McBride said he was confident the extradition request would be denied.US magistrate Lauren Louis set another hearing for next Monday.Prosecutors in Britain said the new charges relate to four new victims and were brought after authorities received evidence from Bedfordshire police in south-eastern England. The allegations, spanning from 2010 to 2017, include rape, assault, trafficking and offenses relating to “indecent images of a child and extreme pornography”.The brothers are already charged with rape, bodily harm, human trafficking and controlling prostitution for gain relating to three other alleged victims in the UK between 2012 and 2015.Their lawyer said in a statement on Saturday that he believed the arrests were “greenlighted by a low-level functionary” at the justice department without input from leadership.The justice department told the Associated Press that the arrests were approved by leadership of its criminal division.The new allegations are the latest in a long-running international legal saga involving the Tates that has spanned the US, Britain and Romania. The brothers have repeatedly denied wrongdoing.Their social media empire promoting male dominance, misogyny and wealth has made them among the world’s most polarizing internet personalities.The dual US and British citizens moved to Romania in 2016. They were arrested there in 2022, accused of participating in schemes to lure women for sexual exploitation. They denied those allegations and the Romanian case has not gone forward because of legal and procedural problems.Last year, they were allowed to leave Romania and flew to Florida.Explore more on these topicsUS newsLaw (US)Social mediaMiaminewsShareReuse this content

title: United Airlines says it will ‘look into’ worker’s threat to call ICE in viral video | San Francisco | The Guardian
description: United staffer told customer ‘you don’t act like at a citizen’ during incident over wait time at San Francisco airport
Author: Roque Planas
Headline: United staffer told customer ‘you don’t act like at a citizen’ during incident over wait time at San Francisco airport
Content: A United Airlines aircraft takes off from Los Angeles international airport on 19 July in Los Angeles, California.Photograph: Weston Hancock/SOPA Images/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenA United Airlines aircraft takes off from Los Angeles international airport on 19 July in Los Angeles, California.Photograph: Weston Hancock/SOPA Images/ShutterstockSan FranciscoUnited Airlines says it will ‘look into’ worker’s threat to call ICE in viral videoUnited staffer told customer ‘you don’t act like at a citizen’ during incident over wait time at San Francisco airportRoque PlanasMon 20 Jul 2026 18.40 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.41 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleUnited Airlinesis “looking into” a viral video showing a United Airlines employee threatening to call Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) on Mexican American man atSan Franciscointernational Airport last week.The scene erupted after Julio Varela of San Ramon grew upset at United Airlines staff for taking a long time to correct an error his family made when entering his daughter’s name to buy a ticket for flight to Canada,according to NBC News.After three hours of waiting and a sour exchange with aUnited Airlinesstaffer, Varela pulled out his phone and demanded the woman to identify her self.“I’m asking for your name so I can report you,” Varela said in avideothat hasattracted wide attention.The United staffer responded by pulling out her own phone and recording him.“Maybe we should call ICE on you,” the woman replied. “You don’t act like a citizen.”Ramon, who is anaturalized citizen, grew still more angry after that comment.“Call ICE on me, lady,” the man says.The two then trade volleys, with Varela repeatedly saying “say it again” as she responds: “Get out my face.”“United Airlines has a racist customer service lady!” Varela yells. “She’s lazy as hell!”Another United Airlines staffer can be seen sighing and rolling her eyes.“We’re looking into the interaction in this video, but don’t have anything additional to share at this time,” the airline wrote in an emailed statement.Varelaexpressed concernabout possibly being detained by ICE upon his return to the United States from Montreal, despite being a US citizen. But nothing happened when he returned, according to NBC News.Explore more on these topicsSan FranciscoUnited AirlinesAir transportICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)US immigrationCaliforniaWest CoastnewsShareReuse this content

title: Five people die in attempt to save struggling swimmer in Ohio river | Ohio | The Guardian
description: Rescue crews find two women, pronounced dead at hospital, and the bodies of three men after motorist calls 911
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Rescue crews find two women, pronounced dead at hospital, and the bodies of three men after motorist calls 911
Content: A section of the Scioto River's shore in the Columbus suburb of Powell, Ohio.Photograph: Patrick Aftoora-Orsagos/APView image in fullscreenA section of the Scioto River's shore in the Columbus suburb of Powell, Ohio.Photograph: Patrick Aftoora-Orsagos/APOhioFive people die in attempt to save struggling swimmer in Ohio riverRescue crews find two women, pronounced dead at hospital, and the bodies of three men after motorist calls 911Associated PressMon 20 Jul 2026 18.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFive people have died in a river inOhioafter several of them went into the water to help another person who was struggling to swim, authorities said Monday.A motorist called 911 after spotting a frightened child who had been with the group and was running on a nearby road, Delaware county sheriff Jeffrey Balzer said at a news conference on Monday.Two children under age 10 have been placed in the care of family services, the sheriff said, including the child who ran for help.The group had gathered on Sunday along the Scioto River in the Columbus suburb of Powell when one of them went into the river to go swimming.“And that’s when the tragedy started to occur,” Balzer said, as the others who jumped in to help also began struggling.Rescue crews searching the water on Sunday found two women, who were pronounced dead at a hospital, Balzer said. The bodies of three men were found in the river on Monday, said Tracy Whited, a spokesperson for the Delaware county sheriff’s department.The tragedy occurred two days after five members of a family were killed in southern Utah after a flash flood swept through canyons during a hiking trip.Explore more on these topicsOhionewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘It needs to end now’: Democrats urge Trump to halt Iran war after US troop deaths | Donald Trump | The Guardian
description: President accused of having ‘lost control of this war’ as deaths of trio in Iranian military strikes fuel rising concern
Author: Robert Tait
Headline: President accused of having ‘lost control of this war’ as deaths of trio in Iranian military strikes fuel rising concern
Content: Trump in the White House Rose Garden earlier this month.Photograph: Evan Vucci/ReutersView image in fullscreenTrump in the White House Rose Garden earlier this month.Photograph: Evan Vucci/ReutersDonald Trump‘It needs to end now’: Democrats urge Trump to halt Iran war after US troop deathsPresident accused of having ‘lost control of this war’ as deaths of trio in Iranian military strikes fuel rising concernRobert Taitin WashingtonMon 20 Jul 2026 17.45 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 17.46 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDemocrats have seized on the deaths of three US troops killed in Iranian strikes to urgeDonald Trumpto urgently reverse his resumption of the war with Iran amid widespread anxiety over climbing casualties.Even as US forces launched a 10th consecutive wave of attacks against Iranian targets, concern over the deaths of American service personnel threatened to complicate moves to escalate the conflict, which has reignited after he last week walked away from a memorandum of understanding agreed withIranin June.The Pentagon released the names of two soldiers killed in a strike on Muwaffaq Salti air base in Jordan on Friday. They were First Lieutenant Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Private Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas.A third member of the armed forces was killed in northern Iraq on Saturday in what US Central Command called a “controlled detonation” of unexploded ordnance. The remains of a possible fourth dead service member were found over the weekend in Jordan.Chris Murphy, a Democratic senator for Connecticut, accused Trump of having “frighteningly lost control of this war”.“For those of us who pay attention to the hour-to-hour pace of the Iran War, things are getting very dark very quickly,” hewroteon social media. “The scale of attacks – and threats of escalation – are increasing rapidly.”The defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, declared that the US would not be deterred by the casualties, writing: “Godspeed, heroes. Their sacrifice only stiffens our resolve.”Iran has vowed to carry out retaliatory strikes against US Gulf allies, which it has branded “collaborators” for providing logistical facilities for strikes on Iranian territory.In a brief telephone conversation with NewsNation, Trump called the deaths “a very sad thing” and said the service members had died “in service of our country”.But he faced a chorus of calls fromDemocratsto step back from his belligerent posture even as Republicans remained supportive.“The reckless and costly war of choice in Iran has been a disaster for the American people,”wrote Hakeem Jeffries, the Democratic leader in the House of Representatives. “May God continue to watch over our troops who have needlessly been put into harm’s way.”Mark Warner, the vice chair of the Senate intelligence committee and a Democrat from Virginia, said there were “hundreds of seriously wounded” US personnel, and added it was “remarkable” that casualty figures had not been higher.“This is what happens when you start a war of choice. And as a member of theGang of Eight, I can say unequivocally there was no imminent threat from Iran,” he told CBS’s Face the Nation.Chris Van Hollen, a Democratic senator from Maryland who has introduced war powers resolutions aimed at ending the Iran campaign unless it receives congressional approval,posted: “This senseless war should never have been started and it needs to end NOW.”Jeanne Shaheen, the party’s ranking members of the Senate foreign relations committee, called for a return to diplomacy. “The President must negotiate an end to this disastrous war,” shewrote.And Ted Lieu, a California representative who has also vocally advocated reining in Trump through a war powers resolution,wrote: “We need Republicans to join us in passing the War Powers Resolution NOW.”The New York Times reportedthat Iranian forces carried out three strikes against US military targets in Jordan last week before Friday’s deadly attacks that were not disclosed by the Pentagon. The newspaper said the Pentagon also did not reveal “dozens” of casualties and damage inflicted by the earlier strikes.The latest casualties brought the number of US service members killed since the start of the war on 28 February to at least 17. More than 1,700 Iranian civilians are estimated to have been killed.The House and Senate have both passed war powers resolutions – requiring the president to ask for congressional approval after 60 days of hostilities – with the aim of restraining Trump. But the White House has contended that there has yet to be 60 days of continuous fighting because the clock stopped after an initial ceasefire in April, which held, shakily, for several weeks.Republicans, meanwhile, have remained publicly supportive of Trump – despite poll figures showing support for the war and the president’s conduct of it low and sliding. A Washington Post-Ipsos survey showed approval for Trump’s handling of the conflict at 29% – lower than the 33% backing his stewardship of the economy, which has been buffeted by soaring fuel prices caused by the war.A statement from New Mexico Republicans mourned the fresh casualties but accused Democrats of trying to exploit them for political reasons.“No matter how much Democrats try to politicize this, President Trump’s actions in Iran have made our country safer from a terrorist regime that has inflicted death and carnage on millions of innocent people around the world, including Americans,” the party said in a statement.James Comer, a representative from Kentucky and one of Trump’s staunchest supporters on Capitol Hill,told Fox Newsthat a military response to the service members’ deaths was essential.“I don’t think anyone would prefer that we have any type of military conflict in Iran, but that’s the world that we live in,” he said. “We have to respond, and we have to respond swiftly and with full force, and that’s what President Trump’s doing.”Riley Moore, a West Virginia Republican,told Foxthat the “precision” of the strikes on the Jordanian base suggested Iran was receiving targeting information from Russia or China. He called on the US’s Gulf allies on the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) to help.“Where do we go from here is also looking at how we’re going to work with those GCC partners in the region because this could tip over … into a regional destabilizing conflict,” he said.Explore more on these topicsDonald TrumpUS-Israel war on IranUS politicsDemocratsIranUS foreign policynewsShareReuse this content

title: DeChambeau accused of threatening to call Trump in row over Open penalty | The Open | The Guardian
description: Bryson DeChambeau has been accused of threatening to call Donald Trump during a row with senior R&A officials over his two-stroke penalty
Author: Ewan Murray
Headline: Bryson DeChambeau has been accused of threatening to call Donald Trump during a row with senior R&A officials over his two-stroke penalty
Content: Bryson DeChambeau is driven by a rules official to the scene of the penalty after his second round.Photograph: Richard Heathcote/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau is driven by a rules official to the scene of the penalty after his second round.Photograph: Richard Heathcote/Getty ImagesThe OpenDeChambeau accused of threatening to call Trump in row over Open penaltyDeChambeau given two-stroke penalty after his second roundGolfer alleged to have used US president’s name during rowEwan MurrayMon 20 Jul 2026 09.14 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBryson DeChambeau has been accused of threatening to call Donald Trump during a row with senior R&A officials on Friday after the American golfer was handed a two-shot penalty at the Open.DeChambeau’s alleged use of the US president’s name as he argued his case marks the latest bizarre turn in what wasalready the dominant storyof the 2026 Open. DeChambeau was handed a two-stroke penalty for improving the line of his swing by standing on long grass behind his ball on Royal Birkdale’s fifth hole during round two. The 32-year-old argued his case furiously, including insisting upon returning to the scene of the incident with the R&A’s head of governance and a senior referee.DeChambeau moves the dial for golf but is his posturing and pantomime worth the hassle? | Ewan MurrayRead moreUpon arrival back at the tournament scoring area and with the R&A unmoved, the chief executive, Mark Darbon, joined the conversation. During that, it is understood DeChambeau said he would phone Trump if the matter was not resolved in his favour. There is no evidence that call actually took place. DeChambeau skipped mainstream media duties throughout the Open. The R&A has not commented on the Trump connection, first reported bythe Quadrilateral.Trump is on regular speaking terms with a number of the world’s most high-profile golfers, including DeChambeau. At last year’s Ryder Cup the pair enjoyed a warm exchange. DeChambeau had said earlier that Trump would “inspire” the US at Bethpage.DeChambeau’s penalty, which Rory McIlroy latersaid was entirely correct, moved him out of second place. After Saturday’s round, DeChambeau headed immediately for Darbon’s office to ask for his round two scorecard. The unusual request was politely turned down by the R&A’s chief executive.DeChambeau has remained in the UK to play in LIV Golf’s stop at the JCBGolfand Country Club from Thursday. His tied-14th finish in the Open was his best in a major tournament in 2026. Dechambeau’s representatives have been approached for comment.Explore more on these topicsThe OpenBryson DeChambeauDonald TrumpPGA TourEuropean TourGolfUS sportsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Angel Reese accepts Brondello’s apology after ‘protected species’ comment lands ban | WNBA | The Guardian
description: Angel Reese has accepted Toronto Tempo coach Sandy Brondello’s apology for an inappropriate comment about the Atlanta Dream star
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Angel Reese has accepted Toronto Tempo coach Sandy Brondello’s apology for an inappropriate comment about the Atlanta Dream star
Content: Angel Reese is in her first season with the Atlanta Dream.Photograph: Brett Davis/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAngel Reese is in her first season with the Atlanta Dream.Photograph: Brett Davis/Getty ImagesWNBAAngel Reese accepts Brondello’s apology after ‘protected species’ comment lands banToronto coach made comment during Friday’s gameTerm common in Australia but could be seen as racistAssociated PressMon 20 Jul 2026 06.50 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAngel Reese has accepted Toronto Tempo coach Sandy Brondello’s apology for an inappropriate comment about the Atlanta Dream star.TheWNBAsuspended Brondello one game for being overheard calling Reese a “protected species” during the Dream’s win over the Tempo on Friday.“First, I appreciate Sandy’s apology, but I also appreciate the love and support that I’ve had for the last 48 hours,” Reese said before the Dream played the Chicago Sky on Sunday. “It has been very overwhelming with a lot of love and also other things. But I am grateful that the league did take action.“There is no place for discrimination or hate in this league from anyone – from fans to coaches to players. And I’m just grateful that we are able to move on from the situation and just play winning basketball. The WNBA is supposed to be positive and we are going in a great light right now, so I just want to continue that and move forward.”Brondello’s comment about Reese was picked up by the broadcast after Toronto player Nyara Sabally went down with an injury and was called for a foul following contact with Reese.Brondello apologized on social media and Reese posted “ARE WE SURPRISED?!” after the game, tagging Brondello’s account, followed by a clown face emoji.Brondello, who is in her first season with the expansion Tempo, is Australian. The “protected species” phrase is used in Australian sports to express frustration for a lack of calls against a player. However, in the US the phrase suggests that someone isn’t human and could be seen as derogatory toward Black people.“Have a ton of respect for her, but I wanted to make sure that [she knows] it was an emotional statement,” Brondello said on Sunday. “It’s a statement that’s very common in Australia, it wasn’t directed at Angel. I’d never do that to any kind of player. That’s not the kind of coach I am or player back in my day. It was just unfortunate. I was pretty emotional with Nyara, her being injured again. I made a comment that, Australians, we say all the time.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Weekly for the 2026 WNBA seasonSign up toWNBA 30A limited run women’s basketball newsletter dedicated to the league’s historic 30th season.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“Unfortunately, I should have took a breath and realized where I am in America because I know racism is such a big issue here. I’ve been in this league for 27 years and, obviously, I never had any issues like this because these are all my teammates and I’ve learnt so much from the Black players that I’ve coached and that I’ve had on staff.”Explore more on these topicsWNBABasketballnewsShareReuse this content

title: A big red speaker, Louis Vuitton trophy trunks and a tattoo bet – how Spain celebrated World Cup win | Spain | The Guardian
description: Luis de la Fuente’s team was the best at the tournament by some margin but are not satisfied yet with focus now on ‘the next game in September’
Author: Sid Lowe
Headline: Luis de la Fuente’s team was the best at the tournament by some margin but are not satisfied yet with focus now on ‘the next game in September’
Content: Gavi, Lamine Yamal and Pedri of Spain with the World Cup trophy after victory against Argentina.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenGavi, Lamine Yamal and Pedri of Spain with the World Cup trophy after victory against Argentina.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesSpainA big red speaker, Louis Vuitton trophy trunks and a tattoo bet – how Spain celebrated World Cup winLuis de la Fuente’s team was the best at the tournament by some margin but are not satisfied yet with focus now on ‘the next game in September’Sid Lowein New YorkMon 20 Jul 2026 15.05 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOn the night that Spain won their first World Cup in 2010, Rodrigo Hernández was on camp somewhere lost in the woods of Connecticut. He had been canoeing when theselecciónbeat Germany in the semi-final, upset to have missed it, and for the final he convinced one of the supervisors to let him watch, alone, on a mini-laptop in the hut where the staff slept. When Andrés Iniesta scored thewinning goalin the 116th minute, he ran off screaming, doing laps of the lake. He was 14.On the night that Spain won their second World Cup 16 years later,Rodrigo Hernández was back in the US, not so far from there. WhenFerran Torres scoredthe winning goal in the 106th minute, he ran off screaming in a straighter line and accompanied this time: towards the corner where his otherfamilywere. And then, when it ended, when the fighting finished too, he collected the trophy, pausing just long enough for Donald Trump to leave centre stage before lifting it, his timing perfect even here.It wasn’t the only trophy he had, but those could be handed over and this could not.By the time Rodri departed at 9.38pm eastern time, the last man out of the dressing room at the New Jersey New York Stadium, he couldn’t carry them all: walking with him, just ahead, Ladis had to lend a hand. A football team is never just the players, nor even just the coaches. When he departed the dressing room nine minutes earlier, the same happened for Pau Cubarsí. Nuria helped him, their boy: just 19, looking like a kid down here in the stadium in real life but not on the pitch where he’s a leader. Luis de la Fuente was accompanied by Paloma.Rodri came with the trophy for the tournament’s best player too. Cubarsí had the young player award,Unai Simón the Golden Glove. They also had two World Cups, every Spain player did: a replica of their own in a black Louis Vuitton trophy trunk, not easy to fit under an arm, and another in Lego. Rodri had three: both of those andthecup. They departed laden down, great big plastic bags full, pieces of the history they made. Shirts, in red and yellow and blue and white. Balls, boots. They had cut the net and taken pieces of that too. They had been given rings, NBA style.View image in fullscreenMikel Merino holds a replica of the trophy in the dressing room after Spain’s triumph.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesBefore the final began, Marc Cucurella had sneaked off towards the touchline and buried a coin in the pitch, like that other left-back Joan Capdevila had done in 2010. Iniesta had been out there, his name chanted, as he showed the cup to the stands. After it, Sergio Ramos brought it back again. Dashing out, excitedly kissing it, he placed it on the plinth for this new generation, achieving the impossible in emulating them, to go and collect. One by one they passed, Borja Iglesias and Lamine Yamal among the most evasive in shaking Trump’s hand, until Rodri took the trophy and lifted it to the sky.Nico Williams went to find his mum in the front row and handed over his medal. Lamine Yamal had made his way across to Lionel Messi, who was sitting but who stood for him, embracingthe boy in the bath, the successor: a rite of passage, an image that will be for ever. The actor Javier Bardem was hugging Iglesias, his new friend and idol. Ferran Torres was talking about a “great liberation”: he had suffered, felt how cruel this game could be, how people could be too, but he said: “Destiny is written: God gives to those who deserve.” AndKeyne was, well, Keyne.
