In the dressing room – the same one, Cucurella noted, where he had been at last year’s Club World Cup – the champagne was cracked open.It was brief – the flight toSpainwas scheduled for 11pm and they wanted to get back – and soon they were on their way again. Behind the screen, along the corridor they came, way down in the depths and the half-light of the stadium. The president, the staff, all the players; the many, many people who made this so. Iglesias carried a bag so big it was like he was a student taking his washing home, and he was struggling to stop that box slipping. In the other hand he wheeled a big red speaker. Now playing: Dirty Cash (Money Talks) by PAWSA & Adventures of Stevie V. Lamine Yamal came, via the anti-doping room, in orange sunglasses, four chains, black cap on backwards. He didn’t wear his medal, but most did, thick discs round their necks. And commemorative shirts, ordered up in case.View image in fullscreenSpain’s Lamine Yamal and Argentina’s Lionel Messi embrace after the match.Photograph: Jeenah Moon/ReutersBack in Dallas, at a celebrationafter the semi-final victory, someone had joked to the federation president, Rafael Louzán, that the shirts should say world champions on one side and Finalissima winners on the other, two trophies in one. Tempting, but these bore just the one trophy, “Spain 26” on the back. The war in the Middle East had prevented the Finalissima from going ahead in Doha as scheduled in March and every time another option was offered, Argentina said no, the impression that of a team, a federation, running scared. “Good job we didn’t play,” Emi Martínez had said then, after they scraped past Mauritania in a hastily arranged friendly that filled the hole. “If we had played [Spain] like this …”Something like this might have happened. Not just because Argentina played like this but because Spain made them play like this. “We gave them a battering,” came the verdict from one of those heading out of the dressing room. This wasn’t just a win; this was more. Has a 1-0 ever been as dominant? There was a moment late in the game, still at 0-0, when the Spain fans chanted: “Look at them! Look at them! How they shit themselves!” and they weren’t wrong. Argentina could not get out all game; as Spain turned the screw, still less, the goal seemingly inevitable; the surprise was that it hadn’t come yet, that there would be just one. When it did it came as a liberation but also an act of irresistible logic.View image in fullscreenFerran Torres scores the only goal of the World Cup final.Photograph: Jewel Samad/AFP/Getty ImagesTorres’s shot was Spain’s 20th. Argentina didn’t manage one until the 115th minute. They didn’t manage any on target, all game. Across 900World Cupgames for which Opta has the stats, it is only the third time that a team has failed to shoot. And this in the final. They had done to Argentina what they did to France and to everyone, choking them. For the whole tournament Spain had an xG against of 0.3 per game. “If my gloves could talk, they would say they didn’t have much work to do,” Simón said. “Many teams depend too much on the back line,” Aymeric Laporte said, but not Spain.This was thetriumph of a collective, of a group; it ended in New York, but it was built in Chattanooga and beyond, over 51 days. It is the triumph of an idea, a conviction and of a coach too: a victory for De la Fuente and the system he leads and the system that made him. He began his coaching career at Deportivo Alavés in the third tier, where he lasted just 11 games. It was November 2011 when he left and he has not had a club job since. For months, no one returned his calls. He was on the dole for a year and a half until he saw an advert for a job: the Spanish federation was looking for a coach for the youth system. He started in the under-17s. He won the European Championship with the under-19s and the under-21s. Then he won it with the senior side; now he has won the World Cup.“They asked me what there is left to win; we said the next game in September because they’re insatiable,” he insisted afterwards. Simón said: “The manager is outstanding; he has been our leader.” Cucurella, meanwhile, was in trouble. He had promised that if Spain won the World Cup, he would get a tattoo of De la Fuente’s face and now he had to decide where to put it to minimise the damage, the flight home an opportunity to think. Not least to think what a fool he had been.View image in fullscreenMarc Cucurella is to have a tattoo of Luis de la Fuente’s face after Spain’s win.Photograph: Lisi Niesner/Reuters“I said: ‘You made a bad mistake’,” the coach joked. “But it’s not like I am so horrendously ugly that you have to put it somewhere it can’t be seen. And they are men of their word.” For all the laughs, that final line really mattered, and he knows: 10 of them played with him at junior level. The first title he ever won was the Under-19 Euros in 2015. In the middle of his midfield that day was Rodrigo Hernández.Eleven years on, 16 since he watched Spain’s first World Cup win, motivation sought and identification found in what they achieved, Rodri was back in the US. The embodiment of that idea, a player who approached perfection here, no one played more passes nor made more tackles than him. No one had his weight either, the captain leading Spain out and leading them back too. Like so many of his teammates at a tournament that was an expression of justice and vindication too, he had suffered for this,a torn cruciate ligament overcome.“It doesn’t matter if you fall, there’s always a reason to get up again,” he said and now he had it right here in his hands. It was 9.40pm when he boarded the bus bound for the airport, carrying the actual World Cup.Explore more on these topicsSpainWorld Cup 2026World CupfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The 2026 World Cup was full of spectacle, scandal and compromise | Soccer | The Guardian
description: This was a good rather than great World Cup on the field. In the background, things were a lot less edifying
Author: Jonathan Wilson
Headline: This was a good rather than great World Cup on the field. In the background, things were a lot less edifying
Content: Folarin Balogun, Leandro Paredes, Gavi, Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino all played roles in the tournament.Composite: Getty Images, Guardian designView image in fullscreenFolarin Balogun, Leandro Paredes, Gavi, Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino all played roles in the tournament.Composite: Getty Images, Guardian designSoccer with Jonathan WilsonSoccerThe 2026 World Cup was full of spectacle, scandal and compromiseThis was a good rather than great World Cup on the field. In the background, things were a lot less edifyingJonathan WilsonMon 20 Jul 2026 12.45 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 12.47 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePerhaps it’s too early, with the shreds of gold tape still fluttering through the New Jersey sky, to make a proper assessment. That will only come when everything has sunk in properly, when memories have had the chance to settle and shape themselves, when the consequences of certain events have become clear. But let’s try anyway to make some sort of assessment of where the World Cup that ended withLeandro Paredes grappling with Gaviranks historically.The Balogun Affaircould yet have major consequences. At the moment, it feels that the world has moved on, but that’s the nature of tournaments. They have a momentum of their own, a drive towards the finish that tends to sweep everything else aside. But there can be little doubt that the bending before political pressure, sweeping aside 64 years of precedent to ensure a particular player could take the stage, just as Gianni Infantinohad done with Cristiano Ronaldo, just as he had done in gerrymandering the qualification criteria for the Club World Cup to ensure Lionel Messi played, represents a milestone.‘Beyond reproach’: Argentinian media praises Scaloni’s team despite World Cup final defeatRead morePerhaps in the future we will simply accept the principle that famous players get dispensation because making money is more important than justice or the good of the game – the prioritisation of revenue generation has been a feature of the 2026 tournament – or perhaps this will stiffen the sinews of opposition to Infantino; it’s been notable, for instance, how many British politicians – and some American ones – have taken to criticising Fifa and its president. An immediate consequence could be the expansion of theWorld Cupto 64 teams as Infantino looks to shore up his base, particularly given the damage US (and Canadian) immigration policy has done to his claims to be a champion of the global south.Other issues that have sunk into the mulch of games will surely resurface.The treatment of Iran, for instance, was scandalous, and sets a worrying precedent. Ticket prices may be lower at the 2030 World Cup, but the idea that a reasonable proportion should be available to the regular fans who go to league football every week is now gone. The World Cup is no longer a mass movement but a luxury event.The price paid by the host cities, similarly, will only become truly apparent in retrospect. That is financial, but it’s also social. In the US and Canada, there have beenstreet sweeps of the homeless, people with very little deprived of what few possessions they have, ID and medication. In Mexico, there has been an outcry about water extraction and environmental damage linked to World Cup projects.The Italian newspaper la Gazzetta dello Sport ran a double page on Saturday calling the World Cup a “disaster”, outlining certain major issues: ticket prices, Balogun,the weather, the increase in games, the decrease of rest. That may be an overstatement, but what followed really was a disaster as the newspaper found its ticket allocation for the final slashed from five to two. It’s not entirely clear whether the two were linked, but the idea that a media outlet could be denied access for rejecting the official narrative that this World Cup has been the greatest ever is dystopian.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice weeklySign up toSoccer Desk: World Cup EditionJonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDonald Trump has to let it linger as he hangs on for Spain’s World Cup celebrationsRead moreThat fitted the generally contemptuous attitude of Fifa towards the media at this tournament. While some venues – notably Houston and Atlanta – went out of their way to be welcoming, basic organisation and facilities were the worst for at least six World Cups. Quite understandably, nobody in the real world cares about journalists and whether the wifi works, but it’s indicative of a more general approach: fans are revenue units to be milked mercilessly; the media an irritating imposition.The football, meanwhile, was good but not great. There were a number of fine and dramatic games – Brazil v Japan, Morocco v the Netherlands, England v Mexico,Argentinav Cape Verde, Norway v Côte d’Ivoire, Belgium v Senegal, Norway v Brazil, Argentina v Egypt – but unfortunately almost all of them were in the last 32 or last 16. The quarter-finals were not especially inspiring. Argentina’s win over England in the semi was thrilling and involved a collapse that will be discussed for years, but Spain’s win over France was almost too easy; Spain quickly established a degree of superiority over the French that was never really challenged.And the final was dismal, an awful affair. The right team won, in their familiar bloodless way, but the grim spectacle was a direct consequence of the indulgence shown Argentina earlier in the tournament. The ending, inevitably, colours what went before, and drags this tournament behind 2022 and 2018, let alone the glorious World Cups of the 1980s or 1998. Posterity can judge where exactly in the middle rank it sits, whether above or below 2014 and 2006. It was not great but it was good and, beyond the Fifa bluster, that’s fine.This is an extract from Soccer Desk, a newsletter from the Guardian.Subscribe for free here.Explore more on these topicsFootballSoccer with Jonathan WilsonWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainArgentinanewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Gangs of New Jersey: how the World Cup final descended into ugly scenes between Argentina and Spain | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: A blow-by-blow account of how Spain and Argentina players fought in the aftermath of the Sunday showpiece
Author: Joe Pinner
Headline: A blow-by-blow account of how Spain and Argentina players fought in the aftermath of the Sunday showpiece
Content: Argentina's Leandro Paredes scrapes with Spain’s Gavi at the New York New Jersey Stadium following victory for Luis de la Fuente’s men.Photograph: Marvin Ibo Guengoer/GES Sportfoto/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenArgentina's Leandro Paredes scrapes with Spain’s Gavi at the New York New Jersey Stadium following victory for Luis de la Fuente’s men.Photograph: Marvin Ibo Guengoer/GES Sportfoto/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Gangs of New Jersey: how the World Cup final descended into ugly scenes between Argentina and SpainA blow-by-blow account of how players from both sides fought in the aftermath of the Sundau showpieceJoe PinnerMon 20 Jul 2026 09.48 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheWorld Cupfinal took an ugly turn at the end of extra time as Spain were crowned champions. Here’s what happened, frame by frame.It’s all over … or is it?View image in fullscreenSpain’s substitutes run on to the pitch in celebration as the final whistle blows in the World Cup final on Sunday.Photograph: Alexandre Martins/DPPI/ShutterstockAs the referee, Slavko Vincic, blows the full-time whistle, Spain’s substitutes run on to the pitch to celebrate World Cup glory with their teammates.Molina strikes firstView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Nahuel Molina takes a swing at Spain’s Rodri.Photograph: BBCRodri is struck by Nahuel Molina near the halfway line, the Argentinian right-back aggressively throwing his arm out at Spain’s captain.… Rodri reactsView image in fullscreenMolina and Rodri square-up following their initial confrontation.Photograph: John Sibley/ReutersRodri turns back and confronts Molina, and the two players square up as their teammates remain unaware of what’s unfolding.Eric García gets involvedView image in fullscreenSpain’s Eric García and Rodri argue with Argentina’s Nahuel MolinaPhotograph: James Lang/Imagn Images/ReutersGarcía sees what is happening and runs over to Rodri and Molina in an attempt to prevent the confrontation from escalating.… so too does ParedesView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Leandro Paredes scuffles with Spain’s Eric García.Photograph: John Angelillo/UPI/ShutterstockNow it’s Leandro Paredes’s turn to enter the fray. He sprints across and attacks García, sending him flying. The linesman attempts to get between the two players, but to no avail.Paredes goes at García againView image in fullscreenLeandro Paredes grabs Eric García by the throat.Photograph: Leandro Paredes grabs Eric Garcia by the throat/ITVGarcía gets up only to be attacked by Paredes once again, theArgentinamidfielder grabbing his Spanish counterpart by the throat as the line assitant continues to try to break up the pair. Argentina’s coaching staff – including the head coach, Lionel Scaloni – surround García and protect him from their own teammate.Ganging up on GaviView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Leandro Paredes and Thiago Almada clash with Spain’s Gavi.Photograph: James Lang/Imagn Images/ReutersParedes turns his attention to Gavi. The 21-year-old midfielder finds himself pushed and pulled in different directions by Paredes, and also the Argentina substitute Thiago Almada, who rip the Spainard’s training bib. Gavi falls to the floor and attempts to get back up before Paredes throws him down again.A happy endingView image in fullscreenGavi lifts the World Cup trophy while surrounded by his jubilant Spain teammates.Photograph: William Volcov/ShutterstockLeandro Paredes is sent off after the final whistle, with Argentina facing disciplinary proceedings: Fifa is to investigate the post-match scenes before deciding whether to take action.Spain meanwhile celebrate winning the World Cup for the second time in the country’s history.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026ArgentinaSpainWorld CupfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Donald Trump has to let it linger as he hangs on for Spain’s World Cup celebrations | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: For the second time in as many years, the US president made sure he was a part of a moment of triumph he had little to do with
Author: Alexander Abnos
Headline: For the second time in as many years, the US president made sure he was a part of a moment of triumph he had little to do with
Content: 0:30Trump and Infantino booed at World Cup final – videoWorld Cup 2026Donald Trump has to let it linger as he hangs on for Spain’s World Cup celebrationsFor the second time in as many years, the US president made sure he was a part of a moment of triumph he had little to do withAlexander Abnosat New York New Jersey StadiumMon 20 Jul 2026 04.30 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHe did it again. There was a world championship trophy, waiting to be held aloft at the same gargantuan stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey. There was Gianni Infantino, waiting alongside the US president. And there was that same US president, Donald Trump, fresh off a round ofstadium-wide booingand jeering, handing off the trophy with Infantino and then staying stubbornly in the frame as another team enjoyed the pinnacle of their professional careers with an octogenarian standing awkwardly in the shot.Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino booed before US president attempts to crash Spain celebrationsRead moreShould we be surprised? Marc Cucurella probably wasn’t – the defender was on stage for both occasions, first as a member of Chelsea as they lifted the 2025 Club World Cup, and then again on Sunday when Trumponce again remained in the shotas Spain celebrated their World Cup victory.The repeated history brought an incongruous end to a night where Trump was – for most of the time – a long way from the center of attention. That would have been a relief to American sports fans, for whom a Drake-like sentiment has grown surrounding Trump’s involvement in their event of choice. Meaning, essentially, that it should be dreaded, and avoided at all costs. The New York Knicks lost their only game of the NBA finals this year with Trump in attendance at Madison Square Garden, where he also drewa lusty round of boos. And, earlier in this World Cup, the US crashed to a 4-1 defeat against Belgium in the last-16 after Trump called Infantino in a successful bid to get striker Folarin Balogun’sred card suspended.If Trump had a similarly ruinous effect on Sunday’s World Cup final, it was in a cosmic sense. Trump watched the game from a glassed-in suite, along withInfantino, Spain’s king, Felipe VI, Canada prime minister, Mark Carney, and Mexico’s president, Claudia Sheinbaum. The US secretary of state Marco Rubio was also in attendance. At one point in the second half, Trump was seen conversing with Carney.What unfolded on the other side of the presumably bulletproof glass was 120 minutes of punishing, physical football – an enervating match that practically cried out for a sideshow.Trump wouldn’t become that sideshow until later. He was briefly shown on the video screens at the stadium after the US national anthem was played, and there were scattered boos from some in the crowd. TV broadcasts showed him touching the trophy from his seat in the early stages of the match, and conversing with Melania Trump in the 85th minute, but he didn’t appear on the screens in the stadium during the match itself.The hostile reception he had received in his appearances at other sporting events wasn’t repeated until he descended from his perch and walked on to the field to present theWorld Cuptrophy along with Infantino and the other heads of state, where the vast majority of the crowd booed and jeered.What followed was routine, up to the key moment. Trump presented medals to the players alongside the other dignitaries, shaking hands and smiling at each player with a minimum of fuss, seemingly telling them “great job” or “congratulations”. Trump and Infantino then held the World Cup trophy together and presented it to Spain’s captain, Rodri, with Infantino ducking away, stage right.Allow content provided by a third party?This article includes content hosted onembed.bsky.app. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as the provider may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueIf there were lessons to be learned from Trump’s crashing of the Chelsea party last year, Infantino hadn’t learned them. Or perhaps he still felt he was not within his right to grab Trump by the arm and pull him away. Trump was then practically given an invite to leave by Rodri, who motioned Trump to the left of the podium as he prepared to set the Spanish celebrations alight. Trump made as if he would descend the staircase, but instead stood just on the edge of celebrations, seemingly not totally sure where to look, forcing Infantino to jog over and make one last plea for him to leave. It was too late. Rodri lifted the trophy, and there was Trump, in the frame. By the time the confetti fell to the grass, Trump had gone, lingering just long enough to be visible.Just as Lionel Messi lifted his first World Cup in 2022 wearing a traditional Arab cloak, Spain hoisted their country’s second title with a reminder of the host nation lingering awkwardly in the frame.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026World CupDonald TrumpGianni InfantinoFootball politicsFifafeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Top 30: from half a million World Cup photos, why I love these the most | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Our picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral, that delicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in Gaza
Author: Jonny Weeks
Headline: Our picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral, that delicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in Gaza
Content: Harry Kane is embraced by Jude Bellingham after his first goal against DR Congo.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenHarry Kane is embraced by Jude Bellingham after his first goal against DR Congo.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Top 30: from half a million World Cup photos, why I love these the mostOur picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral,thatdelicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in GazaJonny WeeksMon 20 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.34 EDTShareWhat makes a greatWorld Cupphotograph? Now there’s a question with myriad answers. Composition, newsworthiness, emotional resonance, expert timing and luck can all contribute to varying degrees. But one thing they all share is the power to capture attention.On the Guardian sport picture desk, we have received more than 500,000 World Cup photographs over the past six weeks, with so many gems among them. Here, with much applause to the photographers who created them and a slight bias towardsEngland, are my favourite images of the tournament.1Messi’s cathedralView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi prepares to take a corner kick against Switzerland at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Julian Finney/Fifa/Getty ImagesAn absolute cracker to start with. In this picture, the cavernous Kansas City Stadium sweeps around a small corner of grass on which the world’s most famous footballer stands alone. You can’t really see it’s Lionel Messi, but somehow you can tell it’s him. It feels as though everybody’s eyes are pointed in his direction, like they’re worshipping a deity in an epic footballing cathedral.2Goalline scrambleView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Endrick flings his boot at a loose ball as Japan’s players scramble to clear their lines.Photograph: Annegret Hilse/ReutersTechnological improvements have led to the creation of high-quality miniature cameras which can be placed inside goalmouths and operated remotely. Seeing the game at such close quarters feels wonderfully dynamic. This frame in particular captures a mad scramble between Brazil and Japan; the wonky angle adds to the sense of chaos.3Tuchel’s close upView image in fullscreenEngland’s manager, Thomas Tuchel, during the national anthem before the match with Croatia at Dallas Stadium.Photograph: Bradley Collyer/PAA self-referential choice, but this was a big news story at the time: England played Croatia in their first game and the England manager, Thomas Tuchel, was so furious he couldn’t see the players during the national anthem that he asked Fifa to move the photographers for subsequent games. In fairness to him, you can see why. Four weeks later, Tuchel would garner headlines again after his substitutions in the semi-final againstArgentinatriggered England’s collapse.4Balogun sees redView image in fullscreenThe USA’s Folarin Balogun fouls Tarik Muharemovic of Bosnia and Herzegovina, leading to a red card after a VAR check.Photograph: Michael Steele/Getty ImagesAt first glance it looks innocuous enough, then you notice Tarik Muharemovic’s right ankle buckling under the pressure applied by Folarin Balogun’s mistimed challenge. Replays of the incident suggested the foul was unintentional, though the Bosnian was lucky to escape injury. Balogun received a red card following a VAR review, but his one-game ban was suspended by Fifa after lobbying from Donald Trump. With Balogun back in the team, the USA were dumped out of their own tournament by Belgium in the next round.5Tartan traumaView image in fullscreenScotland’s Findlay Curtis (left) and Jack Hendry in the tunnel after their defeat by Brazil in Miami.Photograph: Carmen Mandato/Fifa/Getty ImagesI’ve relished seeing so many photographsfrom inside the tunnels at this World Cupbecause the players seem to reveal a little more of themselves away from the public eye. This candid shot of Scotland players Findlay Curtis and Jack Hendry shows them in the wake of their 3-0 loss to Brazil – a defeat that was filled with calamity, and which ultimately led to Scotland’s early exit. They look like they’re outside a head teacher’s office waiting to be reprimanded.6Diving headerView image in fullscreenEngland’s midfielder Jude Bellingham scores his team’s first goal against Mexico at the Azteca.Photograph: Ulises Ruiz/AFP/Getty ImagesI love a good diving header and this one by Jude Bellingham against Mexico was peachy. From a photographic point of view, you seldom get shots from tribune positions like this during a Premier League season, so they have a real novelty value during major tournaments. But it’s more than just novelty: the elevated vantage point helps to remove the surrounding clutter and give a proper fan’s eye view of the goal. I also love the verdant grass, the shocking pink goalkeeper’s jersey and the sense of anticipation because the ball has yet to cross the line. It reminds me of the signed photograph of Coventry’s Keith Houchen, from the 1987 FA Cup final, that hangs proudly on my dad’s office wall.7Bruno’s early bathView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Bruno Guimarães cools down with a splash of water in the first half against Morocco in New Jersey.Photograph: Adam Hunger/APSo many hydration breaks, so much Powerade, but only one player managed to look like a Portuguese water dog fresh from a dip in a lake. The Brazil midfielder Bruno Guimarães is pictured here dousing himself in H2O when his side faced Morocco in their opening game at New York New Jersey Stadium. Brazil ultimately went home for an early bath after losing to Norway in the last 16.8Viking row boatView image in fullscreenNorway supporters gather to perform their rowing celebration on the iconic red steps in Times Square.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesThe mass rowing displays performed by Norway’s fans this summer have been a viral sensation. Seen everywhere from Times Square to Miami Beach, they also became a fixture of the team’s post-match partying, with Erling Haaland and Martin Ødegaard both banging a fan’s drum as the side progressed to the quarter-finals. The Vikings were stopped by England, but their journey was one of the feelgood stories of the tournament.9Light and shadeView image in fullscreenKylian Mbappé of France dribbles the ball against Sweden at New York New Jersey Stadium.Photograph: Justin Setterfield/Getty ImagesA gorgeous pool of afternoon sunlight spilling on to the pitch, surrounded by deep shadows. You couldn’t really create better low-key lighting conditions if you tried. The photographer just had to nail the exposure and wait for a subject to pass through. The fact that it’s Kylian Mbappé who’s skating across the turf in his rich blue France top adds a nice splash of colour and gravity to the scene.10Tunnel visionView image in fullscreenLuka Modric of Croatia prepares in the tunnel before their match with Panama in Toronto.Photograph: Joosep Martinson/Fifa/Getty ImagesLuka Modric has spoken in the past aboutnot wanting to let his people down, and of carrying memories of his grandpa, Luka, with him. He appears to be deep in thought here before playing Panama. Perhaps it’s just a fleeting moment – a man simply massaging his temple or visualising the first tackle – or maybe he’s remembering his war-torn roots and the family member he was named after.11Loving VozinhaView image in fullscreenCape Verde’s Vozinha after the match against Spain in Atlanta.Photograph: Brett Davis/Imagn Images/ReutersAt the ripe age of 40 and with a club career that has taken him to Cyprus, Moldova, Portugal and Slovakia, the Cape Verde goalkeeper Vozinha became the breakout star at this year’s World Cup. His numerous saves againstSpainin the group stage earned him global praise as well as 17 million followers on Instagram. Another stellar display against Argentina, in which he memorably denied a quick free-kick by Messi, further enhanced his reputation. I like his expression in this photograph as he soaks up the adulation from the crowd following the Blue Sharks’ remarkable goalless draw with Spain.12Gaza screeningView image in fullscreenPalestinian football fans gather among war-damaged buildings in Gaza City to watch the match between Argentina and Egypt on a giant outdoor screen.Photograph: Mohammed Skaik/TheNews2/Cover ImagesThis photograph shows the power of football while also putting the World Cup into perspective. Palestinian residents in Gaza gathered amid the ruins to watch a screening of Egypt’s game against Argentina. A moment of normality amid the horror and heartache.13Power headerView image in fullscreenErling Haaland of Norway scores against Brazil at New York New Jersey Stadium.Photograph: Image Photo Agency/Getty ImagesErling Haaland’s first goal against Brazil left his old Premier League rival, Gabriel Magalhães, in a heap. In this shot you can see water (or perhaps sweat) flying off the striker’s forehead, his neck fully strained after connecting with the ball. Haaland scored seven goals in five World Cup games: he netted from inside the box, outside the box, with his left foot, right foot and of course his head. Norway have still never been beaten by Brazil after five matches. No other nation can say that.14Lamine Yamal’s bracesView image in fullscreenLamine Yamal bites the Spain badge during an official Fifa photocall prior to the tournament.Photograph: Karl Bridgeman/Fifa/Getty ImagesThis image was shot before the tournament,as part of Fifa’s official player portraits, but I’ve only recently seen it – and it’s well worth a mention. You don’t see many players with braces, but Lamine Yamal, who only turned 19 this month, wears his with pride. This frame was shot using a 24-70mm lens on a Canon R5 Mark II which has a whopping 45 megapixels, so I suspect it’s been cropped quite hard in post-production to achieve this composition.15Into orbitView image in fullscreenSwitzerland’s Manuel Akanji misses a penalty during the shootout against Colombia.Photograph: Agustín Marcarian/ReutersSome penalty misses are worse than others. Germany’s Jonathan Tah and Manuel Akanji of Switzerland both had Chris Waddle moments as they blew their chances from 12 yards over the bar. Ordinarily I’d say you need to see the crossbar to illustrate that the shot went over, but the way the ball is rocketing skywards – almost as if it could escape the stadium roof – tells the story perfectly.16Neymar’s tearsView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Neymar is consoled by Raphinha after their side were eliminated from the World Cup by Norway.Photograph: James Lang/Imagn Images/ReutersEven when your ego is as big as the sun, sometimes you just need a cuddle. This photograph captures Neymar Jr’s visible distress as he realises his international career is over and his childhood ambitions of lifting the World Cup will never be fulfilled. His teammate Raphina is there to support him. It lacks the humility ofFreddie Flintoff consoling his opponent Brett Leein the Ashes, or thestoicism of Derek Redmond being helped across the finish lineby his father at the 1992 Olympics, but it still packs an emotional gut punch.17Upside downView image in fullscreenJulián Alvarez with teammates after scoring Argentina’s second goal against Switzerland at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Ryan Pierse/Fifa/Getty ImagesThis photograph was flipped 180 degrees by Getty Images before being transmitted to us. Manipulation of this ilk feels almost heretical in the world of photojournalism, but the result is very compelling. It shows Julián Alvarez celebrating his late winner against Switzerland, and it makes me question my fundamental photographic principles. Would I publish it like this in print to illustrate a news story? Absolutely not. Would I include it in an online gallery of images that caught my eye this summer? Evidently so.18Big screenView image in fullscreenA giant video screen signals a goal for Spain against Saudi Arabia in Atlanta.Photograph: Colin Hubbard/APThe stadums in the US have been mind-blowing in scale. Atlanta Stadium, home of the NFL’s Atlanta Falcons and the city’s MLS and NWSL teams, has a maximum capacity of 75,000 people, a climate-controlled environment and a 335-metre display known as the “halo”. This picture from up in the gods looks suitably epic. Initially I assumed the camera was remote-controlled, but it transpires some photographers were actually working up there on the gantry. What a view they had!19Motion blurView image in fullscreenSweden winger Anthony Elanga is challenged by two Japan defenders during their game at Dallas Stadium.Photograph: Lars Baron/Getty ImagesThis is a beautiful example of a photographer using a slow shutter speed while panning gently with the action. I especially like the details within: Anthony Elanga’s facial expression is crisp, and the pink boots leave a painterly trail.20Heroic HarryView image in fullscreenHarry Kane and Jude Bellingham of England celebrate against DR Congo at Atlanta Stadium.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesHarry Kane’s late strikes against the Democratic Republic of Congo in the last 32 led to an outpouring of emotion from the England captain. I was torn over which frame to select – there are some lovely ones of him sprinting across the turf, flanked by Jude Bellingham and Anthony Gordon – but this one of him being embraced by Bellingham serves as a perfect reminder of their twin heroics at the tournament. It’s clean, nicely balanced and full of bromance.21Gómez goes berserkView image in fullscreenGustavo Gómez of Paraguay looks delighted after José Canale scored the winning penalty in a  shootout against Germany in Boston.Photograph: Robert Cianflone/Getty ImagesThe guaranteed agony and ecstasy ofpenalty shootouts always makes for brilliant images. This year, Gustavo Gómez of Paraguay takes the crown for the wildest reaction. After beating Germany during sudden death, Gómez, who scored his own spot-kick, surged across the pitch like a heron taking flight, almost vaulting his teammates José Canale and Orlando Gill as he went to embrace them. He then careered through the photographers behind the goal in pursuit of a mobile phone so that he could document the moment himself.22Living statueView image in fullscreenMichel Nkuka Mboladinga, also known as Lumumba Vea, holds firm in the stands during their match against Colombia.Photograph: Daniel Becerril/ReutersMichel Nkuka Mboladinga, also known as Lumumba Vea, is dubbed a “living statue” because he doesn’t move during games. Instead, the DRC fan poses on a plinth for the entire match, raising his right arm to mimic the statue ofPatrice Lumumba, the first prime minister of the First Congolese Republic, which stands in Kinshasa. Why anyone would go to a football match and not respond to the on-pitch action is beyond me, but Mboladinga insists it’s his patriotic duty.23Senegal swan diveView image in fullscreenYouri Tielemans heads in Belgium’s second goal against Senegal at Seattle Stadium.Photograph: Jared C Tilton/Fifa/Getty ImagesYouri Tielemans scored twice inBelgium’s stunning comeback against Senegalin the last 32. This 89th-minute header might have been a little trickier to control had the defender Moussa Niakhaté competed for the ball instead of diving theatrically to the ground at the first sign of contact. The virtue of modern cameras, many of which shoot 30 frames per second, is that the full sequence of this goal was captured, and we could choose the exact frame when Tielemans’s head connects with the ball.24Comeback kingView image in fullscreenSasa Kalajdzic after scoring Austria’s third goal during a thrilling game against Algeria at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesSasa Kalajdzic overcame three anterior cruciate ligament injuries in the years and months prior to the 2026 World Cup, but the Austrian enjoyed a moment to savour when he netted a 96th-minute equaliser against Algeria to keep his side in the tournament. The game ignited accusations of fixing, because a 3-3 draw ensured both sides went through, but a finale like that – and celebrations like this – cannot be scripted.25Football, bloody hellView image in fullscreenConnor Metcalfe of Australia shows the referee his bloodied forehead against Paraguay at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium.Photograph: Stu Forster/Getty ImagesAustralia’s Connor Metcalfe needed three stitches in his eyebrow after being clipped by a stray boot from Andrés Cubas during the Socceroos’ group match with Paraguay. Maybe it’s the slumped shoulders or the shirt pulled up around his neckline, but he looks like a forlorn child who needs a parent to clean him up.26Big Dan BurnView image in fullscreenEngland’s Dan Burn blocks a bicycle kick from Raúl Jiménez during the last-16 match in Mexico City.Photograph: Jam Media/Getty ImagesYou could say this is just another “two men and a ball” photograph (an expression the Guardian’s photographer, Tom Jenkins, uses to describe generic images of sporting action). But I particularly like this one because of the contrast of sporting prowess – the acrobat versus the brick wall – and for the misshapen ball which appears ready to pop. Dan Burn has become a cult figure for England, in the mould of Peter Crouch. Many supporters call the 6ft 7in defender Big Dan Burn, but Bellingham apparently calls him Burninho. There’s no way Raúl Jiménez, the Mexico striker, was ever getting the ball past him.27Emotional MessiView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi after their 3-2 victory over Egypt at Atlanta Stadium.Photograph: Patrick Smith/Fifa/Getty ImagesLet’s be honest, Lionel Messi is not very good at penalties. He’s missed four of eight in World Cup matches and 34 of 151 in his career, including shootouts. A botched effort against Egypt, in a match which the African side went on to lead 2-0, could have been a pitiful ending to his gilded international career. Instead, Messi scored the equaliser and drove his team to a remarkable 3-2 win in normal time. This picture, taken after the match, speaks to the weight he carries on his shoulders. Is it relief or guilt? Either way, he looks tortured by expectation.28Crowd sceneView image in fullscreenCanada fans take in their game against Bosnia and Herzegovina at Toronto Stadium.Photograph: Joosep Martinson/Fifa/Getty ImagesThe composition of this photograph is simple but beautiful. The intersecting diagonals, the contrast between light and shade, and the depth within the scene … it makes for rich viewing.29Koné’s injury painView image in fullscreenCanada’s Ismaël Koné on the ground after sustaining a broken leg against Qatar.Photograph: Lee Smith/ReutersIsmaël Koné’s eyes say it all. The Canada midfielder had just broken the tibia and fibula bones in his lower left leg after a challenge by Qatar’s midfielder Assim Madibo, who would receive a red card and a five-match ban. As a photo editor, when incidents like this occur you have to weigh up decency and news value before publishing (I still feel traumatised by the uncensored images I saw of Eduardo’s broken leg in 2008). I think this image conveys the severity of the incident without being unduly graphic. Koné left the pitch on a stretcher but waved to fans as he exited.30Cubarsi’s cartwheelView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Enzo Fernández fouls Spain’s Pau Cubarsi and is later shown a second yellow card leading to a red.Photograph: Maddie Meyer/Fifa/Getty ImagesI’d typically include a celebration photograph from the World Cup final among my favourites from a tournament, but this ‘tackle’ by Enzo Fernández was so bad – and the image of Pau Cubarsi flying through the air so striking – that I couldn’t look beyond it when reviewing the match. Cubarsi looks like he’s falling from space. An absolutely dreadful final sparked into life at this defining moment. Cubarsi rose from the turf to claim the title of ‘best young player’ of the tournament and, more importantly, a World Cup winner’s medal.

title: Kevin Keegan, Liverpool legend and former Newcastle manager, dies aged 75  | Soccer | The Guardian
description: Kevin Keegan, the former England and Newcastle manager who was a member of Liverpool’s serial champions in the 1970s, has died aged 75
Author: Louise Taylor
Headline: Kevin Keegan, the former England and Newcastle manager who was a member of Liverpool’s serial champions in the 1970s, has died aged 75
Content: Kevin Keegan, seen here celebrating after scoring for England against Scotland in 1979, has died at the age of 75.Photograph: Eamonn McCabe/GuardianView image in fullscreenKevin Keegan, seen here celebrating after scoring for England against Scotland in 1979, has died at the age of 75.Photograph: Eamonn McCabe/GuardianSoccerKevin Keegan, Liverpool legend and former Newcastle manager, dies aged 75Keegan revealed in June 2026 he had stage four cancerStarred for Liverpool, Hamburg, Newcastle and EnglandLouise TaylorandAndy MartinMon 20 Jul 2026 10.28 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 16.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleKevin Keegan, the former England and Newcastle manager who was twice named European Footballer of the Year, has died aged 75. As a player, Keegan illuminated countless matches and in the 1970s proved a stalwart member of Liverpool’s serial domestic and European trophy winning team.Keegan’s family announced in January that he had cancer and, seven weeks ago, he himself revealed that it was stage four, the most advanced form of the disease. “A double Ballon d’Or winner, Kevin was a much loved husband, father and grandfather,” read a statement. “The family would like to thank Kevin’s incredible medical team for all their support. This is a hugely difficult time and they are requesting space and privacy.”A pioneer and a superstar, Kevin Keegan’s achievements were extraordinaryRead moreAlan Shearer, who played under Keegan with Newcastle andEngland, paid his own tribute on social media. “My hero. My manager. My friend,” wrote the former striker. “Sleep easy Boss.”The Colombian former Newcastle striker Faustino Asprilla wrote: “I am absolutely heartbroken. Kevin wasn’t just a football manager to me. Through his passion, belief and love for the game he inspired so many people, myself included. He taught me that football is about much more than winning.“It’s about passion, loyalty, hope and never giving up. Those lessons have stayed with me throughout my life and, for that, I will be forever grateful.”View image in fullscreenA Newcastle fan leaves flowers outside St James’ Park in tribute to Kevin Keegan.Photograph: Scott Heppell/ReutersNewcastle released a heartfelt statement describing Keegan as “one of the most iconic, influential and deeply loved figures in our club’s history”. As it explained: “Kevin was more than a legendary footballer and manager. He was the beating heart ofNewcastle Unitedfor generations of supporters.” Prince William said he was “incredibly saddened to hear of the passing of Kevin Keegan. A truly remarkable footballer and manager who inspired generations with his talent, passion and love of the game. Above all he will be remembered as a kind and generous man.”By late on Monday afternoon, floral tributes were piling up outside St James’ Park as Newcastle fans paid their respects to an enduring hero. Liverpool, meanwhile, said Keegan’s “indomitable spirit and remarkable contribution will be forever etched in our history and his legacy will live on”.Keegan started his career at Scunthorpe before being signed for Liverpool by Bill Shankly for £33,000 in 1971. It did not take long for the son of a South Yorkshire miner to become “King Kev”, with his invariably imperious attacking performances helping Shankly’s side win three First Division titles, the FA Cup, the Uefa Cup (twice) and the 1977 European Cup.View image in fullscreenLiverpool’s Ian Callaghan, Kevin Keegan and Ray Clemence parade the winning trophy after the 1974 FA Cup final against Newcastle at Wembley.Photograph: Liverpool FC/Getty ImagesBy his own admission not a natural talent, Keegan turned himself into one of the world’s best players. “Mighty Mouse” was relentlessly combative, never ducked a challenge, and remained refreshingly humble even as he became a firm Kop favourite. “I learned so much from Shanks,” Keegan said. “He used to say: ‘If I was a road sweeper my street would be the cleanest in the borough.’ I took that to heart.”Keegan left Liverpool in 1977 for Hamburg, where he became the only British player to twice win the Ballon d’Or. Upon returning from Germany in 1980 he enjoyed further playing stints with Southampton and Newcastle before retiring in 1984. By then he had also won 63 England caps, scoring 21 goals in the course of an international career that involved playing at the 1982 World Cup.Quick GuideThe Keegan years: from player to managerShow1951Kevin Keegan is born on 14 February in Armthorpe, near Doncaster.1968Begins playing career at Scunthorpe United.1971Signed by Bill Shankly for Liverpool at age of 20, initially as a midfielder.1972Makes England debut against Wales in November.1973Liverpool win the league; Keegan scores twice in Uefa Cup final win over Borussia Mönchengladbach.1974Scores twice as Liverpool beat Newcastle 3-0 in the FA Cup final. His first England goal comes against Wales.1976Liverpool win the league title and Keegan scores in each leg of  Uefa Cup final win against Club Brugge. His celebrity is such he appears on the TV show Superstars, and famously crashes his bike during one of the races.1977Another league title for Liverpool, who also win their first European Cup, a 3-1 victory over Mönchengladbach. Keegan moves to Hamburg for £500,000, nearly double the German transfer record.1978Wins Ballon d’Or.1979Wins Ballon d’Or again as Hamburg secure their first German ‌championship title in 19 years, and first in the Bundesliga era.1980Captains England at Euros but they are knocked out in the group stage. Leaves Hamburg to join Southampton for two seasons.1982Transfers to Newcastle in the Second Division. Plays final match for England, after 63 caps (31 as captain) and 21 goals.1984Helps Newcastle secure promotion in his second season, then retires from playing. Across his club career, he scores 204 goals in 592 ‌appearances.1992Takes first coaching role as manager of struggling Newcastle in February. Danger of relegation to the Third Division is averted.1993Leads them to the First Division title and promotion to the Premier League in his first full season in charge.1994Newcastle finish third in Premier League.1996Newcastle finish second in the Premier League, ​despite leading for most ​of campaign – by 12 points at one stage. Season includes famous “I would love it if we beat them” comments when discussing rivals Manchester United on Sky Sports.1997Leaves Newcastle and takes over at Fulham for two seasons.1999Takes charge ​of England national ‌team in February.2000Coaches England at Euro 2000 where they beat Germany but still go out in the group stage. Resigns in ​October after a 1–0 loss to Germany in World Cup qualifying.2001Takes over at Manchester City.2008Returns to Newcastle but lasts eight months after tensions with the board and owner Mike Ashley.Was this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.Keegan was never one to hold back. He was memorably sent off for scrapping with Leeds’ Billy Bremner in the 1974 Charity Shield and harboured a certain volatility allied to a pugnacious intolerance of football’s machinations, real or perceived. “I don’t like people to deceive me and tell me lies,” hetold the Guardian in 2018.Although a private man, Keegan also embraced fame. He was one of the English game’s first players to wear personalised branded football boots, recorded a hit single Head Over Heels in Love, and became an enduring star of the BBC’s Superstars. He was also the public face of 70s aftershave Brut 33 and became close friends with George Best. “I wanted so much to have his ability,” Keegan said. “His skill was unbelievable. I played against [Johan] Cruyff, [Franz] Beckenbauer, [Diego] Maradona but I still say Besty was the best.”Having hung up his boots at the age of 33, Keegan moved to Spain where his two daughters grew up, initially unaware he had been a footballer. “It was the best decision I made in my life,” hetold the Guardian in 2011. “I was at the highest level and the only way was down. I call it the glass mountain – you can’t climb a glass mountain.”View image in fullscreenKevin Keegan in 2008.Photograph: Michael Mayhew/SportsphotoIn 1992 he decided to venture into management at Newcastle, guiding them into the Premier League and to within touching distance of the 1995-96 title, only to miss out to Alex Ferguson’s Manchester United. His finger-jabbing“I will love it”rant from that season has become part of the English game’s lore.After leaving St James’ Park in January 1997 Keegan took charge of Fulham before becoming England manager in 1999. That 20-month, 18-game tenure would end in despair when, following a disappointing Euro 2000 campaign, he resigned after a defeat by Germany at Wembley. The Football Association has announced it will pay tribute to Keegan before England’s Nations League game with Spain at Wembley in September.A happier spell at Manchester City – who on Monday credited Keegan with establishing them as a Premier League club – followed before an emotional return to Newcastle in 2008. That swansongended within eight months, however, amid acute tensions with the club’s then owner Mike Ashley and his board. In 2009 a tribunal awarded Keegan £2m damages for constructive dismissal.Keegan, who was made an OBE in 1982 for services to football, made a fleeting return to public life in June. During an event on Tyneside he talked about his desire to return to St James’ Park to say “goodbye” and joked the club would need to “wait until I die” to erect a statue of him. He was optimistic at the time that gruelling treatment was keeping his cancer at bay.“Kevin’s impact will not be measured by appearances or results,” read Newcastle’s statement. “It will live on in the memories he created with club and country, the dreams he inspired wherever he went and the generations of supporters around the world who fell in love with football because of him.”Explore more on these topicsFootballLiverpoolNewcastle UnitedEnglandnewsShareReuse this content

title: João Gomes seals £38m Aston Villa move, Hall and Robinson on Manchester United list | Transfer window | The Guardian
description: Brazil’s João Gomes has joined Aston Villa from Wolves, and Manchester United are monitoring Newcastle’s Lewis Hall and Fulham’s Antonee Robinson
Author: Jamie Jackson
Headline: Brazil’s João Gomes has joined Aston Villa from Wolves, and Manchester United are monitoring Newcastle’s Lewis Hall and Fulham’s Antonee Robinson
Content: João Gomes was described by the Wolves technical director as someone ‘coaches love and players love’.Photograph: Neville Williams/Aston Villa FC/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenJoão Gomes was described by the Wolves technical director as someone ‘coaches love and players love’.Photograph: Neville Williams/Aston Villa FC/Getty ImagesTransfer windowJoão Gomes seals £38m Aston Villa move, Hall and Robinson on Manchester United listBrazil midfielder Gomes joins from WolvesUnited sign young winger Thompson from SpursPA Media andJamie JacksonMon 20 Jul 2026 07.45 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 09.15 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Brazil international João Gomes has joinedAston Villain a £38m deal. The 25-year-old midfielder, who has 10 caps, ends a three-and-a-half-year spell at Wolves, for whom he made 130 appearances after signing from Flamengo.Gomes’s arrival at Villa follows the departure ofYouri Tielemans to Manchester Unitedand comes with Morgan Rogers on the verge ofa £117m move to Chelsea.The Wolves technical director, Matt Jackson, said: “João deserves everything he gets from the game. He’s the type of professional that coaches love and players love, so he will go on to be the outstanding player that we know he is.”Quick GuideHow do I sign up for sport breaking news alerts?ShowIn the Guardian app, tap the Profile settings button at the top right, then select Notifications. Turn on sport notifications.If you already have the Guardian app, make sure you’re on the most recent version.If you don't have the Guardian app, download it from theiOS App Storeon iPhone or theGoogle Play storeon Android by searching for 'The Guardian'.Was this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.Manchester Unitedare monitoring Newcastle’s Lewis Hall and Fulham’s Antonee Robinson to potentially strengthen Michael Carrick’s options at left-back.The manager is intent on signing a third midfielder after the arrivals ofAndrey Santosand Tielemans, and is also considering a move for a left-back to compete with Luke Shaw.Hall has impressed at Newcastle since being loaned from Chelsea for the 2023-24 season before signing for £28m plus £7m in add-ons. The 21-year-old has four England caps and was considered unfortunate not to be selected in Thomas Tuchel’s squad for the World Cup. He is under contract until June 2029.Robinson, who signed for Fulham from Wigan for £2m in August 2020, was in Maurico Pochettino’s USA squad, making four appearances. Robinson will be 29 next month so is older than United’s preferred profile and his terms expire in June 2028.View image in fullscreenTynan Thompson, Manchester United’s latest signing, has played seven times for England’s under-18s.Photograph: Manchester United/Getty ImagesUnited have signed Tynan Thompson, an 18-year-old winger, from Tottenham for an initial fee of about £4m plus up to £4m in add-ons related to first-team achievement. Spurs have matching rights and a 15% sell-on clause.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThompson, who has played seven times for England’s under-18 side, scored 13 goals and made six assists for Spurs at youth level last season, getting seven goals in seven Uefa Youth League games.Lisandro Martínez was forced out of Argentina’s 1-0 World Cup final defeat against Spain on 41 minutes owing to an injury. United are yet to determine its severity.Explore more on these topicsTransfer windowAston VillaWolverhampton WanderersManchester UnitedTottenham HotspurnewsShareReuse this content

title: A pioneer and a superstar, Kevin Keegan’s achievements were extraordinary | Soccer | The Guardian
description: The former England manager may be remembered by many for his ‘I’d love it’ rant in 1996, but that should not obscure the remarkable nature of his football career
Author: Rob Draper
Headline: The former England manager may be remembered by many for his ‘I’d love it’ rant in 1996, but that should not obscure the remarkable nature of his football career
Content: Kevin Keegan in action for Liverpool in August 1976.Photograph: Colorsport/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenKevin Keegan in action for Liverpool in August 1976.Photograph: Colorsport/ShutterstockSoccerA pioneer and a superstar, Kevin Keegan’s achievements were extraordinaryThe former England manager may be remembered by many for his ‘I’d love it’ rant in 1996, but that should not obscure the remarkable nature of his football careerRob DraperMon 20 Jul 2026 11.02 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleKevin Keegan,who has died aged 75, is the only British player to win the Ballon d’Or twice, was the star of Liverpool’s first European Cup-winning team, a messianic figure at Newcastle United as a player and coach, forging a team so memorable that Fifa’s president wrote to commend him, and was the captain and then manager of the England men’s national team. And yet, as Keegan would ruefully reflect, he will principally be remembered by many for an emotional outburst that precipitated Newcastle’s historic failure in a Premier League title race, a TV interview which presaged the coming era of the modern game.Keegan’s “I’d love it” rantof April 1996, directed at Alex Ferguson, became a meme before the modern meaning of the word and social media even existed, repeatedly played on Sky Sports and becoming indelibly marked in the minds of multiple generations, many not even born when it occurred. It frequently tops polls as the most memorable moment in Premier League history and foreshadowed an era where digital clips and personality narratives would dominate football coverage.Kevin Keegan, Liverpool legend and former Newcastle manager, dies aged 75Read moreThere is a danger, however, of reducing Keegan’s life to a series of ever-more emotional explosions, of which there were many, such as his punch-up with Leeds United’s Billy Bremner in the 1974 Charity Shield. For that would obscure the extraordinary nature of his achievements and the fact he was arguably the most charismatic modern English footballer; more eloquent than David Beckham and Paul Gascoigne and more passionate than Bobby Moore. He had a rare gift for inspiring players and almost everyone who interacted with him at his best came away uplifted.Still, Keegan’s meltdown on TV, where he took issue with Ferguson’s supposed “mind games” in suggesting Leeds players would go easy on Newcastle during a tight title run-in with Manchester United, became a defining moment. “Strangers shout ‘love it’ to me from car windows,” wrote Keegan in his autobiography. “Autograph hunters tell me they would ‘love it’ if I could sign their pieces of paper. When I go to charity events I am often asked to sign photographs showing me in that classic pose: headphones on, finger jabbing, blazing eyes. Those pictures tend to be auctioned for a lot more money than the ones where I am playing forEnglandor any of my clubs.”Keegan claimed to be relaxed about the characterisation, skewed though it was, perhaps because more than any English footballer before he had maximised football’s commercial and media opportunities and accepted the respective bouquets and brickbats that ensued. Even before that meltdown, he would be best remembered by many in the 1970s for his excruciating bike crash on Superstars, a TV show which pitted athletes from different sports against each other in unfamiliar disciplines. Sprawled across the cycle track, Keegan’s bare flesh was ripped red raw by the gravel. Most remember the drama but few recall that, showing characteristic pig-headed determination, he got back on the bike, bloodied and bandaged, for a rematch, which he won before winning the entire contest.Keegan was born in Armthorpe, Doncaster, to Doris and Joe, a war veteran Durham miner who had migrated south in search of work at Markham colliery. His upbringing echoed Monty Python’s Yorkshiremen sketch, only his memories were authentic, a throwback to a different century. Their terrace house “had a detached outhouse where we washed. Our bath was an old zinc tub and the toilet was at the foot of the garden and we never had electricity until I was 10 and we moved into a council house in Balby.”View image in fullscreenKevin Keegan shares a joke with Alex Ferguson after the rollercoaster 1995-96 season.Photograph: Adam Butler/PAKeegan’s ascent to the peak of the game’s elite was unforeseen, few recognising his talent early on, most dismissing him as too small. He always credited Sister Mary Oliver, the nun who ran his Catholic primary school football team and who refereed with a crucifix around her neck, as being as important to him as a mentor as Bill Shankly was. “Kevin’s football must be encouraged,” she wrote in a school report, which gave him immense self-belief.Doncaster Rovers rejected him and he started work at Peglers Brass Works at 16, playing for their football team but only in the reserves and also for a pub team on Sundays, where he fortuitously played against a Scunthorpe scout, Bob Nellis, who got him a trial at the struggling Third Division club. Keegan referred to himself as a “mongrel” and “weasel” and the “least-talented” Ballon d’Or winner, and despite impressing at Scunthorpe it was still a major shock when Shankly, the man he regarded as a second father and progenitor of his success, took him toLiverpool. Shankly told him: “You’re not just a great player – you’re one of the great players!” It seemed an unlikely boast but Keegan scored on his Liverpool debut and was never dropped thereafter. He would inherit from Shankly comparable motivational skills.Quick GuideThe Keegan years: from player to managerShow1951Kevin Keegan is born on 14 February in Armthorpe, near Doncaster.1968Begins playing career at Scunthorpe United.1971Signed by Bill Shankly for Liverpool at age of 20, initially as a midfielder.1972Makes England debut against Wales in November.1973Liverpool win the league; Keegan scores twice in Uefa Cup final win over Borussia Mönchengladbach.1974Scores twice as Liverpool beat Newcastle 3-0 in the FA Cup final. His first England goal comes against Wales.1976Liverpool win the league title and Keegan scores in each leg of  Uefa Cup final win against Club Brugge. His celebrity is such he appears on the TV show Superstars, and famously crashes his bike during one of the races.1977Another league title for Liverpool, who also win their first European Cup, a 3-1 victory over Mönchengladbach. Keegan moves to Hamburg for £500,000, nearly double the German transfer record.1978Wins Ballon d’Or.1979Wins Ballon d’Or again as Hamburg secure their first German ‌championship title in 19 years, and first in the Bundesliga era.1980Captains England at Euros but they are knocked out in the group stage. Leaves Hamburg to join Southampton for two seasons.1982Transfers to Newcastle in the Second Division. Plays final match for England, after 63 caps (31 as captain) and 21 goals.1984Helps Newcastle secure promotion in his second season, then retires from playing. Across his club career, he scores 204 goals in 592 ‌appearances.1992Takes first coaching role as manager of struggling Newcastle in February. Danger of relegation to the Third Division is averted.1993Leads them to the First Division title and promotion to the Premier League in his first full season in charge.1994Newcastle finish third in Premier League.1996Newcastle finish second in the Premier League, ​despite leading for most ​of campaign – by 12 points at one stage. Season includes famous “I would love it if we beat them” comments when discussing rivals Manchester United on Sky Sports.1997Leaves Newcastle and takes over at Fulham for two seasons.1999Takes charge ​of England national ‌team in February.2000Coaches England at Euro 2000 where they beat Germany but still go out in the group stage. Resigns in ​October after a 1–0 loss to Germany in World Cup qualifying.2001Takes over at Manchester City.2008Returns to Newcastle but lasts eight months after tensions with the board and owner Mike Ashley.Was this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.Though reclusive, Keegan also had a streak of extrovertism, embracing every aspect of the game’s growing commercial opportunities, from his famousBrutadvert to appearances on Top of the Pops, where his single Head Over Heels in Lovereached the top 40. Though he enjoyed standing out, he admitted he would never have got away with his trademark bubble perm in the old-school Liverpool dressing room. His wife, Jean, whom he met on the waltzers at the Doncaster St Leger horse racing meeting when she was 16 and he was 19 and to whom he remained married for life, burst into fits of laughter when she first saw it. He had the last laugh, the hairstyle inspiring alarming copycat versions across the football world.Keegan remained true to what he considered to be his working-class, mining roots, declaring himself a Labour man, annoyed that a staged photo of him feigning to kiss then prime minister Margaret Thatcher on the cheek was interpreted as support for economic policies which had ravaged heavy industry. He was more at home playing head tennis with a later PM, Tony Blair, to whom he was close enough to host at his home for dinner.View image in fullscreenThe then Newcastle manager Keegan chats to the Labour leader Tony Blair in the summer of 1996.Photograph: Adam Butler/PAKeegan inherited two mantles from Britain’s greatest players of the 1960s. As George Best, the original footballer sex symbol of the modern era, lapsed into alcoholism and increasing unreliability, Keegan became the more wholesome and dependable commercial face of the superstar footballer in Britain, inundated with deals. For England he effectively became Moore’s successor as captain but at a time when the national team was in decline from his predecessor’s World Cup-winning heights. For Keegan, as the sole outfield world-class player, it was a heavy burden to bear as they missed out on successive World Cup finals.His first major tournament for his country was the ill-fated 1980 European Championship and his World Cup career amounted to 20 minutes in 1982, a campaign which was disrupted by a back injury and where “my only contribution was a dreadful miss against the host nation to send us home. That moment is probably the only moment of my England career that people remember now.” It would prove to be his last game for England at the age of just 31.More notable features of Keegan’s England career were his courage in defying IRA death threats to play against Northern Ireland in Belfast, then walking out on the team when Don Revie dropped him shortly afterwards and being arbitrarily beaten up by Yugoslav custom officials prior to a game in Belgrade, a horrific and wholly underserved ordeal that sparked an international incident. His frustration was best summed up in a story he told about the great Dutch player Johnny Rep. Surveying an England team at Wembley in 1977, Rep turned to Keegan and said: “Kevvy, you have problems here: this is the worst England side I have seen.” The Netherlands won 2-0 and, as Keegan recalled, could have scored at will.Keegan was a pioneering player, negotiating release clauses, unheard of at the time in England, at Liverpool andHamburg, allowing him to leave if a club met a £500,000 fee, thereby controlling his moves. Leaving Liverpool at what was assumed to be their peak – he had just helped them win their first European Cup in 1977 – to join a team sixth in the Bundesliga was demonstrative of both Keegan’s single-mindedness and capacity to challenge himself. He also quadrupled his salary, reflecting the economic state of English football at the time.View image in fullscreenKeegan with the ball at his feet for Hamburg in the 1977-78 Bundesliga season.Photograph: Colorsport/ShutterstockHamburg was not an initial success. Jealous teammates held meetings to get him kicked out of the club and, according to Keegan, refused to pass to him. His nadir came at Christmas 1977 when, in a festive-season friendly, unhappy at his plight and frustrated with over-physical treatment from the Lübeck defender Erhard Press, Keegan punched him on the jaw and subsequently knocked him out, receiving an eight-week ban as punishment. Yet, a year later, he won his first Ballon d’Or and went on to lead Hamburg to their first Bundesliga title since 1960.Keegan embraced Germany, learning the language and was nicknamed “Mighty Mouse”, a tribute to his astonishing strength given his diminutive size. His first daughter, Laura, was born there but his finale, a European Cup final that would have sealed Hamburg’s place in history, was a sad one, ending as it did in defeat to Nottingham Forest. Having won three league titles, two Uefa Cups, an FA Cup and European Cup at Liverpool, the Bundesliga titlewould be the last trophy Keegan ever won. He was just 29 years old.Keegan made a typically unconventional choice in joining Southampton, a promising yet small-market team under a talented, persuasive manager in Lawrie McMenemy. Keegan propelled them to a top-six finish and qualification for Europe, but they faded to seventh in the second season and Keegan, disillusioned, made a decision that would define the second half of his professional life.Newcastle were in one of their periodic financial and footballing crises and in the old Second Division when, with money from brewers Scottish & Newcastle, they persuaded him to join in 1982. His father’s links to the north-east and reverence for Newcastle players such as Jackie Milburn were an emotional pull on Keegan, but one that cost him his England career. The manager, Bobby Robson, didn’t feel it appropriate to pick a second-tier player for England and Keegan, still captain and by far the most iconic national team player, learned from the press he had been “dumped”. He felt “betrayed, hurt and humiliated”.Crowds doubled at St James’ Park with Keegan’s arrival and Newcastle were promoted as champions in Keegan’s second season, 1983-84, by which point he had already decided to quit playing at the age of 33. Typically impulsive, it was when Mark Lawrenson outsprinted Keegan to dispossess him during an FA Cup tie at Liverpool that he decided he would finish his career at the end of the season.View image in fullscreenKeegan in action for England in May 1979. His international playing career ended in acrimonious fashion.Photograph: Peter Robinson/Empics Sport/PAKeegan and family, which now included his second daughter, Sarah, born in 1982, decamped to his Marbella villa and, other than periodic business deals and discussions about a long-held dream to launch a Soccer Circus, he embraced retirement. Yet endless rounds of golf failed to satisfy. The family had moved back to his Hampshire home when Sir John Hall, a dynamic north-east businessman, called in 1992, begging Keegan to help Newcastle avoid relegation to the third tier and potential liquidation. Despite having watched no football for years, Keegan accepted. He threatened to quit after a few games but stayed to keep Newcastle up and promptly announced that he wanted to build a dynasty to rival Liverpool and challenge for the Premier League title.Most considered that quixotic and yet Newcastle won promotion to the Premier League as champions and with judicious youthful signings such as Andy Cole and Rob Lee, and veteran players such as Peter Beardsley, they came third and sixth in the top flight. So great was his authority, Keegan could even placate angry fans on the steps of St James’ Park when the club sold Cole to Manchester United.With that money the club signed David Ginola and Les Ferdinand and won nine of their first 10 games in 1995-96, often in thrilling fashion, establishing a seemingly unassailable 12-point lead by January. But a crucial defeat at home to Ferguson’s United in March 1996 and the Scot’s repeated use of psychological barbs seemed to weigh heavy on Keegan as his team’s form disintegrated. A dramatic yet debilitating 4-3 defeat at Liverpool led Sepp Blatter to write to Keegan but the sight of him slumped over advertising hoardings as Liverpool scored a last-minute winner suggested the toll was too much.Keegan eventually cracked, denouncing Ferguson in his famous TV interview. “You can tell him now, we’re still fighting for this title and I’ll tell you, honestly, I will love it if we beat them. Love it!” United won the title with four points to spare.View image in fullscreenKeegan (left) during a Fulham training session at Roehampton in 1999.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianKeegan initially seemed determined to rebuild as the club signed Alan Shearer for a world-record £15m and they beat United 5-0 at St James’ Park that autumn, but Keegan quit soon after, outraged that a city banker preparing the club for flotation had issued him an ultimatum to sign a contract. Football’s new era seemed untethered from the idealistic foundational values imbued in him by Shankly.Keegan seemed done with football until the now-disgraced Harrods owner Mohamed Al Fayed used speculative talks about his much-vaunted Soccer Circus to persuade him to become chief operating officer and eventually manager of Fulham in the third tier. He almost seemed at his happiest as the club accelerated to promotion, but his time was interrupted by a national crisis when Glenn Hoddle was sacked as England manager for making offensive comments about disabled people. The clamour for Keegan to take over was indicative of his popularity with ordinary fans. However, it proved a decision he came to greatly regret, taking in a humiliating Euro 2000 group-stage exit andending with him resigningin the toilets after an ignominious defeat against Germany in England’s last game at the old Wembley Stadium. It was, he conceded, a job beyond his abilities.That said, Keegan was happy to take on Manchester City in the old First Division, getting them promoted to the Premier League as champions with 99 points. He remained at the club for four years, an almost unprecedented period of stability at the club at that time. When he finally left City he seemed to be done with coaching and finally launched his Soccer Circus in Glasgow, though it closed after a few years. Yet there was to be one last comeback at Newcastle under the ill-starred ownership of Mike Ashley, which proved a disaster and lasted just eight months.The idea that a high-minded, old-fashioned football manager schooled by Shankly could coexist with a modern businessman as ruthless as Ashley and his cast of minions running the club always seemed improbable. When he walked away from the job, eventually winning a lengthy and bitter constructive dismissal claim, it was to be Keegan’s last act in football. So acrimonious was the departure that he was only able to return in disguise to attend a function at the club.The current regime made clear Keegan was always welcome back at St James’ Park. That was after his stage four cancer had been revealed in a series of speaking engagements in May 2026, one of which in Newcastle was, in retrospect, a heartfelt farewell to the fans to whom he had always felt closest. After that draining but exhilarating evening Keegan’s associates reported that he was in high spirits, energised by the reconnection with the fans who loved him most.It was to prove Keegan’s final goodbye. Football, as he observed, had given him everything except for a happy ending.Explore more on these topicsFootballNewcastle UnitedLiverpoolEnglandHamburgEuropean club footballfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Argentina players face bans as Fifa opens investigation into World Cup final brawls | Argentina | The Guardian
description: Leandro Paredes was shown a red card after the final whistle and Fifa said it was looking into the Falklands banner on the pitch after England v Argentina
Author: Matt Hughes
Headline: Leandro Paredes was shown a red card after the final whistle and Fifa said it was looking into the Falklands banner on the pitch after England v Argentina
Content: Gavi is attacked by Thiago Almada and Leandro Paredes of Argentina after the World Cup final.Photograph: Michael Zemanek/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenGavi is attacked by Thiago Almada and Leandro Paredes of Argentina after the World Cup final.Photograph: Michael Zemanek/ShutterstockArgentinaArgentina players face bans as Fifa opens investigation into World Cup final brawlsLeandro Paredes was shown red card after final whistleFifa looking into Falklands banner at England gameMatt Hughesin New YorkMon 20 Jul 2026 16.42 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 17.44 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFifa has opened an investigation into the violent scenes that followed Spain’sWorld Cupfinal win on Sunday, with several Argentina players facing bans.Nahuel Molina appeared to strike Spain’s captain Rodri in the stomach as he ran on to the pitch following the final whistle, while there was also a large melee on the halfway line, where Leandro Paredes appeared to grab Eric García by the throat before throwing Gavi to the ground.Gangs of New Jersey: how the World Cup final descended into ugly scenes between Argentina and SpainRead moreParades was subsequently shown a red card by the referee, Slavko Vincic, who had also sent off Enzo Fernández towards the end of normal time, as well as booking three otherArgentinaplayers and the head coach, Lionel Scaloni.“Following an assessment of the relevant match reports for the Fifa World Cup final between Spain and Argentina, and in line with article 36 of the Fifa Disciplinary Code (FDC), the Fifa Disciplinary Committee has appointed a Disciplinary and Ethics Prosecutor to investigate potential breaches of the FDC with regard to the post-match incidents,” Fifa said in a statement.Fifa is also investigating Argentina’s conduct after their semi-final win over England, when their players celebrated with a banner proclaiming “Las Malvinas son Argentinas”, which was smuggled into the Atlanta Stadium by supporters.Fifa
