prohibits players or teams making political statements, so those involved could be banned.Explore more on these topicsArgentinaWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainnewsShareReuse this content

title: Nearly two million people join Spain’s World Cup celebrations in Madrid | Spain | The Guardian
description: More than a million Spaniards descended on central Madrid on Monday night to welcome the triumphant national team home
Author: Sam Jones
Headline: More than a million Spaniards descended on central Madrid on Monday night to welcome the triumphant national team home
Content: Spain’s players parade the World Cup trophy through Madrid on Monday.Photograph: BALLESTEROS/EPAView image in fullscreenSpain’s players parade the World Cup trophy through Madrid on Monday.Photograph: BALLESTEROS/EPASpainNearly two million people join Spain’s World Cup celebrations in MadridSquad arrived home at 2.30pm local time on MondayVictory festivities to centre around the Plaza de CibelesSam Jonesin MadridMon 20 Jul 2026 16.01 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 10.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAlmost two million Spaniards, many still bleary-eyed and feeling a little delicate after thecountry’s historic World Cup victory against Argentina, descended on central Madrid on Monday night to welcome the triumphant national team home.La Roja, who beat Argentina 1-0 in the final in New Jersey on Sunday night, were afforded the first of many heroes’ welcomes when they touched down at Madrid’s Barajas airport at 2.30pm local time on Monday.World Cup 2026: Spain’s champions land in Madrid for heroes’ welcome after beating Argentina – liveRead moreSpain’s captain, Rodri, waved the trophy aloft as he emerged from the plane, his medal from the previous night around his neck. The team posed for a photo before heading into terminal four where hundreds of delighted fans, many inSpainstrips and carrying the country’s red-and-yellowrojigualdaflag, waited and hoped for a glimpse of the freshly minted champions.Their first stop was an audience with King Felipe, Queen Letizia and their daughters, Princess Leonor and Princess Sofía, at the Zarzuela palace, northwest of Madrid, where the king paid tribute to their talent and determination.“It’s been an epic triumph,” he said. “It’s been a privilege and a joy for the four of us to see you play in the final and to be able to celebrate with you that moment of lifting the cup, with immense thanks from all of Spain.”Felipe also praised their fortitude, saying: “You’ve suffered, you’ve come home bruised and tired, you’ve endured some criticism, but what is all that compared with the joy of bringing the cup home?”View image in fullscreenThousands of Spain supporters gather at Plaza de Cibeles in Madrid on Monday.Photograph: Richard Zubelzu/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockThe team’s next meeting was a visit to the Moncloa palace – the office and residence of the prime minister – to see Pedro Sánchez. The socialist prime minister offered the players and technical staff “massive thanks … for the game, for the effort and for the victory”.Speaking at the Moncloa palace, Rodri said that “winning for you country is the most beautiful thing there is”. He added: “This generation grew up watching [Iker] Casillas and [Andrés] Iniesta lift the cup [in 2010] and having been able to do that today is the biggest thing you can do in football.”The highlight for the fans – who braved the 34C evening heat – was the victory parade that wound its way from Moncloa to the Plaza de Cibeles just outside Madrid city hall. They crowded into the square, some sheltering from the sun under umbrellas, as the team approached in an open-topped bus. The central government’s delegate in Madrid put attendance along the route at around 1.8 million.Madrid’s mayor, José Luis Martínez Almeida, told Radio Nacional de España that the capital had been working to give the team the homecoming they deserved. He also hailed the peaceful and joyous scenes that had greeted Spain’s victory on Sunday night.View image in fullscreenRodri carries the World Cup at Barajas airport on Monday.Photograph: Sergio R Moreno/GTRES/Shutterstock“Even though there were thousands and thousands of people on the streets last night, there were practically no incidents in the city,” he said.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“We’re getting everything ready for tonight … The thing we’re focusing on is how to celebrate and greet a team that will live for ever in our memory.”The victory – Spain’s second men’s World Cup win after their triumph against the Netherlands in South Africa in 2010 – wasseen by hundreds of thousand of Spaniardswho followed the match in bars, parks, fairgrounds, bullrings, and on giant screens in the centre of Madrid. The Spanish press have hailed it as“a unique collective experience”made possible by a young team that reflects all that is best about a modern, diverse and determined country.The win has also been enthusiastically hailed by the country’s political class.“WE ARE WORLD CHAMPIONS!!” Sánchez wrote in a social media post. “What an amazing national squad. Thank you, team.”Alberto Núñez Feijóo, the leader of Spain’s conservative People’s party, was equally free with the capital letters. “Well-deserved WORLD CHAMPIONS! Values, hard work, humility, and faith. You have made history. There are no limits for this team … Long live Spain!!!” he wrote on X.Santiago Abascal, the leader of the far-right Vox party, also offered his congratulations to the team for carrying “the flag of Spain so far and so high”.The Spanish postal service, Correos, has announced the creation of a special stamp to commemorate what it called “one of the greatest achievements in the history of Spanish sport”. The red-coloured stamp will bear the word CAMPEONES and feature two stars – one for each of Spain’sWorld Cupvictories.Explore more on these topicsSpainWorld Cup 2026World CupnewsShareReuse this content

title: A desperate grab for attention. This is the way the World Cup ends | Soccer | The Guardian
description: In today’s Football Daily: the GWC is finally over
Author: Dominic Booth
Headline: In today’s Football Daily: the GWC is finally over
Content: Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino, there, enjoying that winning feeling.Photograph: Alex Livesey/Danehouse/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenDonald Trump and Gianni Infantino, there, enjoying that winning feeling.Photograph: Alex Livesey/Danehouse/Getty ImagesFootball Daily newsletterSoccerA desperate grab for attention. This is the way the World Cup endsSign up now! Sign up now! Sign up now? Sign up now!Dominic BoothMon 20 Jul 2026 10.12 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 10.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAND BREATHE …Maybe Donald Trump was simply confused. He must have seen the ticker tape, a group of men cavorting around an American field clad in red and thought he’d stumbled into one of his Maga rallies. That is the only logical explanation for hisbizarre behaviouron the champions’ plinth in (a stadium near) New York as Spain hoistedtheir freshly wontrophy aloft, Trump having to be dragged off the stage by notorious wallflower Gianni Infantino as Rodri exhibited his trademark composure, patiently waiting to bask in his team’s glory moment. This is the way the Geopolitics World Cup ends, not with a bang but a desperate grab for attention from the US president.The GWC final had already witnessed its own on-field disgrace of course, in the shape of Argentina’s shameful performance.La Albicelestetarnished their proud footballing history with a display completely devoid of ambition, pride and, well, quality. No shots in regulation time, no shots on target in the entire 120 minutes and Enzo Fernández sent off in ignominy. Even Lionel Messi embarrassed himself for snitching to Slavko Vincic, pleading with the referee to brandish a red card after Marc Cucurella dared to put his hand to his mouth. The full-time scenes were even more debasing for Argentina, losing their heads in such a manner it made their first-half roughhouse tacticsin the semi-final against Englandlook like child’s play. This is the way the Geopolitics World Cup ends, not with a bang but a thump from Leandro Paredes. Stay classy, lads.With no little hyperbole, Trump proudly proclaimed the GWC to have been “one of the greatest events of any kind ever held”. Does that sentiment hold muster? Well, it’s certainly been the biggest World Cup ever, with more teams, more matches, even more hosts than ever before. But of the 104 games (up from 64 in 2022), many have felt like a slog. UK readers, can you look Football Daily in the eye and truthfully say you stayed up until 3am to watch the conclusion ofEcuador 0-0 Curaçao? There has been plenty to enjoy, of course. Cape Verde were a genuine breath of fresh air, the only nation to play Spain and come away unscathed, while their near-missin the last 32 against Argentinawill be forever etched in World Cup folklore – oh what might have been but for Messi’s genius. We hope to see the Cape Verde lads back in 2030.Belgium’s late comeback to down Senegalwas pure cinema, whileEngland’s 6-4 win over Francewill live longer in the memory than a third-place playoff should simply for its pure weirdness.Speaking of the Three Lions, who will ever forget Mexico and the Battle of the Azteca? Not Dan Burn and certainly not us. FD also enjoyed Mauricio Pochettino’s swashbuckling USA USA USA side in this tournament before Trump’s attempted assist for Folarin Balogun backfired spectacularly and the co-hosts became co-toast. Ultimately, perhaps a tired final in which the better team were stifled by one that refused to play the game entirely was fitting of the tournament as a whole. It was both exhaustive and exhausting and, as a footballing public we have discovered there is such a thing as too much football. The fatigue is real. Thank god it’s all over …. Now, who fancies watching South Shields versus Harrogate in pre-season on Tuesday? Anyone?LIVE ON BIG WEBSITEThere’s no more live football for a little bit, so for now you’ll have to make do with theGWC farewell news blog.QUOTE OF THE DAY“I like a lot of them artists, but I thought it was crap” – Wayne Rooney with amagnificently succinctlive review of the GWC half-time show.View image in fullscreenEven a frontline of Ronaldinho, Madonna and Ronaldo couldn’t get Rooney’s juices flowing.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesFOOTBALL DAILY LETTERSdouble quotation markFootball 1-0 Sh!thousery. Thank god anti-football failed” – Paul Landaw.double quotation markCan you start a Stop Argentina campaign? – Gordon MacLeod.double quotation markWhen will the photograph of Donald Trump giving a baby Gianni Infantino a bath surface?” – Krishna Moorthy.If you have any, please send letters tothe.boss@theguardian.com.Today’s prizeless letter o’ the day goes to … Gordon MacLeod. Terms and conditions for our competitions, when we run them, arehere.This is an extract from our daily football email … Football Daily. To get the full version,just visit this page and follow the instructions.Explore more on these topicsFootballFootball Daily newsletterWorld Cup 2026World CupSpainArgentinanewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Tuchel is clinging to an outdated English style – he must let players take risks | England | The Guardian
description: Head coach should stop relying on a physical approach that has rarely worked and embrace England’s gifted ball players
Author: Jacob Steinberg
Headline: Head coach should stop relying on a physical approach that has rarely worked and embrace England’s gifted ball players
Content: Thomas Tuchel was hired by the FA to make the difference in the big games.Photograph: Martin Rickett/PAView image in fullscreenThomas Tuchel was hired by the FA to make the difference in the big games.Photograph: Martin Rickett/PAEnglandAnalysisTuchel is clinging to an outdated English style – he must let players take risksJacob Steinbergin MiamiHead coach should ditch a physical approach that has rarely worked and embrace England’s gifted ball playersMon 20 Jul 2026 09.55 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 15.14 EDTShareOnly England can claim the bronze medal at the World Cup by beating France ina 10-goal thrillerand end up even more annoyed with the manager. Yet emotions are still running high after the collapse against Argentina last week and it is going to take more than winning a game low on jeopardy for the criticism of Thomas Tuchel to die down.Hiring Tuchel was centred around the promise that he would make the difference in the big games. When that fell down, all that was left was anger. It exposed the core emptiness of the appointment, of a rich federation covering up the flaws in its coaching development by trying to game the system by throwing money at an expensive outsider.Top 30: from half a million World Cup photos, why I love these the mostRead moreWhat is the Football Association’s vision? The initial idea was Tuchel providing a short, sharp shock and his tactical acumen helping England to get over the line. Then came a change after a successful qualifying campaign against modest opposition. The FA moved the goalposts by handing Tuchel a contract extension, and suddenly winning theWorld Cupdid not seem quite as pressing.Yet the idea of Tuchel as a project builder seemed far-fetched during the bitter recriminations after thedefeat by Argentina. Criticised for his negative substitutions when England led, the German talked dismissively of English DNA, lamenting the lack of care in possession. Fair enough, you thought, because this has long been a weakness – only, hadn’t Tuchel caused some of the damage by leaving out most of the ball players from his squad? Would it have been different if Adam Wharton, Trent Alexander-Arnold and Cole Palmer had been around in Atlanta? Maybe not, but a lot of money has been spent on developing a different type of English player in recent decades.Perhaps Tuchel was thinking of an old English DNA. But maybe this is part of the issue with employing a foreign coach whose focus is on winning by any means possible. This is the third timeEnglandhave been down this road. There is an idea of the manager from abroad as a sophisticate. But Sven-Göran Eriksson was not as tactically adroit as Glenn Hoddle. Eriksson played a traditional 4-4-2 whereas Hoddle was flexible with his formations.Tuchel, too, has struggled to build an identity. He seems taken with an outdated English style. Tuchel wanted to play like a Premier League team, with pace and physicality, but that has rarely worked for England. They tend to be outpassed in big knockout games, which is why there has been such focus in youth football in developing players more at ease with the dominant Spanish passing style.View image in fullscreenEngland players look dejected after the semi-final defeat by Argentina.Photograph: Paul Childs/ReutersSpain have justwon another World Cup, their method too much for Argentina in a dour final, and for those watching from afar a big takeaway will surely be that the last two teams standing were led by homegrown coaches.Luis de la Fuente and Lionel Scaloni have risen through the ranks and are part of a system. They have a feeling for their countries, a sense of the identity. Tuchel has been inserted in the hope he can cover up the FA’s failure to produce its own coaches. No male team led by a foreign manager has secured the World Cup and it should worry the FA when bringing in an outsider with a win-now mentality ends with the senior side using a very different style of play to younger age groups.There is a view within youth coaching circles that Tuchel has taken England backwards at this tournament. The under-21s have won the past two European Championships by being brave on the ball. They have beaten Spain in consecutive tournaments and the demand from Lee Carsley is that his players take risks in possession. Tuchel finished the semi-final by lobbing balls towards Dan Burn. He played a pragmatic style and his England ended up unable to throw caution to the wind. This World Cup underlines that turning to celebrated coaches from the club game is not a recipe for success.Brazil appointed Carlo Ancelotti but went out in the last 16. Roberto Martínez failed with Portugal after sticking with Cristiano Ronaldo. Mauricio Pochettino oversaw the USA sidebeing humiliated by Belgium.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionScaloni came through the national setup before winning the World Cup with Argentina in 2022. Similarly De la Fuente won the European Under-19 Championship with Spain in 2015 and the European Under-21 Championship in 2019. He has brought players through and, having earned a promotion, won the senior European title in 2024.View image in fullscreenLuis de la Fuente was a successful age-group manager before taking over Spain’s senior side and leading them to glory.Photograph: Franck Fife/AFP/Getty ImagesSpain show smooth progression. Players who beat Argentina in the final played youth football for De la Fuente. This is not to say that England’s next step should be to appoint Carsley, who did not seem as if he had the temperament for the senior job when he filled in as caretaker between Gareth Southgate’s departure and Tuchel’s arrival. Even so, Carsley is closer to the Spanish style and has shown it is possible for England teams to be risk-takers.It is food for thought for the FA as it reviews England’s World Cup. As it stands, Tuchel will get another tournament. There are bridges to build but finishing third is not a sackable offence. In any case there is no other idea at the FA. Tuchel was not produced, he was bought. Hopefully he adapts during the Nations League. There should be a shift away from this physical style. But do not be surprised if the hired gun’s views on English DNA end up making him sound out of touch. It would not happen to Spain. De la Fuente knows their identity. He is part of it.Explore more on these topicsEnglandThomas TuchelThe FAWorld Cup 2026World CupanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Melvine Malard hungry for trophies after joining Chelsea from Manchester United | Chelsea Women | The Guardian
description: Chelsea have announced the signing of the France forward Melvine Malard on a four-year deal from Manchester United for about £850,000
Author: Suzanne Wrack
Headline: Chelsea have announced the signing of the France forward Melvine Malard on a four-year deal from Manchester United for about £850,000
Content: Melvine Malard scored six times in the Women’s Super League and four times in the Champions League for Manchester United last season.Photograph: Ian Walton/Ian Walton/Chelsea FCView image in fullscreenMelvine Malard scored six times in the Women’s Super League and four times in the Champions League for Manchester United last season.Photograph: Ian Walton/Ian Walton/Chelsea FCChelsea WomenMelvine Malard hungry for trophies after joining Chelsea from Manchester UnitedUnited get club-record fee of about £850,000 for forwardMalard follows McCabe and Matsukubo into ChelseaSuzanne WrackMon 20 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.17 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleChelsea have announced the signing of the France forward Melvine Malard from Manchester United on a four-year contract. The Guardianrevealed last weekthat a deal was close for the 26-year-old, who has left United after three seasons for about £850,000.Malard had one year on her contract and United were keen to keep the forward. However, once it became clear Malard wanted a move United decided to sell rather than lose a high-value player on a free next summer.Women’s transfer window summer 2026: all deals from world’s top six leaguesRead moreIt is believed United will reinvest the fee, which is a record sale for the club, into the squad, with the transfer window open until 3 September.United have made one summer signing, the full-back Andrea Medina from Atlético Madrid, but more incomings are expected after the departures of Leah Galton, Hannah Blundell, Sienna Wareing, Millie Turner, Lisa Naalsund and Malard.“I’m so happy because I have a plan for my life and my career, and Chelsea were a part of that plan,” Malard said. ”My objective is to become better every single day and to win trophies. I know Chelsea always have that as an objective.”The France international played under Chelsea’s head coach, Sonia Bompastor, at Lyonnes (formerly Lyon) and was recruited to their academy by Bompastor. “I have known Sonia since I was 14 years old, so it is great to work with her again,” Malard said.It is understood Chelsea have been tracking Malard for some time. They have lost a number of players across the forward line, with the club record-goalscorer Sam Kerr and Guro Reitendeparting for Gotham FC, Catarina Macario joining San Diego Wave and Johanna Rytting Kaneryd signing for Lyonnes.Malard is Chelsea’s third signing of the summer, after the former Arsenal full-back Katie McCabe and the forward Manaka Matsukubo, who was with North Carolina Courage.Malard spent seven seasons at Lyonnes, winning four Champions Leagues, three league titles and two Coupes de France. Her first season with United was on loan.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMoving the GoalpostsNo topic is too small or too big for us to cover as we deliver a twice-weekly roundup of the wonderful world of women’s footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLast season she scored four goals in United’s run to a first Champions League quarter-final and six times in 18 Women’s Super League games.Liverpoolhave signed the Japanese attacking midfielder Mao Itamura from Feyenoord. The 19-year-old, who won the Under-20 Women’s Asian Cup in April, was the Eredivisie player of the year last season in the Netherlands. She scored eight times and provided six assists as Feyenoord qualified for European competition for the first time, their third-place finish earning a place in the Europa Cup.Itamura is Liverpool’s fifth summer signing, after Vivien Endemann, Sara Agrez, Mari Ward and Khiara Keating.Explore more on these topicsChelsea WomenManchester United WomenWomen's Super LeagueTransfer windowWomen's footballnewsShareReuse this content

title: Vingegaard’s and Pogacar’s night-time doping tests authorised by Paris judge | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: The International Testing Agency had petitioned the Paris prosecutor’s office to test the duo outside of the normal 6am to 11pm window before stage 15
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: The International Testing Agency had petitioned the Paris prosecutor’s office to test the duo outside of the normal 6am to 11pm window before stage 15
Content: Tadej Pogacar and Jonas Vingegaard were subjected to unexpected nocturnal doping controls before stage 15 of the Tour de France.Photograph: Stéphanie Lecocq/ReutersView image in fullscreenTadej Pogacar and Jonas Vingegaard were subjected to unexpected nocturnal doping controls before stage 15 of the Tour de France.Photograph: Stéphanie Lecocq/ReutersTour de France 2026Vingegaard’s and Pogacar’s night-time doping tests authorised by Paris judgeInternational Testing Agency petitioned for testsDuo tested outside of normal 6am to 11pm windowJeremy Whittlein La Roche sur ForonMon 20 Jul 2026 16.24 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 17.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA Paris judge authorised the night-time doping tests of theTour de France leader, Tadej Pogacar, and his nearest rival, Jonas Vingegaard, in the early hours of Sunday morning.The Paris prosecutor’s office confirmed on Monday that the International Testing Agency (ITA) petitioned the “Paris judge specialising in liberty and detention matters … to authorise the anti-doping tests that took place overnight between July 18 and 19”.Vingegaard crashes out of Tour de France after ‘inhuman’ 2am doping controlRead moreVingegaard and Pogacar were subjected to unexpected nocturnal doping controls, outside the normal window of 6am to 11pm, before stage 15, as the peloton headed to Plateau de Solaison in the Savoyard Alps.Vingegaard was tested at 2am on Sunday by the ITA, while Pogacar’s unexpected wake-up call came at 5am. Pogacar described Vingegaard’s 2am test as “inhuman” and suggested that it “ruined” his longstanding rival’s sleep and could have been afactor in the crashon Sunday, in which he broke his collarbone, and forced him to quit the Tour.Before the stage start on Sunday, Vingegaard said: “I was sleeping very well. It’s good that they test, but when it’s affecting performance and your sleep, I don’t think it’s so good.”Under the anti-doping regulations of the InternationalCyclingUnion (UCI) which delegated its anti-doping programme to the ITA in 2021, the UCI must have “serious and specific suspicions that the rider may be involved in doping activities” to conduct tests between 11pm and 6am. The judges responsible for liberty and detention (JLD) “have concurrent jurisdiction to rule on requests for night-time anti-doping tests”, the Paris public prosecutor’s office told AFP.The timing of the tests was widely condemned by leading riders and team staff, including Remco Evenepoel, now second overall in the Tour, and Vingegaard’s teammate Matteo Jorgenson, who said it showed “a complete lack of respect” towards the riders.The ITA said it was “conscious that night-time testing may be disruptive to riders’ rest and recovery. Due consideration has therefore been given to minimise as much as possible the impact testing activities have on riders. However, the ITA, on behalf of the UCI, has the responsibility to ensure the effectiveness of the cycling anti‑doping programme and the integrity of cycling races.”It is now also believed that up to five teams, including Pogacar’s UAE Team Emirates XRG and Vingegaard’s Visma Lease-a-bike, have been subject to nocturnal testing during this year’s Tour. While not specifying numbers, the ITA confirmed to the Guardian that “other teams also have been subjected to ‘night-time’ or early morning testing”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |FortnightlySign up toThe HotspotA new fortnightly newsletter covering the intersection of sport and the climate crisisPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionNight-time testing was introduced primarily to combat the use of micro-dosing of performance-enhancing products, a practice which allows only a short window for detection.Jonathan Vaughters, now manager of the EF-Education EasyPost team currently riding in the Tour, and a former teammate to Lance Armstrong who was stripped of seven Tour wins for doping, supported the nocturnal tests. Vaughters, posting on X, said: “Two am testing is one of the very effective ways to prevent peptide (EPO, HGH) and testosterone micro dosing. If [the] UCI had tested us in 2001 at 2am? We’d all have been suspended and saved the sport 25 years of drama.”The Tour ends in Paris on Sunday evening but there is no known timescale for the publication of results. The ITA added that the “outcome of these controls, as any other conducted by the ITA, would be published according to our public disclosure policy, only in the case of an apparent anti-doping rule violation”.Explore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Jonas VingegaardTadej PogacarTour de FranceCyclingnewsShareReuse this content

title: England players question ‘brutal’ travel demands in Nations Championship format  | England rugby union team | The Guardian
description: England’s players have questioned the Nations Championship format after being required to play Test matches on three different continents on successive weekends
Author: Robert Kitson
Headline: England’s players have questioned the Nations Championship format after being required to play Test matches on three different continents on successive weekends
Content: Ben Earl described England’s global schedule this summer as a ‘tough way of ending’ the season.Photograph: Joaquin Camiletti/RFU/The RFU Collection/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenBen Earl described England’s global schedule this summer as a ‘tough way of ending’ the season.Photograph: Joaquin Camiletti/RFU/The RFU Collection/Getty ImagesEngland rugby union teamEngland players question ‘brutal’ travel demands in Nations Championship formatPlayed in South Africa, England and Argentina across three weekendsSquad covered 26,000 miles this month in hectic travel scheduleRobert Kitsonin Buenos AiresMon 20 Jul 2026 13.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEngland’s players have questioned the currentNations Championshipformat after being required to play Test matches on three different continents on successive weekends.Steve Borthwick’s squad have covered 26,000 miles this month and at least one senior player believes the tournament should consider reducing the travel demands for all involved.Having spent the past three weeks flitting between Johannesburg, Liverpool and Santiago del Estero for games against South Africa, Fiji and Argentina respectively, the England No 8 Ben Earl is among those with strong views about the logistical demands of the schedule. “It’s been brutal,” said Earl, who was in the starting XV for all three fixtures.Feyi-Waboso gives England more bite but there is no ignoring elephant in the roomRead more“There’s no two ways about it, it’s tough, but we’ve got to do what we can and try to make the best of it. At the end of what’s been quite a long season for most of us, it’s been a tough way of ending: back and forth to England, seeing your families and then going away again. It has seemed a bit weird but it’s something we’re going to have to get used to. I think it’s just the beginning.”England have been the team most obviously affected this month, with Ireland having insisted on facing Japan on neutral ground in Australia rather than trekking to Tokyo as well as Sydney and Auckland. England’s players, however, believe all involved would benefit from more teams being based in the same location.View image in fullscreenBen Earl scored twice for England in their victory against Argentina.Photograph: Gero Uranga/Shutterstock“We’ve been speaking about it,” confirmed Earl. “You just go to one nation and have a festival feel. Why would we not all be playing in one country? I don’t know the financials but how good would that be for the fans? You come to Australia, South Africa or wherever and there’s just so much rugby going on. How cool would that be? [But] I’m not paid to make those decisions. We just get told where to go and where to play, and we just try and do that.”The Nations Championship was launched this year as a broadcaster-friendly alternative to traditional tours, with all gamesavailable to watch free-to-air on ITV. From a playing perspective, however, the extra travel involved has affected the amount of preparation time available to visiting teams. “You just don’t do as much training,” confirmed Earl. “You train a little bit later in the week and you train harder, closer to the game.“You’ve got to do certain things in the week to be able to perform. There are myriad different things that make it tricky but we’ve been adaptable. For those of us who were on the British & Irish Lions tour last summer, it was pretty similar.”With a busy World Cup year in prospect in 2027, however, the likes of Earl currently have no choice other than to grit their teeth if they are to retain their Test positions. “I love playing for my country,” stressed Earl, who rounded off his personal campaignwith two tries against Argentinaon Saturday. “You forget about all that other stuff the moment you pull on the shirt. Once you get to the stadiums and into a warm-up (everything else) pales into insignificance.”Explore more on these topicsEngland rugby union teamNations ChampionshipRugby unionnewsShareReuse this content

title: Tasmania Devils sign Ken Hinkley as first AFL coach in five-year deal | Tasmania Devils | The Guardian
description: Former Port Adelaide coach will prepare club for 2028 entry after Nathan Buckley and John Longmire overlooked for role
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Former Port Adelaide coach will prepare club for 2028 entry after Nathan Buckley and John Longmire overlooked for role
Content: Former Port Adelaide coach Ken Hinkley will join the Tasmania Devils as the club prepare to enter the AFL in 2028.Photograph: Steve Bell/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenFormer Port Adelaide coach Ken Hinkley will join the Tasmania Devils as the club prepare to enter the AFL in 2028.Photograph: Steve Bell/Getty ImagesTasmania DevilsTasmania Devils sign Ken Hinkley as first AFL coach in five-year dealFormer Port Adelaide coach will prepare club for 2028 entryNathan Buckley and John Longmire overlooked for roleAustralian Associated PressSun 19 Jul 2026 22.24 EDTFirst published on Sun 19 Jul 2026 20.22 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleKen Hinkley has been officially named as the Tasmania Devils’ inaugural AFL coach, less than 12 months after beingmoved on from Port Adelaide.The league’s newest expansion franchise, who will enter the competition in 2028, announced on Monday that the 59-year-old had signed a five-year deal.Hinkley presented to Devils chief executive, Brendon Gale, and Tasmania’s board in Hobart on Sunday and got the nod over Nathan Buckley and John Longmire, who had both expressed interest in the job.Collingwood bring Carlton back down to earth just as an AFL wildcard spot seemed in reach | Jonathan HornRead more“It’s a huge privilege to be given the opportunity to build something from the very beginning,” Hinkley said of the deal that will take him to the end of 2031.“The responsibility to lead Tasmania’s entry into theAFLis something very special, it excites me.“Tasmania is a proud football state. I can’t wait to get to know the players, staff, partners and community who have, and will continue to be part of this from day one.”Ex-Sydney Swans boss Longmire had met with the Devils a fortnight ago in what he described as a fact-finding mission.Former Collingwood coach Buckley was favoured for the job last year when he pulled out of the race for Melbourne’s coaching job.It is a big milestone ticked off for theDevils who started playing in the VFL and VFLWcompetitions this season.Allow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continue“We didn’t set out to find the candidate with the single best win-loss record in this process,” Gale said.“We set out to find the right coach for exactly what Tasmania Football Club needs right now. Ken’s ability to build relationships with young players, and to build an environment that people want to be part of, is exceptional.“It is the number one reason we believe he is the right fit for this club at this time.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toFrom the Pocket: AFL WeeklyJonathan Horn brings expert analysis on the week's biggest AFL storiesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionHinkley coached Port Adelaide from 2013 to 2025, instantly taking the club to the finals after a dismal five seasons following the club’s humiliating and record-breaking grand final loss in 2007.Although Hinkley never got to a grand final from three preliminary final appearances, Port were almost always in contention during his time.He walked away with an excellent winning record of 58.6%, having guided Port to 174 victories from his 297 games in charge.The club’s off-field situation also improved dramatically as crowds returned to games because they were competitive.Hinkley was named coach of the year twice (2013 and 2020) but stepped aside when highly rated assistant Josh Carr wanted the Port job.He played 132 games for Fitzroy and Geelong between 1987 and 1995 and spent time as an assistant at Geelong and Gold Coast, as well as a year at St Kilda in 2001, before winning the Port role as a 46-year-old.After leaving Port at the end of the 2025 season, Hinkley has spent this year in the media working for Fox Footy and SEN.Explore more on these topicsTasmania DevilsAFLAustralian rules footballAustralia sportnewsShareReuse this content

title: DeChambeau moves the dial for golf but is his posturing and pantomime worth the hassle? | Bryson DeChambeau | The Guardian
description: Petulant reaction to a justified two-stroke penalty at the Open illustrates why the American is happier on the LIV tour
Author: Ewan Murray
Headline: Petulant reaction to a justified two-stroke penalty at the Open illustrates why the American is happier on the LIV tour
Content: Bryson DeChambeau during his third round of the Open at Royal Birkdale.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianView image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau during his third round of the Open at Royal Birkdale.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianBryson DeChambeauDeChambeau moves the dial for golf but is his posturing and pantomime worth the hassle?Ewan MurrayPetulant reaction to a justified two-stroke penalty at the Open illustrates why the American is happier on the LIV tourMon 20 Jul 2026 11.05 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.38 EDTShareThe potential for stigma was laid bare close to the conclusion of Saturday at the Open Championship. “Bryson, ya cheat,” a lone voice bellowed from the stand adjacent to Royal Birkdale’s 18th green. This is an environment in which being unfaithful to one’s spouse would be seen as preferable to breaching the sacred rules of a sacred game.By Sunday the world No 1, Scottie Scheffler, was riding to the defence ofBryson DeChambeau. “I’ve known him for a long time and he’s a lot of things, but he’s definitely not a cheater,” Scheffler said. Except in this case, nuance is required. No one in authority has actually accused DeChambeau of cheating for the incident which incurred a two-stroke penalty. The R&A’s executive director of governance stipulated that DeChambeau’s action when breaching the rules during round two was “accidental”.Fleetwood, Fox and fury: Tom Jenkins’ best images from the OpenRead moreIt was an odd thing for Grant Moir to say, which smacked not only as if trying to placate an irate DeChambeau but working a little too hard to protect the reputation of the Open. It does not matter whether DeChambeau’s action in stamping down long grass behind his ball was deliberate or not.Intention is irrelevant, just as it was whenShane Lowry incurred a two-stroke penaltyin the Open of 2025. Then, as on Friday, a rule was breached. The R&A’s misstep here was nonetheless a trivial one. DeChambeau was in the wrong, far more in respect of his petulant response to the situation than the fact that it arose in the first place.If sport were pure, this Open would be known forRyan Fox’s nerveless finish. The popular New Zealander earned his maiden major win in emphatic fashion. The pace and simplicity with which Fox plies his trade offers lessons for us all.It was, instead, a tournament dominated by matters involving DeChambeau. By Monday morning, the R&A was fielding requests over whetherDeChambeau threatened to bring Donald Trumpinto heated discussions over his sanction on Friday evening. While this scenario was easy to believe, such action would also be futile; given the R&A has left Trump’s Turnberry resort in the Open wilderness for years, it is hardly ever going to let the US president influence a leaderboard. Trump has always been deferential towards the R&A. DeChambeau’s protests felt telling. This is a player subject to very little external scrutiny on the LIV tour. He never appeared to have any intention of fielding media questions on the Open affair.Golf has endured or enjoyed, depending on one’s outlook, a heated news cycle for years. It has been a notable and intriguing shift. Those who insist that the sport at core level is boring are ignorant to the fact that it is no more or less tedious than cricket, Formula One or cycling.View image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau talks to an R&A rules official on the 18th hole of his third round.Photograph: Tom Jenkins/The GuardianLIV’s arrival and subsequent fallout had just about settled down by the time Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund determined golf was an expense it could do without. Cue the latest, ongoing theme; LIV’s probable demise and how a fractured sport pieces itself together again. Birdies and bogeys, brilliance such as that delivered by Fox, are all still covered but the noise outside the ropes matters more than ever before.In this sense, DeChambeau and associated chaos was an Open gift. Rory McIlroy, once a defender of DeChambeau, said what a lot of players were understood to be thinkingby berating the Americanfor the posturing and pantomime of Friday. The R&A’s hierarchy, who were furious at DeChambeau, surely approved. McIlroy’s remarks lit up the internet far more than Sam Burns’s run to the top of the leaderboard.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |FortnightlySign up toThe HotspotA new fortnightly newsletter covering the intersection of sport and the climate crisisPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe question is whether golf needs the hassle. We may be about to find out. DeChambeau’s clear preference is to remain on the LIV circuit, even in modified form, if fresh funding can be sourced for 2027. If that does not come to pass, or if DeChambeau reckons a lack of competition there is harming his chances on bigger stages, he will have to beat a path back towards thePGA Tour. That circuit will have no intention of making life easy for DeChambeau but what price the eyeballs, the drama, the noise that some insist this sport needs? DeChambeau up against McIlroy on a regular basis would be box office.If DeChambeau has his way, no one will look too closely at a major record for 2026 which involves three missed cuts and a tie for 14th. His Open Sunday was dismal, from an earlier position of great promise. Nonetheless, an expectant golf public awaits DeChambeau’s bulletin on all things Birkdale; most likely coming to a media outlet influenced by the man himself very soon. In DeChambeau, LIV has an attention-grabbing asset.DeChambeau is treading a fine line between force for good and comedic distraction. The sport’s relationship with this player is a complex one. Birkdale did Bryson no favours.Explore more on these topicsBryson DeChambeauThe OpenPGA TourGolfUS sportscommentShareReuse this content

title: Jeff Bezos and UK government invest in £2bn British startup CuspAI | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Company aims to develop AI software that cuts research times and use of rare metals in chipmakers’ supply chains
Author: Mark Sweney
Headline: Company aims to develop AI software that cuts research times and use of rare metals in chipmakers’ supply chains
Content: Nvidia's Groq 3 chip. The firm is part of coalition launched by CuspAI that aims to build software to discover and developed new materials for chipmakers.Photograph: Max A Cherney/ReutersView image in fullscreenNvidia's Groq 3 chip. The firm is part of coalition launched by CuspAI that aims to build software to discover and developed new materials for chipmakers.Photograph: Max A Cherney/ReutersAI (artificial intelligence)Jeff Bezos and UK government invest in £2bn British startup CuspAICompany aims to develop AI software that cuts research times and use of rare metals in chipmakers’ supply chainsBusiness live – latest updatesMark SweneyMon 20 Jul 2026 04.37 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAmazon’s founder,Jeff Bezos, and the UK government have invested in a £2bn British artificial intelligence startup that is aiming to become the “search engine for rare materials” that accelerates the next wave of technological breakthroughs.The Cambridge-based CuspAI hasraised $450m (£330m) in fundingfrom investors including Bezos and the government’s sovereign AI fund, valuing the two-year-old business at $2.6bn.The company has launched the AI Materials Foundry, a coalition of more than 48 tech companies, industrial firms and research facilities, with the aim of building software underpinned by artificial intelligence to discover and develop new materials for chipmakers and other industries.CuspAI hopes to significantly cut research times and reduce or eliminate the use of rare metals – including iridium and ruthenium – in chipmakers’ supply chains.The coalition, which includes the chipmaker Nvidia, the Facebook and Instagram owner, Meta Platforms, and the carmaker Hyundai, aims to use frontier AI, the most advanced, general-purpose artificial intelligence models, to discover new materials that could make energy networks and batteries more efficient and advance the power of semiconductors that underpin the AI revolution.“If we don’t make progress fast, the next 50 years of industrial progress will be constrained by a single challenge: the world needs materials that don’t yet exist,” said the CuspAI co-founders Dr Chad Edwards and Prof Max Welling. “That’s what we’re on a mission to solve – combining frontier agentic AI with deep domain expertise, exclusive data access and close customer partnerships. The world simply can’t make the progress it needs to with known materials, that much is clear.”The company said the round ofseries Bfunding would enable it to expand internationally, opening a new office in Singapore and increasing staff numbers in Cambridge, Amsterdam, Berlin, Tokyo and the US.Josh Coyne, a partner at Kleiner Perkins, the venture capital firm that co-led the latest round of funding, said: “Most big leaps in technology come down to a material, and the next set, from cheaper carbon capture to semiconductors and cleaner water, is stuck waiting on materials nobody has discovered yet. CuspAI built a search engine that changes that.”The latest fundraise followed a $100m series A investment last year.CuspAI is the fourth company to receive funding from the government’s fund targeting investment in early-stage British AI firms to help them scale up to compete on the world stage. The government did not disclose the size of the investment.Criteria for receiving funding from the sovereign AI fund, which makes a typical equity investment in a startup of between £1m and £10m, include having a main office in the UK and the company’s founders being British.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLiz Kendall, the science and technology secretary, said: “CuspAI is putting the power of AI to work to help us tackle some of the biggest challenges facing the UK and the world today, such as energy and climate change. By backing British companies such as this we are paving the way for breakthroughs that are set to grow our economy, protect the natural world and improve everyone’s lives.”CuspAI has brought on board John Giannandrea, a former Apple and Google executive, who is helping set up its US operations.The company is also working with Abhi Talwalkar, a board member at chip maker AMD and chair of Lam Research.Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)Technology startupsJeff BezosTechnology policyTechnology sectorEconomic policyComputingnewsShareReuse this content

title: Experts warn of rise in sadistic online exploitation of vulnerable children | Technology | The Guardian
description: Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material
Author: Katie McQue
Headline: Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material
Content: The cases received by the NCMEC represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say.Photograph: Chainarong Prasertthai/Getty Images/iStockphotoView image in fullscreenThe cases received by the NCMEC represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say.Photograph: Chainarong Prasertthai/Getty Images/iStockphotoTechnologyExperts warn of rise in sadistic online exploitation of vulnerable childrenGlobal increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse materialKatie McQueMon 20 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCases of predators coercing children into producing sexual photos and videos of themselves have surged globally in the past year, a cruel phenomenon known as “sadistic online exploitation”, child safety experts and law enforcement officials warn.In cases of sadistic online exploitation, perpetrators target vulnerable teens on social media platforms and online games. These predators then compel their victims into producing child sexual abuse material (CSAM), harming themselves and others, or, in extreme cases, attempting suicide.“We often see that the offenders will communicate with that child and, through that initial communication, ask them for nude or explicit imagery of themselves,” said Kathryn Rifenbark, a director at the National Center of Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC). “Then they’re using that as a leverage to cause the child to harm themselves or harm other children or other people in their home or create more explicit imagery of them or other children in their home.”Recent cases include theJune sentencingof a 21-year-old Maryland man to 30 years in prison for sexually exploiting 10 female minors and cyberstalking.The perpetrator “used the internet to create and share extreme content – such as gore, violence, and child sexual abuse material – to extort and blackmail vulnerable teenagers”, and the man sexually exploited his victims and encouraged them to engage in self-harm, according to a Department of Justice press release.The NCMEC received more than 3,000 reports of sadistic online exploitation worldwide in 2025, a 125% increase from the previous year. But those cases represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say, because the NCMEC is only notified if child sexual abuse material has been detected and reported.Predators targeting children often belong to online networks, and are often teens or young adults themselves. To recruit their victims, the perpetrators engage with video games popular with children, such as Roblox and Minecraft. A spokesperson for Microsoft, which owns Minecraft, said: “Our goal is to make Minecraft one of the hardest places for bad actors to operate, and we have a variety of tools we use to help foster a safe online experience, such as parental controls, chat filtering, in-game reporting, and dedicated moderation teams.”They may also search social media groups that focus on mental health issues such as eating disorders or suicide ideation because they are specifically looking for vulnerable children to target and groom, child safety experts interviewed said.Perpetrators of sadistic online exploitation who coerce children into inflicting other forms of harm on themselves fall outside those reports.The coerced abuses can include forcing children to cut themselves, eat their own vomit, drink urine, die by suicide, or harm other children and pets in their homes. Some groups also engage in mass casualty planning, doxing and cyber-harassment.A common form of abuse is forcing victims to carve perpetrators’ social media usernames on their bodies, said Amanda Goharian, director of research at Thorn, a child safety non-profit.“One of the key things in these groups is that the level of severity of the harms that are being committed are extreme, and they can rapidly develop into outcomes such as completed suicides,” said Goharian.In February, theFBI issued a warningto parents and caregivers about the “sharp increase” in the activity of violent online networks, including the “764” group, targeting and exploiting children and other vulnerable individuals.FBI opens inquiry into 764, online group that sexually exploits and encourages minors to self-harmRead more“The term nihilistic violent extremism (NVE) refers to what is motivating most of these subjects to commit criminal acts. They want to sow chaos and facilitate the destruction of society,” said the letter. “These subjects may be engaging in criminal activity for sexual gratification, social status, a sense of belonging, or for a mix of other reasons.”The predators target vulnerable children who have disclosed personal information online about the struggles they face, such as mental health challenges, eating disorders, self-harm or suicidal ideation, via gaming or social media platforms. These children are looking for connections and support online, which can render them more susceptible to manipulation, said the experts interviewed.Predators typically invite victims to move the conversation to a messaging app such as Telegram, which, according to Rifenbark, “doesn’t report very much” to the NCMEC, meaning the organization has limited visibility into potential child sexual exploitation.It’s on these messaging platforms where the sadistic online exploitation takes place. Telegram did not respond to a request for comment.Many of the perpetrators have troubled lives or are socially isolated in real life, and are looking for notoriety online as a form of empowerment and attention from others, experts interviewed said.“These groups are focused on harming children and targeting children for the purposes of clout and like gaining hierarchy and power both within their groups and over the victims,” said Maria Thomas, investigator at Rise Information Security Foundation, a non-profit focused on cybersecurity. “Teens join them because it’s seen as a subversive and edgy thing to do.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe perpetrators gain status within their groups from posting evidence of their abuse, images such as dossiers of photos and screenshots of messages with their victims, which are known as “lore books”. Typically, these collections are uploaded on to a Telegram channel labeled as “Victim Name Lore Book”, experts said.“Harm is perpetrated as a way to essentially gain clout within the network, and to be elevated,” said Goharian.Organized nihilistic violent extremism and sadistic online exploitation were first documented in the 2010s, when a youth cybergang called the Com, which focused on hacking and identity theft before some subsets shifted into child exploitation. Splinter networks, such as CVLT, an online group that espoused neo-Nazism, nihilism and pedophilia as its core principles, were formed. The group 764 was formed in 2021 by a teenager in Texas who had learned grooming and exploitation methods as a member of CVLT.Online exploitation can escalate into dramatic real-world violence, experts warn. In February, an 18-year-old man from Pennsylvania wasarrested and chargedwith grooming a 13-year-old girl he met on social gaming platform Discord, allegedly convincing her to send him sexually explicit material of herself and images of self-harm. The man travelled across the country to engage in sexual activity with the girl in a motel, according to the US justice department. “Discord has a longstanding policy onTeen and Child Safetythat prohibits sexual exploitation and predatory behavior, including content or communities that facilitate grooming,” said a Discord spokesperson.“When law enforcement arrived at the motel room, they encountered [the man] and the victim and found condoms, a knife, lubricant, razor blades, bloody tissues,” stated a Department of Justice press release about the case. “The victim told law enforcement that [the man] and she had engaged in sexual conduct and that he had used a knife to repeatedly cut her.”In May, a 16-year-old boy from South Carolina was arrested for engaging inexploitation tied to 764after receiving a tip from Discord regarding possible school violence.“Offenders have a very, very high level of control over the children. They are using that connection that they’ve built with that child to really try to control that child to do some pretty harmful things to themselves,” said Rifenbark.Rifenbark said the NCMEC had received reports from parents who discovered what was happening to their child and took away the child’s phone, only for the child to find another device in the home, such as an old device the family was no longer using, and use it to re-engage with the offenders online, probably due to the high levels of control and grooming they have been subjected to.“A lot of times when victims are coming and reporting to us, they’re very scared, they’re scared of retaliation from offenders, they’re worried that their personal information might get leaked, because that’s something that the offenders are doing as part of their victimization,” Rifenbark said.According to Goharian, the networks are highly persistent, with individual perpetrators often operating hundreds of accounts. If one account is reported for violating a platform’s terms of service and removed, they can quickly switch to “shadow” accounts that are already established and immediately resume their activity.According to a state prosecutor, speaking on the condition of anonymity out of concern for professional reprisals, some juveniles who commit sexual abuse do not outgrow the behavior but often go on to offend as adults. Better interventions are needed for young offenders before their behavior escalates, they said.“We just do a really bad job sometimes of recognizing dangerous kids,” said the prosecutor. “I don’t think these kids are going to grow out of it. I think they’re growing into it.”In the US, call or text theChildhelpabuse hotline on 800-422-4453 or visittheir websitefor more resources and to report child abuse or DM for help. For adult survivors of child abuse, help is available atascasupport.org. In the UK, theNSPCCoffers support to children on 0800 1111, and adults concerned about a child on 0808 800 5000. The National Association for People Abused in Childhood (Napac) offers support for adult survivors on 0808 801 0331. In Australia, children, young adults, parents and teachers can contact the Kids Helpline on 1800 55 1800, orBraveheartson 1800 272 831, and adult survivors can contactBlue Knot Foundationon 1300 657 380. Other sources of help can be found atChild Helplines InternationalExplore more on these topicsTechnologyChildrenChild protectionInternetnewsShareReuse this content

title: AI-altered images on birdwatching forums putting research at risk | AI (artificial intelligence) | The Guardian
description: Experts warn increase in enhanced photos on birding platforms creating fake sightings, threatening credibility of tool used by scientists
Author: Patrick Greenfield
Headline: Experts warn increase in enhanced photos on birding platforms creating fake sightings, threatening credibility of tool used by scientists
Content: ‘Nobody is falling for a toucan sighting in Siberia’ … This image is AI-generated, using ChatGPT.Illustration: ChatGPTView image in fullscreen‘Nobody is falling for a toucan sighting in Siberia’ … This image is AI-generated, using ChatGPT.Illustration: ChatGPTThe age of extinctionAI (artificial intelligence)AI-altered images on birdwatching forums putting research at riskExperts warn increase in enhanced photos on birding platforms creating fake sightings, threatening credibility of tool used by scientistsThe age of extinction is supported byAbout this contentPatrick GreenfieldMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFor many birdwatchers, recording a species outside its normal range is the holy grail. In the UK, the discoveries often make national headlines. The western reef heron, for example, usually found in Africa and southern Europe,was spotted in a seaside town in north Walesin June, and widely celebrated on birding forums.But a new scourge is threatening to disrupt the fun: AI slop.Scientists are appealing to birders to limit their use of AI when editing images over fears that it could undermine the credibility of popular citizen science platforms such as iNaturalist and Macaulay Library, which are routinely used in scientific research to monitor species’ habitat range.The rise of generative AI platforms such asChatGPTand Google Gemini has led to a sharp increase in fake and enhanced images of rare and celebrated birds across wildlife photography forums. Users can create high-quality fake images in seconds – or enhance a photograph by asking the AI to remove a branch or leaf that might be obscuring part of the subject, which can inadvertently introduce significant changes.In arecent commentaryin the science journal Nature, researchers warned that hundreds of fake images have already been discovered on popular databases for recording species. They said that the true scale of the issue is unknown – as many could go undetected – and could contaminate records collected by the public.“My experience of looking at Facebook these days is that a huge volume of wildlife photos now are simply AI-generated imagery,” said Dr Alexander Lees, an ecologist at Manchester Metropolitan University who authored the journal article. “The idea that we could maybe use those photos to help us understand where species are in space and time is very difficult.”Outright hoaxes remain rare and are often easy to spot, said Lees – nobody is falling for a toucan sighting in Siberia – but he says birders often use AI to edit and improve an image, and the algorithm can then introduce parts from different bird species to create the new image.Lees points to the example of a false sighting of a red-winged blackbird in central Brazil, which is usually found in North America and had never been seen in this area of Brazil until the reported sighting. The bird was, in fact, an epaulet oriole – a commonnew world bird species– but the photographer had asked an AI platform to make the picture “look better”, which is when parts of the red-winged blackbird were added, resulting in the false sighting.Bird migration is changing. What does this reveal about our planet? – visualisedRead more“Wildlife photographers can be quite obsessed with getting a beautiful photo, but there’s a risk that the image might actually cause problems down the line when AI has been used to edit it,” said Lees.Citizen science organisations are still working to understand the scale of the issue. On iNaturalist, a social network where nature enthusiasts can record sightings of plants and animals, just 1,400 of the more than 610m images on the platform have been flagged for AI use. From monitoring how plants and animals move in response to climate breakdown to recording new behaviour in species,dozens of discoverieshave been made usingcitizen science.Tony Iwane, iNaturalist’s director of community support, who was a co-author on the Nature paper, said most of these cases were unlikely to be malicious, but appealed to users to be vigilant.“On platforms like ours, regular people are posting information that a scientist could probably never get at scale. It is also almost like a sensor of what is happening on Earth in real time: are plants flowering early? Are species moving north as the climate warms? The more we know about where species are, the better informed we can be as conservationists. But the information needs to be accurate,” he said.Find moreage of extinction coverage here, and follow the biodiversity reportersRebecca Ratcliffe,Phoebe WestonandPatrick Greenfieldin the Guardian app for more nature coverage.Explore more on these topicsAI (artificial intelligence)The age of extinctionBirdsAnimalsWildlifeClimate crisisComputingChatGPTnewsShareReuse this content

title: Tiny yet powerful, the Anker 511 Nano 3 is the best budget USB charger we tested | Life and style | The Guardian
description: Anker 511 Nano 3 is the kind of charger you’ll want a few of, and for $15.99, you can afford to stock up
Author: Mark Knapp
Headline: Anker 511 Nano 3 is the kind of charger you’ll want a few of, and for $15.99, you can afford to stock up
Content: When I tested 22 USB chargers to find the best, this easily took the crown for best budget charger thanks to its strong power delivery, compact size and hard-to-beat price.Photograph: Courtesy of AmazonView image in fullscreenWhen I tested 22 USB chargers to find the best, this easily took the crown for best budget charger thanks to its strong power delivery, compact size and hard-to-beat price.Photograph: Courtesy of AmazonThe Filter USLife and styleTiny yet powerful, the Anker 511 Nano 3 is the best budget USB charger we testedAnker 511 Nano 3 is the kind of charger you’ll want a few of, and for $15.99, you can afford to stock upThis clever router fixed my spotty home wifi – and it’s currently on saleMark KnappMon 20 Jul 2026 14.40 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Abeefy USB charger with half a dozen ports can be great for charging your laptop, phone and evenUSB bike lightall at once, but the real heroes are the pocketable chargers small enough to always be around when you need them. That’s where the Anker 511 Nano 3 fits in.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.When Itested 22 different USB chargersto find the best, this little charger easily took the crown for best budget charger thanks to its strong power delivery, compact size and a very hard-to-beat everyday price. That price just got better, though, with aseasonal dealbringing it down from its usual $19.99 to $15.99.Anker511 Nano 3$15.99Now $12.34, originally $19.99 at AmazonI already have a couple of these chargers floating around in my home and have no shortage of other chargers. But at 20% off, I’m almost tempted to stock up on even more because they’re just that good.First, the 511 Nano 3 is impressively small. It has folding prongs to shrink up for storage, and it weighs just 0.08lbs. If you can find room in your bag or pocket forearbuds, you can find room for this charger. Many peers this size only put out 20W, but the 511 Nano 3 delivers 30W. That’s plenty for phones, tablets and even enough for some low-power laptops including Apple’sMacBook Neo.View image in fullscreenPhotograph: Courtesy of AmazonIt’s also reliable. Despite a compact size that often correlates with overheating, the 511 Nano 3 proved capable of delivering its full 30W for 30 minutes straight, and provided 20W for an hour straight. Some chargers, especially in this price range, can only deliver high power levels for a little while before they have to drop down to lower levels, so the 511 Nano 3’s dependability is notable.Geeks will also appreciate that it supports four different voltages, and a standard called programmable power supply. That means it works with a wider array of devices than cheap competitors, and charges them at the fastest speeds they can handle. Other low-cost chargers I tested weren’t as versatile, such as theAnker 511 Nano ProandAmazon Basics Fast Charging USB-C Nano GaN Wall Charger.Many competitors at this price lack basic safety certifications, but the Anker 511 Nano 3 proudly sports a TÜV Rheinland badge, meaning a trusted third-party lab has certified it safe.With all that in mind, it should be pretty clear why the 511 Nano 3 was already a good deal at $20. With this discount bringing it down to $15.99, you can afford to grab one for your backpack, your kitchen drawer and your car glovebox. The deal even applies to all the fun colors the charger comes in, including lilac purple, misty blue and natural green.View image in fullscreenOther pieces you might enjoy fromthe Filter, the Guardian’s guide to buying fewer, better things:We stress tested 22 USB chargers to find the best for phones, laptops, travel and moreApple Watches are cheaper than ever right now. Here are the ones we’d buyI’m a digital nomad who works on the road. These seven travel gadgets go with me everywhereExplore the FilterExplore more on these topicsLife and styleThe Filter USGadgetsAmazonfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Apple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own family | Games | The Guardian
description: The developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edge
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edge
Content: Someone wants to kill grandma … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesView image in fullscreenSomeone wants to kill grandma … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesSummer games previewsGamesApple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own familyThe developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edgeNicole CarpenterMon 20 Jul 2026 12.35 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 12.36 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSomeone wants to kill grandma. In thisKnives Out-inspired mystery game, each of your family members is suspicious in their own way: your stressed mum, your “midlife crisis uncle” and especially that stranger in your bedroom. Yikes!While Apple Crumble is still a mystery, it is mechanically different from Happy Broccoli Games’ previous game,Duck Detective, in which the player solves crimes in a small office. Here you are largely snooping through your family home. “I love the idea of having a walking sim where you can really open up every drawer, every door, and see what’s behind there,” says founder and creative director Annika Maar. As you learn more about the different grievances and troubles of your family members, the mystery slowly unwinds.View image in fullscreen‘You can open up every drawer, every door, and see what’s behind there’ … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesAfter Duck Detective, the team wanted to make a “sad grandma” game – something that may come as a surprise after the humour of the studio’s previous games. Apple Crumble begins comedically, says Maar, but the tone shifts throughout the game; it gets more serious and a bit sad: “I felt very emotionally attached to the death of my grandma. There’s some baggage there and I thought it would be fun to explore that.”You’ll pick up the plot by digging through stuff. Lots of stuff. Apple Crumble’s storytelling extends into the different made-up fiction placed throughout the house. “There’s a film script that the midlife crisis uncle wrote which he’s convinced is going to get money from investors and become huge,” says Maar. “Just so many small details that I’m hoping will make the world feel very believable.”That depth of hidden detail also applies to the art style, which was inspired, in part, by concept art for Puss in Boots. “In the movie and the concept art, they use a lot of these paint splatters,” says Maar. “From a distance it’s not immediately apparent, but when you look, you’re like, ‘Wow, there are so many different colours in this.’ Your eyes kind of blend it together and it just makes it feel more natural. Less clinical. More exciting to look at. It ended up being a lot more work than I thought it would be, but I think it was worth it.”Maar is currently finishing up the script and will soon begin the process of recording voice actors. Happy Broccoli is booking the actors together so that they can really riff off each other. After all, with a grandma-killer in their midst, there are a lot of arguments to record.Apple Crumble is in development forPC. Release is planned for later in 2026Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsPCfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The trouble with Trump’s latest nominees lies in one word: integrity | Robert Reich | The Guardian
description: As Todd Blanche, Erica Schwartz and Jay Clayton face the Senate, a fundamental disconnect becomes clear
Author: Robert Reich
Headline: As Todd Blanche, Erica Schwartz and Jay Clayton face the Senate, a fundamental disconnect becomes clear
Content: ‘A choice must be made – personal integrityorloyalty to Trump.’Photograph: Michael Brochstein/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreen‘A choice must be made – personal integrityorloyalty to Trump.’Photograph: Michael Brochstein/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockOpinionDonald TrumpThe trouble with Trump’s latest nominees lies in one word: integrityRobert ReichAs Todd Blanche, Erica Schwartz and Jay Clayton face the Senate,  a fundamental disconnect becomes clearMon 20 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 12.08 EDTShareThere’s been a lot of discussion in America this week about the meaning of integrity.You didn’t hear the word used directly; it was alluded to indirectly – in wondering whether Lindsey Graham was an immoral opportunist or clever pragmatist; in suspecting Todd Blanche of being more loyal to Trump than to the nation; in distrusting Erica Schwartz, Trump’s latest pick to lead the CDC, when she said she would never compromise on the science; in doubting Jay Clayton, his pick for director of national intelligence, will tell Trump anything he doesn’t want to hear.The word integrity is rooted in the Latin wordinteger, meaning “whole” or “complete” – living by your principles, choosing honesty over opportunism, making ethical choices that may be inconvenient or unpopular, maintaining your moral compass.Graham’s public record of denouncing Trump on the Senate floor after 6 January 2021, and then becoming Trump’s most loyal Senate apologist revealed someone who traded honesty for influence – a person with little or no integrity.Blanche, in his hearing to be attorney general, said he wanted to bring trust back to the justice department but then accidentallyreferred to himselfas Trump’s lawyer – before clarifying that he “was” –had been–Trump’s lawyer. Blanche’s momentary lapse revealed a deeper conflict, as evidenced by his unswerving loyalty to Trump. He lacks integrity.Schwartz, in her confirmation hearing, undercut her pledge to be guided by science in saying she believed Trump and the health secretary, Robert F Kennedy Jr, wouldnot implement policies that hurt public health. That’s enough to cast doubt on her integrity.Clayton, in his confirmation hearing, repeatedly refused to say Biden won the 2020 election, causing Senator Jon Ossoff to ask: “You refuse to answer a basic question about who won a presidential election? But you ask to lead America’s intelligence community? Isn’t it humiliating to be unable to answer this question? To have to indulge the president’s delusions? We know, you know, everybody in this room knows the truthful answer to that question. Why can you not give it?”It is not possible to have integrityandbe loyal to Trump. The two are in direct conflict.No one can be honest in a regime based on big lies. As the poet, philosopher and statesman Václav Havel put it: “If the main pillar of the system is living a lie, then it is not surprising that the fundamental threat to it is living in truth.”So a choice must be made – personal integrityorloyalty to Trump.In his book The Art of the Deal, Trump suggested he preferred loyalty to integrity – as epitomized by his mentor, Roy Cohn.History remembers Cohn for his ruthless bullying, profane braggadocio, opportunistic bigotry, lying and more lying. Like Trump, Cohn was utterly without principle and prioritized personal power that could be leveraged for wealth, influence and celebrity. In 1986,Cohn was disbarred in New Yorkfor unethical conduct after attempting to defraud a dying client by forcing him to sign a will amendment leaving Cohn his fortune. (Cohn died five weeks later.)Yet in his book Trump praised Cohn for his loyalty, contrasting him with “all the hundreds of ‘respectable’ guys who make careers out of boasting about their uncompromising integrity but have absolutely no loyalty … What I liked most about Roy Cohn was that he would do just the opposite.”It is not possible for Blanche or Schwartz or Clayton or anyone else in Trump’s orbit – nor was it possible for Graham – to be loyal to Trump and simultaneously maintain their own integrity. It must be one or the other.Yet integrity is more important than loyalty. As Trump demonstrates time and again, someone lacking integrity is inherently untrustworthy. Such a person cannot serve the people of the United States.Robert Reich, a former US secretary of labor, is a professor of public policy emeritus at the University of California, Berkeley. He is a Guardian US columnist and his newsletter is atrobertreich.substack.com. His new book, Coming Up Short: A Memoir of My America, isout now in the USandin the UKExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionTrump administrationUS politicscommentShareReuse this content

title: House Democrats are finally shifting on Israel. Party leaders must listen | Norman Solomon | The Guardian
description: More than 100 Democratic lawmakers voted against arms for Israel. They’re listening to the people – their bosses are behind
Author: Norman Solomon
Headline: More than 100 Democratic lawmakers voted against arms for Israel. They’re listening to the people – their bosses are behind
Content: ‘Israel apologism, continuing to stream out of the offices of the House and Senate minority leaders, is a wet blanket on a party that should be vibrant.’Photograph: Mark Schiefelbein/APView image in fullscreen‘Israel apologism, continuing to stream out of the offices of the House and Senate minority leaders, is a wet blanket on a party that should be vibrant.’Photograph: Mark Schiefelbein/APOpinionDemocratsHouse Democrats are finally shifting on Israel. Party leaders must listenNorman SolomonMore than 100 Democratic lawmakers voted against arms for Israel. They’re listening to the people – their bosses are behindMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.12 EDTShareThe Democratic party is in dire need of a mutiny.Top leaders at the party’s helm are unprepared to navigate the political sea change about Israel. Last week, nearly half of House Democratsvotedto block $3.3bn in weaponry and other aid to that country. A recent New York Times pollfoundthat 74% of Democratic voters “opposed providing additional economic and military support to Israel, including large majorities in all age groups”.Such polling numbers arenot new. But the House Democratic leader, Hakeem Jeffries, and his Senate counterpart, Chuck Schumer, still follow an old playbook of unwavering service to Israel.
