The moral bankruptcy amounts to political malpractice, as voters tend to bypass a party with leadership they see as lacking a moral compass.The day before 103 House Democrats ignored his guidance, Jeffries sent out a “dear colleague”letterfilled with the kind of pro-Israel rhetoric that has worn transparently thin. The amendment to cut off aid, he claimed, “would restrict our country’s ability to confront Hamas, Hezbollah and other terrorist organizations in the region who are sworn enemies of both the United States and Israel”.But Nancy Pelosi’svotefor the weapons cutoff was a weathervane for how drastically the Democratic winds have swung away from Israel. Consider the former speaker’s remarkablewordsat an Israeli-American Councilconferencein 2018: “I have said to people when they ask me: if this Capitol crumbled to the ground, the one thing that would remain is our commitment to our aid, I don’t even call it aid – ourcooperation– with Israel. That’s fundamental to who we are.”double quotation markDemocratic leadership has failed to engage with the moral anguish felt by many Americans about US complicity in genocideFears of being slandered as antisemitic have routinely made automatic support forIsraelseem safest. Now, while Aipac and other Israel-backing operations with deep pockets remain menacing, the dramatic shift in public attitudes toward Israel has notably reduced their intimidating power.Worries about alienating big donors have been palpable at the Democratic National Committee (DNC), wherefinancial woesare a huge concern. The business model ofcourting megadonorshas a grim logic when the party leadership is so uninspiring to grassroots Democrats.No one can make a credible case that Jeffries and Schumer, or the DNC chair, Ken Martin, for that matter, have been inspirational. A major factor in their inability to inspire has been failure to engage with the genuine moral anguish felt by so many Americans about their country’s complicity in genocide.Last month, a UN commissionconcludedthatgenocide is continuing, with Israeli forces intentionally targeting children in Gaza. “A significant body of research by legal and rights experts has concluded that Israel is intent on destroying Palestinians, including analyses by UN investigators, rights bodies such asAmnesty InternationalandHuman Rights Watch, andgenocide scholarsworldwide,” the Guardianreportedas this summer began.The incapacity of Jeffries, Schumer and Martin to meaningfully address such realities has mired the party deeper in its leadership crisis. That crisis could hamper turnout needed to win the midterm elections this fall, and very likely will come into full view by the time the presidential primary campaign begins in earnest early next year.In the near term, when the DNC convenes in mid-August for its last meeting before the midterms, the gathering is apt to be a scene of subdued discord about the party’s out-of-step leadership and Martin’s continualdeep-sixing of the Israel issuewithin the committee. Its 450 members have maintained a long tradition of complying with the chair’s wishes.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst ThingOur US morning briefing breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThat the DNC’s culture is akin to the atmosphere of a corporation was just underscored by thenewsthat DNC officials are being asked to sign nondisclosure agreements. A co-chair of the DNC’s national finance team described the move as “consistent with standard practice in the corporate world”.Defenders of the party leadership’s pro-Israel stance do not acknowledge the moral imperative of ending US support for what can fairly be called a genocidal state. Butpollingis clear that such denial won’t fly with the Democratic base. The timeworn verbiage being deployed by the party’s House and Senate leaders is functioning within a self-referential closed loop that has lost credibility with voters.Revulsion at Donald Trump might bringDemocratsappreciable success in the midterm elections. But Israel apologism, continuing to stream out of the offices of the House and Senate minority leaders, is a wet blanket on a party that should be vibrant.Looking ahead at the field of likely contenders for the 2028 Democratic presidential nomination, all but a few have merely updated the standard political double-talk about Israel. The lane is now clearer than ever for a candidate to run with a full-throated anti-establishment campaign, dispensing with equivocation and mobilizing the progressive grassroots with positions that include an end to US support for genocide.Norman Solomon is the director of RootsAction and executive director of the Institute for Public Accuracy. His latest book isThe Blue Road to Trump Hell: How Corporate Democrats Paved the Way for AutocracyExplore more on these topicsDemocratsOpinionIsraelUS midterm elections 2026House of RepresentativesUS SenateUS CongressUS politicscommentShareReuse this content

title: With PM Burnham, we’ll get warmth, audacity and knowhow. For everything else, cling to hope | Polly Toynbee | The Guardian
description: He looks the part, with a rise as impressive as his speeches, but is that enough? You cannot know if someone is up to the job until they have to do it, says Guardian columnist Polly Toynbee
Author: Polly Toynbee
Headline: He looks the part, with a rise as impressive as his speeches, but is that enough? You cannot know if someone is up to the job until they have to do it, says Guardian columnist Polly Toynbee
Content: Andy Burnham delivers a speech outside No 10, 20 July.Photograph: Andy Rain/EPAView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham delivers a speech outside No 10, 20 July.Photograph: Andy Rain/EPAOpinionAndy BurnhamWith PM Burnham, we’ll get warmth, audacity and knowhow. For everything else, cling to hopePolly ToynbeeHe looks the part, with a rise as impressive as his speeches, but is that enough? You cannot know if someone is up to the job until they have to do itMon 20 Jul 2026 13.28 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 16.28 EDTShareOnly vaulting ambition takes anyone to No 10, but Andy Burnham’s ambition reaches phenomenal heights. Can he really make the biggest changes in 40 years? Can he build a new economy? Thenew prime ministerlashes himself to the mast of those mighty aspirations, but adds in something even riskier – anend to rough sleeping, ordered immediately. That’s so highly visible everywhere that it will be a test of the extent of his power – will it reach into each dank alley and shop doorway?He understands the immensity of the tasks ahead. “I know what to do,” he said on taking up the leadership. Indeed, he is the most experienced prime minister to enter that door in recent years. He knows the Treasury as its chief secretary, he knows the inside ways of the cabinet,he has run the NHSand GreaterManchester. He knows Whitehall and Westminster, not to their advantage. Beyond that, he knows the disgruntled mood in the everyday world outside, whereincomes stagnate.“I have a plan,” he dares to claim, making cynics and malevolents smirk because everyone knows that wars, plagues, riots, Donald Trump and unknown unknowns upset the best-laid grids in Downing Street spreadsheets. He knows this is the best of times, the crest of the wave, the rising tide of hope. So he must not waste it: on the progressive side of life, they don’t come round often.As a reminder that the time is short and the path hard, Keir Starmer gracefully bore the gruesome farewell ritual. He rightly says he leaves the country “stronger and fairer”. He was ousted not by plotters but by a public still unrelenting in its hostility: only28% agree he did well, against 64% who don’t. It was his triumph to attract enough votes to sweep away the party that brought Brexit destruction, wrecked the NHS, denuded councils, blocked the courts, packed the prisons, made food banks the default social security system and degraded public services.Burnham arrives asthe most liked PM in recent times:his net popularity isfar higher than that enjoyed by any of Keir Starmer, Boris Johnson, Liz Truss or Rishi Sunak when they arrived in office. It wasn’t long before they all tanked, as Burnham well knows. Expect an avalanche of signature policies in these first 100 days. Read the runes of his cabinet choices, after he has instructed each on precisely what he wants of them: John Healey as chancellor will be key to his plan, whatever it is, as will Ed Miliband as foreign secretary, Yvette Cooper at health and Shabana Mahmood, who remains as home secretary.Expect more of the audacity that he showed in standing for election in Makerfield, where Reform had just swept up every council seat. Had he lost, it would all be over for him and probably for Labour. Look at how he dares to disrespect Margaret Thatcher, blaming her for 40 years of neoliberalism, the parlous state of public utilities and sold-off council housing, causing a “concentration of more wealth and powerin the hands of fewer people and fewer places”. Still in awe, Tony Blair and Gordon Brown both made a public exhibition of inviting her for tea and talk soon after entering Downing Street. That spell is broken.Burnham may be a luckier general than Starmer: the FT reports he has an unexpected extra £16bn for capital spending. Economists pile his desk with suggested billions to be gleaned without breaching manifesto promises or frightening bond markets: one of them, Will Hutton, collects them up, declaring “the cupboard is not bare”.He is lucky in his adversaries. Nigel Farage makes an ass of himself fighting Count Binface in Clacton, while angrily ducking questions about vast sums of money, such as never contaminated British politics before. Meanwhile, Kemi Badenoch leads her party ever-further from voters, misled by her rowdy media ideologues: the net zero climate target is backed bytwice as many as oppose it, yet she thinks abandoning it is a winning ticket,banning any pro-net zero parliamentary candidates, even in the middle of a heatwave. Burnham will hold steady on manifesto pledges on this. As the public regrets Brexit, wanting closer European ties, Badenoch perversely plans to eject any “wets” who back staying in the European convention on human rights. Until her generation of Tories has departed or begged forgiveness for the Brexit disaster, her party looks destined for the wilderness.She has reason to worry. What Badenoch dubs “airy fairy” about Burnham is a warmth and likeability she lacks. It can’t be faked. “Relatable” is a word often used about him – others are “connecting”, “clicking”, “listening” and “understanding”. He is lavish with language and mood music. I have met him over many years and he has always been a man of ideas, who has grown in stature and self-confidence with each promotion. He is good at acknowledging how far he has come without humble-bragging or inverted snobbery.Writing my memoir about class, I asked everyone I met to tell me about a moment when their class embarrassed them, whether they had felt too posh or not posh enough. I liked his answer: at Cambridge a girl invited this working-class boy for dinner, but he turned up for lunch instead of in the evening. That’s the kind of experience that at first gave him impostor syndrome, but he seems at ease now anywhere: proud of home, place and background, he climbed the ladder without tearing up his roots. That was Keir Starmer’s honest story too, yet however often he talked of his tool-maker father, his nurse mother, his care worker sister, their pebble-dash home, some lack in warm communication stopped people seeing him that way. The PM who went to Leeds University was seen as a “sir” in a suit, while the Cambridge-educated PM Andy is Mr Relatable in a T-shirt.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe qualities that make a successful prime minister are mercurial; no manual exists. You never know until too late how firm is their backbone, how steely their resolution. Will they buckle under adversity, or be blown off-course by gales of contradictory advice – or will they know when to bend if necessary? I thought Starmer and Brown would have them: both clever, decent, serious men, but not leaders, it turned out. The public warmed to neither. No doubt the peculiar people who dare aspire to this most monstrous job never know themselves if they really have it. Do they all feel like impostors?Listening to his words in Downing Street, we hear a good speech-maker, with a good political record and a good character who brings goodwill with him. The rest is hope.Polly Toynbee is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionKeir StarmercommentShareReuse this content

title: What’s left after this World Cup? A kind of messy hope | Jonathan Liew | The Guardian
description: Kazakhs cheering Uzbeks, Bengalis wild for Argentina … This tournament was once a playground for nationalism. Now it shows how flimsy that allegiance is, says Guardian columnist Jonathan Liew
Author: Jonathan Liew
Headline: Kazakhs cheering Uzbeks, Bengalis wild for Argentina … This tournament was once a playground for nationalism. Now it shows how flimsy that allegiance is, says Guardian columnist Jonathan Liew
Content: A boy in an Argentina jersey looks at a mural in Dhaka, Bangladesh, 27 June 2026.Photograph: Md Rafayat Haque Khan/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenA boy in an Argentina jersey looks at a mural in Dhaka, Bangladesh, 27 June 2026.Photograph: Md Rafayat Haque Khan/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockOpinionWorld Cup 2026What’s left after this World Cup? A kind of messy hopeJonathan LiewKazakhs cheering Uzbeks, Bengalis wild for Argentina … This tournament was once a playground for nationalism. Now it shows how flimsy that allegiance isMon 20 Jul 2026 11.20 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 14.44 EDTShareIt’s theWorld Cup final, so you pick a side. And don’t worry if you happen to be part of the 98.9% of the human race that is neither Spanish nor Argentinian – just make something up. Maybe you like the Spain kit. Maybe you’ve always been an admirer of Lionel Messi. Maybe you’re still raw about the Falklands or Diego Maradona, maybe you disagree with the Pedro Sánchez government’s stance on Palestine/Israel, maybe you once ate a dodgy empanada on your gap year. Nobody needs to see your working. Only two rules really apply: be partisan and be quick about it.The MetLife Stadium on Sunday night was a melting pot of colliding incongruities and temporary identities. The former heavyweight boxing champion Mike Tyson waswearing an Argentina shirt. The comedian Trevor Noahwore a Spain shirt. Indeed,ticket sales datasuggests that an overwhelming proportion of the crowd was American. The most consequential fixture in international sport was, as it turned out, little more than a grand cosplay for the New Jersey leisure market.And beyond the final, perhaps this has been one of the hidden stories of the World Cup: the gradual reimagining of national allegiance as a kind of single-use consumer brand. For six weeks, the entire nation ofBangladesh went crazy for Argentina. A group of central Asian friends in north London put aside their internecine rivalries to cheer for Uzbekistan. The Arab diaspora swung firmly behind Morocco and Egypt as the tournament progressed. Leftwing progressives who revile the cross of St George when painted on a mini-roundabout were suddenly daubing it on their cheeks.All of which upends what we have been told for decades about theWorld Cup, and international sport in general. A tournament that should in theory be the ultimate playground for nationalism has in fact demonstrated the very opposite: the increasing fragility of nationhood as an idea. What the social psychologist Michael Billig termed banal nationalism – the subliminal everyday symbols of nationhood – has now become so banal as to be in essence disposable. If national identity is now something that can be slipped on and off as easily as a football shirt, then what use is it, really?We live in an age where the boundaries of nationhood are both ever-more piercingly evoked and ever-more hazily defined. Again, football illustrates this confusion better than almost any other human endeavour. Across western Europe, far-right voices preach the doctrine of nationalism while reviling their own national teams for having too many black players. In Colombia and Brazil, many leftwing fans have chosen to spurn the national team shirt as a result of itsappropriation by rightwing populists.This is before we even address the teams themselves: in many cases not so much cogent expressions of national identity or even recognisable products of a system as they are snapshots of a sprawling diaspora at a particular moment in time, splintered on multiple planes by globalisation and migration, assembled out of convenience and expediency. Forty of the 48 competing nationscontained a player born overseas. Only one member of the Curaçao squad was actually born on the Caribbean island. The Cape Verde squad that held the eventual champions, Spain, to a heroic 0-0 draw in the group phase featuredmore players born in Rotterdamthan in the country’s capital.And so the modern World Cup serves two abrupt functions. First, it forces us to confront the idea that most of us have multiple identities cutting across or even against national boundaries, that everywhere is the product of everywhere. Second, it interrogates the criteria by which we pledge allegiance to a country, a process that most of the time lies latent and unspoken, only bursting to the surface in the form of an urgent binary question: “Who do I support for this game?” The historian Eric Hobsbawm’s famous quote about football gestating an “imagined community of millions” takes on a subtly different resonance when you realise that very often this is a community with no defined borders or polity, a community that for 90 minutes draws its number from every corner of the planet.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionHere’s the lesson to learn from England’s World Cup joy: shared purpose is key, not shared ancestry | Maya TudorRead moreWeirdly, this is a phenomenon that has become neither exclusively left- nor right-coded. If nationalism was long regarded as the preserve of the populist right and internationalism as the preserve of the intellectual left, these days the picture is far more nuanced. Today’s rightwing agitators are as likely to pledge allegiance to Donald Trump or wave an Israeli flag as they are to champion the country they’re actually from. The Trump administration has consistently demonstrated greater solidarity withwhite South Africansthan with Black Americans. Across the English-speaking world, conservative outlets freely make alliances across national borders and appeal to abstract concepts such as “Judeo-Christian values” or “western civilisation” while denouncing and dehumanising many of their fellow citizens as traitors and scum.Football’s role as a nation builder is often overstated. France’s World Cup triumph in 1998 was hailed as a vindication of the country’s multiethnic identity, but four years later it sent Jean-Marie Le Pen into the final round of the presidential election. Argentina’s 1978 victory on home soil generated a surge of popularity for the country’s military dictatorship, but by 1983 it had collapsed. England’s run to the European Championship final in 2021 did not, for all the good vibes and open letters, arrest our wider descent into gutter politics and culture war.But occasionally it can hold up a mirror. And perhaps what this World Cup reflects back at us is a kind of messy hope: that a world of hardening physical borders is doomed to disintegrate on contact with reality. That for all the flags and pageantry, nationality is ultimately little more than a brand, a consumer choice, a piece of content – a mask anyone can happily wear for six weeks, and shed just as easily.Jonathan Liew is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026OpinionWorld CupcommentShareReuse this content

title: Australia has had housing crises before: what can we learn from them? | Anne O’Brien | The Guardian
description: When governments make access to housing more equitable, it’s because they recognise its social and economic value
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: When governments make access to housing more equitable, it’s because they recognise its social and economic value
Content: Australian governments’ track record of making housing more equitable has been hard-won, writes Anne O’Brien.Composite: Guardian DesignView image in fullscreenAustralian governments’ track record of making housing more equitable has been hard-won, writes Anne O’Brien.Composite: Guardian DesignPast/PresentHousingAustralia has had housing crises before: what can we learn from them?Anne O’BrienWhen governments make access to housing more equitable, it’s because they recognise its social and economic valueMon 20 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.13 EDTShareThe federal government’s first major tax reform in nearly 30 years was a significant achievement that suggests we may haveturned a corner on the housing crisis.It is a good time to revisit Australia’s housing history within the broader history of social policy.This is a story of public investment for economic growth as well as for the needs of citizens. The railways, telegraphs, hospitals, schools and welfare that gave Australia a reputation for a“pragmatic”rather than “doctrinaire” approach to national development are well known. But governments also invested in housing despite some powerful resistance by interest groups.Capital gains tax changes are already having an impact on wealth inequality – and vested interests are running scared | Greg JerichoRead moreThis public investment in housing shows how democratic processes worked in this difficult policy area. Most was initiated by Labor governments, some by conservatives, but it all recognised the social and economic value of decent housing, and the difficulties of low-paid workers accessing it via the market. When the eminent architect John Sulman declared in 1914 that“the unsolved problem”in Australia was providing housing at a price that “the living wage workers can pay”, he articulated a “big truth” that remains largely unchanged.Some public investments were easier to pull off than others. Australia’s first public housing in Sydney’s Rocks district supported the needs of commerce and shipping when it was constructed in the early 1900s. Part of the redevelopment of the wharves following an outbreak of the dreaded bubonic plague, The Rocks project gave licence to the conservative state government to fund more than 100 new dwellings because wharf labourers needed to live nearby for round-the-clock work.But most builds were more contentious, pressured by citizen protest and supported by advocates of the garden suburb who saw inner-city housing as “slums” and a source of social decay. Daceyville, in Sydney’s south-east, was a prime example. Established after the election in 1910 of New South Wales’s first Labor government, it was precipitated by the demolition of swathes of housing for city improvement. By 1912 more than5,700 people were displaced. Residents, city missionaries and the popular press mounted petitions and led deputations to register their dissent at what journalists were callinginhumanevictions.Daceyville was not the only outcome of this protest. The Strickland Building in Chippendale was the first of four blocks of inner-city flats erected by the Sydney city council between 1914 and 1927. With resident demand for housing near work trumping anti-slum orthodoxy, they are evidence of how contingent outcomes in this high-stakes context could be.The largest and best-remembered investment was in the 1940s, the product of an extreme housing shortage and fear of social unrest. Thousands of families were living in boarding houses and various forms of temporary shelter after the Depression. The Commonwealth Housing Commission promised a “new social order”, which also sought to offset the attractions of communism. It broke new ground in declaring housing to be“a right”that should “cease to be a field of investment yielding high profits”. Between 1945 and 1956, more than 96,000 new dwellings were funded through the commonwealth-state housing agreement.None of these public investments were sufficient but they eased rental pressure on working-class families to a greater extent than any intervention before or since and, leaving behind the orthodoxy of the “economic rent”, it enabled families in greatest need to access it. This investment also underpinned the leap in Australia’s home ownership rates from53% in 1947 to 73% in 1966.‘I would just like to see more people housed’: inside NSW’s social housing changesRead moreA downside of this pivot was that the stock that was sold was not replaced. The Menzies government saw home ownership as an alternative to public rental rather than a complement, so support wound down. Between 1958 and 1973 the proportion of new housing that was public fell from17.8% to 7.7%.The waiting lists have been growing ever since.In 2007, the Rudd government addressed once again the disparity between low incomes and market rents through a new housing policy. Despite its limits, it enabled thousands of people who would otherwise have remained homeless to access secure housing. This investment, part of the global financial crisis stimulus package, demonstrated how such policy can work to normalise fiscal intervention. It was redeployed early in the Covid pandemic, preventing widespread unemployment, and underpins the government’s recent tax reforms.Australian governments’ past investments in housing were hard-won. Despite their limitations, they made access to housing more equitable because they recognised its social and economic value. Understanding their complexity and purpose helps expand and strengthen the options we now see as politically feasible.Anne O’Brien is emeritus professor of history at the University of New South Wales. She is a member of the Australian Historical AssociationExplore more on these topicsHousingPast/PresentcommentShareReuse this content

title: Vamos España? Why this World Cup victory for Spain feels so different from the others  | María Ramírez | The Guardian
description: The success belongs to many more people around the world from Gaza to Latin America – even if the collegiate spirit of the team has yet to be mirrored in our public life, says journalist María Ramírez
Author: María Ramírez
Headline: The success belongs to many more people around the world from Gaza to Latin America – even if the collegiate spirit of the team has yet to be mirrored in our public life, says journalist María Ramírez
Content: Fans gather in the streets of Madrid to celebrate Spain’s World Cup triumph.Photograph: Luis Soto/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenFans gather in the streets of Madrid to celebrate Spain’s World Cup triumph.Photograph: Luis Soto/ZUMA Press Wire/ShutterstockOpinionSpainVamos España? Why this World Cup victory for Spain feels so different from the othersMaría RamírezThe success belongs to many more people around the world from Gaza to Latin America – even if the collegiate spirit of the team has yet to be mirrored in our public lifeMon 20 Jul 2026 08.52 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 10.24 EDTShareJust past midnight, the cheers erupted from blocks of lit flats in my neighbourhood in central Madrid. Hours later, people of all generations were still out in the streets in the middle of another tropical night, and not only because it was warm, with temperatures hovering close to 30C. In the big pedestrianised square Plaza de Olavide, fans had gathered around screens, some sitting on the ground while children and dogs ran around. A girl played on the swings, her Spanish-flag cape fluttering as she swung back and forth.Argentinian Lionel Messi jerseys were visible throughout the crowd. Many fans gathered also at a nearby Argentinian bar. Not too surprising when you consider there aremore than 400,000 Argentiniansliving in Spain. Messi, whose life and career are embedded with Barcelona, is a football hero in Spain too.It was the same across the city and throughout the country. Barcelona had one of the largest public gatherings, with more than 30,000 people watching on a giant screen installedby the city council. Catalonia is the home region of stars including the 19-year-old Lamine Yamal, the national side’s most promising player. There is asmuch regional varietyin Spain’s team as there is in the country. Several come from the Basque Country, once only a reluctant supporter of the national team. One player, Aymeric Laporte, was born in France. Backgrounds are as diverse as the population.It was hard not to agree with Ferran Torres, who scored the extra-time goal that brought us victory, when he said his achievement belonged to “47 million people,not just me or my teammates”. But it felt as though the success belonged to many more people around the world, from Gaza, where Spain has come to represent solidarity with the Palestinian cause,to Brazil.The celebratory mood was just as palpable three years ago when we gathered to watch the women’s national team win the World Cup. But the joy was overshadowed bysexual harassmentand misogyny after the Jenni Hermoso case.View image in fullscreenDisplaced Palestinians watch the World Cup final in Gaza City, 19 July 2026.Photograph: Belal Osama/APAImages/ShutterstockSixteen years ago I watched Spain’s firstWorld Cupvictory among late-night crowds in Brussels, where I then lived. That night felt joyful, but those were painful times. I was covering the eurozone crisis that was already hitting Spain harder than almost anywhere else, as a burst property bubble and soaring unemployment left many people with a sense of hopelessness.This win feels somehow more rounded. The country itself is in a better place despite the deep political polarisation and widespread mood of dissatisfaction that often dominates public debate. Spain’s economy has become one of the strongest performers in the developed world and its cities feel more prosperous, confident and dynamic than many inEurope.The national team is young, diverse and relentlessly professional. The cheers in Lavapiés, one of Madrid’s most diverse neighbourhoods, felt momentous in a country wheremore than 10 million people, about 20% of the population, were born abroad.Spain’s players also offered an example of maturity under pressure. The clarity with whichYamal respondedto former prime minister Mariano Rajoy’s racist comments about the French national team stood out in contrast to the crudeness of a supposedly experienced politician.The contrast between football’s power to promote integration, which this team seems to fully understand, with the constant partisan battles dominating public life inSpaincouldn’t be sharper.The message repeated most often by the coach, Luis de la Fuente, and his players is simple and powerful: “We are a team. We embrace diversity while working together towards a common goal.” Spain’s greatest strength on the pitch lies not in individual brilliance but in a cohesiveness that becomes greater than the sum of its parts. Ironically, that sense of shared purpose and collective responsibility is perhaps what Spanish public life is missing the most.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionYet even if the impact of football seems fleeting and insignificant, especially in these challenging times, its extraordinary appeal can inspire a shared spirit. At its best, it fosters solidarity and camaraderie. At its worst, it can fuel violence and disrespect, as Argentina’s team sadly showed before, during and after the match.Everyone sees what they want to see in the Spanish national team, be it nationalism, diversity or simply great football. Of course, not every message is inclusive, but the variety of fans on the street, regardless of age, origin or gender, is a reminder of the immense power of football to bring together very different people.I keep thinking these days about the motto of the country that hosted the World Cup final:E pluribus unum– out of many, one. Those words reflected the US founding fathers’ thinking. They believed the Declaration of Independence’s ideals would unite people from very different backgrounds without needing centuries of shared religions, cultures and wars, as in Europe.Unity in diversity remains a painfully unfulfilled ideal in the US and in much of the world – also in Spain. But at least for a night, amid the chants, klaxons and fireworks, one could hope that somehow it is still possible.María Ramírez is a journalist and deputy managing editor of elDiario.es, a news outlet in SpainExplore more on these topicsSpainOpinionWorld Cup 2026World CupEuropecommentShareReuse this content

title: A company in the UK offering rape testing kits faces widespread criticism – but shouldn’t we ask why it exists at all?  | Zoe Williams | The Guardian
description: Sexual violence is hugely under-reported and cases rarely make it to court. These are the facts that should shock us, says Guardian columnist Zoe Williams
Author: Zoe Williams
Headline: Sexual violence is hugely under-reported and cases rarely make it to court. These are the facts that should shock us, says Guardian columnist Zoe Williams
Content: A poster outside James Allen’s girls’ school, in response to reports of rape culture being prevalent at nearby Dulwich College, London, 23 March 2021.Photograph: David Levene/The GuardianView image in fullscreenA poster outside James Allen’s girls’ school, in response to reports of rape culture being prevalent at nearby Dulwich College, London, 23 March 2021.Photograph: David Levene/The GuardianOpinionRape and sexual assaultA company in the UK offering rape testing kits faces widespread criticism – but shouldn’t we ask why it exists at all?Zoe WilliamsSexual violence is hugely under-reported and cases rarely make it to court. These are the facts that should shock usMon 20 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 04.12 EDTShareTwo years ago, Katie White set up Enough, which distributes self-swabbing rape kits. These are DIY kits that people who believe they have been raped or sexually assaulted can use at home by collecting DNA and posting it to a private laboratory for analysis.White was responding to a few stark facts about sexual violence in England and Wales: of more than 70,000 rapes reported to police in 2021-22, only2,223 resulted in charges. The number of reports was only a fraction of rapes anyway: 83% of survivors choose not to go to the police.Sixteen- to 35-year-olds are overwhelmingly the targets and crime survey data found a higher proportion offull-time studentswere victims of sexual assault in the past year than any other occupation type. From all this, White inferred that the number of student rapists being brought to justice was only the barest fraction of the total.White says that the kits provide women with an option after a rape to gather evidence at home, before they decide what to do and whether to visit a sexual assault referral centre or contact the police. She tells me that the kits were positively received by students when they were handed out at campuses. The institutional response has been critical.Victim Support and a number of rape charities have reacted stronglyagainst self-swabbingbecause people may wrongly think of it as an alternative to going to a sexual assault referral centre (SARC) and accessing the ancillary support these services offer, such as counselling. The National Police Chiefs Council said the kits may “reduce trust in statutory services” and both warn that evidence collected outside regulated environments may not be admissible in court.All of this is entirely fair: survivors deserve the best support available, and the best evidence gathering, should they decide to press charges. But what none of the criticisms address is the fact that rape survivors are avoiding those channels for legitimate reasons – they see a gruelling process that dominates the victim’s life as much if not more than the perpetrator’s, with a minuscule chance of conviction. If trust is low, it’s most likely because six years ago the then victims’ commissioner for England and Wales, Vera Baird, said that rape had beeneffectively decriminalised. A startup trying to find a workaround is a signifier of the problem, not a problem in itself.Enough’s online materials werereportedto the Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) by Martin Narey, former head of Prisons and Probation in England and Wales. One complaint was about the statement “430,000 rapes happen a year”, which Narey said was “frightening young women and terrifying their parents by exaggerating the likelihood of being raped”. The latest figures from the Office for National Statistics have the number of reported rapes standing at71,000, but these are the ones of which we know. The ASA found against Enough, questioning its methodology for estimating the number of total rapes, including those not reported.It will never be possible to give exact numbers while such a small proportion of rapes are reported. TheONS’s own estimatesare of about 900,000 sexual assaults and attempts of sexual assault of women (including rape) in the year to March 2025 (739,000 females and 162,000 males). It is irrational to accuse Enough of scaremongering when the government’s statistics are this terrifyingly high.The inefficacies of criminal justice have gradually brought us to a place where the functionality of law feels more like a historical artefact than something that protects us. Maybe White’s guerrilla move is a concrete example of dwindling faith in the state’s ability to enact justice – and maybe that’s why everyone is so upset about it.The only way to maintain this culture, which we call “zero tolerance”, and yet tolerate nearly a million actual and attempted sexual assaults a year, is with the fantasy that sexual violence isn’t real. This is why women don’t report it, and don’t access formal services.The promise is to believe the victim, but plenty still prefer tobelieve the rapist: a fifth of men and a quarter of Conservative voters think that women routinely fabricate rape allegations. The classic feminist account is that women are simply seen as less credible than men, but it is time to revisit that, because a significant minority of victims aremen, and they are believed even less. Successivestudiesshowrape myths– foundationally, if you really didn’t want to have sex, you would have fought it off – to be more tenacious where the victim is male.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDavid Lammy’s proposed cohabitation law would be bad for couples – and worse for some women | Ruth DeechRead moreIt’s simply more psychologically comfortable to think a mistake has been made, or someone is lying, than to think you live in a world where some people wilfully violate the physical autonomy of others. Where the evidence is irrefutable, we will do anything to distance those rapists from our own environs, asmemorably describedby the philosopher Manon Garcia, who sat through the Gisèle Pelicot rape trial: “People from Paris would say: ‘This is men from the south’; intellectuals would say: ‘These don’t look like guys who read many books’; her German and American colleagues would say: ‘It’s really a problem with French culture.’” (The UK’s National Crime Agency has since discovered a “truly international” network of drug-facilitated rapists.) Rape is so disturbing that the overwhelming preference is to find some other explanation. Surely there was a misunderstanding. Let’s not think anything hasty.When I was young, we were still arguing about who to educate: women (don’t get drunk, don’t walk alone at night), or men (stop raping people). Now, we often argue in a coded way about who to believe, her or him. The more useful work is to start believing that rape itself is real.Zoe Williams is a Guardian columnistInformation and support for anyone affected by rape or sexual abuse issues is available from the following organisations. In the UK,Rape Crisisoffers support on 0808 500 2222 in England and Wales, 0808 801 0302 inScotland, or 0800 0246 991 inNorthern Ireland. In the US,Rainnoffers support on 800-656-4673. In Australia, support is available at1800Respect(1800 737 732). Other international helplines can be found atibiblio.org/rcip/internl.htmlDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsRape and sexual assaultOpinionCrimeWomencommentShareReuse this content

title: British YouTuber Yung Filly appears in Australian court over rape allegations  | Western Australia | The Guardian
description: Rapper pleads not guilty to six counts of sexually penetrating a 20-year-old woman without consent after a gig in Perth
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Rapper pleads not guilty to six counts of sexually penetrating a 20-year-old woman without consent after a gig in Perth
Content: Andres Felipe Valencia Barrientos, the British YouTuber and rapper known as Yung Filly, seen leaving the Perth magistrates court in 2024.Photograph: Richard Wainwright/AAPView image in fullscreenAndres Felipe Valencia Barrientos, the British YouTuber and rapper known as Yung Filly, seen leaving the Perth magistrates court in 2024.Photograph: Richard Wainwright/AAPWestern AustraliaBritish YouTuber Yung Filly appears in Australian court over rape allegationsRapper pleads not guilty to six counts of sexually penetrating a 20-year-old woman without consent after a gig in PerthAustralian Associated PressMon 20 Jul 2026 02.46 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 03.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA British YouTuber and musician bit and slapped a young female fan while he was raping her after a gig inWestern Australia, a court has been told.Yung Filly, 30, has pleaded not guilty to six counts of sexually penetrating a 20-year-old woman without consent in a Perth hotel room in September 2024.The rapper, whose real name is Andres Felipe Valencia Barrientos, also denies impeding the woman’s breathing by applying pressure to her neck and three counts of assault occasioning bodily harm.Barrientos and the woman initially engaged in consensual sex in the performer’s room at the Intercontinental Hotel, prosecutor Danielle Clarke told the district court of Western Australia on Monday.But Yung Filly became violent and started biting and slapping the woman before raping her multiple times, she said during her opening remarks.“What should have been an exciting night out meeting her celebrity crush Yung Filly was fun until it wasn’t,” she said.The woman allegedly suffered multiple injuries during the assault, including extensive bruising to her face and neck and bite wounds on her right breast.The court heard the woman was a fan of Yung Filly and had gone to the gig at a bar in Perth’s northern suburbs.After Yung Filly’s set, the woman was invited into a VIP area where she met the musician.He told her “you’re coming home with me tonight” before pushing his hand down the back of her pants, Clarke said.The pair left the venue about 3am with the rapper’s all-male entourage in tow.The woman believed she was being taken to an after-party, Clarke said.Instead, they went to Yung Filly’s hotel, where one of his minders took her phone and the performer led her to room 217.During sex, Barrientos allegedly bit the woman’s neck and breast numerous times.“The bites hurt and [the woman] said ouch and asked the accused to be gentle,” Clarke said.Barrientos allegedly told her to stop talking and bit her harder before slapping her.During the incident, Barrientos told her to say “I’m Filly’s dirty whore” but she refused, Clarke said.Prosecutors say it was at this point that the woman started to fear for her own safety and consent to sex was withdrawn.“She didn’t know how to get herself out of the situation and decided to do what the accused wanted,” Clarke said.Barrientos allegedly then raped the woman multiple times before ordering himself a Wagyu burger via room service.As the woman was getting ready to leave the hotel room, one of Barrientos’s minders came into the room and she heard the performer tell him that she was not “good in bed”, the court was told.Barrientos allegedly grabbed her breasts as she was leaving and said to the minder: “How good are her boobs?”The trial continues.Information and support for anyone affected by rape or sexual abuse is available from the following organisations. In Australia, support is available atFull Stop Australia(1800 385 578). In the UK,Rape Crisisoffers support on 0808 500 2222. In the US,Rainnoffers support on 800-656-4673. Other international helplines can be found atibiblio.org/rcip/internl.htmlExplore more on these topicsWestern AustraliaAustralian lawnewsShareReuse this content

title: Why has Britain’s oldest political magazine become such a rabid cheerleader for Islamophobia? | Nesrine Malik | The Guardian
description: A report on the Spectator’s coverage of Muslims makes for grim reading. Conservatism is now focused on this minority to the exclusion of everything else, says Guardian columnist Nesrine Malik
Author: Nesrine Malik
Headline: A report on the Spectator’s coverage of Muslims makes for grim reading. Conservatism is now focused on this minority to the exclusion of everything else, says Guardian columnist Nesrine Malik
Content: Illustration: Nate Kitch/The GuardianView image in fullscreenIllustration: Nate Kitch/The GuardianOpinionIslamophobiaWhy has Britain’s oldest political magazine become such a rabid cheerleader for Islamophobia?Nesrine MalikA report on the Spectator’s coverage of Muslims makes for grim reading. Conservatism is now focused on this minority to the exclusion of everything elseMon 20 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 03.16 EDTShareIstart this column already resigned to the tune out, to the glaze over, the click away. This is not to say that I do not have faith in you, dear reader, to keep reading and caring. But I am aware, and this is central to the problem, that it is now normal, when it comes to a certain minority in this country, to speak of them as if they were some sort of plague.Islamophobiahas become so common as to be mundane in large parts of the British media. Foremost among the offenders is Britain’s oldest political magazine: the Spectator.Alarge and comprehensive reportfrom the Centre for Media Monitoring reviewed 3,733 articles published in the Spectator relating to Islam or Muslims. Released last week, it criticised the publication for “a sustained and systemic pattern of biased coverage of Muslims and Islam”, according to the CMM, “with more than half of all relevant articles rated as biased over an eight-year period”. It is grim, grim reading.Among the conclusions reached by the report are that articles in the magazine regularly portrayed Muslims as a “problem population”, delegitimised the very notion of Islamophobia, and consistently presented Muslims as exceptional or threatening. The overall effect is of a minority that is not only sectarian and separate, but also, ironically, from within its ghetto, dangerous and aggressively colonising British society.Articles cited refer to Muslim concerns about racist abuse as “dainty sensibilities” and “handwringing”. Presumably, this precious response includes objections to statements in the magazine panicking that “organised Islam is strong and vociferous” and, in reference to the growing population of Muslims in the country, that “there are now more than double the number of semi-literate, virgin-obsessed potential murderers among [sic] our midst”.Even the mere existence of Muslims in the UK is presented as something akin to a poisonous foreign presence. “Prominent historians … have noted entirely correctly that Islam seems to have things about it which make it uniquely indigestible to the modern secularised state,”wrote one columnist.“We should have second thoughts about approving mosques,” wrote another contributor.The coverage depicts a country that is going to the dogs. Politicians quake in the face of Muslims, the left aids and abets them, the courts indulge them. The scale of the onslaught is staggering and relentless. It would be unacceptable to speak of another minority group in such terms, but Muslims occupy a sweet spot that makes them convenient subjects for the forces of reaction. They are racialised, they are immigrants or the descendants of immigrants, some of them dare to practise their own religion. Their unique profile makes it easy to channel racism, anti-immigration sentiment and just plain old petty parochialism. Islamophobia itself has metastasised, becoming a vehicle for the sort of things you can’t say out loud about people of colour in general, but which you can say about Muslims.In the foreword of the report, the writer and broadcaster Peter Oborne – the magazine’s former political editor – traces the history of the Spectator, and how it went from condemning the anti-immigration Enoch Powell to “steadily rebuilding his reputation”. Oborne cites a piece from earlier this year that says Powell “had a view of the British polity, values and way of life with which many Britons to this day feel they identify, and that is not least what establishes him as one of the country’s greatest conservative influences”. Now, the publication platforms broader far-right politics, in one articlepraising Tommy Robinsonas likable, “intelligent, quick, articulate, well-informed, good-mannered”.And yet despite such extremism, the Spectator snugly occupies a position of such respectability that its summer party is attended by Labour and Tory politicians and journalists from across the British media, who rub shoulders there with Nigel Farage and the broader Spectator-verse. “The jamboree remains the most sought-after social ticket of the Westminster summer season,”boasted the Spectatorin July after this year’s edition. A refutation of the charge that there is a wholesale coddling of Muslims if there ever was one. There is something crudely and unmistakably symbolic in the magazine’s influence. As the country is riven by far-right politics and protests, as mosques are attacked and hate crimesreach new levels, our elected politicians and opinion-makers are comfortable to drink and make merry among those who mainstream and disseminate inflammatory rhetoric.But something else struck me while reading example after example of quoted texts. It was all tawdry, poorly written, poorly argued stuff. You come away not with the sense that this is an august magazine dedicating its formidable intellectual and journalistic resources towards a problematic minority it is concerned about, but with the sense that it isn’t a serious publication.Its articles are the sort of content you get from GB News, or a bellicose radio phone-in, or a tabloid on a tear. A convergence has occurred among the rightwing media, a radicalisation that has alighted onanger and scaremongering, which feeds readers’ and viewers’ fears and paranoia. It is a successful commercial strategy, but there is no community here, no solace or even loving depiction of the precious country vandalised. There is little to bind other than hate and outrage, no hold on what it is we are ostensibly saving from Muslims or immigrants or the left. Tuning in, what one hears in concert is: “You have once again woken up in Britain. Welcome to hell.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionVandalism, taunts and hijabs torn off: Muslim leaders in UK say hate crime hitting new levelsRead moreThe result is a climate that is hostile and dangerous not just to Muslims but all who it has been decided are stealthily annexing the country. But there is also a wider degradation that comes with the relentless focus on Muslims.The project of developing a vision of conservatism that goes beyond purification has been abandoned. Interested in, say, how the contemporary movement can reconcile austerity and free-market policies with stagnant wages and productivity and declining public services? In the ideological fissures in modern conservatism and its splintering? Perhaps a rant about Muslim public prayer as an act of “public domination” can shed some light on these crucial questions.The Spectator and its broader universe constitute what is now mostly a low-brow media attempting to dress up racist audience-baiting as high-end journalism, all of which is met not with alarm and objection by establishment politics, but a “cheers” in the sunshine. It is not that Muslims have turned into a bigger problem for the country, but that the country itself, and its so-called intellectual elites, have become small. In resisting the feeling that this is all just how it is, in not glazing over, we resist becoming small along with them.Nesrine Malik is a Guardian columnistDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsIslamophobiaOpinionThe SpectatorIslamReligionMagazinesNewspapers & magazinescommentShareReuse this content

title: Europe is at risk of creating a human rights black hole with its ‘return hubs’ for migrants | Michael O'Flaherty | The Guardian
description: Without iron-clad legal safeguards, offshore migration centres can only repeat the worst mistakes of the recent past, says the Council of Europe’s human rights commissioner
Author: Michael O'Flaherty
Headline: Without iron-clad legal safeguards, offshore migration centres can only repeat the worst mistakes of the recent past, says the Council of Europe’s human rights commissioner
Content: Migrants arrive at the Albanian port of Shengjin from Italy under an agreement that rights groups say is a violation of EU lawPhotograph: Adnan Beci/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenMigrants arrive at the Albanian port of Shengjin from Italy under an agreement that rights groups say is a violation of EU lawPhotograph: Adnan Beci/AFP/Getty ImagesOpinionMigrationEurope is at risk of creating a human rights black hole with its ‘return hubs’ for migrantsMichael O'FlahertyWithout iron-clad legal safeguards, offshore migration centres can only repeat the worst mistakes of the recent pastMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 03.29 EDTShareEurope has entered a new era of outsourced migration management. After a vote by the European parliament, the EU’s return regulation is nearing adoption – promising“more efficient” returnsand “swifter” procedures to deport unwanted migrants. This will give countries the legal runway to set up return hubs – that is, offshore facilities to process people deemed to have no right to stay in the EU.Despite opposition from countries such as Spain and France – and an outcry fromrights groups– many other governments appear anxious to join in. Anopen lettersigned by 19 EU member states just days after the vote at the European parliament, urging the “full use of the new possibilities”, indicates that implementation might be coming soon. Frontrunners like Austria, Denmark, Germany, Greece and the Netherlands are already on the starting block, poised to establish joint hubs which are likely to become operational in short order.These developments signal that positions once dismissed as fringe, legally untenable and driven by ideology rather than by evidence have been successfully rebranded as mainstream, commonsense governance. The deep politicisation of this shift was on full display when some MEPs chanted “Send them back” inside the European parliament during the vote, celebrating the removal of human beings with the fervour of a campaign rally. In the pursuit of ever stricter migration policies, the human rights protections that Europe was founded to uphold are increasingly treated as disposable.The decision to pave the way for extraterritorial centres should alarm anyone familiar with the human cost of these schemes in the past. History shows us that they are not harmless administrative workarounds. There isn’t a single initiative to outsource migration policy that has not raised serious human rights concerns.When Australia pioneered offshore processingon NauruandPapua New Guinea, thousands of people, including children, were –according to the UN– subjected to years of arbitrary detention in inhumane conditions, resulting in severe physical and mental trauma and at least 12 deaths. Similarly, human rights organisations called outIsrael’s attempts in the 2010sto press Sudanese and Eritrean asylum seekers into “voluntary” relocation to Rwanda and Uganda, which resulted in abuse, legal limbo or forced onward flight.View image in fullscreenPope Leo at Mimmo Paladino’s Gateway to Europe memorial on Lampedusa, one of the main entry points for migrants crossing the Mediterranean. The pope urged a more humane migration policy during his 4 July 2026 visitPhotograph: Tiziana Fabi/AFP/Getty ImagesIn the US, the 1990s experiments of holding Haitian refugees outside domestic judicial reach –including in Guantánamo Bay– have evolved into agreements to remove people to countries where they have no links. These agreements have led to documented reports of arbitrary detention, torture and other ill treatment, enforced disappearances and denial of access to family and legal counsel. In some cases, receiving states subsequently returned individuals to their country of origin, exposing people to arisk of refoulement(that is, sending someone back to danger).The recent highly publicised collaboration between US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and El Salvador’s Cecot mega jail – where deportees wereheld under extreme conditions– prompts a fundamental question for European policymakers: is this the kind of punitive, outsourced model we want to import here?Anger at ‘send them back’ chants by rightwing MEPs after EU migration law voteRead moreEurope is no stranger to externalisation policies. Longstanding violations of the rights of refugees and migrantsin LibyaandTunisiademonstrate that migration cooperation aimed at preventing arrivals to the EU will probably result in serious and widespread human rights violations if adequate guarantees are not established from the outset.Earlier European attempts at extraterritorial centres confirm this pattern. Italy’sagreement with Albaniahas been beset by questions over its compatibility with EU law. This shows how moving people offshore can severely restrict their access to lawyers, interpreters and judges, thereby jeopardising the effectiveness of the legal remedies that are essential for protecting other human rights. Meanwhile the UK’s cooperation with Rwanda, an attempt to outsource asylum procedures rather than returns,fell foul of the UK supreme courtdue to systemic weaknesses in the Rwandan asylum and judicial system that failed to guarantee that people sent there would be safe from refoulement.How can anyone reasonably believe this time will be any different? Yet European governments pursuing the establishment of return hubs continue to offer the same reassurance: that they will operate in strict compliance with their international human rights obligations.With implementation imminent, vague assurances are no longer enough. The stakes are simply too high. On one hand, European countries are experimenting with human lives. The dangers are well documented, and will only increase if governments partner with countries with poor human rights records. On the other hand, the effectiveness of return hubs remains unproven. This initiative could absorb vast public funds until the courts intervene, damaging Europe’s international credibility.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhy rights groups fear new ECHR declaration could weaken migrant protectionsRead moreBefore a single flight takes off or a single euro is allocated, governments should make four essential commitments.First, to fully assess human rights risks before any agreement is reached, while making sure people have clear avenues to submit complaints and access courts. If violations cannot be reliably prevented, cooperation should not proceed.Second, to allow rigorous, independent, real-time monitoring of conditions on the ground. Findings should trigger follow-up actions, and the entire system should include an off switch that suspends cooperation the moment that evidence of serious human rights violations emerges.Third, to form legally binding agreements with iron-clad, judicially enforceable human rights clauses. If a host country cannot or will not accept binding legal commitments, the deal should be off.Finally, to make all of this public – the assessments, agreements and reports – guaranteeing effective scrutiny from courts, parliaments, the media and the public.Demanding this level of rigour is not asking the impossible. Rather, it is the minimum required to mitigate the risks of the “innovative solutions” outlined in the open letter. If European governments choose to forge ahead into risky territory, they need to accept the guardrails necessary to prevent them setting up human rights black holes.Michael O’Flaherty is the commissioner for human rights of the Council of EuropeDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsMigrationOpinionCouncil of EuropeInternational lawHuman rightscommentShareReuse this content

title: ‘We didn’t want him to be punchable!’: Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck on Ferris Bueller’s Day Off | Ferris Bueller's Day Off | The Guardian
description: ‘Alan and I have neat hair because we’d both just been in a military play. If we’d had crazy 1980s mullets, it wouldn’t have become a timeless classic’
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: ‘Alan and I have neat hair because we’d both just been in a military play. If we’d had crazy 1980s mullets, it wouldn’t have become a timeless classic’
Content: ‘S-wing batter’ … Alan Ruck and Matthew Broderick in Ferris Bueller’s Day Off.Photograph: Cinetext Bildarchiv/Paramount/AllstarView image in fullscreen‘S-wing batter’ … Alan Ruck and Matthew Broderick in Ferris Bueller’s Day Off.Photograph: Cinetext Bildarchiv/Paramount/AllstarHow we madeFerris Bueller's Day Off‘We didn’t want him to be punchable!’: Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck on Ferris Bueller’s Day Off‘Alan and I have neat hair because we’d both just been in a military play. If we’d had crazy 1980s mullets, it wouldn’t have become a timeless classic’Interviews by Simon BlandMon 20 Jul 2026 08.58 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 22.28 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMatthew Broderick, played Ferris BuellerI’d done WarGames and Ladyhawke and was getting sent a lot of “young guy” parts. I was a little concerned at first because Ferris talks to the audience – I’d done that in two plays and didn’t want to it to seem like that’s all I could do. Meanwhile, my agent said: “You’ve been asked to do aJohn Hughesmovie. You’re doing it.” I didn’t know much about John at the time but I kept hearing him being referred to as the “Spielberg of teen films”.I thought Ferris shouldn’t seem to be trying too hard, that things came easy to him. I wanted to be a little cool and aloof. We didn’t want him to be somebody you wanted to punch in the face. That’s always the danger with guys who get away with everything.After a test screening, one young woman’s feedback was that she didn’t like Ferris because “he’d make me do things I don’t want to do” so they got rid of a scene where I try to get Sloane [Mia Sara] not to go back home and keep hanging out having fun.Alan [Ruck, played Cameron] and I have timeless hair-dos because we’d both just been in a military play, Biloxi Blues. Given the date the film was made, had we been able to, we might’ve both had crazy mullets, in which case, it would not have become a timeless classic.John could be a very exacting director. Often, I’d be doing his ideas as carefully as I could but the scenes where I’m alone in the house talking to the camera? We’d just make stuff up. I picked up the clarinet and started playing just because it was there and I pretended I was playing great. The plan was to replace the sound and make it really good but John saw the footage and said: “I’m not doing that.”Marilyn Vance [costume designer] and John came up with my outfit. There’s details you don’t even see. My socks had flags of all the nations on them. The rabbit’s foot in my pocket was just there. John thought Ferris’s leather coat should be simple, like James Dean’s red jacket but we went very much the other way. The beret was my idea.double quotation markThey had a radio contest inviting people to be in a John Hughes movie and 10,000 people showed up. The window washers dancing? The camera guys just noticed themThe parade scene was huge. We shot at an actual parade one weekend, then did closeups the next. They had a radio contest inviting people to be in a John Hughes movie and 10,000 people showed up. It felt real. The window washers dancing? The camera guys just noticed them. They were catching whatever they could.I spent weeks with choreographer Kenny Ortega but blew my knee out doing thoseback yard running scenes. It made the dancing very spontaneous. John would be far away with a megaphone shouting: “Make something up!” I was scared but once everybody’s cheering you on, I felt like I was the king of the world.When John was alive, I always thought, “Maybe we’ll do it again?” but too much time passed. What happened to Ferris? He’s pretty charming and creative but he probably turned into his dad. That’s what most of us do.Alan Ruck, played Cameron FryeJohn Hughes originally thought of Anthony Michael Hall or Emilio Estevez for Ferris but they wanted to move on to different things so he wisely cast Matthew. We were doing this play together, Biloxi Blues. In one scene, he’s writing in his journal and we’re supposed to be asleep. One night, he nudges me. I open my eyes and he shows me his journal. It said: “They offered me Ferris Bueller.”The casting directors didn’t want to see me because I was 28 and thought I was too old. My agent said: “He plays opposite Broderick every night. They look the same age.” I auditioned and they thought, “This’ll work,” but they didn’t offer it to me right away. Matthew’s manager kept laying the idea in John’s ear, saying: “Matthew would like to work with Alan.” That went a long way to getting me the part. Matthew and I were already friends. We didn’t have to make that up.I always felt on the outside of life. Besides having Ferris for his best friend, Cameron’s definitely on the outside too, I figured because of his repressive home life. He wasn’t free and easy. That was also easy to tap into.View image in fullscreen‘When we sent the car flying out the window, the whole crew erupted into an enormous cheer’ … Matthew Broderick, Mia Sara and Alan Ruck.Photograph: Everett Collection Inc/AlamyMatthew has a photographic memory for comedy routines and movie scenes. We were big Richard Pryor fans and he knew every routine. He’d do monologues ofTony Montana from Scarfaceand crack me up. Biloxi Blues director Gene Saks would get mad at us because we were always screwing around. Matthew used to imitate him backstage, going: “I don’t believe you’re soldiers in world war two!” When John told me to come up with an authoritarian voice for Sloan’s dad, that was me imitating Matthew, imitating Gene. I did it just to see if I could make Matthew laugh – and it did.I honestly can’t remember what was going on in my head during the gallery scene. I had a number of personal losses and tragedies in my life, as we all do and I tapped into some of that.We had three cars. One had heavy-duty suspension for stunts. The one we drove was plagued with electrical problems. When we collect it after the parade, we’d do the scene and it wouldn’t start. We did 16 takes. When we sent the third one flying out the window, the whole crew erupted into an enormous cheer.There was a period where I didn’t want anything to do with the film. I was having trouble getting work and everybody kept talking about Ferris Bueller. I thought that’s as good as it’s going to get for me. As soon as I started working again, I really came to appreciate it. My joke is that Cameron’s father came home and threw him out the window. The rest of that day wasn’t a happy one, but maybe things turned out OK. Maybe now he’s a lawyer in Chicago.Matthew Broderick and Alan Ruck will reunite in the upcoming movieThe Best Is Yet to ComeExplore more on these topicsFerris Bueller's Day OffHow we madeMatthew BroderickJohn HughesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Trent Park House of Secrets review – the bugged stately home where German prisoners blabbed their secrets  | Museums | The Guardian
description: A new museum in this redeveloped country house relives its fascinating period as a palatial prison of war camp – with Jewish refugees listening in from the basement
Author: Eddy Frankel
Headline: A new museum in this redeveloped country house relives its fascinating period as a palatial prison of war camp – with Jewish refugees listening in from the basement
Content: Listening in … a mannequin in the basement of Trent Park House of Secrets.Photograph: James O DaviesView image in fullscreenListening in … a mannequin in the basement of Trent Park House of Secrets.Photograph: James O DaviesMuseumsReviewTrent Park House of Secrets review – the bugged stately home where German prisoners blabbed their secretsTrent Park, LondonA new museum in this redeveloped country house relives its fascinating period as a palatial prison of war camp – with Jewish refugees listening in from the basementEddy FrankelMon 20 Jul 2026 11.45 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHistory happened here. I mean, history happens everywhere. But serious, important, world-shaping history happened at Trent Park.Built on the grounds of Enfield Chase, an expanse of Hertfordshire countryside used as hunting grounds by royalty, this country house has lived many lives. It was a Georgian retreat, a Victorian mansion, a post-Edwardian sanctuary, and then, during the second world war, it became a place of secrets. Afterwards, it became a university campus, before falling into dereliction. Now, it has been revived, and its hidden stories brought into the light.The house was built after George III leased some land to his favourite doctor, Sir Richard Jebb, in the 1770s. It ended up in the hands of the Bevan banking family, who expanded and remodelled it, before the Sassoon family acquired it and did the same. Mega-socialite Philip Sassoon spent the equivalent of millions turning it into his dream country house in the 1920s – a suburban party palace frequented by the likes of Churchill and Wallis Simpson.View image in fullscreenRefurbished … Trent Park House.Photograph: James O Davies. Courtesy of Trent Park Museum TrustThe house has been refurbished now, and is largely as Sassoon would’ve left it: pastel walls, floral curtains, countless deep, plush sofas for guests to lounge in. Flanking a huge bookcase in the library are portraits of Sassoon and his sister Sibyl by John Singer Sargent. They’re gorgeous paintings. Philip is intense, quiet, aloof. Sibyl is dark, elegant, sombre.But it’s what is between the portraits that matters. Give that bookcase a shove and – hey presto! – a secret entrance down into the basement.Of all of Trent Park’s transformations, this is its greatest: from aristo bolthole to audacious experiment in spycraft. After Sassoon died in 1939, the house was nabbed by the government and used as a prisoner of war camp for high-ranking German generals. Here, in these palatial surroundings, the prisoners were given time and space to pass the war in comfort, to kick back and let loose. But every inch of the house was bugged, even the rose bushes in the garden, every conversation recorded down in the basement, and listened to largely by German-speaking Jewish refugees. The prisoners felt at ease, so they spilled their secrets, and every word was recorded and transcribed.View image in fullscreenRestored … looking from the saloon to the library and John Singer Sargent’s portrait of Sir Philip Sassoon.Photograph: James O DaviesThe listeners got some pretty big intel, too. Information was fed to the Enigma code-breaking team over in Bletchley. General Ludwig Crüwell let slip to a fellow high-ranking officer about German ballistics, and the allies got the jump on the V2 rocket. Someone else blabbed about how German planes communicated with a system of transmitted beams, and the British kiboshed that too.The basement has been faithfully recreated here: a mannequin sits at his station, headphones on, pen poised. The captain’s bed is neatly made, the housekeeper’s cellar is stocked with tea, herbs, wine and German books. It smells of damp, the wool blankets are rough and itchy, the sheets starched stiff. This was a place of resistance, of subterfuge, a place where the Germans could be lulled into a false sense of security and taken for all they had.It wasn’t all good. Listeners heard conversations about German atrocities, but authorities did nothing with the information. They didn’t even submit the intelligence to the Nuremberg trials in the 1950s, worried their secret tech would be exposed.View image in fullscreenLulled into a false sense of security … prisoners of war at Trent Park.Photograph: Houghton HallGood or bad, it’s all lovingly recreated here, filled with neatly presented information and era-appropriate trinkets and decoration, though it’s a shame there aren’t more original artworks dotted throughout the house instead of copies.The museum largely concentrates on the house’s role in the war, mainly because it’s interesting, and sets it apart from every other gaudy National Trust-style mansion in this country (though it is still definitely one of those). But it’s the clashing layers of history built into the house’s very foundations – caked into its paint and wallpaper – that make it feel special. In its transformation from Georgian to Victorian to mock-Georgian to war-time austerity and postwar dereliction, the house becomes a portrait of England’s changing fortunes and tastes, the way wealth has ebbed and flowed, the impact of immigration in shaping the country, and the way war can tear everything apart. That’s what is amazing about places like this, they’re not just grand houses, or follies of the rich brought back to life, they’re containers of history, and Trent Park sure saw its fair share of that.Trent Park House of Secrets opens in London on 21 JulyExplore more on these topicsMuseumsLondonSecond world warreviewsShareReuse this content

title: From Gary Lineker’s revenge to the bizarre tale of a missing cat: the best viral TV moments of the World Cup | Television | The Guardian
description: A pranked England manager, a bored Beckham, a Viking takeover and an unwise Wayne Rooney wager: here are the TV treasures of the beautiful game’s biggest ever tournament
Author: Michael Hogan
Headline: A pranked England manager, a bored Beckham, a Viking takeover and an unwise Wayne Rooney wager: here are the TV treasures of the beautiful game’s biggest ever tournament
Content: Showboating … fans do the Viking row that has swept the globe.Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersView image in fullscreenShowboating … fans do the Viking row that has swept the globe.Photograph: Marco Bello/ReutersTelevisionFrom Gary Lineker’s revenge to the bizarre tale of a missing cat: the best viral TV moments of the World CupA pranked England manager, a bored Beckham, a Viking takeover and an unwise Wayne Rooney wager: here are the TV treasures of the beautiful game’s biggest ever tournamentMichael HoganMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 00.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAstodgy final saw silky Spain deservedly beat cynical Argentina. Football wasn’t coming home after all as England suffered semi-final heartbreak again, before salvaging some pride to finish third – their best World Cup finish since 1966. The Tartan Army drank America’s bars dry as their team went out with a whimper. Cape Verde lasted longer than Germany. It was the end of the road for Ronaldo and Modric, while the phrase “hydration break” entered everyday parlance. But beyond the pitch, it has been an eventful five weeks, too.Here are 26 viral World Cup moments you might have missed …LOLing StonesAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueWon’t somebody consider Tommy T’s blood pressure? After England defeated Mexico, defender John Stones suddenly clutched his shoulder in the Azteca Stadium dressing room. Teammate Declan Rice, who was in on the gag, called over manager Thomas Tuchel, whose face fell with worry. At which point, perfectly timed with the music pumping out, Stones broke into an air-punching dance. Tuchel saw the funny side and hugged him. Stones reprised the prank after the Norway victory. You can’t kid a kidder.Is Vicky bovvered, though?View image in fullscreenVictoria: the one Beckham non-plussed at England v Norway.Photograph: Javier García/ShutterstockWho says WAGs are all pout and no passion?Victoria Beckham became an instant memeafter failing to react to England’s equaliser during the tense quarter-final against Norway. When Jude Bellingham scored, her husband, David, and son Romeo leapt to their feet in joy. Playing to type, unsmiling Posh Spice was too busy chatting to her hairdresser Ken Pavés to even notice. “She was celebrating inside, I promise,” David said later.Shaggy cat storyCommentary came over all stream-of-consciousness during Côte d’Ivoire v Norway.
