When winger Oscar Bobb came on,the BBC’s Danny Murphy said wistfully:“I used to have a cat called Bob. He jumped in the back of a Royal Mail van and we lost him. Sad, really.” “The game isn’t that bad,” chuckled Murphy’s colleague Steve Bower. When the throwaway remark captured viewers’ hearts,Murphy revisited it during Spain v Austria. “Bob didn’t actually die. We got him back six or seven weeks later. Unfortunately he’d gone feral and we couldn’t reintegrate him into the home. He ended up living with my friend’s parents in Preston.” A furry-tale for the ages.Vikings make wavesNorway fans did it in stadiums, on subway escalators and in Times Square. The synchronised “Viking row” celebration soon spread around the globe. Norwegian politicians put their differences aside to do it, with speaker Masud Gharahkhani turning coxswain. Golf fans did it to celebrate a Viktor Hovland birdie on the PGA Tour. Care home residents did it. Fighter pilots did it in their F-35 cockpits. Trust England to poop the party. After the Three Lions knocked Norway out, fans chanted: “There’ll be no f*cking rowing in New York.”Rowing refusenikNot everyone had a liking for Vikings. One Norway supporter ostentatiously refused to join in and went on news programmes worldwide to explain why. “It drives me crazy,” said Emil Anners Lappen. “The Vikings did not row across the Atlantic. They sailed. They can win whatever they want. I will not row.” All very principled until viewers pointed out thatLappen had a set of oarsmounted on the wall behind him.Belgians troll TrumpIt sparked accusations of cheating when President Trump personally lobbied the Fifa boss, Gianni Infantino, to defer the red card ban given to USA striker Folarin Balogun, allowing him to play in the last-16 tie against Belgium. Justice was done when the co-hosts were thrashed 4-1 by a Belgian side fired up by the scandal. “Overturn this!” they shouted, before gleefully mimicking Trump’s signature YMCA dad-dance. Bigly gratifying.Harry the teenager’s voice breaksAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueSomeone pass that man a lozenge. Having lost his voice during the celebratory rendition of Wonderwall after the nail-biting win over Mexico, England captain Harry Kane conducted a hilariously croaky on-pitch BBC interview. Oasis frontman Liam Gallagher sympathised, tweeting: “It’s hard work that singing, Harry Kane. C’mon England, c’mon Wonderwall.”Jude and Erling’s cute bromanceView image in fullscreenEmbracing … Erling Braut Haaland and Jude Bellingham.Photograph: Roberto Schmidt/AFP/Getty ImagesThey might have been quarter-final opponents but England’s Jude Bellingham and Norway’s Erling Haaland have an endearing friendship, dating back to their Borussia Dortmund days. Social media was awash with pics of the pair embracing and Bellingham kissing Haaland on the cheek mid-interview. Some jokingly compared it to gay ice hockey romanceHeated Rivalryand wrote erotic fan-fic. Even after defeat, Haaland sportingly insisted: “Bellingham has too many critics … He’s one of the best in the world … Anybody would want a Jude in their team.”Allow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueRooney sticks his oar inAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueA decade since Gary Lineker was forced to host Match of the Day in his underpants after vowing to do so if Leicester City won the Premier League, BBC pundit Wayne Rooney made his own unwise on-air wager. The former England captain said that if Norway reached the quarter-finals, he would row down the River Mersey. Norwegian striker Erling Haaland duly called out Rooney. Rooney turned out to be a man of his word and dulyrowed down the Hudsonwith colleagues Joe Hart and Micah Richards.Javier’s X-rated heckleDuring the Mexico v England epic, cameras captured the home team’s coach, Javier Aguirre, calling out to Three Lions winger Anthony Gordon, who was hugging the touchline in front of Aguirre’s technical area. Why? Just to tell him: “Hey Gordon, fuck you!” It was merely potty-mouthed banter and both cracked up laughing. Gordon later said being sworn at was “just a bit of fun” and “a compliment”.Haaland hits the catwalkAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueRussian model Anastasia Kostromitina went viral for being an Erling Haaland lookalike, embracing the resemblance by posting Instagram videos recreating the striker’s facial expressions. As she gained modelling offers and millions of followers, the Moscow-based 24-year-old said it was “like a dream”.A different kind of rowingAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueWhen cameras caught them squabbling in the stands, one couple sparked speculation worldwide about what they were arguing about. The South Africa supporters initially seemed to be having a good time. The mood turned sour when the male fan checked his mobile and she glanced at the screen. He appeared to say “It’s nothing” before swiftly pocketing the phone. She disagreed. He unwisely told her to “calm down”. She sat down looking furious while he repeatedly apologised. As social media users urged her to “file for divorce”, it was like a footballingColdplay kiss cam.Roon or Poch: who wore it best?USA manager Mauricio Pochettino became an unlikely style icon. Pundit Wayne Rooney liked Poch’s touchline look so much, he bought the same outfit andwore it on the BBC coverage. Rooney drew the line at rolling up his sleeves, though. He’s not a complete copycat.Your coach is now departingEngland’s assistant coach Anthony Barry became a cult hero for his pithily revealing interviews in the tunnel at half-time, offering insights into Tuchel’s tactics and the dressing room mood. During the third-place playoff, Barry became tearful talking about his pride in “11 lads with broken hearts”. Just as enjoyable was his habit ofabruptly exitingat the end of the conversation. Well, he had a dugout to get back to.Leads unitedAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueA dog that had been missing for a month was reunited with her owner after appearing in a viral video of celebrating Mexico fans. Gorda was spotted by her owner’s brother in footage from Ciudad Victoria after the co-hosts beat Czechia. “Life is good,” posted Ale Garcia after they were reunited. “Mexico won and I found my dog after a loooong time thanks to a livestream lol.”WonderwalletAllow content provided by a third party?This article includes content hosted onembed.bsky.app. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as the provider may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueArgentina fans took a break from Messi mania and Falklands chants to provide a heartwarming moment. During their victory over Jordan, a fellow fan dropped his wallet. Fans checked the name on the cards inside, then sang it until the owner appeared and was reunited with his lost property.Gary Lingerer, more likeOne gets the distinct impression that Gary Lineker enjoyed making the World Cup uncomfortable for his former BBC bosses. Having criticised his ex-colleagues for broadcasting from “a green box in Salford”, grinning Gaz not only made a cameo as an ITV pundit but unexpectedly returned to the BBC bygatecrashing Micah Richards’ Match of the Day interviewafter England beat Norway. “Look who’s turned up,” said an awkward Richards.Have you missed me?Thanks to mobile phones, people are rarely out of contact nowadays. Hence it felt like a throwback when 65-year-old England fan Michael Hewitt went awol for 10 days. En route to Boston to support the Three Lions, “Little Mick” lost his phone and plane tickets, so decided to turn a brief layover in Spain intoa longer break. Interpol, Spanish police and the British consulate launched a manhunt. Football fans spread the word online. The appeal appeared on TV bulletins. Hewitt turned out to be happily watching games in the bars of Barcelona, according to his relatives, “blissfully unaware that the world was searching for him”.Get outta my cup!Allow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueIt wouldn’t be a World Cup without *checks notes* Ross Kemp filming himself in front of the TV. The EastEnders hard man and documentary-maker has become a cult hero for posting vein-popping videos. “Come on, England, shut that crowd up! Jude, Jude, Jude! Yes, Harry Kane! That’s what I’m talking about!” Bonus points for excessive drooling and having his reading glasses perched atop his head.Nosubtitles requiredIt’s the international language of football. Japanese fan Hirochika Nakakuki went viral after a vox pop interview with Fox Sport, when he didn’t let the language barrier stand in the way. “Excuse me,” he said charmingly. “I cannot speak English but I am EXCITED!”‘Not my finest hour’Veteran midfielder Jordan Henderson suffered a freak injury while celebrating England’s win over Mexico. Having received a yellow card, Hendo climbed over the advertising hoardings at full-time, only to fall badly, break his wrist and get taken to hospital by paramedics. He became the first footballer to be booked and stretchered off without actually playing. “Not my finest hour,” he admitted sheepishly.Danny the champion of the World CupBBC commentator Danny Murphy was every England fan when they missed a chance to score against France andhe drummed the desk in frustration. “Danny, people can hear you if you bang your microphone on the table,” noted colleague Steve Wilson. “Sorry,” muttered Murphy. “It was that or shout really loud.”Kenny Dog-leashAllow Instagram content?This article includes content provided byInstagram. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as they may be using cookies and other technologies. To view this content,click 'Allow and continue'.Allow and continueIt’s been a classic World Cup for canines. During Argentina v Cape Verde, cameras cut to a dog wearing ear defenders, casually sitting in the VIP section of Miami Stadium. Brodie the golden doodle has become a crowd favourite, amassing 2.3 million Instagram followers. He was even made an honorary member of the Tartan Army after partying with Scotland fans earlier in the tournament.Big brother’s little brotherView image in fullscreenLamine Yamal celebrates with his brother Keyne after winning the 2026 World Cup football tournament final.Photograph: Franck Fife/AFP/Getty ImagesSpain’s teenage sensation Lamine Yamal might have won the final buthis three-year-old brother Keyneenjoyed an equally good World Cup, following his gifted elder sibling around. When the pair spotted each other on the big screen after the win over Belgium, broad grins and toddler face-pulling ensued. Wholesome content.Tequila shots on usSouth Korean fans enjoyed sampling the local culture ahead of their group game against Mexico but were foiled when they tried to take a bottle of tequila into Estadio Guadalajara. Refused entry at the gate, they couldn’t let it go to waste, so cracked it open and invited passing home fans to help finish it. Impromptu party! Mexico supporters had already earned credit by dancing to Gangnam Style when South Korea beat Czechia.Everyone’s a criticView image in fullscreen‘My favourite bit was when it finished’ … BTS perform at the Super Bowl-style half-time World Cup final show.Photograph: Carl Recine/Getty ImagesThe final’s Super Bowl-style half-time show kicked off with Madonna arriving in a dune buggy driven by Ronaldo and Ronaldinho. A tag-team production proceeded to take in the Muppets, BTS, Justin Bieber, Shakira, Burna Boy, Coldplay and a children’s choir wanging on about love. When BBC presenter Gabby Logan turned to the pundits for their verdict, Wayne Rooney spoke for the nation: “My favourite bit was when it finished. I like a lot of them artists butI thought it was crap.”Explore more on these topicsTelevisionWorld Cup 2026featuresShareReuse this content

title: Sex, beds and grisly executions: what Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey changed, invented – and left out | Movies | The Guardian
description: From missing scenes and characters to a brand new ending, the director has taken some artistic liberties with Homer’s epic poem
Author: Chris Tryhorn
Headline: From missing scenes and characters to a brand new ending, the director has taken some artistic liberties with Homer’s epic poem
Content: A kingdom for a horse … The OdysseyPhotograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreenA kingdom for a horse … The OdysseyPhotograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesMoviesSex, beds and grisly executions: what Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey changed, invented – and left outFrom missing scenes and characters to a brand new ending, the director has taken some artistic liberties with Homer’s epic poemPeter Bradshaw’s five-star reviewanda less enthused classicist’s takeWill women also enjoy Nolan’s Odyssey?Christopher Nolan interviewedWhy shooting on occupied land feels like a betrayalChris TryhornMon 20 Jul 2026 08.17 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.16 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTurning a 12,000-line poem that takes a day to read aloud into a three-hour film was always going to require some pruning. So it has proved withChristopher Nolan’s The Odyssey, which dispenses with a number of scenes and characters from Homer’s ancient Greek epic. The director has also changed the emphasis in some of the story’s most famous episodes and made a few of his own additions. Here we run down the most significant alterations.The Odyssey review – Nolan goes god-tier with breathtaking epic of men, monsters and moral metamorphosisRead moreWhat Nolan has left outThe godsHomer’s Odyssey opens with a discussion between Zeus and Athena on Olympus; with Zeus’s blessing, Athena plays an active role in guiding Odysseus home, supporting him in vanquishing the suitors and offering encouragement to his son, Telemachus, while adopting multiple disguises. Meanwhile, Poseidon frustrates Odysseus’s progress and Hermes is sent on a couple of key missions. The only god Nolan depicts is Athena (Zendaya), who appears as a vision seen only by Odysseus (Matt Damon) and seems to represent his conscience rather than being a fully realised divine entity.Nausicaaand thePhaeaciansOne of the Odyssey’s most celebrated scenes is the meeting between Odysseus and the young princess Nausicaa; he has washed up naked on a strange island where she and her maids are playing with a ball. After they handle this awkward situation with delicacy, Odysseus follows Nausicaa to the city of the Phaeacians, where he recounts his tales of the Cyclops, Circe, Scylla and Charybdis before being given safe passage back to Ithaca. Nolan leaves out the Phaeacians entirely and has Odysseus tell those stories to Calypso (Charlize Theron) as recovered memories.‘At times I felt I’d bitten off more than I could chew’: Christopher Nolan on sweeping the Oscars, making The Odyssey – and getting a puppyRead moreSome major ghostsThe film shows Odysseus meeting his former commander Agamemnon (Benny Safdie) in the realm of the dead (Nolan is correct not to depict it as an underworld). But we don’t see Achilles, the greatest of the Greek warriors, nor Ajax, who in the poem silently rebuffs Odysseus’s attempt at reconciliation. Neither do we meet Odysseus’s mother, whom he tries and fails to embrace three times. Another character omitted is the luckless Elpenor, who plunges to his death after drinking too much and then pleads to be properly buried. It’s easy to see why Nolan wanted to economise on these spiritual encounters, despite their power and resonance in the poem.View image in fullscreenNo pushover … Anne Hathaway as Penelope with Tom Holland as Telemachus.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesA classicist’s verdict on Nolan’s Odyssey: a soulful hero flatters our times as women and nuance pushed overboardRead moreThe execution of the disloyal womenIt comes as no surprise that Nolan shies away from including the Odyssey’s nastiest scene, in which 12 enslaved women who have been disloyal to the household and slept with the suitors are hanged by Telemachus. But Nolan invents a detail along the same lines when he shows one of those treacherous women, Melantho (Mia Goth), effectively condemned to death by Odysseus’s wife, Penelope (Anne Hathaway), who shoves her into the carnage while the suitors are being slaughtered. He spares us the grisly spectacle of what happens in the poem to the odious goatherd Melanthius, who has his nose, ears, hands, feet and genitals chopped off.Penelope’s bed testHomer’s Penelope is no pushover when Odysseus returns, and to satisfy herself that the beggar who has just slaughtered the suitors is, in fact, her husband, she draws him into describing their marital bed, as only he and Penelope know that Odysseus had fashioned it from an olive tree. Nolan omits this detail, which demonstrates how Penelope matches her husband for guile; instead, Odysseus shows her a figurine of Athena to prove his identity.View image in fullscreenAgamemnon, commander of the Greek forces, seen from behind in The OdysseyPhotograph: Collection Christophel/AlamyWhat Nolan has addedThe wooden horseHomer does not tell the full story of the wooden horse, but it is mentioned on three occasions in the the Odyssey (and not at all in the Iliad). Given that using the horse to break into Troy was Odysseus’s idea and crucial to the Trojan saga, it seems appropriate to include it in the film.SinonThe character of Sinon (Elliot Page), who is shown in the film unwittingly tricking the Trojans into accepting the wooden horse, does not appear anywhere in Homer. Nolan is drawing on Virgil’s account of the fall of Troy in the second book of the Aeneid. He also invents the backstory of Sinon being made to take the place ofAntinous (Robert Pattinson)in the Greek army at Troy.The people from the seaThere is much talk in the film about the world of the Greek heroes being threatened by “people from the sea”. This is not a preoccupation of Homer’s Odyssey. Nor does any Homeric character say, as Nolan’s Odysseus does, that the Trojan war was really all about “trading routes”. But these nods to the concerns of ancient historians show Nolan has done his research.What Nolan has changedCalypsoNolan’s Calypso sedates Odysseus with lotus flowers to make him lose his memories, but somehow develops pity for him and lets him go on his way. The Calypso of the poem doesn’t do drugs but is very much into sex (not something we see in the film), forcing Odysseus to share her bed night after night even though he is desperate to get home. She releases him only after Hermes shows up to tell her that Zeus has decided she must do so; as a mere nymph, she knows she must defer to the authority of Olympus. Once it is clear he can leave, Odysseus is able to enjoy a last bit of lovemaking before heading off on his raft.Christopher Nolan’s Odyssey used occupied land as a film set. That feels like a betrayal | Mohamed Sleiman LabatRead moreThe CyclopsNolan’s Cyclops doesn’t speak (much), whereas in the poem he engages in lethally comic banter with Odysseus. The lack of dialogue in the film makes the one-eyed giant scarier, but it means we lose a couple of important details from the poem: the ruse whereby Odysseus identifies himself as Nobody so the other Cyclopes won’t come to the blinded monster’s aid, and the moment when Odysseus recklessly reveals his real name, which the Cyclops can then use to ask his father, Poseidon, to curse Odysseus’s return journey. These changes fit with Nolan’s decision to downplay Odysseus’s abilities as a trickster and foreground his battle-hardened resilience and trauma.View image in fullscreenDirector Christopher Nolan (left) with Matt Damon and Zendaya during the making of the film.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesCirceIt is never explained why Homer’s Circe turns some of Odysseus’s comrades into pigs. She turns them back into men because Odysseus has proved immune to her potion (thanks to a herb Hermes has given him) and then agrees to have sex with her. She proves to be a very helpful ally and he stays with her for a whole year. Nolan’s Circe (Samantha Morton) seems to be motivated by anger towards animalistic male behaviour, but then meekly agrees to restore Odysseus’s men to their human bodies after he intuits that he really shouldn’t touch that stew and makes her feel sorry for a bunch of guys who just want to get home.Chastity, nodding and enormous pores: will women also love Nolan’s Odyssey?Read moreThe ambush and father-son reunionIn the film, a band of villains hired by Antinous attempt to kill Telemachus (Tom Holland) at a temple, only to be thwarted by Odysseus, who has arrived on the scene in disguise. Soon afterwards, Telemachus realises his saviour is his father. In the poem, the ambush is led by Antinous out at sea and is easily (and undramatically) evaded by Telemachus, who then meets his father back in Ithaca in a reunion carefully stage-managed by Athena.The endingHomer’s Odyssey comes to a notoriously unsatisfactory conclusion. Nolan sensibly discards all the poem’s final scenes: the suitors’ arrival in the realm of the dead; Odysseus’s encounter with his father, Laertes, whom he needlessly upsets by adopting yet another fake persona; and the anticlimactic battle between Odysseus and the relatives of the suitors. In the film, Telemachus becomes king of Ithaca, allowing Odysseus and Penelope to sail off into the sunset on a mission to honour his fallen comrades.Chris Tryhorn is the author of Homer’s Odyssey, a new version of the original story now available as an ebookExplore more on these topicsFilmThe OdysseyChristopher NolanHomerFilm adaptationsMatt DamonAnne HathawayfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘Everything I know about evil I learned in that small town’: John Carpenter on terrifying the world with Halloween, The Thing and The Fog | John Carpenter | The Guardian
description: At 78, the king of horror and sci-fi movies is as much of a rebel as ever. He talks about creativity, compromise (he’s not a fan) and how he became addicted to moviemaking
Author: Steve Rose
Headline: At 78, the king of horror and sci-fi movies is as much of a rebel as ever. He talks about creativity, compromise (he’s not a fan) and how he became addicted to moviemaking
Content: ‘Nightmares for me are personal’ … John Carpenter.Photograph: Philip Cheung/The Guardian; photographer's assistant: Jhovany QuirozView image in fullscreen‘Nightmares for me are personal’ … John Carpenter.Photograph: Philip Cheung/The Guardian; photographer's assistant: Jhovany QuirozThe big interviewJohn CarpenterInterview‘Everything I know about evil I learned in that small town’: John Carpenter on terrifying the world with Halloween, The Thing and The FogSteve RoseAt 78, the king of horror and sci-fi movies is as much of a rebel as ever. He talks about creativity, compromise (he’s not a fan) and how he became addicted to moviemakingMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIn 2024,John Carpenterhad a dream that sounds like one of his movies: it involved a portal to the underworld beneath a disused church, monstrous demons and people being turned inside out. “I woke up and thought: wow! What an elaborate story,” he says. “I rarely have cinematic dreams, but this was one of them.” He liked it so much that he has turned it into a graphic novel, Cathedral, with an accompanying soundtrack. “It seemed to fit this heavy metal groove that we were starting on, and it worked out pretty well.”Most people would consider this a nightmare rather than a dream, I suggest. “No. Nightmares, for me, are personal – something that happened to me,” he replies. “Usually my nightmares are, I’m directing a movie and no one will listen to me. No one will follow my directions. Seriously, that’s what I wake up from.”It doesn’t take a Freudian analyst to unpack that one. Carpenter’s film career effectively wound down at the end of the 20th century, after 25 years of defiantly making movies his own way – often tense, terrifying, violent, gory movies. He is the epitome of the stubbornly independent film-maker, who demands complete creative control, who goes further than Hollywood studios would usually dare and whose name above the title is a seal of cult quality. “‘Don’t tell me what to do’ is John Carpenter’s credo!” he tells me. Few of his movies were commercial hits at the time, though, and a great many were the opposite, including his final two movies, 2001’s Ghosts of Mars and 2010’s The Ward.View image in fullscreenCarpenter and Kurt Russell on the set of Big Trouble in Little China (1986).Photograph: Photos 12/AlamyDoes he miss making movies? “Yes and no,” he says. He is talking on a video call from his home in the Hollywood Hills and, despite his reputation as an uncompromising grouch, the 78-year-old is in good spirits and good health. “It’s really weird. Yes, because I’ve always loved cinema and the process. But no, working in the studio system in Hollywood is very difficult.”He seems to have managed pretty well, I suggest. “You have to fight to do that. That’s the unfortunate thing. And that takes a little bit out of you, as a person, every time it has to happen,” he says. “There’s a reason why directors come out of the other end looking as if they’ve been in a war. Because they have been: it’s a creative war. And so there are a lot of drunks who are directors, that’s one way of coping, or taking other drugs. Anyway, we won’t get into all that.”Was that ever you? I ask.“No, no, no, let’s not,” he waves off the question. “I’m not going to tell you all my secrets.”Carpenter may have lost battles, but he won the war. Many of his films are considered classics: Halloween, The Thing, Assault on Precinct 13, The Fog, Escape from New York. And Carpenter’s film-making style continues to cast a long shadow – not only over horror and sci-fi movies, but also mainstream television, especially anything set in the 80s or with the look of that era (Stranger Things, say, or the current Widow’s Bay), and even philosophical culture-mongers such as Adam Curtis and Slavoj Žižek. Musicians also bow to him: his spare, synth-driven scores have been namechecked by many, from Portishead to Daft Punk, Trent Reznor to Hans Zimmer.Carpenter seems unaware of all this. When I tell him there is almost a paragraph of his Wikipedia entry listing film-makers who have cited him as an influence – Guillermo del Toro, Quentin Tarantino, Edgar Wright, James Cameron (who worked for Carpenter once) – he seems genuinely oblivious, pained even. “What does that mean to me? I can’t do anything about that. It’s wonderful, I think it’s fantastic, I don’t knowwhy. If you were gonna emulate a director, you’d emulate somebody whose moviesaren’tpanned.” It sounds as if he is not online much then? “No! I rebelled against mobile phones. I rebelled against computers. I can’t do that, I don’t want to do that.”View image in fullscreenCarpenter’s 1974 debut, Dark Star.Photograph: United Archives/FilmPublicityArchive/Getty ImagesCarpenter’s rebellious streak goes way back, and is inseparable from his art. His father was a music teacher and an orchestra violinist. In 1953, when John was five, the family relocated from northern New York when his dad got a teaching job at Western Kentucky University. “It was the Jim Crow south in the 1950s. Boy, were we fish out of water,” Carpenter says. “Everything I know about evil I learned in that small town of Bowling Green, Kentucky.”His father had encouraged John to learn violin too. “You’re walking to school in the morning and you have a violin under your arm; these country boys in Kentucky decided I was great to beat up. I was a mark for violence with my violin, which made me hate it all the more.”Classical music’s loss would ultimately be synthwave’s gain, but Carpenter was also just the right age to hook into the 60s counterculture. He rebelled against everything, he says: the town he grew up in, the Vietnam war, the system, “all forms of authority”. But he came to love and appreciate his father. “He told me early on: just create something, whether it’s music, writing, painting; whatever it is, create. And here I am, still doing it.”View image in fullscreen‘Working in the studio system in Hollywood is very difficult’ … Carpenter in his office.Photograph: Philip Cheung/The Guardian; photographer’s assistant: Jhovany QuirozHis mother regularly took him to the movies. “She gave me the gift of fantasy, and my father gave me a rigorous approach to creating.” Seeing the sci-fi movie Forbidden Planet when he was eight was a transformative experience: “I remember walking out and thinking: I’m gonna be a movie director.”While near-contemporaries such as Francis Ford Coppola and Martin Scorsese were taking over Hollywood through the front door, Carpenter operated on the genre sidelines. His 1974 debut, Dark Star, was a lo-fi hippy parody of Kubrick’s 2001: A Space Odyssey, which saw space travel less as a symphonic leap into the future and more as a grotty flatshare: boring, unhygienic and fractious. It was made on a shoestring, with egg cartons for spaceship interiors and an inflatable beach ball for an alien, but the movie did reasonably well, alerting Hollywood to the fact he could produce results on a small budget.It all came together with Halloween, in 1978. Led by a then unknown Jamie Lee Curtis and made for $325,000, it grossed over $70m. It opened the floodgates for highly profitable low-budget horror films and is often cited as the foundational slasher movie. Carpenter makes no such claims. “I put in what I thought was the summation of all the horror that I had seen. I wanted to use the audience, manipulate them like a ride at a carnival and have them scream, jump, not knowing where the thing is. Is it a man? Is it not a man?”View image in fullscreenA then relatively unknown Jamie Lee Curtis, in Halloween (1978).Photograph: Falcon International/Kim Gottlieb/AllstarHalloween was an assault on cosy smalltown domesticity: the horror wasn’t confined to a haunted house or a gothic castle; it was in your neighbourhood, in your home, preying on you and your children. “That’s the whole opening,” Carpenter says, “when it pulls back with this little kid with a knife in his hand [young Michael Myers, the future slasher] and it reveals we’re in the suburbs. That’s the whole idea.”Inevitably, the studio wanted Carpenter to repeat the trick with a sequel, especially since Halloween left the fate of its anti-hero open-ended. “I didn’t think there was any story left,” he says. “But they – meaning Universal Pictures and the producers – were gonna do it whether I wanted to or not. So what are you gonna do?” Carpenter wrote, but did not direct, Halloween 2, and after that: “They took it out of my hands, but they had to pay me every time they made a movie. I sit on the couch and a cheque arrives in my hand. What’s wrong with that? I have to admit, with all the rebellion in the world, I am a happy capitalist.”Nevertheless, Carpenter continued to stick it to the man, puncturing his era’s cheery certainties and self-congratulation, ridiculing authority figures, standing up for the underclass. It’s there in Assault on Precinct 13, where racial, gender and law-enforcement hierarchies are upended during a relentless night-time siege. It’s there in Escape from New York, where Kurt Russell’s snarling anti-hero is forced to rescue Donald Pleasence’s feeble American president, but has zero respect for him. It’s even there in his goofy action comedy Big Trouble In Little China – a movie where the ostensible leading man (Russell again) is actually a klutzy sidekick and the Asian cast are the real action heroes.View image in fullscreenHorror masterpiece … Kurt Russell in The Thing.Photograph: Universal Pictures/AllstarCarpenter was possibly at his most out of step with American sensibilities in the summer of 1982, when he released The Thing – a paranoid sci-fi thriller centred on a shape-shifting alien picking off members of an Antarctic research station, aided by some fantastically disgusting special effects. It is now recognised as a horror masterpiece. Back then, it arrived in cinemas two weeks after Steven Spielberg’s ET the Extra-Terrestrial. The contrast could not have been more jarring.Carpenter recalls going to see ET that summer: “I remember seeing the boys on their bicycles and the ET wrapped up, and then they go up into the sky magically. And that’s when I threw up.” He’s joking, but only half. “I was like: ‘Come on, don’t do this.’” He’s actually full of admiration for Spielberg, not least for reading the 80s zeitgeist. But he prefers his version: “If you really want to know the truth about alien encounters, I think that my movies are more realistic about what will happen. Think of the settlers who came to the United States and their encounters with the Indians. It didn’t really go so well for the Indians, OK? Our encounters with aliens – I imagine it won’t go so well for us.”That said, Carpenter did make a relatively feelgood alien story two years later: 1984’s Starman, in which a visiting alien inhabits the body of Karen Allen’s deceased husband, Jeff Bridges (who earned an Oscar nomination for his performance). The result was almost a grownup ET; a tender, comical, road-trip romance. It showed Carpenter’s surprising capacity for sensitive, emotional drama. “I wanted to show I could do it,” he says. “Bicycles taking off and flying – I wasn’t going to go that far, but …”View image in fullscreenJohn Carpenter with his son,Cody (left), and godson, Daniel Davies.Photograph: Sophie GransardThen there’s They Live, from 1988, which seems to have taken on a life of its own. The story concerns a homeless worker (played by wrestler “Rowdy” Roddy Piper) who discovers some sunglasses that reveal subliminal propaganda messages beneath every advert, magazine and piece of packaging he sees: “Obey”, “Marry and Reproduce”, “Stay Asleep”. Even the dollar bills secretly say: “This is your God.” The glasses also reveal the aliens who walk among us undetected, controlling humanity.Not exactly subtle, and They Live is by no means his best film (Piper is no Kurt Russell), but it has become part of the culture, endlessly mimicked, turned intomemesand even co-opted intoa successful clothing brandby the graphic artist Shepard Fairey – the ultimate irony, perhaps. Just last week, prankstersdoctored billboards for Meta’s AI glassesThey-Live-style, revealing Kylie Jenner to be an alien and the ad’s subliminal slogan to be “We’re always watching”.Carpenter was specifically targeting the Reagan era in They Live, he says, the hyperconsumerism, but also the inequality – contrasting Piper’s homeless community with the affluent heights of 80s Los Angeles. “It’s a documentary, and it didn’t end. It’s still going on now, even worse.” He stops himself. “You’ll get me ranting here, and that’s not a good thing.” But, almost needless to say, he’s depressed by the state of his country. “Things are bad here. I guess you guys realise that over there?”Žižek, the Slovenian philosopher, called They Live “one of the forgotten masterpieces of the Hollywood left” in his 2012 documentary The Pervert’s Guide to Ideology, which saw the film as a metaphor for not just consumerism but ideology as a whole: “When you put the glasses on, you see dictatorship in democracy. It’s the invisible order which sustains your apparent freedom.”“I don’t know if I’d go that far,” says Carpenter (he has never heard of Žižek, and asks me to send him the clip). “It’s actually democracy under pressure from the dictator. I’m a real sucker for American values. There’s a lot of people like me and we’re constantly fighting, but we’re doing it within the system. I vote. That’s the only power I have. I’m not a rich guy like these guys. And I keep believing that we’ll fight them off one day. We’re all going to be like Roddy Piper, we’re going to give ’em the finger at the end there.”View image in fullscreen‘Targeting the Reagan era’ … an alien policeman  in Carpenter’s 1988 film They Live.Photograph: MCA/Universal/AllstarOf course, voting is not the only power Carpenter has; his cinema is a textbook lesson in how beliefs and values can be transmitted through popular culture, even when they are in opposition to the beliefs of those who control that culture. But he is happy for others to take up the fight these days. He didn’t realise how much film-making was taking out of him until he stopped doing it, he says. “I was exhausted, and then I took this extended vacation and I got happier the more I was away from the thing I love. I thought: what’s going on here? I was addicted to moviemaking and I needed rehab. So I had to get away from it.”‘We used a beachball as an alien!’ John Carpenter on his gloriously shonky sci-fi comedy Dark StarRead moreSince then, he has toured the world playing his film music, with his son Cody and his godson, Daniel Davies, as well as recording new material. He has no regrets. “I’ve had great experiences with actors. I loved working with Sam Neill [who led Carpenter’s 1994 mind-bender In The Mouth of Madness], Jeff Bridges, Kurt, and my leading ladies have been great. I love them all. I’ve had a fantastic time. Now I’m playing music. I mean, it doesn’t get better than this. It really doesn’t.”Most of his days are spent at a leisurely pace, he says. “Basketball, the news and video games. That’s John’s entertainment. Then I go to have long, beautiful sleeps – and dream.”John Carpenter’s new album, Cathedral, is released on 7 August on Sacred Bones Records. His first graphic novel is released on 4 August4 via Storm King ComicsDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsJohn CarpenterThe big interviewHorror (TV)US politicsinterviewsShareReuse this content

title: The Odyssey storms global box office with $264m opening | The Odyssey | The Guardian
description: Epic film marks Christopher Nolan’s best opening since 2012’s The Dark Knight Rises when adjusted for inflation
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Epic film marks Christopher Nolan’s best opening since 2012’s The Dark Knight Rises when adjusted for inflation
Content: Christopher Nolan’s film The Odyssey reportedly cost US$250m to make. In its opening weekend, it made $264m at the box office worldwide.Photograph: Album/AlamyView image in fullscreenChristopher Nolan’s film The Odyssey reportedly cost US$250m to make. In its opening weekend, it made $264m at the box office worldwide.Photograph: Album/AlamyThe OdysseyThe Odyssey storms global box office with $264m openingEpic film marks Christopher Nolan’s best opening since 2012’s The Dark Knight Rises when adjusted for inflationPeter Bradshaw’s five-star reviewanda less enthused classicist’s takeWill women also enjoy Nolan’s Odyssey?Christopher Nolan interviewedWhy shooting on occupied land feels like a betrayalWhat Nolan left out – and put inGuardian staff and agenciesSun 19 Jul 2026 21.39 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Odyssey has stormed the global box office, taking US$264m (£196m, A$378m) in its opening weekend, according to studio Universal Pictures.Starring Matt Damon, Tom Holland, Anne Hathaway and Zendaya, the blockbuster is an adaptation of Homer’s ancient Greek epic poem, which follows Odysseus, the king of Ithaca, as he makes his perilous 10-year journey home after fighting in the Trojan war.Released on Friday, the adaptation scored an even bigger debut than Nolan’s 2023 blockbusterOppenheimer, which ended up making $975m at the global box office.The Odyssey review – Nolan goes god-tier with breathtaking epic of men, monsters and moral metamorphosisRead moreNolan’s adaptation of the 2,700-year-old poem reportedly cost a whopping $250m (£185m), with the film-maker saying he was only afforded the chance to make it after Oppenheimer’s financial and critical success.The Odyssey is Nolan’s best ever opening outside of the Batman franchise. His 2012 film The Dark Knight Risesopened globally with US$249m in 2012, or US$362m when adjusted for inflation.In North America alone, The Odyssey made $124.5m, giving it the third-largest opening of the year after two family films: Toy Story 5 ($159m) and The Super Mario Galaxy Movie ($131m), an impressive feat given The Odyssey’s R rating limits the audience that can see it.Universal Pictures initially projected a $100m opening weekend in North America. There, men made up 56% of the general audience and more than three-quarters of ticket buyers older than 25. Half of the film’s audience were aged between 18 and 34.The UK and Ireland took the top spot for the territory with the biggest international open – with £12.9m (US$17.4m) across three days.The Odyssey is the first feature movie to be filmed entirely with Imax cameras – a system of very heavy and loud cameras that capture a sharper, brighter and higher-resolution content.Screenings for the film were sold out at the BFI Imax cinema in London for the opening weekend, with fans arriving as early as midnight and 4am to watch the film.View image in fullscreenChristian Campbell, from Atlanta, Georgia attends the world-first screening of The Odyssey at the BFI Imax.Photograph: Christian Sinibaldi/The GuardianThe Odyssey was rolled out internationally across 73 markets over the weekend and launched across more than 12,800 locations and 26,000 screens around the world, including 440 Imax screens. In North America, “premium large formats”, which include Imax, accounted for 53% of box office.‘At times I felt I’d bitten off more than I could chew’: Christopher Nolan on sweeping the Oscars, making The Odyssey – and getting a puppyRead moreNolan recently told Today that it will be “at least” three years until he makes another movie, due to the challenges of The Odyssey’s shoot.“I’m so desperate to have a period I have nothing to do,”he told the Guardian. “It feels so long since I had a time like that. It’s true – I get bored very quickly and that’s one of the reasons I like to go back to work. I get restless. At the moment, all I can see is just trying to get through this, put the film out and then take a little break.”The Odyssey has been hailed as Nolan’s “best film to date” in a string of glowing reviews and also stars Robert Pattinson, Lupita Nyong’o, Samantha Morton and Charlize Theron.Press Association contributed to this reportExplore more on these topicsThe OdysseyChristopher NolannewsShareReuse this content

title: A Ruhr treat: can a giant pigeon and a pink basketball revive Germany’s depressed industrial heartland?  | Architecture | The Guardian
description: The Manifesta biennale is taking over a dozen tiny churches in German towns ravaged by division and decline – and filling them with ball games, bouncy castles and a radical community spirit
Author: Owen Hatherley
Headline: The Manifesta biennale is taking over a dozen tiny churches in German towns ravaged by division and decline – and filling them with ball games, bouncy castles and a radical community spirit
Content: Printed pigeon … Friend by artist Gülbin Ünlü at Thomaskirche.Photograph: Ivan ErofeevView image in fullscreenPrinted pigeon … Friend by artist Gülbin Ünlü at Thomaskirche.Photograph: Ivan ErofeevArchitectureA Ruhr treat: can a giant pigeon and a pink basketball revive Germany’s depressed industrial heartland?The Manifesta biennale is taking over a dozen tiny churches in German towns ravaged by division and decline – and filling them with ball games, bouncy castles and a radical community spiritOwen HatherleyMon 20 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.25 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfter Germany’s Ruhr conurbation – the third-largest urban area in western Europe – was pummelled during the second world war, planners came up with an ingenious idea. About 80% of the sprawl – which incorporates big cities such as Essen and Duisburg, and dozens of smaller towns like Bochum and Gelsenkirchen – had been destroyed. And so, for the reconstruction, a newly formed Diocese of the Ruhr built literally hundreds of closely packedPantoffelkirchenor “slipper churches”, so named because they were close enough to housing developments for churchgoers to walk to mass in their slippers.Today,Pantoffelkirchenare declining sharply. Estimates of the amount of disused or derelict churches are as high as 300, and 100 have been sold off in the last year alone. Until October, 12 of them are home to Manifesta, the travelling art biennale. They stand as centrepieces of communities that have been ravaged this time not by war, but by deindustrialisation, mass unemployment and environmental decline.Manifesta 16 Ruhr is not the Venice Biennale. Think more a Capital of Culture event held in the West Midlands – which the Ruhr closely resembles, as the stricken driver of national growth. Instead of exhibits in, say, Birmingham and Coventry, you’d be expected to travel to Bilston, Smethwick, Stourbridge. This is much easier to do in the Ruhr, where trams are ubiquitous and even tiny Bochum has a U-Bahn – though you’ll also find these networks have been neglected since they were built in the 1970s. At first, the cities and towns appear greener, sleeker and better connected than their British cousins, but this is a little deceptive. The A40 autobahn slices much of it into poorer and more affluent enclaves, seldom walkable one to the other.View image in fullscreenSurreal ...a basketball court where a miss triggers a blast of the church organ.Photograph: Rainer SchlautmannMuch of the Ruhr is today poorer than east Germany, after decades of deindustrialisation and the dismantlement of the welfare state. Gelsenkirchen has the highest vote for the far-right AfD in western Germany, and the highest rate of unemployment. So Manifesta goes into some very charged territory by taking over and trying to re-imagine the social infrastructure of what can seem a dying place, with divided communities and a dysfunctional economy. It avoids big, sexy industrial spaces, focusing instead on tiny derelict churches in suburbs and housing estates. “I’ll be honest: I don’t care about churches, I care about neighbourhoods,” says Catalan architect and Manifesta curator Josep Bohigas. “[The churches] were built for people who are no longer there.” The festival tries to use these apparently useless, if often beautiful, structures to bridge this gap between the past and the future.double quotation markThe churches were built for people who are no longer thereIt doesn’t all work. Some of the bigger empty churches contain some big empty art – an inflatable blue bubble containing a beach, comes to mind, mounted in the 19th century neo-gothic St Josef, Gelsenkirchen. Essen’s gothic St Gertrud, deconsecrated and now used by the local School of Fine Arts, is a distant cousin of Coventry Cathedral; the surviving ruins were patched up and joined on to a modern church in the 50s. Now, their polychrome expressionist-Byzantine murals face upturned pews, turned into abstract sculptures by Nasan Tur, an unsubtle gesture.The flagship is the Kulturkirche Liebfrauen, Duisburg, of 1958, which was turned into an arts centre in 2011: a flamboyant modernist building with the pulsating drama of Liverpool’s Catholic cathedral. Some installations here struggle to compete with the gorgeous murals and reliefs, made originally for the Vatican Pavilion at Expo 58, between luminescent walls of Plexiglas. But a fascinating two-screen film by Elizabeth Price works as an introduction to the biennale’s ideas, juxtaposing memories of second world war bombing with a history of those modernist Catholic churches built in postwar Britain for Irish immigrants.View image in fullscreenWeimar vision … Manifesta 16 artwork inside Bochum’s Christ-König.Photograph: Ivan ErofeevMostly, these churches were architecturally radical. One, Christ-König in Bochum, is a brick roar of Fritz Lang melodrama built in the last year of the Weimar Republic – but 10 of the churches are postwar. Hedwig Fijen, director of Manifesta Ruhr, describes them as places where architects and congregations tried “to imagine a different kind of society”, after “years of war, genocide and destruction”. But they did so within the confines of the old world. The new churches were keenly supported by the local industrial families, Thyssen and Krupp, beneficiaries of nazism who preferred their workers devout. The immigration of “guest workers” was encouraged; many of the miners and factory workers who rebuilt the conurbation and powered the “economic miracle” were from Catholic southernEurope, alongside Greece and, especially, Turkey.Their story is told here in the slipper churches, sparkling architectural miniatures that have clearly elicited the most thought on the part of the curators and artists. The first I saw, St Marien, Essen, was a harsh contrast with the tidy city-centre churches. Designed in 1963, with explosively exciting stained glass by Manfred Ott, it’s tough, red brick and semi-ruined, located in a working-class multicultural area opposite a retail park where the factories once stood. The church has been disused for many years and the artworks play with a bomb-site theme, all fragments and detritus. You realise the sheer range of architectural expression in the slipper churches when you go from there to Essen’s Protestant Markuskirche, of 1961, with its gorgeous wall of gauzy abstract stained glass. It is also tightly integrated with its community, next to a kindergarten and a care home. The major “artwork” here is a set of wooden seats and play areas by the Portuguese Pele Collective; when I visit, it feels more like a school fete than a ponderous art exhibition.View image in fullscreenPostwar planning … Kulturkirche Liebfrauen in Duisburg was turned into an arts centre in 2011.Photograph: Ivan ErofeevThe most powerful, convincing projects are less cute. Both are curated by Gürsoy Doğtaş, an art historian whose parents were Turkish “guest workers”. His team renamed two churches in Gelsenkirchen: St Bonifatius, as the Ferdane Satır Tea Garden, after one of seven Turkish guest workers murdered by fascists in an arson attack in Duisburg in 1984; and Thomaskirche, after Hava Güleç, a local factory worker considered a pillar of the community. St Bonifatius (1963) is in the hard, red brick expressionist tradition, and stands in a densely urban area along a main road. It has become effectively an art museum of the guest worker experience.At Thomaskirche, the crystalline space, overlooked by Friend, a massive printed pigeon by artist Gülbin Ünlü, includes Judith Raum’s film about the German-engineered Baghdad Railway, which works as prehistory: German industrialists make approving comments about docile Ottoman workers who don’t strike and don’t ask for pay rises; below, photographs document Turkish guest workers’ struggles, and strikes, in Germany 70 years later. Thomaskirche is mostly devoted to textiles, feminised factory labour in an area of old mills – there are public weaving workshops in the church hall. Impressively, the Gelsenkirchen churches are full of artbyworkers, rather than work about them. Many began as guest workers – Abuzer Güler, whose expressionist Anatolian scenes are hung on industrial frames in Thomaskirche, or Hanefi Yeter, whose When the Earth Burns, a Diego Rivera-like apocalyptic Ruhr allegory of 1984, hangs in St Bonifatius.View image in fullscreen‘Gorgeous gauzy abstract stained glass’ … more Manifesta artwork inside Markuskirche in Essen.Photograph: Silviu GuimanBut the Ruhr’s first boom, in the late 19th and early 20th century was significantly powered by Polish labour. In Bochum’s disused Gethsemane church – one of several “emergency churches” designed by the Weimar modernist Otto Bartning, built literally from rubble by the local community in 1947 – the Polish curator Krzysztof Kosciuczuk brings together more ironic, game-playing work on migration. Standing outside the church, in a sleepy area of modest houses and allotments, a bouncy castle in the shape of a church bell: designed by Belarusian artist Marina Naprushkina, it initially looks like a silly, friendly gesture, until you read the legend, in big letters: “We’ll Never Go Back!”All of this is, obviously, temporary. One work in a 1960s slipper church, Bochum’s tiny, angular St Ludgerus, has been turned into a basketball court. This isn’t new – in Gelsenkirchen, St Josef had one for a time – but what this one, designed by Catalan artists Cabosanroque does, is encourage ineptitude. If you miss the net, a big blast from the church organ is triggered. You can sit in “stands” of church pews and watch a bizarre scene unfold, as local youth and biennale-hoppers, lit by stained glass, try to miss their goals. It’s interesting precisely because of its unrepeatable surrealism and incongruity.These micro-modernist slipper churches were based on a consensus: their strident modernism was founded on beliefs their working-class congregants all held. In the absence of a similar unity, this montage of protest, whimsy and spatial experiment suggests diversity might just be able to create spaces that are equally extraordinary – if given the money and time, both of which are usually so lacking in places like the Ruhr.Manifesta 16 Ruhris on until 4 OctoberExplore more on these topicsArchitectureGermanyEuropefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: BBC defeats ITV again in World Cup final viewing figures | BBC | The Guardian
description: BBC’s average audience was 9.7m, four times as much as its rival – similar to its lead four years ago
Author: Michael Savage
Headline: BBC’s average audience was 9.7m, four times as much as its rival – similar to its lead four years ago
Content: Gabby Logan, Wayne Rooney, Joe Hart and Micah Richards in the New York New Jersey Stadium for the final.Photograph: BBCView image in fullscreenGabby Logan, Wayne Rooney, Joe Hart and Micah Richards in the New York New Jersey Stadium for the final.Photograph: BBCBBCBBC defeats ITV again in World Cup final viewing figuresBBC’s average audience was 9.7m, four times as much as its rival – similar to its lead four years agoMichael SavageMedia editorMon 20 Jul 2026 12.20 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.57 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheir respectiveWorld Cupstudios have been pored over, their lineup of pundits analysed almost as much as the matches. But when it came to the tournament’s final, the result was clear – audiences once again picked the BBC over ITV.Four times as many people turned to the BBC to watchSpain secure victoryover a belligerent Argentina, very similar to its lead over ITV four years ago.The BBC enjoyed an average audience of 9.7 million viewers, withITVrecording 2.4 million, according to the Digital i agency.The BBC’s main coverage, presented by Gabby Logan and featuring Alan Shearer, Micah Richards, Joe Hartand Wayne Rooney, peaked at 13.6 million viewers. Guy Mowbray took on commentary duties.ITV’s coverage, presented by Mark Pougatch and including Gary Neville, Ian Wright and Roy Keane, peaked at 3.4 million viewers. Sam Matterface was its commentator, alongside expert summariser Lee Dixon.It means ITV has not closed the gap with theBBCfor the tournament’s conclusion. Four years ago in Qatar, the BBC had an overnight average audience of 9.2 million, with ITV recording 2.8 million, according to Digital i.The result will reassure BBC bosses over their decision not to cover the tournament from the US until its very late stages. Its choice to use a new set in Salford was compared unfavourably to ITV’s Brooklyn studio.However, most in the television world believe it is simply the lack of adverts that gives the BBC a huge advantage over its rival.ITV has also been praised by TV insiders for its shrewd move in bringing in pundits Neville and Keane, ensuring it would benefit from the viewers attracted by the pair’s hit podcast Stick to Football.The BBC’s decision not to broadcast from the US is still a contentious one in the TV world, despite the popularity of its final coverage. “You have to show up for the World Cup,” said one senior figure in sports broadcasting.It will not be confronted with the same decision atEuro 2028in two years’ time. The tournament is being hosted in the UK and Ireland.The viewing figures come as huge cuts are taking place at the corporation, with more coming to BBC Sport later in the year. Part of the decision to host most of the World Cup coverage from a new UK studio was to ensure it could then be used for Match of the Day.All broadcasters have also been trying to adapt to the huge changes in consumption as audiences turn to phones and digital platforms for football content. The BBC’s new football YouTube channel generated 100m views. Across social media, BBC clips, interviews and analysis attracted 2.55bn views.“Our ambition was to be there for audiences every step of the way, not just during the matches, but every hour in between,” said Alex Kay-Jelski, the director of BBC Sport.“We wanted to tell the stories behind the tournament, bring fans closer to the biggest moments and innovate in ways that made the experience richer than ever before. The response has been extraordinary.”Explore more on these topicsBBCITVWorld Cup 2026BBC OneITV1Television industryWorld CupnewsShareReuse this content

title: Avengers: Doomsday trailer reveals star-packed Marvel adventure | Avengers: Doomsday | The Guardian
description: The first full look at the big-budget December tentpole features returning heroes such as Chris Hemsworth, Patrick Stewart, Florence Pugh and Chris Evans
Author: Benjamin Lee
Headline: The first full look at the big-budget December tentpole features returning heroes such as Chris Hemsworth, Patrick Stewart, Florence Pugh and Chris Evans
Content: Chris Hemsworth in Avengers: Doomsday.Photograph: YouTubeView image in fullscreenChris Hemsworth in Avengers: Doomsday.Photograph: YouTubeAvengers: DoomsdayAvengers: Doomsday trailer reveals star-packed Marvel adventureThe first full look at the big-budget December tentpole features returning heroes such as Chris Hemsworth, Patrick Stewart, Florence Pugh and Chris EvansBenjamin LeeMon 20 Jul 2026 10.17 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 10.24 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe first full trailer forAvengers: Doomsdayhas been released online offering a look at Marvel’s big-budget big hope.“Something’s coming, something we may not be able to deter,” is the first line heard in the new preview which proceeds to bring back multiple characters from variousMarvelmovies and universes.How Marvel deals with Doctor Doom is make or break for the MCU. No one wants a watered-down Tony StarkRead moreReturning Avengers featured in the trailer include Chris Hemsworth as Thor, Paul Rudd as Ant-Man and Chris Evans as Captain America while Fantastic Four’s Pedro Pascal, X-Men’s Patrick Stewart, Black Panther’s Letitia Wright and Thunderbolts’ Florence Pugh also appear.There’s also a glimpse of Ian McKellen, returning as Magneto, whorecentlyspoke about the film at the Piazza festival in Rome. The actor told the audience that “they got me at one point to destroy New Jersey”. He added that directing duo the Russo brothers “told me to look more furious: make it look as if you hate what you’re destroying. So I stood there and I shouted: ‘Mar-a-Lago!’”The trailer teases an “unthinkable decision” as well as the arrival of Robert Downey Jr’s Doctor Doom. The actor, who previously played Iron Man, is returning to play the famed villain in a move that isreportedlyearning the actor an estimated $100m for both Doomsday and its follow-up Secret Wars.Returning co-director Joe Russo hassaidthey are “back to phase zero” of the MCU with the new film. “This is starting over from scratch,” he said. “We want to make sure everybody feels like this isn’t leaning on anything from the past.”The film arrives at a difficult time for Marvel, once box office champions but now struggling to return to former glories. The last few years have seen adventures such as Thunderbolts, Fantastic Four: First Steps and Captain America: Brave New World achieving mediocre results.Toronto film festival: Helen Mirren, Chris Rock and Seth Rogen lead lineupRead moreBut in 2019, Avengers: Endgame became one of the highest-grossing films of all time with a box office total of almost $2.8bn.The Guardian’s Ben Childrecentlywrote: “the success or failure of this next superhero extravaganza might well decide the future of the entire franchise.”Avengers: Doomsday is releasing on 18 December, the same date as Dune: Part Three leading box office pundits to predict a Barbenheimer-style showdown.Explore more on these topicsAvengers: DoomsdayMarvelSuperhero moviesRobert Downey JrnewsShareReuse this content

title: Toronto film festival: Helen Mirren, Chris Rock and Seth Rogen lead lineup | Toronto film festival | The Guardian
description: The 51st edition of the Canadian festival will also feature world premieres starring Taron Egerton, Amanda Seyfried and Chase Infiniti
Author: Benjamin Lee
Headline: The 51st edition of the Canadian festival will also feature world premieres starring Taron Egerton, Amanda Seyfried and Chase Infiniti
Content: Helen Mirren will play Patricia Highsmith in A Talent for Murder.Photograph: John Nacion/Variety/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenHelen Mirren will play Patricia Highsmith in A Talent for Murder.Photograph: John Nacion/Variety/Getty ImagesToronto film festivalToronto film festival: Helen Mirren, Chris Rock and Seth Rogen lead lineupThe 51st edition of the Canadian festival will also feature world premieres starring Taron Egerton, Amanda Seyfried and Chase InfinitiBenjamin LeeMon 20 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 13.33 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWorld premieres of films led by Helen Mirren, Seth Rogen and Chris Rock lead the lineup for this year’sToronto film festival.The September festival, taking place after the Venice film festival, has previously seen the premieres of Oscar-winning movies such as Green Book, The Eyes of Tammy Faye, Still Alice, Dallas Buyers Club and Silver Linings Playbook.Mirren will play Patricia Highsmith in A Talent for Murder, a twisty thriller starring recent Tony-winner Alden Ehrenreich as a literary agent who is sent to Switzerland to persuade the author to write one more Ripley novel. The film is directed by Anton Corbijn, whose previous credits include Control and A Most Wanted Man.View image in fullscreenA still from Babies.Rogen, who was at last year’s Toronto with Aziz Ansari’sGood Fortune, will star alongside Anna Kendrick in Babies, a comedy drama written and directed by his wife, Lauren Miller Rogen. The film, which also stars Issa Rae, Kate Berlant and Sharon Stone, centres on a childless couple who are forced into parenting when their divorced friend moves in with her three-year-old.Rock will return to the festival with his latest film as writer-director, the Hollywood-set comedy Misty Green. The star, whose last effortTop Fivealso premiered at Toronto, also leads the A24 film which focuses on a director who offers a comeback opportunity to an actor whose career has been derailed by her vices. The starry cast also includes Adam Driver, Daniel Kaluuya, Kendrick, and the British actor Rosalind Eleazar in the lead role.The festival will also see the world premiere of I Play Rocky, a drama about the making of Sylvester Stallone’s Oscar-winning triumph. Directed by Peter Farrelly, whose 60s-set drama Green Book went from a Toronto premiere to a best picture Oscar win, the film stars Anthony Ippolito as Stallone.Courteney Cox’s Evil Genius will also make its bow at the festival. The crime thriller, which marks the actor’s second film as director after Talhotblond, is based ona true crime docuseriesof the same name that tells the story of the so-called “pizza bomber” Brian Wells. The cast includes Patricia Arquette and David Harbour.“It’s stranger than fiction,” Cox has said of the project. “At moments darkly funny and yet deeply emotional. A story about love, loneliness, manipulation and the people on the fringes who get pulled into something much bigger than themselves.”View image in fullscreenA still from Girl Group.Photograph: Toronto film festivalAfter legal issues affected the release of her first film as director, Rebel Wilson’s follow-up Girl Group will also premiere at Toronto. The film stars Wilson as a singer kicked off her own’s group’s reunion tour who then stages a comeback by attaching herself to a younger teen group. The cast also includes Jennifer Coolidge, Sheridan Smith, Melanie C andNicole Scherzinger.The Shakespeare in Love director John Madden will also head to Toronto with the drama Onwards and Sideways, a film starringLaura Linneyand Rhys Ifans as two people who receive a Parkinson’s diagnosis on the same day.Other premieres include the comedy thriller Everybody Wants to Fuck Me starring Taron Egerton about the world of modern dating, the prison-set drama The Life and Deaths of Wilson Shedd starring Amanda Seyfried, supernatural drama Faith starring James McAvoy and Erin Doherty, Takashi Miike’s Bad Lieutenant: Tokyo with Lily James, a coming-of-age drama called The Julia Set starring One Battle After Another breakout Chase Infiniti, the Joy Luck Club director Wayne Wang’s drama Diary of a Mad Old Man, and Up Against It, a Trudie Styler-directed romcom led byAntonio Banderas.View image in fullscreenA still of Cynthia Erivo from Prima Facie.Photograph: Nicola Dove / Toronto film festivalPreviously announced premieres include the romance Love of Your Life starringMargaret Qualley, Patrick Schwarzenegger and Aaron Pierre; a big-screen adaptation of the hit stage drama Prima Facie starring Cynthia Erivo; revenge thriller Your Mother Your Mother Your Mother led by Mahershala Ali; and Being Heumann, the new film from the Coda director, Sian Heder.Last year’s Toronto saw the premiere of the hit horror Obsession which has since become the highest-grossing festival acquisition of all time after it made $442m worldwide.This year’s festival takes place between 10 and 20 September.Explore more on these topicsToronto film festivalHelen MirrenTaron EgertonFestivalsChris RockSeth RogenAnna KendricknewsShareReuse this content

title: BBC broadcaster Edward Stourton dies aged 68 | BBC | The Guardian
description: Corporation pays tribute to ‘outstanding’ journalist who presented shows including Today and One O’Clock News
Author: Kevin Rawlinson
Headline: Corporation pays tribute to ‘outstanding’ journalist who presented shows including Today and One O’Clock News
Content: Edward Stourton ‘leaves a huge gap’, the BBC said.Photograph: David Hartley/Rex FeaturesView image in fullscreenEdward Stourton ‘leaves a huge gap’, the BBC said.Photograph: David Hartley/Rex FeaturesBBCBBC broadcaster Edward Stourton dies aged 68Corporation pays tribute to ‘outstanding’ journalist who presented shows including Today and One O’Clock NewsKevin RawlinsonMon 20 Jul 2026 04.37 EDTFirst published on Mon 20 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe journalist Edward Stourton has died aged 68, it has been announced.The BBCRadio 4presenter was best known for fronting current affairs programmes including Today, World at One and its religious and ethical current affairs series Sunday.He died on Saturday surrounded by his family after being diagnosed with prostate cancer in 2014, theBBCsaid in a statement.Former colleagues paid tribute to a man they called an outstanding broadcaster.“It’s far too soon to lose Ed,” said Jonathan Munro, who runs BBC News and Current Affairs. “I worked as his producer when he was ITN’s diplomatic editor – unflappable then and ever after. Many years later, I was listening to his superb analysis of the new pope for the BBC – another of his many areas of expertise.“Not many broadcasters can present Today, BBC One’s One O’Clock News, The World at One and Sunday, but he did. He leaves a huge gap.”Mohit Bakaya, the controller of BBC Radio 4, said Stourton was an “absolutely outstanding broadcaster”. He added: “He brought intelligence, authority and an extraordinary hinterland to everything he did. When Ed was behind the mic, whether on Sunday or the various news programmes he regularly presented for Radio 4, you felt you understood the world that little bit better. Our thoughts are with his family and friends at this sad time.”Stourton had a distinguished career in broadcasting, working for the BBC and ITN, which produces Channel 4 News. He spent nearly a decade presenting Radio 4’s flagship Today programme, and also presented the One O’Clock News and Any Questions, as well as Radio 4’s Sunday and BBC One’s Panorama.View image in fullscreenStourton (left) with James Naughtie behind the scenes on the Today programme.Photograph: Martin Argles/The GuardianHe joined ITN from university in 1979 and became a founding member of Channel 4 News three years later. Starting out as a scriptwriter, he went on to cover foreign news, and became the programme’s first Washington correspondent in 1986, reporting on the final years of the Reagan presidency.In 1988 he started a two-year stint as the BBC’s Paris correspondent, which ended when he rejoined ITN as diplomatic editor. He reported from Baghdad during the Gulf war, Bosnia during the siege of Sarajevo and from Moscow in the final days of the Soviet Union.He returned to the BBC in 1993 and presented the One O’Clock News for six years. He also presented editions of Assignment during his time with the broadcaster and had his own Radio 4 phone-in programme, CallEd Stourton. He joined the Today programme in 1999 and, having presented it, later became a regular contributor.From 2009 Stourton began presenting The World at One and The World This Weekend.He was a Roman Catholic and hosted Radio 4’s Sunday and BBC One’s The Heaven and Earth Show, as well as documentaries such as Absolute Truth, a four-part BBC Two series about the Catholic church.He was born in Nigeria, where his father was based, in 1957, and was raised in the UK. Stourton studied English literature at the University of Cambridge. He is survived by his wife, Fiona Stourton, and four children.In his 2023 memoir Confessions, Stourton revealed he had been diagnosed with prostate cancer nearly a decade earlier. Amy Rylance, the director of health services, equity and improvement at Prostate Cancer UK, said: “We’re grateful to him for speaking openly about his diagnosis in his memoir, and in doing so raising lifesaving awareness with men across the country.“Prostate cancer often doesn’t have symptoms at the early stages, but the earlier you find it, the easier it is to treat. That’s why it’s so important for men to be aware of the disease, to know their own risk and to know what they can choose to do about it. You can find out right now by using Prostate Cancer UK’s 30-second online risk checker.”Explore more on these topicsBBCEd StourtonThe news on TVTelevisionRadio 4Radio industrynewsShareReuse this content

title: Top 30: from half a million World Cup photos, why I love these the most | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: Our picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral, that delicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in Gaza
Author: Jonny Weeks
Headline: Our picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral, that delicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in Gaza
Content: Harry Kane is embraced by Jude Bellingham after his first goal against DR Congo.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenHarry Kane is embraced by Jude Bellingham after his first goal against DR Congo.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Top 30: from half a million World Cup photos, why I love these the mostOur picture editor chooses his favourite images from the tournament including Lionel Messi’s cathedral,thatdelicious diving header from Jude Bellingham and a humbling scene in GazaJonny WeeksMon 20 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.34 EDTShareWhat makes a greatWorld Cupphotograph? Now there’s a question with myriad answers. Composition, newsworthiness, emotional resonance, expert timing and luck can all contribute to varying degrees. But one thing they all share is the power to capture attention.On the Guardian sport picture desk, we have received more than 500,000 World Cup photographs over the past six weeks, with so many gems among them. Here, with much applause to the photographers who created them and a slight bias towardsEngland, are my favourite images of the tournament.1Messi’s cathedralView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi prepares to take a corner kick against Switzerland at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Julian Finney/Fifa/Getty ImagesAn absolute cracker to start with. In this picture, the cavernous Kansas City Stadium sweeps around a small corner of grass on which the world’s most famous footballer stands alone. You can’t really see it’s Lionel Messi, but somehow you can tell it’s him. It feels as though everybody’s eyes are pointed in his direction, like they’re worshipping a deity in an epic footballing cathedral.2Goalline scrambleView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Endrick flings his boot at a loose ball as Japan’s players scramble to clear their lines.Photograph: Annegret Hilse/ReutersTechnological improvements have led to the creation of high-quality miniature cameras which can be placed inside goalmouths and operated remotely. Seeing the game at such close quarters feels wonderfully dynamic. This frame in particular captures a mad scramble between Brazil and Japan; the wonky angle adds to the sense of chaos.3Tuchel’s close upView image in fullscreenEngland’s manager, Thomas Tuchel, during the national anthem before the match with Croatia at Dallas Stadium.Photograph: Bradley Collyer/PAA self-referential choice, but this was a big news story at the time: England played Croatia in their first game and the England manager, Thomas Tuchel, was so furious he couldn’t see the players during the national anthem that he asked Fifa to move the photographers for subsequent games. In fairness to him, you can see why. Four weeks later, Tuchel would garner headlines again after his substitutions in the semi-final againstArgentinatriggered England’s collapse.4Balogun sees redView image in fullscreenThe USA’s Folarin Balogun fouls Tarik Muharemovic of Bosnia and Herzegovina, leading to a red card after a VAR check.Photograph: Michael Steele/Getty ImagesAt first glance it looks innocuous enough, then you notice Tarik Muharemovic’s right ankle buckling under the pressure applied by Folarin Balogun’s mistimed challenge. Replays of the incident suggested the foul was unintentional, though the Bosnian was lucky to escape injury. Balogun received a red card following a VAR review, but his one-game ban was suspended by Fifa after lobbying from Donald Trump. With Balogun back in the team, the USA were dumped out of their own tournament by Belgium in the next round.5Tartan traumaView image in fullscreenScotland’s Findlay Curtis (left) and Jack Hendry in the tunnel after their defeat by Brazil in Miami.Photograph: Carmen Mandato/Fifa/Getty ImagesI’ve relished seeing so many photographsfrom inside the tunnels at this World Cupbecause the players seem to reveal a little more of themselves away from the public eye. This candid shot of Scotland players Findlay Curtis and Jack Hendry shows them in the wake of their 3-0 loss to Brazil – a defeat that was filled with calamity, and which ultimately led to Scotland’s early exit. They look like they’re outside a head teacher’s office waiting to be reprimanded.6Diving headerView image in fullscreenEngland’s midfielder Jude Bellingham scores his team’s first goal against Mexico at the Azteca.Photograph: Ulises Ruiz/AFP/Getty ImagesI love a good diving header and this one by Jude Bellingham against Mexico was peachy. From a photographic point of view, you seldom get shots from tribune positions like this during a Premier League season, so they have a real novelty value during major tournaments. But it’s more than just novelty: the elevated vantage point helps to remove the surrounding clutter and give a proper fan’s eye view of the goal. I also love the verdant grass, the shocking pink goalkeeper’s jersey and the sense of anticipation because the ball has yet to cross the line. It reminds me of the signed photograph of Coventry’s Keith Houchen, from the 1987 FA Cup final, that hangs proudly on my dad’s office wall.7Bruno’s early bathView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Bruno Guimarães cools down with a splash of water in the first half against Morocco in New Jersey.Photograph: Adam Hunger/APSo many hydration breaks, so much Powerade, but only one player managed to look like a Portuguese water dog fresh from a dip in a lake. The Brazil midfielder Bruno Guimarães is pictured here dousing himself in H2O when his side faced Morocco in their opening game at New York New Jersey Stadium. Brazil ultimately went home for an early bath after losing to Norway in the last 16.8Viking row boatView image in fullscreenNorway supporters gather to perform their rowing celebration on the iconic red steps in Times Square.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesThe mass rowing displays performed by Norway’s fans this summer have been a viral sensation. Seen everywhere from Times Square to Miami Beach, they also became a fixture of the team’s post-match partying, with Erling Haaland and Martin Ødegaard both banging a fan’s drum as the side progressed to the quarter-finals.
