The Vikings were stopped by England, but their journey was one of the feelgood stories of the tournament.9Light and shadeView image in fullscreenKylian Mbappé of France dribbles the ball against Sweden at New York New Jersey Stadium.Photograph: Justin Setterfield/Getty ImagesA gorgeous pool of afternoon sunlight spilling on to the pitch, surrounded by deep shadows. You couldn’t really create better low-key lighting conditions if you tried. The photographer just had to nail the exposure and wait for a subject to pass through. The fact that it’s Kylian Mbappé who’s skating across the turf in his rich blue France top adds a nice splash of colour and gravity to the scene.10Tunnel visionView image in fullscreenLuka Modric of Croatia prepares in the tunnel before their match with Panama in Toronto.Photograph: Joosep Martinson/Fifa/Getty ImagesLuka Modric has spoken in the past aboutnot wanting to let his people down, and of carrying memories of his grandpa, Luka, with him. He appears to be deep in thought here before playing Panama. Perhaps it’s just a fleeting moment – a man simply massaging his temple or visualising the first tackle – or maybe he’s remembering his war-torn roots and the family member he was named after.11Loving VozinhaView image in fullscreenCape Verde’s Vozinha after the match against Spain in Atlanta.Photograph: Brett Davis/Imagn Images/ReutersAt the ripe age of 40 and with a club career that has taken him to Cyprus, Moldova, Portugal and Slovakia, the Cape Verde goalkeeper Vozinha became the breakout star at this year’s World Cup. His numerous saves againstSpainin the group stage earned him global praise as well as 17 million followers on Instagram. Another stellar display against Argentina, in which he memorably denied a quick free-kick by Messi, further enhanced his reputation. I like his expression in this photograph as he soaks up the adulation from the crowd following the Blue Sharks’ remarkable goalless draw with Spain.12Gaza screeningView image in fullscreenPalestinian football fans gather among war-damaged buildings in Gaza City to watch the match between Argentina and Egypt on a giant outdoor screen.Photograph: Mohammed Skaik/TheNews2/Cover ImagesThis photograph shows the power of football while also putting the World Cup into perspective. Palestinian residents in Gaza gathered amid the ruins to watch a screening of Egypt’s game against Argentina. A moment of normality amid the horror and heartache.13Power headerView image in fullscreenErling Haaland of Norway scores against Brazil at New York New Jersey Stadium.Photograph: Image Photo Agency/Getty ImagesErling Haaland’s first goal against Brazil left his old Premier League rival, Gabriel Magalhães, in a heap. In this shot you can see water (or perhaps sweat) flying off the striker’s forehead, his neck fully strained after connecting with the ball. Haaland scored seven goals in five World Cup games: he netted from inside the box, outside the box, with his left foot, right foot and of course his head. Norway have still never been beaten by Brazil after five matches. No other nation can say that.14Lamine Yamal’s bracesView image in fullscreenLamine Yamal bites the Spain badge during an official Fifa photocall prior to the tournament.Photograph: Karl Bridgeman/Fifa/Getty ImagesThis image was shot before the tournament,as part of Fifa’s official player portraits, but I’ve only recently seen it – and it’s well worth a mention. You don’t see many players with braces, but Lamine Yamal, who only turned 19 this month, wears his with pride. This frame was shot using a 24-70mm lens on a Canon R5 Mark II which has a whopping 45 megapixels, so I suspect it’s been cropped quite hard in post-production to achieve this composition.15Into orbitView image in fullscreenSwitzerland’s Manuel Akanji misses a penalty during the shootout against Colombia.Photograph: Agustín Marcarian/ReutersSome penalty misses are worse than others. Germany’s Jonathan Tah and Manuel Akanji of Switzerland both had Chris Waddle moments as they blew their chances from 12 yards over the bar. Ordinarily I’d say you need to see the crossbar to illustrate that the shot went over, but the way the ball is rocketing skywards – almost as if it could escape the stadium roof – tells the story perfectly.16Neymar’s tearsView image in fullscreenBrazil’s Neymar is consoled by Raphinha after their side were eliminated from the World Cup by Norway.Photograph: James Lang/Imagn Images/ReutersEven when your ego is as big as the sun, sometimes you just need a cuddle. This photograph captures Neymar Jr’s visible distress as he realises his international career is over and his childhood ambitions of lifting the World Cup will never be fulfilled. His teammate Raphina is there to support him. It lacks the humility ofFreddie Flintoff consoling his opponent Brett Leein the Ashes, or thestoicism of Derek Redmond being helped across the finish lineby his father at the 1992 Olympics, but it still packs an emotional gut punch.17Upside downView image in fullscreenJulián Alvarez with teammates after scoring Argentina’s second goal against Switzerland at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Ryan Pierse/Fifa/Getty ImagesThis photograph was flipped 180 degrees by Getty Images before being transmitted to us. Manipulation of this ilk feels almost heretical in the world of photojournalism, but the result is very compelling. It shows Julián Alvarez celebrating his late winner against Switzerland, and it makes me question my fundamental photographic principles. Would I publish it like this in print to illustrate a news story? Absolutely not. Would I include it in an online gallery of images that caught my eye this summer? Evidently so.18Big screenView image in fullscreenA giant video screen signals a goal for Spain against Saudi Arabia in Atlanta.Photograph: Colin Hubbard/APThe stadums in the US have been mind-blowing in scale. Atlanta Stadium, home of the NFL’s Atlanta Falcons and the city’s MLS and NWSL teams, has a maximum capacity of 75,000 people, a climate-controlled environment and a 335-metre display known as the “halo”. This picture from up in the gods looks suitably epic. Initially I assumed the camera was remote-controlled, but it transpires some photographers were actually working up there on the gantry. What a view they had!19Motion blurView image in fullscreenSweden winger Anthony Elanga is challenged by two Japan defenders during their game at Dallas Stadium.Photograph: Lars Baron/Getty ImagesThis is a beautiful example of a photographer using a slow shutter speed while panning gently with the action. I especially like the details within: Anthony Elanga’s facial expression is crisp, and the pink boots leave a painterly trail.20Heroic HarryView image in fullscreenHarry Kane and Jude Bellingham of England celebrate against DR Congo at Atlanta Stadium.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesHarry Kane’s late strikes against the Democratic Republic of Congo in the last 32 led to an outpouring of emotion from the England captain. I was torn over which frame to select – there are some lovely ones of him sprinting across the turf, flanked by Jude Bellingham and Anthony Gordon – but this one of him being embraced by Bellingham serves as a perfect reminder of their twin heroics at the tournament. It’s clean, nicely balanced and full of bromance.21Gómez goes berserkView image in fullscreenGustavo Gómez of Paraguay looks delighted after José Canale scored the winning penalty in a  shootout against Germany in Boston.Photograph: Robert Cianflone/Getty ImagesThe guaranteed agony and ecstasy ofpenalty shootouts always makes for brilliant images. This year, Gustavo Gómez of Paraguay takes the crown for the wildest reaction. After beating Germany during sudden death, Gómez, who scored his own spot-kick, surged across the pitch like a heron taking flight, almost vaulting his teammates José Canale and Orlando Gill as he went to embrace them. He then careered through the photographers behind the goal in pursuit of a mobile phone so that he could document the moment himself.22Living statueView image in fullscreenMichel Nkuka Mboladinga, also known as Lumumba Vea, holds firm in the stands during their match against Colombia.Photograph: Daniel Becerril/ReutersMichel Nkuka Mboladinga, also known as Lumumba Vea, is dubbed a “living statue” because he doesn’t move during games. Instead, the DRC fan poses on a plinth for the entire match, raising his right arm to mimic the statue ofPatrice Lumumba, the first prime minister of the First Congolese Republic, which stands in Kinshasa. Why anyone would go to a football match and not respond to the on-pitch action is beyond me, but Mboladinga insists it’s his patriotic duty.23Senegal swan diveView image in fullscreenYouri Tielemans heads in Belgium’s second goal against Senegal at Seattle Stadium.Photograph: Jared C Tilton/Fifa/Getty ImagesYouri Tielemans scored twice inBelgium’s stunning comeback against Senegalin the last 32. This 89th-minute header might have been a little trickier to control had the defender Moussa Niakhaté competed for the ball instead of diving theatrically to the ground at the first sign of contact. The virtue of modern cameras, many of which shoot 30 frames per second, is that the full sequence of this goal was captured, and we could choose the exact frame when Tielemans’s head connects with the ball.24Comeback kingView image in fullscreenSasa Kalajdzic after scoring Austria’s third goal during a thrilling game against Algeria at Kansas City Stadium.Photograph: Alex Pantling/Fifa/Getty ImagesSasa Kalajdzic overcame three anterior cruciate ligament injuries in the years and months prior to the 2026 World Cup, but the Austrian enjoyed a moment to savour when he netted a 96th-minute equaliser against Algeria to keep his side in the tournament. The game ignited accusations of fixing, because a 3-3 draw ensured both sides went through, but a finale like that – and celebrations like this – cannot be scripted.25Football, bloody hellView image in fullscreenConnor Metcalfe of Australia shows the referee his bloodied forehead against Paraguay at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium.Photograph: Stu Forster/Getty ImagesAustralia’s Connor Metcalfe needed three stitches in his eyebrow after being clipped by a stray boot from Andrés Cubas during the Socceroos’ group match with Paraguay. Maybe it’s the slumped shoulders or the shirt pulled up around his neckline, but he looks like a forlorn child who needs a parent to clean him up.26Big Dan BurnView image in fullscreenEngland’s Dan Burn blocks a bicycle kick from Raúl Jiménez during the last-16 match in Mexico City.Photograph: Jam Media/Getty ImagesYou could say this is just another “two men and a ball” photograph (an expression the Guardian’s photographer, Tom Jenkins, uses to describe generic images of sporting action). But I particularly like this one because of the contrast of sporting prowess – the acrobat versus the brick wall – and for the misshapen ball which appears ready to pop. Dan Burn has become a cult figure for England, in the mould of Peter Crouch. Many supporters call the 6ft 7in defender Big Dan Burn, but Bellingham apparently calls him Burninho. There’s no way Raúl Jiménez, the Mexico striker, was ever getting the ball past him.27Emotional MessiView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Lionel Messi after their 3-2 victory over Egypt at Atlanta Stadium.Photograph: Patrick Smith/Fifa/Getty ImagesLet’s be honest, Lionel Messi is not very good at penalties. He’s missed four of eight in World Cup matches and 34 of 151 in his career, including shootouts. A botched effort against Egypt, in a match which the African side went on to lead 2-0, could have been a pitiful ending to his gilded international career. Instead, Messi scored the equaliser and drove his team to a remarkable 3-2 win in normal time. This picture, taken after the match, speaks to the weight he carries on his shoulders. Is it relief or guilt? Either way, he looks tortured by expectation.28Crowd sceneView image in fullscreenCanada fans take in their game against Bosnia and Herzegovina at Toronto Stadium.Photograph: Joosep Martinson/Fifa/Getty ImagesThe composition of this photograph is simple but beautiful. The intersecting diagonals, the contrast between light and shade, and the depth within the scene … it makes for rich viewing.29Koné’s injury painView image in fullscreenCanada’s Ismaël Koné on the ground after sustaining a broken leg against Qatar.Photograph: Lee Smith/ReutersIsmaël Koné’s eyes say it all. The Canada midfielder had just broken the tibia and fibula bones in his lower left leg after a challenge by Qatar’s midfielder Assim Madibo, who would receive a red card and a five-match ban. As a photo editor, when incidents like this occur you have to weigh up decency and news value before publishing (I still feel traumatised by the uncensored images I saw of Eduardo’s broken leg in 2008). I think this image conveys the severity of the incident without being unduly graphic. Koné left the pitch on a stretcher but waved to fans as he exited.30Cubarsi’s cartwheelView image in fullscreenArgentina’s Enzo Fernández fouls Spain’s Pau Cubarsi and is later shown a second yellow card leading to a red.Photograph: Maddie Meyer/Fifa/Getty ImagesI’d typically include a celebration photograph from the World Cup final among my favourites from a tournament, but this ‘tackle’ by Enzo Fernández was so bad – and the image of Pau Cubarsi flying through the air so striking – that I couldn’t look beyond it when reviewing the match. Cubarsi looks like he’s falling from space. An absolutely dreadful final sparked into life at this defining moment. Cubarsi rose from the turf to claim the title of ‘best young player’ of the tournament and, more importantly, a World Cup winner’s medal.

title: TV tonight: a serial killer’s sick, twisted game in The Dark | Television | The Guardian
description: Laura Donnelly’s DI Kennedy thinks she has guessed the next victim but can she save him? Plus, sex, chicks and ostriches in Chris Packham’s Evolution
Author: Hollie Richardson
Headline: Laura Donnelly’s DI Kennedy thinks she has guessed the next victim but can she save him? Plus, sex, chicks and ostriches in Chris Packham’s Evolution
Content: Deeper into the woods … Laura Donnelly as DI Monica Kennedy in The Dark.Photograph: Mark Mainz/ITVView image in fullscreenDeeper into the woods … Laura Donnelly as DI Monica Kennedy in The Dark.Photograph: Mark Mainz/ITVTV tonightTelevisionTV tonight: a serial killer’s sick, twisted game in The DarkLaura Donnelly’s DI Kennedy thinks she has guessed the next victim but can she save him? Plus, sex, chicks and ostriches in Chris Packham’s EvolutionHollie RichardsonPhil Harrison,Graeme Virtue,Jack SealeandPriya ElanMon 20 Jul 2026 01.15 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Dark9pm, ITV1With the serial killer taking their third victim in the last cliffhanger, it’s been a rough week at work for DI Kennedy (Laura Donnelly) in this sinister detective drama. Local men in their teens and 20s are the targets, and Kennedy thinks she knows who the next one will be – but will she save him? Or is she just playing into the killer’s sick, twisted game? And what about her own worrying home dilemma with her daughter?Hollie RichardsonThere’s a Gangster in My Flat: Panorama8pm, BBC OneThis week’s investigation presupposes that viewers can find an iota of sympathy in their hearts for buy-to-let landlords. It explores the plight of flat owners who, despite paying agencies to do tenancy checks, end up with criminals and gang members in their properties. A presumably unpleasant but probably quite niche problem.Phil HarrisonUniversity Challenge8.30pm, BBC TwoThe no-nonsense quiz where overconfident undergraduates can make fools of themselves is still in the early days of its 33rd series. But this first-round heat offers a chance to gauge the current form of defending champions Manchester. They take on 2023 winners Durham, with Amol Rajan presiding.Graeme VirtueHouse of the Dragon9pm, Sky AtlanticAfter two episodes concentrating more on political machinations than the armed conflicts that define life in Westeros, the Game of Thrones prequel is ready to burst back into bloody, fiery life. Possession of the Iron Throne has been a tainted blessing for Rhaenyra, but she wants to keep it. However, more than one branch of her tangled family tree violently disagrees.Jack SealeEvolution9pm, BBC TwoView image in fullscreenOstrich chicks in South Africa …Evolution.Photograph: BBC Studios/Will EdwardsThere’s lots of sex in this episode of Chris Packham’s revelatory series about life’s origins. Don’t expect raunch, though; instead, there’s a detailed explanation of why it was so difficult for trilobites to get busy. Packham’s ultimate destination is the massive eggs produced by ostriches but, as ever, he takes a fascinatingly scenic route.PHSecret Lives of Gypsy Wives10pm, Channel 4“We’re like the Kardashians but if they were fat, broke and on benefits.” So says Gypsy community influencer Trewley Precious of herself and her sisters in this new reality series. They’re part of a new wave of glamorous, Juicy Couture-loving, so-called “Gypsy girls” who are breaking free of the older generation’s strict rules.Priya ElanExplore more on these topicsTelevisionTV tonightChris PackhamTV crime dramaDocumentaryfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: A Sudden Flicker of Light by David Thomson review – a brilliantly eccentric history of the movies | Film books | The Guardian
description: From the golden age of Hollywood to harder times today, this is a rapturous account of more than a century of cinema
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: From the golden age of Hollywood to harder times today, this is a rapturous account of more than a century of cinema
Content: Tom Ewell and Marilyn Monroe in The Seven Year Itch (1955).Photograph: Cinematic/AlamyView image in fullscreenTom Ewell and Marilyn Monroe in The Seven Year Itch (1955).Photograph: Cinematic/AlamyBook of the dayFilm booksReviewA Sudden Flicker of Light by David Thomson review – a brilliantly eccentric history of the moviesFrom the golden age of Hollywood to harder times today, this is a rapturous account of more than a century of cinemaPeter BradshawMon 20 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFilm critic, historian and author of more than 40 books, David Thomson is back with another three-hour free jazz solo on the subject of the movies, though it’s a performance delivered with the magnificent elan of a 1940s big band leader. He’s as brilliant, eccentric, exasperating and absorbing as ever.By “the movies”, incidentally, he means Hollywood, with a side order of established European and east Asian arthouse: he takes us from the cine-prehistory of photographerEadweard Muybridgeto Fritz Lang’s Metropolis, DW Griffith’s Birth of a Nation with its sinister Ku Klux Klan hero-worship, Chaplin, Stroheim, Hitchcock, Capra, Renoir, Bogart, Marilyn Monroe in The Seven-Year Itch, Monroe Stahr in The Last Tycoon, Scorsese’s GoodFellas, David Lynch’s Mulholland Drive (“Is it drive or dream?”), Kirk Douglas and his alleged rape of Natalie Wood, Barbara Loden’s Wanda, Sean Baker’s Anora, Tracey Emin’s Why I Never Became a Dancer and much more. He subtitles his book a “revisionist” history.Revisionist? Well, it certainly takes us to wintry and sombre ponderings on the movies’ (perennial) tendency to coarsen and to cheapen, to enforce cultural mob-rule. He cites King Vidor’s 1928 film The Crowd, starring James Murray, a former extra plucked from the ranks and groomed for glory but whose awful destiny was to descend into alcoholism and homelessness – back into the crowd. He emphasises movies’ amazing populist-democratic power and reach, their power embodied by the fact that, unlike in the theatre or concert hall, a film’s action could not be stopped or affected by the crowd in front of it. Like a theologian dragged by doubts to the foot of the cross, Thomson wonders if the movies will always lack literature’s way of allowing us to enlarge our imaginations, as opposed to forcing a submission to the screen.He descants on the triumph of Stephen Graham’s Netflix drama Adolescence and uses it to suggest that the big-screen theatrical experience may indeed be on the way out, because “the image on our home screens is brighter and more transporting. If you are inclined to dispute that, look very closely.” Well, I have looked closely and I do dispute it. All the same, Thomson’s contrarian, non-handwringing opinion on the subject is refreshing. Only he could have got away with that gleeful heresy, designed to wake us up to the commercial forces that brought big screens into being in the first place.So it is revisionist. In a way. But the book’s body language is non-revisionist: it’s a straightforwardly rapturous, rhapsodic celebration of the extraordinary adventure of cinema in the 20th century and beyond with an old-fashioned feel. The author is a great believer in the importance of profits and grosses, in telling us how much money the key films made, as if that proves anything, though he’s also a great respecter of the importance of Sight and Sound magazine’s decennialGreatest Film of All Timepoll.The point is that Thomson, with that marvellous ambition that is all his own, always wants to recount the whole story, to start again with that task, to encompass the giant shape in its awe-inspiring entirety, the “whole equation”, to borrow F Scott Fitzgerald’s phrase which he brilliantly used as the title for a previous book. That is his revisionism: always to take on the whole thing. He rockets through his story while firing off into the slipstream provocative generalisations and opinions, gags and barbs.He contrasts the darker moods of the American New Wave with the contemporary sunlit positivity of Steven Spielberg, who, he says, is “the most decisive figure in the history of film, a guy who came upon an art experimenting with difficulty or doubt and talked it in off the ledge”. He says that the film industry’s pious and glib dedication to the shopworn concept of “love” has brought it to an evolutionary crisis: “The species is at a point where LOVE can start to feel as hollow as HOLLYWOOD above Los Angeles.”But weirdly, Thomson has a priestly tendency to intone the names of the accepted greats and to expect a kind of cinephile genuflexion in each case. Clive James did something like it in his TV documentary series Fame in the 20th Century: he would give an introductory upward-lift of acclaim to the first mention of each new famous name, as if bringing them on to a chatshow, and there is something of that in Thomson’s prose which can stimulate, amuse and bewilder all at once.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toBookmarksDiscover new books and learn more about your favourite authors with our expert reviews, interviews and news stories. Literary delights delivered direct to youPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotiondouble quotation markAre the movies beginning to flicker in another sense, flicker out into darkness?He takes his title from a moment in Henry James’s 1881 novel The Portrait of a Lady, which he regards a classic proto-cinematic revelation: heroine Isabel Archer has married Gilbert Osmond and one day comes home to find their mutual friend Madame Merle and Osmond in an attitude of disconcerting intimacy and ease, he seated and she standing: “… the thing made an image, lasting only a moment, like a sudden flicker of light”. Thomson enthuses about that moment, whose vividness hints that James has mastered the arts of cinematographer, director and editor, and capriciously adds: “I want to bring this scene to life, with maybe Meryl Streep as Madame Merle, and who would you like as Osmond?” (It is a characteristically wilful and faintly baffling aside: Thomson appears here to disregard the excellent film version with John Malkovich as Osmond and Barbara Hershey as Madame Merle, directed by Jane Campion and starring Nicole Kidman, whose superfan biography Thomson has written. To be fair, Campion’s film doesn’t actually include that flickering moment.)So how about now? Are the movies beginning to flicker in another sense, flicker out into darkness? Well, the gloomy-elegaic insistence on the end of cinema is a tiresome kind of cinephile affectation that’s been around for decades, usually from writers who are in fact mourning the passing of their own movie-filled youth and middle age. Thomson thankfully does not indulge it. But there are flickers of anxiety whose implications the author doesn’t explore at any length.In the midst of discussing Sergei Eisenstein, whose longing to make films was shared by so many, Thomson remarks: “That longing lasted until … well, the end of the century? But I think it’s in retreat now. Do we feel we’ve seen it all?” That throwaway lands in our lap. In retreat? Seen it all? If he is serious, shouldn’t the author stop what he’s doing and pursue the point more rigorously?Well, it could be that this book is not a conventional critical work at all, nothing so workaday; it is rather quasi-fictional, like an action painting made from melted celluloid in which Thomson is the creator-hero, splashing around on the canvas in his multicoloured liquid element. Or simply an epic novel: he alludes repeatedly to EL Doctorow’s 1975 book Ragtime, about immigration and the American dream in the first years of the 20th century, that mixes real figures with imaginary ones. Here, Thomson is both the narrator and underdog, meeting with legends in the crowded streets of Los Angeles or New York, feeling their pain, sensing their euphoria, foretelling their downfall or triumph. I very much hope that this is not his last book. I can’t imagine film criticism or films without him.A Sudden Flicker of Light: A Revisionist History of the Movies by David Thomson is published by Allen Lane (£25). To support the Guardian order your copy atguardianbookshop.com. Delivery charges may apply.Explore more on these topicsFilm booksBook of the dayreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Acting the fool: why have Hollywood press tours become so gimmicky? | Movies | The Guardian
description: From playing would you rather to ranking snacks, A-listers navigating the promo trail are now forced on a set schedule of silliness
Author: Jesse Hassenger
Headline: From playing would you rather to ranking snacks, A-listers navigating the promo trail are now forced on a set schedule of silliness
Content: The Odyssey cast on The Tonight Show.Photograph: YouTubeView image in fullscreenThe Odyssey cast on The Tonight Show.Photograph: YouTubeMoviesActing the fool: why have Hollywood press tours become so gimmicky?From playing would you rather to ranking snacks, A-listers navigating the promo trail are now forced on a set schedule of sillinessJesse HassengerMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 06.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThis summer, we’ve been blessed with new movies from the legendary director Steven Spielberg and one of his biggest and most beloved successors, Christopher Nolan. In both cases, the movies have been heavily sold on the directors’ names, and, befitting two of the most famous film-makers to ever do it, Spielberg and Nolan have been making the press rounds. This has meant a wealth of interviews and insights – a bonus feature unfolding alongside the selling of the actual movies that will delight movie nerds the world over.Celebrity on celebrity: are we losing the art of the big star interview?Read moreSo at first, when I saw a60-second videoof Christopher Nolan answering a barrage of yes-or-no questions come across my social-media feed, of course I stopped and watched as a famously buttoned-up (if obviously still passionate) film-maker attempted some spontaneity with no danger of follow-up questions (or obligation to follow-up answers). From this, there is admittedly plenty of information to be gleaned: he has no designs on retiring, Tarantino-style. He will not make another superhero movie or a TV show. He’s fine with people watching movies on their phones or laptops. He will neither confirm nor deny that Interstellar is his favorite of his films. He will also neither confirm nor deny that he would like to make a Star Wars movie. (Perhaps the most intriguing, given that you’d rather expect that to be a firm “no”.)But after the rush of information subsided came a feeling of profound emptiness. One of the world’s most beloved film-makers is talking to some kid on some channel called Back2FilmSchool? And come to think of it, hasn’t he answered all of these questions before, in greater detail? He seems like a perfectly good sport in the video, but then, plenty of brave hostages probably come across with similar composure, to say nothing of those who can keep cool in the face of a nuisance. A great interview might have some friction, but it will probably not have you wondering if the subject was genuinely annoyed for the entire thing, or simply half-smiling politely. Why do so many press-tour stops these days suddenly feel like the halfway point between TikTok and a cult’s humiliation ritual?Now, not every Nolan or Spielberg interview has such punishing time limits or gimmicky hooks. In fact, if you don’t want to watch Nolan interviewed in 60 seconds, he had asegmenton 60 Minutes (technically only 13 minutes, and lamentably the version of the show run by the eminently unqualified Bari Weiss, but, you know, still a marquee attraction). Throughout the history of film promotion, stars and film-makers have participated in a range of interviews, for a range of outlets, with a range of detail or seriousness.But this year in particular, it’s not even the TikTok-iness of many interviews that feels so uniform – as mentioned, you can find the extra-long counterparts to balance out the minute-long nothing-bites. What’s become even more prevalent is what an older person might call the Fallon-ification of journalism, where a high-profile cast sits not for an interview but a series of games. If you look at the website LADBible, for example, you might assume that it’s probably not scoring interviews with Matt Damon or Anne Hathaway. (As someone who has occasionally arranged interviews for less famous people than Damon or Hathaway, for websites not even called LADBible, I can assure you this assumption would be entirely reasonable.) And it’s not – not really. It is, however, hosting a YouTube-distributed round ofWould You Ratherfeaturing some of the biggest stars in the world, under the guise of promoting their excellent film. Pursue their channel for stars doing impromptu ratings and talking about snacks. Anne Hathaway in particular will play a countless number of these games in a year when she’s appearing in five films; she’s already logged time with the LADs forThe Devil Wears Prada 2earlier this summer.It makes sense that people enjoy this stuff in its native formats: TikTok,YouTube, The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon, and so on. Fallon may be frequently insufferable, but his refresh of Jay Leno’s Tonight Show did dispense with a lot of canned interview anecdotes and accompanying shtick, all as tired as Celebrity Twister Trivia or whatever might seem now. What, is Fallon suddenly going to become an incisive and unpredictable interviewer at his age? That would be great, but it’s not happening.But as Fallon-ification and broader social-video trends feed into each other, they threaten to create less enjoyable, more inexplicable hybrid franchises, free-floating from the comforting bounds of an actual TV show. Somehow, The Tonight Show is able to do this, too, making content not actually involving Fallon himself (although maybe that makes sense; why create even a moment of suggestion that Jimmy Fallon might turn up in your adaptation of classic literature?). One recurring bit is a rhythmic, quasi-hooky, all-starplot recap, where celebs do a Fallon-approved cutesy semi-rap based on short phrases and fast cuts, assembled to form some rudimentary yet forced rhymes about the project at hand. This erases even the novelty of seeing Matt Damon, Anne Hathaway, Zendaya, Charlize Theron, Robert Pattinson, Tom Holland and Lupita Nyong’o interact, however superficially, or even watching them at any length on their own. It’s a minute-long segment by design, so you get microdoses of famous faces, reciting fragments. Has it really convinced a single person to see The Odyssey? Publicists will doubtless claim that it’s the awareness of the movie, not the specific desire to see it, that these videos aid. Attentive viewers, meanwhile, might study the screen to figure out who looks genuinely fine with it and who looks like they’re considering poisoning themselves or their agents.And look, forcible irritation of celebrities and diminishment of artists will probably always have a place in the promotional cycle. But the sheer volume of it, at a time when name publications staffed with people who genuinely enjoy and are good at conducting interviews are bought, sold and gutted on the regular, can feel particularly smug – a bonus victory pivot following yet another pivot to video putting writers out of work in favor of off-screen prompts.Of course, viewers can’t be expected to think about journalists when they’re enjoying celebrities goofing around. I am a journalist, and I don’t think about journalists when I watch literallyany videofeaturingany combinationof Saturday Night Live cast members doing anything together. What viewers might think about, however, is why, exactly, they’re watching Jon Bernthal and Charlize Theron grimace through a Fallon-sponsored assemblage of stupid rhymes. We’ve all had moments of why-did-I-click-that remorse involving online videos; I would humbly suggest that genuine interviews, while not always as revealing as you would hope, rarely produce those sinking feelings. Studios, in the meantime, are moving toward making some of the most famous faces and revered creative minds in the entertainment world the unlikely mascots for endless-scroll regret.Explore more on these topicsFilmThe OdysseyFilm industryYouTubeInternetfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Mockbuster review – how to survive the nightmare of directing a terrible low-budget horror film | Movies | The Guardian
description: Struggling to make it in Australia, aspiring director Anthony Frith cold-calls the team behind Sharknado and bites off more than he can chew in this making-of documentary
Author: Phil Hoad
Headline: Struggling to make it in Australia, aspiring director Anthony Frith cold-calls the team behind Sharknado and bites off more than he can chew in this making-of documentary
Content: Career shock therapy … director Anthony Frith.View image in fullscreenCareer shock therapy … director Anthony Frith.MoviesReviewMockbuster review – how to survive the nightmare of directing a terrible low-budget horror filmStruggling to make it in Australia, aspiring director Anthony Frith cold-calls the team behind Sharknado and bites off more than he can chew in this making-of documentaryPhil HoadMon 20 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Abad movie is better than no movie” is the motto of this behind-the-scenes documentary, which charts director Anthony Frith’s quest to make his debut feature for ultra-schlock studio The Asylum; it doubles up as an affable salute to film-making savoir-faire.Stuck making corporate videos to support his young family, Adelaide-based Frith is in danger of letting his artistic ambitions atrophy. So he opts for career shock therapy by cold-calling The Asylum (ofSharknado notoriety)in Burbank, California and proposing he oversee one of their straight-to-the-digital-cesspit features.It turns out that if working for The Asylum were a film genre, it would be the against-all-odds suicide mission type of story. Frith is given six days to complete filming on a new version of The Land That Time Forgot, which will be the studio’s first movie shot in Australia. The budget is subatomic, there is no finished script when he signs the contract and he is told, only days before the shoot, to secure his own production team. Relying on this extreme frugality to ensure their profit margins, The Asylum executives laughingly dote on their directors with quasi-sadistic fondness. Frith might just make it through this, they say – if he doesn’t try to get “artistic”.Any such notions quickly go out the window. Facing the schedule from hell, Frith canvasses survival advice from The Asylum veterans, such as actor Eric Roberts, and low-budget grafters includinghorror wonderkid Danny Philippou, who scoffs that Frith looks broken before he’s begun. On the executive side, there’s something almost refreshing in The Asylum’s cynicism – the studio’s credo is that the majority of viewers have no patience for arthouse complexity, so why should film-makers? As the LA movie ecosystem dries up, these survivalist cockroaches may well be the last outfit standing; movie-biz expediency in its most atavistic form, where “the genre is the star”.Frith can be candid too: chivvied through by Brendan Petrizzo, a faintly unsettling in-house producer with a frosted fringe, the director reflects that he may have found his natural level. Maybe … or maybe not. Presumably too harassed by shooting his disasterpiece, he sometimes struggles to break down his experience into dramatically parsable portions for this making-of documentary, leaning on voiceover to tell the story. But, ironically, he succeeds in making quite a good film; a companionable reflection on artistic aspirations, compromise and on-set camaraderie, all – true to The Asylum’s spirit – in a solid 90 minutes.Mockbuster is on digital platforms in the US now and in the UK from 27 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmDocumentary filmsHorror filmsFilm industryreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Out of Sight review – mountain idyll in the Austrian uplands is a gilded cage for distressed asylum seekers | Movies | The Guardian
description: Young men share their heartbreaking experiences of an Alpine ‘deportation camp’ in Lisa Polster’s impactful, gentle documentary
Author: Phuong Le
Headline: Young men share their heartbreaking experiences of an Alpine ‘deportation camp’ in Lisa Polster’s impactful, gentle documentary
Content: No freedom … Out of Sight.Photograph: True StoryView image in fullscreenNo freedom … Out of Sight.Photograph: True StoryMoviesReviewOut of Sight review – mountain idyll in the Austrian uplands is a gilded cage for distressed asylum seekersYoung men share their heartbreaking experiences of an Alpine ‘deportation camp’ in Lisa Polster’s impactful, gentle documentaryPhuong LeMon 20 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.13 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLocated in the Austrian Tyrol, the Bürglkopf mountain is a picture-perfect Alpine landscape, with its verdant fields and scenic trails. The tranquil idyll is a popular holiday spot, with thousands of tourists each year. Some visitors, however, are less welcome than others. Nestled among the lush forest on the mountaintop is a so-called deportation camp, where asylum seekers are held for months, even years. In Lisa Polster’s impactful documentary, one of our first glimpses of the facility and its surroundings is through the window of a moving car. The tightly composed shot suggests the secrecy that enshrouds this place, described by its inhabitants as a prison rather than a shelter.Through interviews with current residents, Polster’s film weaves a sobering tapestry of experiences – in spite of the centre’s resistance to the documentary project. Many asylum seekers speak of poor living conditions, lack of access to legal support, and the impossibility of social integration due to the camp’s remote location. Their legal limbo, caused by excessive delays in application processing, leads to further psychological distress. In one heartbreaking scene, a detainee looks wistfully at the cattle herds that graze nearby, envious of their freedom of movement. With strict curfew hours and few recreational activities on offer, time at the Bürglkopf camp stretches on seemingly infinitely.While much of the film unfolds in a straightforward talking-head style, there’s a gentleness to how the detainees are photographed. These young men are constantly demonised by the far-right media, yet in Polster’s film they are framed as slight figures against the vastness of the landscape. To rob these men of their time, the only valuable currency they can call their own, is to deny them any kind of future at all.Out of Sight is on True Story from 24 July.This article was amended on 20 July 2026 to correct the location of the camp, which is in Austria, but not on the country’s border withSwitzerlandas an earlier version said.Explore more on these topicsFilmDocumentary filmsAustriaMigrationEuropeSwitzerlandreviewsShareReuse this content

title: A Useful Ghost review – exhilarating black comedy summons ghosts of Thai past via a haunted hoover | Movies | The Guardian
description: Ratchapoom Boonbunchachoke’s stylish and unique movie features an unquiet spirit that inhabits a vacuum cleaner, before political interests intervene
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: Ratchapoom Boonbunchachoke’s stylish and unique movie features an unquiet spirit that inhabits a vacuum cleaner, before political interests intervene
Content: Disquieting, erotic and terribly sad … A Useful Ghost.Photograph: MubiView image in fullscreenDisquieting, erotic and terribly sad … A Useful Ghost.Photograph: MubiMoviesReviewA Useful Ghost review – exhilarating black comedy summons ghosts of Thai past via a haunted hooverRatchapoom Boonbunchachoke’s stylish and unique movie features an unquiet spirit that inhabits a vacuum cleaner, before political interests intervenePeter BradshawMon 20 Jul 2026 04.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 18.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHere is a stylish and exhilaratingly confident debut from 39-year-old Thai film-maker Ratchapoom Boonbunchachoke, which won thecritics’ week grand prize at Cannes last year. It is a supernatural black-comic fantasy of national grief executed with passion and panache, about the ghosts who have refused their afterlife confinementafter the country’s succeeding catastrophes: the bloody results of a military crackdown in 2010, coup d’etats in 2006 and 2014, and before them, the 1976 Thammasat University demonstrations, along with loved ones that went to an unquiet grave of their own.A Useful Ghost is heartfelt and elegant in its compositional flair; dreamlike tableaux are crisply juxtaposed in the edit and the whole effect is disquieting, erotic and terribly sad. It’s in some ways a humorous twist on the serious spiritual realities laid out in the movies of Thai master Joe Weerasethakul, likeUncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past LivesandCemetery of Splendour. And you might also sense the spectral shimmer of western films like Ghost and Truly, Madly, Deeply.Boonbunchachokeenvisions a Thailand which is vigorously effacing and pulverising its inconvenient past; roughly removing, for example, a frieze fromBangkok’s 1940 Democracy Monument, creating clouds of metaphorical toxic dust. A queer academic (Wisarut Homhuan), writing a paper on the iconography of this frieze, is astonished when his new vacuum cleaner starts coughing up the dust it has only just inhaled. Repairman Krong (Wanlop Rungkumjad) who arrives with eerie speed after his phone call to customer services, tells him the problem is that the factory which made the hoover is haunted … and we flashback to the strange story he has to tell.A worker called Tok (Krittin Thongmai) haunted this factory as a poltergeist after he collapsed on the shop floor due to ingesting poisonous dust. And the bad karma extends to the factory owner’s own family: Nat (Davika Hoorne), the young daughter-in-law of the owner’s widow Suman (Apasiri Nitibhon) died in childbirth due to dust inhalation and she returns to invade one of the factory’s vacuum cleaners; it comes to life and tries to have sex with her grieving husband March (Wisarut Himmarat) who is poignantly ready to have this consolatory contact. But Nat’s ghost is prevailed upon by Suman and then by government minister Dr Paul (Gandhi Wasuvitchayagit) to make herself useful to the state by invading the dreams of workers who are keeping all sorts of commercially and politically inconvenient ghosts alive by remembering them.Suman has to get these undesirables forgotten again – she is the ghost in the machine of repression, a ghost who the machine itself tries to turn against all the other ghosts. And there is something deeply troubling in the way Nat’s ghost can be “turned” by exploiting her yearning to have a baby, even in her own ectoplasmic state. Without the written permission that would otherwise be necessary, her widower is told he can use the eggs she had frozen for this purpose, and Nat’s ghost is presented with a macabre “death day” gift at the minister’s luxurious modernist home. It is a movie with flesh-and-blood intensity.A Useful Ghost is on Mubi from 24 July and on SBS On Demand in Australia now.Explore more on these topicsFilmDrama filmsScience fiction and fantasy filmsThailandAsia PacificreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Flavours of Iraq review – vivid childhood memories of 80s Baghdad from writer in Paris exile | Movies | The Guardian
description: Powerful animation portrays experiences of a French-Iraqi journalist Feurat Alani, with anecdotes about his father, a prominent leftist exile
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: Powerful animation portrays experiences of a French-Iraqi journalist Feurat Alani, with anecdotes about his father, a prominent leftist exile
Content: Piercingly insistent memories … Flavours of Iraq.Photograph: DocHouseView image in fullscreenPiercingly insistent memories … Flavours of Iraq.Photograph: DocHouseMoviesReviewFlavours of Iraq review – vivid childhood memories of 80s Baghdad from writer in Paris exilePowerful animation portrays experiences of a French-Iraqi journalist Feurat Alani, with anecdotes about his father, a prominent leftist exilePeter BradshawMon 20 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 02.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWith a title translated a little inelegantly from Le Parfum d’Irak to “flavours of Iraq” when “the perfume of Iraq” might have been simpler and better, this film is a powerful, vivid animation co-directed by Léonard Cohen with its author, French-Iraqi journalist Feurat Alani, robustly narrated in Alani’s first-person by the actor Malik Zidi. The animation style is stylised and expressionist: shapes and forms like silhouette cutouts suggest the piercingly insistent forms of memory.It is about Alani’s childhood and fractured family experience growing up in Paris in the 80s as the son of a prominent leftist Iraqi exile who was forced out of his native country on account of his opposition toSaddam Hussein. The story itself is told through a loose series of flashbacks occasioned by his father’s funeral, at which mourners, members of Iraq’s 1970s socialist diaspora brought together by his death, start recounting anecdotes. To these, Alani adds his own reminiscences of going back to see his extended family in Iraq at the end of the 80s, and experiencing the madeleines of those flavours and smells of Baghdad and Falluja: the delicious taste of ice-cream and the fragrances of young men’s exotic hair oil.Alani lives a double life as a next-generation Iraqi in French exile – and then, when he arrives in US-occupied Iraq as a French journalist, he is an exiled westerner in the Middle East, deeply alienated by Islamist extremism and angry at the US-led occupation that created the circumstances for this toxic jihadism (represented by a ghostly caterpillar wriggling across the screen). Perhaps inevitably, the film is at its strongest when Alani is a child, and the film is surely inspired bythe Iranian animation Persepolis by Marjane Satrapi. It has urgency and muscular force.Flavours of Iraq is at Bertha DocHouse, London from 24 July.Explore more on these topicsFilmDocumentary filmsAnimation in filmIraqFranceSaddam HusseinreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Shania Twain: Little Miss Twain review – rootsy revival strays from the dirt track to the middle of the road | Shania Twain | The Guardian
description: The country legend’s seventh album revisits her troubled early life but plays it safe with zany humour and fluffy songs about young love
Author: Katie Hawthorne
Headline: The country legend’s seventh album revisits her troubled early life but plays it safe with zany humour and fluffy songs about young love
Content: Little Eilleen … Shania Twain.Photograph: Timothy WhiteView image in fullscreenLittle Eilleen … Shania Twain.Photograph: Timothy WhiteShania TwainReviewShania Twain: Little Miss Twain review – rootsy revival strays from the dirt track to the middle of the road(Republic Nashville)The country legend’s seventh album revisits her troubled early life but plays it safe with zany humour and fluffy songs about young loveKatie HawthorneMon 20 Jul 2026 03.16 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘When I was a kid, I started on stages just like this,” Shania Twain told the 200 fanssardined into a London pub back in June, trailing her seventh album Little Miss Twain. A reintroduction to the Canadian country legend, the record mines memories from her Ontario childhood for hints of the arena-filling star she would soon become.View image in fullscreenThe artwork for Little Miss Twain.Photograph: Publicity imageOver wistful Mellotron and pedal steel, the title track marvels at little Eilleen, as she was called back then, escaping the rural north and making it big in Nashville. Yet, somehow, it all feels inevitable – as if Twain’s career was conjured into being by the conviction of her mother, who drove her to those tiny bars in a beaten-up Chevy. And yes, from the vantage point of 2026, it’s difficult to imagine it going any other way. Twain’s icon status is certified by five Grammys and the way her free-spirited hits have become karaoke classics – not to mention the fact that her 1997 album Come on Over remains the highest-selling studio album by any female artist in history.But it was far from inevitable. The first decades of Twain’s life were shaped by poverty, violence and the shocking death of her parents, which left her responsible for raising three younger siblings. Northern Town, a noirish track with upright bass and flourishes of classical guitar, gives a veiled insight into those early years, its lyrics thick with cigarette smoke and the irony of being hard-up in gold rush territory. On a double-time bridge, she fantasises about cutting loose and driving south to sing: “Just so you know, I’m ready to go,” she murmurs.Shania Twain: Dirty Rosie – videoUnfortunately, the rest of Little Miss Twain is vague on the details. Songs such as Quit My Life (an upbeat number about taking the day off) could be imagined from the perspective of young Twain weighed down by responsibility – but truthfully, there’s little to suggest it. Instead, memory lane is left in the rear-view, and Twain pivots from tender autobiography to her tried-and-tested comfort zone: zany humour and unadventurous but catchy melodies, all within the pop-rock-country Venn diagram she made her own in the 90s.Much of it feels far too safe, from the soul-lite Take It to the River, featuring Ronnie Wood and The War and Treaty, to the clutch of fluffy, overproduced tracks about young love. The best of them, New Love, has a cantering beat, blushing strings and a lovely lightness of touch, so it’s a shame its themes are rehashed on the hokey saloon song Down the Middle and the forgettable Scary Little Thing.Grab your Stetsons! How country music is taking over the UKRead moreThankfully, recent single Dirty Rosie is hoedown pop with real personality. Doing doughnuts around truck-obsessed country bro stereotypes, it puts Twain’s increasingly textured voice to great use. By contrast, I’d Be Loving Me sounds vocally strained, but in a way that brings much-needed gravity and ache to the record. Ditching the denim and dirt roads of country cliche, this deceptively simple ballad wonders how Twain would apologise if she were in the shoes of her cheating partner, and whether, roles reversed, her partner would accept that apology – all with the sinking feeling that little will change.On the title track, Twain sings of two dreams: she wanted to ride horses, while her mother wanted her to become “the next Tanya Tucker”. The two dreams intertwined: Tucker duets on those very lines, and Twain sighs over a lovely horse on the insouciant That’s the Money. Little Miss Twain may not be a return to peak powers, but amid country’s fresh crest in popularity it’s a sharply timed reminder of Shania’s rich legacy and influence. The genre would be far blander without her.Explore more on these topicsShania TwainCountryPop and rockreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Spanish National Orchestra/Afkham review – Proms debut sees orchestra’s celebrations kick off early | Proms 2026 | The Guardian
description: Outgoing chief conductor David Afkham brought Spanish repertoire, castanets and a certain team’s football scarf in a programme heavy with national treasures
Author: Alexandra Coghlan
Headline: Outgoing chief conductor David Afkham brought Spanish repertoire, castanets and a certain team’s football scarf in a programme heavy with national treasures
Content: Viva España! … David Afkham conducts the Spanish National Orchestra at the BBC Proms.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouView image in fullscreenViva España! … David Afkham conducts the Spanish National Orchestra at the BBC Proms.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouProms 2026ReviewSpanish National Orchestra/Afkham review – Proms debut sees orchestra’s celebrations kick off earlyRoyal Albert Hall, LondonOutgoing chief conductor David Afkham brought Spanish repertoire, castanets and a certain team’s football scarf in a programme heavy with national treasuresAlexandra CoghlanMon 20 Jul 2026 07.14 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 08.34 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhat a night for Spain. No, not because ofsporting happenings in New Jersey, but because – nearly 90 years since their founding – their National Orchestra finally made their Proms debut under chief conductorDavid Afkhamto a packed Royal Albert Hall. It was a beginning, but also a celebratory end: Afkham hands over the baton to Kent Nagano next season. They came prepared, suitcases stuffed with Spanish repertoire and castanets – oh, and a certain team’s football scarf, as modelled by Afkham during the encore.Afkham’s more than a decade with the orchestra hit its heights with Spanish repertoire, building on a legacy that earned the ensemble a Latin Grammy for best flamenco album under Josep Pons in 2010. Afkham didn’t stray far from the formula here in a programme heavy with national treasures, though noticeably lacking in Spanish contemporary music.View image in fullscreenGuitarist Rafael Aguirre, with Afkham conducting.Photograph: Chris ChristodoulouWe were promised the “rhythms of Spain” and they got straight to work in the wriggling seguidilla of Ravel’s Alborada del gracioso, solo bassoon playing the mournful jester. Like De Falla’s Fantasia baetica which followed (heard for the first time at the Proms in Franciso Coll’s colourful 2022 orchestration), it is originally a solo piano piece; flashiness is built in. But in these comfortable accounts, mid-paced and spacious, virtuosity became something softer and more relaxed.GuitaristRafael Aguirre(winner of an astonishing 13 first prizes at international competitions) rode this wave of sun-warmed reverie in a lean-back account of Rodrigo’s Concierto de Aranjuez. Good-natured and folk-direct in the framing outer movements, he found another colour for the wistful slow movement, his cadenza beckoning us in rather than reaching out. Sensitive to his soloist, Afkham kept the orchestra on a tight rein. The effect was oddly muted, subdued even in key moments.Two suites whipped us efficiently through the highlights of De Falla’s ballet The Three-Cornered Hat, leaving it to Ravel’s Boléro to bring us home with the most famous rhythm in the business. The work’s slow-build arc from flickering pianissimo pulse to thumping heartbeat is a tough ask in this difficult acoustic, and tension was lost as we waited for gestures to become sounds. But perhaps the real tension was off-stage last night, orchestral hearts in the US even as their heads and fingers took us into extra time with an exuberant encore from Gerónimo Giménez’s zarzuela La boda de Luis Alonso.Available on BBC Soundsuntil 12 October. The Promscontinue until 12 September.Explore more on these topicsProms 2026Classical musicBBCPromsRoyal Albert HallreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Apple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own family | Games | The Guardian
description: The developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edge
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edge
Content: Someone wants to kill grandma … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesView image in fullscreenSomeone wants to kill grandma … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesSummer games previewsGamesApple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own familyThe developer of Duck Detective reveals details of her ‘sad grandma game’ follow-up: a whodunnit – or who-plans-to-do-it? – with a darker edgeNicole CarpenterMon 20 Jul 2026 12.35 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 12.36 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSomeone wants to kill grandma. In thisKnives Out-inspired mystery game, each of your family members is suspicious in their own way: your stressed mum, your “midlife crisis uncle” and especially that stranger in your bedroom. Yikes!While Apple Crumble is still a mystery, it is mechanically different from Happy Broccoli Games’ previous game,Duck Detective, in which the player solves crimes in a small office. Here you are largely snooping through your family home. “I love the idea of having a walking sim where you can really open up every drawer, every door, and see what’s behind there,” says founder and creative director Annika Maar. As you learn more about the different grievances and troubles of your family members, the mystery slowly unwinds.View image in fullscreen‘You can open up every drawer, every door, and see what’s behind there’ … Apple Crumble.Photograph: Happy Broccoli GamesAfter Duck Detective, the team wanted to make a “sad grandma” game – something that may come as a surprise after the humour of the studio’s previous games. Apple Crumble begins comedically, says Maar, but the tone shifts throughout the game; it gets more serious and a bit sad: “I felt very emotionally attached to the death of my grandma. There’s some baggage there and I thought it would be fun to explore that.”You’ll pick up the plot by digging through stuff. Lots of stuff. Apple Crumble’s storytelling extends into the different made-up fiction placed throughout the house. “There’s a film script that the midlife crisis uncle wrote which he’s convinced is going to get money from investors and become huge,” says Maar. “Just so many small details that I’m hoping will make the world feel very believable.”That depth of hidden detail also applies to the art style, which was inspired, in part, by concept art for Puss in Boots. “In the movie and the concept art, they use a lot of these paint splatters,” says Maar. “From a distance it’s not immediately apparent, but when you look, you’re like, ‘Wow, there are so many different colours in this.’ Your eyes kind of blend it together and it just makes it feel more natural. Less clinical. More exciting to look at. It ended up being a lot more work than I thought it would be, but I think it was worth it.”Maar is currently finishing up the script and will soon begin the process of recording voice actors. Happy Broccoli is booking the actors together so that they can really riff off each other. After all, with a grandma-killer in their midst, there are a lot of arguments to record.Apple Crumble is in development forPC. Release is planned for later in 2026Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsPCfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Chris Packham takes on a terrifying crime ring: best podcasts of the week | Television & radio | The Guardian
description: The popular broadcaster investigates what happened after a large pile of murdered animals were dumped outside a primary school in Hampshire. Plus, Lena Headey tackles an idea from an ‘ethical pornographer’
Author: Alexi Duggins
Headline: The popular broadcaster investigates what happened after a large pile of murdered animals were dumped outside a primary school in Hampshire. Plus, Lena Headey tackles an idea from an ‘ethical pornographer’
Content: Chris Packham, one of the stars of Buried: Dead Rabbit.Photograph: Dan Kitwood/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenChris Packham, one of the stars of Buried: Dead Rabbit.Photograph: Dan Kitwood/Getty ImagesBest podcasts of the weekTelevision & radioChris Packham takes on a terrifying crime ring: best podcasts of the weekThe popular broadcaster investigates what happened after a large pile of murdered animals were dumped outside a primary school in Hampshire. Plus, Lena Headey tackles an idea from an ‘ethical pornographer’Alexi Duggins,Hollie RichardsonandHannah J DaviesMon 20 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 02.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePick of the weekBuried: Dead RabbitMangled pheasants, mutilated foxes, weeping kids: the third series of the multi-award-winning show about environmental crime opens with a huge pile of murdered animals outside a primary school. The investigation leads to revelations about the world of illegal bloodsports and rural organised crime. It even recruits Chris Packham. “It’s low-level terrorism, isn’t it?” he exclaims at one point – and it’s hard not to agree. An essentiallisten.AlexiDugginsBBC Sounds, all episodes availableMystic MatesSecretly curious about tarot cards, crystals, astrology, reading tea leaves and the spiritual world? For actor mates Helen George and Yvette Robinson, this hobby has been a “dirty little secret” for 20 years. Now they’re ready to ask the big questions andshareexperiences – while having chummy fun.Hollie RichardsonWidely available, episodes weeklyIntimacyView image in fullscreenChanging everything … Lena Headey.Photograph: Maya Dehlin Spach/WireImage“This job is going to change everything!” So says Liza (Lena Headey), an intimacy coordinator with a major film gig that quickly becomes disturbingly personal. Written and directed by Headey, this funny, shockingaudio dramais based on an idea by “ethical pornographer” Erika Lust and is executive produced by Sharon Horgan. Hannah Waddingham, Alison Steadman and Maisie Williams also star.Hannah J DaviesBBC Sounds, episodes weeklyPretty Scary With Clair Buckley“Some people relax with a bubble bath or a meditation app; I relax by reading about things lurking in the woods …” Clair Buckley, best known forpodcastingwith her actor husband James, branches out with a series about unexplained mysteries, UFOs, true crime and more. While the premise isn’t that original, she is an excellent, eerie compere.HJDWidely available, episodes weeklyskip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toWhat's OnGet the best TV reviews, news and features in your inbox every MondayPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionDoing It AnywaySelf-belief is a passion project for the host of The Rest Is Politics US, Katty Kay, who has penned books on the subject. Each weekshe tacklesissues around confidence through an expert interview, starting with Mika Brzezinski, founder of the Know Your Value movement – a community helping women succeed at work.ADWidely available, episodes weeklyExplore more on these topicsTelevision & radioBest podcasts of the weekPodcastsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: A Canterbury trail: my pilgrimage from the cathedral to the white cliffs of Dover | Canterbury holidays | The Guardian
description: After exploring the cathedral city, I battled the heatwave while walking to the Kent coast on the Via Francigena
Author: Peter Ross
Headline: After exploring the cathedral city, I battled the heatwave while walking to the Kent coast on the Via Francigena
Content: Hiking along the top of the white cliffs of Dover.Photograph: Mark Sunderland Photography/AlamyView image in fullscreenHiking along the top of the white cliffs of Dover.Photograph: Mark Sunderland Photography/AlamyCanterbury holidaysA Canterbury trail: my pilgrimage from the cathedral to the white cliffs of DoverAfter exploring the cathedral city, I battled the heatwave while walking to the Kent coast on the Via FrancigenaPeter RossMon 20 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Mon 20 Jul 2026 11.26 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCanterbury for Evensong. Is there a more delightful cluster of syllables in the English language? Old English, I should say, asæfensangcomes from that tongue, an update on the Latin-derived “vespers”. But whatever you call the service, Canterbury Cathedral does it in style. How beautiful the singing from the choristers in their white and purple. How beautiful the light as it honeys the stone of the quire. I had been in the city only an hour or so, and considered this early evening worship a sort of overture for my stay.What a relief to get inside the great stone building. The lovely old city was horrible with heat. A statue of Chaucer on High Street appeared overdressed in robe, cloak and hat. He held out a page inscribed with the opening lines of The Canterbury Tales: “When in April the sweet showers fall … Then people long to go on pilgrimages.” The bronze had been hot to the touch and I began to suspect that I’d made a mistake starting my own pilgrimage at midsummer.Walking to the cathedral, I had passed the former site of the Royal Fountain, where the travel writer HV Morton stayed in 1939. Morton chose the inn because it was quiet, Canterbury being very noisy in his experience, forever busy with tourist traffic. “And who can deny,” he wrote, “that the mobility given to masses of people by the cheap motor-car and the motor-bus has revived a wave of secular pilgrimage in Protestant England within the past 20 years and multiplied relics in a way unparalleled since the heyday of the Middle Ages?”View image in fullscreen‘Medieval air’ … the nave of Canterbury CathedralPhotograph: AlamyOne could voice similar sentiments now. Pilgrimage is having a moment and has been for a while. According toYouGov research for the British Pilgrimage Trustin 2025, 3.7 million British adults have already made a pilgrimage, and almost one in five are considering making one. What is going on?Torin Brown is the man to ask. The pilgrim officer at the cathedral meets many arriving in the city on foot and others for whom it is the start of a long, challenging walk. “We’ve seen a resurgence in pilgrimage in the last 10 years, especially since Covid. People are looking back to the old ways in their search for meaning. Even those who declare themselves atheists are engaging in this ancient religious ritual of walking to sacred spaces.”double quotation markWe’ve seen a resurgence in pilgrimage, especially since Covid. People are looking to the old ways in their search for meaningCanterbury’s popularity as a pilgrimage site was accelerated by the murder in the cathedral of archbishop Thomas Becket in 1170 and his speedy elevation to sainthood. His shrine was among the most popular in Europe.There are a number of historic routes to Canterbury, among them thePilgrims’ Wayfrom Winchester, theOld Wayfrom Southampton, and theAugustine Caminofrom Rochester. Sarah Mullally, the new archbishop, spent six dayswalking from St Paul’s Cathedralahead of her installation in March this year. My plan was to walk the English leg of theVia Francigena, which links Canterbury with Rome. This would mean reaching Dover in a day – about 20 miles. I had an OS map, theVia Francigena app, and a pilgrim passport, which Torin stamped before my departure. “You are now part of this wonderful history and tradition of pilgrimage,” he said.I wouldn’t be leaving until the following day, so there was time for a proper look around the cathedral. They were tuning the organ, the building echoing with eerie drones and groans, which somehow gave a medieval air. I sat in the nave, beneath a vault built during the reign of Henry IV, and watched as a woman with a silver bob worked her way down the south aisle, cleaning memorials and tombs. She had a feather duster in one hand, a yellow cloth in the other and carried a pale blue handbag in the crook of her left arm. Her name, she told me, was Cilla.View image in fullscreenThe Geoffrey Chaucer statue in Canterbury.Photograph: Slawek Staszczuk /Alamy“I’m one of the Holy Dusters! We’re the lowest of the low!” In the cathedral hierarchy, she said, the volunteer cleaners were below the florists and the welcomers. “And the older I get, the more that suits me.”Cilla didn’t seem the sort of lady whose age one asks, so I contented myself with observing that the building was beautiful. “Isn’t it! I grew up in Salisbury, so I’m used to a more modern cathedral, but this is growing on me. Well, it would after 47 years.”double quotation markThe sun, so gentle at rising, turned vicious. Nine felt like noon. What would noon feel like?And she went on her way up the north aisle, dusting recumbent effigies of archbishops and deans, not neglecting to wipe beneath the ruff ofSir John Boys, who died in 1612. Forgetmemento mori, here was the true equality of the grave, every great man coming to the same humble pass – his stone face tickled by the feathers of the Holy Duster.Next morning, I was out at first light. The heatwave was intensifying and I wanted to get ahead of the sun. A gull sat high on the cathedral gate, looking down as I touched the scallop shell, symbol of pilgrimage, carved into the wooden door.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Twice a weekSign up toThe TravellerGet travel inspiration, featured trips and local tips for your next break, as well as the latest deals from Guardian HolidaysEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe route is waymarked with the logo of the Via Francigena: a pilgrim carrying a staff. It follows theNorth Downs Way, so you can also look for the acorn sign.Mist lay in the fields, working its alchemy. Copses became floating islands. Pylons were robot ramblers; I envied the length of their stride. Still, despite my shorter pace, I reached Shepherdswell by 8am and refilled my water bottle from a tap set into the church wall.I had thought of stopping at this village and catching the train to Dover. To press on meant another 10 miles. But I was too much in love with the idea of walking to the coast – until the land, not my legs, gave out.View image in fullscreenThe Via Francigena walk follows the route of the North Downs Way.Photograph: Kay Roxby/AlamyOver the next hours, I had cause to regret that decision. The sun, so gentle at rising, turned vicious. Nine felt like noon. What would noon feel like? The earth in field paths was hard and cracked. Nodules of flint lay around like bones. I considered lying down and adding my own. Pilgrimage? More like an act of atonement. Henry II, in penance for Becket’s murder, walked barefoot into Canterbury and was whipped by the monks. That was my whole vibe now.Once in a while, I’d pass a garden and someone would look up from watering their roses and say good morning. I said it back, but wondered: “How can itstillbe morning?” Worst of all was a man floating in his pool, happy as a hippo in a mudhole. I was too Scottish and reserved to ask if I could join him.Arriving in Dover at midday, I went to the museum to get my pilgrim passport stamped. If continuing the Via Francigena, I’d catch a ferry to Calais. But I had another destination in mind. I passed a sign saying Welcome to England and another for Dame Vera Lynn Way and then there they were – the White Cliffs. So bright, so beautiful. The Channel was a blue haze; France a shadow.I hope one day, before long, to be on the other side of that water, exploring the cathedrals of Europe. But, for now, this footsore pilgrim was on a more humdrum quest: ice-cream and a pack of blister plasters.Explore more on these topicsCanterbury holidaysKent holidaysWalking holidaysWalkingEngland holidaysFitnessHealth & wellbeingfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Rukmini Iyer’s quick and easy recipe for courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishes | Food | The Guardian
description: Perfect for an al fresco lunch spread, these bruschetta come together as quickly as they disappear …
Author: Rukmini Iyer
Headline: Perfect for an al fresco lunch spread, these bruschetta come together as quickly as they disappear …
Content: Rukmini Iyer’s courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishes.Photograph: Beca B Jones/The Guardian: Food stylist: El Kemp. Prop stylist: Florence Blair. Food stylist assistant: Caitlin Macdonald.View image in fullscreenRukmini Iyer’s courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishes.Photograph: Beca B Jones/The Guardian: Food stylist: El Kemp. Prop stylist: Florence Blair. Food stylist assistant: Caitlin Macdonald.Quick and easyFoodRukmini Iyer’s quick and easy recipe for courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishesPerfect for an al fresco lunch spread, these bruschetta come together as quickly as they disappear …Rukmini IyerMon 20 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFresh, light but filling – these bruschetta are beautiful to bring outside as part of a lunch spread. They take minutes to whip together (sorry) and, for extra Marie Antoinette at Le Petit Trianon vibes, pick mint (or/and baby courgettes) from your own garden. You can get ahead by making the whipped feta and lemon dressing in advance, and keeping the courgette ribbons and radishes in cold water until needed. Drain and dress the courgettes just before serving – they’re lovely with the contrast of the toasted baguette and cool cheese.Courgette and whipped feta bruschetta with radishesPrep10 minCook15 minServes4 as a starter/side1 small yellow courgette1 small green courgette1bunch radishes, halved8 slices good baguette200g feta, roughly broken100g natural yoghurtZest of ½ lemon,plus 15ml lemon juice15ml extra-virgin olive oilBig pinch sea salt flakesUse a speed peeler to peel the courgette into thin ribbons, then transfer them to a bowl of cold water along with the halved radishes (this will help crisp them up).Toast the baguette slices in a single layer under a hot grill (I set mine at 200C) for two minutes, until well toasted on one side.Put the feta, yoghurt and lemon zest in a food processor, and blitz smooth. Alternatively, mash the feta in a large bowl with a fork, then whisk in the yoghurt and lemon zest as furiously as possible.Whisk the lemon juice, olive oil and salt until they’re a pale, buttercup yellow. Drain the courgettes and radishes well. Gently pat the courgettes dry with kitchen paper or a clean tea towel, then toss them with the dressing.Spread the untoasted side of the baguette slices with some of the whipped feta and arrange the courgette ribbons on top. Put the remaining whipped feta in a bowl and serve with the radishes alongside for dipping.Explore more on these topicsFoodQuick and easyCheeseVegetablesVegetarian food and drinkrecipesShareReuse this content

title: Pesto Trapanese, pasta pomodoro and torta caprese: Dara Klein’s favourite Italian recipes | Food | The Guardian
description: Rustic, authentic and utterly delicious – three dishes that bring the taste of the trattoria straight to your table
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Rustic, authentic and utterly delicious – three dishes that bring the taste of the trattoria straight to your table
Content: Dara Klein's pesto trapanese.Photograph: Ola O Smit/The Guardian. Food styling: Aya Nishimura. Prop styling: Polly Webb-Wilson. Food styling assistant: Laura Lawrence.View image in fullscreenDara Klein's pesto trapanese.Photograph: Ola O Smit/The Guardian. Food styling: Aya Nishimura. Prop styling: Polly Webb-Wilson. Food styling assistant: Laura Lawrence.FoodPesto Trapanese, pasta pomodoro and torta caprese: Dara Klein’s favourite Italian recipesRustic, authentic and utterly delicious – three dishes that bring the taste of the trattoria straight to your tableDara KleinMon 20 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePesto trapanese combines almonds, tomatoes, pecorino and a hint of garlic and basil to create a fresher version thanthe typical Genovese variety, and I especially love the way the richness of the almonds is lifted by the fresh tomatoes – when they’re pureed together, it creates a wonderful mouthfeel. For me, though, pasta pomodoro symbolises home. My mum’s was a cornerstone of our trattoria, as it is at Italian restaurants around the world, and every family has their own fiercely guarded recipe. I developed this particular method when I startedPasta Princess, which delivered hand-rolled pasta and pomodoro sauce all over London during lockdown. It kept me grounded when I was in between kitchens, and resurfaced at Tiella, where it features regularly on the menu. Finally, torta caprese is a wonderfully rich flourless chocolate cake made with ground almonds. Mum often made a similar cake called torta gianduia, which uses hazelnuts instead of almonds, a recipe that hails from Piedmont, the heart of Italy’s hazelnut production (the Tonda Gentile delle Langhe variety is particularly prized for its incredible quality and flavour, carrying the IGP label to denote regionality and strict production standards). I remember her once buying these special hazelnuts on a trip to Italy, and taking them back home to New Zealand so she could makegianduia. When the cake came out of the oven, the aroma was so incredible that she decided to bring it home rather than putting it on the menu, and shared it with us instead.Pesto trapanese (pictured top)I prefer to make this in a mortar to achieve a rustic paste. You can use a blender, but operate it gently so you don’t end up with a smooth paste. Once the pesto is tossed with the pasta, finish with a touch of lemon juice and black pepper just before plating to add some brightness. You can omit the cheese for a dairy-free pesto: it still blends beautifully, and has a greater concentration of almonds, tomatoes and basil.Prep10 minCook30 minServes485g blanched almondsSea salt and freshly ground black pepper250g tomatoes,finely diced75ml extra-virgin olive oil, plus extra to serve1 garlic clove,peeled, halved and germ removed10g basil,leaves picked, plus extra to serve45g pecorino, grated, plus extra to serve320-400g pasta– busiate, penne, spaghetti, bucatini or fusilliLemon juice,to serveHeat the oven to 180C (160C fan)/350F/gas 4.
